content: This is your study system. 25 domains across 8 levels, organized by dependency — each level builds on the one before it. Links point to either existing prep files (in the parent folder) or new domain files (in this folder). Depth tiers are marked on each domain: - 🔴 DEEP — You will be tested hard. Know cold, explain fluently, apply under pressure. - 🟡 WELL — Confident conversation. Can explain clearly, answer follow-ups, give examples. - 🟢 AWARE — Know it exists, what it does, and why. 2-3 sentences max. ---

Equities (earnings, sectors, rotation, crowding), fixed income (duration, convexity, curve), credit (IG/HY, spread deterioration signals), FX (pairs, carry, intervention), commodities (physical/financial, storage, cracks, seasonality). → A Cash Instruments 08 Asset Classes And Portfolios

Every product in detail: crude (WTI, Brent, Dated Brent, Dubai), refined products (RBOB, ULSD, cracks), natural gas by region (HH, TTF, NBP, JKM), US power markets (ISOs, merit order, spark spread), base metals (LME copper, aluminium, nickel), precious metals (gold real-rate relationship), agriculture (US/EU/Asia), contract sizes, spreads, RM monitoring. → A2 Commodities Deep Dive

Futures, options, swaps, exotics (awareness). Pricing: Black-Scholes, binomial, Monte Carlo, risk-neutral, no-arbitrage. Strategies: spreads, straddles, strangles, collars, butterflies, risk reversals — payoffs, when to use. → B Derivatives 15 Derivatives Deep Dive

Trade lifecycle, listed vs OTC, margin (IM/VM/maintenance), leverage (how funds lever, why it kills), liquidity (bid-ask, market impact, cascade), dealer positioning & order flow. → C Market Mechanics 09 Market Intuition 14 Hedge Fund Mechanics ---

Delta, gamma, vega, theta, rho. Second-order: vanna, volga, charm, speed. Position Greeks, portfolio Greeks, interactions. Long vs short gamma dynamics. → D Greeks 07 Risk Reference 18 Round1 Trader BattlePrep (Section 1)

Implied vs realized, vol risk premium, surface/skew/term structure, SABR/Heston/local vol calibration, vol as tradeable (straddles, VIX, var swaps), dealer gamma dynamics. → E Volatility 07 Risk Reference 03 Natixis Defense Guide (SABR) 18 Round1 Trader BattlePrep (Section 6) ---

VaR (3 methods + limitations), ES/CVaR, stress testing (3 types), DV01/CS01, Sharpe/Sortino/Calmar, drawdown, alpha/beta decomposition. → F Risk Measures 07 Risk Reference 18 Round1 Trader BattlePrep (Section 2)

Correlation (unconditional vs conditional), concentration (hidden), basis risk, liquidity risk (funding vs market), factor exposure, tail risk/fat tails. → G Portfolio Risk 18 Round1 Trader BattlePrep (Section 3)
Decomposing daily P&L into delta/gamma/vega/theta/rho/residual. Explaining the P&L to a PM. Finding and investigating the unexplained. → H PnL Attribution ---

Puts, collars, spreads, futures, pairs. Decision framework (horizon, upside tolerance, cost, which risk specifically). Dynamic hedging & rebalancing. Stop-loss limitations. → I Hedging Strategies 18 Round1 Trader BattlePrep (Section 5) 08 Asset Classes And Portfolios
Sizing vs conviction, broken thesis (3 tests), drawdown / risk budgets per PM, risk budgeting frameworks. → J Position Management 18 Round1 Trader BattlePrep (Section 5) 14 Hedge Fund Mechanics

Anchoring, confirmation bias, loss aversion, disposition effect, overconfidence, thesis refitting. Recognizing them in real time. The difference between a PM who's right-and-early vs wrong-and-stubborn. → K Behavioral Biases ---
LP/GP, management fee, performance fee, HWM, clawback, NAV, redemptions/gates/lock-ups, prime brokerage, repo, CCPs, margin. → L Fund Structure 14 Hedge Fund Mechanics

How each works, how RM differs between them, Squarepoint's model (systematic core + discretionary build-out), your role embedded with traders. → M Disc vs Systematic 05 Inside Systematic Fund 06 Inside Discretionary Fund
CVA/DVA, wrong-way risk, ISDA/CSA/netting. Fat finger, settlement risk, system failures. Awareness level. → NO Counterparty Op Regulatory
FRTB (ES replacing VaR), Basel III/IV, Dodd-Frank, EMIR, MiFID — why they exist, how they constrain. Awareness only. → NO Counterparty Op Regulatory ---

GDP, employment, inflation mechanics (core/headline, CPI components, transmission), monetary policy (how rate changes flow through the economy), fiscal policy, credit cycle, business cycle phases. WHY things move — not just THAT they moved. → P Macro Fundamentals

Cascade chains (oil → inflation → rates → equities → FX → EM), correlation regimes, flight to quality, risk-on/risk-off, carry trade unwind dynamics. → Q Cross Asset Relationships 09 Market Intuition 10 Market Context Today
All numbers cold. Iran/Hormuz cascade. Catalysts (CPI Friday, April 29-30 CB gauntlet). Q1 hedge fund drawdowns. The full cross-asset picture. → R Current Market Context 10 Market Context Today
Reading a blotter, prioritizing risks, red flags, first-day-on-the-desk workflow. What GOOD risk management looks like vs bad. Being useful, not just correct. → S Practical Risk Judgment

Forming and defending a risk-informed market view. "What does the Fed do April 29 and what does it mean for our book?" Having a view ≠ being a trader. Having no view = being useless. → T Opinion Formation
Live scenario practice. Oil spike, PM drawdown, vol breach, correlation breakdown. The framework: facts → exposure check → scenarios → communication → recommendation. → U Scenario Analysis

LTCM, Archegos, Barings, London Whale, Amaranth, March 2020 Treasury basis, April 2020 negative oil. Each one mapped to the concepts it illustrates. Lessons that apply to your daily work. → V Case Studies ---

Stationarity, autocorrelation, GARCH/EWMA, PCA/factor decomposition, backtesting (in-sample/out-of-sample), overfitting, model validation (Kupiec), alpha decay, signal vs noise. Not doing their job — challenging their output intelligently. → W Quant Mindset
Distributions (normal, log-normal, fat tails), Bayes, hypothesis testing, regression, correlation vs causation, p-values, confidence intervals. → X Math Stats Probability
Python (pandas/numpy — practical data manipulation), SQL (joins, aggregation, window functions), kdb+ (awareness). Practical, not leetcode. → Y Coding Data ---
Resume walkthrough (3 versions, timed, out loud). Story bank (17 STAR stories). Probe defense for every resume claim. Squarepoint knowledge. Questions to ask by round. → Z Personal Narrative ---

A (Instruments), D (Greeks), F (Risk Measures), G (Portfolio Risk), I (Hedging), P (Macro), Q (Cross-Asset), R (Market Context), S (Practical Judgment), T (Opinion Formation), U (Scenario Analysis), Z (Delivery)

B (Derivatives Pricing/Strategies), C (Market Mechanics), E (Volatility), H (P&L Attribution), J (Position Management), K (Behavioral Biases), L (Fund Mechanics), M (Disc vs Sys), W (Quant Mindset), X (Math/Stats)
N (Counterparty/Op Risk), O (Regulatory), Y (kdb+) ---

Round Primary Domains Secondary --- --- --- Round 1 — Trader A, D, I, P, Q, R, S, T, U, Z B, C, E, G, K, V Round 2 — Senior RM F, G, J, S, V, Z A, H, K, L, M Round 3 — Quant Researcher W, X, Y B, D, E, F, H Round 4 — Constantine S, T, Z L, M, Q, R, V ---

Once you've studied all domains, you should be able to handle this in 90 seconds without notes: > "I'm long $80M in a single energy name, short $30M in index puts, and oil just spiked 8%. Walk me through what I should be thinking about." That requires: A (commodities), D (delta/gamma on the short puts), G (concentration + correlation), I (is the index put acting as a hedge or adding risk?), S (prioritize what to flag first), and U (scenario: what if oil goes to +15%?) — all at once, in real time. If you can do that, you're ready.

> One-liner: A stock is an ownership claim on a company's residual cash flows — you get what's left after everyone else (debt holders, employees, government) has been paid. Analogy: Imagine a company is a building with tenants. Bondholders are the bank that holds the mortgage — they get paid first no matter what. Equity holders are the building owner — they keep whatever rent is left after the mortgage, maintenance, and taxes. In a good year, they keep a lot. In a bad year, they might keep nothing. If the building burns down, the bank gets the insurance payout first; the owner gets whatever (if anything) is left. That's why equity is the riskiest part of the capital structure — but also why it has the highest return potential. Where equity sits in the capital structure: Senior Secured Debt ← paid first, lowest risk, lowest return Senior Unsecured Debt Subordinated Debt Mezzanine / Convertibles Preferred Equity Common Equity ← paid LAST, highest risk, highest return If a company goes bankrupt, recoveries follow this exact order. Equity holders typically recover 0-10 cents on the dollar. Senior secured lenders often recover 60-80 cents. This hierarchy matters because it explains why stocks are volatile and why credit analysis starts from the top of the stack.

Type What it is Key feature --- --- --- Common stock Standard ownership share Voting rights, residual claim, dividends not guaranteed Preferred stock Hybrid between equity and debt Fixed dividend (like a coupon), senior to common in liquidation, usually no voting rights. Trades more like a bond — sensitive to interest rates. ADR / GDR Foreign stock traded on US/global exchanges Represents shares in a foreign company (e.g., BABA ADR = claim on Alibaba shares held by a depositary bank). Introduces FX risk. ETF Basket of securities that trades like a stock SPY tracks S&P 500. Offers diversification but can create crowding (passive flows all hitting the same names). Can trade at premium/discount to NAV. REIT Company that owns income-producing real estate Must distribute 90%+ of taxable income. Sensitive to rates (higher rates → higher discount rate → REIT prices fall). Why the RM cares about equity type: - Preferred stock looks like equity on the balance sheet but behaves like a bond — don't confuse them when measuring rate sensitivity - ADRs have hidden FX exposure — the underlying shares are denominated in another currency - ETFs can create forced buying/selling from rebalancing flows — index inclusion/exclusion events cause massive volume

> The master equation: Price = Earnings × Multiple. Full stop. Every stock price movement is either earnings changing, the multiple changing, or both. What is the multiple? It's how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. A P/E of 20x means "$20 for every $1 of annual earnings." The multiple reflects: - Growth expectations — higher expected growth → higher multiple (you're paying today for future earnings) - Interest rates — higher rates → lower multiple (future cash flows are discounted more heavily) - Risk/uncertainty — more uncertainty → lower multiple (investors demand a discount) - Sentiment — fear lowers multiples, greed raises them, independent of fundamentals Analogy: If a friend's lemonade stand earns $1,000/year, how much would you pay to buy it? If you think she'll double her revenue next year, maybe $25,000 (25x). If you think the city is about to ban lemonade stands, maybe $5,000 (5x). Same earnings, different multiples — because your expectations changed.

Approach What it does When to use it Limitation --- --- --- --- Relative valuation (multiples) Compare P/E, EV/EBITDA to peers or history Quick and dirty, most common "Cheap relative to expensive peers" isn't necessarily cheap DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) Project future free cash flows, discount to present value at WACC Theoretically correct, used for deep analysis Extremely sensitive to terminal value and discount rate assumptions — small changes in inputs → massive changes in output Sum-of-the-parts Value each business segment separately and add them up Conglomerates, companies with diverse divisions Ignores synergies/dis-synergies between segments The DCF in plain English: A company is worth the sum of all the cash it will ever generate, discounted back to today. If a company will generate $100M per year forever, and your discount rate is 10%, it's worth $100M / 0.10 = $1B. The discount rate (WACC) is critical — it reflects both the time value of money and the risk of the cash flows. Higher risk → higher discount rate → lower valuation. $$\text{Value} = \sum {t=1}^{n} \frac{FCF t}{(1 + WACC)^t} + \frac{\text{Terminal Value}}{(1 + WACC)^n}$$ The dirty secret of DCF: 60-80% of the value typically comes from the terminal value (everything beyond your forecast horizon). That's a single number driven by an assumed perpetual growth rate. Change it from 2% to 3% and your valuation can jump 30%. This is why DCFs can be used to justify almost any price — and why good analysts stress-test the terminal assumptions ruthlessly.

section: Key Equity Metrics — What They Actually Tell You
content: Metric Formula What it really means When it misleads --- --- --- --- P/E Price / EPS "How many years of current earnings am I paying for?" A P/E of 20 = 20 years of today's earnings. Useless for companies with negative earnings. Distorted by one-time items. Forward P/E Price / Next year's expected EPS More forward-looking than trailing P/E — what you're paying for FUTURE earnings Only as good as the earnings estimate (analyst consensus is often wrong) EV/EBITDA (Market cap + Debt - Cash) / EBITDA "How much am I paying for the operating business, regardless of how it's financed?" Better than P/E for comparing companies with different leverage. EBITDA ignores capex — a company spending all its EBITDA on capex isn't actually generating cash Free Cash Flow Yield FCF / Market cap "What cash return am I getting?" A 5% FCF yield = the company generates 5 cents of free cash for every dollar of market cap. Higher = cheaper. FCF can be manipulated by delaying capex or stretching payables Dividend Yield Annual dividend / Price Current income. A 3% yield means $3/year per $100 invested. High yield can signal distress (price dropped, not dividend raised). "Yield trap." Short Interest Shares short / Float How many investors are betting the stock will fall. >15% is elevated. >25% is extreme squeeze risk. High short interest might be right — the company might actually be in trouble Beta Cov(stock, market) / Var(market) Sensitivity to broad market moves. Beta 1.3 = stock moves 30% more than the market on average. Backward-looking. Beta changes in stress (everything becomes high-beta in a crash).

section: Market Cap Segments — Why Size Matters
content: Segment Market Cap Typical characteristics --- --- --- Mega cap >$200B AAPL, MSFT, NVDA. Extremely liquid. Index heavyweights — when SPX moves, these drive it. Large cap $10B-$200B Core of most portfolios. Reasonable liquidity. Well-covered by analysts. Mid cap $2B-$10B Sweet spot for stock pickers — less analyst coverage = more potential for mispricing. Less liquid. Small cap $300M-$2B Higher return potential but much wider bid-ask spreads, harder to exit in stress. Higher idiosyncratic risk. Micro cap <$300M Very illiquid. Easier to manipulate. Most hedge funds can't trade these at size. Why the RM cares: Position sizing must account for liquidity. A $50M position in AAPL can be exited in minutes. A $50M position in a small cap might take days or weeks to unwind — and trying to exit fast moves the price against you. ADV (Average Daily Volume) is the key metric: as a rule of thumb, your position shouldn't exceed 10-15% of ADV unless you're comfortable being locked in.

When you own a stock, you're exposed to multiple overlapping risks: - Market risk (systematic / beta): The tide that lifts or sinks all boats. When SPX drops 5%, a stock with beta 1.2 drops ~6%. You cannot diversify this away — it's the risk you're compensated for holding. - Sector risk: Stocks in the same industry are correlated. Energy moves with oil. Banks move with interest rates and credit quality. Tech moves with growth expectations. Sector concentration is a hidden risk — "I have 30 stocks" doesn't mean diversified if 20 are tech. - Idiosyncratic risk (company-specific): Earnings miss, accounting fraud, CEO dies, FDA rejects the drug. This is diversifiable — across 30+ stocks, idiosyncratic shocks roughly cancel out. But in a concentrated portfolio (10-15 names), a single blow-up can be catastrophic. - Factor risk: Academics decompose equity returns into systematic factors: - Value (cheap vs expensive by book/price): value stocks tend to outperform long-term but can underperform for years - Momentum (recent winners vs losers): what's been going up continues going up — until momentum crashes (violent and sudden reversals) - Size (small vs large cap): small caps have historically outperformed but with much higher volatility - Quality (high vs low profitability): high-quality companies with stable margins tend to be defensive - Low volatility : low-vol stocks have historically beaten high-vol stocks (the "low-vol anomaly") — contradicts the idea that more risk = more return The RM takeaway on factor risk: If a PM says "I'm diversified across 40 names," check factor exposure. They might be long 40 different high-momentum growth stocks — that's one big factor bet dressed up as diversification. The 2020 momentum crash hit these portfolios hard.

> One-liner: Crowding is when too many funds own the same stocks — the trade works until everyone tries to exit through the same door at the same time. How to spot it: - 13F filings — quarterly hedge fund holdings disclosures. If 40 funds own the same name, that's crowded. - Short interest — >20% of float short = crowded short. - Factor scores — if a stock scores high on every factor screen (momentum, growth, quality), every quant fund owns it. Why it's dangerous: - Crowded longs: one fund liquidating forces selling → price drops → other funds' risk limits trigger → they sell too → cascade. August 2007 quant meltdown was exactly this. - Crowded shorts: stock rises → shorts cover (buy) → price rises more → more covering → short squeeze. GME (2021) is the extreme case. - Crowding turns idiosyncratic risk into systemic risk — positions that look independent are actually correlated through ownership. ---

> One-liner: A bond is a loan that you can trade — the issuer borrows money from you, pays you regular interest (coupons), and returns your principal at maturity. Analogy: A bond is like lending your friend $1,000. They agree to pay you $50/year in interest (5% coupon), and after 10 years they'll return the $1,000. The difference from a personal loan: you can sell your "IOU" to someone else if you want your money back early. The price they'll pay depends on what interest rates look like at the time. The fundamental relationship: Bond prices and yields move in OPPOSITE directions. This is not a correlation — it's an identity. A bond is a promise to pay fixed cash flows. When market interest rates rise, those fixed cash flows are worth less (discounted at a higher rate), so the bond price falls. When rates fall, those same fixed flows are worth more, so the price rises. Analogy: You locked in a 4% savings account for 10 years. If new accounts now offer 6%, your 4% account is worth less — you'd have to pay someone to take it. If new accounts offer 2%, your 4% account is valuable — someone would pay a premium for it. That's exactly how bond prices work.

Type Issuer Key feature Risk profile --- --- --- --- Treasury / Government US Government (or sovereign) "Risk-free" rate benchmark. Full faith and credit. Interest rate risk only (for US). Sovereign risk for other countries. Corporate (IG) Investment-grade companies (BBB- and above) Higher yield than Treasuries. Spread reflects credit risk. Rate risk + credit risk. In normal times, behaves like a bond. Corporate (HY / Junk) Below investment-grade companies (BB+ and below) Much higher yield. Significant default risk. In a crisis, HY trades like equity — it's credit risk that dominates, not rates. Municipal State/local governments Tax-exempt income (for US investors) Credit risk varies (Detroit went bankrupt). Liquidity can be poor. MBS (Mortgage-Backed) Pools of mortgages securitized by banks/agencies Prepayment risk creates negative convexity. When rates fall, homeowners refinance → you get principal back early at the worst time. When rates rise, nobody prepays → you're stuck with below-market rates. Lose-lose. TIPS US Government Principal adjusts with CPI inflation Pays a real yield. Breakeven inflation = nominal yield minus TIPS yield. Floating rate notes Various Coupon resets with a reference rate (SOFR + spread) Very low duration (coupon adjusts to current rates). Used to reduce rate risk. Convertible bonds Corporations Can be converted into equity at a set price Hybrid: bond downside protection + equity upside. Has embedded option → Greeks apply.

A bond's price is the present value of all its future cash flows: $$P = \sum {t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{FV}{(1+y)^n}$$ Where C = coupon, FV = face value (usually $100 or $1,000), y = yield, n = number of periods. - Par (100): Bond trades at face value. Yield = coupon rate. - Premium (>100): Coupon is higher than current market rates. Investors pay extra for the higher income. - Discount (<100): Coupon is lower than current market rates. Investors won't pay full price since they can get better rates elsewhere.

section: Yield Measures — Which One to Use
content: Measure What it is When to use it --- --- --- Coupon rate Annual coupon / Face value Tells you the cash income. Does NOT tell you your return if you don't buy at par. Current yield Annual coupon / Market price Better than coupon rate because it uses actual price paid. But ignores capital gain/loss at maturity. Yield to maturity (YTM) Total annualized return if you hold to maturity, assuming all coupons reinvested at the same rate The standard measure. But the reinvestment assumption is unrealistic. Yield to worst (YTW) YTM calculated to the worst possible call/put date Use this for callable bonds — the issuer will call if rates fall (taking away your upside). Spread to benchmark YTM minus Treasury yield of same maturity Isolates the credit risk component. This is what credit traders actually watch.

> One-liner: The yield curve plots interest rates by maturity — it tells you what the market collectively believes about the future of growth, inflation, and monetary policy. The shapes and what they mean: - Normal (upward sloping): Short rates < long rates. Investors demand more yield for locking money up longer (term premium). Economy is healthy, no imminent policy shift. - Flat: Short and long rates roughly equal. Typically a transition — the economy is slowing, the market is uncertain about what's next. - Inverted: Short rates > long rates. The market expects the central bank to CUT rates in the future (because it expects a recession). Historically, every US recession since 1970 was preceded by a 2s10s inversion. Not every inversion causes a recession, but every recession had an inversion. - Steepening: The gap between short and long rates is widening. Can be "bull steepening" (short rates falling faster) or "bear steepening" (long rates rising faster). Each has very different implications. - Bear flattening: Short rates rising faster than long rates (Fed hiking aggressively). This is the pre-recession shape. Key segments: - Front end (0-2yr): Driven by central bank policy and expectations. Moves with Fed Funds rate. - Belly (2-5yr): Most sensitive to expected policy path. Where the uncertainty is highest. - Long end (10-30yr): Driven by inflation expectations AND term premium. Also affected by supply (government issuance) and demand (pension funds, insurance companies).

section: Bootstrapping — How You Actually Build the Yield Curve
content: > One-liner: Bootstrapping is the process of extracting zero-coupon (spot) rates from observed market prices — you solve one maturity at a time, each step using the rates you already found. The problem: Markets don't hand you a clean set of zero-coupon rates. What you observe are prices/yields of coupon-bearing bonds, deposit rates, futures, and swap rates — all of which bundle multiple cash flows at different maturities. Bootstrapping untangles them. The process, step by step: Step 1 — Short end (0–6 months): Use deposit rates directly - SOFR overnight, 1M T-bill, 3M T-bill, 6M T-bill - These are effectively zero-coupon instruments → their yields ARE spot rates - Example: 6M T-bill yields 4.80% → the 6-month spot rate z(0.5) = 4.80% Step 2 — Medium term (6M–2Y): Use futures (Eurodollar/SOFR futures) - Futures imply forward rates between consecutive periods - Convert forward rates to spot rates by compounding: $$z(T) = \left \prod {i=1}^{n}(1 + f i \times \Delta t i)\right ^{1/T} - 1$$ - Each forward rate comes from the futures price: $f = (100 - \text{futures price}) / 100$ Step 3 — Long end (2Y–30Y): Use swap rates - A par swap has PV(fixed leg) = PV(floating leg). The fixed swap rate is known. You solve for the unknown spot rate at that maturity. - For a 2-year annual swap at rate $c$ with $1 notional: $$c \times \frac{1}{(1+z 1)} + (1+c) \times \frac{1}{(1+z 2)^2} = 1$$ - You already know $z 1$ from Step 1/2. Solve for $z 2$. - For 3-year: you know $z 1$ and $z 2$, solve for $z 3$. And so on — each step "bootstraps" from the previous ones. Worked example — bootstrapping a 2-year spot rate: - 1-year spot rate (from T-bill): $z 1$ = 5.00% - 2-year par swap rate: $c$ = 4.80% - Solve: $0.048 / 1.05 + 1.048 / (1+z 2)^2 = 1$ - $0.04571 + 1.048 / (1+z 2)^2 = 1$ - $1.048 / (1+z 2)^2 = 0.95429$ - $(1+z 2)^2 = 1.09811$ - $z 2 = 4.789\%$ - The 2-year spot rate (4.789%) is slightly below the 2-year swap rate (4.80%) — this is typical when the curve is inverted at the front. What you get: A full term structure of zero-coupon (spot) rates at every maturity. From spot rates you can derive: - Discount factors: $DF(T) = \frac{1}{(1+z T)^T}$ — used to price ANY fixed cash flow - Forward rates: The market-implied rate between two future dates: $f(t 1, t 2) = \left \frac{(1+z 2)^{t 2}}{(1+z 1)^{t 1}}\right ^{1/(t 2-t 1)} - 1$ - Par rates: The coupon rate at which a bond prices at par — the swap curve itself Instruments used at each maturity: Maturity Instrument Why this instrument --- --- --- O/N – 1M SOFR, Fed Funds, repo rates Direct observation of overnight borrowing costs 1M – 6M T-bills, CP, SOFR term rates Zero-coupon, liquid, minimal credit risk 6M – 2Y SOFR futures (SR3) Most liquid, tightest bid-ask, imply forward rates 2Y – 30Y Interest rate swaps Most liquid long-dated instruments, standardized, cleared 30Y+ Long bonds, ultra-long swaps Limited liquidity, more interpolation needed Why it matters for an RM: - Every derivative is priced off this curve.

> One-liner: Duration tells you how much your bond's price will change for a 1% move in rates — a bond with duration 7 loses ~7% when rates rise by 1%. Analogy: Duration is like a seesaw. The fulcrum is the current moment. The coupons and principal repayment are weights placed at different points in the future. Duration tells you where the balance point is — how far into the future, on average, you receive your money. The further out (higher duration), the more sensitive you are to rate changes. Types of duration: - Macaulay duration: The weighted-average time to receive cash flows (in years). A 10-year zero-coupon bond has Macaulay duration of 10. A 10-year 5% coupon bond has lower Macaulay duration (maybe 7.5) because you receive some cash earlier. - Modified duration: Macaulay duration / (1 + y). This is the actual price sensitivity measure: ΔP/P ≈ -D mod × Δy - Effective duration: Used for bonds with embedded options (MBS, callables) where cash flows change with rates. Numerically estimated by bumping rates up and down. - Key rate duration (KRD): Duration to specific points on the yield curve (2yr, 5yr, 10yr, 30yr). A portfolio can have overall duration of 5 but very different KRD profiles — critical for understanding exposure to curve shape changes. What makes a bond longer or shorter duration? Factor Effect on duration Why --- --- --- Longer maturity Higher duration Cash flows are further in the future Higher coupon Lower duration You receive more cash earlier (pulling the balance point forward) Higher yield Lower duration Future cash flows are discounted more heavily, making early cash flows relatively more important Zero coupon Duration = maturity There's only one cash flow, at the very end Dollar duration & DV01: - DV01 (Dollar Value of a Basis Point) = how much the position gains or loses for a 1 basis point (0.01%) move in yield. - DV01 = Duration × Market Value × 0.0001 - Example: You hold $10M face value of a bond with modified duration 6, priced at 98. Market value = $9.8M. DV01 = 6 × $9.8M × 0.0001 = $5,880. So a 1bp rate rise costs you $5,880. A 50bp rise costs ~$294,000. - Portfolio DV01 = sum of all position DV01s. This is the number you monitor daily as an RM — "how much does our portfolio lose per basis point?"

> One-liner: Convexity measures the curvature of the price-yield relationship — it tells you how accurate (or inaccurate) your duration estimate will be for large rate moves. Analogy: Duration is the straight-line approximation of a curve. For small moves, the straight line is close enough. For big moves, the curve matters. Convexity measures how much the actual curved relationship deviates from the straight-line duration approximation. $$\frac{\Delta P}{P} \approx -D \times \Delta y + \frac{1}{2} \times C \times (\Delta y)^2$$ Positive convexity (most vanilla bonds): - When rates rise, bond prices fall LESS than duration alone predicts - When rates fall, bond prices rise MORE than duration alone predicts - This is a "good thing" — your downside is cushioned and your upside is amplified - Higher convexity = more of this benefit → investors pay a premium for convexity Negative convexity (MBS, callable bonds): - When rates fall, prepayments accelerate → you lose your high-coupon bond and get cash back that you have to reinvest at lower rates. The price doesn't rise as much as a non-callable bond. - When rates rise, nobody prepays → you're stuck holding a below-market rate bond that keeps losing value - You lose on both sides. The worst of both worlds. This is why MBS investors demand higher yields — the negative convexity is a genuine risk.

> One-liner: A credit spread is the extra yield a corporate bond pays above the risk-free Treasury rate — it's the price tag of default risk. What's inside a spread: $$\text{Spread} = \text{Default probability} \times \text{Loss given default} + \text{Liquidity premium} + \text{Risk premium}$$ - Default probability: Chance the issuer can't pay you back - Loss given default (LGD): How much you lose if they default. Senior secured bonds might recover 60-70 cents; unsecured bonds 30-40 cents. - Liquidity premium: Compensation for the fact that corporate bonds are less liquid than Treasuries — harder to sell at a fair price quickly - Risk premium: Extra return demanded for bearing uncertainty about the above Rating Typical spread (bps) What it means Real-world context --- --- --- --- AAA/AA 20-60 Near risk-free. Think MSFT, JNJ. Spread mostly reflects liquidity, not default A 60-120 Very low default risk. Most blue chips. BBB 120-200 Lowest investment grade — the "cliff edge" Largest pool of corporate bonds. Downgrade to BB = disaster. BB 200-400 High yield ("junk"). First to blow out in stress. Still decent companies, just more levered B 400-700 Significant default probability Highly leveraged, cyclical businesses CCC and below 700+ Distressed. Trading on expected recovery value, not yield. At this point, yield is meaningless — it's a bet on what you get in bankruptcy BBB cliff risk — the most important structural risk in credit: Over $3 trillion in US corporate bonds are BBB-rated. If macro conditions deteriorate and a wave of BBBs get downgraded to BB ("fallen angels"), IG-only funds and mandates are FORCED to sell. This creates a supply flood in the HY market → HY spreads blow out → cascading losses. This is not theoretical — Ford was downgraded to junk in 2020.

section: Investment Grade vs High Yield — They Behave Like Different Asset Classes
content: Feature Investment Grade High Yield --- --- --- Rating BBB- and above BB+ and below Typical spread 50-200bps 300-800bps Historical default rate <1% per year 3-10% (cycle dependent) Primary risk driver Interest rate risk (duration) Credit risk (default) Duration Higher (companies can issue at long maturities) Lower (shorter maturities, but default risk dominates) Correlation to equities Low in normal times High — HY trades like equity in a crisis Behavior in stress Modest spread widening, flight to quality offsets Aggressive sell-off, liquidity vanishes The RM insight: Don't treat IG and HY as "both bonds." IG portfolios are rate bets. HY portfolios are equity bets with a fixed-income wrapper. A PM who says "I'm reducing equity risk by moving into HY bonds" is fooling themselves — in a crisis, HY will fall with equities. ---

> One-liner: An FX rate is the price of one currency in terms of another — EUR/USD = 1.08 means one euro costs $1.08. Quote conventions (often confusing — know these): - EUR/USD = 1.08: Price of 1 euro in dollars. EUR is the "base," USD is the "quote." If EUR/USD goes UP, the euro is strengthening / dollar is weakening. - USD/JPY = 155: Price of 1 dollar in yen. If USD/JPY goes UP, the dollar is strengthening / yen is weakening. - Convention rule: EUR, GBP, AUD, NZD are typically quoted as XXX/USD (foreign currency as base). JPY, CHF, CAD, EM are typically quoted as USD/XXX. - "Cable" = GBP/USD. "Fiber" = EUR/USD. Traders use these casually. Pips and lot sizes: - A pip = the fourth decimal place for most pairs (0.0001). EUR/USD from 1.0800 to 1.0801 = 1 pip move. - Exception: JPY pairs — a pip is the second decimal place (0.01). USD/JPY from 155.00 to 155.01 = 1 pip. - Standard lot = 100,000 units of base currency. So 1 lot of EUR/USD = €100,000 notional.

Driver How it works Example --- --- --- Interest rate differentials Capital flows to higher-yielding currencies. Higher rates → stronger currency. If US rates are 5% and EU rates are 3%, money flows to USD → dollar strengthens Growth differentials Stronger economy attracts investment → stronger currency US GDP growing 3% vs EU at 1% → capital flows to US → USD up Central bank policy Rate hikes strengthen, cuts weaken. Forward guidance matters as much as the actual decision. Fed hawkish surprise → USD jumps Risk appetite Risk-on → investors sell safe havens (USD, JPY, CHF), buy EM. Risk-off → reverse. Market panic → JPY strengthens (carry unwind + safe haven) Trade balance Countries with trade surpluses accumulate foreign currency, must convert back → strengthens home currency Japan's trade surplus historically supported JPY Capital flows FDI, portfolio flows, M&A. A foreign company buying a US company must buy USD. Massive cross-border M&A can move FX Central bank intervention Direct FX market participation, buying or selling own currency BOJ intervention to defend JPY at 152 in 2022. PBOC managing USD/CNY band.

Pair Why it matters Personality --- --- --- EUR/USD Most traded pair globally (~25% of FX volume). US vs Eurozone rate and growth differential. Relatively stable. Moves in trends. Break of 1.05 or 1.15 is a big deal. USD/JPY Risk appetite barometer + carry trade vehicle. JPY is funded in carry trades (low rates). Can trend for months, then snap back violently on carry unwinds. BOJ intervention threat above 155. GBP/USD UK economy + BOE policy. Sterling is sensitive to political risk (Brexit aftermath). More volatile than EUR/USD. "Flash crash" risk (thin liquidity windows). USD/CNH China's managed float. PBOC sets a daily fixing band. Not a free market — the fix signals policy intent. Devaluation signals can cascade through EM. EM pairs (USD/TRY, USD/ZAR, USD/BRL) EM risk barometers. Sensitive to commodity prices, political risk, and global USD strength. Can move 5-10% in days during EM crises. Liquidity vanishes in stress. DXY (Dollar Index) Basket: EUR 57.6%, JPY 13.6%, GBP 11.9%, CAD 9.1%, SEK 4.2%, CHF 3.6% Not a perfect measure (heavily EUR-weighted) but the standard reference for "USD strength"

> One-liner: Borrow in a low-rate currency, invest in a high-rate currency, and pocket the rate difference — it works until a risk event triggers a violent reversal. Mechanics: Borrow JPY at 0.5%, convert to MXN, invest at 10%. Earn 9.5% annualized. The risk: if JPY strengthens 10% against MXN, you lose your entire year's carry profit in a day. Analogy: The carry trade is like picking up pennies in front of a steamroller. Steady small profits day after day… until the steamroller speeds up and you can't run fast enough. Carry unwinds are FAST, VIOLENT, and clustered because: 1. The trade is crowded (everyone sees the same rate differential) 2. It's leveraged (you borrow to amplify the carry) 3. Losses trigger margin calls → forced liquidation → more selling of the high-yielder → more losses → cascade Historical carry blowups: JPY carry unwind Q3 2024 (JPY rallied 12% in weeks), SNB EUR/CHF floor removal Jan 2015 (CHF jumped 30% in minutes — brokerages went bankrupt).

section: FX Risk for a Discretionary Fund
content: - Every non-USD position has embedded FX exposure. Long European stocks = long EUR implicitly. If EUR/USD falls 3%, your equity return is reduced by 3% in USD terms — even if the stock went up in local currency. - Translation risk vs transaction risk: Translation = your NAV changes because FX moved (unrealized). Transaction = actual cash flows converted at different rates (realized). - Hedging approaches: - FX forwards (most common): Lock in a future exchange rate. Simple, liquid, low cost. BUT: creates rollover risk at each forward maturity. - FX options: More expensive, but provide tail protection. Useful when you want to maintain FX upside but protect against a sharp move. - Natural hedge: Match assets and liabilities in the same currency. If you have EUR revenues and EUR costs, FX risk nets out. - The RM question: "Is our FX exposure intentional or accidental?" A PM long European equities might have a view on the stocks but no view on EUR. That unhedged FX exposure could dominate the P&L. ---

> One-liner: A commodity is a standardized physical good (oil, gold, wheat, copper) traded on exchanges, mostly via futures contracts rather than physical delivery. Key difference from financial assets: Commodities have physical constraints. Oil needs to be stored in tanks. Wheat needs silos. Gold needs vaults. These physical constraints create unique pricing dynamics (contango, backwardation) that don't exist in stocks or bonds. How commodities are accessed: - Futures (primary method): Contracts to buy/sell a specific quantity at a specific future date and price. Most positions are closed before delivery. - Physical: Actual delivery of the commodity. Mostly done by producers/consumers (Glencore, Cargill), not financial investors. - ETFs/ETNs: Track commodity indices or individual commodity futures. Subject to roll costs (see below). - Equity proxies: Buy shares in commodity producers (ExxonMobil for oil, Barrick for gold). But this adds company-specific risk on top of commodity risk.

Commodity Key drivers Key risk characteristics --- --- --- Crude Oil (WTI & Brent) OPEC+ supply decisions, global demand (China critical), geopolitics (Strait of Hormuz, Russia), US shale production, inventory levels (EIA weekly report) Most important commodity — its price feeds into inflation, rates, FX, and equities globally. WTI = US benchmark (delivery at Cushing, OK). Brent = global benchmark (North Sea). Natural Gas Weather (heating demand in winter, cooling demand in summer), storage levels, LNG export capacity, pipeline infrastructure Extremely volatile. Seasonal patterns. Amaranth Advisors blew up here (2006, lost $6B on NG spreads). Gasoline / Distillates Crack spreads (refining margins), seasonal driving demand, refinery outages Crack spread = gasoline price minus crude oil price = refiner's margin

Commodity Key drivers Key risk characteristics --- --- --- Gold Real interest rates (inverse relationship — higher real rates = lower gold), USD strength (inverse), central bank buying, safe haven demand Not an inflation hedge per se — it's a REAL RATE hedge. Gold rises when real rates fall (even if inflation is constant). Silver Gold correlation (~0.8) + industrial demand (solar, electronics) Higher beta version of gold. More volatile. Copper Global industrial demand (China = 50%+ of global copper demand), construction, EV transition "Doctor Copper" — leading indicator of global growth because it's used in everything.

Commodity Key drivers Key risk characteristics --- --- --- Wheat / Corn / Soybeans Weather, trade policy, fertilizer costs, planting intentions Weather-driven supply shocks can be enormous. Ukraine conflict (2022) disrupted global wheat supply. Coffee / Cocoa / Sugar Weather in specific growing regions, crop disease, currency of producing countries (BRL for coffee) Concentrated supply — a single freeze in Brazil can spike coffee prices 50%+

section: The Futures Curve — A Concept Unique to Commodities
content: > One-liner: The futures curve plots the price of a commodity at different delivery dates — its shape tells you whether the market is paying for immediate supply (backwardation) or penalizing you for storage (contango). Contango (upward-sloping curve): - Front month CHEAPER than later months - Normal for storable commodities: the higher future price reflects storage costs + insurance + financing - Analogy: It's like a parking garage that charges monthly rent. The longer you want to keep your car (commodity), the more you pay. - Implication for investors: Rolling futures in contango is a drag — you sell the cheap expiring contract and buy the more expensive next month contract. You lose money on every roll. This is why commodity ETFs (like the old USO) secretly bleed value even if spot prices are flat. Backwardation (downward-sloping curve): - Front month MORE expensive than later months - Signals tight current supply — buyers are paying a scarcity premium to get oil/copper/wheat NOW - Analogy: Like surge pricing on Uber — supply is tight right now, so the immediate price is inflated. But the market expects supply to normalize, so the future price is lower. - Implication for investors: Rolling in backwardation is beneficial — you sell the expensive expiring contract and buy the cheaper next month. Positive roll yield. Roll yield calculation example: - You're long June WTI at $75. It's about to expire. July WTI is $77 (contango). - You sell June at $75, buy July at $77. You just lost $2 per barrel on the roll. - If you hold 1,000 barrels (one futures contract), that's a $2,000 loss just from rolling — not from oil prices moving.

- Physical delivery risk: WTI futures expire to physical delivery at Cushing, Oklahoma. If storage at Cushing is full and you're long an expiring contract, you must take delivery — but there's nowhere to put it. This is how WTI went to -$37 in April 2020. Brent is cash-settled, so it can't go negative the same way. - Basis risk: WTI and Brent are "both oil" but their spread can move independent of oil direction. If you hedge a WTI exposure with Brent futures, you still have WTI-Brent basis risk. In 2011, the WTI-Brent spread blew out to $25 due to Cushing storage glut — purely a logistical problem, not a global oil supply issue. - Seasonality: Natural gas is winter-peaking (heating demand). Gasoline peaks in summer (driving season). Agricultural commodities follow planting/harvest cycles. These seasonal patterns are predictable but can be amplified by weather anomalies. - Geopolitical risk: ~20% of global oil supply passes through the Strait of Hormuz. A disruption there doesn't just move oil — it cascades to inflation expectations, which moves rates, which moves equities. - Unit conversions to know: - 1 barrel of oil = 42 US gallons - 1 WTI futures contract = 1,000 barrels - 1 gold futures contract = 100 troy ounces - 1 corn futures contract = 5,000 bushels ---

> One-liner: Credit is the risk that a borrower won't pay you back — it's traded via corporate bonds (cash market) and credit default swaps (synthetic market). Credit is NOT the same as "bonds." A Treasury bond has rate risk but no credit risk (assuming the US government doesn't default). A corporate bond has BOTH rate risk and credit risk. The credit component is what's captured by the spread.

Instrument What it is Key characteristic --- --- --- Corporate bonds Debt issued by companies in the cash market You own the actual loan obligation. Receives coupons. Illiquid compared to CDS. Credit Default Swap (CDS) Insurance contract against a company defaulting You don't own the bond — you're just trading the default risk. More liquid than bonds for single-name credit. Leveraged loans Bank loans to sub-investment-grade companies Floating rate (SOFR + spread), so low duration. Senior in capital structure. Used in LBOs. CLO Securitized pool of leveraged loans, sliced into tranches AAA tranche gets paid first (safe), equity tranche gets paid last (risky). Correlation risk — if defaults cluster, even senior tranches get hit.

> One-liner: A CDS is an insurance contract — the buyer pays a regular premium (spread) and gets paid if the reference company defaults. Mechanics: - Buyer of protection: Pays a quarterly premium (quoted as annual spread in bps). If the reference entity has a "credit event" (default, restructuring), the protection seller pays the buyer par minus recovery value. - Seller of protection: Collects the spread. Bears the default risk. This is economically equivalent to being LONG the credit — similar exposure to owning the bond. - No need to own the bond: You can buy CDS protection without owning the underlying bond. This means CDS can be used for speculation, not just hedging. - Example: Company X 5-year CDS trades at 200bps. Buyer pays 2% per year on the notional ($200K/year on $10M notional). If X defaults with 40% recovery, buyer receives $10M × (1 - 0.40) = $6M. CDS spread movement: - Spread widening = market perceives higher default risk = bearish on the credit - Spread tightening = market perceives lower default risk = bullish on the credit - Speed of spread moves is information: sudden widening from 100bps to 300bps = the market knows something CDS-bond basis: - Basis = CDS spread minus the equivalent cash bond spread - Positive basis (CDS spread > bond spread): Can indicate counterparty risk concerns, or that CDS market is pricing in more risk than the cash market - Negative basis (CDS spread < bond spread): Can indicate a buying opportunity (buy the cheap CDS protection, short the expensive bond) but usually exists for structural reasons (funding costs, liquidity) ---

section: PART 6: WHAT THE RISK MANAGER WATCHES — INSTRUMENT BY INSTRUMENT
content: For each asset class, the RM has a core set of metrics and red flags:

Metric What you're watching Red flag --- --- --- Gross / net exposure Total long + short vs long minus short Gross > 200% = very leveraged. Net drifting from target = unintended directional bet Concentration Top 5 position weight >25% in one name = single-name blow-up risk Factor exposure Beta, momentum, value, sector tilt Unexpected factor loading ("diversified" but actually one big momentum bet) Liquidity (ADV) Days to liquidate each position Any position >10% of ADV = liquidity risk Short interest in longs Are our longs in crowded short names? High short interest = potential squeeze (good), but also potential bear thesis you missed (bad)

Metric What you're watching Red flag --- --- --- Portfolio duration / DV01 Interest rate sensitivity DV01 exceeding risk budget. Unhedged duration mismatch. Key rate durations Exposure to different curve points >80% of exposure in one maturity bucket = concentrated curve bet Convexity Positive or negative Large negative convexity positions (MBS) without awareness of prepayment scenarios Credit quality distribution Rating breakdown Excessive BBB exposure near potential downgrade = fallen angel risk Spread duration Sensitivity to spread widening Large spread duration in HY = big loss if spreads blow out

Metric What you're watching Red flag --- --- --- Net currency exposure USD, EUR, JPY, GBP, EM FX Large unhedged FX position that doesn't match PM's stated view Carry exposure Net carry trade position Concentrated carry in illiquid EM pairs = unwind risk Correlation to rates How FX positions interact with rate positions Being long EM FX AND long EM rates = double exposure to same risk

Metric What you're watching Red flag --- --- --- Roll cost / curve shape Contango/backwardation and rolling schedule Large long position in deep contango = bleeding roll cost every month Concentration Oil vs diversified commodities "Commodities" that are really just an oil bet Physical delivery dates Contract expiry calendar Approaching expiry with large position and no roll plan = operational disaster Basis risk Hedging instrument vs actual exposure Hedging jet fuel with WTI → substantial basis risk

Metric What you're watching Red flag --- --- --- CDS spread levels Single-name and index (CDX, iTraxx) Sudden widening in names we have exposure to BBB proportion How much portfolio is near the IG/HY cliff >30% in BBB with negative watch = potential forced selling wave Default rate trend Trailing 12-month HY default rate Rising above 3% = macro deterioration underway Recovery rate assumptions What we assume we'll get back in default Using historical 40% recovery when current environment suggests 25% ---

If asked "Walk me through the major asset classes": "Cash instruments fall into five core categories: equities, fixed income, FX, commodities, and credit. An equity is a residual claim on a company's cash flows — last in line in the capital structure, highest risk, highest return potential. Equity prices are driven by earnings times the multiple, where the multiple reflects growth expectations, interest rates, and sentiment. As an RM, I focus on concentration, factor exposure, and liquidity — a portfolio of 30 stocks isn't diversified if they're all high-momentum tech names. A bond is a tradable loan — the issuer pays you regular coupons and returns principal at maturity. The key risk measure is duration: a bond with duration 7 loses approximately 7% for every 1% rate increase. Duration is sensitivity. Convexity is the second-order correction — it helps you in vanilla bonds (positive convexity) and hurts you in MBS (negative convexity, because of prepayment risk). FX rates are driven by interest rate differentials, growth differentials, and risk appetite. Every non-USD position has embedded FX risk that may or may not be intentional. The carry trade — borrowing low-rate currencies to invest in high-rate currencies — works steadily until it unwinds violently. Commodities are unique because of physical delivery constraints. The futures curve shape — contango versus backwardation — creates roll costs or roll yield that can dominate returns independent of the commodity's spot price. Credit is the risk of default, traded via cash bonds and CDS. Investment-grade credit behaves like bonds — it's a rate bet. High-yield credit behaves like equity — it's a default bet. The BBB cliff is the single biggest structural risk in credit markets today." --- Related files: → B Derivatives — options, futures, swaps built on top of these cash instruments → P Macro Fundamentals — what drives these markets at the macro level → Q Cross Asset Relationships — how shocks cascade across asset classes → D Greeks — measuring risk on derivatives written on these instruments → F Risk Measures — VaR, stress testing, and other risk metrics applied to these instruments

section: Cash vs Physical vs Futures — The Three Layers
content: Layer What it is Who trades it Example --- --- --- --- Physical (spot) Actual barrels, tonnes, bushels changing hands for near-term delivery Producers (Shell, Saudi Aramco), consumers (airlines, refiners), physical trading houses (Vitol, Trafigura, Glencore) A refiner buys 500,000 barrels of crude for delivery next week at the Cushing terminal Cash / Forward OTC contracts for physical delivery at a negotiated future date, priced vs a benchmark Physical traders, large consumers, producers locking in prices An airline buys jet fuel forward for Q3 delivery tied to Platts assessment Futures Exchange-traded standardized contracts for a specific commodity, quantity, delivery point, and date Financial investors, hedge funds, CTAs, banks, producers hedging, specs A fund buys 100 lots of Dec-26 Brent ICE futures > One-liner: Physical is the real stuff. Cash/forward is the OTC deal. Futures is the exchange-traded, standardized, margined version. Financial players mostly trade futures. Physical players trade all three and arbitrage the gaps between them. Why this distinction matters: - Futures prices CONVERGE to physical prices at expiry (otherwise there's free money). But BEFORE expiry, futures can trade at premiums or discounts to physical — this is the basis. - A "paper" trader at a hedge fund sees futures prices. A physical trader at Vitol sees actual cargo economics — shipping costs, refinery margins, storage constraints. Sometimes they diverge, and the physical trader has an edge. - As an RM at Squarepoint, you're almost certainly dealing with futures and swaps, not physical. But understanding the physical layer helps you understand WHY futures behave the way they do.

Commodities don't have a single "price" like a stock. They have a web of benchmarks: - Exchange settlement prices: ICE Brent, NYMEX WTI, COMEX Gold — official daily settlement from the exchange - Price reporting agencies (PRAs): Platts (S&P Global), Argus, ICIS — these agencies collect transaction data and publish reference prices (assessments) used to price physical contracts worldwide - Why Platts matters: Most physical oil contracts globally are priced as "Platts Dated Brent + premium/discount." If Platts assesses Dated Brent at $82, and your contract is Dated Brent +$0.50, you pay $82.50. The Platts window (4:00-4:30 PM London time) is when physical traders submit bids/offers that set this benchmark. ---

Benchmark Exchange Delivery/Settlement Contract Size What it represents --- --- --- --- --- WTI (West Texas Intermediate) NYMEX (CME Group) Physical delivery at Cushing, Oklahoma 1,000 barrels (42,000 US gallons) US domestic crude benchmark. Light sweet crude (API ~39.6°, sulfur ~0.24%). Brent (ICE Brent) ICE (Intercontinental Exchange) Cash-settled against ICE Brent Index 1,000 barrels Global crude benchmark. ~75% of world's traded crude priced off Brent. Dated Brent OTC (assessed by Platts) Physical cargoes loading 10-25 days forward from North Sea fields Cargo size (~600,000 barrels) The physical benchmark. What the actual barrels are worth for near-term delivery. ICE Brent futures converge to this. Dubai/Oman DME (Dubai Mercantile Exchange) Physical delivery (Oman crude) 1,000 barrels Asian crude benchmark. Sour crude (higher sulfur). Prices Middle East exports to Asia. Shanghai INE Shanghai Int'l Energy Exchange Physical delivery (medium sour crude) in bonded storage in China 1,000 barrels (CNY-denominated) China's attempt at a domestic benchmark. Growing but still secondary.

Dated Brent (physical) ←→ ICE Brent Futures (financial) ←→ WTI (US) ←→ Dubai/Oman (Asia) - Brent-WTI spread: Reflects the Atlantic Basin supply/demand balance. Normally Brent trades $2-5 above WTI (more global demand, fewer logistical bottlenecks). In 2011, WTI collapsed to $20+ discount when Cushing was flooded with Canadian/shale crude and pipeline capacity was insufficient. - Brent-Dubai spread (EFS): Reflects the light-sweet vs heavy-sour differential and the relative supply balance between the Atlantic and Pacific basins.

Not all oil is the same. Two key specifications: Spec What it measures Impact --- --- --- API gravity Density. Higher API = lighter crude. WTI ~39.6° (light). Arab Heavy ~27° (heavy). Light crude yields more valuable products (gasoline, diesel). Heavy crude yields more residual fuel oil. Light commands a premium. Sulfur content "Sweet" (<0.5% sulfur) vs "Sour" (>0.5%). WTI is sweet. Most Middle East crude is sour. Sweet crude is cheaper to refine (less desulfurization needed). IMO 2020 shipping fuel regs increased the sweet premium. Why this matters for risk: If a PM is trading the "Brent-Dubai spread," they're not just trading oil — they're trading the light-sweet vs heavy-sour quality differential, which is driven by refinery configurations, regulatory changes, and regional demand patterns.

section: WTI Delivery Mechanics and the -$37 Event
content: WTI futures expire to PHYSICAL delivery at Cushing, Oklahoma: - Cushing is a landlocked storage hub with ~76 million barrels of capacity - At expiry, if you're still long, you must take delivery of oil AT Cushing - April 2020: COVID crushed demand → storage at Cushing was ~80% full → no one could accept delivery → sellers had to PAY buyers to take oil off their hands → WTI went to -$37.63/bbl - This can NEVER happen to Brent futures because they're cash-settled — no physical delivery obligation RM lesson: Always know whether a contract is physically or cash-settled, and monitor position sizes relative to open interest as expiry approaches. CME now has negative price functionality permanently. ---

section: PART 3: REFINED PRODUCTS — WHAT OIL BECOMES
content: A barrel of crude yields different products depending on the refinery configuration. This is the "crack spread."

Product What it is Futures contract Contract size Seasonality --- --- --- --- --- RBOB Gasoline Reformulated gasoline blendstock. Motor fuel. NYMEX RBOB (RB) 42,000 gallons (1,000 barrels) Summer peak (driving season, May-Sep). "Summer blend" is more expensive to produce. ULSD (Ultra-Low Sulfur Diesel) Heating oil / diesel. Formerly called "Heating Oil" on NYMEX. NYMEX ULSD (HO) 42,000 gallons (1,000 barrels) Winter peak (heating demand). Also industrial demand (trucking, shipping). Jet fuel (Kerosene) Aviation fuel. OTC (priced vs Platts or ULSD) No standard exchange contract — traded as crack to ULSD Usually trades at small premium to diesel. Airline hedging is massive. Fuel oil (HSFO / VLSFO) Heavy residual fuel for shipping and power generation. ICE, SGX Various IMO 2020 split the market: VLSFO (0.5% sulfur) vs HSFO (3.5%). HSFO discounted heavily. Naphtha Light fraction used as petrochemical feedstock (ethylene, plastics). OTC (Platts) Cargo-based Driven by petrochemical margins and ethylene cracker economics. LPG (Propane / Butane) Light hydrocarbons for heating, petrochemicals. NYMEX Propane (PN) 42,000 gallons Winter heating peak. US exports increasingly important.

> One-liner: A crack spread is the difference between the price of refined products and crude oil — it's the refiner's gross profit margin. Crack spread Formula What it tells you --- --- --- Simple gasoline crack RBOB price − Crude price Margin on turning crude into gasoline Simple diesel crack ULSD price − Crude price Margin on turning crude into diesel 3-2-1 crack (2 × RBOB + 1 × ULSD) − 3 × Crude oil, divided by 3 Approximate US Gulf Coast refiner margin (assumes 3 barrels crude → 2 barrels gasoline + 1 barrel diesel) Gasoline-diesel spread RBOB − ULSD Relative value within the products complex Why crack spreads matter: - When cracks are wide: Refiners are printing money → they maximize crude throughput → increases crude demand → supports crude prices - When cracks compress: Refiner margins squeezed → maintenance shutdowns (cut throughput) → reduces crude demand → weighs on crude - Seasonal pattern: Cracks typically widen before driving season (gasoline demand) and before winter (heating oil demand) - 2022 example: Diesel cracks exploded to $70+/bbl (normally $15-25) due to Russia sanctions removing refinery capacity + low diesel inventories. Signaled massive supply disruption. ---
Natural gas is NOT one global market. It's three separate regional markets connected by LNG:

Feature Detail --- --- Benchmark Henry Hub (HH) — pipeline delivery point in Erath, Louisiana Exchange NYMEX (CME Group) Contract size 10,000 MMBtu (million British thermal units) Pricing unit $/MMBtu Current range ~$2-4/MMBtu (historically low due to shale revolution) Key drivers Weather (heating/cooling degree days), storage injections/withdrawals (EIA Thursday report), LNG export volumes, associated gas production from shale oil drilling, power generation demand Key US gas concepts: - Basis differentials: Henry Hub is the benchmark, but actual gas prices vary by location. Permian Basin gas trades at deep discount to HH (associated gas with no pipeline capacity). Northeast gas (Algonquin) trades at massive premium in winter (heating demand, pipeline constraints). - Storage report: EIA releases weekly storage data every Thursday at 10:30 AM ET. The market reacts to the deviation from consensus: a build of +80 Bcf when the market expected +90 Bcf is bullish (less gas injected than expected = supply tighter). - Shoulder months: March-April and October-November — transition between heating and cooling seasons. Prices typically lowest here. - Associated gas: Gas produced as a byproduct of oil drilling. When oil prices rise → more shale drilling → more associated gas produced → gas prices FALL even though energy demand is up. This decoupled US gas from oil prices.

section: US Gas Hubs and Delivery Locations
content: > One-liner: The US gas market is a web of interconnected pipeline hubs — Henry Hub sets the benchmark, but every region has its own hub price that reflects local supply, demand, and pipeline capacity. Henry Hub — the benchmark: - Physical delivery point in Erath, Louisiana, where 13 interstate and intrastate pipelines interconnect - Why it became the benchmark: massive pipeline connectivity means gas can flow in and out efficiently, making it a natural meeting point for supply and demand - NYMEX NG futures settle to physical delivery at Henry Hub - All other US locations price as a basis differential to HH (e.g., "SoCal Citygate at HH + $0.35") Major US Gas Hubs: Hub Location Key characteristic Typical basis to HH --- --- --- --- Henry Hub (HH) Erath, Louisiana THE benchmark. 13 pipelines converge. Gulf Coast supply center. — (is the benchmark) Waha West Texas (Permian Basin) Massive associated gas production from Permian oil drilling. Often oversupplied — pipelines can't move gas out fast enough. Deep discount (sometimes negative — producers PAY to get rid of gas) Dominion South Appalachia (Marcellus/Utica) Major shale gas production hub. Cheap gas but constrained by pipeline takeaway to demand centers. Discount — reflects pipeline bottlenecks out of Appalachia Algonquin Citygate New England delivery The demand endpoint for Northeast heating/power. Infamous for winter spikes when pipeline capacity is maxed out. Sharp premium in winter ($10-30+ vs HH in polar vortex events), near parity in summer SoCal Citygate Southern California West Coast delivery for power generation. Vulnerable to pipeline outages (Aliso Canyon storage leak 2015). Moderate premium reflecting transport costs Chicago Citygate Midwest Major demand center for heating + industrial. Well-connected by interstate pipelines. Small premium or parity AECO Alberta, Canada Canadian gas. Connects via pipelines to US Midwest and West Coast. Often at discount to HH. Discount — long on supply, dependent on US export capacity Transco Zone 6 (NY) New York City delivery Urban Northeast demand center. Extremely high winter premiums. Premium, especially winter Katy Hub Southeast Texas Reflects Gulf Coast industrial and LNG export demand near Freeport and Sabine Pass LNG terminals. Near parity or slight premium Why basis matters for risk: - A PM trading "US natural gas" at Henry Hub is NOT exposed to the same price as the gas burning in a New England power plant in January. In the 2014 polar vortex, HH traded at $4.50/MMBtu while Algonquin Citygate hit $78/MMBtu — that's a 17x difference at the same moment in time. - Basis risk example: If you hedge a New England power plant's gas cost using HH futures, you're exposed to the Algonquin-HH basis. If a winter storm hits and pipelines into New England are maxed, HH might move $0.20 while Algonquin moves $30. Your hedge covers almost nothing. - Pipeline constraints are the driver: Most basis blowouts happen because gas physically can't get from where

> One-liner: Electricity can't be economically stored at scale, so power prices are set in real-time by the marginal cost of the generator needed to meet demand at that exact moment. Market (ISO/RTO) Region Key feature --- --- --- PJM Mid-Atlantic + Midwest (13 states) Largest US power market. Heavy coal/gas mix. Capacity market. ERCOT Texas (isolated grid) NOT connected to the rest of the US. No capacity market → extreme price spikes. Feb 2021 freeze → prices hit $9,000/MWh (cap). CAISO California Heavy renewables (solar). "Duck curve" — oversupply midday, sharp ramp at sunset. NYISO New York Congested transmission. NYC prices can diverge massively from upstate. ISO-NE New England Winter gas/power price spikes (pipeline constraints, competing heating demand). MISO Midwest Wind-heavy. Low prices in windy periods. SPP Central US (Plains) Very wind-heavy. Negative prices (generators pay to dispatch) when wind oversupplies. How power prices work: - Merit order / supply stack: Generators are dispatched from cheapest to most expensive. Renewables (zero marginal cost) → nuclear → coal → gas (CCGT) → gas (peaker). The marginal unit (last one needed to meet demand) sets the price for ALL power that hour. - Heat rate: The efficiency of converting gas to electricity. Measured in MMBtu/MWh. A heat rate of 7 is efficient (combined cycle). A heat rate of 10+ is inefficient (peaker). Implied heat rate = power price / gas price — tells you whether it's profitable to burn gas to make electricity. - Spark spread: Power price − (Gas price × Heat rate) = Generator's margin. If negative, it's uneconomic to run. - Dark spread: Power price − (Coal price × Emission cost) = Coal generator's margin. - Congestion: Transmission lines have limited capacity. When a cheap generator can't deliver power to a high-demand area because wires are full, the high-demand area pays more (locational marginal pricing / LMP). - Capacity markets (PJM, ISO-NE, NYISO): Generators are paid just to be available, even if they don't run. Ensures reliability. ERCOT has NO capacity market, which is why Texas has both lower average prices and more extreme spikes. - Renewable duck curve: In CAISO, midday solar floods the market → prices drop (sometimes negative) → at 5 PM, solar dies and everyone turns on AC → prices spike as gas peakers ramp. This 3-hour ramp is the most volatile part of the day. Negative power prices: Yes, power prices can go negative. When wind or solar output exceeds demand plus transmission capacity, generators must PAY to stay connected (because they have contractual take-or-pay obligations or receive renewable subsidies per MWh that exceed the negative price penalty). This is increasingly common in ERCOT, CAISO, and SPP.

Feature Detail --- --- Benchmark TTF (Title Transfer Facility) — virtual trading hub in the Netherlands Exchange ICE Endex Contract size 1 lot = 1 MWh per day for the delivery period Pricing unit €/MWh (NOT $/MMBtu — you must convert to compare to HH) Conversion 1 MMBtu ≈ 0.293 MWh. So $3/MMBtu ≈ €9.5/MWh at current FX (rough). NBP UK's National Balancing Point — historically the benchmark, but TTF has overtaken it for European price discovery since ~2020 Key European gas concepts: - TTF has become the global LNG benchmark. When the Europe 2022 energy crisis hit (Russia cut gas), TTF spiked from ~€30/MWh to €340/MWh. This repriced ALL global LNG because cargoes were diverted to Europe for the premium. - NBP vs TTF: NBP is UK-specific (connected to the continent via interconnectors but separated by the channel). NBP trades at a spread to TTF reflecting UK storage levels, interconnector flows, and LNG terminal capacity. - LNG import dependency: Europe pivoted from Russian pipeline gas (40% of supply pre-2022) to seaborne LNG. This means European gas prices are now linked to global LNG shipping economics: freight rates, liquefaction costs, and Asian LNG demand compete for the same cargoes. - Storage: European gas storage (Gasunie, Storengy) follows a fill/draw cycle: inject April-October, withdraw October-March. EU now mandates 90% fill by November 1. Storage levels are THE anxiety metric for European gas prices. - JKM (Japan-Korea Marker): Asian LNG spot benchmark, assessed by Platts. When JKM > TTF, LNG cargoes go to Asia. When TTF > JKM, cargoes come to Europe. This is the "LNG arbitrage."

Feature Detail --- --- Benchmark JKM (Platts Japan-Korea Marker) Pricing Spot: JKM ($/MMBtu). Long-term contracts: historically oil-indexed (e.g., 14.85% × JCC + $0.50) Major importers Japan, South Korea, China, India, Taiwan Key feature Most LNG is sold under long-term contracts (10-20 years) with oil-linked pricing — NOT spot. But spot share is growing. Oil-linked LNG pricing explained: Historically, Asian LNG buyers signed long-term contracts priced as a percentage of crude oil (JCC — Japan Customs-cleared Crude). A typical "slope" is 14-15%: if JCC oil = $80/bbl → LNG price = 0.145 × $80 = $11.60/MMBtu. This means Asian gas prices move with oil, not with Henry Hub — a completely different dynamic. ---

section: London Metal Exchange (LME) — How It Works
content: The LME is unique: - Open-outcry ring trading (still exists alongside electronic). The "ring" is where official prices are set. - Daily prompt dates: Unlike other futures where you trade monthly contracts, the LME allows trading for any business day up to 3 months out, then monthly to 63 months. This gives extreme flexibility for hedging physical delivery. - 3-month price is the benchmark. "LME Copper" usually means the 3-month forward price. - Physical delivery via LME-registered warehouses worldwide. Metal stored in LME warehouses can be tendered for delivery. - Warehouse queues: Historically, long waiting times to withdraw metal from LME warehouses created artificial tightness (Goldman's aluminum warehouse controversy in 2013).

Metal LME Code Contract Size Key Drivers LME 3M Price Range (indicative) --- --- --- --- --- Copper CA 25 tonnes China demand (50%+), construction, EVs, green energy transition, Chilean/Peruvian supply $8,000-10,000/tonne Aluminium AH 25 tonnes Energy costs (smelting is electricity-intensive), China production cuts, construction, packaging $2,200-2,800/tonne Zinc ZS 25 tonnes Galvanizing steel (anti-corrosion), construction, mine supply disruptions $2,400-3,000/tonne Nickel NI 6 tonnes Stainless steel (65% of use), EV batteries (Class 1 nickel for cathodes), Indonesian supply dominance $15,000-25,000/tonne Lead PB 25 tonnes Batteries (car starter batteries), declining demand in developed markets $2,000-2,300/tonne Tin SN 5 tonnes Soldering (electronics), semiconductor supply chain $25,000-35,000/tonne

> One-liner: Copper goes into everything — wiring, plumbing, electronics, EVs, grid infrastructure — so its price is a real-time read on global industrial health. Why copper matters disproportionately: - China consumes 50%+ of global copper. When China stimulus is announced, copper rallies before GDP data confirms it. - Green energy transition is structurally bullish: An EV uses 4x more copper than an ICE car. Offshore wind farms use 6-10 tonnes per MW. Grid upgrades for electrification are copper-intensive. - Supply is constrained: Major mines (Escondida, Grasberg) are mature. New mine development takes 10-15 years from discovery to production. Grade decline is real (ore quality is deteriorating). - Concentrate market: Most copper is mined as concentrate (30% copper), then smelted. Treatment/refining charges (TC/RCs) reflect the tightness between miners and smelters. US copper futures: COMEX (CME Group). Contract size = 25,000 lbs (~11.3 tonnes). Priced in USD cents/lb. To convert: LME $/tonne ÷ 2,204.6 × 100 = COMEX cents/lb. (e.g., $9,000/tonne ≈ 408 c/lb)

The LME nickel market broke: - Chinese tycoon Xiang Guangda (Tsingshan) was massively short nickel (hedging physical production) - Russia-Ukraine war spiked nickel (Russia = 10% of global supply) → prices doubled in days - Tsingshan faced margin calls it couldn't meet → forced buying → nickel spiked from $25K to $100K/tonne in 24 hours - LME cancelled trades and suspended nickel trading for a week — unprecedented - Destroyed LME's credibility as a venue (many participants moved hedging to CME) - RM lesson: Short hedges on physical production can blow up if margin calls arrive faster than physical revenue. Liquidity in LME nickel has never fully recovered. ---

Metal Exchange Contract Size Key Drivers What makes it unique --- --- --- --- --- Gold COMEX (CME) 100 troy oz Real rates (inverse), USD (inverse), central bank buying, safe haven, jewelry (India/China) The anti-fiat currency. Rises when faith in monetary system falls. Silver COMEX (CME) 5,000 troy oz Gold correlation (~0.8), industrial demand (solar panels, electronics), retail speculation Higher beta gold. More volatile. Industrial demand component. Platinum NYMEX (CME) 50 troy oz Autocatalysts (diesel vehicles), hydrogen fuel cells, South African supply (70%+) Post-diesel scandal demand collapsed. Platinum now cheaper than gold (historically rare). Palladium NYMEX (CME) 100 troy oz Autocatalysts (gasoline vehicles), Russian supply (40%), South African supply Russia sanctions + supply concentration = supply shock risk. Spiked to $3,000+ in 2022.

> Key insight: Gold is NOT an inflation hedge. Gold is a REAL RATE hedge. Higher real rates (nominal rate minus inflation) = lower gold. Lower real rates = higher gold. Why? Gold has no yield. It just sits there. When real rates are high, you're giving up a meaningful return by holding gold instead of TIPS. When real rates are low or negative, there's no opportunity cost to holding gold — and it has no counterparty risk. The formula that drives gold: Watch 10-year TIPS yield (real yield). When it falls, gold rises. When it rises, gold falls. This correlation was very strong 2006-2021. It temporarily broke in 2022-2024 due to massive central bank buying (China, Turkey, Poland buying gold regardless of rates). Gold contract details: - COMEX 100 oz contract: ~$250,000 notional at $2,500/oz. Initial margin ~$10,000 (~4%). - London gold fix: LBMA Gold Price, set twice daily (10:30 AM and 3:00 PM London). This is the price used by central banks, ETFs, and physical contracts. - Gold ETFs (GLD, IAU): Hold physical gold in vaults. When inflows increase, the ETF buys physical gold → supports price. When outflows occur, they sell → weighs on price. ETF flow data is a leading indicator. ---

Agricultural commodities are fundamentally different from energy and metals because supply is weather-dependent and seasonal — you can't decide to "produce more wheat" right now the way you can turn on an oil well.

Product Contract Size Pricing Unit Key Drivers --- --- --- --- Corn 5,000 bushels US cents/bushel US planting (Iowa, Illinois), ethanol demand (40% of US corn), China imports, weather (Midwest drought/flood) Soybeans 5,000 bushels US cents/bushel China crush demand (70% of global soy imports go to China), Brazilian supply competition, meal vs oil value split Wheat (SRW) 5,000 bushels US cents/bushel Global weather, Black Sea exports (Russia, Ukraine → 30% of global wheat trade), USDA WASDE report Soybean Oil 60,000 lbs US cents/lb Biodiesel demand, palm oil competition, food use Soybean Meal 100 short tons $/short ton Animal feed demand, crush margins Cotton 50,000 lbs US cents/lb Textile demand (China, SE Asia), weather in US Cotton Belt, polyester substitution Live Cattle 40,000 lbs US cents/lb Cattle cycle (herd liquidation/rebuild), feed costs, beef demand, drought Key US agriculture concepts: - WASDE Report: USDA's World Agricultural Supply and Demand Estimates, released monthly. The single most market-moving ag report. Supply/demand revisions of even 1-2M tonnes can move prices 3-5%. - Crush spread (soybeans): Soybean processors buy beans and crush them into meal + oil. Crush margin = (meal revenue + oil revenue) − bean cost. When crush margins are high, processors buy more beans → supports bean prices. - Ethanol blend wall: ~40% of US corn goes to ethanol. The RFS mandate creates a floor under corn demand. But E10 (10% ethanol) is roughly the max blend for standard cars — beyond that, demand is political. - Key growing season: April-June planting, July-August pollination/critical weather period, September-November harvest. A Midwest drought in July can spike corn/soy 30%+ in weeks.

Product Exchange Contract Size Key Feature --- --- --- --- Milling Wheat Euronext (Paris) 50 tonnes European benchmark. French wheat is a key Black Sea competitor. Rapeseed Euronext (Paris) 50 tonnes Main European oilseed. Used for biodiesel. Competes with palm oil and soy oil. White Sugar ICE London 50 tonnes Refined sugar benchmark. Priced in $/tonne. Raw Sugar (No. 11) ICE US 112,000 lbs Global raw sugar benchmark. Brazil is dominant producer (>40% of global exports). Cocoa ICE London / ICE US 10 tonnes (London), 10 metric tonnes (US) West Africa (Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire = 60%+ of supply). 2024 cocoa prices tripled due to disease + weather in West Africa. Robusta Coffee ICE London 10 tonnes "Cheaper" coffee variety. Vietnam is 1 producer. Used in instant coffee and blending. Arabica Coffee ICE US 37,500 lbs "Premium" coffee variety. Brazil is 1 producer. A single frost event can spike prices 40%+.

Product Exchange Key Feature --- --- --- Palm Oil (CPO) Bursa Malaysia (MDEX) World's most consumed vegetable oil. Malaysia + Indonesia = 85%+ of global production. Deforestation controversy limits new supply. Contract = 25 tonnes, priced in MYR/tonne. Rubber TOCOM (Tokyo), SGX Thailand, Indonesia, Malaysia dominate. Used in tires (70%). Natural rubber competes with synthetic (made from oil). Rice CBOT (rough rice), various Asian exchanges Thinly traded on exchanges — most rice is traded government-to-government. But food security concerns make it politically sensitive. Iron Ore SGX, DCE (Dalian) Not agriculture but traded similarly. DCE contract = 100 tonnes (CNY-denominated). SGX = 100 tonnes (USD). China = 70%+ of seaborne imports. Iron ore is really a China real estate / infrastructure bet. ---

Product Exchange Ticker Size Quote Min Tick Settlement --- --- --- --- --- --- --- WTI Crude NYMEX CL 1,000 bbl $/bbl $0.01 ($10/contract) Physical (Cushing) Brent Crude ICE B 1,000 bbl $/bbl $0.01 ($10) Cash (ICE Brent Index) RBOB Gasoline NYMEX RB 42,000 gal $/gal $0.0001 ($4.20) Physical (NYHARBOR) ULSD (Heating Oil) NYMEX HO 42,000 gal $/gal $0.0001 ($4.20) Physical (NYHARBOR) Henry Hub Nat Gas NYMEX NG 10,000 MMBtu $/MMBtu $0.001 ($10) Physical (Henry Hub) TTF Nat Gas ICE Endex TFM Varies (MWh) €/MWh €0.001 Cash/Physical NBP Nat Gas ICE NBP 1,000 therms/day p/therm 0.01p Cash

Product Exchange Size Quote Settlement --- --- --- --- --- Gold COMEX 100 troy oz $/troy oz Physical (COMEX vaults) Silver COMEX 5,000 troy oz $/troy oz Physical Copper COMEX 25,000 lbs US cents/lb Physical Copper LME 25 tonnes $/tonne Physical (LME warehouses) Aluminium LME 25 tonnes $/tonne Physical Nickel LME 6 tonnes $/tonne Physical
