Product Exchange Size Quote Settlement --- --- --- --- --- Corn CBOT 5,000 bu US cents/bu Physical Soybeans CBOT 5,000 bu US cents/bu Physical Wheat (SRW) CBOT 5,000 bu US cents/bu Physical Milling Wheat Euronext 50 tonnes €/tonne Physical Palm Oil (CPO) Bursa Malaysia 25 tonnes MYR/tonne Physical Coffee (Arabica) ICE US 37,500 lbs US cents/lb Physical Cocoa ICE London 10 tonnes £/tonne Physical ---

section: PART 9: KEY SPREADS AND RELATIVE VALUE TRADES
content: These are the trades that commodity desks actually focus on — not outright direction: Spread What it is What it tells you --- --- --- Calendar spread (time spread) Same commodity, different delivery months (e.g., Jun WTI vs Dec WTI) Curve shape (contango/backwardation), storage economics, supply expectations Crack spread Products vs crude (e.g., RBOB − WTI) Refining margin. Wide = refiner profit → runs at capacity → pulls crude Spark spread Power price − (Gas price × Heat rate) Gas-fired power generation margin Crush spread Soy products (meal + oil) − soybeans Soybean processing margin Frac spread NGL price − gas price Whether it's economic to extract NGLs from gas Brent-WTI Atlantic Basin crude supply balance US export capacity, Cushing storage, pipeline economics Brent-Dubai (EFS) Atlantic vs Pacific basin balance, light-sweet vs heavy-sour differential Asian refinery demand, Middle East supply, arbitrage flows JKM-TTF Asian vs European LNG price LNG cargo diversion direction Location basis (gas) Regional hub vs Henry Hub (e.g., Algonquin vs HH) Pipeline capacity, local supply/demand, weather Gold-Silver ratio Gold price / Silver price Risk appetite. Ratio >80 = stressed (silver underperforming). Ratio <65 = risk-on. ---

section: PART 10: WHAT THE RM MONITORS IN COMMODITIES
content: Area What to watch Red flag --- --- --- Position vs OI Position size relative to open interest >5% of OI = you ARE the market. Liquidation will move prices. Delivery / expiry Calendar of contract expirations Large position approaching expiry with no roll plan = operational crisis Curve shape Contango/backwardation levels Deep contango on large long = bleeding roll cost monthly Physical vs financial Is the PM exposed to physical delivery? WTI positions near expiry when Cushing is full Margin requirements Exchange margin calls, especially in stressed markets Margin increases during vol spikes can force liquidation Cross-commodity correlations Are "diversified" commodity bets actually all correlated? Long crude + long NatGas + long copper = all positioned for global growth, not diversified Geopolitical event risk Hormuz choke point, Black Sea corridor, EM coups Positions with binary geopolitical risk that could gap overnight Regime / seasonality Are positions aligned with seasonal norms? Long NatGas in April = fighting seasonal pattern (requires strong thesis) ---

If asked "Tell me about commodity markets": "Commodity markets have a physical layer that financial markets don't — real barrels need to go somewhere, real tonnes need warehouse space. This creates unique dynamics. In crude oil, there are two main benchmarks: WTI for US delivery at Cushing (physically settled) and Brent for the global market (cash-settled). The fact that WTI is physically settled is why it went negative in April 2020 — there was nowhere to put the oil. Brent can't go negative the same way. Natural gas is regional, not global. Henry Hub in the US trades at $3/MMBtu while TTF in Europe traded at the equivalent of $60+ during the 2022 energy crisis. LNG is what connects these markets — cargoes flow to wherever the premium is highest. I focus on the curve shape — contango versus backwardation tells you about storage economics and supply tightness. I'd flag to a PM if they're holding a large long in deep contango because the roll cost bleeds their return every month regardless of where spot goes. For risk, the key questions are: position size relative to open interest (are we the market?), delivery obligations approaching expiry, and whether what looks like diversified commodity exposure is actually one correlated macro bet." --- Related files: → A Cash Instruments — overview of all asset classes including commodities → Q Cross Asset Relationships — how commodity shocks cascade to other markets → V Case Studies — negative oil, Amaranth, and other commodity blow-ups → P Macro Fundamentals — macro drivers of commodity demand

> One-liner: A derivative is a contract whose value is derived from an underlying asset — it lets you take exposure to (or hedge away) the price of something without owning it. The three purposes: 1. Hedging — Remove risk you don't want. An airline buys oil futures to lock in fuel costs. A PM buys puts to protect a portfolio. 2. Speculation — Take directional or volatility bets with leverage. Buy calls instead of stock to amplify returns. 3. Price discovery — Futures markets aggregate information from thousands of participants and produce benchmark prices. Analogy: A derivative is like a side bet on a football game. You don't own the team (underlying asset), but your bet (derivative) gains or loses value based on how the team performs. The bet is "derived" from the game's outcome. Why derivatives matter more than cash instruments for risk management: Cash instruments have linear payoffs — buy a stock, it goes up 5%, you make 5%. Derivatives can have non-linear payoffs, embedded leverage, and multiple risk dimensions (Greeks). A single options position can generate losses that are multiples of the initial investment. That's why they demand more sophisticated risk monitoring. ---

> One-liner: A future is a binding agreement between two parties to buy or sell a specific asset at a specific price on a specific future date — both sides are OBLIGATED to perform. Analogy: If you pre-order a car at a locked-in price for delivery in 6 months, that's a forward contract. A future is the same thing, but standardized by an exchange and with daily settlement of gains/losses.

Feature Detail --- --- Standardization Exchange-traded: fixed contract size, fixed delivery dates, fixed quality specifications. You can't customize. Margin You post initial margin (~5-15% of notional), not the full price. This creates LEVERAGE. Daily mark-to-market Every day, the exchange calculates your P&L and settles it in cash. If you lose → cash debited from your margin account. If margin falls below maintenance level → margin call → post more or get liquidated. Central clearing The clearinghouse (CME Clearing, ICE Clear) sits between buyer and seller. Both sides face the clearinghouse, not each other. This eliminates bilateral counterparty risk. Delivery Physical (WTI → actual oil at Cushing) or Cash-settled (S&P E-mini → cash difference). KNOW WHICH ONE you're trading. Margin and leverage — a worked example: - You buy 1 E-mini S&P 500 futures contract. Notional = index level × $50 = 5,400 × $50 = $270,000. - Initial margin ≈ $13,000 (~5% of notional). That's the cash you post. - Leverage = $270,000 / $13,000 = ~21x. - If SPX moves +2%: your gain = $270,000 × 2% = $5,400. That's a 42% return on your $13,000 margin. - If SPX moves -5%: your loss = $270,000 × 5% = $13,500. You just lost MORE than your entire margin. You owe the exchange money beyond what you posted. Margin call. This is why leverage kills. The upside looks great. The downside can exceed your entire investment. And the margin call comes at the worst possible time — when the market is moving against you the fastest.

$$F = S \times e^{(r - q) \times T}$$ Variable What it is Effect --- --- --- $S$ Spot price Higher spot → higher futures $r$ Risk-free rate (financing cost) Higher rates → higher futures (costs more to finance holding the asset) $q$ Yield / convenience yield Higher dividends or convenience yield → lower futures (you're missing out by not holding spot) $T$ Time to expiry More time → more carry cost > In plain English: The futures price = spot price + what it costs to hold the asset until delivery (financing, storage, insurance) minus any income you'd earn from holding it (dividends, convenience yield). If futures deviate from this, arbitrageurs trade the gap until it closes. Convenience yield — a concept unique to commodities: The benefit of physically owning the commodity right now (you can use it, avoid production shutdowns, meet customer orders). When supply is tight, convenience yield is high → futures trade below spot → backwardation.

State Futures vs Spot Why Roll impact Who benefits --- --- --- --- --- Contango Futures > Spot Ample supply. Carry costs dominate. Market expects prices flat or higher. Negative roll yield (sell cheap near, buy expensive far) Short futures positions Backwardation Futures < Spot Tight supply NOW. Convenience yield dominates. Scarcity premium. Positive roll yield (sell expensive near, buy cheap far) Long futures positions Roll mechanics: - Futures expire. To maintain exposure, you "roll" — close the expiring contract, open the next month. - In contango: You sell the near-month at $75, buy the next month at $77. You lost $2/barrel on the roll. Over a year, this can add up to 10-20% return drag. - In backwardation: You sell near-month at $77, buy next month at $75. You earned $2/barrel. Roll yield adds to your return. - Real-world impact: The USO ETF (oil) lost approximately 40% of its value over several years purely from roll costs in contango — even as oil prices were relatively stable. This is the "stealth tax" of commodity investing.

Underlying Exchange Ticker Contract Size Settlement Margin (~) --- --- --- --- --- --- S&P 500 E-mini CME ES $50 × Index Cash ~$13,000 Nasdaq 100 E-mini CME NQ $20 × Index Cash ~$17,000 Euro Stoxx 50 Eurex FESX €10 × Index Cash ~€5,000 10-Year Treasury CBOT ZN $100,000 face value Physical (eligible bonds) ~$2,000 2-Year Treasury CBOT ZT $200,000 face value Physical ~$1,000 Eurodollar/SOFR CME SR3 $2,500 per bp on $1M notional Cash ~$1,000 WTI Crude NYMEX CL 1,000 barrels Physical (Cushing) ~$7,000 Brent Crude ICE B 1,000 barrels Cash ~$7,000 Gold COMEX GC 100 troy oz Physical ~$10,000 EUR/USD CME 6E €125,000 Physical / Cash ~$2,500

- Leverage: 20x leverage means a 5% adverse move wipes out your entire margin. Losses can exceed the initial investment. - Margin calls: Arrive at the worst time. In fast markets, you may be liquidated at a loss before you can post additional margin. - Basis risk: Futures ≠ spot. The gap (basis = spot − futures) changes unpredictably. Hedging with the "wrong" contract (e.g., Brent hedging jet fuel) means basis risk can blow out. - Liquidity at expiry: Volume concentrates in front months. Back-month liquidity can evaporate, leaving you unable to exit. - Physical delivery: Holding a physically-settled contract to expiry without delivery capability = catastrophe (WTI, April 2020, -$37/bbl). ---

> One-liner: An option gives you the RIGHT but not the obligation to buy or sell an asset at a specified price (strike) by a specified date (expiry) — the premium you pay is the price of that right. Analogy: An option is like a cancellable reservation. You pay a small fee (premium) for the right to buy the house at an agreed price by December. If house prices rise, you exercise and buy cheap. If they fall, you walk away and lose only the reservation fee. The premium is the most you can lose. - Call option: Right to BUY at the strike price. You want the underlying to go UP. - Put option: Right to SELL at the strike price. You want the underlying to go DOWN. - Long an option: You paid premium. Your risk is limited to the premium. You benefit from movement. - Short an option: You received premium. Your risk can be unlimited (short call) or very large (short put). You benefit from NO movement.

Type When can you exercise? Pricing model Where it's used Why it matters --- --- --- --- --- European ONLY at expiry Black-Scholes (analytical, closed-form solution) Index options (SPX, Euro Stoxx 50), FX options, most OTC derivatives Simpler to price and hedge because exercise is predictable American ANY TIME before expiry Binomial tree (CRR model) — must check at every node whether early exercise is optimal US equity options (AAPL, MSFT — all single-stock options on US exchanges), commodity options Early exercise right makes them more expensive than European options. A deep ITM American put may be exercised early to capture the time value of cash. An American call on a dividend-paying stock may be exercised early just before the ex-date. Bermudan On specific dates (e.g., quarterly) Lattice / Monte Carlo with early exercise Swaptions, callable bonds, structured products "Between" European and American. Common in fixed income derivatives. Asian At expiry, but payoff based on AVERAGE price over the life Monte Carlo simulation (no closed-form solution because the average is path-dependent) Commodity hedging (airlines lock in average fuel cost), FX hedging over a period Cheaper than vanilla options because averaging reduces volatility of the payoff. Less sensitive to manipulation at expiry. Barrier Depends on whether barrier is hit Analytical (simple barriers) or Monte Carlo Structured products, FX, commodities Knock-in: option activates only if underlying hits a level. Knock-out: option dies if underlying hits a level. Pin risk near the barrier — delta jumps massively. Digital (Binary) At expiry — fixed payout if ITM Modified Black-Scholes with discontinuous payoff Structured products, betting markets Pays fixed amount (e.g., $100) if ITM, regardless of how far ITM. Sharp payoff discontinuity at the strike.

section: Why Different Models for Different Types?
content: > The key insight: Black-Scholes gives you a formula you can solve on a calculator — but it only works when you know WHEN exercise happens (European = at expiry, period). The moment you add optionality about WHEN to exercise (American) or path-dependency (Asian), you need numerical methods (trees, simulation) because there's no closed-form shortcut. Black-Scholes (European): 5 inputs → 1 formula → 1 price. Fast, elegant, but assumes constant vol, normal returns, no early exercise. Binomial tree (American): Build a tree of possible price paths, step by step from today to expiry. At each node, check: "Is it better to exercise now or hold?" Work backward from expiry to today. More computational but handles early exercise correctly. Monte Carlo (Asian/path-dependent): Simulate thousands of random price paths. For each path, calculate the option payoff (based on the average price, the path taken, etc.). The option price = the average of all simulated payoffs, discounted back. Slow but handles ANY payoff structure.

section: Option Value: What You're Paying For
content: Option Price = Intrinsic Value + Time Value (Extrinsic Value) Component What it is Example (Call with K=100, S=105, premium=$8) --- --- --- Intrinsic value The value if exercised RIGHT NOW. Call: max(0, S−K). Put: max(0, K−S). $5 (stock is $5 above strike) Time value The premium for the POSSIBILITY that the option becomes more valuable before expiry. Reflects uncertainty. $3 ($8 premium minus $5 intrinsic) Time value is highest for ATM options (maximum uncertainty about whether they'll expire ITM or OTM) and decays as expiry approaches (theta).

State Call condition Put condition Delta (approx) Key characteristics --- --- --- --- --- Deep ITM S >> K S << K ~±0.90-1.00 Moves almost 1:1 with underlying. Mostly intrinsic value. Small time premium. ITM S > K S < K ~±0.60-0.90 Has intrinsic value plus some time value. ATM S ≈ K S ≈ K ~±0.50 Maximum time value. Maximum gamma. Where most vol trading happens. OTM S < K S > K ~±0.10-0.40 Zero intrinsic value — pure time value. Cheap but unlikely to pay off. Deep OTM S << K S >> K ~±0.01-0.10 Very cheap "lottery tickets." Huge percentage gains if the underlying moves far enough. Tail protection.

section: What Makes an Option More Expensive or Cheaper?
content: This is crucial — you need to understand what you're paying for and what's a good or bad deal. Factor Higher → option premium is... Why --- --- --- Higher implied vol (σ) MORE expensive More uncertainty → higher chance of a big move → option is worth more More time to expiry (T) MORE expensive More time for something to happen (time value) Higher interest rates (r) Calls MORE expensive, Puts CHEAPER Higher rates increase the forward price (forward = spot × e^rT), benefiting calls Higher dividends (q) Calls CHEAPER, Puts MORE expensive Dividends reduce the forward price (stock drops by dividend amount on ex-date) ATM strike MORE expensive (most time value) Maximum uncertainty about ITM/OTM outcome Deep OTM strike CHEAPER Very unlikely to pay off American vs European (same terms) American ≥ European Early exercise right has value (or at minimum, no negative value) Asian vs European (same terms) Asian CHEAPER Averaging reduces payoff volatility → less valuable option > The practical interview insight: If a PM says "I want to buy downside protection," the key variables are: how much vol am I paying (implied vol vs realized vol), how much time am I buying (theta), and how far out-of-the-money is the strike (delta / probability of exercise). Expensive protection means vol is high and you're paying a lot of theta rent. Cheap protection means vol is low — which is often when you should be buying it (before the market gets scared, not after).

section: Black-Scholes — What It Does, What It Assumes, Where It Breaks
content: The formula (for a European call): $$C = S \cdot e^{-qT} \cdot N(d 1) - K \cdot e^{-rT} \cdot N(d 2)$$ Where: $$d 1 = \frac{\ln(S/K) + (r - q + \sigma^2/2)T}{\sigma\sqrt{T}}, \quad d 2 = d 1 - \sigma\sqrt{T}$$ You don't need to derive this. But you need to understand: - $N(d 1)$ and $N(d 2)$ are cumulative normal distribution values — probabilities under a bell curve - $N(d 2)$ ≈ probability the option expires ITM (risk-neutral probability) - The formula takes 6 inputs and produces 1 output (option price) - All inputs are observable EXCEPT implied volatility — which is backed out from the market price Implied volatility (IV): The market price tells you what vol traders are pricing in. If the BS formula gives $5 for a call at 20% vol, but the call trades at $7, the "implied vol" is higher than 20% — the market expects more volatility than the formula's 20% assumption. IV is the market's consensus estimate of future realized volatility. Where Black-Scholes breaks: Assumption Reality Consequence --- --- --- Returns are log-normally distributed (no jumps) Returns have fat tails — crashes happen far more often than a bell curve predicts OTM puts are underpriced by BS → market adds skew (higher IV for OTM puts) Volatility is constant Vol changes constantly and correlates with price (vol up when market down) The volatility surface exists — IV varies by strike and maturity Continuous trading, no gaps Markets close overnight and on weekends. Stocks gap on earnings. Gap risk isn't captured by BS → actual hedging costs exceed model predictions No transaction costs Every hedge costs bid-ask + commissions In practice, you can't delta-hedge continuously — you rebalance discretely Risk-free rate is constant Rates change Minor for short-dated options, material for LEAPS No early exercise American options can be exercised early Must use binomial models for American options The vol surface (the consequence of BS being wrong): - If BS were perfectly correct, all strikes and maturities would have the same IV - In reality, OTM puts have higher IV than ATM options (the "skew") because the market fears crashes - Different maturities have different IV levels (the "term structure") - The full picture is a 3D surface: IV as a function of both strike and maturity → covered in depth in E Volatility

$$C - P = S \cdot e^{-qT} - K \cdot e^{-rT}$$ > In plain English: A call minus a put (same strike and expiry) equals a forward position. If this relationship breaks beyond transaction costs, there's an arbitrage opportunity — buy the cheap side, sell the expensive side, and lock in risk-free profit. This is one of the few true "no arbitrage" relationships in finance. Why it matters for risk: Put-call parity means if you know 3 of the 4 values (call, put, spot, strike), you can derive the 4th. It also means a long call + short put = synthetic long stock (same P&L, funded differently). ---

Strategy What you do Your view Max gain Max loss Greeks profile --- --- --- --- --- --- Long call Buy a call Bullish, want move UP Unlimited Premium paid +Δ, +Γ, −Θ, +ν Short call Sell a call Neutral/bearish, want no move UP Premium received Unlimited −Δ, −Γ, +Θ, −ν Long put Buy a put Bearish, want move DOWN Strike − premium Premium paid −Δ, +Γ, −Θ, +ν Short put Sell a put Neutral/bullish, want no move DOWN Premium received Strike − premium +Δ, −Γ, +Θ, −ν > Pattern: Long options = long gamma, long vega, short theta (you pay rent for protection). Short options = short gamma, short vega, long theta (you collect rent but bear tail risk). Always.

section: Payoff Diagrams — The Four Building Blocks
content: > Every multi-leg strategy is a combination of these four shapes. Memorize them. LONG CALL SHORT CALL (Buy call at strike K, pay P) (Sell call at strike K, receive P) P&L P&L ^ / ^ K / +P + \ / \ 0 +-- -- -- -- -- -------> Price 0 +-- -- -- -- -- -- -------> Price K+P K+P -P + K \ \ Max gain: Unlimited Max gain: Premium (P) Max loss: Premium (P) Max loss: Unlimited B/E: K + P B/E: K + P LONG PUT SHORT PUT (Buy put at strike K, pay P) (Sell put at strike K, receive P) P&L P&L ^ ^ \ +P + K \ / \ 0 +----- -- -- -- -- -- --> Price 0 +----- -- -- -- -- --> Price K-P K-P / -P + K / / Max gain: K - P (price to 0) Max gain: Premium (P) Max loss: Premium (P) Max loss: K - P (price to 0) B/E: K - P B/E: K - P

Strategy Construction View What it does Cost Max gain Max loss --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Bull call spread Buy lower-strike call + sell higher-strike call Moderately bullish Caps your upside but reduces premium cost Net debit Difference in strikes − net debit Net debit Bear put spread Buy higher-strike put + sell lower-strike put Moderately bearish Caps your downside profit but cheaper than outright put Net debit Difference in strikes − net debit Net debit Bull put spread Sell higher-strike put + buy lower-strike put Moderately bullish (credit) Collect premium, hope stock stays above short strike Net credit Net credit Difference in strikes − net credit Bear call spread Sell lower-strike call + buy higher-strike call Moderately bearish (credit) Collect premium, hope stock stays below short strike Net credit Net credit Difference in strikes − net credit Analogy for spreads: A spread is like buying insurance with a deductible. The outright option covers everything (full insurance). The spread covers a range — cheaper, but capped. You're giving up the tail to reduce cost. BULL CALL SPREAD BEAR PUT SPREAD (Buy K1 call, sell K2 call) (Buy K2 put, sell K1 put) P&L P&L ^ ^ Max +........./ K2 Max + \ / K1 \ 0 +-- -- - -- -- -- -- --> Price 0 +-- -- -- -- -- -- -- --> Price K1 K2 -D + / -D + \ Max gain: (K2-K1) - Debit Max gain: (K2-K1) - Debit Max loss: Net debit (D) Max loss: Net debit (D) View: Moderately bullish View: Moderately bearish

Strategy Construction View Breakeven What to know --- --- --- --- --- Long straddle Buy ATM call + Buy ATM put (same strike, same expiry) Big move expected, DON'T KNOW direction Strike ± total premium paid Most expensive vol bet. Works if realized vol > implied vol. You're paying double theta. Long strangle Buy OTM call + Buy OTM put Cheaper than straddle, needs BIGGER move Call strike + premium (upside) / Put strike − premium (downside) Wider breakevens but cheaper premium. Better for "it'll move a lot or not at all" views. Short straddle Sell ATM call + Sell ATM put Market will NOT MOVE Strike ± total premium collected Maximum premium collected but UNLIMITED risk both ways. The classic "selling vol" trade. Feb 2018 Volmageddon. Short strangle Sell OTM call + Sell OTM put Market stays in a range Wider range than straddle — profits if stock stays between the two strikes Same unlimited risk as short straddle but with more breathing room. LONG STRADDLE LONG STRANGLE (Buy ATM call + ATM put at K) (Buy OTM put at K1 + OTM call at K2) P&L P&L ^ ^ \ / \ / \ / \ / \ / \ / 0 +----- -------- -----> Price 0 +---- -- -- -- -- -- ----> Price K-2P K+2P K1-P K2+P -2P +..........V -P +.....\ / K K1 K2 Max gain: Unlimited (both ways) Max gain: Unlimited (both ways) Max loss: 2 x Premium (2P) Max loss: Total premium paid B/E: K +/- 2P B/E: K1-P (down) / K2+P (up) View: Expect big move View: Same, cheaper but wider B/E

Strategy Construction View What it does Key insight --- --- --- --- --- Collar Long stock + buy put + sell call Protect downside, cap upside Creates a range: protected below put strike, capped above call strike Can be structured at zero cost (premium from sold call pays for bought put). Extremely common hedge for concentrated stock positions. Covered call Long stock + sell OTM call Slightly bullish, want income Earn premium on stock you own. Give up upside beyond the call strike. "The income strategy." Popular with retail. Actually a short put synthetically (via put-call parity). Protective put Long stock + buy put Bullish but worried about crash Floor on losses at put strike. Keep all upside. Insurance policy. Expensive in high-vol environments (exactly when you want it most). COLLAR COVERED CALL (Long stock + buy K1 put + (Long stock + sell K call) sell K2 call) P&L P&L ^ ^ Max +........./ K2 Max +............. / / K 0 +-- -- /-- -- -- -- --> Price 0 +-- -- -- - -- -- -- -- --> Price K1 / Min + / / / Max gain: K2 - entry (capped) Max gain: (K - entry) + premium Max loss: entry - K1 (floored) Max loss: entry - premium View: Protect downside, cap up View: Mildly bullish, earn income

Strategy Construction View What it does Key insight --- --- --- --- --- Butterfly Buy 1 low K call + sell 2 middle K calls + buy 1 high K call Stock stays near middle strike Cheap bet on low volatility and pinning near a specific price. Max profit if stock closes exactly at middle K at expiry. Very defined risk. Iron condor Bear call spread + bull put spread (all OTM) Range-bound Profit if stock stays between the two short strikes Defined risk version of short strangle. Premium collected is max gain. Popular with systematic premium-selling funds. Risk reversal Sell OTM put + buy OTM call (or vice versa) Strongly directional (bullish or bearish) Net zero or near-zero cost. Full upside exposure funded by downside risk. One-for-one with directional view. The spread between ATM call and ATM put implied vols IS the "risk reversal" — it measures skew. Calendar spread (time spread) Sell near-dated option + buy far-dated option (same strike) Low vol now, higher vol later, or theta decay play Near-dated option decays faster than far-dated → you profit from the differential Profits if stock stays near strike while near-dated bleeds theta. Sensitive to vol term structure. Diagonal spread Buy far-dated option + sell near-dated option at different strike Directional + theta play Combines calendar (time decay) with directional tilt More complex but allows you to express view on direction + time + vol simultaneously. BUTTERFLY IRON CONDOR (Buy K1, sell 2x K2, buy K3 calls) (Bull put spread + bear call spread) P&L P&L ^ ^ /\ Max +......../ .\ +P +------ ------ / \ / K2 K3 \ 0 +------ ------ -------> Price 0 +-- - -- -- -- -- -- --- Price K1 K2 K3 K1 K4 -D + / \ Max + / \ Loss Max gain: (K2-K1) - debit Max gain: Net premium received Max loss: Net debit Max loss: Width of spread - premium View: Stock pins at K2 View: Range-bound, defined risk

Situation Best strategy Why --- --- --- "I'm bullish with limited budget" Bull call spread Cheaper than outright call, defined risk "I want downside protection, hate paying premium" Collar (zero-cost) or put spread Sell away upside or tail to fund the protection "Earnings next week, stock will move big but I don't know which way" Long straddle or strangle Pays for movement in either direction "Vol is too high, stock will stay in a range" Iron condor or short strangle Collect premium, profit from vol overestimation "I want to express max conviction on direction" Risk reversal Most directional exposure per dollar "I want income on a stock I own" Covered call Collect premium, give up tail upside "I think stock pins at exactly $150 by expiry" Butterfly centered at $150 Cheap, defined risk, max payout at the pin ---

> One-liner: A swap is an OTC agreement between two parties to exchange cash flows over time — typically one side pays a fixed amount and the other pays a floating amount, based on an underlying reference. Analogy: A swap is like two neighbors agreeing to swap their electricity bills. One pays a flat $200/month (fixed), the other pays whatever the actual bill is (floating). If electricity prices rise, the fixed payer wins. If they fall, the floating payer wins. Neither neighbor buys electricity for the other — they just settle the difference.

> One-liner: An IRS is an agreement to exchange fixed-rate payments for floating-rate payments on a notional amount — the most traded derivative in the world. Role Pays Receives Wins when --- --- --- --- Fixed payer (pays fixed, receives floating) Fixed rate (e.g., 4.00%) SOFR (floating, resets periodically) Rates RISE (you locked in low fixed, receive higher floating) Fixed receiver (pays floating, receives fixed) SOFR Fixed rate (e.g., 4.00%) Rates FALL (you locked in high fixed, pay lower floating) Key features: - Notional amount is never exchanged — it's just a reference for calculating payments. A $100M notional swap doesn't mean $100M changes hands — just the net interest differential. - Tenor: Common: 2Y, 5Y, 10Y, 30Y. Longer tenor = more rate sensitivity (higher DV01). - DV01 of a swap: A 5Y IRS with $100M notional has DV01 ≈ $50,000. Meaning: a 1bp move in the swap rate changes the swap's value by $50K. - Swap rate = the fixed rate that makes the swap fair (PV of fixed leg = PV of floating leg at inception). - Swap curve = swap rates plotted by tenor. It's the market's expectation of future short-term rates, incorporating credit and liquidity premiums. Who uses IRS: - Banks: Convert floating-rate assets to fixed income streams (or vice versa) - Corporates: Issue floating-rate debt but want fixed → enter swap as fixed payer - Hedge funds: Express rate views with leverage. A $100M swap requires much less capital than buying $100M in bonds. - Pension funds: Match long-dated liabilities (pension payments) with long-dated fixed-rate swap receipts

> One-liner: A TRS gives you the total economic exposure (price change + dividends) to an asset WITHOUT owning it — in exchange for paying a financing rate. Party Receives Pays --- --- --- Total return receiver Price appreciation + dividends on reference asset SOFR + spread (financing cost) Total return payer SOFR + spread Exposes themselves to price depreciation + pays dividends Why TRS matters — the Archegos lesson: - No ownership: You never buy the stock, so no 13F disclosure (for equity TRS). No voting rights. But you have full economic exposure. - Leverage: Initial margin on a TRS might be 10-15%, giving you 7-10x leverage. Archegos used TRS to build $30B+ in notional equity exposure on ~$3B of capital. - Opacity: Each prime broker only sees their own TRS positions with you. No single PB saw Archegos' total exposure until it was too late. - Risk: Counterparty risk (PB can demand margin or terminate), leverage (amplifies both ways), concentration (TRS on a single name is a leveraged single-stock bet). - Post-Archegos regulations: The SEC now requires TRS reporting on large positions. Prime brokers improved cross-margining visibility. But the fundamental risk of synthetic leverage remains.

> One-liner: A CDS is insurance against a company defaulting — the buyer pays a regular premium and gets paid if the reference entity has a "credit event." See A Cash Instruments Part 5 for full CDS mechanics, spreads, and basis.

Swap What it is Who uses it --- --- --- Cross-currency swap Exchange principal AND interest payments in two different currencies. Includes FX exchange at inception and maturity. Corporates with foreign-currency borrowing. Banks managing multi-currency books. Basis swap Exchange two floating rates (e.g., 3M SOFR vs 6M SOFR, or SOFR vs Fed Funds rate) Banks managing funding spread; relative value traders exploiting basis gaps Commodity swap Exchange floating commodity price for fixed price. Airline pays fixed for fuel, counterparty pays floating. Airlines, utilities, producers locking in revenue/cost Equity swap TRS variant — exchange equity index return for financing rate Funds gaining synthetic equity exposure, tax-efficient access to foreign markets Variance swap Pay fixed variance (strike), receive realized variance. Pure vol bet with NO delta exposure. Vol traders, hedge funds. Short variance = unlimited downside (Volmageddon 2018). Inflation swap Exchange fixed rate for realized CPI inflation Pension funds matching inflation-linked liabilities ---

You won't be asked to price these, but you should know what they are and why they're risky: Exotic What it is Pricing approach Key risk --- --- --- --- Barrier option Knock-in (activates at barrier) or knock-out (dies at barrier) Analytical (simple) or Monte Carlo Pin risk near barrier — delta jumps violently. A knock-out at $100 means at $99.90 you have an option and at $100.10 you don't. Asian option Payoff based on AVERAGE price over the option's life Monte Carlo (path-dependent, no closed-form) Lower vol than vanilla → cheaper. Useful for hedging average costs (e.g., monthly fuel purchases). Lookback option Payoff based on the maximum or minimum price achieved during the life Monte Carlo Very expensive — like being told "you can buy at the lowest price of the year." High path dependency. Quanto option Payoff in one currency, underlying in another, with FX rate FIXED Modified BS with correlation adjustment Removes FX risk from foreign asset exposure. Requires modeling correlation between asset and FX. Cliquet / Ratchet Series of forward-starting options — locks in periodic returns Monte Carlo or semi-analytical Popular in structured products. Complex vol surface dependency. Swaption Option on a swap — right to enter an IRS at a future date at a set rate Modified BS (Black's model for swaptions) Vol-of-rates exposure. Critical for rates vol trading. Used by mortgage servicers to hedge prepayment risk. Variance swap Pays realized variance minus strike variance × notional No option pricing needed — traded as pure swap Short variance = unlimited downside. In Feb 2018, XIV (short VIX ETN) lost 96% of its value overnight. Convertible bond Bond with embedded call option on the issuer's equity BS + credit model + bond pricing Complex Greeks (delta, gamma, rho, credit spread sensitivity). "Cheap equity" if conversion value exceeds bond value. Arb strategy: long convert, short stock, harvest gamma. ---

> Every derivative has implicit leverage. A $500K premium on SPX options can give you $20M notional exposure. If SPX moves 10%, your option P&L scales to the $20M notional, not the $500K premium. This is by design.

Options don't move 1:1 with the underlying — gamma accelerates or decelerates P&L. A short gamma position can go from "manageable" to "catastrophic" in a single session because losses ACCELERATE as the market moves against you. The sell-off doesn't just cost you delta × move — it costs you delta × move PLUS gamma × move² / 2. That squared term is what kills.

In calm markets, OTC derivatives show tight bid-ask spreads. In stress, market makers widen quotes or stop quoting entirely. Your mark-to-market valuation may not reflect what you can actually sell for. This is especially true for: - Long-dated options - OTM options in stressed names - Exotic structures with no active secondary market

Exotic derivatives are priced by models. If the model's assumptions break (vol surface shifts in an unexpected way, correlations spike to 1, liquidity vanishes), your "perfectly hedged" position isn't hedged. This happened in: - Feb 2018 (vol models didn't anticipate VIX > 50) - March 2020 (correlation assumptions broke in credit derivatives) - LME nickel 2022 (price moves exceeded model boundaries)

The deadly spiral: 1. Market moves against you → exchange increases initial margin requirements during the vol spike 2. You must post more cash → sell other positions to raise cash 3. Those forced sales push down prices → trigger margin calls at OTHER counterparties 4. They sell → prices drop further → more margin calls → spiral This is how Nick Leeson (Barings), Amaranth (NG), and MF Global (European sovereign bonds) all died.

OTC derivatives (swaps, forwards, exotic options) expose you to the counterparty's ability to pay. If your counterparty defaults while you have a large unrealized gain, you lose that gain. AIG in 2008 sold massive CDS protection and couldn't pay when defaults spiked — required a $182B government bailout. Post-2008 reforms (central clearing, margin requirements, CSA agreements) reduced but didn't eliminate this risk. ---

If asked "Explain the difference between a future and an option": "A future is an obligation — both sides MUST transact at expiry. An option gives the holder the RIGHT but not the obligation. This asymmetry is the fundamental difference, and it's why options cost a premium while futures are theoretically free to enter. The risk profiles are completely different. Futures have linear payoffs — you gain or lose dollar-for-dollar with the underlying, amplified by leverage. Options have non-linear payoffs — if you're long, your max loss is the premium, but your exposure changes as the underlying moves because of gamma. For risk management, futures are simpler — just offset the delta. Options require managing multiple dimensions simultaneously: delta, gamma, vega, theta. A single Greek in isolation is misleading — it's the interaction of all of them together that determines the real risk." If asked "How do you price different types of options?": "It depends on the exercise style and payoff structure. European options — where exercise only happens at expiry — can be priced with Black-Scholes, which gives a closed-form analytical solution. American options — where you can exercise early — need a binomial tree, because at every point you have to check whether early exercise is optimal. Asian options — where the payoff depends on the average price over the life — require Monte Carlo simulation because the average is path-dependent and there's no analytical shortcut. The practical implication: American options are always at least as expensive as European options because the early exercise right has value. Asian options are cheaper than European because averaging reduces the payoff volatility. So when a PM asks 'how should I hedge my quarterly fuel costs?' an Asian option structure makes more sense — and it's cheaper — than a chain of European options." If asked "What makes options more or less expensive?": "Five things drive option premiums. Implied vol is the dominant factor — higher vol means bigger expected moves means the option is worth more. Time to expiry matters because more time = more opportunity for the option to become valuable. Interest rates affect the forward price, making calls pricier and puts cheaper when rates rise. Dividends do the opposite — they reduce the forward, making calls cheaper. And moneyness: ATM options have maximum time value, while deep OTM options are cheap but have low probability of paying off. For the RM, the key question is: 'Are we paying too much?' If implied vol is well above recent realized vol, we're overpaying for protection. If it's below, we're getting cheap insurance — which is often the best time to buy, even though it feels like we don't need it." --- Related files: → D Greeks — measuring the risk of derivative positions (delta, gamma, theta, vega and second-order Greeks) → E Volatility — implied volatility, vol surface, skew, term structure → A Cash Instruments — the underlyings these derivatives are based

section: PART 1: TRADE LIFECYCLE — FROM CLICK TO SETTLEMENT
content: > One-liner: A trade isn't done when you click "buy" — it has to be routed, executed, cleared, and settled, and things can go wrong at every step.
1. Order Decision → 2. Order Placement → 3. Routing → 4. Execution → 5. Confirmation → 6. Clearing (CCP novation) → 7. Settlement (delivery vs payment)

The PM decides to buy 50,000 shares of XOM at ~$115. The execution desk assesses: current ADV ≈ 20M shares/day, so 50K is 0.25% of ADV — no market impact concern. But if the order were 5M shares (25% ADV), the approach changes completely.

> One-liner: The order type you choose determines whether you prioritize speed or price — and the wrong choice in the wrong market can cost you millions. Order Type What it does Guaranteed Risk When to use --- --- --- --- --- Market order Execute immediately at the best available price Execution ✓ NOT price. In a thin book, you can cross multiple levels. Flash Crash 2010: market orders sold into empty order books. Small, liquid positions. Urgency trumps cost. Limit order Execute only at my price or better Price ✓ NOT execution. You may never get filled if the price moves away. You have a target price and can afford to wait. Stop order Becomes a market order when a trigger price is hit Execution (once triggered) ✓ Gap risk — stock can gap through your stop and execute far worse than expected. Risk management: automatic exit if position moves against you. Stop-limit Becomes a limit order when trigger is hit Price (once triggered) ✓ May NOT execute if price gaps past both levels. The "worst of both worlds" risk. Want stop protection but refuse to sell below a floor. MOC (Market on Close) Execute at the official closing price Closing price ✓ Must submit before cutoff (~3:45 PM). Large MOC imbalances move the close. Benchmark to close. Index rebalancing. Pension flows. Iceberg Shows only a small "visible" portion; the rest is hidden Stealth execution Only partially hidden — sophisticated algos can detect the pattern. Large orders: minimize information leakage to other participants. Pegged Automatically adjusts with the NBBO (midpoint peg, primary peg) Best available price In fast markets, the peg may lag and result in adverse fills. Dark pool orders seeking price improvement.

When the order is large relative to ADV, you use an algo to minimize market impact: Algorithm How it works Best for Weakness --- --- --- --- VWAP (Volume-Weighted Average Price) Splits order across the day proportional to historical volume profile Benchmark to VWAP, minimize deviation Predictable — HFTs can detect VWAP algos and trade ahead TWAP (Time-Weighted Average Price) Splits order evenly across fixed time intervals Even execution when volume profile doesn't matter Doesn't adapt to actual volume Implementation Shortfall (IS) Front-loads execution to minimize slippage from decision price Minimize total cost vs arrival price More market impact upfront, but less slippage from delay Liquidity-seeking Only executes when it finds pockets of liquidity (dark pools, crosses) Minimize market impact at all costs Slow — may not complete if liquidity is thin Percentage of Volume (POV) Participates at a fixed % of real-time volume (e.g., 10% of volume) Adapt execution to live market conditions Slow in low-volume periods, fast in high-volume Market impact cost — the hidden tax: If you're buying 500K shares of a mid-cap stock (ADV = 2M), your buying pressure itself pushes the price up. Suppose you buy all day at 25% POV: - Shares 1–100K: filled at $50.00–$50.05 (barely noticed) - Shares 100K–300K: filled at $50.05–$50.20 (market starts sensing the buyer) - Shares 300K–500K: filled at $50.20–$50.40 (aggressive sellers pull back, market adjusts) - Your VWAP: ~$50.18 vs arrival price of $50.00 → market impact cost = 36bps ($90K on $25M notional) This is called slippage and it can eat into returns more than commissions for any fund of meaningful size.

> One-liner: The clearinghouse (CCP) steps between buyer and seller, becoming the counterparty to both — this eliminates bilateral default risk but concentrates systemic risk in the CCP itself. The novation process: 1. Trade is matched and reported to the CCP 2. CCP "novates" — the original contract between A and B becomes TWO contracts: A→CCP and CCP→B 3. Both sides post initial margin to the CCP 4. Daily variation margin is exchanged based on mark-to-market Key CCPs: CCP What it clears Region --- --- --- DTCC / NSCC US equities, corporate bonds US CME Clearing Futures, options on futures, cleared OTC IRS US ICE Clear Futures (energy, credit), CDS US/Europe LCH (SwapClear) ~90% of cleared IRS globally Global Eurex Clearing Euro Stoxx futures/options, Euro rates Europe OCC Listed equity options in the US US CCP risk management — the "waterfall": If a clearing member defaults, the CCP uses this sequence to absorb losses: 1. Defaulter's initial margin — first line of defense 2. Defaulter's contribution to the guaranty/default fund 3. CCP's own capital ("skin in the game") — usually small 4. Other members' default fund contributions — this is where contagion risk lives. You can lose money from SOMEONE ELSE's default. 5. Assessment powers — CCP can demand additional contributions (up to a limit) The systemic risk tension: CCPs eliminate bilateral counterparty risk but create a single point of failure. If a CCP itself fails, the entire market freezes. This is why CCPs are designated as "systemically important financial market utilities" (SIFMUs) and are subject to central bank backstops.

> One-liner: Settlement is the actual exchange of securities for cash — until settlement, you have counterparty risk even on exchange-traded instruments. Market Settlement Cycle Key detail --- --- --- US equities T+1 Shortened from T+2 in May 2024. Reduces counterparty exposure but requires faster cash/stock delivery. US Treasuries T+1 Same-day settlement available for some. European equities T+2 TARGET2-Securities platform. Moving toward T+1. FX spot T+2 CLS Bank settles ~$6T/day, eliminating "Herstatt risk" (time zone mismatch). Futures Daily MTM No end settlement for financially-settled. Physical delivery at expiry for physical. OTC derivatives Per ISDA/CSA Bilateral or cleared. CSA governs collateral exchange. Settlement risk (Herstatt risk): In 1974, Bankhaus Herstatt received DEM payments in Frankfurt (morning) but was closed by regulators before paying out USD in New York (afternoon). Counterparties paid but never received. This is why CLS Bank was created — simultaneous (payment-vs-payment) settlement of FX across time zones. Failed trades: When a seller can't deliver securities by settlement date. Fails spiked during March 2020 (Treasury market stress) and September 2019 (repo crisis). Chronic fails signal market dysfunction and can trigger regulatory penalties.

section: Listed vs OTC — The Two Worlds
content: Feature Listed (Exchange-traded) OTC (Over-the-counter) --- --- --- Standardization Fixed contract specs (strike, expiry, size) Fully customizable terms Counterparty risk CCP guarantees both sides Bilateral (unless centrally cleared post-Dodd-Frank) Transparency Public prices, volume, open interest Private pricing (price reported post-trade for some, e.g., TRACE for bonds) Liquidity Concentrated in standard contracts Fragmented, dealer-dependent Margin Exchange-set, rules-based (SPAN/TIMS) Per CSA negotiation or cleared margin rules (SIMM) Examples Equity options, futures, ETFs IRS, CDS, swaptions, TRS, exotic options, FX forwards Regulation Exchange rules, SEC/CFTC oversight ISDA Master Agreement, Dodd-Frank/EMIR clearing mandates ---

section: PART 2: THE OTC INFRASTRUCTURE — ISDA, CSA & COLLATERAL
content: > One-liner: The ISDA Master Agreement is the constitution of OTC derivatives — it governs what happens when things go wrong between two counterparties.

Every OTC derivative trade between two institutions is governed by a single overarching ISDA Master Agreement. Key provisions: Component What it does Why it matters --- --- --- Netting If you have multiple trades with the same counterparty, only the NET amount is owed if one side defaults Without netting, you'd owe on losing trades but be an unsecured creditor on winning trades. Netting can reduce exposure by 80-90%. Close-out If a "credit event" occurs (default, bankruptcy, failure to pay), ALL trades are terminated and netted to a single amount Prevents cherry-picking: the defaulter can't keep profitable trades and walk away from losses. Events of default Failure to pay, breach of agreement, bankruptcy, credit downgrade triggers Defines when you can terminate all trades and demand payment. CSA (Credit Support Annex) Governs collateral posting for mark-to-market exposure Determines margin requirements, eligible collateral, thresholds, minimum transfer amounts.

section: Credit Support Annex (CSA) — The Collateral Framework
content: The CSA defines HOW counterparties exchange collateral on bilateral OTC trades: CSA Term What it means Impact --- --- --- Threshold The amount of exposure BELOW which no collateral is required Higher threshold = more unsecured exposure = more counterparty risk Minimum transfer amount (MTA) Don't bother exchanging collateral for tiny moves. Typical: $500K–$1M Reduces operational burden but creates small uncollateralized gaps Eligible collateral What you can post: cash (USD, EUR), G10 govt bonds, sometimes equities/gold Non-cash collateral gets a "haircut" (e.g., 10Y UST posted at 98% of market value) Haircuts Discount applied to non-cash collateral to protect against collateral's own price risk Higher haircut = more conservative = need to post more Frequency How often margin is called — daily is standard now (post-2008 reforms) Before 2008, some CSAs were weekly or even monthly — huge gap risk Rehypothecation Can the collateral receiver RE-USE posted collateral (lend it, pledge it)? If yes: more capital efficient. If the receiver defaults: your collateral may be gone. Lehman's collapse trapped $65B+ of rehypothecated client collateral. Post-2008 reforms (UMR — Uncleared Margin Rules): - Bilateral OTC derivatives now require MANDATORY initial margin AND variation margin - IM must be segregated with a third-party custodian (can't be rehypothecated) - Standard Initial Margin Model (SIMM) — ISDA's formula for calculating IM based on sensitivities - Goal: reduce the unsecured exposure that amplified the 2008 crisis ---

section: PART 3: MARGIN — THE MECHANICS OF LEVERAGE
content: > One-liner: Margin is the collateral you post to hold a position — it creates leverage, and the margin cycle is what kills funds in a crisis.

Type What it is When it's posted Example --- --- --- --- Initial margin (IM) Deposit required to OPEN a position. Performance bond against future losses. At trade inception Futures: 5-15% of notional. E-mini S&P: ~$13K on $270K notional. Variation margin (VM) Daily mark-to-market settlement. Losing side pays, winning side receives. Daily (or intraday in extreme moves) You're short oil at $75. Oil goes to $77. You owe $2 × 1,000 barrels = $2,000 per contract. Maintenance margin The MINIMUM margin level you must maintain. Below this → margin call. Ongoing monitoring IM = $13,000, maintenance = $10,500. Account drops to $9,000 → margin call for $4,000 (back to IM). How margin is calculated — the models: Model Used by Method --- --- --- SPAN (Standard Portfolio Analysis of Risk) CME, most futures exchanges Simulates 16 scenarios of price and vol moves. Margin = worst-case loss. Portfolio-based (offsets recognized). TIMS (Theoretical Intermarket Margin System) OCC (US equity options) Similar scenario-based approach for options. SIMM (Standard Initial Margin Model) Bilateral OTC (ISDA) Sensitivity-based: margin = weighted sum of delta, vega, curvature sensitivities across risk factors. VaR-based Some prime brokers Historical or Monte Carlo VaR at 99% confidence, 10-day horizon.

section: The Margin Call Cascade — How Funds Die
content: Price moves against your position → Account falls below maintenance margin → MARGIN CALL: post additional cash by deadline (same day, sometimes hours) → You don't have cash → sell other positions to raise it → Selling pressure pushes THOSE positions down → More margin calls on those newly-declining positions → Other leveraged funds in the same trade face the same pressure → Coordinated forced selling → prices collapse → Clearinghouse raises IM requirements (pro-cyclical) → Even solvent funds face calls they can't meet → SPIRAL Real examples: - March 2020 Treasury basis trade: Hedge funds leveraged 50-100x (via repo), long cash Treasuries, short Treasury futures. Margin calls spiked → forced selling of "safe" assets into an illiquid market → Treasury prices dropped (yields spiked) → more margin calls. The Fed bought $1T+ of Treasuries to stop the spiral. - LME Nickel, March 2022: Tsingshan's short position faced margin calls as nickel doubled. LME cancelled trades, suspended the market for a week — breaking market integrity to prevent a clearing member default. - Archegos, March 2021: TRS positions concentrated in a few stocks. One stock dropped → margin call → couldn't pay → prime brokers liquidated → fire-sale of $30B+ in positions crashed other names → contagion to Credit Suisse ($5.5B loss), Nomura ($3B loss).

> One-liner: Exchanges raise margin requirements when volatility spikes — demanding more cash from you exactly when your positions are losing money and markets are illiquid. In calm markets (VIX 12): E-mini IM ≈ $10,000. Exchange says: "Low risk, low margin." In volatile markets (VIX 40): E-mini IM ≈ $25,000. Exchange says: "High risk, need more collateral." The problem: That $15,000 increase hits EVERY contract you hold into a spike. If you have 200 contracts: that's an additional $3M in margin needed — exactly when your positions are losing money, your credit lines are maxed, and everyone else is scrambling for cash too. Regulatory response: Post-2008 reforms introduced anti-procyclicality buffers — CCPs must set floors below which margin can't fall, and use longer lookback periods so margin doesn't spike as violently. But the fundamental tension remains. ---

section: PART 4: REPO — THE PLUMBING OF FINANCIAL MARKETS
content: > One-liner: A repo (repurchase agreement) is a short-term collateralized loan — I sell you a Treasury today and agree to buy it back tomorrow at a slightly higher price. The difference is the interest rate.

Day 1 (trading): Fund A: sells $100M of Treasuries to Dealer B Dealer B: pays Fund A $99M (haircut = $1M = 1%) Fund A now has $99M cash to deploy Day 2 (maturity): Fund A: buys back Treasuries from Dealer B Fund A: pays $99M + interest ($99M × repo rate × days/360) Dealer B: returns the Treasuries It's a LOAN with extra steps. The Treasuries are collateral. The haircut is the lender's cushion. The repo rate is the interest rate on the loan.

Term What it is Typical --- --- --- Repo rate The interest rate on the loan Close to SOFR (currently ~4.3% for overnight UST repo) Haircut % discount applied to collateral value. $100M bonds → receive $99M cash. UST: 1-2%. IG corporates: 3-5%. HY: 10-25%. Equities: 15-25%. Term Duration of the repo. Overnight (most common), term (1 week to 3 months), or open (rolls daily). 70%+ is overnight General collateral (GC) Any Treasury of acceptable quality. No specific bond requested. Most repo is GC Special A SPECIFIC bond is demanded (e.g., the on-the-run 10Y). Commands a lower repo rate because the collateral is scarce. When a bond is heavily shorted or in high demand

The mirror image: you LEND cash and receive securities as collateral. The Fed's Reverse Repo Facility (ON RRP) became a $2T+ parking facility — money market funds lent cash to the Fed overnight and received Treasuries as collateral, effectively setting a floor on short-term rates.

Leverage via repo — how basis trades work: 1. Buy $100M cash Treasuries 2. Repo them out → receive $99M cash 3. Use the $99M to buy $99M MORE Treasuries 4. Repo those out → receive $98M cash 5. Repeat → you now have $500M+ of Treasury exposure on maybe $10M of equity 6. Leverage = 50x. A 2% adverse move wipes out your equity 10 times over. Repo market stress signals: - Repo rate spikes above SOFR/Fed Funds: Indicates collateral shortage or cash hoarding. September 2019: overnight repo rate spiked to 10% (from ~2%) → Fed launched repo operations. - Haircut increases: A lender suddenly demanding 5% haircut instead of 2% is effectively CUTTING your leverage by 60%. If you built positions at 2% haircuts, you now need more of your own capital. - Repo rollover risk: If you fund a 10-year position with overnight repo (duration mismatch), you depend on rolling the repo every single day. If one day the dealer says "no" — you're forced to sell the bonds. Rehypothecation in repo: The collateral you posted can be re-used by the lender, who repos it to someone else, who repos it again. This creates a "collateral chain" — the same Treasury can support multiple repos simultaneously. This is efficient but creates systemic fragility: if one link in the chain fails, multiple parties are affected. ---
> One-liner: Leverage is borrowed risk — it amplifies returns in both directions and transforms manageable volatility into existential losses.

Method How it works Typical leverage Key risk --- --- --- --- Prime broker financing Borrow cash from PB against portfolio as collateral. Pay SOFR + spread. 1.5-5x (discretionary), up to 8x (quant) PB can change terms or pull financing at any time Futures Post 5-15% margin for 100% notional exposure 7-20x on the position Daily MTM, margin calls, forced liquidation Options (selling) Collect premium but bear full notional risk (or more for naked calls) Varies — effective leverage can be enormous Non-linear: small move can create massive loss Total return swaps Synthetic exposure, post only TRS margin (10-15%) 7-10x (Archegos had estimated 5-8x) Opacity — no position disclosure until post-Archegos rules Repo Collateralized borrowing against securities 10-50x for govt bonds, 3-5x for corporates Rollover risk, haircut changes, collateral chain fragility CFDs (Contracts for Difference) Synthetic delta-one exposure, margin-based 5-20x (retail FX up to 50x pre-regulation) Retail graveyard. ESMA capped leverage for EU retail.

Gross leverage = (Long notional + Short notional ) / NAV Net leverage = (Long notional − Short notional ) / NAV Example: Fund NAV = $100M. Long $200M equities, short $150M equities. - Gross = ($200M + $150M) / $100M = 3.5x → total market exposure - Net = ($200M − $150M) / $100M = 0.5x → directional exposure Profile Gross Net Interpretation --- --- --- --- High gross, low net 6x 0.2x "Market neutral" — lots of positions, little directional risk. But exposed to: factor risk, pair correlation, basis risk, crowding. High gross, high net 6x 4x Levered directional. Maximum risk. Low gross, high net 1.5x 1.2x Low leverage, directional. Conviction portfolio. Low gross, low net 1.5x 0.1x Low leverage, hedged. Conservative.

Notional alone is misleading. $100M notional in 2Y Treasuries (DV01 ≈ $200/bp) is NOT the same risk as $100M notional in 30Y Treasuries (DV01 ≈ $2,000/bp). Better measures: Measure What it captures Why it's better --- --- --- Risk-adjusted leverage Notional × risk factor (e.g., vol, duration) / NAV $100M in SPY (vol 15%) vs $100M in MSTR (vol 80%) → wildly different risk despite same notional DV01/NAV Total portfolio DV01 relative to NAV Captures rate risk regardless of notional. A $500M 2Y position and a $50M 30Y position may have similar DV01. Marginal VaR leverage Portfolio VaR / NAV Shows the actual loss potential normalized to capital. Accounts for correlations and netting. Effective leverage Gross exposure × average beta / NAV A $200M portfolio of beta-0.5 stocks has effective leverage of only 1x on $100M NAV, even though notional leverage is 2x.

- 2x leverage: A 10% decline → 20% of equity gone. A 50% decline → 100% of equity → wiped. - 10x leverage: A 10% decline → 100% of equity → wiped. - 50x leverage (Treasury basis): A 2% decline → 100% of equity → wiped. But losses are worse than gains because: 1. Drawdown asymmetry: -50% requires +100% to recover. -75% requires +300%. 2. Forced selling: Margin calls force you to sell at the bottom — locking in the loss and removing any chance of recovery. 3. Path dependency: A levered position that drops 30% then recovers 30% does NOT return to starting value. You lost more dollars on the way down (larger base) than you gained on the recovery. The Kelly Criterion insight: Even if your trades have positive expected value, too much leverage guarantees eventual ruin. The optimal bet size (Kelly fraction) is much smaller than most traders intuit. Over-leveraging a winning strategy still blows up because one bad draw can wipe out years of gains. ---

section: PART 6: LIQUIDITY — THE RISK THAT DOESN'T SHOW UP IN VaR
content: > One-liner: Liquidity is the ability to transact at fair value in size — it's abundant when you don't need it and vanishes when you do.

section: Market Liquidity — Can You Trade at Fair Value?
content: Metric What it measures Signal --- --- --- Bid-ask spread Cost of immediate execution. The "tax" for transacting. Tight spread = liquid. Wide = illiquid. SPY bid-ask ≈ $0.01 (1bp). Distressed corporate bond ≈ $2 (200bps). Depth How much size is available at the current bid and ask? (Level 2 order book) If best bid shows 5,000 shares but you need to sell 500,000 → you'll walk the book and eat through multiple price levels. Market impact How much does YOUR trade move the price? Measured in bps. Impact ∝ √(order size / ADV). Doubling order size doesn't double impact — it increases by ~40% (square root law). ADV (Average Daily Volume) Normal daily turnover Position > 10% ADV → takes multiple days to exit. In stress, ADV drops 30-50%. 3-day exit becomes 6-day. Resilience How quickly does the market recover after a large trade? Liquid markets snap back fast (S&P futures). Illiquid markets stay dislocated (HY bonds, small-cap equity).

section: Funding Liquidity — Can You Access Cash?
content: Source Normal conditions Stress conditions --- --- --- Cash reserves Available immediately Being drawn down to meet margin calls Credit lines (PB) Readily available at SOFR + spread PB may REDUCE or REVOKE lines. They have discretion. "Fair weather friends." Repo availability Haircuts 1-2%, easy to roll Haircuts jump to 5-10%, some collateral types unacceptable. Rollover refused. LP/investor redemptions Predictable, quarterly Investors queue to redeem. You must return capital while positions are losing. Asset sales You choose what/when to sell Forced selling at worst possible prices

Market liquidity and funding liquidity feed on each other in a doom loop: Market becomes illiquid (spreads widen, depth evaporates) → Positions can't be sold at fair value → mark-to-market drops → Funding pressure (margin calls, PB haircut increases, LP redemptions) → FORCED SELLING into an illiquid market → Your selling makes the market EVEN MORE illiquid → Prices drop further below fundamental value → Other funds in similar positions face the same pressure → Correlation spikes to 1 — everything sells together → CONTAGION → CCP raises margin requirements (pro-cyclical) → SPIRAL Key insight: This spiral is WHY seemingly safe portfolios (low-vol, hedged, "liquid" names) can have catastrophic losses. The positions aren't dangerous individually — the COMBINATION of leverage + illiquidity + correlated exits is what kills.

Your position in any name should be less than X days × Y% of ADV where you're comfortable with the exit timeline. Position as % of ADV Exit time (5% POV) Exit time (10% POV, more impact) --- --- --- 5% 1 day Half day 25% 5 days 2.5 days 50% 10 days 5 days 100% 20 days 10 days In stress, ADV drops by 30-50%. Double your exit estimates.

Tier Exit time Examples RM treatment --- --- --- --- Tier 1 < 1 day G10 FX, SPY, major index futures, on-the-run Treasuries Full mark-to-market confidence Tier 2 1–5 days Large-cap single stocks, IG corporate bonds, liquid options Mark is reliable, but exit creates market impact Tier 3 5–20 days Small-cap stocks, HY bonds, emerging market credit, off-the-run Treasuries Mark may be stale. Apply liquidity discount. Tier 4 > 20 days / uncertain Distressed debt, exotic OTC derivatives, private placements, illiquid structured products Mark to model, not market. Real exit price could be 10-30% worse. RM monitoring: Track portfolio mix by tier. If Tier 3+4 exceeds X% of NAV, the fund has a liquidity mismatch risk — especially if LPs can redeem quarterly.

The fund offers quarterly redemptions but holds positions that take 3 weeks to liquidate → structural mismatch . In a crisis: 1. LPs rush to redeem (first-mover advantage) 2. Fund must sell illiquid positions to raise cash 3. Fire-sale prices → NAV drops → remaining LPs face worse performance 4. Remaining LPs redeem too → death spiral Real example — Woodford Equity Income (2019): Open-ended fund held 20%+ illiquid positions. Redemptions surged. Fund was "gated" — investors couldn't withdraw. Eventually liquidated at massive losses. ---

section: PART 7: MARKET MICROSTRUCTURE — HOW PRICE DISCOVERY WORKS
content: > One-liner: Price discovery is the process by which the market aggregates information from thousands of participants into a single number — and understanding the mechanics tells you when to trust that number and when not to.

SELL SIDE (ASKS/OFFERS) ┌──────────────┬──────────┐ │ Price │ Size │ │ $50.05 │ 2,000 │ ← Best ask (best offer) │ $50.06 │ 8,000 │ │ $50.07 │ 15,000 │ │ $50.08 │ 5,000 │ │ $50.10 │ 20,000 │ └──────────────┴──────────┘ SPREAD = $0.02 (2 cents) ┌──────────────┬──────────┐ │ $50.03 │ 3,000 │ ← Best bid │ $50.02 │ 10,000 │ │ $50.01 │ 7,000 │ │ $50.00 │ 25,000 │ │ $49.98 │ 12,000 │ └──────────────┴──────────┘ BUY SIDE (BIDS) Reading the book: - Thick book (lots of size at each level): resilient market. Your order won't move the price much. - Thin book (small size): fragile market. A 50,000-share market order eats through multiple levels. - Asymmetric book (heavy bids, thin asks): potential bullish pressure — lots of buyers, few sellers. - Spoofing (illegal): Placing large fake orders to mislead other traders about supply/demand, then cancelling before execution.

Venue How it works % of US equity volume Key feature --- --- --- --- NYSE / NASDAQ Lit exchanges. Public order book. Price/time priority. ~55-60% combined Transparent price discovery. Maker/taker fees. Dark pools No pre-trade transparency. Orders match privately at or within NBBO. ~15-18% Minimizes information leakage. Large blocks. Wholesale market makers (Citadel, Virtu) Internalize retail orders. "Payment for order flow" (PFOF). ~20-25% (retail flow) Retail gets price improvement vs NBBO. But the market maker profits from the information in retail flow. Crossing networks Match buy and sell orders at scheduled times (auctions). ~3-5% Block trading, reduced market impact. ECNs Electronic matching, similar to exchanges but often with different fee structures. Included in lit exchange % IEX (speed bump), BATS (now Cboe). Reg NMS (US) / MiFID II (Europe): Mandates best execution — brokers must route orders to the venue offering the best price. Creates competition between venues but also fragments liquidity.

Exchanges incentivize liquidity provision: - Maker (adds liquidity — posts limit order): RECEIVES a rebate (~$0.002/share) - Taker (removes liquidity — sends market order that hits a resting limit): PAYS a fee (~$0.003/share) This creates a perverse incentive: some HFTs post fake limit orders to earn rebates, creating "phantom liquidity" that disappears when you try to trade against it.

> One-liner: Dealers are not neutral pipes — their hedging of options positions creates systematic buying and selling pressure that amplifies or dampens market moves. Dealer gamma exposure (GEX): Dealer position Hedging behavior Effect on market --- --- --- Long gamma (dealers bought options from clients) Buy dips, sell rallies (to stay delta-neutral) DAMPENS moves. Market stays range-bound. Low realized vol. Short gamma (dealers sold options to clients) Sell into declines, buy into rallies (to stay delta-neutral) AMPLIFIES moves. Market becomes more volatile. Moves accelerate. Gamma flip level: The spot price where dealers' aggregate gamma switches from positive to negative. BELOW this level, dealers are short gamma → they sell into declines → moves accelerate → this is why sell-offs speed up past a certain point. Dealer hedging flows that matter: - Monthly/quarterly OpEx: Options expiring removes the associated delta hedges. Large open interest at a strike can "pin" the stock there as dealers adjust hedges. After expiry, the pin releases and the stock moves freely. - End-of-month rebalancing: Pension funds, target-date funds rebalance to fixed allocation targets. If stocks rose, they sell stocks and buy bonds at month-end. These flows are predictable and large (estimated $5-15B on big months). - 0DTE (zero-days-to-expiry) options: Rapidly growing volume. Gamma is enormous (tiny time value, huge sensitivity to price). Creates intraday volatility spikes as dealers constantly rebalance.

Time (US Eastern) What happens RM implication --- --- --- 9:30–10:00 AM Opening auction. Overnight order flow, gap fills, knee-jerk reactions to news. DON'T overreact. The first 15 minutes of price action is noise, not signal. Wait for real flow. 8:30/10:00 AM Economic data releases (CPI, NFP at 8:30; PMI, JOLTS at 10:00). Can completely reverse the opening move. These are genuine information events. 11:30–1:30 PM Lunch lull. Volume drops 40-60%. Thin order books. Don't trust moves in thin markets. A single large order can create a "false" breakout. 3:00–3:50 PM "Power hour." Real institutional flow appears. Last chance for portfolio adjustments. MORE informative than morning. Moves in the last hour tend to have higher conviction. 3:50–4:00 PM MOC (Market on Close) imbalance published at 3:50. Rebalancing flow. Closing auction. Can create large last-minute moves. $2B+ MOC imbalance on rebalancing days is common. 4:00–8:00 PM & pre-market After-hours / pre-market. VERY thin. Earnings announcements move stocks ±10% on 1/10th of normal volume. Marks may gap at open.

Trigger Response Key detail --- --- --- Level 1: SPX -7% 15-minute halt (before 3:25 PM only) Triggered 4 times in March 2020 (2 weeks). Only once before that (Oct 1997). Level 2: SPX -13% 15-minute halt (before 3:25 PM only) Never triggered since current rules enacted (2013). Level 3: SPX -20% Market closes for the day Never triggered. Would signal a systemic crisis. Single-stock LULD Halt if price moves outside price band (5-20% depending on tier) for 15 seconds Prevents flash crashes in individual names. Bands tighten for more liquid stocks. Why halts matter for RM: During a halt, you CANNOT exit your position. If you're levered and the halt resolves with a gap against you, your losses can exceed any stop-loss level you set. Halts also create uncertainty — margin calls may be calculated on pre-halt prices, but the actual liquidation price may be far worse. ---

section: PART 8: SHORT SELLING — MECHANICS AND RISKS
content: > One-liner: Shorting is selling something you don't own by borrowing it first — the potential loss is unlimited because there's no ceiling on how high a stock can go.

Step 1: LOCATE — find shares to borrow from PB inventory, securities lending desks, beneficial owners (pension funds, ETFs, mutual funds) ↓ Step 2: BORROW — borrow shares, agree to borrow fee (annualized rate) ↓ Step 3: SELL — sell borrowed shares into the market (receive cash proceeds) ↓ Step 4: WAIT — position earns if stock drops, loses if stock rises You pay: borrow cost + any dividends on the shares ↓ Step 5: COVER — buy back shares in the open market and return to lender Profit = sell price − buy price − borrow costs − dividends paid

Component Detail Example --- --- --- Short sale proceeds When you sell borrowed shares, cash goes into your account — but it's RESTRICTED. Can't withdraw it. Sell 10,000 shares at $50 → $500K proceeds. This collateralizes the borrow. Short rebate You earn interest on the restricted cash proceeds (at a rate below SOFR) $500K × (SOFR − spread) = ~$18K/year income Borrow fee The cost to borrow shares. Expressed as annualized rate. Easy-to-borrow (GC): 0.25-0.50%. Special/hard-to-borrow: 5-50%+ annualized Net carry Short rebate − borrow fee. Can be positive (you earn) or negative (you pay). GC short: you EARN ~3.5%. HTB short at 30%: you PAY ~26%. Dividends If the stock pays a dividend while you're short, YOU pay the dividend to the lender. Short $500K of a 3% dividend stock = $15K/year cost on top of borrow. Buy-in risk If the lender recalls shares and you can't find a new borrow → forced buy-in at market price. Recall on a hard-to-borrow stock that's up 40% → catastrophic forced cover.

Metric What it is Signal --- --- --- Short interest (SI) Total shares currently sold short High SI = crowded short, squeeze risk Short interest ratio (SIR) SI / ADV = "days to cover" SIR > 10 days = very crowded. Squeeze potential. Borrow utilization SI / total shares available to borrow >90% = almost all available shares are borrowed. New shorts hard to open. Squeeze risk extreme. Cost to borrow Current annualized borrow rate Spike in cost signals: increased short demand, tighter supply, or upcoming catalyst

> One-liner: A short squeeze is a forced-buying feedback loop — shorts are compelled to buy, their buying pushes the price higher, which forces MORE shorts to buy. Stock rises on positive catalyst (or coordinated buying) → Shorts face mark-to-market losses → Some shorts hit stop-losses or margin limits → forced to cover (buy) → Their buying PUSHES the price higher → Lenders recall shares (want to sell into the rally) → forced buy-ins → More buying pressure → price goes parabolic → Remaining shorts capitulate → cover at massive losses GameStop (Jan 2021): 140% short interest (more shares sold short than existed — possible because of rehypothecation in the borrow chain). Coordinated retail buying via r/WallStreetBets triggered the squeeze. Stock went from $17 to $483. Melvin Capital lost 53% in January alone. VW (Oct 2008): Porsche disclosed it controlled 74% of VW shares + 31.5% via options = 95.5%. Only 5% of float was available. Shorts couldn't cover. VW briefly became the world's most valuable company at €1,000/share (from €200). A classic "infinite squeeze."

Rule What it does Impact --- --- --- Reg SHO (US) Must "locate" shares to borrow BEFORE selling short. Prevents naked shorting. Creates a cost/friction for shorting. Hard-to-borrow names are genuinely hard to short. Short Sale Rule (SSR/uptick rule) If a stock drops >10% in a day, short selling is restricted to upticks only (next day too) Reduces SHORT pressure in rapid declines but doesn't prevent it entirely Disclosure (EU SFTR, US 13F-2) Must disclose large short positions Alerts the market to crowded shorts (can trigger squeezes) Temporary bans Regulators can BAN shorting in a crisis (2008: financial stocks banned in US, UK, EU) Removes price discovery. Historically INCREASES long-term volatility, doesn't help stock prices. ---

section: PART 9: PUTTING IT ALL TOGETHER — RM MONITORING DASHBOARD
content: Metric What to watch Red flag --- --- --- Margin utilization Current margin used / total margin available > 70% in calm markets → NO cushion for vol spike Projected margin (stress) What margin would be at VIX +20 pts > 90% → must reduce exposure NOW Gross leverage Total exposure / NAV Discretionary: >4x is aggressive. Above 6x: extreme. Net leverage Directional exposure / NAV Diverging from gross → check if hedges are effective Portfolio liquidity (DTC) Days-to-close: position size / (ADV × participation rate) Any position > 5 days DTC deserves attention. > 10 days is a problem. Tier 3+4 as % of NAV How much is in illiquid positions? > 15% for an open-ended fund = redemption mismatch risk Repo rollover concentration How much is funded by overnight repo? > 50% funded overnight on a portfolio of long-dated assets = duration mismatch Short interest in book Borrow utilization on your shorts Any position > 80% utilization = squeeze risk. Monitor daily. Bid-ask spread widening Track spreads vs 30-day average on top positions Spreads > 2x normal = liquidity deteriorating. Reduce before it gets worse. MOC imbalance direction Large systematic flows at close Consistent one-way imbalance may signal forced flows (index rebalance, pension) ---

If asked "Walk me through the lifecycle of a trade": "It starts with the order decision — the PM decides to buy and the execution desk assesses market impact. For a large order, you'd use an algo — VWAP to match the volume profile, implementation shortfall to minimize cost from decision price, or a liquidity-seeking algo to reduce market impact in a thin name. The order gets routed to the best venue under Reg NMS — could be the lit exchange, a dark pool for large blocks, or matched by a wholesale market maker. Once executed, the trade goes to clearing. The CCP novates the trade, stepping between buyer and seller. Both sides post initial margin. Daily variation margin settles the P&L. The CCP is the critical infrastructure here — it eliminates bilateral counterparty risk but creates concentration risk in the CCP itself. Settlement is T+1 for US equities — actual delivery of shares and cash via DTC. Until settlement completes, there's settlement risk." If asked "How does margin create systemic risk?": "Margin is inherently pro-cyclical. In calm markets, exchanges set low margin requirements — which encourages leverage. In a vol spike, they raise margin requirements, demanding more cash precisely when positions are losing money and liquidity is drying up. This creates the margin cascade: price drops → margin call → forced selling → more price drops → more margin calls. The March 2020 Treasury basis trade is the perfect example. Hedge funds were leveraged 50-100x via repo, long cash Treasuries versus short futures. When markets panicked, margin requirements spiked, repo haircuts increased, and the supposedly safest trade in the world — long US government bonds — turned into forced liquidation. The Fed had to buy over a trillion dollars of Treasuries to stop the cascade. As an RM, I'd monitor margin utilization under STRESSED scenarios — not current utilization but projected utilization at VIX +20 — because by the time the crisis hits, it's too late to reduce." If asked "How do you think about liquidity risk?": "I think about liquidity in two dimensions that are connected in a doom loop. Market liquidity — can I sell at fair value? — measured by bid-ask spreads, depth, ADV, and market impact. Funding liquidity — can I access cash? — measured by margin availability, repo access, and credit lines. The danger is when both collapse simultaneously, which is exactly what happens in a crisis. For a discretionary fund, I'd tier every position by days-to-close. If more than 15% of the portfolio is in Tier 3 or worse — positions taking more than a week to exit — and we offer quarterly redemptions, we have a structural mismatch. The first investors to redeem get out at fair value, but the remaining investors bear the cost of the fire sale. This is what killed Woodford in 2019.
