I'd set hard limits on the illiquid bucket and stress test the exit timeline under 50% ADV reduction — because in a real crisis, volume doesn't just drop, it shifts to the most liquid name

At a systematic-at-its-core fund like Squarepoint, Greeks aren't abstract theory — they're the operating system. Every risk report, every morning call, every P&L decomposition, every limit breach speaks in Greeks. When a PM says "I'm long 500 gamma at the 6400 strike with $150K vega and bleeding $40K theta/day," you need to IMMEDIATELY understand: - The dollar P&L for a 2% move in either direction - How the profile changes if vol spikes 5 points - Whether the gamma-theta ratio is efficient or they're overpaying - Where the tail risk lives Greeks ARE the risk. Delta tells you WHERE you are. Gamma tells you WHERE YOU'LL BE. Vega tells you WHAT HAPPENS IF FEAR ARRIVES. Theta tells you THE DAILY COST. Learn them like your own reflexes. ---

> One-liner: Delta is how many dollars you make or lose for each $1 the underlying moves — it's your effective stock-equivalent position. > Analogy: Delta is a volume knob . It controls how loud the stock's moves play through to your P&L. Deep ITM (Δ=0.95) = cranked to 10, you hear everything — the option moves nearly dollar-for-dollar. ATM (Δ=0.50) = halfway, you catch half the signal. Deep OTM (Δ=0.05) = turned almost off, the stock is screaming but your option barely whispers. And the knob isn't fixed — gamma is the hand that keeps turning it.

The first derivative of option price with respect to the underlying: $$\Delta = \frac{\partial V}{\partial S}$$ Option Delta range ATM Deep ITM Deep OTM --- --- --- --- --- Call 0 to +1 ≈ +0.50 → +1.00 (behaves like stock) → 0 (nearly worthless) Put −1 to 0 ≈ −0.50 → −1.00 (behaves like short stock) → 0 (nearly worthless)

> The number that matters is dollar delta, not option delta. $$\text{Dollar delta} = \Delta \times \text{contracts} \times \text{multiplier} \times S$$ Worked example: You own 200 SPX calls, delta = 0.45, multiplier = 100, SPX = 5,400 - Dollar delta = 0.45 × 200 × 100 × $5,400 = $48.6M - That's your effective equity exposure — you're behaving as if you own $48.6M of SPX Portfolio delta = Σ of all position dollar deltas. A book with +$60M call delta and −$35M put delta = +$25M net delta (net long, gains if market rises).

Delta ≈ risk-neutral probability the option expires ITM. A 0.25 delta call ≈ 25% chance of finishing ITM. NOT a real-world probability (it's risk-neutral), but useful intuition for sizing and discussion.

> One-liner: Delta hedging removes directional risk, leaving your P&L driven by gamma, vega, and theta — the "pure Greeks" that options traders actually trade. Process: If you're long $25M dollar delta → short $25M of the underlying (sell stock or sell futures). Now you're "delta-neutral" — a small move up or down has near-zero P&L. But: - Your delta changes after the move (because of gamma) - You need to re-hedge → "dynamic hedging" - How often you re-hedge determines your realized gamma P&L vs theta cost Hedging frequency tradeoff: - Hedge every tick → perfectly capture gamma, but massive transaction costs (bid-ask × volume) - Hedge once a day → lower cost, but miss intraday moves and leave delta drift - Optimal frequency depends on: gamma size, spread costs, realized vol, how much variance you need to capture

Factor Effect on call delta Effect on put delta --- --- --- Spot rises Delta increases (→ 1) Delta increases (→ 0, less negative) Spot falls Delta decreases (→ 0) Delta decreases (→ −1, more negative) Vol rises OTM deltas increase, ITM deltas decrease (smiling toward 0.50) Same — deltas compress toward ±0.50 Vol falls OTM deltas decrease, ITM deltas increase (polarize toward 0 or 1) Same — deltas polarize Time passes OTM deltas decrease, ITM deltas increase (same polarization as lower vol) Same Key insight: High vol and long time to expiry make delta LESS extreme (everything gravitates toward 0.50). Low vol and near expiry make delta BINARY (near 0 or near 1). This is why near-expiry ATM options have explosive delta moves — they can swing from 0.50 to 0.95 in an hour. ---

section: PART 2: GAMMA (Γ) — THE MOST IMPORTANT GREEK FOR RISK
content: > One-liner: Gamma is how fast your delta changes — it's the acceleration of your P&L, and it determines whether big moves help or destroy you.

The second derivative of option price with respect to the underlying: $$\Gamma = \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2} = \frac{\partial \Delta}{\partial S}$$ Analogy — the airbag: Gamma is the airbag in your car. When you're long gamma , the airbag deploys on impact — it cushions you, absorbs the shock, and can even turn crashes into profit (P&L ∝ move²). When you're short gamma , the airbag has been ripped out — any collision accelerates the damage, and the harder you crash, the worse it gets. That's why gamma is THE 1 Greek for risk managers: it's the cushion that either protects you or exposes you. Second analogy — speed vs acceleration: Delta is your speed. Gamma is your acceleration. Long gamma = you accelerate in the right direction (speeding up when markets move). Short gamma = you accelerate in the WRONG direction (losses get faster and faster).

section: Dollar Gamma — The Number You Actually Use
content: > Dollar gamma = P&L for a 1% move in the underlying (attributed to gamma alone): $$\text{Dollar gamma P\&L} = \frac{1}{2} \times \Gamma \$ \times (\%\Delta S)^2$$ Where: $$\Gamma \$ = \Gamma \times S^2 \times \text{contracts} \times \text{multiplier} / 100$$ Worked example: You own 100 ATM SPX calls. - Γ (per option) = 0.0004, S = 5,400, multiplier = 100 - Dollar gamma = 0.0004 × 5,400² × 100 × 100 / 100 = $1,166,400 per 1% move - If SPX drops 3%: Gamma P&L = ½ × $1.17M × 3² = +$5.25M (long gamma → you gain from big moves, either direction) - If SPX drops 3% and you were SHORT those same options: −$5.25M (loss ACCELERATES) The asymmetry that kills: - Long gamma: P&L ∝ move² → ALWAYS positive for big moves regardless of direction. Small moves → small gains. Big moves → big gains. Convex. - Short gamma: P&L ∝ −move² → ALWAYS negative for big moves. Small moves → small losses (offset by theta). Big moves → catastrophic. Concave.

Factor Gamma effect Why --- --- --- ATM options Maximum gamma Delta is most uncertain (0.50). Tiny move determines ITM/OTM. Near expiry Very high gamma Less time → delta must resolve to 0 or 1 FAST. ATM + near expiry = explosive. Low vol Higher gamma (ATM) Lower vol concentrates the probability around current price → delta is more sensitive. OTM/ITM options Low gamma Delta is already near 0 or 1 — not much room to change. 0DTE (zero-days-to-expiry) gamma: SPX 0DTE options now account for ~50% of SPX options volume. Their gamma is ENORMOUS — delta can flip from 0.10 to 0.90 in 30 minutes. This creates intraday volatility that didn't exist before 2022. Dealers hedging 0DTE gamma create violent intraday swings.

section: Gamma Scalping — How You Monetize Long Gamma
content: Start: Delta-neutral, long gamma Market rises 1%: → Delta increases (long gamma → you're now net LONG) → SELL to re-hedge back to delta-neutral → Lock in profit on the move Market falls 1%: → Delta decreases (you're now net SHORT) → BUY to re-hedge back to delta-neutral → Lock in profit on the move Net result: BUY LOW, SELL HIGH automatically But: paying theta every day for this privilege Break-even realized vol: For a gamma scalper: $$\text{Daily gamma P\&L} = \frac{1}{2} \times \Gamma \$ \times (\text{daily realized return})^2$$ $$\text{Break-even: when } \sigma {\text{realized}} = \sigma {\text{implied}}$$ If you bought options at 20% implied vol, you need the market to actually realize 20% vol to break even. If it only realizes 15%, your theta costs exceed your gamma gains → net loss.

section: Pin Risk — Where Gamma Becomes Dangerous
content: > One-liner: Pin risk is the nightmare scenario where the underlying closes RIGHT at the strike on expiry day — you don't know if your options will be exercised or not. At expiry, an ATM option's gamma goes to infinity theoretically. Delta oscillates between 0 and 1 with tiny price moves. If you're short 10,000 ATM calls expiring today and the stock is at $100.01: - Are they exercised? → You're short 1M shares at the close - Stock drops to $99.99 → NOT exercised → You have zero position - The difference between $100.01 and $99.99 is $0.02 on the stock, but the difference in your position is $100M of risk This is why risk managers watch OpEx dates closely and require reduced positions into expiry. ---

section: PART 3: THETA (Θ) — THE COST OF OPTIONALITY
content: > One-liner: Theta is the daily rent you pay to hold an option — every day the clock ticks, your option loses time value. > Analogy: Theta is a parking meter running . Every day costs you coins, and you can't pause it. Far from expiry, the meter ticks slowly — a few cents a day. Near expiry, it's eating quarters every hour. The critical question: are you feeding the meter (long options — paying rent and hoping the move comes before time runs out) or do you OWN the meter (short options — collecting coins from everyone else's ticking clock)?

The first derivative of option price with respect to time: $$\Theta = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$ - Long options: Theta is NEGATIVE. You PAY rent. The option decays. - Short options: Theta is POSITIVE. You EARN rent. Time decay works for you.

$$\text{Daily theta} = \Theta \times \text{contracts} \times \text{multiplier}$$ Worked example: You own 150 SPX puts with θ = −$3.20/day per option, multiplier = 100. - Daily theta cost = −$3.20 × 150 × 100 = −$48,000/day - Monthly cost = −$1.04M (22 trading days) - Your puts need to gain $1M+ from delta/gamma/vega just to break even each month

$$\Theta \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$$ Days to expiry Relative daily decay Behavior --- --- --- 90 days 1x (slow) Gentle bleed — barely noticeable day-to-day 30 days 1.7x Accelerating — weekly check-ins matter 7 days 3.6x Fast — meaningful daily P&L from time alone 1 day 9.5x Extreme — ATM options can lose 30-50% of remaining value in the last day The square root rule: An option with 64 days has roughly double the time value of an option with 16 days (√64/√16 = 2). Not 4x, even though it has 4x the time.

Options lose value over weekends and holidays (2-3 calendar days of theta for a 1-day hold over weekend). This means: - Friday close: Long options lose extra theta. Short option sellers receive it. - Holiday weeks: 3-day weekends → accelerated decay. PMs who are long gamma sometimes reduce before long weekends to avoid paying 3 days of theta for 1 day of possible movement. - IV adjustment: Some of this is priced in — Friday IV may be slightly lower than Monday IV, meaning the market partially prices the weekend decay into vol.

section: The Gamma-Theta Tradeoff — THE Fundamental Relationship
content: > There is NO free lunch. You CANNOT have positive gamma AND positive theta simultaneously with vanilla options. Profile Gamma Theta P&L shape Who does this --- --- --- --- --- Long gamma, short theta + − Many small losses, occasional big wins Vol buyers, hedgers, gamma scalpers Short gamma, long theta − + Many small wins, occasional BIG losses Market makers, premium sellers, income strategies The efficiency metric — Gamma/Theta ratio: $$\text{Gamma/Theta} = \frac{\Gamma \$}{\Theta \$}$$ - High ratio: You're getting a lot of gamma per dollar of theta paid. Good deal — cheap optionality. - Low ratio: You're overpaying for gamma. Time decay will eat you alive before you can scalp enough. When is gamma cheap? Low implied vol (market complacent). When is it expensive? High implied vol (post-crash, panic). The paradox: gamma is cheapest when you don't feel like you need it and most expensive when you're desperate for it. > Interview line: "Long gamma means your delta automatically adjusts in your favor — you're protected against big moves — but you pay theta every day for that protection. Short gamma is the opposite: you earn rent daily but you're writing insurance against tail events. The RM's job is to make sure the PM is aware of which regime they're in and whether the gamma/theta tradeoff is efficient — are we paying a fair price for the optionality, or are we overpaying because vol is expensive?" ---

section: PART 4: VEGA (ν) — EXPOSURE TO VOLATILITY ITSELF
content: > One-liner: Vega is how much your option's price changes when implied vol moves by one point — it's your bet on the market's fear level. > Analogy: Vega is your bet on the weather forecast, not the weather itself . You don't need the storm to actually hit — you just need the forecast to get scarier (IV up) and your options gain value. Long vega profits from increasing uncertainty , not from the event itself. Short vega is betting the forecast stays calm. That's why you can buy straddles before CPI and LOSE even if CPI moves the market — if the forecast (IV) was already pricing a hurricane and the actual storm was a drizzle, the forecast improves (IV crushes) and you're underwater.

$$\nu = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \sigma}$$ - Long options (bought): Positive vega. Vol up → you gain. Vol down → you lose. - Short options (sold): Negative vega. Vol up → you lose. Vol down → you gain.

$$\text{Dollar vega} = \nu \times \text{contracts} \times \text{multiplier}$$ Worked example: Portfolio has $300K vega (sum of all positions). - Vol rises 3 points: P&L = +$300K × 3 = +$900K - Vol drops 2 points: P&L = −$300K × 2 = −$600K - VIX spike from 15 to 40 (25 pts): P&L = +$300K × 25 = +$7.5M (if you're long vega, this is your payday)

Factor Vega effect Why --- --- --- ATM options Maximum vega Highest uncertainty → most sensitive to vol changes Long-dated options Much higher vega More time for vol to compound → vol matters more Near-expiry options Very low vega So little time left, vol changes barely matter OTM/ITM options Lower vega Less uncertainty about ITM/OTM outcome Key relationship: Vega ∝ √T. A 1-year option has ~2x the vega of a 3-month option (√12/√3 ≈ 2). This means: - Long-dated options are your main vega exposure - Near-dated options contribute gamma but little vega - A portfolio with long near-dated + short far-dated can be long gamma but short vega → different risk profiles at different horizons

section: Vega Bucketing — What Sophisticated Desks Do
content: > At Squarepoint's level, "total vega" is not enough. You bucket vega by tenor: Bucket What's in it What drives it --- --- --- 0-30 day vega Near-dated options Event risk, realized vol, gamma scalping P&L 30-90 day vega Medium-dated Earnings cycles, vol term structure rolls 90-365 day vega Far-dated Structural vol regime, macro uncertainty 1Y+ vega LEAPS, long-dated structures Monetary policy regime, secular vol trends Why it matters: You can be net FLAT total vega but LONG 1-month vega and SHORT 6-month vega. That's a trade on the vol term structure — you're betting near-term vol is cheap relative to far-term vol. Very different risk from being outright long or short vol.

section: Vol-Spot Correlation — Why Vega and Delta Compound in Crashes
content: > One-liner: Vol spikes when markets drop. If you're short vega AND long delta, a sell-off hits you on BOTH dimensions simultaneously. Empirically: - SPX drops 1% → VIX rises ~2-4 points (on average) - SPX drops 5% → VIX rises ~15-25 points - This is NOT symmetric: SPX rallies 5% → VIX drops ~5-8 points Consequence: Short vega is correlated with long delta. Both hurt in a sell-off. A portfolio that's "+$20M delta, −$300K vega" has compounding downside: the 5% drop costs you $1M on delta, and the associated 20-point vol spike costs you $6M on vega. Total loss = $7M, not $1M. ---

> One-liner: Rho measures how much an option's price changes for a 1% shift in rates — usually minor for short-dated equity options, critical for rates derivatives and LEAPS. > Analogy: Rho is the current in a river . On a short kayak trip (weekly options), you barely notice it — paddle a few strokes and you're done. On a multi-day expedition (LEAPS, swaptions), that same current carries you miles off course. In the zero-rate era (2010–2021), the river was a dead canal — rho was irrelevant. At 4%+ rates (2023–2026), there's a real current, and if you're floating long-dated positions, you'd better know which way it's pulling. $$\rho = \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}$$ Option Rho sign Why When it matters --- --- --- --- Long call Positive Higher rates → higher forward → calls worth more LEAPS, FX options, swaptions Long put Negative Higher rates → higher forward → puts worth less LEAPS, FX options Short tenors Negligible Rate change × short time = tiny impact Not a concern for weekly/monthly options Current environment (2026): With rates at 4%+, rho is more material than during the zero-rate era. A 1-year LEAPS call with $10K rho loses $10K if rates fall 1% — not negligible for large positions. ---

section: PART 6: GREEKS BY POSITION TYPE — THE REFERENCE TABLE
content: Know this cold. For EVERY option position, know every Greek sign and behavior: Greek Long Call Short Call Long Put Short Put --- --- --- --- --- Delta (Δ) + (0 to +1) − (0 to −1) − (−1 to 0) + (0 to +1) Gamma (Γ) + (always) − (always) + (always) − (always) Theta (Θ) − (pay rent) + (earn rent) − (pay rent) + (earn rent) Vega (ν) + (want vol up) − (want vol down) + (want vol up) − (want vol down) Rho (ρ) + (rates up helps) − (rates up hurts) − (rates up hurts) + (rates up helps) The universal pattern: - LONG any option → always +Γ, +ν, −Θ - SHORT any option → always −Γ, −ν, +Θ - The ONLY difference between calls and puts: sign of delta and rho

section: What Each Position Is Really Saying
content: Position Translation --- --- Long Call "I'm bullish. I want a BIG move UP, FAST (before theta eats me). Vol spike also helps." Short Call "I'm bearish/neutral. I earn theta. Calm markets are my friend. Exposed to upside breakouts." Long Put "I'm bearish or hedging. I want a BIG move DOWN. Vol spike helps. Paying daily rent for crash protection." Short Put "I'm neutral/bullish. I earn theta. Selling insurance against a crash — if it happens, I'm on the hook."

Position Small move (+/−1%) Big move (+5%) Big move (−5%) Time (no move) Vol spike (+10pts) --- --- --- --- --- --- Long Call Small Δ gain/loss Big gain (convex) Loss capped at premium Lose (Θ) Gain (ν) Short Call Small Δ gain/loss Big loss (unlimited) Gain capped at premium Gain (Θ) Lose (ν) Long Put Small Δ gain/loss Loss capped at premium Big gain (convex) Lose (Θ) Gain (ν) Short Put Small Δ gain/loss Gain capped at premium Big loss (to K) Gain (Θ) Lose (ν) ---

section: PART 7: SECOND-ORDER GREEKS — THE DERIVATIVES OF DERIVATIVES
content: > These measure how first-order Greeks change. You won't price with these, but you need to know what they do because they explain why your risk SHIFTS in a crisis.

section: Vanna — How Delta Changes with Vol
content: > One-liner: Vanna is how your directional exposure shape-shifts when fear arrives — it's why your hedges get stronger (or weaker) exactly when it matters most. $$\text{Vanna} = \frac{\partial \Delta}{\partial \sigma} = \frac{\partial \nu}{\partial S}$$ In plain English: When vol spikes, how does your delta move? Why it matters in a crisis: - You hold OTM puts as hedges (delta = −0.20). Vol spikes 15 points → vanna causes delta to shift toward −0.55. Your puts now hedge 2.5x more of your portfolio. - This is GOOD — it's built-in tail protection. Your hedges become more effective exactly when you need them. - But if you're long OTM calls, a vol spike pushes their delta TOWARD 0.50 — your calls become "less OTM" in delta terms, even if the stock hasn't moved. Vanna flows (dealer impact): Dealers short OTM puts → they have negative vanna exposure. Vol spikes → their delta shifts (they become shorter delta) → they need to BUY to re-hedge → supportive for the market. Vol drops → their delta shifts the other way → they SELL → adds selling pressure. These systematic "vanna flows" are a major driver of daily market mechanics.

section: Volga (Vomma) — How Vega Changes with Vol
content: > One-liner: Volga is the convexity of your vol bet — it tells you whether a vol spike makes things progressively better or progressively worse for your book. $$\text{Volga} = \frac{\partial \nu}{\partial \sigma} = \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \sigma^2}$$ In plain English: When vol goes up, does your vega increase or decrease? Position Volga What it means --- --- --- OTM options (wings) Positive As vol rises, your vega INCREASES → you become MORE long vol in a crisis. Convexity of vol. Tail protection. ATM options Near zero Vega doesn't change much with vol. Linear vol exposure. For the RM: A portfolio that's long volga has built-in vol tail protection — it becomes increasingly hedged as vol rises. Short volga is the opposite: your vega exposure grows against you as conditions deteriorate. This is why selling OTM puts (wings) is particularly dangerous — you're short volga, so as vol spikes, your vega loss ACCELERATES.

section: Charm (Delta Bleed) — How Delta Changes with Time
content: > One-liner: Charm is the silent thief — it steals your delta hedge overnight, so you wake up unhedged even though nothing moved. $$\text{Charm} = \frac{\partial \Delta}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial \Theta}{\partial S}$$ In plain English: How much does your delta drift overnight just from one day passing? Why it matters: - A PM delta-hedges perfectly on Friday. Over the weekend, 2 days pass. Charm shifts delta. Monday morning, they're no longer hedged — even though the stock opened unchanged. - Near-expiry OTM options: charm pulls delta toward 0 (losing delta). Near-expiry ITM options: charm pushes delta toward ±1. This delta drift is systematic and needs to be accounted for. - 0DTE positions: Charm is enormous. Delta changes continuously just from the clock ticking.

section: Speed — How Gamma Changes with Spot
content: > One-liner: Speed tells you whether your gamma itself is stable or about to shift — it's the gamma of gamma. $$\text{Speed} = \frac{\partial \Gamma}{\partial S}$$ Tells you: if the market moves, does your gamma exposure increase or decrease? Important for: - Barrier options (gamma can jump discontinuously near the barrier) - Large gamma positions where the gamma itself is unstable - Understanding whether your risk is linear in gamma or gamma itself is changing

section: Color — How Gamma Changes with Time
content: > One-liner: Color is gamma's clock — it measures how fast your gamma concentrates as expiry approaches. $$\text{Color} = \frac{\partial \Gamma}{\partial t}$$ Gamma increases as options approach expiry (for ATM options). Color tells you the rate. Important for understanding how your gamma exposure evolves day-to-day, especially in the final week.

Greek What it measures Key insight --- --- --- Vanna ∂Δ/∂σ How your delta shifts when vol moves. Drives dealer hedging flows. Volga ∂ν/∂σ How your vega changes when vol moves. Long volga = tail protection. Charm ∂Δ/∂t How delta drifts overnight. Critical near expiry and over weekends. Speed ∂Γ/∂S How gamma changes with spot. Important near barriers. Color ∂Γ/∂t How gamma evolves with time. Gamma spikes into expiry. ---
All Greeks are additive across positions: $$\text{Portfolio } \Delta = \sum {i} \Delta i \times n i \times m i$$ Same for Γ, Θ, ν. Sum them across every position to get the book-level Greeks.

section: The Greeks Dashboard — What You Monitor Daily
content: Greek What you're checking How it's reported Red flag --- --- --- --- Net delta ($) Directional exposure $M equivalent Exceeds mandate/risk budget (e.g., ±$30M limit) Dollar gamma (1%) P&L for a 1% move (from gamma alone) $K or $M per 1% Large short gamma near current spot → danger zone Theta ($/day) Daily time decay cost or income $/day Theta cost exceeding realistic gamma capture expectations Vega ($/vol pt) Exposure to IV change $K per 1 vol point Large short vega in low-vol environment (complacency trap) Vega by tenor Bucketed: 0-30d, 30-90d, 90d+ $K per bucket Mismatches between tenor buckets (long front, short back = vol curve bet) Gamma/theta Efficiency of optionality spend Ratio Low ratio = overpaying for gamma. PM bleeding unnecessarily. Vanna exposure Delta sensitivity to vol $M delta shift per vol pt Large vanna → delta will shift violently in a vol spike. Need to anticipate.

section: Greek Limits — How an RM Sets Them
content: At a fund like Squarepoint, each PM desk has Greek limits: Limit Typical constraint Why --- --- --- Delta limit ±$50M per desk Caps directional exposure. Prevents a single PM from betting the fund. Gamma limit Short gamma < $X per 1% Caps convexity risk. Limits how much losses can accelerate. Vega limit ±$Y per vol point Caps vol exposure. Prevents one vol spike from wiping a desk. Theta limit −$Z/day max spend Prevents excessive bleed from buying overpriced optionality. Concentration limit Max % of Greeks from a single name/strike Prevents pin risk and single-name gamma concentration. Expiry limit Max gamma exposure within 5 days of expiry Controls pin risk and explosive near-expiry gamma. The aggregation challenge: PM A is long gamma, PM B is short gamma. At the PORTFOLIO level, they may offset. But that offset only works if both positions are on the SAME underlying at the SAME strike. A systematic RM system needs to calculate Greeks at the portfolio level AND flag concentrated risks at the position level.

section: Long Gamma vs Short Gamma — The Risk Manager's Decision Framework
content: Dimension Long Gamma Book Short Gamma Book --- --- --- Daily P&L Volatile — swings with realized vol Smooth — steady theta income Tail risk Protected — gains from big moves Exposed — large moves cause accelerating losses P&L distribution Negative skew: many small losses, occasional big wins Positive skew: many small wins, occasional BIG losses Regime preference High realized vol Low realized vol What can kill it Prolonged low vol → theta bleed exhausts capital Single large move → losses exceed all prior theta income RM monitoring Is theta cost sustainable? How long can we bleed? What's worst-case on a 3σ/5σ day? Can the fund survive it? ---

section: Scenario 1: Market Drops 5%, Vol Spikes 12 Points
content: Portfolio: - Net delta: +$30M (long equities) - Dollar gamma: −$800K per 1% (short gamma — sold puts for income) - Vega: −$250K per vol point (short vega from the same sold puts) - Theta: +$45K/day (earning from the short premium) Decomposition: Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- Delta +$30M × −5% −$1,500,000 Gamma ½ × (−$800K) × 5² −$10,000,000 Vega −$250K × 12 pts −$3,000,000 Theta +$45K × 1 day +$45,000 TOTAL −$14,455,000 The PM thought: "I'm only $30M long delta. A 5% drop costs me $1.5M. Manageable." Reality: Short gamma turned a $1.5M delta loss into $11.5M. Short vega added another $3M. Total: $14.5M — nearly 10x the naïve delta-only estimate. What the RM should have flagged BEFORE: - "Your gamma profile means a 5% move costs $10M+ beyond your delta loss" - "Your vega is correlated with your delta — a sell-off creates compounding losses" - "Your P&L profile is CONCAVE — you lose more than linearly in tail events" - "Stress scenario: SPX −5%, VIX +12 → $14.5M loss. Is that within your book's risk budget?"

section: Scenario 2: Market Grinds Sideways for 30 Days
content: Same portfolio as above: Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- Delta SPX ±0% $0 Gamma No moves → no gamma P&L $0 Vega Vol drops 2 pts (complacency) +$500,000 Theta +$45K × 22 trading days +$990,000 TOTAL +$1,490,000 This is the seduction of short gamma. In calm months, you earn $1.5M for "doing nothing." It FEELS like free money. But one bad month (Scenario 1) takes away 10 months of income. This is the classic premium-seller distribution — steady, steady, steady, CRASH.

section: Scenario 3: The Vol Crush After an Event
content: PM bought straddles ahead of CPI: - Gamma: +$500K per 1% - Vega: +$400K per vol point - Theta: −$80K/day CPI comes in line with expectations. Market moves 0.3%. Vol drops 4 points. Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- Delta Straddle is delta-neutral → $0 $0 Gamma ½ × $500K × 0.3² = tiny +$225 Vega +$400K × (−4) −$1,600,000 Theta −$80K × 1 day −$80,000 TOTAL −$1,679,775 The PM was right about CPI creating a move — but the move was too small to cover the vol crush. The pre-event vol premium collapsed, and the gamma gain on a 0.3% move was negligible. This is the "vol event" trap: you need the event to produce a move large enough to overcome the IV crush. If IV was 25% pre-CPI and realized vol on the day was 10%, you overpaid. ---

section: PART 10: THE GREEKS SURFACE — HOW GREEKS CHANGE ACROSS STRIKE AND EXPIRY
content: Understanding WHERE Greeks are highest helps with position construction: GAMMA SURFACE Short expiry ┃ Long expiry ┃ OTM ▪ ┃ · OTM ▪▪ ┃ ·· ATM ▪▪▪▪▪▪ ┃ ··· ATM ← Gamma peaks ATM, spikes near expiry ▪▪ ┃ ·· OTM ▪ ┃ · OTM ┃ CONCENTRATED SPREAD OUT (explosive) (gentle) VEGA SURFACE Short expiry ┃ Long expiry ┃ OTM · ┃ ▪ OTM · ┃ ▪▪ ATM ·· ┃ ▪▪▪▪▪▪ ATM ← Vega peaks ATM, grows with time · ┃ ▪▪ OTM · ┃ ▪ OTM ┃ LOW VEGA HIGH VEGA (time too short) (vol compounds) The key insight for position construction: - Want gamma (movement exposure)? → Buy near-dated ATM - Want vega (vol exposure)? → Buy far-dated ATM - Want gamma but NOT vega? → Buy near-dated, hedge vega by selling far-dated (calendar spread) - Want vega but NOT gamma? → Buy far-dated OTM wings (low gamma, meaningful vega + volga) ---

If asked "Explain gamma and why it matters for risk management": "Gamma measures the rate of change of delta — it's the acceleration of P&L. If you're long gamma, big moves help you: your delta automatically adjusts in your favor, and you profit proportionally to the SQUARE of the move. Short gamma is the opposite: losses accelerate as markets move against you. For risk management at a systematic-leaning fund, gamma is more important than delta because delta tells you where you are NOW but gamma tells you where you'll be AFTER a move. A desk that's earning $45K/day in theta from sold premium looks great on a calm day — but if they have $800K of short gamma per 1% and the market drops 5%, that theta income is wiped out by a $10M gamma loss in a single session. My job is to quantify that BEFORE it happens — stress the portfolio under 3σ and 5σ moves and make sure the tail loss is within the desk's risk budget." If asked "Walk me through the gamma-theta tradeoff": "There's no free lunch. Long gamma means you profit from realized volatility — your position naturally buys low and sells high through dynamic hedging — but you pay theta every day for that privilege. The break-even point is when realized vol equals implied vol. If you buy options at 20% IV and the market only realizes 15%, you bleed. Short gamma is the mirror: you earn theta daily, but you're writing insurance against tail events and your losses go as the square of the move. At the portfolio level, I'd track gamma/theta ratio as an efficiency metric. If a PM is paying $80K/day in theta but only has $300K/1% gamma, their break-even daily move is large — they need big, frequent moves to justify the cost. Conversely, a PM earning $45K/day in theta with $800K/1% short gamma has a Sharpe-killer hiding in the tail. I'd run the historical distribution of daily moves and show them: 'On 95% of days, you earn. On the other 5%, you lose everything from the past 3 months.'" If asked "What are second-order Greeks and why should I care?": "Second-order Greeks measure how first-order Greeks change — and in a crisis, that's what determines whether your hedges hold. Vanna tells you how delta shifts when vol moves. In a sell-off, vol spikes and vanna causes your put hedge deltas to become MORE negative — so your hedges automatically become more effective. That's a feature. Volga tells you how vega changes with vol — if you're short OTM puts and vol spikes, volga makes your vega loss ACCELERATE, compounding the damage. At the portfolio level, these second-order effects also drive systematic flows. Dealers who are short OTM puts have negative vanna exposure — when vol drops, their delta shifts and they sell, adding to downward pressure. When vol spikes, they buy, adding support. These 'vanna flows' move billions of dollars daily and are one reason markets behave differently around option-heavy environments like monthly expiry or around the gamma flip level." --- Related files: → B Derivatives — the instrumen

section: WHY THIS IS CORE AT SQUAREPOINT
content: At a systematic-at-its-core fund running discretionary strategies, volatility isn't just a parameter in a model — it's a tradeable asset, a risk indicator, and a regime signal all at once. Every PM's book has implicit or explicit vol exposure. Every straddle, every protective put, every variance swap, every gamma scalp is a vol position. The risk manager's job is to: 1. Decompose — what vol level, surface shape, and dynamics are priced into the current book? 2. Monitor — is the implied-realized spread compressing or expanding? Is skew telling us something? 3. Anticipate — what happens to the portfolio if we shift from a 15-vol regime to a 35-vol regime overnight? 4. Challenge — is the PM paying too much for vol? Selling it too cheaply? Ignoring the term structure? Vol is the connective tissue between Greeks, P&L attribution, hedging, and regime analysis. If you don't understand vol deeply, you can't do the job. ---

> One-liner: Realized vol measures how much the asset ACTUALLY moved — the backward-looking truth. The annualized standard deviation of log returns over a lookback window: $$\sigma {\text{realized}} = \sqrt{\frac{252}{n-1} \sum {i=1}^{n} (r i - \bar{r})^2}$$ Where $r i = \ln(S i / S {i-1})$ and 252 = trading days per year. Worked example: SPX daily returns over 10 days: +0.8%, −1.2%, +0.3%, −0.5%, +1.5%, −2.0%, +0.4%, +0.1%, −0.7%, +0.6% - Standard deviation of these returns ≈ 1.01% - Annualized: 1.01% × √252 ≈ 16.0%

Window What it captures Use case --- --- --- 5-day Very recent micro-regime. Noisy. Intraday/gamma scalping reference 10-day Short-term regime. Responsive to events. Near-term vol comparison vs 10d IV 20-day (~1 month) Standard "recent" realized vol Main comparison vs 30-day IV 60-day (~3 months) Medium-term trend Structural regime assessment 252-day (1 year) Long-term baseline Vol regime classification (historically low/high?) The divergence problem: In January 2018, 252-day realized vol was ~7% (historic lows). 10-day realized was ~4%. VIX was 11. Then Feb 5 hit — VIX tripled in one day. ALL lookback windows were backward-looking by definition. None warned you.

Estimator Formula concept Why it exists --- --- --- Close-to-close Standard deviation of log returns Simple, standard. Misses intraday. Parkinson (High-Low) Uses daily range (H−L) ~5x more efficient than close-to-close. Captures intraday moves. Garman-Klass Uses O, H, L, C Most efficient unbiased estimator for continuous processes. Yang-Zhang O, H, L, C + overnight jumps Handles overnight gaps AND intraday — best all-around for equities. Realized variance (tick) Sum of squared intraday returns Highest frequency. Used in academic research. Microstructure noise issues. At Squarepoint's level: You don't just compute "realized vol" — you choose the estimator that matches the question. Close-to-close for comparison with BS-implied vol (which is a close-to-close concept). Parkinson for true intraday activity. Yang-Zhang if overnight gaps matter (single stocks, EM).

> One-liner: Implied vol is the market's consensus forecast of future volatility — backed out of option prices. It's the weather forecast, not the weather. You observe an option's market price. You have all Black-Scholes inputs except σ. Solve for σ: $$C {\text{market}} = \text{BS}(S, K, T, r, q, \sigma {\text{implied}})$$ Newton-Raphson or bisection → you get σ implied. This single number tells you what vol the market is collectively pricing. Critical distinction: - Implied vol is FORWARD-LOOKING. It reflects expectations + risk premium for the FUTURE period until expiry. - Realized vol is BACKWARD-LOOKING. It tells you what ALREADY happened. - They answer different questions. Comparing them tells you whether options are "cheap" or "expensive."

Implied vol contains TWO components: 1. Expected future realized vol — the market's best guess of actual movements 2. Volatility risk premium (VRP) — the extra charge for bearing vol risk (insurance premium) $$\sigma {\text{implied}} = \sigma {\text{expected realized}} + \text{VRP}$$ You CANNOT assume IV = expected vol. The VRP means options are systematically "overpriced" relative to what actually realizes. This is a feature, not a bug — it compensates sellers for bearing tail risk.

> One-liner: Implied vol is almost always higher than subsequent realized vol. The difference is the insurance premium — and it's what makes selling vol profitable on average, and catastrophic in the tail. Empirical facts for SPX: - Long-run average VRP: ~2-4 vol points (implied > realized) - VRP is present ~85% of months - VRP is LARGER in low-vol regimes (complacency → sellers tolerate less premium) - VRP collapses or INVERTS in crises (realized > implied → options were too cheap → sellers get destroyed) Worked example — the VRP trade: - Sell 1-month ATM SPX straddle at 18% IV, delta-hedge daily - Over the month, SPX realizes 14% vol - Your P&L ≈ vega × (IV − RV) ≈ $300K × 4 = +$1.2M (simplified) - You "harvested" the VRP When it inverts (the blowup): - February 5, 2018 ("Volmageddon"): VIX went from 17 to 50 in one day. XIV ETN lost 96% of value OVERNIGHT and was terminated. Realized vol hit ~100% annualized for that day. Anyone short vol at 17 IV ate a 5σ+ loss. - March 2020: VIX from 14 to 82. Realized vol hit 130%+ (annualized). Short vol positions destroyed across the industry. - The VRP trade is picking up pennies in front of a steamroller — positive expected value on most days, existential risk on the worst day.

Asset Typical VRP (IV − RV) Notes --- --- --- SPX options +2 to +4 pts Most studied. Reliable positive VRP. Single stocks +3 to +8 pts Higher VRP (more demand for hedging + earnings events) FX options +1 to +2 pts Smaller — FX vol is lower overall Commodity options Varies widely Oil: large VRP. Gold: smaller. Seasonals distort. VIX options Large positive VIX options on VIX futures — double layer of premium ---

Black-Scholes assumes ONE constant σ for all strikes and maturities. Reality: implied vol varies by strike (skew/smile) and expiry (term structure), creating a 2D surface. THE VOL SURFACE IV ← OTM Puts (high IV) │ │ ╲ ╱ ← OTM Calls (smile in some assets) │ ╲ ╱ │ ╲ ╱ │ ╲──────────────╱ ← ATM (lowest for equities) │ └──────────────────────────── Strike (K) 80% 90% 100% 110% 120% For EQUITIES: "smirk" — left side elevated, right side modestly lower or flat For FX/commodities: "smile" — both wings elevated, more symmetric
> One-liner: Skew measures how much more expensive downside protection is relative to ATM options — it's the market's fear of crashes priced in real-time.

section: Why Equity Skew Exists (Three Reinforcing Reasons)
content: 1. Demand/supply imbalance: The entire institutional world buys puts (pension funds, endowments, insurance companies). Nobody systematically buys OTM calls at scale. This demand asymmetry inflates put IV. 2. Fat-tailed returns: Equity returns are negatively skewed with excess kurtosis. The probability of SPX −20% in a month is MUCH higher than a normal distribution predicts. Options need to be priced for real-world tail risk, not Gaussian tails. Post-1987 crash, the market permanently repriced this — skew didn't exist before Black Monday. 3. Leverage effect (Black, 1976): When stock prices fall, the debt/equity ratio rises mechanically → the firm becomes riskier → vol should be higher at lower prices. Self-reinforcing feedback loop.

section: Measuring Skew — The Numbers You'll Use
content: Metric Definition Typical SPX value What it tells you --- --- --- --- 25Δ risk reversal (RR) IV(25Δ put) − IV(25Δ call) −5 to −8 pts (negative = put premium) Direction of skew. More negative = steeper. 25Δ butterfly (BF) ½ × IV(25Δ put) + IV(25Δ call) − IV(ATM) +2 to +5 pts (positive = wings expensive) Curvature / kurtosis pricing. Higher = fatter tails priced. 90-100 skew IV(90% moneyness) − IV(ATM) +4 to +8 pts How much more puts cost than ATM. Simple hedge cost indicator. Skew index (CBOE SKEW) Derived from SPX OTM option prices 100-150 (higher = more tail risk priced) Aggregate tail risk indicator.

Market condition Skew behavior Why --- --- --- Sell-off (SPX −3%+) Skew steepens sharply Put demand surges, call IV compressed. Fear spike. Grinding rally Skew flattens Put demand fades, upside calls get bid. Complacency. Vol spike (VIX +10) Skew can flatten RELATIVELY When ATM vol explodes, relative skew may compress even as absolute put IV rises Pre-event (FOMC/CPI) Short-dated skew steepens Hedging demand concentrates in near-dated puts Post-event resolution Skew normalizes rapidly Uncertainty resolved → hedging demand drops Sticky-strike vs sticky-delta: - Sticky-strike: Implied vol at a given strike stays fixed as spot moves. Implies: if spot drops 5%, ATM vol rises (because you're now ATM at a lower strike which had higher vol). This is the empirical behavior for equities — vol rises when spot drops. - Sticky-delta: The vol at a given delta stays fixed. ATM vol doesn't change when spot moves. Less realistic for equities, more applicable to FX in calm markets. - Why it matters: The assumption determines how you delta-hedge. Sticky-strike delta-hedging uses a different delta than sticky-delta — the difference can be 0.02-0.05 for OTM options, which on 10,000 contracts is material.

> One-liner: The vol term structure tells you whether the market expects current vol conditions to persist or mean-revert. Shape Name What it means When you see it --- --- --- --- Upward sloping Contango Longer = higher IV. Normal state. Calm markets — uncertainty increases with horizon. Flat Flat Vol expectations similar across tenors. Transition periods. Inverted Backwardation Short-dated IV > long-dated. Near-term fear. Event risk (earnings, election, crisis). Market expects the event to resolve and vol to drop. Humped Humped Peak at intermediate tenor (e.g., 3-month). Specific event at a known future date (e.g., 3 months out = election). Worked example — VIX term structure in crisis: - Normal: VIX = 15, 2-month VIX futures = 17, 4-month = 18 (contango) - Crisis: VIX = 45, 2-month = 35, 4-month = 28 (steep backwardation — market expects vol to normalize) - The SLOPE encodes mean-reversion expectations. Steep backwardation = "this crisis is temporary."

section: Term Structure as a Trading Signal
content: Signal Interpretation Trade --- --- --- Steep contango Short vol is expensive short-dated, cheap long-dated Calendar spreads: sell near-dated vol, buy far-dated Steep backwardation Near-term panic. Vol likely to mean-revert. Sell near-dated vol (IF you can survive the mark-to-market) Contango flattening Front vol catching up — early warning of trouble Reduce short vol. Add near-dated protection.

section: The Full Surface — Strike × Time Together
content: Every point on the vol surface $\sigma(K, T)$ is an implied vol. The surface must satisfy no-arbitrage conditions: 1. Calendar spread arbitrage: Total implied variance must be non-decreasing in time: $\sigma^2(K, T 2) \times T 2 \geq \sigma^2(K, T 1) \times T 1$ for $T 2 > T 1$ 2. Butterfly arbitrage: The risk-neutral density must be non-negative everywhere → constrains how fast vol can change across strikes 3. Put-call parity: Calls and puts at same strike/expiry must imply the same vol (otherwise direct arbitrage) Surface interpolation matters: Between observed strikes and expiries, how do you interpolate? SVI (Stochastic Volatility Inspired) parameterization is standard for equities. SABR for rates/FX. Getting this wrong → model produces arbitrageable prices → your Greeks are wrong → your hedges leak. ---

section: PART 3: VOLATILITY MODELS — HOW YOU GENERATE THE SURFACE
content: > At Squarepoint, you won't run these models yourself as a risk manager. But you need to understand what the quants are doing, why the model matters, and where it breaks.

Model class Key idea Strengths Weaknesses Used for --- --- --- --- --- Black-Scholes Constant vol Simple, closed-form Greeks Wrong (vol isn't constant) Quick pricing, reference point Local vol (Dupire) σ = σ(S,t), deterministic Fits any surface exactly. Unique. Wrong dynamics — forward skew too flat. Vol moves wrong with spot. Exotic pricing with surface-consistent calibration. Stochastic vol (Heston) Vol follows its own stochastic process Better dynamics. Mean-reverting vol. Hard to calibrate. Can't fit short-dated skew well. Exotics. Multi-asset. Research. SABR Stochastic vol, analytical approx. Fast, closed-form. Industry standard for rates/FX. Single-expiry. Wing issues. No jumps. Swaptions, FX vanillas, caps/floors. Local-stochastic vol (LSV) Combines local + stochastic. Lever function. Best of both: fits surface AND has good dynamics. Complex. Calibration is expensive. Front-office exotic pricing at top banks. Jump-diffusion (Merton) Adds Poisson jumps to GBM Captures overnight gaps. Hard to hedge jumps. Incomplete market. Equity options around events (earnings, M&A). Rough vol Vol is driven by fractional Brownian motion (H ≈ 0.1) Matches empirical vol dynamics perfectly. Fast timescale clustering. Computationally expensive. New. Research frontier. Not yet standard in production.

section: SABR — In Depth (Because You Used It at Natixis)
content: $$dF = \alpha F^\beta dW 1$$ $$d\alpha = \nu \alpha dW 2$$ $$dW 1 dW 2 = \rho \, dt$$ Parameter What it controls Calibration intuition --- --- --- α (alpha) ATM vol level Set from ATM market vol β (beta) Backbone — how vol moves with spot. β=0: normal model. β=1: lognormal. β=0.5: CIR-like. Usually FIXED (not calibrated). Equities: β≈0.5. Rates: β=0 or 0.5. ρ (rho) Spot-vol correlation → controls SKEW Negative ρ → downside skew (equities: ρ ≈ −0.3 to −0.7) ν (nu) Vol of vol → controls CURVATURE/WINGS Higher ν → steeper smile/fatter tails Hagan approximation provides closed-form σ implied(K) given (α, β, ρ, ν, F, K, T). Makes calibration fast — Newton-Raphson on 3 parameters (α, ρ, ν with β fixed) per expiry slice. Limitations you should be able to discuss: - Arbitrage in the wings: for extreme strikes, the Hagan formula can produce negative probability densities. Various fixes exist (Oblój correction, full numerical PDE). - No term structure consistency: calibrated independently per expiry, so no guarantee that calendar spread arbitrage is excluded. - No jumps: cannot capture overnight gaps, earnings moves, flash crashes.

$$\sigma {\text{local}}^2(K,T) = \frac{\frac{\partial C}{\partial T} + (r-q)K\frac{\partial C}{\partial K} + qC}{\frac{1}{2}K^2 \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial K^2}}$$ - Requires a smooth, arbitrage-free call price surface C(K,T) — interpolation quality is critical. - Produces a UNIQUE local vol function that reprices all vanillas exactly. - The fatal flaw: Local vol predicts that when spot drops 10%, the vol smile SHIFTS to the left but FLATTENS. In reality, when spot drops 10%, the smile shifts left AND steepens. This means local vol underestimates the cost of OTM puts in a crisis — dangerous for risk management.

$$dS = \mu S \, dt + \sqrt{v} S \, dW S$$ $$dv = \kappa(\theta - v) dt + \xi \sqrt{v} \, dW v$$ $$dW S dW v = \rho \, dt$$ Parameter Meaning Typical equity values --- --- --- v₀ Current instantaneous variance (ATM IV)² θ Long-run variance (mean-reversion target) 0.04 (= 20% long-run vol) κ Mean-reversion speed 1-5 (faster = snaps back quicker) ξ Vol of vol 0.3-1.0 ρ Spot-vol correlation −0.5 to −0.8 (equities) Feller condition: $2\kappa\theta > \xi^2$ guarantees variance stays positive. If violated, variance can hit zero — numerically problematic. Semi-analytic pricing: Heston has a characteristic function solution → Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pricing → much faster than Monte Carlo for vanillas. Exotics still need MC. ---

section: PART 4: VOLATILITY REGIMES — THE RISK MANAGER'S FRAMEWORK
content: > One-liner: Vol doesn't just have a level — it has a REGIME. And regime shifts are the most dangerous events in finance because everything correlated changes simultaneously.

Regime VIX range Realized vol Term structure Skew Correlation Market behavior --- --- --- --- --- --- --- 1. Low vol 10-15 5-12% Steep contango Moderate Low (15-25%) Grinding rally. Carry trades work. Short vol prints money. Complacency building. 2. Normal vol 15-22 12-18% Mild contango Normal Moderate (25-40%) Healthy market. Two-way flow. Risk management is standard. 3. Elevated vol 22-35 18-30% Flat to mild backwardation Steep High (40-60%) Corrections, event clusters. Hedging costs up. 4. Crisis vol 35-80+ 30-100%+ Sharp backwardation Extremely steep Very high (60-80%+) Panic. Liquidity evaporates. Correlations → 1. Models break.

Transition Speed Damage Historical examples --- --- --- --- Low → Crisis Hours to days Catastrophic for short vol Feb 2018 (Volmageddon), Mar 2020 (COVID) Crisis → Elevated Days to weeks Vol sellers rush in too early, get burned by aftershocks Mar→Apr 2020 multiple VIX re-spikes Elevated → Normal Weeks to months Gradual. Short vol finally profitable again. 2020 H2 normalization Normal → Low Months to years Slow grind. Short vol becomes more and more crowded. 2016-2017 (VIX spent months at 10-12) The asymmetry: Vol regimes shift UP in days/hours but shift DOWN over weeks/months. This is the fundamental reason why being short vol has negative skew — you earn slowly and lose fast.

Empirical fact: Large moves cluster. A 3σ day is much more likely to be followed by another 3σ day than the unconditional probability suggests. This is called volatility clustering — formalized by GARCH/EWMA models: $$\sigma^2 {t+1} = \lambda \sigma^2 t + (1-\lambda) r t^2 \quad \text{(EWMA, } \lambda \approx 0.94 \text{)}$$ For the RM: If you see one 3σ day, don't relax. Expect elevated vol to persist for days to weeks. Reduce gross exposure. Tighten stops. The worst thing you can do after a big down day is assume "it's over." ---
> One-liner: VIX is the 30-day expected volatility of SPX, derived from option prices. You CAN'T trade it directly — only through derivatives on VIX.

VIX uses a model-free approach — it's NOT derived from Black-Scholes. It aggregates prices of OTM SPX options across all strikes: $$\text{VIX}^2 = \frac{2}{T} \sum i \frac{\Delta K i}{K i^2} e^{rT} Q(K i) - \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{F}{K 0} - 1\right)^2$$ Where Q(Kᵢ) is the midpoint price of the OTM option at strike Kᵢ. This is a weighted portfolio of OTM options that replicates expected variance. Key implication: VIX is sensitive to OTM put prices → tail risk pricing affects VIX directly. This is why VIX can spike even if ATM vol doesn't move much — if panic buying inflates OTM puts, VIX rises.

VIX Implied daily SPX move Historical context RM posture --- --- --- --- 10-14 ±0.6-0.9% 2017 average (~11), 2019 average (~15) Complacency. Short vol crowded. Tail risk CHEAP — buy it. 15-20 ±0.9-1.3% Long-run median ~18 Normal. Standard risk limits apply. 20-30 ±1.3-1.9% Corrections, policy uncertainty Elevated. Tighten limits. Review hedges. 30-40 ±1.9-2.5% Dec 2018, Aug 2015, Oct 2022 Fear. Active hedge adjustments. Daily IC calls. 40-60 ±2.5-3.8% Feb 2018 peak, Q4 2008, 2011 Severe stress. Consider reducing gross. 60-80+ ±3.8-5.0%+ Mar 2020 (82.69, the record), Fall 2008 (80+) Existential. Liquidity is gone. Circuit breakers likely. Daily move conversion: VIX / √252. VIX of 20 → 20/15.87 = 1.26% daily move implied.

Property Detail --- --- Negative correlation with SPX ρ ≈ −0.75 to −0.85. SPX down → VIX up. NOT symmetric. Mean-reverting VIX always comes back. Spikes to 80 → back below 30 within months. But it can STAY elevated longer than you think. Non-tradeable directly VIX is a calculation. You trade VIX futures, options, or ETPs. Each has different properties than spot VIX. VIX of VIX (VVIX) Measures implied vol of VIX options. High VVIX → market expects VIX itself to be volatile → extreme uncertainty about uncertainty. Spot-futures basis VIX futures ≠ VIX spot. The basis is typically 1-3 pts in contango. In crisis, basis can go negative (backwardation).

section: VIX Futures — The Actual Trading Instrument
content: Feature Detail --- --- Settlement Cash-settled to VIX Special Opening Quotation (SOQ) on expiry morning Typical contango 30-day VIX future trades 1-3 pts above spot VIX in calm markets Contango roll cost If VIX spot = 15, front month future = 17, you buy at 17 and it converges to ~15 → you LOSE ~2 pts per month if VIX doesn't move. Annualized ~24 pts. Devastating for long VIX holders. Backwardation Occurs in crises. Front month > back months. Roll EARNS money for long VIX. Beta to spot VIX Front month ≈ 0.5 beta to spot VIX. Back months ≈ 0.2-0.3. VIX spot moves 20 pts → front month moves ~10, back month ~5. The VIX futures curve as dashboard: Normal (Contango): Crisis (Backwardation): VIX 15 · VIX 45 · Fut M1 · 17 Fut M1 · 42 Fut M2 · 18.5 Fut M2 · 36 Fut M3 · 19.5 Fut M3 · 32 Fut M4 · 20 Fut M4 · 29 → Roll cost eats long VIX → Roll EARNS for long VIX → Short vol systematically → Short vol getting profitable via roll destroyed on mark-to-mkt

Product Exposure Long-run return Why --- --- --- --- UVXY (2x long VIX) 2× daily front-month VIX futures → 0 Contango roll + daily rebalancing + 2x leverage = guaranteed decay VXX (1x long VIX) 1× daily blend of M1/M2 VIX futures → 0 (was terminated 2019) Contango roll. Lost 99.9% since inception. SVXY (−0.5x VIX) −0.5× daily front-month VIX futures Positive over time (harvests contango) Reduced from −1x to −0.5x after Feb 2018 blowup XIV (−1x VIX) Was −1× daily VIX futures TERMINATED Lost 96% on Feb 5, 2018. Terminated next day. The XIV lesson for risk managers: XIV had a negative acceleration trigger — as VIX spiked, XIV's short vol position grew (percentage of NAV) → needed to buy MORE VIX futures to cover → this buying pushed VIX futures HIGHER → which made XIV lose MORE → death spiral. The product wasn't just short vol — it was convexly short vol. This is the textbook illustration of why short gamma + auto-rebalancing = existential risk.

> One-liner: Variance swaps are pure vol bets — linear in VARIANCE (= vol²), which makes them convex in vol. Long variance is a crash bet on steroids. Mechanics: - Two legs at expiry: floating leg pays realized variance, fixed leg pays strike variance - Payoff = Notional × (σ² realized − K² var) - Quote convention: strike in vol terms (e.g., "strike 20 var swap" → K² var = 400) Worked example: - Buy $100K vega notional variance swap struck at 20 vol - Scenario A: Market realizes 25% vol → Payoff = $100K × (25² − 20²) / (2 × 20) = $100K × 225/40 = +$562,500 - Scenario B: Market realizes 40% vol → Payoff = $100K × (40² − 20²) / (2 × 20) = $100K × 1200/40 = +$3,000,000 - Scenario C: Market realizes 15% vol → Payoff = $100K × (15² − 20²) / (2 × 20) = $100K × −175/40 = −$437,500 Key insight — nonlinearity: Going from 20→25 realized (5pts higher) = +$562K. Going from 20→40 (20pts higher) = +$3M (not 4×$562K = $2.25M, but $3M). The squaring amplifies moves. This is why long variance swaps are MUCH more profitable in true crises than straddles — and why short variance swaps can blow up spectacularly. Variance vs volatility swap payoff comparison: Instrument Payoff depends on Big move behavior Short position risk --- --- --- --- Straddle ~Linear in vol (approximately) Linear gains/losses Capped-ish (if delta-hedged, losses ∝ vol) Vol swap Linear in vol Linear P&L Limited (vol can't go below 0) Variance swap Linear in variance (= vol²) Convex in vol → accelerating gains Unlimited → tail losses are quadratic

Product What it is Who uses it --- --- --- Vol swap Pays realized vol − strike vol (linear in vol, not variance) Cleaner than variance swap. Less tail exposure. Corridor variance swap Variance accrues only when spot is within a range Targeted vol bets. Cheaper than outright var swap. Conditional variance swap Variance accrues only on up-days or down-days "Upside variance" vs "downside variance" — isolate skew VIX options Options on VIX (which are options on expected vol) Tail hedging. Vol of vol trading. Gamma swaps Like variance swaps but weighted by spot/spot₀ Reduces blowup risk vs var swap — weight goes to zero as spot drops ---

> Understanding WHO buys and sells options, and WHY, is critical for interpreting vol signals. Participant Typical position Why Vol impact --- --- --- --- Pension funds / Insurance Buy OTM puts (systematic hedging programs) Mandate requires downside protection Pushes put IV up → steepens skew Covered call funds Sell OTM calls monthly (BuyWrite, QYLD) Generate yield for retail investors Pushes call IV down → flattens skew Hedge funds (vol trading) Both sides — dispersion, RV, cross-asset Alpha generation Two-way flow — can amplify or dampen Retail (post-2020) Buy OTM calls (meme stocks, 0DTE) Speculation Can push call IV higher than put IV (rare "positive skew") Corporates Buy puts / collars on FX, commodity exposures Hedging operating risk Consistent demand for OTM puts Dealers / Market makers Take the other side of ALL of the above. Dynamically delta-hedge. Intermediation Their aggregate gamma determines market impact

When dealers are net long gamma (the typical state — they've BOUGHT options that clients have sold, or they're net long from hedging put sales): Market drops → dealer delta becomes more negative → dealer BUYS stock to re-hedge Market rises → dealer delta becomes more positive → dealer SELLS stock to re-hedge NET EFFECT: Buy low, sell high. DAMPENS volatility. Realized vol < implied vol (the VRP in action). Market feels "sticky" — dips get bought, rallies get faded.

When dealers are net short gamma (typically after selling puts that crossed below strike, or in certain skew environments): Market drops → dealer delta becomes more positive → dealer SELLS stock to re-hedge Market rises → dealer delta becomes more negative → dealer BUYS stock to re-hedge NET EFFECT: Sell low, buy high. AMPLIFIES volatility. Realized vol > implied vol. Market feels "slippery" — dips cascade, rallies squeeze.

The spot price at which aggregate dealer gamma crosses from positive to negative: Spot position relative to GEX flip Market behavior Realized vol RM action --- --- --- --- Well above Suppressed, mean-reverting Low → short vol works Normal monitoring Near the flip Transitioning, unstable Moderate, increasing Heightened attention Below the flip Amplified, trending High → long vol works Reduce exposure, add hedges Data sources: SpotGamma, SqueezeMetrics (GEX/DIX), Goldman/JPM flow desks publish gamma estimates. These aren't precise — but the direction matters.

section: Vanna & Charm Flows — The Second-Order Dealers
content: Beyond gamma hedging, dealers also hedge second-order Greeks: Vanna flows: - Dealers short OTM puts → short vanna → when vol drops, their delta shifts → they SELL → adds selling pressure - When vol rises, delta shifts → they BUY → supportive - Net effect: Vol changes → delta changes → forced hedging flows. These are systematic and predictable. Billions of dollars daily. Charm flows: - Dealers' delta decays overnight (charm). Near expiry, this is large. - If dealers are short near-expiry puts → charm increases their delta → they need to SELL at open → selling pressure - This creates systematic morning selling around OpEx weeks

Since CBOE launched daily SPX option expiries in 2022, 0DTE options now account for ~50% of SPX option volume. Feature Impact --- --- Massive gamma at strikes Concentrated at round SPX levels (100s). Creates pin/de-pin intraday. Intraday vol spikes A large 0DTE trade → dealers hedge → magnifies move → more 0DTE trading. Feedback loop. Traditional vol metrics less useful VIX measures 30-day vol. 0DTE gamma operates on a 1-day or intraday horizon. VIX can be low while intraday vols are extreme. Gamma drops to zero at 4pm Options expire → gamma exposure vanishes → next day starts fresh. Unlike monthly options where gamma builds. The "reset" effect Daily expiry means dealers don't accumulate gamma over weeks. Each day's positioning is quasi-independent. RM concern: Traditional overnight VaR models don't capture intraday vol spikes from 0DTE gamma. A fund with concentrated SPX options positions near round strikes needs an intraday gamma monitoring framework — not just end-of-day risk reports.

Timing Effect --- --- 5 days before OpEx Gamma exposure is high → market is suppressed → low realized vol ("gamma pin") OpEx day Options expire → massive gamma drops off → suppression mechanism disappears Week after OpEx Market can "unpin" → potentially large moves. Historically higher realized vol in the week after OpEx vs week before. Quad witch (Mar, Jun, Sep, Dec) Equity options + index options + equity futures + index futures all expire → massive rebalancing flows ---

Model Key idea Parameters Pros Cons --- --- --- --- --- EWMA σ²ₜ₊₁ = λσ²ₜ + (1−λ)r²ₜ λ ≈ 0.94 (RiskMetrics) Simple, fast, no mean reversion assumed No long-run level. λ choice matters. GARCH(1,1) σ²ₜ₊₁ = ω + αr²ₜ + βσ²ₜ ω, α, β (α+β < 1) Mean-reverting. Academically standard. Symmetric — up and down moves have same vol impact. GJR-GARCH Adds asymmetric term for negative returns ω, α, β, γ Captures leverage effect (down moves → more vol). Better for equities. More parameters → overfitting risk. EGARCH Log variance is modeled (ensures v > 0) Similar to GJR Guaranteed positive variance. Handles asymmetry. Harder to interpret. HAR-RV RVₜ₊₁ = α + β₁RV daily + β₂RV weekly + β₃RV monthly Three lag coefficients Captures multi-horizon vol persistence. Simple linear regression. Best forecast performance in practice. Requires high-frequency data. For the interview: "GARCH captures volatility clustering — the tendency for large moves to follow large moves. The key parameters are α (sensitivity to recent shocks), β (persistence of current vol), and their sum (α+β) tells you how long it takes vol to mean-revert. Close to 1 means vol shocks are very persistent." Practical comparison — which model to cite: - EWMA: "We use EWMA at λ=0.94 for daily VaR." → Standard, RiskMetrics legacy. - GARCH(1,1): "Our vol forecasts use GARCH for mean-reverting estimates." → Academic credibility. - HAR-RV: "For vol forecasting, HAR-RV using multi-horizon realized vol inputs outperforms GARCH out-of-sample." → Shows you know the frontier.

Approach Method Finding --- --- --- IV as forecast Use 30-day ATM IV to forecast next-30-day realized vol IV is a biased predictor (too high on average — because of VRP). But it's MORE informationally efficient than any time-series model. IV-adjusted forecast IV × 0.85 (empirical adjustment for VRP) Better unbiased forecast than raw IV for SPX IV vs GARCH Compare forecasting accuracy IV generally wins — it aggregates more information than any single time-series model because it incorporates market participants' private information ---

section: PART 8: CORRELATION — THE OTHER DIMENSION OF VOL
content: > One-liner: Vol tells you how much individual assets move. Correlation tells you whether they move TOGETHER — and in a crisis, they all do.

$$\sigma {\text{index}}^2 = \sum i w i^2 \sigma i^2 + 2\sum {i<j} w i w j \sigma i \sigma j \rho {ij}$$ If you know index vol (from index options) and individual stock vols (from stock options), you can back out the average implied correlation. CBOE ICJ / implied correlation indices track this.

Market condition Implied correlation What it means --- --- --- Calm market 20-35% Stocks moving independently. Sector rotation. Stock-picking works. Correction 40-55% Macro factors dominating. Sector correlations rising. Crisis 60-80%+ "All correlations go to 1." Systematic risk only. No diversification. Everything dumps together. The diversification illusion: A portfolio of 30 uncorrelated stocks at 25% individual vol has portfolio vol ≈ 4.6% (1/√30 effect). At 80% correlation (crisis), the SAME portfolio has vol ≈ 22%. Diversification "vanishes" when you need it most.

The most important vol trade at funds like Squarepoint: The trade: Sell index vol (sell SPX straddles or var swaps) + buy single-stock vol (buy straddles/var swaps on constituents). Why it works: - Index vol ≈ avg stock vol × √correlation - If realized correlation < implied correlation → index vol is "too expensive" relative to single-stock vol - You profit from the correlation risk premium — the market systematically overprices correlation P&L decomposition: - Profit when stocks move INDEPENDENTLY (low realized correlation → index moves less than sum of parts) - Lose when everything correlates (crisis → index and stocks move together) Risk: Short correlation. A correlation spike (crisis) destroys the trade. This is a premium-collection strategy with left-tail correlation risk. ---

Metric Source What you're watching Red flag --- --- --- --- VIX level Market data Fear level VIX > 25 and rising VIX term structure VIX futures curve Contango/backwardation Inversion (backwardation) = near-term panic IV/RV ratio Computed (30d ATM IV vs 20d realized) Options expensive or cheap? Ratio < 0.90 → market may be underpricing risk 25Δ risk reversal Options desk / Bloomberg Skew — how afraid is the market of downside? RR more negative than −8 vol pts = extreme fear Implied correlation COR index or computed Are stocks moving together? Over 60% = macro risk dominating Dealer gamma (GEX) SpotGamma / internal Are dealers amplifying or dampening? Negative GEX = volatility-amplifying environment VVIX CBOE Vol of vol — uncertainty about uncertainty Over 130 = extreme Cross-asset vol MOVE (rates), EVZ (EUR), OVX (oil) Is vol broad or equity-specific? Multiple asset classes spiking = systemic

Current state RM priority Tactical adjustments --- --- --- Low vol + contango 1. Ensure hedges are in place (they're cheap!). 2. Check for short-vol concentration. 3. Size tail risk. Buy OTM puts while cheap. Flag PMs who are short a lot of gamma/vega. Rising vol, contango flattening 1. Review hedge effectiveness. 2. Check for margin calls. 3. Stress-test delta + vega combined. Consider adding near-dated hedges. De-risk short gamma positions. High vol + backwardation 1. Daily or intraday risk communication. 2. Check counterparty risk. 3. Liquidity of positions. Tighten ALL limits. Hedge in the back of the curve (cheaper, avoids buying panic-priced near-dated vol). Vol declining from elevated 1. Don't rush back. Aftershocks common. 2. Selective vol selling now profitable. Gradually re-allocate. Roll hedges from expensive near-dated to cheaper far-dated.

section: Hedging with Vol — Strategic vs Tactical
content: Approach Instruments Cost Protection profile When to use --- --- --- --- --- Rolling 30Δ puts SPX OTM puts, rolled monthly 1-3% annualized drag Pays off in crash. Bleeds in calm. Standard institutional hedge Put spread collars Buy 95-90% put spread, sell 105% call Near zero-cost Capped downside + capped upside PM who wants protection without cost VIX calls Buy VIX 30-strike calls Premium only Pays off in vol spike (not necessarily equity down) Pure tail hedge Long variance (var swap) Buy variance struck at ATM Carry cost Convex payout in crash Best tail hedge, highest cost if nothing happens Dispersion (long stock vol, short index vol) Pair of var swaps Carry depends on premium Pays if correlation drops Alpha trade, not directional hedge ---

"Explain implied vol versus realized vol and why the gap matters": "Realized vol is the standard deviation of actual returns — it tells you what happened. Implied vol is backed out of option prices — it tells you what the market expects, PLUS an insurance premium. Implied is almost always higher than realized. That gap is the volatility risk premium — it's ~2-4 points for SPX on average. It explains why selling options is profitable on average: you're collecting insurance premium. But the premium exists for a reason — when realized vol spikes above implied (Feb 2018, Mar 2020), the losses are catastrophic and nonlinear, especially for variance swaps where payoffs are quadratic in vol. For risk management, I monitor three things: the IV/RV ratio (options cheap or expensive?), the slope of the vol term structure (is near-term fear elevated?), and skew (is the market pricing tail risk?). Together they tell me whether the portfolio's vol exposure is well-positioned for the current regime." "Walk me through the vol surface — what is it and why does it matter?": "The vol surface maps implied volatility across two dimensions: strike and expiry. Along the strike axis you get skew — for equities, OTM puts have higher IV than ATM, which creates a characteristic smirk. This reflects three things: demand for downside protection, fat-tailed returns, and the leverage effect where lower prices mean higher leverage and therefore higher vol. Along the time axis you get the term structure — normally upward-sloping (more uncertainty over longer horizons) but it inverts when there's near-term event risk. Why it matters operationally: the surface determines all option prices, which means it determines all Greeks. A PM who sells an OTM put at 25% IV in a 20% ATM vol environment isn't selling at '5 points of edge' — the skew IS the fair price for that strike. Understanding the surface is understanding whether a trade has genuine edge or is just harvesting a risk premium." "How does dealer gamma affect markets?": "Options dealers take the other side of client flow and delta-hedge dynamically. When they're long gamma — the normal state — they buy dips and sell rallies because their delta moves against the market. This SUPPRESSES realized vol — it's why realized vol is often below implied. When they're short gamma — typically after a selloff pushes spot through large put strikes — the dynamics flip. They SELL into selloffs and BUY into rallies, amplifying moves. This is the mechanism behind cascading sell-offs. As a risk manager, I'd monitor estimated dealer gamma positioning. Below the gamma flip level, the market loses its natural shock absorber. Realized vol will likely exceed implied vol, meaning any short-vol positions in the book face compounding losses. That's when I'd flag for hedge adjustments." "Explain the VIX and how you'd use it in risk management": "VIX is the 30-day expected SPX volatility derived from OTM option prices. It's model-free — not Black-Scholes-

section: WHY THIS IS YOUR OPERATING SYSTEM
content: At Squarepoint, everything is measured. Every PM has a VaR budget, a drawdown limit, a set of stress scenarios they must survive, and a decomposition of their P&L into risk factors. As a Junior Risk Manager, your daily job involves: 1. Reading risk reports — "Desk A has 95% VaR of $3.2M, ES/VaR ratio of 1.8, 65% utilization of risk budget" 2. Flagging anomalies — "Desk B's parametric VaR dropped 20% but their gamma exposure doubled — VaR is misleading because of nonlinearity" 3. Running stress tests — "What happens to the fund under SPX −15%, VIX +30, UST 10Y +100bp?" 4. Challenging PMs — "Your Sharpe is 1.8 but your Sortino is 0.6 — your return is coming from selling tail risk, not alpha" Risk measures are not abstract math. They are the WORDS you use to communicate risk, set limits, and protect capital. Get them wrong, or use them naively, and you miss what kills the fund. ---
> One-liner: VaR is the maximum loss at a given confidence level over a given horizon under NORMAL conditions — and it says absolutely nothing about what happens when conditions stop being normal.

$$\text{VaR} \alpha = \inf \{ x : P(\text{Loss} > x) \leq 1 - \alpha \}$$ In English: "99% 1-day VaR = $5M" means: "On 99 out of 100 days, the loss will NOT exceed $5M." Equivalently: "On 1 in 100 days, losses WILL exceed $5M — and VaR is completely silent on HOW FAR beyond $5M." Key parameters: Parameter Common choices Impact --- --- --- Confidence level 95%, 97.5%, 99%, 99.5% Higher confidence → larger VaR number → more tail captured Time horizon 1 day, 10 days 10-day VaR ≈ 1-day VaR × √10 (scaling assumption — crude but standard) Regulatory context: Framework VaR spec Notes --- --- --- Basel II/III (banks) 99% 10-day VaR Plus multiplier (3× or higher if backtesting fails) FRTB (new) 97.5% ES replacing VaR Moving to ES — more tail-sensitive Hedge fund (internal) 95% or 99% 1-day VaR Fund-specific. Squarepoint uses internal risk budgets.

Assumption: Portfolio returns are normally distributed. $$\text{VaR} {99\%} = z {0.99} \times \sigma p \times \text{Portfolio Value}$$ Where $z {0.99} = 2.326$, $z {0.975} = 1.96$, $z {0.95} = 1.645$. For a portfolio of N assets: $$\sigma p^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$$ Where w = weight vector, Σ = covariance matrix. Worked example — Two-asset portfolio: - $60M in SPX (σ = 18%), $40M in UST 10Y (σ = 8%), correlation ρ = −0.30 - Portfolio variance = (0.6)²(0.18)² + (0.4)²(0.08)² + 2(0.6)(0.4)(0.18)(0.08)(−0.30) = 0.011664 + 0.001024 − 0.003456 = 0.009232 - Portfolio σ = √0.009232 = 9.61% - 99% 1-day VaR = 2.326 × 9.61%/√252 × $100M = 2.326 × 0.605% × $100M = $1.41M - Note: without correlation benefit (ρ=0), VaR would be $1.53M. The −0.30 correlation saves $120K of daily VaR. Strengths Weaknesses --- --- Fast — closed-form, scales to 10,000+ positions Assumes normality — misses fat tails, skewness Transparent — can decompose into factor contributions Fails for non-linear positions (options). Delta-normal only. Easy to communicate Covariance matrix can be unstable — 500 assets need 125,000 pairwise correlations Component VaR and marginal VaR derivable analytically Correlation breakdown in crisis — the matrix estimated from calm times doesn't apply in stress Option-unfriendly: Parametric VaR uses delta (linear) approximation. An option P&L is nonlinear (gamma). Parametric VaR UNDERESTIMATES risk for short gamma positions and OVERESTIMATES risk for long gamma positions. At a fund running options, this is dangerous.

Process: 1. Collect N days of historical returns for all risk factors (typically 250-1000 days) 2. Apply each day's actual returns to today's portfolio positions 3. Generate N simulated P&L outcomes 4. Sort them. The loss at the (1−α)×N-th percentile = VaR. Worked example: - 500 trading days of history - Apply each day's returns to current $100M portfolio - Sort the 500 simulated P&Ls - 99% VaR: the 5th worst day (500 × 1% = 5) → say that's −$4.8M - 95% VaR: the 25th worst day → say that's −$2.1M Strengths Weaknesses --- --- No distribution assumption — captures actual fat tails, skewness Hostage to history — if the window doesn't include a crisis, VaR misses it Naturally handles non-linear positions (options repriced each scenario) Equal weighting — a return from 3 years ago counts the same as yesterday (unless you use EWMA weighting) Captures cross-asset correlation as it actually occurred Look-back window dilemma : long window = smoother but includes stale data; short window = responsive but noisier Easy to explain to non-quants Ghost effects : a single extreme day drops OUT of the window → VaR drops suddenly, even though nothing changed in the portfolio Improvement — BHS (Bootstrapped Historical Simulation): Resample with replacement from the historical window to generate more scenarios. Reduces dependence on a single ordering. Also allows confidence intervals around VaR itself. Improvement — Filtered Historical Simulation (FHS): Use GARCH to standardize returns (remove time-varying vol), then resample. Combines historical tail shape with current vol estimate. Best of both worlds.

Process: 1. Specify return distributions and correlation structure for all risk factors 2. Generate 10,000-100,000+ random scenarios 3. For each scenario, fully reprice the portfolio (including options, exotics) 4. Build the P&L distribution. Take the percentile. Strengths Weaknesses --- --- Most flexible — any distribution, any payoff, any correlation structure Computationally expensive — tens of thousands of full portfolio repricings Handles non-linear instruments properly Model risk — only as good as your distributional assumptions Can incorporate fat tails (t-distribution), jumps, stochastic vol Convergence — need enough simulations for stable tail estimates Can model regime changes, dynamic correlations False precision risk — fancy simulation doesn't mean accurate if the model is wrong When MC is essential: Portfolios heavy in exotic options (barriers, Asian, lookbacks), structured products, or complex multi-asset derivatives. Parametric and historical VaR can't handle these — only full repricing captures the nonlinearity.

section: VaR Decomposition — Where the Risk Lives
content: > At Squarepoint, a single aggregate VaR number is useless. You need to know WHERE the risk is.

$$\text{Component VaR} i = \rho {i,p} \times \sigma i \times w i \times z \times V$$ The contribution of position i to total portfolio VaR. Component VaRs SUM to total VaR (they account for diversification). The power: "Your total VaR is $5M. Desk A contributes $3.2M. Desk B contributes $2.8M. Desk C contributes −$1.0M." Desk C is a diversifier — its risk REDUCES total VaR because it's negatively correlated with the rest.

$$\text{Marginal VaR} i = \frac{\partial \text{VaR}}{\partial w i}$$ How much does VaR change if you add $1 more to position i? Tells you which positions are most efficient to add or trim from a risk perspective.

$$\text{Incremental VaR} i = \text{VaR} {\text{with position}} - \text{VaR} {\text{without position}}$$ Unlike marginal (infinitesimal change), incremental measures the FULL effect of adding/removing a position. Critical for new trade approval: "This new position adds $400K of incremental VaR. Do we have risk budget?"

> Every interviewer at Squarepoint will test whether you blindly trust VaR or understand its failures. Limitation Why it matters Real-world consequence --- --- --- Not a worst case VaR = threshold, not a range beyond it. The 1% tail could be $5M or $500M. LTCM, Bear Stearns — losses far beyond VaR Assumes normal conditions Crisis behavior (correlation spike, liquidity dry-up, gap moves) ≠ normal behavior Every major blowup violates the conditions VaR assumed Backward-looking Computed from recent history. If recent history was calm → VaR is low → just when risk is building 2008: VaR was LOW entering the crisis because 2005-2007 was calm Not subadditive VaR(A+B) can > VaR(A) + VaR(B) — violates coherence Portfolio combination can look RISKIER than sum of parts — nonsensical for allocation Gameable Sell deep OTM options → premium income, VaR looks clean, massive hidden tail risk Every "low-VaR, high-Sharpe" strategy should be checked for hidden concavity Point-in-time Computed at close. Positions change intraday. VaR at 4pm may not reflect 2pm risk. Intraday blowups not captured by EOD VaR Correlation instability Calm-period correlations used in VaR → wrong in crisis (correlations spike to 0.8+) Diversification assumed by VaR vanishes in the tail The interview line: "VaR is necessary but far from sufficient. I think of it as a speedometer — useful for daily monitoring, but it doesn't tell you there's a cliff ahead. For that you need stress testing, ES, and judgment." ---

section: PART 2: EXPECTED SHORTFALL (ES / CVaR / Tail VaR)
content: > One-liner: ES answers the question VaR refuses to ask — "When we breach VaR, how bad does it actually get?"
