$$\text{ES} \alpha = E \text{Loss} \mid \text{Loss} > \text{VaR} \alpha = \frac{1}{1-\alpha} \int \alpha^1 \text{VaR} u \, du$$ In English: The average of ALL losses in the tail beyond VaR. If 99% VaR = $5M, ES averages the losses on the 1% worst days.

Property VaR ES --- --- --- Tail severity Blind — only the threshold Measures the whole tail Subadditivity Violates (not coherent) Always satisfies (coherent) Sensitivity to tail shape None — two portfolios with same VaR but different tail sizes look identical Higher for fatter tails Regulatory trend Old regime (Basel II/III) New regime (FRTB) — banks moving to 97.5% ES Gameability Easy to manipulate (sell tail) Much harder to game

Portfolio A (selling OTM puts): - 99% VaR = $5M - On the 1% worst days: losses are $10M, $15M, $25M, $40M, $80M - ES = average($10M, $15M, $25M, $40M, $80M) = $34M - ES/VaR ratio = 34/5 = 6.8 ← FAT tail. Concave payoff. Hidden risk. Portfolio B (diversified long equity): - 99% VaR = $5M - On the 1% worst days: losses are $5.5M, $6M, $6.5M, $7M, $7.5M - ES = average = $6.5M - ES/VaR ratio = 6.5/5 = 1.3 ← thin tail. Linear payoff. What you see is what you get. Both have IDENTICAL VaR. Only ES reveals that Portfolio A has 5× worse tail risk.

section: ES/VaR Ratio — The Tail Shape Indicator
content: ES/VaR ratio Tail shape What it means Typical position type --- --- --- --- 1.0-1.5 Thin / normal VaR captures most of the risk Diversified long equity, futures 1.5-2.5 Moderate Some tail concentration Mixed books, moderate option overlay 2.5-4.0 Fat Significant hidden tail risk Concentrated trades, short gamma positions 4.0+ Very fat / cliff Extreme concavity — DANGER Short deep OTM options, short var swaps, illiquid credit > RM rule of thumb: If a desk's ES/VaR ratio exceeds 3.0, investigate. They're likely running a concave P&L profile — earning steady premium but exposed to catastrophic tail loss.

For normal distribution: $\text{ES} {99\%} = \sigma \times \frac{\phi(z {0.99})}{0.01} = \sigma \times 2.665$ Compare to $\text{VaR} {99\%} = 2.326 \sigma$ → ES/VaR = 2.665/2.326 = 1.15 (thin tail for normal). For Student-t (ν=5) : ES/VaR ≈ 1.83 . Fatter tails → bigger gap between VaR and ES. For actual equity markets (empirical): ES/VaR typically 1.4-1.6 for diversified portfolios, 2.0-5.0+ for option-heavy books. ---

section: PART 3: STRESS TESTING — THE COMPLEMENT TO VaR
content: > One-liner: VaR tells you what happens on a normal bad day. Stress tests tell you what happens on the WORST day — the one that actually threatens the fund's survival.

VaR and ES are statistical — they rely on historical distributions or model assumptions. Stress testing is scenario-based — you DEFINE the scenario and measure the impact. This makes it: - Not backward-looking — you can test events that haven't happened yet - Not model-dependent — you specify the shocks directly - Intuitive — "If SPX drops 20% and VIX hits 60, we lose $X" is understood by everyone

> Replay actual crises with current positions. Event Equity Vol Rates Credit Commodities Duration --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Black Monday 1987 SPX −22% (1 day) N/A (pre-VIX) −50bp +100bp −5% 1 day LTCM 1998 SPX −19% +40pts −100bp (flight to quality) +300bp Russia, +200bp HY −15% 3 months GFC 2008 (Sep–Nov) SPX −40% VIX 80+ −200bp +400bp HY Oil −55% 3 months Flash Crash 2010 SPX −9% intraday VIX 40 −20bp +30bp −2% 36 minutes Volmageddon Feb 2018 SPX −10% VIX 14→50 +30bp +50bp −5% 2 weeks COVID Mar 2020 SPX −34% VIX 14→82 −100bp then +50bp +600bp HY Oil −65% 5 weeks Russia/Ukraine 2022 SPX −10% VIX 36 +100bp (inflation) +100bp Oil +50% Several months SVB/banking Mar 2023 SPX −5% VIX 26 −50bp (flight to quality) +80bp bank CDS −10% oil 2 weeks Japan carry unwind Aug 2024 Nikkei −12%, SPX −5% VIX 38 −30bp UST +30bp −3% 3 days How to use: Take each event's factor moves, apply to current portfolio positions, aggregate P&L. "If 2008 replayed today, we'd lose $X on equities, $Y on credit, gain $Z on rates = net −$W."

> Plausible scenarios that haven't occurred — often MORE valuable because the next crisis is never identical to the last. Examples calibrated for 2026: Scenario Equity Vol Rates FX Commodities Probability assessment --- --- --- --- --- --- --- US-China military escalation (Taiwan) −20% +45pts −100bp (quality) USD +8% Oil +40% Low prob, extreme impact Simultaneous US + EU recession −25% +35pts −150bp EUR/USD flat Oil −30% Medium prob Fed emergency hike (inflation surge) −12% +25pts 2Y +100bp, 10Y +50bp USD +5% +15% Low prob US Treasury downgrade / auction fail −15% +30pts UST SELL OFF +80bp USD −10% Gold +20% V.low prob, v.high impact Systematic strategy crowding unwind −10% +20pts ±20bp ±3% Mixed Medium prob, correlations spike Cyber attack on major exchange/clearinghouse −8% +30pts −30bp ±5% −5% Low prob

> Systematic grid of factor bumps — the "heat map." Example grid — Equity × Vol shock: SPX −2% SPX −5% SPX −10% SPX −15% SPX −20% --- --- --- --- --- --- Vol unch −$0.6M −$1.5M −$3M −$4.5M −$6M Vol +5 −$0.4M −$1.8M −$5M −$8M −$12M Vol +15 +$0.2M −$3M −$9M −$16M −$25M Vol +30 +$1M −$5M −$15M −$28M −$42M Reading the heat map: The upper-left is normal. The lower-right is crisis. If losses in the lower-right quadrant exceed the fund's drawdown limit, ACTION is required NOW, not during the crisis. Rate sensitivity grid (for FI book): Parallel +50bp Parallel +100bp Steepener (2s10s +50bp) Flattener (2s10s −50bp) Twist (short end +50, long end −50) --- --- --- --- --- --- DV01 P&L −$XM −$2XM Depends on KRD Depends on KRD Depends on KRD

> Start from the loss. Work backward to find the scenario that causes it. "What scenario causes a $30M loss (our drawdown limit)?" → solve for the combination of factor moves. This is arguably the most important stress test because it directly answers: "What kills us?" Process: 1. Define the loss threshold (e.g., max drawdown limit, fund-breaking loss) 2. Optimize over scenarios: find the minimum-severity scenario that hits the threshold 3. Ask: "Is this scenario plausible?" If yes → you have a problem. 4. The scenarios that produce fund-death with PLAUSIBLE moves are the ones to hedge against. ---

section: DV01 (Dollar Value of a Basis Point)
content: > One-liner: How much money you make or lose for a 1bp move in yield. THE unit of rates risk. $$\text{DV01} \approx \frac{\text{ModDur} \times \text{Price} \times \text{Notional}}{10{,}000}$$ Worked example: - $50M par of 10Y UST, modified duration = 8.5, price = 95 - DV01 = 8.5 × 95/100 × $50M / 10,000 = $40,375 per bp - If yields rise 25bp: loss = 25 × $40,375 = −$1,009,375 - If yields fall 50bp: gain = 50 × $40,375 = +$2,018,750 DV01 ladder — decomposition by maturity: Tenor DV01 What it means --- --- --- 2Y bucket +$15K/bp Long duration at the short end (benefits from rate cuts) 5Y bucket −$8K/bp Short duration at 5Y (hedging?) 10Y bucket +$40K/bp Main risk — long 10Y 30Y bucket +$5K/bp Small long-end exposure Net +$52K/bp Net long duration — loses if yields rise Why the ladder matters more than total DV01: A portfolio can have DV01 = $0 (duration-neutral) but have +$50K/bp at 2Y and −$50K/bp at 30Y — that's a massive curve steepener bet that nets to zero DV01. The RM must see the LADDER, not just the total.

Duration decomposed by tenor point. Instead of one number, you get a vector: $$\text{KRD} i = -\frac{1}{P} \frac{\partial P}{\partial y i}$$ Where $y i$ is the yield at tenor i. This captures non-parallel yield curve moves — flattening, steepening, butterfly — that parallel duration completely misses.

> P&L for a 1bp widening in credit spreads. The credit equivalent of DV01. $$\text{CS01} \approx \text{Spread Duration} \times \text{MV} / 10{,}000$$ Worked example: - $20M corporate bond, spread duration = 4.5 - CS01 = 4.5 × $20M / 10,000 = $9,000 per bp of spread widening - If credit spreads widen 100bp (stress): loss = 100 × $9,000 = −$900,000 Portfolio CS01 by rating bucket: Rating CS01 Risk contribution --- --- --- IG (A/BBB) $25K/bp Main exposure — investment-grade credit HY (BB) $8K/bp Higher-yielding, more volatile HY (B/CCC) $3K/bp Small but spreads can widen 500bp+ in crisis

section: CR01 (Credit Rho 01) and JTD (Jump to Default)
content: Measure Definition When it matters --- --- --- CR01 P&L for a 1bp parallel shift in the credit curve Standard credit sensitivity JTD Loss if a single name defaults immediately (recovery assumed at ~40%) Concentration risk — what if our biggest credit position defaults tomorrow? JTD worked example: $10M notional in Company X CDS (protection buyer). If X defaults: you receive (1 − Recovery) × Notional = 0.60 × $10M = $6M gain. If you're the protection SELLER: you PAY $6M. JTD monitors this single-name concentration. ---

> These metrics evaluate WHETHER the risk being taken is generating adequate return. A PM with high return but higher risk is not necessarily good — and a PM with low return but well-controlled risk might be excellent.

$$\text{Sharpe} = \frac{R p - R f}{\sigma p}$$ Where: - $R p$ = annualized portfolio return - $R f$ = risk-free rate (typically the T-bill or OIS rate) - $\sigma p$ = annualized portfolio volatility (standard deviation of returns) The numerator ($R p - R f$) is the excess return — what you earned above the risk-free alternative. The denominator is how much risk you took to get it. Sharpe Assessment Reality check --- --- --- < 0.3 Poor — not justifying the risk Why is the fund open? 0.3-0.7 Below average Typical for beta-heavy strategies 0.7-1.0 Good Solid risk-adjusted performance 1.0-2.0 Very good Most successful hedge funds live here 2.0-3.0 Exceptional Either genuine edge, short track record, or illiquidity premium > 3.0 Suspicious Too good — likely short vol/tail risk, illiquidity marks, or data mining

section: Sharpe Limitations — Why You Can't Trust It Alone
content: Issue Explanation Example --- --- --- Symmetric vol penalty Penalizes upside vol equally with downside A fund that makes 50% in one month and 2% in 11 months has "high vol" but spectacular returns Short-vol gaming Selling OTM options generates steady premium + low vol → high Sharpe — until the blowup Many "Sharpe 2.0+" funds blew up in Feb 2018. Pre-blowup Sharpe was excellent. Time dependence Sharpe from the last 6 months ≠ Sharpe over 5 years Short-period Sharpe is noisy: √(1/n) standard error Non-normal returns Sharpe assumes return/risk = mean/stdev, which assumes normality. Skew/kurtosis ignored. A fund with negative skew has worse TRUE risk-adjusted performance than Sharpe suggests Benchmark-blind Sharpe doesn't tell you if returns come from skill (alpha) or market exposure (beta) A PM with β=1.2 in a bull market has high Sharpe but zero alpha Statistical significance of Sharpe: To distinguish Sharpe = 1.0 from Sharpe = 0 at 95% confidence, you need: $$n \geq \left(\frac{1.96}{\text{Sharpe}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.96}{1.0}\right)^2 \approx 4 \text{ years of monthly data}$$ Two years of good performance is NOT enough to conclude skill.

$$\text{Sortino} = \frac{R p - R f}{\sigma {\text{downside}}}$$ Same $R p$ and $R f$ as Sharpe, but the denominator is downside deviation — standard deviation computed only from returns BELOW a target (usually 0 or $R f$). Upside volatility is ignored entirely. Why Sortino > Sharpe for hedge funds: A fund that generates its return through positively skewed bets (long gamma, event-driven, distressed credit) has LOW downside vol and HIGH upside vol. Sharpe penalizes both. Sortino correctly rewards the upside skew. RM insight: Compare Sharpe to Sortino. If Sortino >> Sharpe → positive skew (good). If Sortino << Sharpe → NEGATIVE skew (P&L distribution has a fat left tail — investigate).

> The largest peak-to-trough decline before a new peak. $$\text{MDD} = \max {t \in 0,T } \left( \max {s \leq t} V s - V t \right) / \max {s \leq t} V s$$ Why drawdown matters more than vol for survival: - A fund with 12% annualized vol can survive indefinitely. - A fund with a −40% drawdown might not survive — investors redeem, margin calls hit, the PM is fired. - Drawdowns are path-dependent and NOT captured by VaR/σ. A fund can have low vol and still hit a massive drawdown through a slow grind lower.

Drawdown Return required to recover Time at 10% annual return --- --- --- −5% +5.3% ~6 months −10% +11.1% ~1.1 years −20% +25.0% ~2.3 years −30% +42.9% ~3.6 years −50% +100% ~7.3 years −75% +300% >15 years (fund is dead) > RM implication: This is why drawdown limits are HARD limits. Once you're past −20%, recovery becomes a multi-year project. Past −50%, the fund is effectively done — even if it could recover mathematically, investors will have redeemed.

$$\text{Calmar} = \frac{\text{Annualized return}}{ \text{Max drawdown} }$$ Return per unit of worst loss. Typical for CTAs and macro funds. Calmar Assessment --- --- < 0.5 Poor — the drawdown is too large relative to returns 0.5-1.0 Decent 1.0-2.0 Very good > 2.0 Excellent or too-short track record

$$\text{IR} = \frac{R p - R b}{\text{Tracking Error}}$$ Alpha divided by tracking error. Measures the CONSISTENCY of outperformance relative to benchmark. IR Assessment --- --- < 0.3 No distinguishable skill. Could be noise. 0.3-0.5 Some evidence of skill 0.5-1.0 Good — consistent outperformance > 1.0 Rare. Very high skill or very concentrated bets that happened to work.

$$R p = \alpha + \beta \times R m + \epsilon$$ Component What it is What to check --- --- --- β (beta) Market exposure — how much of the return is just riding the index β > 0.3 → significant market dependency. Not pure alpha. α (alpha) Excess return after removing market exposure This is what justifies management fees. Should be positive and significant. ε (residual) Idiosyncratic — unexplained by market factor High residual vol = concentrated bets. Check factor exposures. Multi-factor decomposition (Fama-French, Barra): $$R p = \alpha + \beta 1 R {\text{market}} + \beta 2 R {\text{size}} + \beta 3 R {\text{value}} + \beta 4 R {\text{momentum}} + \epsilon$$ A PM who claims alpha but has β momentum = 0.8 is just running momentum — the "alpha" is factor exposure, not skill. Real alpha should be the residual after ALL known factor exposures are removed.

$$\Omega = \frac{\int r^\infty (1-F(x)) \, dx}{\int {-\infty}^r F(x) \, dx}$$ The ratio of gains above a threshold to losses below it. Captures the ENTIRE distribution — no normality assumption. Higher is better. Unlike Sharpe, properly accounts for skew and kurtosis. ---

section: PART 6: VaR BACKTESTING — VERIFYING THE RISK MODEL
content: > One-liner: A VaR model that's never tested is just an expensive guess. Backtesting asks: "How many times did actual losses exceed VaR?" and if the answer is too many (or too few), the model is broken.

Process: Compare daily realized P&L against the previous day's VaR prediction. Count "exceptions" (days where loss > VaR). For a 99% VaR model over 250 trading days: expected exceptions = 250 × 0.01 = 2.5 per year .

Tests whether the number of exceptions is statistically consistent with the stated confidence level. $$LR {\text{POF}} = -2 \ln \left \frac{(1-p)^{n-x} p^x}{(1-x/n)^{n-x} (x/n)^x} \right \sim \chi^2(1)$$ Where p = expected failure rate (1% for 99% VaR), x = actual exceptions, n = total observations. Traffic light system (Basel): Exceptions (250 days, 99% VaR) Zone Consequence --- --- --- 0-4 🟢 Green Model is acceptable 5-9 🟡 Yellow Model under review. Multiplier increases. 10+ 🔴 Red Model rejected. Major multiplier increase or model replacement.

Kupiec tests whether exceptions are the RIGHT NUMBER. Christoffersen tests whether they're INDEPENDENT (not clustered). Why independence matters: If your model has 3 exceptions per year but they ALL occur in the same week, that's worse than 3 exceptions spread across the year — it means your model fails exactly when risk is highest (volatility clustering → model is too slow to adapt).
Combines both: correct number AND independent. The joint test is the gold standard for VaR backtesting.

Result Diagnosis Action --- --- --- Too FEW exceptions (0 in 2 years) VaR is too conservative — overstating risk Capital inefficiency. PM is being constrained unnecessarily. Recalibrate(lower vol estimates or update correlation). Correct exceptions (evenly spaced) Model is well-calibrated Continue monitoring. Too MANY exceptions (10+ per year) VaR underestimates risk — dangerous Investigate: stale correlations? Missing risk factors? Nonlinearity not captured? Increase multiplier or switch methods. Exceptions clustered Model doesn't adapt to regime changes Switch from equal-weight historical to EWMA/GARCH-weighted. Or use Filtered Historical Simulation. ---
> At a fund like Squarepoint, risk measures aren't academic — they're the LIMITS that constrain PMs and protect capital.

Level Limit type Set by Examples --- --- --- --- Fund level Total fund VaR, gross/net exposure, drawdown CRO / Board Fund VaR < $15M (99% 1d). Max DD = −15%. Desk / PM level VaR budget, Greeks limits, concentration Risk team Desk A: VaR ≤ $3M, short gamma < $1M/1%, max single-name = 5% of NAV Position level Size limits, liquidity thresholds Risk team + compliance No single equity > 3% of ADV. No illiquid bond > $5M notional. Factor level Factor exposure limits Risk team Beta < ±0.3. Sector concentration < 20%. Duration < ±2Y.

$$\text{Fund VaR budget} = \sum i \text{Desk} i \text{ VaR allocation} + \text{Reserve}$$ Example: - Fund 99% 1-day VaR budget: $10M - Desk A (equity vol): $4M (40%) - Desk B (macro rates): $3M (30%) - Desk C (credit): $2M (20%) - Reserve / unallocated: $1M (10%) Key concepts: - Utilization: Desk A running $3.2M actual VaR on $4M budget = 80% utilization. Normal. - Over-utilization: Running $4.5M on $4M budget = 112%. Requires risk approval or position reduction. - Correlation benefit: If desks are uncorrelated, total fund VaR < sum of desk VaRs. This is the diversification benefit — the reserve accounts for the possibility that correlations spike.

Limit Level Action --- --- --- Soft drawdown (−5%) Warning PM briefs IC. Risk reviews positions. No forced action yet. Hard drawdown (−10%) Action required Risk budget cut by 50%. PM must present recovery plan. IC approval for any new trades. Kill switch (−15%) Book liquidation Systematic position reduction. PM may be suspended. Book handed to risk desk for orderly unwind. Drawdown measurement: Typically measured from high-water mark on a rolling 12-month basis. Intraday marks may apply for systematic strategies.

section: Marginal Contribution to Risk — Capital Allocation
content: $$\text{MCR} i = \frac{\partial \text{VaR} {\text{fund}}}{\partial w i}$$ Tells you: "If Desk A gets $1M more capital, how much does fund VaR increase?" Used for: - Capital allocation decisions — allocate more to desks with low MCR (they add return efficiently relative to risk) - Performance evaluation — RAROC (Risk-Adjusted Return On Capital) = Return / Economic Capital consumed $$\text{RAROC} i = \frac{\text{Desk} i \text{ P\&L}}{\text{Desk} i \text{ VaR allocation}}$$ ---
> One-liner: A position isn't just risky because it can lose value — it's risky because you might not be able to EXIT when you need to.

Standard VaR assumes you can close positions at mid-price. Reality: you close at bid (selling) or ask (buying), and the spread widens in stress. $$\text{LVaR} = \text{VaR} + \text{Liquidation Cost}$$ Where: $$\text{Liquidation Cost} = \frac{1}{2} \sum i \text{Spread} i \times \text{Position Size} i \times \text{Stress Multiplier} i$$ Worked example: - Standard VaR = $5M - Portfolio has $200M notional with average normal spread of 5bp, stress spread 25bp - Normal liquidation cost = ½ × 0.0005 × $200M = $50K (negligible) - Stress liquidation cost = ½ × 0.0025 × $200M = $250K - LVaR = $5M + $250K = $5.25M For ILLIQUID positions (HY bonds, small-cap, EM): stress spreads can be 200bp+ → liquidation cost dominates.

$$\text{DTL} = \frac{\text{Position Size}}{\text{ADV} \times \text{Participation Rate}}$$ Participation rate Meaning Typical --- --- --- 10% of ADV Conservative — minimal market impact Standard assumption 25% of ADV Moderate Acceptable for liquid names 50%+ of ADV Aggressive — significant market impact Emergency only Worked example: - Hold $20M of stock X, ADV = $5M, 10% participation - DTL = $20M / ($5M × 0.10) = 40 days - Your 1-day VaR assumes you can exit in 1 day. You can't. Liquidity-adjusted horizon should be 40 days. VaR should be scaled: VaR 40d ≈ VaR 1d × √40 = 6.3× larger .

Tier Liquidity profile DTL VaR treatment --- --- --- --- 1 (Liquid) ADV > $50M, tight spreads, deep book < 3 days Standard 1-day VaR 2 (Semi-liquid) ADV $5-50M, moderate spreads 3-10 days Apply √DTL scaling 3 (Illiquid) ADV < $5M, wide spreads, thin book 10-30 days Apply √DTL + stress spread + redemption gate consideration 4 (Very illiquid) Bespoke OTC, no secondary market 30+ days or impossible Mark uncertainty. Full position size at risk in stress. May need side-pocket. ---
> One-liner: Every risk measure is the output of a model. If the model is wrong, the risk measure is worse than useless — it creates false confidence.

Source What goes wrong Impact on risk --- --- --- Wrong distribution Assumed normal, actual is fat-tailed VaR underestimates tail risk by 2-5× Stale parameters Correlations estimated from calm period used in crisis Diversification benefit evaporates — actual risk much higher Missing risk factors Model has 5 factors, but the 6th (liquidity, correlation, convexity) is the one that kills you "Unknown unknowns" — risk model shows green while the portfolio bleeds Wrong pricing model Options priced with BS (constant vol), actual dynamics are stochastic vol Greeks are wrong → hedges are wrong → P&L leaks Overfitting Model calibrated to fit history perfectly, fails on new data Backtests look perfect, live performance is terrible Implementation errors Bugs in risk system, data feed errors, stale prices "Garbage in, garbage out" — most common source of risk model failure

Check What you do Frequency --- --- --- Backtesting Compare VaR predictions to realized P&L Daily (automated) Benchmarking Compare internal model to external/vendor models Quarterly Sensitivity analysis Perturb model inputs — does output change reasonably? Quarterly or on model change Challenger model Run a completely different model in parallel Continuous P&L explain Compare model-predicted P&L (from Greeks) to actual P&L Daily Expert review Quants review model assumptions, limitations, edge cases Annual or on model change P&L explain as model validation: If your model predicts today's P&L should be −$2.3M (from delta + gamma + vega + theta) but actual is −$4.1M, the −$1.8M "unexplained" is model error. Persistent unexplained P&L = model is wrong. Investigate. ---

"What are the limitations of VaR?" "VaR is a useful speedometer but a terrible crash detector. Five key limitations: First, it tells you nothing about losses BEYOND the threshold — a $5M VaR could be hiding $50M of tail risk, and you'd only know from Expected Shortfall or stress testing. Second, it's computed from historical data under normal conditions — but the distribution changes in a crisis: correlations spike, liquidity vanishes, and your calm-period covariance matrix is wrong. Third, it's not subadditive — combining portfolios can produce higher VaR than the sum, which violates basic intuition and makes it unreliable for capital allocation. Fourth, it's gameable — selling deep OTM options generates premium income with low measured VaR while embedding massive tail risk. Finally, it's a point-in-time snapshot at market close that misses intraday risk. That's why I'd always supplement VaR with ES for tail severity, stress tests for extreme scenarios, and the ES/VaR ratio as a quick diagnostic — if that ratio exceeds 3, there's a concave payoff hiding in the book." "How would you set up a stress testing framework?" "Three layers. Historical stress tests — replay 2008, COVID, Volmageddon with current positions — because you need to know if the portfolio survives past crises. Hypothetical scenarios — events that haven't happened, like a Taiwan conflict or US Treasury auction failure — because the next crisis won't look like the last one. And sensitivity grids — systematic factor bumps across equity × vol × rates dimensions — to map out the P&L heat map and find exactly where the portfolio breaks. I'd add a fourth: reverse stress testing. Start from the loss threshold — say our drawdown limit is $30M — and work backward to find the minimum-severity scenario that causes it. If that scenario is plausible, we have an actionable problem to address TODAY, not during the crisis." "Walk me through the Sharpe ratio and its problems": "Sharpe is excess return over risk-free divided by vol. It's the standard risk-adjusted performance metric — most successful hedge funds are in the 0.7-2.0 range. But it has three fatal flaws for evaluating hedge fund strategies. First, it treats upside and downside vol equally, so a positively skewed fund is penalized for its best feature. The Sortino ratio fixes this by only measuring downside deviation. Second, it can be gamed — any strategy that sells tail risk generates steady premium with low measured vol, producing Sharpe 2.0+ until the tail event arrives and it goes to zero. I'd check Sortino/Sharpe ratio: if Sortino is much lower than Sharpe, the fund has hidden negative skew. Third, short-period Sharpe is statistically noisy — you need ~4 years of monthly data to distinguish Sharpe 1.0 from zero at 95% confidence. Two quarters of good performance proves nothing." "Explain the difference between VaR, ES, and stress testing": "These three form a risk measurement pyramid. VaR tells you the boundary of normal losses —

section: WHY THIS IS THE SPINE OF YOUR JOB
content: At Squarepoint, individual position risk is table stakes. Every PM manages their own book. Your job as Junior Risk Manager is the PORTFOLIO level — aggregating across desks, detecting hidden commonalities, and ensuring the whole fund survives scenarios no single PM is thinking about. This file covers the intellectual framework for doing that. The core reality: risk is not additive. A fund with four $3M-VaR desks doesn't have $12M of total VaR. It could be $6M (diversification) or $11M (hidden correlation). Understanding WHY — and monitoring the transition between those states — is the entire job. ---

section: PART 1: CORRELATION — THE MOST DANGEROUS NUMBER IN FINANCE
content: > One-liner: Correlation is a chameleon — it looks stable in calm markets and shape-shifts violently the moment you need it to stay put.

$$\rho {X,Y} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma X \cdot \sigma Y} = \frac{E (X - \mu X)(Y - \mu Y) }{\sigma X \cdot \sigma Y}$$ Ranges from −1 (perfect inverse) to +1 (perfect co-movement). Zero = no LINEAR relationship (but non-linear dependence can still exist — see copulas).

Five properties that make correlation dangerous: Property What it means Consequence --- --- --- Regime-dependent ρ changes with market state (risk-on/off, vol level, macro regime) The ρ you estimated from 2017-2019 is useless in March 2020 Asymmetric ρ is typically HIGHER in sell-offs than rallies Diversification vanishes when you need it most Non-stationary Even within a regime, ρ drifts over time as fundamentals change Last year's correlation matrix is already stale Only captures linear dependence ρ=0 does NOT mean independent. Assets can have strong tail dependence with zero linear correlation Copula methods needed for tail co-movement Estimation error For N assets: N(N−1)/2 pairwise correlations. 500 assets → 124,750 parameters → massive estimation noise Covariance matrix is unreliable without shrinkage or factor structure

Unconditional (average): ρ computed over the whole sample. This is what most risk models use. It's an average of calm and crisis, which means it's wrong in BOTH states. Conditional (stress-specific): ρ computed only when the market is under stress (e.g., SPX down >2%). This is what actually drives tail P&L. Pair Unconditional ρ Conditional ρ (stress) What flipped --- --- --- --- SPX vs single stocks 0.30 0.70+ Idiosyncratic washes out; beta dominates SPX vs HY credit 0.50 0.85+ Both driven by risk appetite SPX vs UST 10Y −0.30 −0.50 (usually) BUT +0.40 in 2022 Inflation regime flips the sign SPX vs gold −0.10 −0.30 (usually) BUT +0.20 if real rates dominate Real rate sensitivity overrides safe-haven Cross-PM portfolios 0.10-0.20 0.50-0.80 Hidden common macro factors emerge Momentum vs value −0.60 −0.90 (reversal) or +0.40 (both crash) Depends on whether it's rotation or deleveraging The 2022 case study: Stocks-bonds correlation flipped from −0.30 to +0.40. Every "60/40" portfolio, every equity-bonds hedge, every duration overlay ASSUMED negative correlation. When it flipped positive, hedges became additional losses. This regime change affected trillions in AUM globally. The RM who saw it coming was invaluable; the one who didn't was running a model that made things WORSE.

section: Correlation Models the Quant Desk Uses
content: Model How it works Strengths Weaknesses --- --- --- --- Rolling window ρ = last N-day sample correlation Simple, intuitive Jumpy, equal-weights old data, ghost effects EWMA Exponentially weight recent observations more heavily (λ=0.94 RiskMetrics) Responds to regime shifts faster Still backward-looking, one decay parameter for everything DCC-GARCH (Dynamic Conditional Correlation) Two-stage: GARCH each series individually, then model conditional correlation dynamics Captures time-varying ρ with momentum and mean-reversion Computationally heavy for 500+ assets Factor model implied ρ ij = β i·β j·σ² market / (σ i·σ j) + residual Shrinks estimation error. Structured. Misses idiosyncratic co-movement Copula Models JOINT distribution separately from marginals Captures tail dependence even when linear ρ=0 Hard to calibrate, model-selection risk What Squarepoint likely uses: Factor-model-implied correlation matrix (Barra or internal PCA factors) with DCC overlays for liquid assets and stressed-correlation overrides for tail scenarios. The single-number ρ from a spreadsheet is not what drives the risk system.

section: Eigenvalue Decomposition of the Correlation Matrix
content: A powerful diagnostic from linear algebra. Decompose the N×N correlation matrix into eigenvalues and eigenvectors: $$\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{Q} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{Q}^T$$ What the eigenvalues tell you: Eigenvalue pattern Diagnosis Action --- --- --- One dominant eigenvalue (explains >60% of variance) Portfolio dominated by a single factor (usually market beta) PM is running a directional bet, not a diversified book Top 3 eigenvalues explain >85% Portfolio has 3 principal risk drivers Identify them — likely market, sector, style Many similar eigenvalues Genuinely diversified — many independent bets Healthy. Diversification is REAL. Smallest eigenvalue near zero Near-singular matrix — multicollinearity Risk system outputs can be numerically unstable. Requires regularization. Worked example — 4 desks: - Eigenvalue decomposition of desk-level P&L correlation matrix: λ = 2.8, 0.6, 0.4, 0.2 - First eigenvalue explains 2.8/4 = 70% of total variance - Finding: all four desks are driven by the same factor (turns out: implicit SPX beta) - The fund THINKS it has 4 independent desks. It actually has 1.3 effective bets.

section: What the RM Needs to Do About Correlation
content: 1. Never trust the trailing ρ at face value. Ask: "Which regime produced this estimate? Would it hold in stress?" 2. Run stressed-correlation scenarios. Re-compute VaR with ρ = 0.7 and ρ = 0.9 for all risk-on pairs. How much does VaR increase? 3. Monitor stocks-bonds ρ daily. In an inflation regime, positive ρ changes everything about hedging. 4. Aggregate across PMs. The most dangerous risk is when the CRO sees four "independent" desks but eigenvalue decomposition shows one factor. 5. Use implied correlation from options markets as a forward-looking estimate alongside backward-looking statistical estimates. ---

section: PART 2: DIVERSIFICATION — THE MATH AND ITS LIMITS
content: > One-liner: Diversification is the only free lunch in finance — until the restaurant closes during a crisis and you discover everyone was eating at the same table.

For a portfolio of N assets with equal weights: $$\sigma p = \sigma \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} + \frac{N-1}{N} \bar{\rho}}$$ Where σ = average individual asset vol, $\bar{\rho}$ = average pairwise correlation. Key insight from this formula: - As $N \to \infty$: $\sigma p \to \sigma \sqrt{\bar{\rho}}$ - You can NEVER diversify below $\sigma \sqrt{\bar{\rho}}$ — this is the systematic risk floor - If $\bar{\rho} = 0.30$ and σ = 25%: floor = 25% × √0.30 = 13.7% — no matter how many stocks you hold Diversification benefit table (assuming σ = 25%, equal weight): N positions ρ = 0.0 ρ = 0.20 ρ = 0.40 ρ = 0.60 ρ = 0.80 --- --- --- --- --- --- 1 25.0% 25.0% 25.0% 25.0% 25.0% 5 11.2% 13.4% 16.0% 18.7% 21.5% 10 7.9% 12.1% 15.0% 18.2% 21.1% 30 4.6% 11.5% 14.4% 17.8% 20.8% 100 2.5% 11.2% 14.1% 17.7% 20.7% ∞ 0% 11.2% 15.8% 19.4% 22.4% Reading the table: At ρ=0.40 (realistic for equities in the same market), going from 10 to 100 stocks only reduces vol from 15.0% to 14.1% — diminishing returns are extreme. Most diversification benefit is captured by 15-20 uncorrelated positions. Beyond that, you need to reduce CORRELATION, not add positions.

$$\text{DR} = \frac{\sum i w i \sigma i}{\sigma p} = \frac{\text{Weighted average vol}}{\text{Portfolio vol}}$$ DR Interpretation --- --- 1.0 No diversification (all assets perfectly correlated or single asset) 1.2-1.5 Moderate diversification 1.5-2.0 Good diversification 2.0+ Strong diversification — portfolio vol much less than weighted-average vol RM use: Track DR daily. If it DROPS (toward 1.0), correlations are rising and diversification is evaporating — typically in stress. DR dropping from 1.8 to 1.3 is an early warning.

$$\text{ENB} = \frac{(\text{DR})^2}{1} = \left(\frac{\sum w i \sigma i}{\sigma p}\right)^2$$ Alternative (from PCA): $\text{ENB} = \exp\left(-\sum i p i \ln p i\right)$ where $p i = \lambda i / \sum \lambda j$ (Shannon entropy of eigenvalues). A fund with 50 positions but ENB = 3 has three effective bets. This number should be tracked and compared to what the PM claims.

Mechanism: In tail events, correlations spike → diversification benefit collapses → portfolio vol jumps → margin calls → forced selling → more correlation → feedback loop. Crisis Pre-crisis ρ avg Crisis ρ avg Diversification loss --- --- --- --- LTCM 1998 0.25 0.70 ~60% of diversification benefit gone GFC 2008 0.30 0.80 ~70% gone COVID 2020 0.25 0.75 ~65% gone (recovered within weeks) 2022 inflation stocks-bonds −0.30 stocks-bonds +0.40 Sign reversal — hedge became loss RM implication: Your VaR model uses calm-period correlations showing portfolio vol of 10%. In a crisis, actual correlations spike and real portfolio vol is 18%. Your VaR was understated by 80%. This is precisely the scenario you must stress-test for. ---

section: PART 3: FACTOR MODELS — X-RAYING THE PORTFOLIO
content: > One-liner: Factor models look through the 500 individual positions and tell you the 8 real risks driving the fund — because 500 positions with the same factor exposure is not diversification, it's a leveraged bet on that factor.

A factor model decomposes every asset's return into systematic (explainable) and idiosyncratic (residual) components: $$r i = \alpha i + \sum {k=1}^K \beta {ik} f k + \epsilon i$$ Where $f k$ = factor return, $\beta {ik}$ = asset i's loading on factor k, $\epsilon i$ = idiosyncratic return. Portfolio-level: The portfolio's risk is then: $$\sigma p^2 = \mathbf{b} p^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} f \mathbf{b} p + \sum i w i^2 \sigma {\epsilon i}^2$$ Where the first term = systematic (factor) risk and the second = specific (idiosyncratic) risk . In a well-diversified portfolio: Specific risk → 0 as N increases. Factor risk dominates. This means understanding factor exposures IS understanding portfolio risk.

Pre-defined factors based on company characteristics. Barra equity model example (70+ factors): Category Factors What they capture --- --- --- Country US, UK, JP, DE, CN, ... Geographic return drivers Industry Energy, Banks, Tech, Healthcare, ... (30+) Sector-specific returns Style Size, Value, Momentum, Volatility, Growth, Quality, Leverage, Yield, Liquidity Cross-sectional attributes Currency USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, ... FX exposure within equity returns How Barra works: Each stock gets factor loadings updated monthly (e.g., AAPL: Size = −1.2 large , Momentum = +0.8, Value = −0.5 growth ). The model estimates factor returns daily. Portfolio risk = f(aggregate factor exposures × factor covariance matrix + specific risk). Squarepoint context: Almost certainly runs a multi-factor risk model (Barra, Axioma, or proprietary PCA-based). Daily reports show: "Your top risk contributors are: Market factor (40% of variance), Momentum (25%), Tech sector (15%), Specific (20%)."

Factors are observable macro variables. Factor Proxy What it captures --- --- --- Equity market SPX return Broad market risk premium Interest rates ΔUS10Y Rate sensitivity Credit ΔIG spread Credit cycle Inflation ΔTIPS breakeven Inflation expectations Commodity ΔBCOM / ΔWTI Real asset sensitivity Currency ΔDXY Dollar strength Volatility ΔVIX Risk appetite Use case: For a discretionary macro fund, decompose: "How much of our P&L came from our rate view vs our equity view vs our commodity view?"

No economic interpretation — purely statistical. Extract principal components from the return covariance matrix. $$\text{PC} 1 = \text{direction of maximum variance}$$ $$\text{PC} 2 = \text{direction of maximum remaining variance, orthogonal to PC} 1$$ Typical equity PCA results: - PC1 ≈ market factor (explains 40-60% of variance) - PC2 ≈ growth vs value rotation (~10-15%) - PC3 ≈ small vs large (~5-8%) - PC4+ ≈ sector rotations, less interpretable Strengths over fundamental: No assumptions about what the factors ARE. Purely data-driven. Captures hidden factors the fundamental model misses. Weaknesses: Hard to interpret ("PC3 is a long banks/short utilities factor… why?"). Unstable — the PCs rotate over time. Factor loadings change every recalibration.

section: Factor Risk Decomposition — The Power Tool
content: Worked example — Fund with 3 desks: Factor Desk A exposure Desk B exposure Desk C exposure Fund aggregate --- --- --- --- --- Market β +0.30 +0.15 −0.10 +0.35 Momentum +0.50 +0.30 +0.20 +1.00 ← DANGER Value −0.20 +0.10 +0.05 −0.05 Size (small) +0.10 +0.30 +0.10 +0.50 Rates (DV01) $0K −$5K +$3K −$2K Diagnosis: Each desk looks individually reasonable. But aggregate momentum exposure = +1.00 — that's a MASSIVE hidden concentration. If momentum reverses (as it did violently in Q4 2018 or Sep 2020), all three desks lose simultaneously. No single PM sees this. Only the RM aggregating across desks can detect it. Factor risk contribution: $$\text{Factor risk} k = \frac{\beta {p,k}^2 \sigma k^2}{\sigma p^2} \times 100\%$$ Factor Contribution to portfolio variance --- --- Momentum 52% ← majority of risk is ONE factor Market 22% Size 12% Rates 8% Specific 6% Action: Momentum is >50% of risk. Either (a) the CIO consciously wants this as the house view, or (b) it's unintentional and needs to be reduced. The RM's job is to surface this and have the conversation.

$$R p = \underbrace{\alpha} {\text{skill}} + \underbrace{\sum k \beta k f k} {\text{factor exposure}} + \underbrace{\epsilon} {\text{residual}}$$ Component What the RM checks --- --- α > 0 (significant) PM is generating genuine alpha. Justify the fees. α ≈ 0, R² high PM's return is ALL factor — no skill, just exposure. "You're charging 2-and-20 for levered beta." α < 0, R² high PM is DESTROYING value relative to factor exposure. Worse than an ETF. R² low Model doesn't explain the returns. Need better factors or PM is truly idiosyncratic. ---

section: PART 4: CONCENTRATION RISK — THE SILENT KILLER
content: > One-liner: A portfolio with 100 names can be more concentrated than a portfolio with 10 — if those 100 names are all loading on the same factor, you don't own 100 bets, you own one.

Metric — Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI): $$\text{HHI} = \sum {i=1}^N w i^2$$ Where $w i$ = weight of position i. HHI Effective N (= 1/HHI) Concentration level --- --- --- 0.01 100 Very diversified 0.05 20 Moderate 0.10 10 Concentrated 0.20 5 Highly concentrated 0.50+ 2 or fewer Single-bet portfolio Worked example: - Portfolio: 5 positions at weights 40%, 25%, 15%, 12%, 8% - HHI = 0.16 + 0.0625 + 0.0225 + 0.0144 + 0.0064 = 0.266 - Effective N = 1/0.266 = 3.8 - Despite "5 positions," the effective diversification = ~4 independent bets (because the 40% position dominates) Limit framework: Measure Typical limit (discretionary HF) Why --- --- --- Largest single position <5% of NAV Single-stock gap risk Top 10 positions <40% of NAV Concentration in top holdings Effective N (1/HHI) >10 Minimum diversification threshold

Even 50 stocks can be ONE bet if they're all in the same sector. Example: A PM holds 20 semiconductor names across the US, Taiwan, and Korea. They claim geographic diversification. But semis are globally correlated (same cycle, same end-demand). When the CHIPS Act or AI capex cycle reverses, ALL 20 positions move together. The PM had sector concentration disguised as geographic diversification.

This is what kills multi-PM funds. Each PM picks stocks independently but they all end up loading on the same systematic factors. How it hides: - PM A is long tech (growth exposure) - PM B is long banks and short utilities (rate-sensitivity exposure) - PM C is long NVIDIA and short defensive names (AI momentum exposure) - All three are implicitly: long growth, long risk-on, short safety → ONE factor bet across three "independent" desks Detection: Factor regression on aggregate portfolio, eigenvalue decomposition of cross-PM correlation matrix, track effective number of bets.

"We trade with 3 prime brokers. Our biggest PB holds 60% of our margin balance." If that PB pulls lines (as Bear Stearns and Lehman clients learned), 60% of the fund's positions face forced liquidation. Limits: No single PB > 40% of margin. Diversify clearing across at least 3 CCPs.

Your fund is diversified. But every other hedge fund holds the same positions. In aggregate, the trade is crowded. When one fund de-leverages, everyone must follow → amplified crash. Detection tools: - 13F overlap: Count how many large funds hold each position - Short interest: Positions with >25% SI are crowded shorts → short-squeeze risk (GameStop Jan 2021) - Factor-crowding score: Measure aggregate HF positioning in momentum, value, etc. - Hedge Fund Research (HFR) crowding indices Historical crowding blow-ups: Event Date What was crowded Loss --- --- --- --- Quant quake Aug 2007 Statistical arbitrage positions (momentum, mean-reversion) Multi-billion across quant funds Short vol Feb 2018 VIX ETPs (XIV et al.), short variance swaps XIV lost 96% in one day Momentum reversal Sep 2020 Tech/growth vs value rotation 10-20% drawdowns for momentum funds Japan carry unwind Aug 2024 Short JPY / long risk assets Nikkei −12%, multi-strat funds hit ---

section: PART 5: BASIS RISK — THE GAP BETWEEN YOUR HEDGE AND YOUR POSITION
content: > One-liner: Basis risk is the risk that your insurance policy pays out on a DIFFERENT house than the one that burned down.

Basis = price of the hedged asset − price of the hedging instrument. If basis is stable, the hedge works. If basis moves, you're exposed — you can lose on BOTH the position AND the hedge simultaneously.

Position Hedge Basis = What can go wrong --- --- --- --- Long XOM stock Short XLE ETF XOM − (β × XLE) XOM-specific news (earnings, M&A) creates idiosyncratic move ETF doesn't cover Long Brent crude Short WTI futures Brent − WTI spread Spread blew to $25 in 2011 (Libya). Normally $2-3. Long IG corp bond Short Treasury futures Credit spread Rate hedge works; credit spread widens → still lose money Long single-stock Short index put Stock − index Stock drops 20% on fraud; index drops 2%. Put covers 2 of 20. Long physical commodity Short front-month future Spot − future (calendar basis) COVID: oil futures went NEGATIVE while physical was still $20+ Long EM equity Short EM FX Equity × FX cross-rate FX hedge tenor ≠ equity holding period

Hedge effectiveness (R²): $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{Var}(\text{hedged portfolio})}{\text{Var}(\text{unhedged portfolio})}$$ R² Interpretation --- --- >0.95 Excellent hedge — basis is tight 0.80-0.95 Decent but meaningful basis risk remains 0.50-0.80 Hedge is directional only — basis will cause P&L <0.50 Not really a hedge — you're trading TWO positions, not hedging one Optimal hedge ratio: $$h^ = \rho \times \frac{\sigma {\text{position}}}{\sigma {\text{hedge}}}$$ If the position and hedge aren't perfectly correlated or don't have the same volatility, the hedge ratio ≠ 1.0. A naive 1:1 hedge leaves basis risk.

section: Why Basis Risk Can Be Worse Than No Hedge
content: Scenario: PM is long $50M of corporate bonds, hedges with $50M short in Treasury futures. - Rates rise 50bp: Treasury hedge gains $1.6M ✓ - BUT credit spreads also widen 80bp: corporate bond loses ADDITIONAL $2.0M from credit - Net: hedge gained $1.6M, position lost $1.6M (rate) + $2.0M (credit) = net loss $2.0M - PM thought they were hedged. They were hedged against RATES but not CREDIT. The hedge created false confidence. RM lesson: Always ask: "What is this hedge ACTUALLY hedging? What risks remain?" ---

section: PART 6: TAIL RISK & FAT TAILS — PREPARING FOR "IMPOSSIBLE" EVENTS
content: > One-liner: Normal distributions say a 5-sigma daily move happens once every 14,000 years. Markets produce them every few years. Any risk framework built on normality is a framework for losing money in the tail.

Sigma event Normal: frequency Market reality Underestimation factor --- --- --- --- 3σ (e.g., SPX −4.5%) 1 in 370 days 1 in 50-70 days ~6× 4σ (e.g., SPX −6%) 1 in 31,560 days (126 years) 1 in 500-700 days (2-3 years) ~50× 5σ (e.g., SPX −7.5%) 1 in 3.5 million days 1 in 5,000-8,000 days (20-30 years) ~500× 6σ+ (e.g., SPX −10%+) "Never" Oct 1987, Oct 2008, Mar 2020 ∞ Excess kurtosis = Kurtosis − 3 (above the normal benchmark): - Normal distribution: 0 - SPX daily returns: ~20-25 (massively fat-tailed) - Individual stocks: 5-50+ - Credit spreads: 30+

Rather than modeling the ENTIRE distribution, EVT focuses specifically on the tail. Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD): For losses exceeding a high threshold u: $$P(\text{Loss} > u + y \mid \text{Loss} > u) = \left(1 + \frac{\xi y}{\beta}\right)^{-1/\xi}$$ Where ξ (xi) = shape parameter (tail index): ξ value Tail type Distribution family --- --- --- ξ = 0 Exponential (thin) tail Normal-like ξ > 0 Power-law (fat) tail — extreme events more likely Pareto, Student-t. THIS IS FINANCE. ξ < 0 Bounded tail Physical limits (temperatures, etc.) For equities, ξ ≈ 0.2-0.4 → moderately fat tails. For credit, ξ can be >0.5 → very fat tails. Why EVT matters for RM: Standard VaR at 99% uses the whole distribution. But the 99.9th or 99.99th percentile (the "fund-killing" event) requires EVT because you're extrapolating into the tail where you have almost no data.

section: Tail Dependence — When Everything Crashes Together
content: Linear correlation misses tail dependence — the tendency of extreme moves to occur simultaneously. Copula language: - Upper tail dependence (λ U): Probability of joint extreme GAIN - Lower tail dependence (λ L): Probability of joint extreme LOSS Copula family λ L (lower tail) λ U (upper tail) Financial relevance --- --- --- --- Gaussian 0 0 No tail dependence → UNDERESTIMATES joint crashes Student-t >0 >0 Symmetric tail dependence → better for crashes Clayton >0 0 Lower-tail only → models crashes without upside linkage Gumbel 0 >0 Upper-tail only → less relevant for risk RM implication: If your risk model uses a Gaussian copula (or equivalently, normal joint distribution), it SYSTEMATICALLY underrates the probability of multiple assets crashing simultaneously. This was a key failure in 2008 CDO pricing → Gaussian copula said joint default probability was negligible → it wasn't.

Instrument Mechanism Annual cost (approx) Crisis payoff Rebalance --- --- --- --- --- OTM index puts (5% OTM, 3M) Direct floor on portfolio 1.5-3.0% of NAV 5-20× payout in −20% crash Roll quarterly Put spreads (buy 5% OTM, sell 15% OTM) Cheaper floor, capped payout 0.5-1.5% 2-5× payout, capped Roll quarterly VIX calls (strike 30-40) Vol convexity 1.0-2.5% 3-10× in vol spikes Roll monthly Variance swap (buy var) Pure vol exposure, convex payout Carry cost in low-vol Massive payout if rv >> iv Maturity-based Dynamic delta hedging Increase short delta as market drops (portfolio insurance) Execution costs + whipsaw Scales with move Continuous Cash buffer Hold 5-15% cash for margin calls Opportunity cost Prevents forced liquidation Continuous The RM's tail-hedge conversation: - "The tail hedge costs $3M/year in premium decay." - "The scenario it protects against: SPX −25%, all desks hit simultaneously, forced liquidation → $80M loss." - "Is $3M/year good insurance against an $80M loss with, say, 3-5% annual probability?" - Expected tail loss = $80M × 4% = $3.2M → break-even. The hedge is WORTH it on expected value alone, before considering survival value. ---

section: PART 7: LIQUIDITY RISK — THE RISK OF NOT BEING ABLE TO EXIT
content: > One-liner: A position isn't risky because it can lose value — every position can lose value. It's risky because you might not be able to EXIT when you desperately need to.

Market impact model (Almgren-Chriss framework): $$\text{Cost} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} s} {\text{spread}} + \underbrace{\eta \cdot v} {\text{temporary impact}} + \underbrace{\gamma \cdot \frac{Q}{\text{ADV}}} {\text{permanent impact}}$$ Where s = bid-ask spread, η = temporary impact coefficient, v = execution speed, γ = permanent impact, Q = total shares. Key insight: Market impact is NON-LINEAR. Trading 1% of ADV costs X. Trading 5% of ADV doesn't cost 5X — it costs 10-15X because you're consuming liquidity depth levels. ADV concentration: Position / ADV Classification RM action --- --- --- <1% Liquid No concern 1-5% Manageable Monitor 5-10% Illiquid Require liquidity plan 10-25% You ARE the market Hard limit — must reduce or justify >25% Trapped Forced to accept fire-sale prices or side-pocket

section: Funding Liquidity Risk (Can you meet CASH obligations?)
content: Obligation Urgency What drives it --- --- --- Margin calls (daily) Immediate (T+1) P&L losses, vol spike, correlation spike Redemptions Monthly/quarterly (with notice) Investor sentiment, relative performance Collateral calls (CSA) Daily Mark-to-market on OTC derivatives Margin requirement increases Same-day to T+1 Exchange or PB increases margin rates The liquidity spiral (Brunnermeier & Pedersen): Losses → Margin calls → Forced selling → Prices drop further → More margin calls → More selling → Prices drop more → ... SPIRAL: Funding liquidity ↔ Market liquidity FEEDBACK LOOP This is the mechanism that kills leveraged funds. It's why LTCM failed (1998), why Bear Stearns' hedge funds failed (2007), and why every multi-strategy fund has a kill switch.

Tier Characteristics DTL (days to liquidate at 10% ADV) VaR treatment --- --- --- --- 1 Listed, ADV >$100M, spread <5bp <1 day Standard 1-day VaR 2 Listed, ADV $10-100M, spread 5-20bp 1-5 days LVaR = VaR × √DTL 3 listed/OTC, ADV $1-10M, spread 20-100bp 5-20 days LVaR + stress spread + concentration haircut 4 OTC, bespoke, ADV <$1M or no market 20+ days Full notional at risk in stress. May need side-pocket.

Scenario What happens to liquidity RM action --- --- --- Vol spike (VIX 15→40) Spreads widen 3-5×. Market depth drops 50-80%. ADV for small-caps collapses. Run stressed-DTL. Check if margin buffer survives 3 days of losses with impaired liquidity. Redemption wave Need to liquidate 10-20% of AUM in a week. But most liquid positions are sold first → remaining portfolio becomes MORE illiquid. Pre-plan the liquidation waterfall: sell Tier 1 first, Tier 2 next. Never sell Tier 4 in stress. PB credit pullback Prime broker reduces line or increases haircuts. Margin utilization jumps. Maintain relationships with 3+ PBs. Keep 15%+ margin headroom. Correlated forced selling Every fund is selling the same crowded positions simultaneously → market impact explodes Crowding analysis: if you hold the same names as 50 other hedge funds, your "liquid" positions become illiquid in a crowded unwind ---

> One-liner: A PM constructs portfolios for return. The RM constructs CONSTRAINTS for survival. The intersection — portfolios that maximize return subject to risk constraints — is where the real edge lives.

$$w i \cdot \text{MCR} i = \frac{\text{Total Risk}}{N} \quad \forall i$$ Every position contributes the same amount of risk. High-vol positions get smaller weights; low-vol positions get larger. Worked example: - 3 assets: Equities (σ=15%), Bonds (σ=5%), Commodities (σ=20%) - Equal-weight (33/33/33): Equities contribute 55% of risk, commodities 35%, bonds 10% - Risk parity weights (approx): Equities 20%, Bonds 60%, Commodities 15% - Now each contributes ~33% of total risk Pro: Truly diversified at the RISK level, not the dollar level. Con: Requires leverage (lots of bonds to match equity vol). Low return per unit of capital without leverage.

$$f^ = \frac{p(b+1) - 1}{b} = \frac{\text{Edge}}{\text{Odds}}$$ Or for continuous returns: $f^ = \frac{\mu - r f}{\sigma^2}$ Where $\mu$ = expected return, $r f$ = risk-free rate, $\sigma^2$ = variance. Practical Kelly: - Full Kelly is too aggressive (assumes perfect parameter estimates, no tail risk) - Most practitioners use Half-Kelly or Quarter-Kelly - Over-betting (above full Kelly) is WORSE than under-betting — expected growth rate DECREASES Fraction of Kelly Risk profile Use case --- --- --- Full Kelly Maximizes long-run geometric growth — extremely volatile Theoretical benchmark only Half Kelly 75% of growth rate, ~50% of drawdown Aggressive but practical Quarter Kelly 50% of growth rate, ~25% of drawdown Conservative — most institutional

Set a target portfolio volatility (e.g., 10%) and scale position sizes to achieve it: $$\text{Leverage} = \frac{\sigma {\text{target}}}{\sigma {\text{realized}}}$$ When realized vol is low → lever UP. When realized vol spikes → lever DOWN. This automatically de-risks in crises. RM consideration: Vol targeting creates PROCYCLICAL behavior in the short term (selling into drops, buying into rallies) but STRUCTURAL risk management (lower leverage in high-vol regimes where tail events are more likely).

The fund's total risk budget flows down through layers: FUND LEVEL: Total VaR ≤ $15M (99%, 1-day) Max Drawdown ≤ −15% from HWM Gross Leverage ≤ 5× │ ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ DESK A DESK B DESK C VaR ≤ $5M VaR ≤ $4M VaR ≤ $3M DD ≤ −8% DD ≤ −6% DD ≤ −5% (Equity Vol) (Macro Rates) (Credit) │ │ │ ┌────┴────┐ ┌────┴────┐ ┌───┴───┐ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ ▼ Single- Greeks DV01 Curve CS01 Single- name limits limits limits limits name JTD ≤5% ±$50K/bp ≤$10M Reserve / unallocated: Typically 10-15% of fund VaR budget is held in RESERVE for: - Correlation spikes that reduce diversification benefit - New opportunity set (PM finds a great trade, needs risk budget) - Model error (VaR underestimates) Utilization monitoring: Utilization Status RM response --- --- --- <50% Under-utilized PM may be too cautious. Capital inefficiency. 50-80% Normal Monitor. 80-95% Elevated Proactive conversation with PM. Review positions. 95-100% At limit Any new trade requires trade reduction elsewhere. >100% Breach Immediate action. PM must reduce risk by EOD or get CRO waiver. ---

section: PART 9: THE RM'S DAILY RISK DASHBOARD — PUTTING IT ALL TOGETHER
content: > One-liner: The dashboard is your cockpit — the 20 numbers that tell you whether the fund is safe or whether you need to act before the next market open.

When you look at a portfolio's risk, you need ALL of these layers: Layer 1: WHAT do we own? → Position-level review (names, sizes, Greeks, notional) Layer 2: IS it concentrated? → HHI, effective N, sector weights, counterparty exposure Layer 3: WHY is it risky? → Factor decomposition (what's driving the risk?) → Eigenvalue analysis (how many effective bets?) Layer 4: HOW MUCH can we lose? → VaR (99% 1-day), ES, ES/VaR ratio → Stress test P&L (historical + hypothetical) Layer 5: WHAT IF things get worse? → Stressed correlations, stressed vol, stressed liquidity → Reverse stress test: "what scenario kills us?" Layer 6: CAN WE GET OUT? → DTL by position, liquidity tiering → Margin headroom under stress → Redemption-adjusted liquidation plan

Metric What it tells you Threshold / flag --- --- --- --- Exposure 1 Net delta ($) Directional exposure Within PM mandate 2 Gross exposure / NAV Total leverage <4× (discretionary), <8× (stat arb) 3 Long/short ratio Directional tilt Within mandate. Shifts signal PM is getting directional. Concentration 4 Largest single position (% NAV) Single-name risk <5% 5 Top 10 positions (% NAV) Top-heavy risk <40% 6 HHI / Effective N Diversification Effective N >10 7 Largest sector (% gross) Sector concentration <25% Factors 8 Top 3 factor exposures Hidden systematic bets No single factor >40% of risk 9 Market β (aggregate) Directional drift β < 0.3 for market-neutral 10 ENB (effective number of bets) True diversification >5 for multi-strat Risk measures 11 VaR (99%, 1-day) Probabilistic loss Within VaR budget 12 ES / VaR ratio Tail shape <2.5. If >3.0 → concave payoff, investigate 13 Stress P&L (worst scenario) Tail survival Within drawdown limit Drawdown 14 Current drawdown (from HWM) Distance to kill switch Flag at >50% of hard limit 15 MTD / QTD / YTD P&L Performance trajectory Context for risk conversations Liquidity 16 % of book liquidable in 1 day Emergency exit capacity >60% 17 DTL (95th percentile position) Worst-case exit time <10 days for 95% of book 18 Margin utilization Funding headroom <70% normal, <85% stress Greeks (if options) 19 Net gamma ($ per 1% move) Convexity of P&L Short gamma within limits 20 Net vega ($000s per 1 vol pt) Vol exposure Within limits

6:30 AM → Overnight P&L from Asian/European sessions → Any position gaps >2%? → Any correlation regime shifts? 7:00 AM → Run EOD risk reports (generated overnight): VaR, ES, factor decomposition, stress tests → Compare to limits. Flag any breaches or near-breaches. 7:30 AM → Pre-market risk meeting: - "Desk A at 87% VaR utilization after yesterday's move" - "Aggregate momentum factor exposure increased 20% — two PMs added same direction" - "If VIX hits 30, margin requirement increases $12M — we have $15M headroom" 8:00 AM → Markets open. Intraday monitoring begins. → Real-time P&L. Alert if any PM hits soft drawdown limit. 4:00 PM → EOD mark. Generate tomorrow's risk reports. → Any limit breaches? Document and escalate. → Any stress test results exceeding thresholds?

Trigger Who Action Timeline --- --- --- --- Approaching limit (>80% utilization) RM → PM "You're at 85% of VaR budget. Be aware." Informational. Same day. Soft limit breach RM → PM + desk head PM presents rationale. Risk review of positions. 24 hours. Hard limit breach RM → CRO Mandatory position reduction. IC approval for any new trades. Immediate. EOD compliance. Kill switch CRO → all Systematic risk reduction. PM may be suspended. Book handed to risk desk. Immediate. Orderly unwind begins. Model validation failure (backtesting red) RM → quant team Model review. May increase VaR multiplier. Within 5 business days. Correlated PM exposures detected RM → CRO Factor exposure review across all desks. Potential fund-level limit applied. Same day discussion. ---

"How would you think about portfolio risk beyond VaR?" "VaR gives me one number — the 99th percentile loss under normal conditions — but it's the START of risk analysis, not the end. I'd layer on five additional views. First, Expected Shortfall — what's the average loss in that 1% worst case? And critically, the ES/VaR ratio: if it's above 3, there's a concave payoff hiding in the book — likely short gamma or short tail risk — and VaR is dangerously misleading. Second, factor decomposition — what's actually DRIVING the risk? Two portfolios can have the same VaR but very different factor profiles. One might be diversified across 10 uncorrelated bets. The other might be one momentum bet disguised as 30 positions. The effective number of bets is the real diversification metric. Third, stress testing — how does the portfolio perform under 2008, COVID, or a hypothetical Taiwan scenario? This tests what VaR misses: regime changes, correlation spikes, non-linear payoffs. Fourth, liquidity analysis — can we actually EXIT these positions if we need to? A portfolio that takes 3 weeks to liquidate but faces daily margin calls has a fundamental mismatch. Fifth, and most critically at a multi-PM fund: cross-desk aggregation. Each PM's risk might look fine individually, but if all four desks are loading on the same macro factor, eigenvalue decomposition of the cross-desk correlation matrix will show it. That's the hidden concentration that kills multi-strategy funds." "What's the most underappreciated risk in a multi-PM fund?" "Hidden factor correlation across PMs. Each PM manages their own book and genuinely believes their risk is independent. But when I run factor attribution across ALL desks simultaneously, I often find they're loading on the same two or three macro factors — rates, momentum, risk appetite — without realizing it. In calm markets, this looks like diversification because the idiosyncratic component masks the common factor. In stress, correlations spike and you discover that 'four independent strategies' are actually one big bet. If I do eigenvalue decomposition of the inter-desk correlation matrix and the first eigenvalue explains 60%+ of variance, that fund has one and a half effective bets, not four. The fix is systematic: daily cross-desk factor aggregation, limits on aggregate factor exposure (not just per-desk), and an effective-number-of-bets metric that the CRO monitors. At a fund like Squarepoint with many desks, this aggregation is probably the single highest-value-add function of the risk team." "How would you evaluate whether a PM is generating alpha vs just running factor exposure?" "Multi-factor regression: regress PM returns against market, size, value, momentum, quality, and relevant macro factors. Three things I'm looking for. First, the R² — how much of the return is EXPLAINED by known factors? If R² is 0.85, only 15% of the return is truly idiosyncratic. The PM is mostly delivering factor exposure, which could be replicated

section: WHY THIS IS YOUR MOST IMPORTANT DAILY TASK
content: At Squarepoint, every morning starts with the same question: "Why did we make or lose money yesterday?" The number is not what matters. The DECOMPOSITION is everything. P&L attribution is the bridge between risk measures (VaR, Greeks, factor exposures) and real-world money. It turns abstract risk into concrete dollars. As a Junior Risk Manager, P&L attribution is how you: 1. Diagnose risk — "The $3M gain was 80% vega. The PM thinks they're a stock picker — they're actually a vol trader." 2. Validate models — "Our Greeks predicted −$1.5M. Actual was −$3.2M. The $1.7M unexplained means the model is missing a risk factor." 3. Challenge PMs — "Your Sharpe is 2.0, but 70% of your return is theta collection on short gamma. You're earning premium with hidden tail risk." 4. Detect fraud or error — "Persistent large unexplained P&L = stale marks, wrong Greeks, hidden positions, or something worse." ---

> One-liner: P&L attribution splits yesterday's dollar outcome into the building blocks that caused it — so you know whether the PM was RIGHT about their thesis, LUCKY from market moves, or EXPOSED to a risk they don't understand.

For a portfolio containing options and linear instruments: $$\text{Total P\&L} = \underbrace{\Delta \text{P\&L}} {\text{direction}} + \underbrace{\Gamma \text{P\&L}} {\text{convexity}} + \underbrace{\Theta \text{P\&L}} {\text{time decay}} + \underbrace{\mathcal{V} \text{P\&L}} {\text{vol change}} + \underbrace{\rho \text{P\&L}} {\text{rates}} + \underbrace{\text{Cross/Higher-order}} {\text{vanna, charm, volga}} + \underbrace{\text{Unexplained}} {\text{residual}}$$

$$\Delta \text{P\&L} = \sum i \delta i \times \Delta S i$$ Where $\delta i$ = dollar delta of position i, $\Delta S i$ = price change in underlying i. What it tells you: - This is the "was the PM right about direction?" component - If delta P&L dominates, the PM is running a directional book — their performance is driven by market moves, not skill unless those directional calls are consistently correct - For a market-neutral fund: delta P&L should be small relative to total. If it's consistently large → the fund isn't market-neutral Components within delta: Source Calculation Interpretation --- --- --- Stock/equity delta Position × ΔPrice Direct equity exposure Options delta Σ(contracts × multiplier × Δ option × ΔS) Directional option exposure Futures delta Contracts × multiplier × ΔFutures Futures directional Cross-asset delta FX delta, commodity delta, etc. Non-equity directional

$$\Gamma \text{P\&L} = \frac{1}{2} \sum i \gamma i \times (\Delta S i)^2$$ What it tells you: - Gamma P&L is ALWAYS positive for long gamma positions (regardless of direction — up or down) - Gamma P&L is ALWAYS negative for short gamma positions when moves are large - This is the "insurance payoff" — long gamma pays when markets move big; short gamma pays in quiet markets but bleeds during big moves Key insight for RM: Move size Long gamma P&L Short gamma P&L Who wins --- --- --- --- Small (ΔS < 0.5σ) Small positive Small negative (offset by theta) Short gamma (theta > gamma cost) Medium (ΔS ≈ 1σ) Moderate positive Moderate negative Break-even zone Large (ΔS > 2σ) Large positive (quadratic) Large negative (quadratic) Long gamma dominates The math of why short gamma kills: Gamma P&L scales with $({\Delta S})^2$. Double the move → 4× the gamma P&L. A −3σ day produces 9× the gamma P&L of a −1σ day. Short gamma positions look fine in calm markets and EXPLODE in stress.

section: Theta P&L — The Cost of Time
content: $$\Theta \text{P\&L} = \sum i \theta i \times \Delta t$$ Where $\Delta t$ = 1 calendar day / 365 (or 1 trading day / 252, depending on convention). What it tells you: - Long options → negative theta (paying rent for optionality) - Short options → positive theta (collecting rent, but exposed to gamma/vega) - Theta is the MOST PREDICTABLE component of P&L — you know roughly what it will be before the day starts Daily theta as a P&L constraint: - If theta = −$80K/day, the PM needs to earn $80K/day from delta/gamma/vega just to break even - Over 250 trading days, that's −$20M of theta drag - If realized vol < implied vol → theta OVERPAYS for the gamma you receive → net loss on the options position - If realized vol > implied vol → gamma gains exceed theta cost → net gain Theta-Gamma breakeven: The daily move needed for gamma P&L to offset theta: $$\Delta S {\text{BE}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \theta }{\gamma}}$$ If the underlying moves MORE than this daily, long gamma wins. If it moves LESS, short gamma wins.

$$\mathcal{V} \text{P\&L} = \sum i \nu i \times \Delta \text{IV} i$$ Where $\nu i$ = dollar vega, $\Delta \text{IV} i$ = change in implied volatility. What it tells you: - If vega P&L dominates, the PM is a VOL TRADER whether they know it or not - A PM who "buys options for hedging" but makes all their money from vol expansion is running a vol strategy — they should be evaluated on vol forecasting, not directional views - Vega P&L can swamp delta P&L: a 5-vol-point move in IV on a $50M vega book = $250K, regardless of what the stock did Bucketed vega P&L (important for vol surfaces): Tenor bucket Vega ($K/vol pt) ΔIV Vega P&L --- --- --- --- 1M expiry +$30K +2 pts +$60K 3M expiry +$50K +3 pts +$150K 6M expiry −$20K +4 pts −$80K 1Y expiry +$10K +5 pts +$50K Total vega P&L +$180K The PM is long short-dated vol and short 6M vol — a calendar spread. The attribution reveals this structure.

section: Rho P&L — The Interest Rate Effect
content: $$\rho \text{P\&L} = \sum i \rho i \times \Delta r$$ Usually small for short-dated equity options. Material for: - Long-dated options (LEAPS) - Convertible bonds (embedded option + bond = rate-sensitive) - Fixed income derivatives (pure rate expression) - Dividend swaps and forwards (discount rate matters)

Greek Formula When it matters --- --- --- Vanna P&L $\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial \sigma} \times \Delta S \times \Delta \sigma$ Delta change due to vol change. Large in crash: spot drops + vol spikes = delta shifts. Charm P&L $\frac{\partial \delta}{\partial t} \times \Delta t \times \Delta S$ Delta changes as time passes. Important near expiry — delta becomes more binary. Volga P&L $\frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \sigma^2} \times (\Delta \sigma)^2$ Vega convexity. Matters for large vol moves (10+ points). Speed P&L $\frac{1}{6} \frac{\partial^3 V}{\partial S^3} \times (\Delta S)^3$ Gamma-of-gamma. Matters for very large spot moves. When cross-effects matter: - Normal days: cross-effects < 5% of total P&L. Ignorable. - Crisis days (SPX −5%, VIX +15): cross-effects can be 20-30% of total P&L. Vanna dominates. - 0DTE options: charm effects dominate as delta accelerates toward 0 or 1 through the day.

section: Greek P&L Ratio Diagnostics — The RM's Blood Panel
content: The individual Greek P&L components are useful. The RATIOS between them are diagnostic — they reveal the risk profile the PM is actually running. Ratio / Metric How to compute What it tells you Healthy range Red flag --- --- --- --- --- Vega % of total P&L \ Vega P&L\ / \ Total P&L\ Is the PM a directional trader or a vol trader? <30% for equity PM >50% → PM is trading vol, not stocks Theta % of total P&L \ Theta P&L\ / \ Total P&L\ How much of the return is carry (renting options) vs active? Depends on strategy >70% consistently → short gamma collector Gamma / Theta ratio Cumulative Gamma P&L / Cumulative \ Theta P&L\ Is the PM paying fair rent for gamma protection? >0.8 (realized vol ≥ implied) <0.5 → theta overpaying for gamma received Cumulative Theta / Worst-case tail loss Σ Theta collected / Stress-day gamma+vega loss How many months of theta does one tail event wipe out? >0.3 (tail < 3× theta) <0.1 → earning pennies in front of a steamroller Delta % of total P&L \ Delta P&L\ / \ Total P&L\ Is the PM a directional bettor? Varies by mandate >90% consistently → PM is just riding beta, no edge in convexity or vol Factor P&L / Total P&L Σ factor-attributed P&L / Total P&L Is "alpha" really factor exposure? <50% → genuine alpha >80% → PM delivers factor, not skill Unexplained / Total P&L \ Unexplained\ / \ Total P&L\ Is the model capturing reality? <5% >15% → model is broken, escalate Cross-effects / Total P&L \ Vanna + Charm + Volga\ / \ Total P&L\ Are higher-order risks material? <5% on normal days >20% → large gamma/vega positions in a crisis; model needs full repricing Worked example of ratio diagnosis: > PM's monthly P&L = +$2.4M. Decomposition: Delta = +$600K (25%), Gamma = +$200K (8%), Theta = +$1.8M (75%) , Vega = −$200K. > > Diagnosis: Theta is 75% of total P&L → this PM is a short gamma collector. They're earning $90K/day in theta. On a 3σ day with +10 vol points, gamma + vega loss could be $8M+. The PM has collected $1.8M to risk $8M — a 4.4:1 ratio against them. The Sharpe looks great (theta is smooth), but the P&L distribution is negatively skewed. Action: stress-test the book against a tail event, verify drawdown limits cover the $8M scenario, and flag to CRO. > PM's monthly P&L = +$1.5M. Decomposition: Delta = +$300K (20%), Gamma = −$100K, Vega = +$1.3M (87%) , Theta = $0. > > Diagnosis: Vega is 87% of P&L → this PM is an implied-vol trader. They're long vol and it's been expanding. If vol compresses, they give it all back AND potentially lose on delta too. Action: "Is the vol expansion trade your thesis, or a side-effect of your position structure?" ---

$$\text{Unexplained} = \text{Actual P\&L} - \text{Sum of attributed P\&L}$$ Unexplained Interpretation Action --- --- --- < 2% of total Normal model noise No action 2-5% of total Worth investigating Check prices, marks, missing Greeks 5-15% of total Material model gap Formal investigation. Missing risk factor? Wrong pricing model? > 15% of total Red flag Escalate immediately. Possible: hidden position, stale marks, model error, rogue trading Common causes of unexplained P&L: 1. Stale marks — illiquid OTC positions not re-marked → sudden catch-up creates unexplained jump 2. Wrong Greeks — model calculates Greeks with wrong vol surface, wrong spot, or wrong dividend 3. Missing risk factor — correlation move, basis move, or liquidity premium not in the model 4. Discrete vs continuous — Greeks assume infinitesimal moves; actual moves are discrete → Taylor expansion residual 5. Gap risk — overnight moves not captured by Greeks computed at EOD 6. Dividend/corporate action — ex-div adjustments, spin-offs, special dividends not modeled The P&L explain as model validation: If daily unexplained P&L is consistently 10%+, your RISK MODEL IS WRONG. The VaR, stress tests, and Greeks reports generated by the same model are also wrong. Persistent unexplained P&L is the canary in the coal mine for model risk. ---

> One-liner: Greek-based attribution tells you WHICH INSTRUMENTS drove P&L. Factor-based attribution tells you WHICH BETS drove P&L — market direction, sector rotation, style tilt, or genuine stock selection.

$$R p = \underbrace{\alpha} {\text{skill (residual)}} + \underbrace{\beta {\text{MKT}} \times R {\text{MKT}}} {\text{market}} + \underbrace{\beta {\text{SECT}} \times R {\text{SECT}}} {\text{sector}} + \underbrace{\beta {\text{STYLE}} \times R {\text{STYLE}}} {\text{style factors}} + \underbrace{\epsilon} {\text{idiosyncratic}}$$

How much P&L came from market/sector exposure (beta) vs stock selection (alpha)? Brinson attribution (for long-only): $$\text{Allocation effect} = \sum j (w {p,j} - w {b,j}) \times (R {b,j} - R b)$$ $$\text{Selection effect} = \sum j w {b,j} \times (R {p,j} - R {b,j})$$ $$\text{Interaction effect} = \sum j (w {p,j} - w {b,j}) \times (R {p,j} - R {b,j})$$ Component What it measures Example --- --- --- Allocation Did the PM overweight the right sectors? Overweight tech in a tech rally → positive allocation Selection Within each sector, did the PM pick the right stocks? Within tech, bought NVDA not INTC → positive selection Interaction Combined effect of being in the right sector AND picking the right stock Overweight tech AND best stock in tech → interaction bonus

For a multi-factor model (Barra, Axioma, or internal): $$\text{Factor P\&L} k = \beta {p,k} \times f k \times \text{Portfolio Notional}$$ Where $\beta {p,k}$ = portfolio's loading on factor k, $f k$ = factor return on the day. Worked example — Daily factor P&L: Factor Portfolio β Factor return Factor P&L ($100M fund) --- --- --- --- Market +0.25 −1.2% −$300K Momentum +0.40 +0.8% +$320K Value −0.15 +0.3% −$45K Size (small) +0.10 −0.5% −$50K Rates (DV01=$20K/bp) N/A +3bp −$60K VIX (vega=$50K/pt) N/A +1.5 pts −$75K Total factor −$210K Specific (stock selection) +$450K TOTAL +$240K Diagnosis: "You made $240K. But factor P&L was −$210K — the market went against you, and your momentum/size/rates/vol exposures all dragged. Your ACTUAL stock-picking P&L was +$450K — that's genuine alpha. Today, the factor headwinds masked your skill." Why this matters at Squarepoint: A systematic-at-its-core fund KNOWS the factor exposures. The RM's job is to verify that the alpha component (specific return) is real and persistent, and that the factor exposures are intentional and within limits.

Separate P&L into decisions that require TIMING (active bets) versus returns from simply holding the position (carry/dividend/roll): Component Source Example --- --- --- Carry Coupon income, dividend yield, theta income, roll yield Bond pays 4.5% coupon → daily carry = $12.3K Rolldown Move down the yield curve over time → price appreciation 5Y bond "becomes" 4.9Y bond → gains from curve steepness Market move Changes in yields, spreads, prices Credit spreads widen 5bp → CS01 loss Active trading Intraday/intraweek repositioning — buy/sell decisions PM traded around the position → $X gain from timing For a rates desk specifically: $$\text{P\&L} = \underbrace{\text{Carry}} {\text{coupon}} + \underbrace{\text{Rolldown}} {\text{curve}} + \underbrace{\text{Duration P\&L}} {\text{ΔYield × DV01}} + \underbrace{\text{Curve P\&L}} {\text{KRD moves}} + \underbrace{\text{Spread P\&L}} {\text{ΔCredit × CS01}} + \underbrace{\text{Convexity P\&L}} {\text{½ × Conv × ΔY²}} + \underbrace{\text{Residual}} {\text{unexplained}}$$ ---

Setup: PM runs long-short equity with options overlay. - Long 10,000 shares XOM at $110 (δ = +10,000 shares, $1.1M dollar delta) - Short 5,000 shares CVX at $155 (δ = −5,000 shares, −$775K dollar delta) - Long 50 XOM Jun $115 calls (δ per contract = 0.45, γ = 0.03, θ = −$0.15/day, ν = $0.22/vol pt, each contract = 100 shares) Yesterday's moves: - XOM: $110 → $113 (+$3, +2.7%) - CVX: $155 → $157 (+$2, +1.3%) - XOM IV: 28% → 31% (+3 vol points) Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- Delta — XOM shares +10,000 × $3 +$30,000 Delta — CVX shares −5,000 × $2 −$10,000 Delta — XOM calls 50 × 100 × 0.45 × $3 +$6,750 Gamma — XOM calls 50 × 100 × ½ × 0.03 × $3² +$675 Theta — XOM calls 50 × 100 × (−$0.15) −$750 Vega — XOM calls 50 × 100 × $0.22 × 3 +$3,300 Rho Negligible (short-dated, no rate move) ~$0 Cross-effects Vanna/charm small (moderate move, mid-life option) ~+$50 TOTAL ATTRIBUTED +$30,025 What you tell the PM (30 seconds): "You're up $30K. Main driver: delta — XOM rallied $3, your net long paid off. You also picked up $3.3K from vol expanding on XOM, which offset the $750 theta cost. Gamma added $675. One flag: your effective XOM delta grew via gamma — you're now long ~12,600 share-equivalents of XOM vs 12,250 yesterday. And the XOM-CVX pair spread widened — XOM outperformed CVX by $1 ratio — your 'pair trade' is becoming a directional oil bet." What you flag internally: - Net delta grew automatically (gamma). If XOM rallies another $3, delta grows further → exposure is self-reinforcing - XOM IV jumped 3 pts → is there a catalyst? Earnings? Geopolitical? If vol is pricing an event, gamma exposure changes around the event - The "pair trade" (long XOM / short CVX) isn't hedging well — XOM outperformed by 1.4% → basis risk

Setup: PM runs a curve steepener: long 2Y, short 10Y. - Long $100M 2Y UST, DV01 = $18K/bp - Short $40M 10Y UST, DV01 = $34K/bp - Net DV01 = $18K − $34K = −$16K/bp (net short duration) Yesterday's curve move: - 2Y yield: 4.20% → 4.15% (−5bp) - 10Y yield: 4.50% → 4.55% (+5bp) - Curve steepened: 2s10s spread went from 30bp to 40bp (+10bp) Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- 2Y position $18K/bp × (−5bp) = price UP +$90,000 10Y position −$34K/bp × (+5bp) = price DOWN, you're short → GAIN +$170,000 Carry Net coupon (2Y yield − 10Y yield × position sizes) / 252 +$2,500 Convexity Small — rates move not large enough +$500 TOTAL +$263,000 Factor decomposition: Factor P&L --- --- Parallel rates move (avg = 0bp) ~$0 (parallel was ~0) Curve steepening (+10bp 2s10s) +$260K (the active bet) Carry +$2.5K Convexity + residual +$0.5K What you tell the PM: "You made $263K. Essentially all of it is from the curve steepening — 2s10s went from 30 to 40bp, your steepener paid off.
