Your parallel duration risk was roughly neutral, so you weren't exposed to the level moving — just the shape. Net DV01 is still −$16K/bp, so you'd lose $160K on a 10bp parallel selloff."

section: Scenario 3: Short Gamma — The Quiet Bleed and the Crisis
content: Setup: PM sells 1-month ATM straddles on SPX. - Sold 200 SPX straddles (100 calls + 100 puts) - Total theta collected: +$150K/day - Total gamma exposure: −$500K per 1% SPX move (dollar gamma) - Total vega: −$800K per vol point Day 1-19: calm market (SPX moves ±0.3% daily, IV steady) Day Delta P&L Gamma P&L Theta P&L Vega P&L Total --- --- --- --- --- --- Each calm day ±$15K −$5K +$150K ~$0 ~+$130K/day 19-day total ~$0 (random walk) −$95K +$2,850K ~$0 ~+$2,470K Day 20: SPX drops 4%, VIX jumps 12 points Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- Delta Approximately −$200K (delta was near-flat but gamma creates delta during the move) −$200K Gamma −$500K × (4%)² / (1%)² = −$500K × 16 = −$8,000K Theta +$150K (still collect one day's theta) +$150K Vega −$800K × 12 pts −$9,600K Cross Vanna + Volga (significant in crash + vol spike) −$1,200K Day 20 total −$18,850K Running total: +$2,470K − $18,850K = −$16,380K One bad day wiped out 19 good days and then some. The Sharpe ratio for days 1-19 was stellar (~3.0+). Including day 20, cumulative P&L is deeply negative. This is the prototypical short-gamma/short-vol strategy that P&L attribution reveals. RM lesson: When you see a PM with consistently positive theta, low delta P&L, and near-zero gamma P&L for weeks → they're short gamma/short vol. The P&L attribution is SCREAMING the risk profile. The blowup is a matter of WHEN, not IF. Monitor theta/gamma ratio, stress-test the position against a 3σ + vol-spike day, and ensure the drawdown limits can absorb the tail event. ---

section: PART 4: P&L EXPLAIN — THE MODEL VALIDATION TOOL
content: > One-liner: P&L explain compares what your model PREDICTED the P&L should be (from Greeks and factor sensitivities) against what ACTUALLY happened. The gap is your model error — and persistent gaps mean your risk system is lying to you.

$$\text{Predicted P\&L} = \sum k \text{Sensitivity} k \times \Delta \text{Factor} k$$ $$\text{P\&L Explain Ratio} = \frac{\text{Predicted P\&L}}{\text{Actual P\&L}}$$ Ratio Model quality --- --- 0.95-1.05 Excellent — model captures >95% of P&L drivers 0.80-0.95 Acceptable — small gaps, monitor 0.60-0.80 Poor — significant unexplained. Formal review needed. <0.60 Failed — model is fundamentally wrong

Gap pattern Likely cause Fix --- --- --- Unexplained always POSITIVE Model overestimates losses → conservative Greeks Check vol surface calibration. Are Greeks computed with correct IV? Unexplained always NEGATIVE Model underestimates losses → DANGEROUS Missing risk factor. Possibly: correlation move, basis shift, liquidity premium. Most concerning direction. Unexplained spikes on high-vol days Gamma/convexity not captured correctly Taylor expansion truncation. Need higher-order terms or full repricing. Unexplained on specific names Wrong Greeks for those positions Pricing model issue: wrong dividend assumptions, wrong borrow rate, exercise style mismatch. Unexplained on OTC positions Mark uncertainty OTC marks are dealer quotes or models — may differ from actual liquidation value.

Asset class Key sensitivities to explain Common unexplained sources --- --- --- Equities Delta, beta, sector exposure Corporate actions (splits, dividends), short borrow costs Equity options Delta, gamma, theta, vega, vanna, charm Vol surface move (skew/term structure rotation), discrete dividends Rates / FI DV01, KRD, convexity, carry, rolldown Curve shape changes (butterfly, twist), repo rate changes Credit CS01, JTD, recovery rate, carry Rating transitions, recovery assumption changes, CDS-bond basis FX Spot delta, forward points, vol Central bank intervention, cross-currency basis Commodities Spot delta, roll yield, basis, storage Physical delivery dynamics, weather, transportation constraints ---

section: PART 5: COMMON P&L PATTERNS THE RM MUST RECOGNIZE
content: > One-liner: Certain P&L signatures are diagnostic — like a doctor reading an ECG, the RM reads daily P&L to diagnose the risk the PM is actually taking.

Signature: PM buys options "for hedging" but 60%+ of monthly P&L is from vega, not delta. Diagnosis: They're not hedging — they're running a vol book they don't recognize as a vol book. Their delta views may be wrong, but they're making money because vol is expanding. Risk: When vol COMPRESSES, they'll lose on vega AND potentially on their wrong delta view simultaneously. Double hit. RM action: "80% of your month's return came from vol expansion. Is that the trade you intended? If vol hadn't moved, you'd be down $X from delta alone."

Signature: Consistently negative daily attribution from theta: −$80K/day. Monthly cost: −$1.6M. Diagnosis: PM holds long options. The carry cost is significant. They need delta/gamma/vega to earn $1.6M/month just to BREAK EVEN. Key question: Is realized vol higher than implied vol? If yes → gamma gains offset theta → option position is well-priced. If no → PM is paying above fair value for the optionality. RM action: "Your option book costs $80K/day in theta. SPX would need to move ±$X daily for your gamma to offset this. Over the last 20 days, actual daily moves averaged $Y. You're above/below breakeven."

Signature: Daily P&L: +$100K, +$110K, +$95K, +$105K... then suddenly −$2M. Small consistent gains followed by sharp losses. Diagnosis: Classic short gamma/short vol profile. Theta income is steady and positive. Gamma P&L is slightly negative on normal days, catastrophically negative on big-move days. The give-away metrics: - Sharpe looks excellent (2.0+) before the blowup - Theta P&L is consistently +$100K/day → PM is SHORT options - Gamma P&L is −$5K/day on calm days → tolerable - BUT tail gamma P&L on a 3σ day: −$3M+ RM action: Track the cumulative theta collected vs worst-case gamma/vega loss. "You've collected $1.5M in theta YTD. A 4% SPX drop with +15 vol points costs you $18M. Your risk/reward on this position is asymmetric — you're earning $1.5M to risk $18M."

Signature: Unexplained P&L is 2% of total in month 1, 5% in month 2, 12% in month 3. Diagnosis: Progressive model deterioration. Either: - Market regime changed and the model hasn't adapted (stale parameters) - PM is adding positions/instruments the model doesn't cover - Data feed degradation (stale prices, missing marks) RM action: Escalate. Formally document the trend. Request quant/model review.

Signature: PM's gross alpha is +$2M/month. Factor attribution shows: momentum factor contributed +$1.8M. Genuine alpha = +$200K. Diagnosis: The PM is charging alpha fees for delivering momentum exposure. A momentum ETF would deliver roughly the same return at 1/10th the cost. RM action: Not a risk issue per se — but the CIO/allocators should know. "Your returns decompose as 90% momentum factor, 10% idiosyncratic alpha. Your information ratio on the alpha component alone is 0.15."

Signature: PM runs a hedged book (long stock, short index). Delta P&L is small. But basis P&L (stock vs index spread) is growing — sometimes positive, sometimes negative, but increasingly volatile. Diagnosis: The hedge is drifting. The stock and the index are becoming less correlated, or the hedge ratio is stale. RM action: "Your hedge effectiveness (R²) was 0.93 last month, now 0.78. The basis between your position and hedge has widened. You may need to adjust the hedge ratio or find a better instrument." ---

section: PART 6: TALKING TO PMs — THE 30-SECOND BRIEF
content: > One-liner: The PM wants to know three things: Am I up or down? Why? What changed in my risk? Deliver all three in 30 seconds or less.

> " PM , you're up/down $X today. Main driver: delta on NAME moving $Y / vol expansion-compression / theta / curve steepener-flattener . Your key exposure metric is now $Z , up/from/unchanged vs yesterday. One flag: specific risk — growing position, approaching limit, upcoming catalyst, basis drift, correlation shift ."

What they ask What they mean What to say --- --- --- "Was I right?" Did my thesis work? Focus on delta P&L for their key position "Where did the money come from?" attribution by Greek/factor "60% delta, 25% vega, 15% theta" "Am I bigger?" Did risk grow? Report effective delta, gamma, VaR change "What should I watch?" Upcoming risks to my position Catalyst, event, approaching limit, correlation shift "Is my hedge working?" Basis risk check Report hedge ratio, correlation, basis P&L separately

- A 10-minute lecture on second-order Greeks - Being told they're wrong (approach: ask questions, don't accuse) - Math they didn't ask for (save the derivations for the quant meeting) - Caveats without conclusions ("it could be this OR it could be that" → pick one and explain why)

Instead of Say --- --- "Your position is too risky" "Your effective delta has grown 30% via gamma. Was that intentional, or should we look at rebalancing?" "You're bleeding theta" "The options book is costing $80K/day. What's the thesis for continuing to hold?" "You're just running momentum" "Factor attribution shows 85% of your month's alpha is from momentum. What's your view on the sustainability of the momentum factor?" "Your unexplained is too high" "We're seeing 12% unexplained on this book. I want to make sure we're capturing all the risk — can we walk through the positions?" ---
> At a fund like Squarepoint, P&L attribution is NOT a human exercise done on a spreadsheet. It's an automated pipeline that runs every night and produces reports by morning.

EOD MARKET DATA (prices, vols, curves) │ ▼ POSITION DATA (from OMS/PMS) │ ▼ RISK ENGINE (compute Greeks, factor exposures) │ ▼ YESTERDAY'S Greeks × TODAY'S factor moves = PREDICTED P&L │ ▼ COMPARE to ACTUAL P&L (from prime broker/custodian) │ ▼ ATTRIBUTION REPORT (by Greek, by factor, by desk, by PM) │ ▼ UNEXPLAINED P&L REPORT → flags if > threshold │ ▼ RM DASHBOARD → morning meeting

Check What it catches Frequency --- --- --- Price validation Stale prices, outlier marks, failed feeds Every EOD run Position reconciliation PM system vs prime broker mismatch (trades not booked, wrong quantities) Daily, automated Greek validation Greeks that are outliers vs historical (e.g., delta >1.0, negative gamma for a long call) Every EOD Corporate action check Ex-div dates, splits, spin-offs that change position economics Daily, semi-automated New position check Newly opened positions that may not have Greeks computed yet Daily

The same P&L gets sliced multiple ways: Cut What it shows Who uses it --- --- --- By position Which specific names/instruments made or lost money PM By Greek Delta vs gamma vs theta vs vega PM + RM By factor Market, sector, style, macro factor contributions RM + CIO By desk Each desk's contribution to fund-level P&L CRO + CIO By strategy Performance of each strategy within a desk PM + RM Alpha vs factor Genuine skill vs systematic exposure CIO (for compensation, allocation) Carry vs active Passive income vs active trading P&L RM (carry is predictable, active is not) ---

section: PART 8: P&L ATTRIBUTION AS A FRAUD DETECTION TOOL
content: > One-liner: Every major rogue trading scandal has one thing in common — the P&L didn't match the risk. If the P&L attribution had been taken seriously, the fraud would have been caught earlier.

Event What happened What P&L attribution would have shown --- --- --- Barings (1995) Nick Leeson hid losses in error account 88888. Reported profits from "arbitrage" that required no risk. P&L showed profit but NO CORRESPONDING RISK EXPOSURES. Attribution would show: "You're making $X/day but your Greeks are zero — where is this coming from?" SocGen (2008) Kerviel built massive long equity futures (~€50B). Hedged with fictitious offsetting trades. Delta P&L on real positions was enormous. The "hedge" P&L was fabricated. Position reconciliation between PM system and exchange would have caught the mismatch. London Whale (2012) Bruno Iksil at JPM accumulated massive CDS positions. Marked them at mid-market (favorable) while actual liquidation prices were much worse. Reported P&L (at model marks) vs theoretical P&L (from Greeks × market moves) had growing divergence. The MODEL was being manipulated — marks were moved to minimize apparent losses. Archegos (2021) Total return swaps to avoid 13F disclosure. Multiple PBs didn't know about each other's exposure. At the PB level, each PB would see that P&L attribution on single-name swaps was dominated by a few names. Concentration risk was visible within each PB's book — they just didn't aggregate across PBs.

Red flag What it might mean --- --- Profit with no risk Fabricated trades or mismarked positions Large unexplained P&L that always goes ONE direction (positive) Marks being smoothed in the PM's favor Greeks show big positions but P&L is suspiciously flat Offsetting fictional trades or unauthorized loss-hiding P&L is dominated by ONE illiquid position with no observable market price Mark manipulation on OTC/illiquid instruments P&L explain ratio consistently < 0.70 on specific positions Those positions aren't what they appear to be ---

In a discretionary multi-asset fund (like Squarepoint's discretionary team), a single PM might have: - Equity positions (delta P&L) - Options overlay (gamma, theta, vega P&L) - Rate hedges (DV01 P&L) - FX exposure (spot delta + forward points P&L) - Commodity trades (spot delta + roll P&L) The attribution must capture the CROSS-ASSET INTERACTION: - "SPX dropped 3% and your equity delta lost $1.5M. But your SPX put hedge gained $800K (delta + gamma + vega). Your rate hedge gained $200K (flight to quality). Your commodity short gained $100K (risk-off). Net: −$400K — the PM was RIGHT to have the hedge structure." Without the integrated multi-asset attribution, each asset class would show a different story, and the PM's actual risk management (the hedge structure) would be invisible.

EOD attribution is the standard. But for significant intraday moves (SPX opens −2%, or a name gaps on news), intraday attribution can be critical: - What time did the loss occur? - Was the PM in the position before the move, or did they trade INTO the loss? - Did the PM's hedges respond in real-time? - Was the loss from market impact (trading into an illiquid market)? Intraday attribution data sources: - Trade timestamps from execution management system - Intraday price snapshots (typically every minute or 5 minutes) - Greeks at each snapshot

Daily attribution doesn't tell the whole story. Aggregate over: Horizon What it reveals --- --- Daily Immediate diagnosis. What just happened? Weekly Short-term P&L trends. Is theta dominating? Is factor exposure drifting? Monthly Strategy evaluation. Is the PM's stated thesis matching their P&L source? Quarterly Performance review. Alpha vs factor. Sharpe on alpha component. Annual Compensation review. Genuine value-add. Cumulative factor P&L chart: Plot cumulative alpha, cumulative factor contributions, and cumulative total P&L over time. If cumulative alpha is flat while total P&L rises → the PM is delivering factor, not alpha. ---

"How would you explain a PM's daily P&L?" "I'd decompose it along two dimensions. First, Greek-based: delta tells me if the directional bet worked, gamma shows convexity payoff — critical for understanding how exposure changes with market moves — theta shows the time decay cost, and vega shows the impact of vol changes. Each Greek isolates a different source of risk. Second, factor-based: I'd attribute the return to market beta, sector, style factors like momentum or value, and macro factors like rates and vol. What's left after removing all factor exposure is genuine alpha — the PM's actual skill. The attribution isn't just accounting — it's diagnostic. If a PM thinks they're trading direction but 60% of P&L is from vega, they're actually a vol trader. If a PM claims alpha but factor attribution shows 90% momentum exposure, they're selling factor at alpha prices. And if unexplained P&L is consistently large, it means our risk model is missing a factor or — worse — someone is manipulating marks. At a multi-PM fund, the highest-value use is cross-desk attribution: are all four desks making money from the same factor? Because then the 'diversified' fund isn't diversified — it's four levered bets on one factor." "What would you do if unexplained P&L keeps growing?" "I'd treat persistent unexplained P&L as a model risk red flag with escalating severity. First, quantify: is it 2% of total P&L (noise) or 15% (broken model)? Is it consistently one direction (systematic bias) or random (missing risk factor)? Then diagnose: check data quality — stale prices, missed corporate actions, position mismatches between PM system and prime broker. Check model calibration — are Greeks computed with the correct vol surface, correct dividend assumptions, correct repo rates? Check for missing risk factors — is there a correlation move, basis shift, or liquidity premium change the model doesn't capture? If the gap persists after data and calibration checks, escalate formally. A model that can't explain its own P&L is a model whose VaR, stress tests, and Greeks outputs should not be trusted. The P&L explain is the canary in the coal mine — it tells you the entire risk framework is degraded before the tail event that proves it." "How do you distinguish a PM's alpha from factor exposure?" "Multi-factor regression: regress daily PM returns against market, size, value, momentum, quality, rates, and sector factors. Three outputs I focus on. The R² tells me how much is explained by known factors — if it's 0.90, only 10% is idiosyncratic. The intercept (alpha) tells me whether there's systematic outperformance after removing factor exposure — I need a t-stat above 2.0 with at least 3-4 years of data for statistical significance. And the factor loadings tell me whether the PM's ACTUAL exposures match their STATED strategy — a PM claiming 'stock picking' with β momentum of 0.7 is running a momentum strategy. I'd also plot cumulative factor P&L vs cumulative alpha P&L over time.

section: WHY HEDGING IS THE RM'S HIGHEST-VALUE SKILL
content: At Squarepoint, PMs are paid to take risk. The RM's job is NOT to eliminate risk — it's to ensure risk is INTENTIONAL, SIZED CORRECTLY, and SURVIVABLE. Hedging is the mechanism that converts "we're exposed and hoping" into "we're exposed and protected at defined cost." The RM adds value in three ways: 1. Making hidden costs explicit — "This collar is 'free' but you're giving up $3M of potential upside. Is that the trade?" 2. Challenging false hedges — "Your SPX put covers 7% of a 15% loss on your tech-heavy book. You're 53% hedged. Is that acceptable?" 3. Timing the hedge — "Vol is at the 15th percentile. Protection is cheap. NOW is when to buy — not after the sell-off when VIX is 35 and puts cost 3×." ---

section: PART 1: THE FIVE TRADEOFFS — EVERY HEDGE HAS A PRICE
content: > One-liner: There is no free hedge. Every form of protection extracts payment in some currency — premium, upside, basis risk, complexity, or illiquidity. The RM's job is to make the currency explicit. Tradeoff The tension What the PM needs to decide --- --- --- Cost vs protection Better protection = more premium. Deeper OTM = cheaper but further from current price. "How much annual drag can you absorb in your Sharpe?" Precision vs liquidity Single-name put = perfect hedge but expensive/illiquid. Index put = liquid/cheap but basis risk. "Do you need exact coverage or directional protection?" Horizon mismatch Position has no expiry. Hedge expires. Roll costs compound. "Are you hedging an event (1 week) or a regime (6 months)?" Upside surrender Collars and call overwriting cap gains. Zero-cost structures mean the cost IS the cap. "What maximum return would you accept in exchange for zero premium?" Timing Cheapest when you don't need it (low vol). Most expensive when you do (crisis vol). "Are you willing to pay insurance when the sun is shining?" The cost of NOT hedging: If you don't hedge… What happens when the tail hits --- --- No puts, no tail protection Full drawdown. Potential forced liquidation. Career risk. No basis-adjusted hedge Hedge covers 50% of the loss. You thought you were protected. You weren't. No roll plan Hedge expires unnoticed. Position runs unprotected for days/weeks. ---
> One-liner: Before picking an instrument, diagnose the disease. A put doesn't help if the risk is basis. A futures hedge doesn't help if the risk is convexity. Match the hedge to the SPECIFIC risk.

section: 1. What SPECIFIC risk am I hedging?
content: Risk type The exposure Right hedge family Wrong hedge --- --- --- --- Directional (delta) Stock goes down Puts, futures, shorts VIX calls (wrong risk) Volatility (vega) IV drops, long options lose value Sell vol, vega-neutral structures Equity futures (wrong Greek) Convexity (gamma) Short gamma: large moves kill you Buy options (go long gamma) Futures (linear, no convexity) Rate risk (DV01) Yields rise, bond portfolio loses Rate futures, IRS Equity puts (wrong asset) Credit (CS01) Spreads widen CDS, short HY ETF Treasury hedge (misses credit) Concentration 15% of NAV in one name Single-name puts, partial exit Index hedge (basis risk) Tail / left-tail 5σ event Deep OTM puts, VIX calls ATM puts (expensive for tail defense) Basis Hedge and position diverge Better correlated instrument More of the same wrong hedge Liquidity Can't exit in stress Cash buffer, fewer illiquid positions No derivative fixes this

Horizon Use case Instruments Roll frequency --- --- --- --- 1-5 days Earnings, FOMC, event Weekly options, overnight futures None — expires 1-4 weeks Near-term uncertainty Monthly puts/collars None or once 1-6 months Medium-term thesis adjustment Quarterly options, futures strips Roll quarterly 6M-permanent Structural portfolio protection LEAPs, rolling put spreads, tail program Roll semi-annually

section: 3. How much upside am I willing to surrender?
content: Upside tolerance Structure Cost --- --- --- Surrender nothing Outright puts Full premium (1-3% annualized for ATM) Cap at +X% Collar (buy put, sell call at +X%) Reduced or zero net premium Cap protection at −Y% Put spread (buy put, sell lower put) ~50% of outright put cost Both cap upside AND cap protection Collar + put spread combo Near-zero cost, bounded up and down

Rule of thumb for hedge cost relative to expected alpha: Strategy alpha Max hedge cost (% of NAV/year) Rationale --- --- --- 2-4% 0.3-0.5% Hedge can't eat >15% of alpha 5-8% 0.5-1.5% Room for meaningful protection 10%+ 1.5-3.0% Can afford genuine tail protection If premium budget = $0: The PM is choosing between (a) upside surrender (collar), (b) accepting basis risk (imperfect hedge), or (c) no hedge at all. There is no fourth option. Make this explicit.

section: 5. What basis risk am I accepting?
content: Basis risk hierarchy — from best to worst hedge precision: Hedge instrument Basis risk Cost --- --- --- Single-name put on exact position Zero Most expensive Sector ETF put (XLE for XOM) Low-moderate Less expensive Index put (SPX for any equity) Moderate-high Cheapest, most liquid Cross-asset (TLT for equity) High (correlation-dependent) Variable Quantifying basis risk: $$\text{Hedge effectiveness} = R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{Var(hedged portfolio)}}{\text{Var(unhedged portfolio)}}$$ $$\text{Optimal hedge ratio} = h^ = \rho \times \frac{\sigma {\text{position}}}{\sigma {\text{hedge}}}$$ If ρ = 0.75 between XOM and XLE, optimal hedge ratio = 0.75 × (σ XOM/σ XLE), NOT 1:1. ---

What: Buy put options on the asset or index. Pure downside protection with defined cost. $$\text{Put cost} = f(\text{strike, time, IV, rates, dividends})$$ Structure: P&L │ ╱ Unhedged │ ╱ │ ╱ ──┼────╱──────────── Stock price │ ╱ ╱ Hedged (long stock + put) │ ╱ ╱ │ ╱ ╱ │╱ ╱ ← Floor at strike − premium │ Worked example — SPX at 5,400: - Buy SPX 5,300 put (1.9% OTM), 3-month expiry, IV = 16% - Cost: ~$45 per contract (~$4,500 per contract at 100 multiplier) - 100 contracts protecting $54M of equity → cost = $450K = 0.83% of protected notional (annualized ~3.3%) - Protection: below 5,300, every $1 of SPX drop → $100 per contract recovered (100 contracts = $10K per point) - Maximum loss on protected portfolio: (5,400 − 5,300) × $100 × 100 + $450K premium = $1,450K instead of unlimited Pros Cons --- --- Clean — defined cost, no upside surrender Expensive — theta bleeds every day Works through gaps (overnight, weekend) Need to roll repeatedly → compound cost Asymmetric: defined loss, unlimited gain If vol is high when you buy → overpaying No margin requirement (you're buying) Premium is sunk — lost even if market rallies When to use: Event protection (earnings, FOMC), known-horizon hedges, when PM refuses to cap upside.

What: Buy a put at strike K₁ + sell a put at lower strike K₂. Protection between K₁ and K₂ only. P&L │ │ ╱ Unhedged │ ╱ ──┼──────╱────────────── Stock price │ ╱ ╱ Hedged (put spread) │ ╱ ╱ │ ╱ ╱ ← Capped protection │ ╱ (exposed below K₂) │ ╱ Worked example — SPX at 5,400: - Buy SPX 5,300 put ($45), sell SPX 5,100 put ($20). Net cost = $25 per contract ($2,500). - Protection: 200 points between 5,300 and 5,100 = $20K per contract - Max payout: $20K − $2.5K = $17.5K per contract (7:1 payoff ratio) - Below 5,100: no additional protection — you're exposed again - Cost: 55% cheaper than outright put Pros Cons --- --- ~50% cheaper than outright put Capped protection — exposed in a crash Still defined-cost, known max loss If SPX drops 15%, the 200-point coverage isn't enough Good for "normal correction" hedging Not a tail hedge — doesn't protect against the worst scenarios When to use: Budget-constrained, hedging against 3-5% corrections, when the PM believes >10% drops are unlikely or acceptable. RM judgment call: "A put spread protects you in a correction but NOT in a crash. If SPX drops 20%, you're covered for the first 200 points and naked for the next 880. Is that what you want?"

What: Long stock + buy OTM put + sell OTM call. Call premium finances the put. Payoff │ ← Capped at call strike │ ╱ │ ╱ ──┼─────────╱────────── Stock price │ ╱ │ ╱ ← Floor at put strike │ Worked example — XOM at $113: - Buy $108 put ($3.20), sell $120 call ($2.80). Net cost = $0.40 (nearly zero-cost) - Below $108: hedged. Max loss = $5.40/share ($113 − $108 + $0.40 premium) - Above $120: capped. Even if XOM → $150, you get $120 - Between $108-$120: normal exposure minus the $0.40 cost Zero-cost collar: Adjust strikes until put cost = call premium. E.g., buy $107 put, sell $119 call → exactly zero cost. The cost is ENTIRELY in upside surrender. Pros Cons --- --- Near-zero or zero premium cost Caps upside — this IS the cost, just in a different currency PM budget-friendly If the stock rallies through the call strike → PM is frustrated and blames RM Clear bounded outcome (min and max returns) In trending markets, the collar is a drag on performance When to use: PM says "I don't want to pay for protection." Collar makes the tradeoff explicit: protection costs upside, not premium. RM warning: "A 'free' collar isn't free — you're paying with the gain between $120 and wherever XOM would have gone. If XOM rallies to $130, this collar cost you $10/share. That's $500K on 50,000 shares."

What: Sell index or sector futures to reduce portfolio delta/beta. Linear instrument — no convexity. Beta hedge sizing: $$N {\text{contracts}} = \frac{V p \times (\beta {\text{current}} - \beta {\text{target}})}{V f}$$ Where $V p$ = portfolio value, $V f$ = futures notional per contract. Worked example: - Portfolio: $80M, β = 1.3. Target: β = 0.5. Reduction: 0.8 - ES futures (E-mini SPX): SPX at 5,400 → $50 × 5,400 = $270K per contract - Contracts = ($80M × 0.8) / $270K = 237 contracts short - Cost: margin only (~$15K per contract initial margin = $3.6M). No premium. DV01 hedging (for rates): $$N {\text{contracts}} = \frac{\text{Portfolio DV01}}{\text{Futures DV01 per contract}}$$ Example: Portfolio DV01 = $50K/bp. 10Y Treasury futures DV01 = $75/bp per contract. Contracts = $50K / $75 = 667 contracts short Pros Cons --- --- Most liquid instruments on earth (ES, ZN, ZB) Linear : no floor — you give up ALL downside AND upside No premium — cost is only margin + roll You can still lose unlimited on the hedged portfolio if basis moves Instant execution, tight spreads Margin drain — uses capital that could be deployed elsewhere Easy to size precisely Futures have expiry → roll risk (quarterly for equity, monthly for some) When to use: Temporary delta reduction (overnight, over weekend), beta hedging, rate duration hedging, quick risk-off. When NOT to use: As a substitute for options when convexity matters. Futures don't protect against GAP risk — if SPX opens −5%, your delta hedge was computed at yesterday's close and you're already behind.

What: Long one asset + short a correlated asset. Isolate the idiosyncratic (alpha) bet, hedge the systematic (factor) exposure. Hedge ratio for a pair: $$h^ = \frac{\text{Cov}(R {\text{long}}, R {\text{short}})}{\text{Var}(R {\text{short}})} = \beta {\text{long vs short}}$$ Worked example — Long XOM / Short CVX (oil pair): - Long $10M XOM, β XOM vs CVX = 0.85 - Hedge: Short $8.5M CVX (= $10M × 0.85) - The pair is MARKET-NEUTRAL on oil — moves in crude affect both positions similarly - Alpha bet: XOM will outperform CVX (better management, better assets, etc.) - $1.5M of "unhedged" residual = the idiosyncratic bet the PM is making Risk What goes wrong How to mitigate --- --- --- Basis risk XOM and CVX diverge for non-fundamental reasons (index reconstitution, ETF flows) Monitor rolling correlation. If ρ drops below 0.6, hedge ratio is stale. Short borrow CVX becomes hard-to-borrow → borrow cost spikes or shares recalled Check utilization rate before entering. Diversify across PBs. Crowding Every L/S fund runs the same pair → synchronized unwind Check 13F overlap. High overlap = crowded. Leverage L/S pairs use 2× capital (long + short) for gross exposure Size by NET risk, not gross dollars.

What: Hedge one asset class's risk using a DIFFERENT asset class, exploiting cross-asset correlations. Risk being hedged Cross-asset hedge Mechanism Correlation regime --- --- --- --- Equity sell-off Long UST (TLT, ZN, ZB) Flight to quality: stocks ↓ → bonds ↑ Usually works. FAILED in 2022 (stocks-bonds both fell). Equity sell-off Long gold (GLD, GC futures) Safe haven — uncorrelated in moderate stress Works in geopolitical crisis. May fail if real rates dominate. Equity crash Long VIX calls / short VIX puts VIX spikes in crashes — convex hedge Reliable in acute crashes. Expensive to carry (contango). Inflation surge Long TIPS / commodities Direct inflation sensitivity Works for unexpected inflation. Not for expected (already priced). Dollar weakness Long non-USD assets / gold Inverse USD correlation Generally reliable but unstable. China/EM contagion Long put on FXI, short AUD AUD proxies China risk via trade linkage Moderate. AUD-CHN correlation can break. CRITICAL WARNING — Correlation flips: Regime Stocks-bonds ρ Strategy implication --- --- --- Deflation fear / growth scare (1998-2021) −0.30 Bonds are a great equity hedge Inflation regime (2022-2023) +0.40 Bonds AMPLIFY equity losses — hedge becomes additional risk Financial stress (March 2020 briefly) +0.50 (both sold) Everything correlates to 1 — only cash protects RM responsibility: Monitor the stocks-bonds correlation WEEKLY. If it's been positive for >3 months, any portfolio relying on Treasury duration as an equity hedge is structurally broken. Alert the CRO.
What: Deep OTM protection designed to pay off ONLY in extreme scenarios (>3σ events). The insurance policy for the fund's survival.

Structure How it works Cost (annualized) Crisis payoff Complexity --- --- --- --- --- Deep OTM puts (5-10% OTM, 3M) Simple floor 1.5-3.0% 5-20× Low Put ratio spread (buy 1 ATM, sell 2-3 OTM) Cheaper entry, magnified protection in moderate drop, but creates naked short below lower strikes 0.3-0.8% 3-8× (then reverses!) Medium — DANGEROUS if too far OTM strikes breached Put ladder (buy at 5%, 10%, 15% OTM) Layered protection across severity levels 2.0-4.0% Covers full crash spectrum Medium VIX call spreads (buy 30 strike, sell 50 strike) Convex payoff in panic. Cheap carry. 0.5-1.5% 3-10× Medium Variance swap (long var) Pure vol bet. Convex: payoff scales with vol² Carry cost Enormous in crashes High SPX/VIX call ratio Buy 2 VIX calls + sell 1 SPX put to finance ~0% 4-8× in vol spike High Systematic tail program Rolling 3M 5% OTM puts, rebalanced monthly 1.5-2.5% Consistent 10-15× Low (if automated)

Expected value framework: $$\text{EV} = P(\text{tail event}) \times \text{Hedge payoff} - (1 - P(\text{tail event})) \times \text{Annual cost}$$ Example: - Annual cost of tail program: $3M (1.5% of $200M fund) - Probability of 3σ+ event per year: ~5% (historically) - Hedge payoff in 3σ event: $60M (10× cost, protecting against $80M+ drawdown) - EV = 5% × $60M − 95% × $3M = $3.0M − $2.85M = +$0.15M → slightly positive expected value - PLUS: the hedge prevents forced liquidation, keeps PMs in seats, preserves investor relationships → unquantifiable survival value The RM's pitch to the CIO: "The tail hedge costs us ~40bp of Sharpe annually. But it prevents the −30% drawdown that would trigger $100M of redemptions, cost two PMs their jobs, and take 3 years to recover from. The math is almost break-even on direct EV, but the survival value is enormous." ---

section: PART 4: DYNAMIC HEDGING — ADJUSTING AS THE MARKET MOVES
content: > One-liner: Static hedging is "set and forget." Dynamic hedging is "adjust as conditions change." Both have costs — dynamic hedging costs execution/slippage; static hedging costs missed opportunities to optimize.

section: Delta Hedging — The Core Dynamic Strategy
content: Continuous delta hedging (Black-Scholes ideal): - Sell $Δ of stock for every 1 long call (or vice versa) - As spot moves → Δ changes → rebalance - Long gamma: sell as market rises, buy as it falls → BUY LOW, SELL HIGH - Short gamma: buy as market rises, sell as it falls → BUY HIGH, SELL LOW (adverse selection) The gamma-theta tradeoff in delta hedging: $$\text{P\&L} {\text{delta-hedged}} = \frac{1}{2} \Gamma (\Delta S)^2 - \Theta \Delta t$$ If the underlying moves MORE than the gamma-theta breakeven → long gamma wins. If it moves LESS → short gamma wins (collects theta). Breakeven daily move: $$\Delta S {\text{BE}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Theta }{\Gamma}} = \sigma {\text{implied}} \times S \times \sqrt{\Delta t}$$ This is exactly the implied vol translated to a dollar move. If you're delta-hedging a long option position, you're betting that REALIZED vol will exceed IMPLIED vol. That's the entire game of gamma scalping.

Trigger type Rule When to use Pros Cons --- --- --- --- --- Time-based Rebalance every N hours/days Routine, predictable Easy to implement May miss sharp intraday moves Delta-band Rebalance when Δ drift > $X or X% Responsive to big moves More trades in high-vol (expensive) VaR trigger Rebalance when VaR exceeds Y% of limit Risk-based — responds to true risk Tighter control Requires real-time VaR computation Event-based Rebalance before/after earnings, FOMC, data releases Proactive risk management Targeted Doesn't help with surprise events Drawdown-based Add protection when DD > Z% of soft limit Capital-preserving Prevents spiral Reactive — may be too late in fast market

As the underlying moves, your hedge Greeks change — especially if you're hedging with options: Move What happens to your hedge Action needed --- --- --- Stock rallies 5% Put moves further OTM → delta ↓, gamma ↓, vega ↓ Put becomes less effective. Consider rolling up the strike. Stock drops 5% Put moves closer to ATM → delta ↑, gamma ↑ Put becomes MORE effective. Good. May want to take profit and re-establish at new lower strike. Vol spikes 10 pts Put value increases (good for holder). Delta increases. Hedge ratio changed. May need to sell some futures delta because put now covers more. Time passes (1 month) Theta decays. Gamma concentrates near strike. Vega ↓. Less time, less protection. Roll decision approaching. Monitoring intensifies.

Feature Stop-loss Put option --- --- --- Cost Free (no premium) Premium ($) Gap protection None — stock can open below stop Full — put pays through gaps Whipsaw risk High — triggered then market reverses None — put is always in force until expiry Certainty of execution Uncertain in illiquid names Guaranteed at expiry Unlimited re-entry Yes (re-enter for free) Need to buy new put Max loss guarantee No (gap risk) Yes (strike − spot + premium) Best use case Liquid names, slow markets Illiquid names, event risk, overnight risk, gap risk RM guidance: "Use stops for liquid, unlevered positions where gaps are unlikely. Use options for concentrated, leveraged, or illiquid positions where a gap could breach the drawdown limit." ---

section: PART 5: HEDGE GREEKS — HOW YOUR HEDGE CHANGES YOUR PORTFOLIO RISK
content: > One-liner: Adding a hedge doesn't just reduce risk — it changes your ENTIRE Greek profile. A put doesn't just add delta protection — it adds gamma, theta, vega, and cross-Greeks that interact with your existing book.

Hedge Δ (delta) Γ (gamma) Θ (theta) ν (vega) Net effect on portfolio --- --- --- --- --- --- Long put Negative (reduces net Δ) Positive (adds convexity) Negative (costs theta) Positive (benefits from vol spike) Safer book. Costs carry. More convex. Put spread Negative (moderate) Mixed (long near + short far) Less negative than outright Mixed (partial vega) Cheaper. Less convex. Capped protection. Collar Negative (put) + Positive (short call) ≈ modest net Gamma ≈ 0 (long and short cancel) ~Zero (theta of put ≈ theta of call) ~Zero Budget-neutral. Bounded outcomes. Near-zero Greek change. Short futures Negative (reduces net Δ) Zero Zero Zero Pure delta. No convexity benefit. No cost. VIX calls Near-zero directly Near-zero on SPX Negative (theta cost) Positive (benefits from VIX rise) Vol hedge, not delta hedge. Expensive carry. Short equity (pair) Negative on short leg Zero (equity is linear) Zero Zero Reduces net delta. Introduces basis risk + borrow cost. Key insight: A PM who hedges with INDEX FUTURES gets delta reduction but NO GAMMA. In a gap-down, the futures hedge responds linearly while the portfolio may gap nonlinearly (concentrated names, options). A PM who hedges with PUTS gets delta + gamma + vega — the hedge IMPROVES as the crash deepens (gamma kicks in, vega benefits from vol spike). This is why puts cost more — you're buying convexity that futures don't provide.

Scenario SPX −5% SPX −10% SPX −20% --- --- --- --- No hedge −$4.0M −$8.0M −$16.0M Futures hedge (50%) −$2.0M −$4.0M −$8.0M ATM put −$1.5M −$1.0M +$2.0M (put in the money, vega spike helps) 5% OTM put spread (200pt wide) −$3.5M −$2.0M −$6.0M (protection capped at 200 pts) Collar −$2.2M −$1.8M −$1.8M (floor at put strike) The convexity payoff is visible: The ATM put MAKES money in a crash because gamma acceleration + vega spike exceed the initial theta cost. Futures just reduce exposure linearly. The deeper the crash, the greater the advantage of convex (option) hedges over linear (futures) hedges. ---

section: PART 6: ROLL MANAGEMENT — THE HIDDEN COST
content: > One-liner: Rolling a hedge is not neutral — you're closing one position at a loss (theta decayed) and opening a new one at market price (potentially higher IV). The roll is where hedge cost compounds.

Rolling: Close the expiring hedge instrument and open a new one with a later expiry. $$\text{Roll cost} = \text{Close cost (selling decayed hedge)} + \text{Open cost (buying new hedge at current IV)} + \text{Bid-ask friction}$$

Instrument Roll frequency Typical cost per roll Annualized drag --- --- --- --- Monthly OTM puts 12×/year 0.15-0.30% of protected notional 1.8-3.6% → EXPENSIVE Quarterly OTM puts 4×/year 0.35-0.60% per roll 1.4-2.4% 6-month puts 2×/year 0.60-1.00% per roll 1.2-2.0% → more efficient Equity index futures (ES) 4×/year ~1-2bp spread per roll ~0.05% → negligible Treasury futures 4×/year (quarterly) 1-3bp per roll ~0.05% VIX futures Monthly Contango roll cost: 3-5%/month in steep contango 30-50%+ → catastrophic for long positions Variance swaps At maturity (3M, 6M, 1Y) Mark-to-market at close, re-enter at new IV Variable Key insight: Monthly put rolling costs 2-3× more than quarterly. Monthly VIX rolling can destroy 30%+ of notional annually through contango. ALWAYS calculate the annualized roll cost before committing to a hedge program.

Strategy When to roll Advantage Disadvantage --- --- --- --- Calendar roll Fixed date (e.g., 3rd Friday) Predictable, auditable May roll at terrible vol levels Vol-based roll Roll when IV dips below target Buy protection cheap May not get the opportunity — IV stays high Delta-based roll Roll when hedge delta has decayed to <X% of initial Maintains protection level More complex to monitor Expiry-based Roll at N days before expiry Avoids rapid theta decay near expiry Standard approach RM best practice: Roll at ~2 weeks before expiry (for monthlies) or ~3 weeks (for quarterlies). This avoids the sharpest theta decay zone while maintaining sufficient time value. ---

section: PART 7: HEDGE EFFECTIVENESS — IS THE HEDGE ACTUALLY WORKING?
content: > One-liner: A hedge that doesn't perform in stress is worse than no hedge — because it created FALSE CONFIDENCE that the position was protected.

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{Var(hedged portfolio)}}{\text{Var(unhedged portfolio)}}$$ R² Interpretation Action --- --- --- >0.90 Excellent — hedge covers >90% of risk Maintain 0.70-0.90 Good — meaningful residual basis risk Monitor basis, consider tighter instrument 0.50-0.70 Mediocre — half the risk is unhedged Evaluate: is the cost justified for partial protection? <0.50 Poor — this is not a hedge, it's a separate position Replace instrument or remove

More important than overall R²: how does the hedge perform specifically in the tail? $$R^2 {\text{stress}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Var(hedged P\&L} \mid \text{stress days)}}{\text{Var(unhedged P\&L} \mid \text{stress days)}}$$ A hedge with R² = 0.85 overall but R² stress = 0.40 is MISLEADING — it works in normal markets but fails when you need it most. This is the typical failure mode of cross-asset hedges (stocks-bonds in 2022).

For options hedges: - Hit rate: What % of hedges expire with value? (For 5% OTM puts: ~20-30% historically) - Payoff ratio: When the hedge hits, how much does it pay relative to cost? (Should be >3× to justify the cost over many cycles) $$\text{Break-even} = \text{Hit rate must be at least} = \frac{1}{1 + \text{Payoff ratio}}$$ If payoff ratio = 5×: need hit rate > 1/6 = 17% → for most tail hedges, this is achievable. ---

section: PART 8: COMMON HEDGING MISTAKES — AND HOW THE RM PREVENTS THEM
content: Mistake What happens How to prevent --- --- --- Hedging too late Buying puts when VIX = 35. Premium is 3× normal. Buy when vol is LOW. Build hedge program during calm. Track VIX percentile. Over-hedging Portfolio can't make money. Theta drag > alpha. Size hedges to protect against DRAWDOWN LIMIT, not against all movement. Wrong instrument (basis risk) SPX put on a tech-heavy book → basis = 40% of the drop isn't covered Quantify basis: R² < 0.70 → find a better instrument Set-and-forget Put expires unnoticed. PM runs unprotected for 2 weeks during rollover. Roll calendar with automated alerts. No gap in coverage. Ignoring roll costs Monthly put buying costs 3% annualized. Eats most of the alpha. Use quarterly rolls. Calculate annualized drag before committing. "Free" hedges that aren't free Zero-cost collar caps $5M of upside on a $50M position. PM blames RM when stock rallies past cap. Quantify upside surrender in DOLLARS. Get PM's written acknowledgment. Hedging the wrong Greek PM hedges delta with futures but real exposure is gamma/vega Match hedge to the SPECIFIC Greek. Futures = delta only. Puts = delta + gamma + vega. Correlation-dependent hedge in a regime change Long Treasuries as equity hedge fails when correlation flips positive Monitor stocks-bonds ρ weekly. Have a Plan B hedge (gold, vol, cash). Paying for convexity you don't need Buying ATM puts ($$$) when the risk is a −20% crash Buy OTM puts for tail protection. Pay for ATM only near defined events. Concentrated hedge on diversified book Single large SPX put to hedge a 50-stock portfolio → strike/delta mismatch Use a combination: partial SPX + sector puts + single-name puts on concentrated positions ---

Risk Primary hedge Secondary Notes --- --- --- --- Market β Short SPX futures Short ETF, buy puts Futures = cheapest. Puts = convex but expensive. Single-name concentration Single-name put Collar, partial exit Collar if zero premium budget Sector concentration Short sector ETF Sector puts XLE for energy, XLF for financials, etc. Earnings event Short-dated puts/straddles Collar over earnings Weekly options around earnings Tail (5σ+) Deep OTM puts, VIX calls Systematic tail program Accept the bleed. It's insurance.

Risk Primary hedge Secondary Notes --- --- --- --- Duration (parallel) Treasury futures (ZN, ZB) IRS (pay fixed, receive floating) Match DV01 Curve Butterfly/steepener futures Swaptions Match KRD exposure Credit spread CDS (buy protection) Short HY ETFs (HYG, JNK) Match CS01 Single-name default (JTD) Single-name CDS Credit put options Match notional × (1−R)

Risk Primary hedge Secondary Notes --- --- --- --- Spot FX Forward contracts FX options Forwards = cheapest but lock in rate Vol on FX exposure FX options (straddles) FX vol swaps For known event risk (elections, BoJ) Cross-currency basis Cross-currency swaps N/A Structural, not tactical

Risk Primary hedge Secondary Notes --- --- --- --- Price (directional) Futures (WTI, Brent, HH) Swaps Calendar basis between future and physical Basis (location) Location swap Physical storage play Critical: WTI Cushing vs Brent vs local netback Vol Options on futures Calendar spreads Commodity vol is seasonal + supply-driven ---

"How would you hedge a concentrated equity position?" "I'd start with the framework: what specific risk, what horizon, what premium budget, and how much upside is the PM willing to surrender. For a single-name concentration — say 15% of NAV in XOM — the cleanest hedge is a single-name XOM put. That's delta + gamma + vega in one instrument — the hedge IMPROVES as the crash deepens because gamma kicks in and vol spikes. But it's expensive: roughly 3-4% annualized for an ATM put. To reduce cost: a put spread — buy the $108 put, sell the $100 put — halves the premium but caps protection at $8 per share. Or a collar — buy $108 put, sell $120 call — near-zero cost but caps upside at $120. If the PM refuses both premium and upside surrender, we can partially beta-hedge with SPX futures, but I'd flag the basis risk: XOM-to-SPX R² is about 0.6, so 40% of XOM's risk isn't covered by the SPX hedge. In a scenario where XOM drops 15% on company-specific news while SPX drops only 2%, the futures hedge covers 2 of 15 percentage points. The key value I add is making the tradeoff explicit: 'You can pay $400K for full protection, pay zero but cap upside at 6%, or accept 40% basis risk with a free futures hedge. Which do you prefer?' That's a conscious decision, not a hope." "When would you NOT hedge?" "Three situations. First, when the hedge costs more than the risk warrants — if a tail event would cost the fund $2M but protecting against it costs $1.5M annually, the expected value is negative. Second, when the hedge introduces MORE risk than it removes — a cross-asset hedge relying on a correlation that's unstable can turn a $5M loss into a $10M loss if the correlation flips. I'd rather size the position smaller than rely on a fragile hedge. Third, when the PM's thesis IS the risk — if the PM is explicitly making a directional bet and it's within risk limits, hedging the direction removes the trade itself. The RM's job isn't to eliminate risk — it's to ensure risk is intentional and survivable." "How do you evaluate whether a hedge is working?" "Two levels. First, statistical: I'd compute R² — the proportion of position variance explained by the hedge. If R² is below 0.70, the hedge is covering less than 70% of the risk — it might not justify its cost. Second, and more importantly, CONDITIONAL R² — how does the hedge perform specifically on stress days? A hedge with R² = 0.85 overall but R² = 0.40 on days when SPX is down >2% is a false promise. It works in normal markets and fails in crises. I'd also track cumulative hedge P&L versus cumulative position P&L over time — if the hedge is consistently LOSING without the position GAINING, the basis has drifted and the instrument needs to be reassessed." "Explain the tradeoff between puts and futures as hedges." "Futures are free, liquid, and precise for delta — the best instrument for temporary risk reduction. But they're LINEAR. In a gap-down, the futures hedge responds proportionally while the position might behave

section: WHY POSITION MANAGEMENT IS THE RM'S DAILY OPERATING SYSTEM
content: Stock selection gets the headlines. Position management determines the actual returns. A PM who picks 60% winners but sizes them poorly — overweight busted trades, underweight winners, concentrated in correlated names — will UNDERPERFORM a PM who picks 50% winners but sizes them intelligently, cuts losers fast, and maintains diversification. The RM's role in position management: Function What you do What you DON'T do --- --- --- Sizing Flag when position size exceeds conviction/liquidity/risk budget Tell the PM WHICH stocks to buy Monitoring Track every position's risk contribution, P&L, thesis status Trade for the PM Escalation Surface drawdown protocols, force the broken-thesis conversation Override the PM's investment judgment Architecture Ensure portfolio construction rules are applied consistently Design the PM's trading strategy At Squarepoint, the discretionary desk is embedded in a systematic-at-core firm. The CRO expects the same RIGOR in position management from the discretionary PM that the quant models apply automatically. You are the bridge — translating discretionary judgment into systematic discipline. ---

section: PART 1: POSITION SIZING — THE MOST IMPORTANT DECISION
content: > One-liner: The PM decides WHAT to trade. The RM helps decide HOW MUCH. Sizing is the largest single driver of portfolio P&L variance.

section: Why Sizing Matters More Than Selection
content: The math is stark: - A fund with 60% win rate and EQUAL sizing across positions has a Sharpe of ~0.5 - The SAME fund with CONVICTION-WEIGHTED sizing (larger in high-conviction winners) has Sharpe of ~0.8 - The SAME fund with POOR sizing (overweight losers due to averaging down) has Sharpe of ~0.2 Sizing explains 40-60% of P&L variance. Selection explains 20-30%. The remainder is timing and luck.

Every position gets the same notional allocation. $$w i = \frac{1}{N}$$ Pros Cons --- --- Simple, no overconfidence bias Ignores conviction — best idea = worst idea Mechanically diversified Ignores volatility — 50-vol biotech = 12-vol utility Easy to implement and monitor Rebalancing on every new position entry When it's right: High-turnover statistical strategies, initial deployment of new desk, when PM track record is unproven.

Size proportional to PM confidence level, within a structured tier system. Conviction tier Size (% NAV) of positions typical Total allocation --- --- --- --- Tier 1 — Anchor (top 3-5 ideas) 5-8% each 3-5 15-40% Tier 2 — Core 2-4% each 8-12 20-40% Tier 3 — Satellite 0.5-2% each 10-20 10-30% Tier 4 — Exploratory 0.25-0.5% each 5-10 2-5% RM checks on conviction sizing: - Does the PM have enough Tier 1 positions to justify concentration? - Are Tier 4 positions consuming disproportionate attention relative to their P&L contribution? - Is there "conviction creep" — positions that started as Tier 3 growing to Tier 1 through price appreciation without the PM consciously deciding to increase conviction?

Size so each position contributes EQUAL RISK (volatility contribution), not equal dollars. $$\text{Notional} i = \frac{\text{Target risk budget per position}}{\sigma i}$$ Where $\sigma i$ = annualized volatility of asset $i$. Worked example: Stock Annualized vol (σ) Target risk $/position = $200K Notional allocation --- --- --- --- JNJ 15% $200K / 0.15 $1.33M AAPL 25% $200K / 0.25 $800K TSLA 50% $200K / 0.50 $400K MSTR 80% $200K / 0.80 $250K Each contributes ~$200K of annual risk to the portfolio despite wildly different notionals. Without vol-weighting, equal notional in TSLA vs JNJ means TSLA dominates portfolio risk 3:1.

Full Kelly: $$f^ = \frac{p \cdot b - q}{b} = \frac{\text{Edge}}{\text{Odds}}$$ Where: - $f^ $ = fraction of capital to bet - $p$ = probability of winning - $b$ = win/loss ratio (amount won per dollar risked if correct) - $q = 1 - p$ = probability of losing Worked example: - Trade has 55% win rate ($p = 0.55$), with 2:1 payoff ($b = 2$) - $f^ = (0.55 \times 2 - 0.45) / 2 = 0.65 / 2 = 0.325$ = 32.5% of capital That's insane. Full Kelly implies enormous bets and drawdowns of 50%+ routinely. In practice: Kelly fraction Max drawdown (approximate) Use case --- --- --- Full Kelly 50%+ periodic Never used in discretionary Half Kelly ~25% Aggressive quant strategies Quarter Kelly ~12% Discretionary with good track record Eighth Kelly ~6% New PM, untested strategy RM insight: Kelly is a CEILING, not a target. The edge estimate ($p$ and $b$) is always uncertain — if your edge estimate is wrong by even 20%, full Kelly turns into overbet → ruin. The PM's confidence in the edge should determine how far below Kelly they size.

Size so the defined stop-loss represents an acceptable portfolio hit: $$\text{Notional} = \frac{\text{Acceptable loss per position}}{\text{Stop-loss distance (%)}}$$ Worked example: - Portfolio: $100M. Max loss per position: 0.5% of NAV = $500K - Stop-loss distance: 8% (PM will exit if the stock drops 8%) - Max notional = $500K / 0.08 = $6.25M (6.25% of NAV) If the stop is tighter (3%): max notional = $500K / 0.03 = $16.7M — but this risks whipsaw exits. If the stop is wider (15%): max notional = $500K / 0.15 = $3.3M — smaller position, more room. The tradeoff: Wide stop × small position = less whipsaw, lower aggregate risk per position. Tight stop × large position = more whipsaw but higher individual position conviction. ---

section: PART 2: LIQUIDITY-ADJUSTED SIZING — THE CONSTRAINT MOST PMs IGNORE
content: > One-liner: You can't size a position larger than you can EXIT. Liquidity is not a soft constraint — it's a hard wall.

The core rule: Never own more than N days of ADV in a single position, where N represents the maximum acceptable liquidation timeline. $$\text{Max notional} = \text{ADV}(\$) \times \text{Participation rate} \times \text{Max exit days}$$ Where: - $\text{ADV}$ = average daily dollar volume (20-day average) - $\text{Participation rate}$ = % of daily volume you can trade without moving the market (typically 10-25%) - $\text{Max exit days}$ = how many days to fully exit (depends on urgency and market conditions) Worked example — normal markets: Stock ADV ($) 15% participation 5-day exit Max position --- --- --- --- --- AAPL $12B $1.8B/day 5 days $9.0B (unconstrained) XOM $2.5B $375M/day 5 days $1.9B (unconstrained) Mid-cap stock $50M $7.5M/day 5 days $37.5M Small-cap stock $5M $750K/day 5 days $3.75M Micro-cap $500K $75K/day 5 days $375K Stress-adjusted ADV: In a crisis, volumes can DROP 30-50% (bid disappears) or SPIKE 300%+ (panic selling floods the market but at dislocated prices). The RM should size to the STRESS ADV, not the calm-weather ADV. $$\text{Stress ADV} = \text{Normal ADV} \times \text{Stress factor}$$ Market condition Stress factor Effect on max position --- --- --- Normal 1.0 Full position Moderate stress 0.6-0.7 40% smaller max position Crisis 0.3-0.5 50-70% smaller max position Flash crash / circuit breaker 0.1-0.2 80%+ reduction — may not be able to exit at all

$$\text{Impact cost} \approx \sigma \times \sqrt{\frac{Q}{V}} \times k$$ Where: - $\sigma$ = daily volatility - $Q$ = quantity being traded - $V$ = daily volume - $k$ = impact coefficient (typically 0.5-1.5 depending on asset class) Example: Selling $5M of a $50M ADV stock: - σ = 2%/day, Q/V = 0.10, k = 1.0 - Impact = 2% × √0.10 × 1.0 = 2% × 0.316 = 0.63% per execution - On $5M: slippage = $31.5K — manageable Example: Selling $5M of a $5M ADV stock: - σ = 3.5%/day, Q/V = 1.0, k = 1.0 - Impact = 3.5% × √1.0 × 1.0 = 3.5% per execution - On $5M: slippage = $175K — THIS is the liquidity tax

Before EVERY new position, the RM should verify: Check Formula Threshold --- --- --- Days to exit (normal) Position / (ADV × 15%) < 5 days for most; < 3 for volatile names Days to exit (stress) Position / (ADV × stress factor × 15%) < 10 days Position as % of ADV Position / ADV < 100% for most; < 50% for small/mid Estimated impact Almgren-Chriss or simple model < 1% one-way; < 0.5% preferred Crowding check Ownership % of float < 5% of free float ---

section: PART 3: CONVICTION AND THE BROKEN-THESIS FRAMEWORK
content: > One-liner: Conviction isn't "I feel strongly." It's a structured assessment of variant perception, catalyst, downside, and edge durability — each of which can break independently.

Pillar Question the PM must answer Red flag if absent --- --- --- Variant perception What do I see that the market doesn't? "I just like the stock" → no edge Catalyst identification What event will make the market agree with me — and WHEN? No catalyst = a dead-money trade Downside quantification If I'm wrong, how much do I lose? Where's the floor? "I don't know" → position is unsized Edge durability Why won't this edge get arbitraged away before my catalyst? Edge is common knowledge → already priced Conviction scoring (the RM's internal framework): Score Each pillar Size implication --- --- --- 4/4 — All four solid Clear edge, known catalyst, defined downside, durable edge Tier 1: 5-8% of NAV 3/4 — One pillar weak One area of uncertainty Tier 2: 2-4% 2/4 — Two pillars weak Significant uncertainty Tier 3: 0.5-2% 1/4 — Only one pillar Speculative Tier 4: 0.25-0.5% max 0/4 — None articulated Gambling Reject or challenge
When a position is losing money, the PM (and RM) must answer three sequential questions:

section: Test 1: Is the original thesis intact?
content: "We bought XOM at $110 because we expected Brent above $85 through Q3 and XOM's breakeven at $55 gives 55% margin of safety." - Brent is now at $78. XOM is at $102. The PM is down $800K. - But: XOM breakeven is STILL $55. Brent at $78 is STILL profitable. The thesis thesis might be intact — the market is just disagreeing temporarily. - If thesis is intact → HOLD or ADD (if risk budget allows). The loss is unrealized and the fundamental setup hasn't changed.

section: Test 2: Has new information arrived that wasn't in the original analysis?
content: New information Impact Action --- --- --- OPEC signals production increase Supply thesis broken Cut or reduce significantly New regulation on carbon emissions Long-term fundamental shift Reassess from scratch XOM management change / strategy pivot Company-specific thesis changed May need to cut Competitor discovers major new field Market share thesis weakened Reduce position Brent just drifted lower on no news No new information — just price Thesis may still be intact Critical distinction: Price movement is NOT new information. Price moving against you doesn't break the thesis — only NEW FUNDAMENTAL INFORMATION breaks the thesis. The RM's job is to separate the two.

section: Test 3: Would you put this position on TODAY at the current price?
content: - Forget your entry. You have NO position. Would you buy XOM here at $102 with Brent at $78? - If YES → hold. Loss aversion isn't driving the decision. - If NO → the position is alive only because of anchoring bias. Cut it. - If MAYBE → the conviction has degraded. Trim to Tier 3 or Tier 4 size. This is the most powerful test because it strips out: - Anchoring to entry price - Sunk cost fallacy ("I've already lost $800K, I need to make it back") - Loss aversion ("I'll just wait for it to come back to even") - Ego ("I can't be wrong about this") RM role: When a PM is underwater and anchoring, don't argue about the stock. Simply ask: "Would you put this on today?" If the PM hesitates, the position is dead. The PM already knows — they just need someone to say it out loud. ---
> One-liner: A risk budget is a CONCESSION of trust from the firm. Breach it and you lose trust. Manage it well and you earn more.

Each PM operates within a multi-dimensional risk budget allocated by the CRO: Dimension Metric Soft limit Hard limit Breach consequence --- --- --- --- --- Daily P&L Worst daily loss −$500K −$1M Soft: RM conversation. Hard: mandatory de-risk to 50% gross. Weekly P&L Cumulative week loss −$1M −$2M Hard: position review + gross cap reduction Monthly P&L Cumulative month loss −$2M −$4M Hard: forced liquidation of worst risk/reward positions YTD drawdown Peak-to-trough −$5M −$8M Hard: desk shutdown discussion with CRO VaR (1-day 99%) Parametric or historical $3M $5M Hard: no new risk-adding trades Gross exposure \ L\ + \ S\ $80M $100M Hard: reduce positions to limit Net exposure L − S ±$30M ±$50M Hard: bring within band Single name % of NAV 5% 8% Hard: trim immediately Sector % of gross 25% 35% Hard: diversify or hedge Top 5 concentration Top 5 as % of gross 40% 55% Hard: spread risk across more positions

As the PM consumes more of their loss budget, risk limits TIGHTEN progressively: Zone Loss budget consumed RM response PM restrictions Escalation --- --- --- --- --- 🟢 Green 0-25% Normal monitoring None None 🟡 Yellow 25-50% Daily position review with PM Review all Tier 1 positions. Run broken-thesis tests. RM flags to CRO in morning report 🟠 Orange 50-75% Twice-daily check-ins Gross capped at 80% of limit. No new Tier 1 positions without RM approval. CRO briefed directly. Recovery plan requested. 🔴 Red 75-100% Continuous monitoring Gross capped at 60%. Only risk-reducing trades. All new trades require RM + CRO approval. PM presents recovery plan to CRO within 24 hours. ⚫ Stop-out 100% Full de-risk All positions closed or hedged to delta-flat. PM sits out until reset period. Formal review with CRO + management. Squarepoint context: The discretionary desk is NEW. Initial loss budgets will be TIGHT: - Monthly: probably $2-3M soft, $4-5M hard - YTD: probably $6-8M before mandatory review - VaR: $3-5M 1-day 99% As the PM builds track record, budgets expand. The RM is the credibility bridge — documenting disciplined risk-taking is how the desk earns more capital.

Setup: PM has $100M AUM, $4M monthly loss budget, starting fresh on May 1. Date Event Cumulative loss Zone RM action --- --- --- --- --- May 3 TSLA position drops 8% −$320K 🟢 8% Normal monitoring May 8 Sector sell-off hits 3 positions −$1.1M 🟡 28% Daily review initiated. Run broken-thesis on all losers. May 12 PM adds to TSLA (averaging down) −$1.1M (same — new position, no new loss yet) 🟡 28% Flag: "You're adding to your biggest loser at 28% of budget. Are you sure thesis is intact?" May 15 TSLA drops another 5% −$1.9M 🟠 48% → Orange Gross capped. No new positions without RM discussion. CRO briefed. May 19 Two positions recover partially −$1.3M 🟡 33% → back to Yellow Restrictions ease. But RM notes the TSLA averaging-down pattern. May 23 FOMC surprise, broad sell-off −$2.8M 🔴 70% → Red All new risk frozen. PM presents recovery plan. Debate: trim losers or ride it out? May 28 Partial recovery −$2.1M 🟠 53% Restrictions partially relaxed. PM has 5 days of month remaining. RM judgment throughout: The hardest call was May 12 — the PM wants to average down on TSLA. The RM doesn't say no (that's the PM's investment decision), but surfaces: "You're at 28% of budget, your biggest loss is TSLA, and you want to ADD to it. Run the three broken-thesis tests with me right now." ---
> One-liner: The PM builds the portfolio. The RM monitors whether the portfolio the PM ACTUALLY has matches the portfolio they MEANT to have.

Metric Formula Interpretation --- --- --- Gross exposure $\lvert L \rvert + \lvert S \rvert$ Total capital deployed. Higher = more leverage/activity. Net exposure $L - S$ Directional bias. +$20M = bullish. −$10M = bearish. Net/Gross ratio $\text{Net} / \text{Gross}$ 0.0 = market-neutral. 0.5 = moderately long. 1.0 = fully long (no shorts). Beta-adjusted net $\sum w i \beta i$ True market exposure accounting for betas. A portfolio can be $0 net exposure but net long beta if longs have higher β than shorts. Effective leverage Gross / NAV How much capital is at work relative to equity. >2× is aggressive for discretionary. Worked example: Longs Shorts Total --- --- --- --- Notional $65M $35M Gross $100M Net $30M (long biased) Net/Gross 0.30 Avg beta (long) 1.15 Avg beta (short) 0.90 Beta-adjusted net $65M × 1.15 − $35M × 0.90 $43.3M (MORE long than net $ suggests) RM insight: The PM says "I'm only modestly net long at $30M." But beta-adjusted net is $43.3M — the PM's longs are higher-beta than the shorts. In a sell-off, the portfolio behaves like a $43M long portfolio, not a $30M one. This is a common surprise that the RM must surface.

Factor How it sneaks in Detection Management --- --- --- --- Market β Net long → hidden beta exposure β-adjusted net calculation Index futures to neutralize Sector PM's circle of competence → sector overweight Sector % vs benchmark Sector ETFs to rebalance Style (value/growth) PM's natural bias → unintentional factor bet Factor attribution in risk system Acknowledge or hedge with factor ETF Momentum Letting winners run → high-momentum tilt Factor loading analysis Conscious decision to accept or trim Size Preference for large-cap → small-cap underweight Cap band analysis Diversify if unintentional Currency Non-USD positions without FX hedge FX exposure report FX forwards Crowding Popular positions → correlated with L/S universe 13F overlap score Monitor; reduce if overlap > 30% Daily factor check: "PM, you've drifted to 1.4 beta exposure over the past week — that was 1.1 last Monday. Was this intentional or did it happen because your shorts covered faster than your longs?"

Metric Green Yellow Red --- --- --- --- Top 1 position (% gross) <8% 8-12% >12% Top 5 positions (% gross) <35% 35-50% >50% Single sector (% gross) <20% 20-30% >30% Correlated cluster (ρ > 0.7, combined) <15% 15-25% >25% Single-name (% NAV) <5% 5-8% >8% Effective N (1/HHI) >15 8-15 <8 ---

section: PART 6: POSITION LIFECYCLE — FROM ENTRY TO EXIT
content: > One-liner: Every position has a birth, a life, and a death. The RM tracks all three stages with a different lens at each.

Before EVERY new position, the RM runs a structured check: Check What the RM verifies Pass/Fail criteria --- --- --- Risk budget Does adding this breach any limit? All limits remain below soft threshold Marginal VaR What's the INCREMENTAL VaR of this position? Marginal VaR < position's pro-rata share Correlation How does this position correlate with existing book? If ρ > 0.7 with any existing position → flag Factor exposure Does this add unwanted factor tilt? Factor loads within ±0.5 of target Liquidity Can we exit in <5 days at <1% impact? Days to exit (normal) < 5 Conviction tier Does size match conviction? Size within tier band Crowding Are we entering a crowded position? Ownership < 5% of float; 13F overlap < 30% Hedge plan If this goes wrong, what's the exit plan? PM articulates stop-loss level and/or hedge The RM doesn't have VETO power (at most firms). But they can: - Flag concerns in writing - Escalate to CRO if risk budget is threatened - Require the PM to explicitly acknowledge risks - Document the conversation (CYA is real)

Frequency What the RM checks --- --- Daily P&L, VaR contribution, drawdown status, any limit breach Weekly Thesis status for top 10 positions, factor drift, correlation changes, concentration metrics Monthly Full portfolio review with PM: every position discussed, broken-thesis tests on losers, winner management Quarterly Performance attribution: are we making money from selection, sizing, or luck? Red flags during holding period: Signal What it means RM response --- --- --- Position grows from Tier 3 to Tier 1 size via appreciation, no PM action "Conviction creep" — PM didn't consciously decide to increase size "This position has grown to 7% of NAV. Do you want to be Tier 1 conviction, or should we trim to target weight?" High P&L volatility relative to conviction Position dominates book risk despite being a Tier 2 idea Re-examine sizing. Is the vol-adjusted size appropriate? Correlation with existing positions increasing Portfolio becoming less diversified over time Run stress scenario: "What if all your correlated positions drop together?" PM stops talking about a position Often a sign it's not working and PM is avoiding the conversation Force the conversation: "Let's review all positions that haven't been discussed in 2+ weeks."

Signal Action --- --- Catalyst hit: stock re-rated as thesis predicted Close or trim to Tier 4 "monitoring" size Valuation target reached Close and reallocate capital to next-best idea Risk/reward no longer attractive at new price Trim. Don't hold winners forever without thesis. Disposition effect warning: PMs cut winners too early ("let me lock in profit") and hold losers too long ("it'll come back"). The RM should challenge BOTH impulses.

Run the three tests (Part 3). If thesis is broken: - Cut immediately. Do NOT wait for a bounce. - The entry price is IRRELEVANT. The only question is: would you buy this today? - If position is large enough that exit moves the market, use an execution algo (VWAP/TWAP) over 1-3 days.

section: 3. Thesis Intact Exit — Risk Budget Drives Exit
content: Sometimes the thesis is fine but the PORTFOLIO situation forces the exit: - Drawdown protocol at 🔴 Red → all new risk frozen, may need to trim existing positions - VaR limit breach → must reduce risk contribution - Correlation event → position is no longer diversifying Hardest conversation in RM: "The thesis is right, but we need to sell because we're out of risk budget." The PM hates this. But the alternative is breaching the hard limit and losing 100% of risk authority.

Trigger Action --- --- Regulatory (e.g., sanctions, compliance) Exit immediately regardless of P&L Short borrow recall Cover short immediately Liquidity event (stock halts, ADV collapses) Reduce as fast as market allows Fund-level redemption Pro-rata reduction across all positions ---

section: PART 7: POSITION SCALING — WHEN AND HOW TO ADD OR TRIM
content: > One-liner: Scaling in and out of positions is the PM's primary expression of changing conviction. The RM ensures scaling decisions are CONSCIOUS, not reactive.

When to add When NOT to add --- --- Thesis confirmed by new data (earnings beat expectation, catalyst approaching) Averaging down on a broken thesis ("it's cheaper now!") Stock at better entry point with thesis INTACT Adding because of loss aversion ("I need it to recover") Risk budget has capacity and correlations support it Risk budget is at Orange or Red zone PM has explicit pre-planned scaling schedule Impulse reaction to a price drop Pre-planned scaling approach: "I want to build a 5% position in XOM. I'll enter 2% initially, add 1.5% if Q2 earnings confirm thesis, and add the final 1.5% if Brent stays above $80 through July." This is GOOD because: - Each tranche has explicit conditions - The position grows WITH evidence, not against it - If conditions aren't met, the position stays small RM check: "You planned to add at 2 specific trigger points. Earnings MISSED. Why are you adding now?"

When to trim When NOT to trim --- --- Valuation target partially reached Panic — price dropped and you're scared Position has grown too large relative to conviction tier Disposition effect — taking profit too early on a working thesis Concentration risk emerging (>5% NAV single name) Forced by irrelevant noise Risk budget constraints (approaching Orange/Red) To "be doing something" — activity is not progress Approaching known risk event (earnings) where PM wants to reduce exposure Worked example — Scaling out of a winner: Phase XOM price Position size % NAV PM action Rationale --- --- --- --- --- --- Entry $108 $4.2M 4.2% Buy full Tier 2 position Thesis: Brent > $85, XOM breakeven $55 +3 months $118 $4.6M (appreciated) 4.6% Hold — thesis intact Brent at $87, everything tracking +5 months $128 $5.0M (appreciated) 5.0% Trim $1.0M → $4.0M Position hit 5% NAV → concentration risk. Trim to original dollar amount. +7 months $135 $4.2M 4.2% Trim $1.2M → $3.0M Reaching valuation target ($135). Move from Tier 2 to Tier 3. +9 months $140 $3.2M 3.2% Close fully Thesis fully realized. Re-deploy capital. Total P&L: Entry at $108, exits at $128/$135/$140. Weighted avg exit ~$133. Gain = ~23%. On ~$4M avg position = ~$920K. ---

section: PART 8: RISK BUDGETING — DECOMPOSING PORTFOLIO RISK
content: > One-liner: A 5% position in a high-beta, high-correlation stock might contribute 20% of portfolio VaR. The RM's job is to make RISK CONTRIBUTION visible, not just dollar weight.

$$\text{Marginal VaR} i = \frac{\partial \text{VaR} p}{\partial w i} = \beta {i,p} \times \text{VaR} p$$ Where $\beta {i,p}$ = beta of position $i$ to the portfolio. Component Risk Contribution: $$\text{Component VaR} i = w i \times \text{Marginal VaR} i$$ $$\sum {i=1}^{N} \text{Component VaR} i = \text{VaR} p$$ This decomposes total portfolio VaR into each position's contribution. The sum of all component VaRs equals the total portfolio VaR.

Portfolio: $80M, 5 positions Position Weight ($) Weight (%) Daily vol Correlation w/ port Component VaR ($K) Risk contribution (%) --- --- --- --- --- --- --- TSLA $12M 15% 3.5% 0.85 $380K 33% XOM $20M 25% 1.8% 0.70 $280K 24% AAPL $18M 22.5% 2.0% 0.75 $270K 23% JNJ $15M 18.75% 1.0% 0.50 $90K 8% TLT (short) $15M 18.75% 1.2% −0.30 −$68K −6% (reduces risk) TOTAL $80M 100% $952K 82% (short TLT diversifies) RM observations: 1. TSLA is 15% of dollars but 33% of risk . This is the classic vol-weight vs risk-weight divergence. 2. JNJ is 18.75% of dollars but only 8% of risk. It's barely contributing to portfolio volatility. 3. TLT short has NEGATIVE risk contribution — it's diversifying. This is a functioning hedge. 4. "PM, TSLA is your smallest dollar position but your biggest risk. If TSLA drops 10%, you lose ~$120K MORE than if any other single position drops 10%. Is that intentional?"

Target each position to contribute EQUAL risk: $$\text{Risk Contribution} i = \frac{1}{N} \times \text{Total VaR}$$ With 5 positions: each should contribute 20% of total VaR. Currently: - TSLA: 33% → OVERWEIGHT in risk terms (need to trim from 15% to ~9% of capital) - JNJ: 8% → UNDERWEIGHT in risk terms (could increase or accept as intentional) RM doesn't enforce risk parity — discretionary PMs deliberately concentrate risk on their best ideas. But the RM SHOWS the risk decomposition so the PM's concentration is INTENTIONAL, not accidental. ---
> One-liner: Every morning, the RM should be able to answer 10 questions about the book in under 60 seconds by looking at their dashboard.

Category Metric Current Limit Status --- --- --- --- --- Exposure Gross ($) $92M $100M 🟡 92% Net ($) $28M ±$50M 🟢 56% Net/Gross 0.30 n/a Moderately long Beta-adjusted net $38M ±$50M 🟢 76% Risk VaR (1d 99%) $3.8M $5M 🟡 76% Worst stress scenario −$6.2M −$8M 🟡 78% P&L Daily P&L −$180K −$1M soft 🟢 18% MTD P&L −$1.4M −$4M hard 🟡 35% YTD P&L +$2.1M Positive Concentration Top 1 position (% gross) 8.5% 10% 🟡 Top 5 (% gross) 42% 55% 🟢 Worst sector (% gross) 28% (Energy) 35% 🟡 Liquidity Worst single-name days-to-exit 7 days 5 days 🔴 Breach Drawdown Peak-to-trough MTD −$1.4M / $4M 35% consumed 🟡 Yellow zone Zone YELLOW Review top positions

Time Activity Duration --- --- --- 6:30 AM Pull overnight marks, check P&L, update dashboard 10 min 6:45 AM Review any limit breaches or near-breaches 5 min 7:00 AM Pre-market check: any news on top 10 positions? 10 min 7:15 AM Flag items for PM discussion 5 min 7:30 AM Morning risk meeting with PM (or CRO if escalation needed) 15 min 8:00 AM Document any decisions, update position notes 5 min

Severity Trigger Escalate to Timeline --- --- --- --- Low Single metric at yellow PM only Morning meeting Medium Multiple metrics yellow OR one metric red PM + RM lead Same day High Any hard limit breach OR drawdown zone Orange+ PM + CRO Within 1 hour Critical Stop-out triggered OR multiple hard breaches PM + CRO + Head of desk Immediately ---

"A PM wants to add a new position but is already at 85% of their risk budget. What do you do?" "First, I'd look at the MARGINAL risk contribution. If the new position is diversifying — low correlation to the existing book — it might barely change portfolio VaR. A negatively correlated position could actually REDUCE VaR even though it adds gross exposure. In that case, 85% might only go to 86%. But if it's correlated — same sector, same factor exposure — it could push to 95%+. That's one bad day from breaching the hard limit and triggering mandatory de-risking. My response isn't 'no.' It's: 'You're at 85%. This position takes you to 94% because it's 0.75 correlated with your two largest holdings. If any of those three names drops 3%, you'll breach the hard limit and I'll have to reduce your gross by 30%. Do you want to trim an existing position to make room, or is this idea worth that risk?' I'm making the tradeoff transparent. The PM makes the investment decision — I make sure it's a CONSCIOUS one." "How do you know when a position is too large?" "Three lenses. First, RISK CONTRIBUTION: if one position contributes more than 25% of portfolio VaR, it's the portfolio — everything else is noise. That concentration better be the PM's highest-conviction idea with a clear catalyst. Second, LIQUIDITY: if the position takes more than 5 days to exit at 15% participation, it's too large for the strategy's risk profile. In stress, that 5-day exit becomes 15 days when volumes dry up. Third, LOSS IMPACT: if the defined stop-loss distance on this position would consume more than 30% of the monthly loss budget in a single trade, the position is too large relative to the risk budget. You can't afford to be right on 4 positions and wrong on 1 if that 1 takes out half your budget." "Walk me through how you'd manage a drawdown." "It's a structured escalation. At 25% of monthly budget consumed, I have a conversation. We review the top losers and run broken-thesis tests: is the thesis intact? Has new information arrived? Would you put this on today? At 50%, restrictions kick in — gross exposure capped, no new positions without my review. This isn't punishment; it's capital preservation. The worst outcome is a PM losing 75% of budget in the first two weeks and having to sit out the remaining two weeks of the month. At 75%, we're in active defense. Only risk-reducing trades. The PM presents a recovery plan: 'Here's what I'm keeping, here's what I'm cutting, and here's why I expect to recover X% by month end.' And at 100% — stop-out. Positions closed or hedged flat. Reset at the next period. This sounds harsh, but it protects the PM's career. A systematic fund would have cut risk automatically at −5%. The discretionary desk gets more rope, but the rope has a defined length." "How would you handle a PM who keeps averaging down on losing positions?" "That's one of the most common and dangerous patterns. Averaging down is rational IF the thesis is intact, the PM planned to

section: WHY BEHAVIORAL BIASES ARE THE RM'S BEST DIAGNOSTIC TOOL
