Dimension Systematic RM Discretionary RM --- --- --- Primary tool Dashboards, model monitoring, kill switch Conversations, judgment, analysis Key risk Model failure, crowding, technology Behavioral bias, concentration, thesis refitting Position count 1,000–5,000 20–80 Interaction mode Engineer-to-system Human-to-human Decision speed Seconds (automated stops) Hours to days (deliberation + judgment) Data dependency Extreme — bad data = catastrophic Moderate — PM uses data but also qualitative Override frequency Rare (kill switch in emergencies) Daily (sizing conversations, thesis challenges) Factor control Automated neutrality by construction Manual monitoring + PM dialogue Stop-loss Algorithmic, no discretion Framework-guided but involves discussion Portfolio construction Optimization engine PM judgment + RM guardrails Worst-case event Quant quake / model break Single-name blow-up / PM revenge trading Emotional intelligence needed Low High Quant skills needed High Moderate-to-high ---

section: PART 3: RISK METRICS — SAME INPUT, DIFFERENT EMPHASIS
content: Both paradigms use the same risk metrics but weight them differently: Metric Systematic emphasis Discretionary emphasis --- --- --- VaR Highly relied upon — model calibrates positions to VaR Useful but insufficient alone — concentrated books need stress + scenario Factor exposure Zero tolerance — model enforces neutrality Monitored weekly, flagged if drifting, but PM may intentionally take factor bets Gross/net exposure Constrained by optimizer Constrained by RM policy + PM judgment Concentration Very low by construction (3,000 positions) Critical monitoring — the 1 discretionary risk Turnover Constrained to target (cost optimization) can spike if PM is overtrading or revenge trading Drawdown Model auto-reduces risk on drawdown (vol-scaling) Requires manual escalation protocols (green/yellow/red/stop-out) Beta Neutralized by optimizer May be intentional — RM queries the PM: "is this beta bet deliberate or accidental?" Liquidity Important — but spread across many positions so each is small Critical — a single position at 15% ADV can't be exited quickly Correlation Monitored against model assumptions Monitored against PM's stated diversification thesis Stress test Run on the entire model universe; results are statistical Run on 20–80 specific names; results are ACTIONABLE (which positions to cut) ---

Squarepoint Capital was founded as a systematic fund. The firm's DNA is quantitative: Feature Detail --- --- Heritage Spun out of Deutsche Bank's systematic trading group. Deep quant pedigree. Strategies Multiple systematic strategies: stat arb, mid-frequency alpha, machine learning signals, multi-asset systematic macro Technology Proprietary trading infrastructure. Co-located servers. Low-latency execution. Risk infrastructure Built for monitoring thousands of positions, factor-level analytics, automated controls Data Massive data infrastructure: tick data, alternative data, NLP pipelines AUM ~$11B+ (as of recent reports). Global operations.

Squarepoint is building discretionary capability. This is the team you'd join as RM. Why Squarepoint wants discretionary: Reason Detail --- --- Alpha diversification Systematic and discretionary alphas are largely UNCORRELATED. Combining them improves the firm's Sharpe ratio. If corr(sys, disc) = 0.2 and each has Sharpe of 1.0, combined Sharpe ≈ 1.3. Opportunity capture Some trades CAN'T be captured by models: management change, activist situations, regulatory shifts, geopolitical events, special situations. Human judgment is required. LP demand Allocators increasingly want multi-strategy platforms with diversified alpha engines. "Give me systematic + discretionary under one roof." Regime robustness When systematic models break (regime change, factor crowding), discretionary PMs may still perform — and vice versa. The firm's returns are more robust across market regimes. Competitive positioning Citadel, Millennium, Point72, Balyasny all run both. Squarepoint needs discretionary to compete for LP capital. What makes this integration challenging: Challenge Why it's hard RM relevance --- --- --- Culture Quants think in data, statistics, p-values. Discretionary PMs think in narratives, conviction, catalysts. You must speak BOTH languages fluently Infrastructure Risk systems built for 3,000-position systematic books need adaptation for a concentrated 40-position discretionary book Each discretionary position needs qualitative context the system wasn't designed to store Risk language Systematic: "Expected shortfall breached 2.5σ." Discretionary: "The PM is anchoring to a broken thesis." You must translate between these two languages daily Trust Firm leadership is systematic. Discretionary desk must PROVE alpha before getting more capital. You help build credibility by demonstrating disciplined risk management from Day 1 Correlation monitoring If the discretionary desk is accidentally long the same factor as the systematic book, it's not diversification — it's DOUBLING risk You must monitor cross-desk correlation and flag overlap ---
As the RM for the discretionary desk inside a systematic firm, you occupy a unique position in the organization:

Direction You translate Example --- --- --- PM → CRO PM conviction into quantitative risk language "PM wants to go 8% in NVDA" → "This adds $3.5M incremental VaR, 25% tech concentration, and increases portfolio beta from 0.15 to 0.35" CRO → PM Firm-level risk constraints into actionable guidance "CRO wants disc VaR under $5M" → "You can keep current positions but need to hedge the beta or reduce your 3 most correlated names" Systematic → PM Quant insights that inform discretionary views "Systematic desk is seeing mean-reversion signals in energy — consistent with your long XOM thesis timing" PM → Systematic Fundamental context that enriches risk monitoring "PM sees M&A risk in this name — flag it for the systematic book so their model doesn't take a short into a takeout"

Time Activity Your role --- --- --- 6:30 AM Review overnight — Asian session, European open, futures, FX Identify anything that moved your positions >1% overnight 7:00 AM Pre-market risk report: P&L, VaR, exposures, limit utilization, top movers Generate and review; highlight any position that needs attention 7:30 AM Morning meeting with PM Review: positions, catalysts today (earnings, data, events), discuss risk metrics. "Your energy concentration is 22%. OPEC meets tomorrow. Want to trim?" 8:00 AM Cross-desk check with CRO / central risk Report discretionary desk status. Flag any overlap with systematic positions. Confirm firm-level limits. 9:30 AM Market open Monitor real-time P&L, delta exposure, intraday VaR 10:00–3:30 PM Continuous monitoring + PM dialogue Flag intraday limit approaches, discuss new trade ideas ("If you add this, here's the impact on VaR and concentration"), thesis checks on lagging positions 4:00 PM Market close Run EOD marks, reconcile positions, calculate T+1 risk 4:30 PM EOD risk report Updated risk report to PM + CRO: today's P&L, limit utilization, VaR, overnight exposure Weekly Portfolio deep dive Factor decomposition, stress scenarios, correlation with systematic book, thesis audits, behavioral pattern review Monthly Formal IC review Present portfolio risk to Investment Committee: risk budget utilization, drawdown status, concentration, notable positions

The risk nobody talks about until it blows up: If the systematic book is long momentum and the discretionary PM is also long momentum stocks (without realizing it), the firm ISN'T diversified — it's DOUBLED up on the same factor. Scenario Systematic position Discretionary position Correlation Firm risk --- --- --- --- --- Independent alpha Long statistical arb signals Long deep-value thesis in healthcare Low (ρ ≈ 0.1) Truly diversified ✅ Accidental overlap Long momentum factor (50 stocks) Long NVDA, META, AMZN (momentum leaders) High (ρ ≈ 0.7) Concentrated in momentum ❌ Offsetting Long value factor Long growth stocks Negative (ρ ≈ −0.3) Internal hedge — may be intentional or wasteful RM responsibility: Run weekly correlation analysis between discretionary book returns and systematic factor returns. If R² > 0.3 against any single factor in the systematic book → flag to PM and CRO. ---

What happened: - Multiple quant funds running similar market-neutral factor strategies (value, momentum, mean reversion) - One large fund (likely Goldman Sachs Global Alpha) began liquidating due to losses in其他 mortgage-related strategies - Their equity positions were highly correlated with other quant funds - Selling triggered a cascade: quant fund A sells → prices of quant-favored stocks drop → quant fund B's risk model triggers stop → fund B sells → cascade Timeline: Date Event --- --- Aug 6 (Mon) Unusual losses across quant equity funds. −3 to −5σ day. Aug 7 (Tue) Losses accelerate. Goldman Global Alpha down 10% in a week. Aug 8 (Wed) Goldman injects $3B of capital into Global Alpha. Aug 9 (Thu) Worst day — many funds see −10σ to −12σ returns (should be impossible under normal distributions). Aug 10 (Fri) Partial recovery as selling exhausts. Total industry losses: ~$100B across quantitative equity funds in one week. Root cause: CROWDING. Everyone owned the same positions because they were running the same models. When one unwinds, everyone unwinds. The diversification across 3,000 positions was an ILLUSION — they were diversified across stocks but CONCENTRATED in factors. Systematic RM lessons: 1. Monitor strategy overlap with the industry (13F analysis, factor return correlation) 2. Track "uniqueness" of signals — if everyone has the same signal, it's not alpha 3. Stress-test against COORDINATED unwind, not just market moves 4. The model's "worst case" isn't actually the worst case — tail events exceed model assumptions

What happened: - Bill Hwang ran a family office (~$20B in exposure from $10B equity through TRS leverage) - Concentrated in <10 names: ViacomCBS, Discovery, Baidu, Tencent Music, etc. - Positions were held via Total Return Swaps at multiple prime brokers → invisible to the market (no 13F filing required) - ViacomCBS did a secondary offering on March 22. Stock dropped. Margin calls triggered. - Hwang couldn't meet calls. All PBs liquidated simultaneously in "block trade" fire-sales over March 26–29. Losses: Bank Loss --- --- Credit Suisse $5.5B Nomura $2.9B Morgan Stanley $911M (got out faster) Goldman Sachs ~$0 (liquidated first) Root cause: CONCENTRATION + OPACITY + LEVERAGE. One PM with extreme conviction, no diversification, massive leverage, and zero transparency to counterparties. Discretionary RM lessons: 1. Concentration limits are non-negotiable — no single name >5–8% of NAV 2. Leverage amplifies concentration risk non-linearly 3. Transparency to PBs is a FEATURE, not a weakness — it prevents multiple PBs from enabling the same risk 4. TRS/synthetic positions must be treated identically to physical for risk purposes 5. "Conviction" is not a justification for bypassing position limits

Systematic Discretionary --- --- --- Initial impact Models had never seen −30% in 3 weeks. Many signals became noise. Vol-scaling models de-risked aggressively at the bottom. PMs with strong fundamentals held through the dip (if they could stomach it). Many panic-sold. Recovery Models that de-risked missed the V-shaped rally. Some took weeks to re-risk. PMs who held were rewarded enormously. PMs who sold locked in losses permanently. RM lesson Models trained on 10 years of low-vol data are useless in tail events. Need long-lookback and scenario-based stress The PM who has a PLAN before the crisis makes better decisions during it. Pre-agreed drawdown protocols save careers. Key failure Vol-targeting models sold at the EXACT bottom — systematic risk management caused pro-cyclical de-risking PMs who revenge-traded or froze had no framework to fall back on ---

The line between systematic and discretionary is disappearing: Model What it means Example --- --- --- Discretionary + quant tools PM uses data science to screen and monitor, but makes final decisions PM uses NLP sentiment to prioritize earnings call review, but makes the buy/sell call Systematic + human overlay Quant model generates signals, but human has veto power on execution in extreme events CIO can prevent the model from selling into a liquidity vacuum "Quantamental" Fundamental research enhanced by data science and ML at every step Point72 Academy model: analysts trained in both fundamental and data tools Multi-strat platforms Both systematic and discretionary pods under one risk infrastructure Citadel, Millennium, Balyasny, Squarepoint AI-augmented discretionary LLM-based research assistants, automated channel checks, real-time news analysis Emerging — most firms experimenting, few in full production

section: What This Means for a New RM
content: The RM of 2026 must be: Capability Why --- --- Quantitatively fluent Run factor decompositions, understand model output, speak to the systematic CRO in their language Behaviorally intelligent Read the PM, catch biases, push back diplomatically, coach without accusing Data-literate Use alternative data, visualization tools, Python/SQL — not just Excel Adaptable Understand that the discretionary PM will increasingly use systematic tools, and the risk framework must evolve with them Cross-functional Communicate equally well with quants, PMs, traders, operations, compliance, and LPs

section: How Squarepoint's RM Uses Quantitative Tools for Discretionary Risk
content: Tool Systematic origin Discretionary application --- --- --- Factor decomposition (Barra/Axioma) Standard for systematic books Decompose the discretionary PM's portfolio into factor bets: "You think you're stock-picking, but 60% of your variance is explained by momentum and beta" Regime detection HMM models switching between vol regimes "We're in a high-vol regime. Your position sizes should reflect the higher per-position VaR, not the backtest average" Correlation heatmap Real-time cross-asset correlation monitoring "Your 'diversified' 8-name book has a first principal component explaining 70% of variance. You have ONE bet, not eight." Scenario engine Automated stress testing across 10K+ scenarios "I ran your 35-position book through 500 historical and hypothetical scenarios. Here are the 10 worst — all involve a China hard landing. You have more China exposure than you think." Alt data signals Short interest, options flow, sentiment, positioning "Short interest in your largest long just hit 25%. Be prepared for headline risk and potential squeeze disruption." ---

CEO / CIO ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐ Head of CRO / COO / Systematic Head of Risk Operations Quant PMs ┌───────┼───────┐ Researchers Systematic Discretionary Engineers Risk Team Risk Team ← YOU Risk Analysts Risk Analysts Discretionary PM(s) Traders Key point: The discretionary RM reports to the CRO (not to the PM). This is critical for independence. Your job is to support the PM but your accountability is to the CRO and the firm.

To the PM To the CRO --- --- Provide useful risk analytics Report compliance with limits Translate risk system output into actionable insights Flag limit breaches and behavioral patterns Support portfolio construction decisions Present honest risk assessment — even if PM disagrees Build trust → PM WANTS to talk to you Maintain independence → you escalate when necessary The tension: The PM wants the RM to be a partner. The CRO wants the RM to be a watchdog. You need to be BOTH. If you're only a partner, you miss risks. If you're only a watchdog, the PM stops talking to you. The best RMs earn enough trust that when they say "I'm concerned about this position," the PM LISTENS. ---

Framework element Systematic implementation Discretionary implementation --- --- --- Position limits Coded. Model cannot exceed. Set by policy. PM may need IC approval to exceed. VaR limits Portfolio VaR auto-calculated and constrained in optimizer Portfolio VaR calculated daily, PM notified if approaching limit Stop-loss Algorithmic. Position auto-closed at trigger. Discussion-based. RM presents data. PM makes decision (or escalates to IC). Factor neutrality Enforced by construction. Model optimizes for neutrality. Monitored. PM may intentionally take factor bets if justified. Drawdown protocol Vol-scaling: model automatically reduces size as losses accumulate Escalation ladder: green → yellow → orange → red → stop-out (see file J) Thesis documentation N/A — model IS the thesis Required at inception. Reviewed monthly. Audit for thesis refitting. Stress testing Automated across full portfolio. Statistical. Targeted at top 10-20 positions. Highly interpretable. Behavioral monitoring N/A — no human behavior to monitor Core function. Win/loss ratios, hold periods, conviction calibration. Kill switch Halt ALL trading instantly Full de-risk of portfolio (takes 2-5 days depending on liquidity) ---

"Why do you want to do discretionary risk management specifically, especially at a systematic firm?" "Discretionary RM is where judgment matters most day-to-day. In a systematic setup, the model handles most real-time risk decisions — position sizing, factor neutrality, stop-losses. The RM monitors systems. Important, but much of the value is in model construction, not daily risk dialogue. In discretionary, I'm the PM's risk counterpart. I'm running factor decomposition on their portfolio to surface exposures they might not see — 'you think you're running 8 independent stock picks, but 60% of your variance is explained by momentum and beta.' I'm tracking behavioral patterns against data — 'your average loser is held 3× longer than your average winner, and the thesis changed twice. Is this position alive because of the idea, or because of loss aversion?' And I'm running scenario analysis that's specific and actionable: not 'if a recession, VaR goes to $8M,' but 'if a recession, HERE are the 3 positions that would lose $2M each, and here's how I'd hedge or trim.' Doing this at Squarepoint is uniquely valuable because the infrastructure exists. The factor engines, the scenario tools, the data pipelines — they're already built for the systematic side. I get to apply that quantitative toolkit to a 40-position discretionary book where each position has a STORY, a CATALYST, and a THESIS that I can audit. It's the intersection of quantitative rigor and human judgment. That's where I want to be." "How would you handle a situation where the discretionary PM's positions are correlated with the systematic book?" "This is the central risk of running both engines under one roof, and it's actually one of the most important things the discretionary RM monitors. I'd run a weekly regression of the discretionary book's returns against the systematic strategy's factor loadings. If I find R² > 0.3 against, say, the momentum factor — and the systematic book is also long momentum — that's not diversification, that's doubling up on the same bet at the firm level. I'd bring this to the PM with data, not accusations. 'Your portfolio has 60% return variance explained by momentum. The systematic desk has 40% in momentum. At the firm level, we're 50% momentum-exposed. If momentum reverses — August 2007 style — both desks lose simultaneously and the firm's diversification benefit disappears.' The solutions might be: (1) the PM hedges their momentum exposure via a factor overlay, (2) the PM shifts into more idiosyncratic positions where alpha is company-specific rather than factor-driven, or (3) the CRO reduces the combined firm-level exposure by de-risking one or both desks. My role is to surface the problem, quantify it, and present options — not to make the allocation decision." "What's the biggest challenge of being a discretionary RM inside a systematic firm?" "Credibility in both directions. The systematic team thinks in terms of Sharpe ratios, information coefficients,

section: WHY NON-MARKET RISKS KILL MORE FUNDS THAN BAD TRADES
content: Every fund that has ever blown up catastrophically was destroyed by a non-market risk — or by market risk combined with structural/operational failures: Fund Market loss? What ACTUALLY killed it --- --- --- Barings (1995) Nikkei adverse move Operational: Leeson controlled BOTH trading AND back office. No segregation of duties. LTCM (1998) Spread widening Counterparty + structural: Leverage 25×+ with no margin buffer. PBs withdrew financing. Amaranth (2006) Nat gas spread Concentration + operational: Single trader controlled >50% of fund risk. No position limit enforcement. Knight Capital (2012) N/A (no market event) Technology: Erroneous code deployed to production. Lost $440M in 45 minutes. Archegos (2021) ViacomCBS drop Counterparty opacity: TRS positions invisible across PBs. No aggregate exposure monitoring. Market risk is what you lose money ON. Operational, counterparty, and regulatory risk is what PREVENTS you from managing that market risk. ---

Counterparty risk = the risk that the entity on the other side of your trade can't fulfil its obligations. If you trade a swap with Bank X and Bank X defaults, your in-the-money position is worth zero — even though you were right about the trade.

Instrument Counterparty risk Why --- --- --- Listed futures/options Minimal CCP (exchange) stands between buyer and seller OTC derivatives (bilateral) High Direct exposure to the other party Total Return Swaps (TRS) High Counterparty is the PB/dealer providing the swap Repo / reverse repo Moderate Collateralized, but haircut and collateral quality matter Securities lending Moderate Borrower may fail to return shares; cash collateral may be insufficient Bank deposits Low but non-zero FDIC covers only $250K; fund cash balances can be $100M+ Prime brokerage custody Moderate Lehman 2008 — assets held at PB frozen in bankruptcy

PFE measures the MAXIMUM expected exposure to a counterparty at a given confidence level over a given time horizon: $$\text{PFE} {95\%} = \text{Current MTM} + \text{Potential increase at 95th percentile over horizon}$$ Trade Current MTM 95th percentile potential increase PFE (95%) --- --- --- --- 5Y interest rate swap (receive fixed) +$2M +$8M $10M 3Y equity TRS +$5M +$12M $17M FX forward (6 month) −$1M +$4M $3M PFE captures the fact that even a trade currently LOSING money could become an exposure if it swings in your favor — because then you're owed money by the counterparty.

EE is the AVERAGE exposure over the life of the trade (not the peak): $$\text{EE} = \mathbb{E}\left \max(V t, 0)\right $$ EE is used for CVA calculation (below). PFE is used for counterparty limit monitoring. ---

section: CVA — The Market Price of Counterparty Risk
content: CVA answers: "How much less is this derivative worth because my counterparty might default?" $$\text{CVA} = \sum {i=1}^{n} \text{EE}(t i) \times \text{PD}(t {i-1}, t i) \times \text{LGD} \times \text{DF}(t i)$$ Where: - EE(tᵢ) = Expected Exposure at time tᵢ - PD(tᵢ₋₁, tᵢ) = Probability of Default during the interval - LGD = Loss Given Default (typically 60% for senior unsecured) - DF(tᵢ) = Discount Factor Simplified intuition: $$\text{CVA} \approx \text{Expected Exposure} \times \text{Probability of Default} \times \text{Loss Given Default}$$ Worked example: Input Value --- --- Average expected exposure over 5Y $15M Counterparty 5Y CDS spread 200bp (implies ~2% annual PD) Cumulative 5Y PD ~10% LGD 60% CVA $15M × 10% × 60% = $900K This means the derivative is worth $900K LESS than its risk-free value because of the counterparty's credit risk. Who bears CVA? Both parties. The fund adjusts its mark. The dealer adjusts their mark. CVA is a real economic cost that must be charged to the trading desk or absorbed as a portfolio-level cost.

DVA = the market price of YOUR OWN credit risk to counterparties. $$\text{DVA} \approx \text{Counter-party's Expected Exposure to YOU} \times \text{YOUR PD} \times \text{LGD}$$ The perverse accounting result: As your own credit deteriorates → your DVA increases → you book an accounting GAIN. "We're closer to defaulting, so our liabilities are worth less, so — congratulations — we made money." Your credit quality DVA P&L impact --- --- --- Improving DVA decreases Loss (liabilities worth more) Deteriorating DVA increases Gain (liabilities worth less) This is technically correct under mark-to-market but economically bizarre. Many firms and regulators are uncomfortable with it.

FVA accounts for the cost of FUNDING the collateral required by derivatives positions: $$\text{FVA} = \text{Expected Exposure} \times (\text{Funding Spread} - \text{OIS Rate}) \times \text{Duration}$$ If the fund borrows at SOFR + 100bp to post collateral, and the derivative is collateralized at OIS, the fund incurs a funding cost that reduces the trade's value. Why this matters for RM: A trade that looks profitable on a risk-free basis may be UNPROFITABLE after CVA + FVA. The RM should ensure that the PM's P&L reflects these adjustments — or at minimum, that the risk budget accounts for them. ---

Wrong-way risk (WWR) occurs when your exposure to a counterparty INCREASES at the same time as their creditworthiness DECREASES. The correlation between exposure and default probability is POSITIVE — exactly when you need them most, they're least able to pay.

Type Definition Example --- --- --- Specific WWR Direct causal link between counterparty and reference entity Buy CDS on Italian sovereign debt from an Italian bank General WWR Exposure increases in scenarios where counterparty credit also worsens Long emerging market via TRS with an EM-exposed bank; EM crisis → exposure rises AND bank weakens

Trade Counterparty Scenario What happens --- --- --- --- Buy CDS on Italy from Unicredit Italian bank Italy sovereign stress CDS payout owed to you increases; Unicredit's ability to pay collapses Long oil via TRS with energy-focused bank Energy bank Oil crash TRS losses accrue to counterparty; bank's other portfolios also bleeding FX forward: short USD/buy TRY from Turkish bank Turkish bank Turkish lira crisis Your exposure grows as TRY crashes; bank may fail Interest rate swap: receive fixed from a highly-leveraged bank Levered bank Rate spike Bank's bond portfolio loses value; your swap exposure to them increases

Standard CVA models assume exposure and default probability are INDEPENDENT. Wrong-way risk means they're CORRELATED: $$\text{CVA} {\text{WWR}} = \text{CVA} {\text{standard}} \times \text{WWR multiplier}$$ Typical WWR multipliers: 1.3–2.0× for general WWR; 2.0–5.0× for specific WWR. RM action: When approving an OTC trade, ask: "In a scenario where this counterparty is under stress, does our exposure to them INCREASE?" If yes → wrong-way risk. Apply higher CVA, consider collateralization, or find a different counterparty. ---

The International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) Master Agreement governs virtually all bilateral OTC derivative transactions: Component What it covers --- --- Master Agreement Standard legal terms: default events, termination events, close-out netting Schedule Customized terms: specific elections, modifications to standard terms Credit Support Annex (CSA) Collateral terms: what, when, how much Confirmations Individual trade terms under the master framework

Without netting: - You owe Bank X: +$10M on Trade A - Bank X owes you: −$8M on Trade B - If Bank X defaults: you STILL owe $10M on Trade A, but you can only claim $8M as an unsecured creditor on Trade B - Net loss: potentially the full $8M (recovery rate on unsecured claims in bankruptcy: ~40 cents on the dollar → loss = $8M × 0.6 = $4.8M) With netting: - Net position: you owe Bank X $2M - If Bank X defaults: your exposure is $2M, not $8M - Netting REDUCES counterparty exposure by 50–90% in practice Legal enforceability: Netting is ONLY valid if legally enforceable in the counterparty's jurisdiction. In some jurisdictions, bankruptcy "cherry picking" laws allow the administrator to enforce profitable trades and reject unprofitable ones — destroying the netting benefit. ISDA has worked to get netting legal opinions in 70+ jurisdictions.

CSA parameter Typical terms RM concern --- --- --- Valuation frequency Daily Less frequent = more gap risk Threshold $0 – $10M (below this, no collateral required) Higher threshold = more unsecured exposure Minimum transfer amount (MTA) $250K – $1M Prevents nuisance calls; but creates exposure in the gap Eligible collateral Cash (USD, EUR, GBP), G7 government bonds Equities, corp bonds → haircuts apply; non-cash adds complexity Haircuts Cash: 0%. G7 bonds: 1–5%. Corporate bonds: 5–15%. Equities: 15–30% After haircuts, collateral may not fully cover exposure Independent amount (IA) Additional margin beyond MTM — similar to initial margin Protects against gap between collateral calls Rehypothecation Can the receiving party re-use the collateral? Yes → counterparty risk on YOUR collateral. No → safer but ties up assets. RM monitoring: What to track Why Frequency --- --- --- Gross and net exposure by counterparty Concentration Daily Collateral coverage ratio Under-collateralized? Daily Counterparty CDS spreads Credit deterioration Daily Threshold utilization Approaching unsecured limit? Weekly Number of disputes Operational friction / pricing disagreements Monthly Wrong-way risk flags Correlated exposure At trade inception + quarterly ---

Dodd-Frank (US) and EMIR (EU) mandated central clearing for standardized OTC derivatives: Before (bilateral) After (cleared) --- --- Direct exposure to each dealer CCP stands between all parties Negotiated collateral (CSA) Standardized margin (IM + VM) Counterparty risk concentrated in banks Counterparty risk concentrated in CCPs Opaque — regulators couldn't see total exposures Transparent — CCP has full position data

CCP Products Region --- --- --- LCH (SwapClear) Interest rate swaps (70%+ of global IRS) Global (London HQ) CME Clearing Futures, options, IRS, CDS US ICE Clear CDS, energy, equity derivatives US / EU Eurex Clearing Euro-denominated derivatives EU JSCC Yen IRS, CDS Japan

If a clearing member defaults, losses are absorbed in this order: Layer Source Typical size Who bears it --- --- --- --- 1 Defaulter's initial margin $500M+ Defaulter 2 Defaulter's default fund contribution $50M+ Defaulter 3 CCP's own capital ("skin in the game") $100M–$500M CCP shareholders 4 Non-defaulting members' default fund contributions $5B+ collectively All other members (mutualized loss) 5 CCP recovery tools: VM gains haircutting, additional assessments, partial tear-ups Uncapped All members + end-users Layer 4 is critical for hedge funds: Your fund (through its clearing broker) pays into the default fund. If someone ELSE defaults, YOUR money covers THEIR losses. This is mutualized risk — the opposite of bilateral where you only face your own counterparties. Layer 5 is existential: VM gains haircutting means the CCP can take AWAY your profits to cover the default. Partial tear-ups mean the CCP can unilaterally cancel your trades.

Risk Description RM response --- --- --- Margin pro-cyclicality IM increases in stress → fund needs cash exactly when cash is scarce Stress-test margin at 1.5–2× current levels Concentration CCP is a single point of failure for $100T+ notional Monitor CCP health; diversify across CCPs where possible Default fund exposure Fund pays for others' defaults Know maximum assessment; build into liquidity buffer Model risk CCP's risk model determines margin → if wrong, under-margined Monitor CCP margin methodology changes Operational CCP technology failure → can't process trades/margin BCP: what if CCP is down for the day? ---

Operational risk = risk of loss from inadequate or failed internal processes, people, systems, or external events. Basel II defines it formally. For a hedge fund, it's everything that goes wrong that ISN'T a market view being wrong.

Risk Example Control --- --- --- Rogue trading Nick Leeson at Barings — unauthorized positions hidden in error account 88888 Segregation of duties, independent reconciliation, position limits Unauthorized trading Jérôme Kerviel at SocGen — €50B in hidden futures positions Daily P&L attribution, independent position verification Fat finger Deutsche Bank accidentally transferred €28B to a single counterparty (2018) Pre-trade checks, size limits, 4-eye principle Key-person risk Star PM leaves → strategy collapses, LP redemptions follow Documentation of strategy, succession planning, institutional knowledge transfer Fraud Madoff — fictitious trades, fake statements for decades Independent custody, third-party NAV calculation, audited statements Insider trading PM trades on MNPI from management meetings Information barriers, compliance monitoring, trade surveillance

Risk Example Control --- --- --- Failed reconciliation Front office shows +$5M P&L; back office shows +$3M. Who's right? Daily 3-way reconciliation (front office ↔ back office ↔ PB) Settlement failure Trade doesn't settle on T+2 → counterparty exposure extends Settlement monitoring, fail-tracking, escalation Incorrect trade booking Wrong notional, wrong sign (long vs short), wrong maturity Trade confirmation, automated booking checks, independent review Documentation gaps OTC trade executed without signed ISDA confirmation Confirmation tracking, legal review, escalation for unsigned trades NAV miscalculation Wrong marks, missing accruals, incorrect fee calculation Independent fund administrator, pricing committee, Level 3 review

Risk Example Control --- --- --- System outage Trading system down during market hours → can't manage risk Redundancy, failover, BCP drills Bad code deployment Knight Capital: erroneous algorithm deployed; $440M loss in 45 minutes Code review, staged rollout, kill switch, regression testing Data feed failure Pricing engine using stale prices → wrong marks, wrong risk Multiple data vendors, automated stale-data detection Cybersecurity Ransomware locks trading systems; data theft exposes positions Network security, penetration testing, incident response, encryption Algo malfunction Systematic strategy sends orders 10× intended size Pre-trade size checks, circuit breakers, real-time monitoring

Risk Example Control --- --- --- Natural disaster Hurricane destroys data center Geographic redundancy, cloud backup, remote capability Pandemic COVID-19 → entire firm works from home overnight WFH infrastructure, secure remote access, tested BCP Third-party failure Bloomberg terminal outage; data vendor delivers corrupted data Vendor diversification, SLA monitoring, backup data sources Legal/litigation Fund sued by LP for NAV misrepresentation Legal review, compliance documentation, D&O insurance Regulatory action SEC investigation, license revocation Compliance function, regulatory reporting, proactive engagement

section: The Knight Capital Case Study — Technology Risk in Detail
content: Date: August 1, 2012 What happened: 1. Knight Capital deployed new software for NYSE's Retail Liquidity Program 2. A technician failed to deploy the new code to ONE of 8 servers 3. Old code on that server executed a defunct test function — the "Power Peg" flag — which was designed to BUY at the ask and SELL at the bid (the opposite of normal trading) 4. At market open (9:30 AM), the server began aggressively buying high and selling low across 154 stocks 5. By 9:58 AM (28 minutes), the error was identified 6. By 10:15 AM (45 minutes), trading was halted Result: Knight Capital lost $440 million in 45 minutes. The firm had ~$365M in capital. It was dead. Root causes: Failure What went wrong --- --- Code deployment No automated deployment system — manual copy to 8 servers Testing No integration test with the new code + old code on same network Monitoring No real-time P&L monitoring flagged the losses for 28 minutes Kill switch No circuit breaker that would halt trading when P&L hit a threshold Code hygiene Dead code ("Power Peg") left in production for 8 years — never removed RM lessons: 1. Technology IS market risk if it can generate unintended positions 2. Every trading system needs a kill switch with automatic triggers 3. Real-time P&L monitoring must flag anomalies within SECONDS, not minutes 4. Code review, automated deployment, and staged rollout are not optional ---

Line Who Role --- --- --- 1st line Front office (PMs, traders) Own the risk. Follow procedures. Report errors. 2nd line Risk management, compliance Monitor, challenge, guide. Set policies. Escalate. 3rd line Internal audit, external audit Independent verification. Test that controls work.

Control What it does Frequency --- --- --- 3-way reconciliation Front office positions = Back office = PB records Daily Independent pricing Marks verified against independent sources (not just PB/dealer) Daily P&L attribution Explain P&L by position, factor, residual — catch unexplained P&L Daily Trade confirmation All OTC trades confirmed with counterparties within T+1 Daily 4-eye principle Every material action (trade entry, limit override, cash transfer) requires 2 approvals Every occurrence Access controls Restricted system access: who can trade, who can modify limits, who can approve exceptions Continuous Segregation of duties Trader ≠ back office ≠ risk. No single person controls the full chain. By design Limit breach escalation Any limit breach → immediate notification → required response within defined timeframe Every occurrence BCP testing Business continuity plan tested: can the fund operate if the primary office is unavailable? Semi-annual Disaster recovery drill Can systems fail over to backup? How long? Is data preserved? Annual

Indicator What it signals Red flag --- --- --- Number of trade breaks (recon mismatches) Process quality >3 unresolved breaks per week Settlement failure rate Counterparty/process issues >1% of trades failing System downtime (hours/month) Technology reliability >2 hours unplanned downtime P&L unexplained residual Missing/wrong positions, pricing errors Residual >5% of daily P&L Compliance exceptions Control bypasses Increasing trend Staff turnover in operations Knowledge loss, key-person risk >20% annual turnover Time to confirm OTC trades Documentation risk >T+3 average ---

section: Basel Framework (Banks) — Why It Matters for Hedge Funds
content: You're not a bank. You won't be regulated under Basel. But your PRIME BROKERS are. Their capital constraints DIRECTLY affect you: Basel requirement Impact on PB Impact on YOUR fund --- --- --- CET1 ratio (4.5%+) PB must hold capital against risk-weighted assets Higher capital charge for PB → higher financing cost for you Leverage ratio (3%) Limits PB's total balance sheet regardless of risk weights PBs cut low-margin businesses (e.g., repo financing) → less capacity for you LCR (Liquidity Coverage Ratio) PB must hold liquid assets against 30-day stress outflows PB may treat your deposits as "runnable" → penalizing you NSFR (Net Stable Funding Ratio) PB must match illiquid assets with stable funding Short-term financing to HFs is "unstable" → PB charges more or reduces capacity SLR (Supplementary Leverage Ratio) Constrains bank balance sheets including derivatives exposure Particularly impacts repo-heavy strategies and large derivative books

section: FRTB (Fundamental Review of the Trading Book)
content: The new market risk capital standard replacing the internal models approach: Feature Old approach (VaR-based) FRTB --- --- --- Risk metric VaR (99%, 10-day) Expected Shortfall (97.5%, variable horizon) Tail sensitivity Cuts off at VaR — ignores what's beyond ES captures average loss beyond VaR Liquidity horizon Single 10-day horizon Instrument-dependent: 10 days (liquid) to 120 days (illiquid) Non-modellable risk factors (NMRFs) Not explicitly captured Must capitalize NMRFs separately (often large) P&L attribution test Not required Desks must prove their model's P&L matches actual P&L Boundary Trading book vs banking book somewhat flexible Stricter boundary → less regulatory arbitrage Impact on hedge funds: - Strategies using ILLIQUID instruments (credit, distressed, structured products) → PBs face higher capital charges → higher financing costs to you - NMRFs mean exotic/bespoke OTC products become more expensive to hold for dealers → wider spreads to you - PBs may EXIT certain businesses entirely if capital charges make them uneconomical

Provision What it does HF impact --- --- --- Volcker Rule Prohibits bank proprietary trading Talent migration from banks to HFs. PBs can't warehouse risk as easily → less liquidity. OTC clearing mandate Standardized swaps must clear via CCP Margin requirements increase; but counterparty risk reduced Swap dealer registration Dealers must register, report, follow conduct rules More transparency; compliance cost Form PF Large HFs (>$1.5B) file quarterly; smaller ($150M+) file annually AUM, leverage, exposure, concentration, counterparty data reported to SEC/CFTC Systemic risk monitoring FSOC can designate non-bank SIFIs Theoretically a large HF could be designated → bank-like regulation (hasn't happened yet)

Requirement Detail HF impact --- --- --- Central clearing Mandatory for standardized OTC derivatives (IRS, CDS index) Must clear through authorized CCPs; margin requirements Trade reporting ALL OTC derivative trades reported to trade repositories Compliance cost; but improves market transparency Risk mitigation for non-cleared Timely confirmation, portfolio reconciliation, dispute resolution, margin exchange Must post IM and VM for bilateral trades above thresholds EMIR Refit (2024) Simplified reporting, reduced burden for smaller firms Some relief for smaller HFs

section: MiFID II (EU — Markets in Financial Instruments Directive)
content: Provision What it does HF impact --- --- --- Best execution Must demonstrate best execution across venues; document execution quality Need TCA (Transaction Cost Analysis) infrastructure Pre/post-trade transparency Expanded to non-equity instruments (bonds, derivatives) More data available; but also more scrutiny of fund's execution Research unbundling Research payments must be separated from execution commissions Funds must pay for research explicitly (RPA) — can't bundle with commissions Position reporting Commodity derivative position limits and reporting Affects commodity-trading funds Systematic internaliser regime Firms executing OTC at scale treated as quasi-exchanges Affects execution venues used by funds

section: AIFMD (Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive — EU)
content: Requirement Detail --- --- Authorization EU-based AIFM must be authorized by national regulator Leverage reporting Must report leverage levels (gross and commitment method) Depositary Must appoint independent depositary (custodian) for fund assets Remuneration Senior management compensation subject to deferral and clawback rules Marketing passport Authorized AIFM can market to professional investors across the EU Leverage limits NCA can impose leverage limits in systemic risk situations

section: SEC / CFTC (US) — Key Rules for Hedge Funds
content: Rule What it requires Gap/issue --- --- --- 13F filing Quarterly disclosure of long equity positions (>$100M manager AUM) SHORT positions NOT reported. TRS NOT reported (Archegos gap). Form PF Quarterly/annual reporting of AUM, leverage, exposure, counterparties Filed confidentially — not public Regulation SHO Short-sale rules: locate requirement before shorting, circuit breaker on -10% moves Doesn't capture synthetic shorts via TRS Regulation D Exemption allowing HFs to raise capital without SEC registration Fund must only accept accredited/qualified investors Advisers Act HF managers with >$150M AUM must register as investment advisers with SEC Compliance, ADV filing, books and records, custody rules CFTC registration Commodity pool operators / commodity trading advisors must register If trading futures/options on futures/swaps in significant volume

section: The 13F Reporting Gap — The Archegos Problem
content: What 13F captures What 13F MISSES --- --- Long equity positions (stocks, ETFs, convertibles, call options) Short positions — no public reporting Held at quarter-end with >$200K market value TRS / synthetic positions — not beneficial ownership Manager-level aggregation Cross-PB exposure — each PB sees only their slice Archegos exploited ALL three gaps: 1. Used TRS → no 13F filing → market didn't know about $20B in concentrated positions 2. Used 5 different PBs → no single PB saw the total picture 3. Regulators had no visibility until the collapse Post-Archegos reforms (SEC, 2023-2025): - Proposed: expanded 13F to include shorts and TRS - Proposed: enhanced Form PF with more granular position data - Status: partially implemented, still evolving ---

section: PART 9: REGULATORY RISK — THE RISK OF REGULATION ITSELF
content: Beyond COMPLIANCE with regulation, there's the risk that regulation CHANGES in ways that damage your strategy: Regulatory change risk Example Impact --- --- --- Short-selling bans EU banned short-selling during COVID (March 2020) L/S equity funds couldn't hedge → forced to go net long or degross entirely Position limits CFTC commodity position limits tightened Commodity funds must reduce positions Margin increases EMIR margin requirements for non-cleared derivatives Bilateral OTC trades become more expensive Tax changes Transaction tax (FTT) proposals in EU Would significantly impact high-turnover systematic strategies Reporting burden New reporting requirements → compliance cost increase Diverts resources from investing to reporting Strategy prohibition Volcker Rule eliminated bank prop trading Banks exited → reduced liquidity AND reduced competition for HFs RM responsibility: Track regulatory pipeline. When regulation is PROPOSED (not just enacted), begin assessing impact: 1. Which positions/strategies are affected? 2. What's the compliance cost? 3. Does this create forced selling/buying that moves markets? 4. Can we benefit from others' forced compliance? (Regulatory arbitrage) ---

section: The RM's Integrated Non-Market Risk Monitor
content: Risk type Key metrics Monitoring frequency Escalation trigger --- --- --- --- Counterparty Net exposure by CP, CVA, PFE, CDS spreads Daily Single CP > 5% of NAV; CDS widens >100bp Collateral Coverage ratio, disputes, rehypothecation level Daily Under-collateralized > $5M; dispute >3 days CCP IM requirements, default fund exposure Daily IM increase >20%; assessment notice received Operational Trade breaks, settlement failures, system uptime Daily >3 breaks; >1% fail rate; unplanned downtime >30 min Cyber Incident count, phishing attempts, access violations Weekly Any successful breach; privilege escalation Regulatory Reporting deadlines, limit compliance, pipeline changes Weekly Filing deadline <5 days; regulatory inquiry received People Key-person concentration, turnover, compliance exceptions Monthly Key departure; rising exception trend ---

"What types of risk exist beyond market risk?" "Three main categories. Counterparty risk — the risk that the other side of an OTC trade defaults. This is managed through ISDA netting agreements, CSA collateral posting, and central clearing via CCPs. The subtle version is wrong-way risk, where your exposure to a counterparty increases exactly as their credit deteriorates — buying CDS on Italian debt from an Italian bank is the classic example. Operational risk — the risk of internal failures in people, processes, and technology. This is what killed Barings — Leeson controlled both trading and back office with no segregation of duties. And Knight Capital — $440M lost in 45 minutes because of a failed code deployment. As an RM, I'd focus on daily 3-way reconciliation, independent pricing verification, and ensuring no single person controls the full trade lifecycle. Regulatory risk — both compliance risk and the risk that regulation changes and impacts our strategy. Short-selling bans during COVID forced L/S funds to degross. FRTB is increasing capital charges for PBs, which flows through as higher financing costs. And the 13F reporting gap — where TRS positions are invisible — is exactly what enabled Archegos. That gap is slowly being closed but isn't fully fixed yet." "How would you monitor counterparty risk?" "Three layers. First, ongoing monitoring — daily net exposure by counterparty, daily collateral coverage ratios, and daily CDS spread tracking for each major counterparty. If CDS widens 100bp in a week, that's an early warning. Second, structural protection — ensuring we have enforceable ISDA netting, CSA collateral with daily margin calls and low thresholds, and where possible, central clearing. Third, wrong-way risk screening. At every new trade approval, I'd ask: in a stress scenario where this counterparty is weakening, does our exposure to them increase? If yes, we need to either collateralize more heavily, find a different counterparty, or adjust the CVA charge to reflect the correlation. And at the portfolio level, I'd monitor single-counterparty concentration. No single counterparty should hold more than 5% of our net exposure. This is the Lehman lesson — when one counterparty fails, you need to survive without them." "Walk me through the key operational controls you'd implement." "The foundation is segregation of duties — the person who trades cannot reconcile their own positions. That's Barings, and it's non-negotiable. On top of that: daily 3-way reconciliation between front office, back office, and the prime broker. If those three don't match, SOMETHING is wrong — and we find it before it becomes a problem. For pricing: independent mark verification. We don't just take the PB's marks — we get independent sources. For Level 3 assets especially, where there's no observable market price, we need a pricing committee and potentially third-party valuations. For technology: real-time P&L monitoring with automatic alerts. Knight Capital l

A trader says "I'm adding to my long-duration position because the Fed is behind the curve." A quant shows a regime-switch model predicting stagflation with 72% probability. Constantine asks "where are we in the cycle, and what does that mean for cross-asset correlation?" If you can't engage with these statements — decompose the macro logic, challenge the assumptions, quantify the portfolio consequences — you're reading Bloomberg headlines, not managing risk. Macro fundamentals are the connective tissue between individual trades and portfolio-level risk. Every position has embedded macro assumptions: a curve steepener assumes growth or term premium is rising. A long equity / short rates trade assumes Goldilocks. A PM who says "I have no macro view" is lying — they're just not aware of the macro embedded in their book. ---

$$Y = C + I + G + (X - M)$$ Component US share What drives it Lead indicators --- --- --- --- C — Consumption ~68% Disposable income, wealth effect, credit conditions, confidence Retail sales, Michigan sentiment, credit card data I — Investment ~18% Interest rates, CEO confidence, capacity utilization Durable goods orders, CAPEX surveys, permits G — Government ~17% Fiscal policy, appropriations, defense spending CBO projections, Treasury issuance calendars NX — Net exports ~-3% FX rates, relative growth, tariffs, supply chains Trade balance, container volumes, PMI export orders Real vs Nominal: $$\text{Real GDP growth} \approx \text{Nominal GDP growth} - \text{GDP deflator}$$ If nominal GDP grows 5% and the deflator is 4%, real growth is ~1%. The economy feels like it's growing but mostly from price increases. This distinction matters enormously for risk — nominal earnings can grow while real economic activity contracts.

$$\text{Output Gap} = \frac{Y {\text{actual}} - Y {\text{potential}}}{Y {\text{potential}}} \times 100$$ Gap Meaning Inflation pressure Policy response --- --- --- --- Positive (>0) Economy above trend Inflationary — demand > supply Tighten Zero At potential Neutral Hold Negative (<0) Economy below trend Disinflationary — slack Ease Why it matters for risk: The output gap determines whether inflation pressures are cyclical or structural. A positive output gap with rising inflation = overheating (rate hikes coming). A negative output gap with rising inflation = supply-side shock (policy dilemma). The distinction drives completely different portfolio outcomes.

$$\Delta U \approx -0.5 \times (g Y - g Y^ )$$ For every 1% GDP growth above trend (~2%), unemployment falls ~0.5pp. This relationship is approximate but gives a quick sanity check: if someone claims GDP will grow 3% with rising unemployment, something is inconsistent.

section: The Taylor Rule — Policy Rate Framework
content: $$r^ = r {\text{neutral}} + 0.5({\pi} - {\pi^ }) + 0.5(y - y^ )$$ Where: - $r^ $ = prescribed policy rate - $r {\text{neutral}}$ = neutral real rate (~2.5% nominal currently) - $\pi - \pi^ $ = inflation gap (actual minus 2% target) - $y - y^ $ = output gap Worked example (April 2026): - Neutral rate: 2.5% - Core PCE: 3.2%, target 2.0% → gap = +1.2% - Output gap estimate: +0.3% - Taylor Rule: $2.5 + 0.5(1.2) + 0.5(0.3) = 2.5 + 0.6 + 0.15 = 3.25\%$ - Actual fed funds: 3.75% Interpretation: The Fed is above the Taylor Rule prescription. But with headline inflation re-accelerating from oil, the "correct" rate is debatable. Hawks argue effective inflation gap is larger; doves argue the oil shock is supply-side and shouldn't be fought with rates.

Indicator What it signals Frequency Key thresholds --- --- --- --- ISM Manufacturing PMI Factory sector health Monthly, 1st biz day >50 expansion, <50 contraction, <45 recession ISM Services PMI 80% of economy Monthly, 3rd biz day >50 expansion, <53 slowing Initial jobless claims Real-time labor distress Weekly, Thursday <220K strong, >300K recession signal Continuing claims Length of unemployment Weekly Rising = difficulty finding work Conference Board LEI Composite of 10 leading indicators Monthly 6+ months declining = recession Atlanta Fed GDPNow Real-time GDP tracking Multiple/week Tracks consensus vs surprise direction Yield curve (2Y-10Y) Recession predictor Continuous Inversion precedes recessions by 6-18 months NFIB Small Business Optimism Small-biz hiring/capex intent Monthly Below 90 = pessimistic ---

Component CPI-U weight What drives it Lag structure --- --- --- --- Shelter (rent + OER) ~36% Rents, home prices, vacancy rates 12-18 month lag to new leases Food at home ~8% Agricultural supply, transport, weather 2-4 month pass-through Food away from home ~5% Wages (labor-intensive), rent Sticky — adjusted 1-2x/year Energy ~7% Crude oil, natural gas, electricity Immediate to 2-week pass-through New vehicles ~4% Supply chain, incentives, input costs 1-3 month pass-through Used vehicles ~2.5% Supply/demand, fleet age, credit Fast-moving, mean-reverting Medical care ~6.5% Insurance resets, drug prices, labor Annual reset cycle (January) Core services ex-housing ~25% Wages, labor market tightness 3-6 month wage pass-through

Measure What it captures Who uses it Current level --- --- --- --- CPI-U YoY Headline, all items Media, Treasury (TIPS) Elevated from oil Core CPI YoY Ex food/energy Markets, tactical Was declining, now re-accelerating Core CPI MoM Monthly momentum Traders — THE number >0.3% = too hot, <0.2% = progress PCE YoY Broader basket, chain-weighted Fed's official target Typically 30-40bp below CPI Core PCE YoY Fed's actual target gauge FOMC decisions ~3.2% vs 2.0% target Supercore (core services ex-housing) Wage-driven inflation Powell specifically cites this Sticky — the persistence signal Trimmed mean (Dallas Fed) Removes outliers both ways Underlying trend Smooths energy/food noise Sticky CPI (Atlanta Fed) Items that change slowly Persistence forecast High = inflation entrenched

$$\pi t = \pi^e + \beta(U^ - U t) + \epsilon t$$ Where: - $\pi^e$ = inflation expectations - $U^ $ = NAIRU (non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment ≈ 4.0%) - $U t$ = actual unemployment - $\epsilon t$ = supply shocks When $U t < U^ $: Labor market tighter than sustainable → wage pressure → inflation rises. When $U t > U^ $: Slack in labor market → wage pressure eases → inflation falls. The problem today: The Phillips curve has been flat for a decade — low unemployment didn't produce proportional inflation. Then 2021-2023 showed it can steepen violently when hit with simultaneous demand and supply shocks. The curve's slope is regime-dependent. Risk managers should not assume a stable relationship.

section: Inflation Transmission — Oil Shock → Sticky Inflation
content: Stage 1 (Immediate): Oil spike → gasoline, heating, jet fuel ↑ Stage 2 (1-2 months): Transport costs ↑ → shipping, trucking, rail Stage 3 (2-4 months): Food prices ↑ (transport + fertilizer inputs) Stage 4 (2-4 months): Business input costs ↑ → margins compress Stage 5 (3-6 months): Businesses pass costs to consumers → core CPI ↑ Stage 6 (6-12 months): Workers demand wage increases to offset cost of living Stage 7 (12-18 months): Wages ↑ → businesses raise prices → wage-price spiral Stages 1-4 = cost-push (transitory if shock reverses). Stages 5-7 = demand-pull feedback (self-reinforcing). The Fed can look through Stages 1-4. If Stage 6 begins, they MUST respond or expectations de-anchor. Monitoring the transition: - Stage 1-2: Energy weight in CPI, PPI pipeline - Stage 3-4: PPI finished goods, ISM Prices Paid - Stage 5: Core CPI acceleration, trimmed mean - Stage 6: Atlanta Fed Wage Tracker, ECI (Employment Cost Index), quits rate - Stage 7: Michigan/NY Fed 1Y and 5Y inflation expectations, TIPS breakevens

Measure Type Anchored signal De-anchoring signal --- --- --- --- Michigan 1Y Survey (consumers) 2.5-3.5% >5% sustained Michigan 5-10Y Survey (long-run) 2.5-3.0% >3.5% sustained TIPS 5Y breakeven Market-derived 2.0-2.5% >3.0% sustained 5Y5Y forward Market, further out 2.0-2.3% >2.5% sustained NY Fed 3Y median Survey (richer data) 2.5-3.0% >4.0% SPF 10Y Professional forecasters 2.0-2.3% >2.5% Current risk: Hormuz oil shock has pushed gasoline to visible levels. Consumer surveys are the first to move because gas prices are the most salient price households see. If 1Y expectations hit 5%+ for 3+ months and 5Y expectations start drifting above 3.5%, we're in de-anchoring territory. That triggers aggressive Fed response regardless of growth. ---

1. Maximum employment (~4.0% NAIRU) 2. Price stability (2.0% PCE target, symmetric) When these objectives conflict — rising inflation AND weakening labor market — the Fed faces a dilemma. Historical precedent: they almost always prioritize inflation first (credibility), then pivot to growth once inflation is contained. Volcker (1980), Powell (2022-2023).

Central bank Mandate Target Current rate Balance sheet Key difference --- --- --- --- --- --- Federal Reserve Dual (employment + inflation) 2% PCE 3.75% ~$7.5T (QT running) Dual mandate → more dovish toggle ECB Price stability (primary) 2% HICP 3.25% ~€5T Single mandate → more hawkish on inflation Bank of England Price stability (primary), growth (secondary) 2% CPI 4.50% ~£750B UK politics adds pressure; gilt market fragility (2022 LDI crisis) Bank of Japan Price stability + YCC legacy 2% CPI 0.50% ~¥760T (>100% GDP) Exiting 30 years of deflation; JPY weakness; carry trade anchor PBoC Multiple (growth, FX, stability) None explicit MLF 2.50% Capital controls State-directed; USDCNY managed float; RRR as primary tool

section: Why Central Bank Divergence Matters for Risk
content: When central banks move at different speeds, FX rates move. The carry trade connects them: $$\text{Carry} = r {\text{invest}} - r {\text{fund}} - E \Delta \text{FX} $$ If the Fed holds at 3.75% while the BoJ is at 0.50%, the rate differential incentivizes borrow-JPY/buy-USD. This keeps USDJPY elevated. If the BoJ hikes aggressively or the Fed cuts, the carry unwinds violently (see August 2024 JPY carry unwind — Nikkei -12% in 2 days). For risk managers: Check PM books for embedded carry assumptions. A PM long EM local-currency bonds is implicitly short dollar strength and long EM central bank credibility. If the Fed signals higher-for-longer while EM central banks cut, that position gets crushed on FX.

Policy rate change ├── SHORT END (immediate) │ ├── Money market rates (fed funds, SOFR, repo) │ ├── Commercial paper, T-bill yields │ └── Bank funding costs ├── FINANCIAL CONDITIONS (1-3 months) │ ├── Mortgage rates → housing demand │ ├── Corporate bond yields → capex decisions │ ├── Equity valuations (discount rate effect) │ ├── Credit availability (bank lending standards) │ └── Dollar strength/weakness → trade competitiveness ├── REAL ECONOMY (3-12 months) │ ├── Consumer spending (credit cost, wealth effect) │ ├── Business investment (hurdle rate comparison) │ ├── Housing construction (starts, permits) │ └── Net exports (FX effect on competitiveness) └── INFLATION (12-24 months) ├── Demand reduction → output gap widens ├── Labor market loosens → wage pressure falls └── Expectations adjust → self-reinforcing disinflation The lag problem: Monetary policy operates with long and variable lags (Friedman). The full impact of a rate change takes 12-24 months. This means the Fed is always flying partially blind — they're reacting to data that reflects past policy, not current conditions.

section: The Fed's Toolkit — Ranked by Usage
content: Tool Mechanism When deployed Current status --- --- --- --- Fed funds rate Overnight interbank rate → anchors all short rates Primary (always) 3.75%, on hold Forward guidance Shapes expectations → moves term rates without action Continuous "Data dependent" = ambiguous Dot plot FOMC median rate projections → anchors expectations Quarterly (SEP) Showing path uncertainty QE Buy Treasuries/MBS → suppress long rates, inject reserves Crisis (2008, 2020) Not active QT Let bonds mature → drain reserves, raise long rates Normalization ~$60B/month roll-off Reverse repo (RRP) Absorb excess liquidity → floor under short rates Daily operations Declining as reserves normalize Discount window Lender of last resort → emergency liquidity Bank stress Available; stigma reduced post-SVB Emergency facilities BTFP, CPFF, etc. → targeted market dysfunction Acute crisis Authorization lapsed ---

The yield curve plots yields against maturity. Its shape encodes market expectations about growth, inflation, and risk: $$y n = \frac{1}{n} \sum {t=1}^{n} E r t + \text{Term Premium} n$$ Theory Explanation Implication --- --- --- Expectations hypothesis Long rates = average of expected future short rates Curve shape reflects rate path expectations Liquidity preference Investors demand premium for holding longer maturities Curve normally upward-sloping (term premium > 0) Preferred habitat Supply/demand at each maturity segment matters independently QE at long end can depress long rates Market segmentation Different investors at different maturities → can create kinks Pension demand at 30Y → ultra-long bid

Shape What it signals Historical accuracy Portfolio implication --- --- --- --- Normal (steep) Growth optimism, policy accommodation Standard in expansions Pro-risk, banks outperform (NIM ↑) Flat Transition — late cycle, policy uncertainty Precedes regime shift Reduce risk, increase liquidity Inverted (2Y > 10Y) Market expects rate cuts → recession coming 8/8 last recessions (12-18 month lead) Defensive, long duration (if recession materializes) Bear steepening Long rates rising faster than short → term premium ↑ Fiscal concern, supply worry Risk-off for levered positions; mortgages crushed Bull steepening Short rates falling faster → Fed cutting Early easing cycle Pro-risk transition, rate-sensitive rally Bear flattening Short rates rising faster → Fed hiking Active tightening cycle Credit spreads widen, equity multiple compression Bull flattening Long rates falling faster → flight to quality Risk-off, recession fear Defensive, TLT rallies, equities sell

Spread Calculation What it signals Current level matters because --- --- --- --- 2Y-10Y 10Y yield - 2Y yield Recession predictor Was inverted, now re-steepening: normalization or term premium? 3M-10Y 10Y - 3M T-bill Fed's preferred recession signal More sensitive to near-term policy expectations 10Y-30Y 30Y - 10Y Ultra-long term premium Pension demand, fiscal sustainability worry TIPS breakeven (5Y) Nominal 5Y - TIPS 5Y Market inflation expectation De-anchoring monitor 5Y5Y forward Derived from forward curve Long-run inflation expectation The Fed's preferred market measure TED spread 3M LIBOR/SOFR - 3M T-bill Banking system stress Spikes in crises (2008: 450bp)

section: Worked Example — Reading a Curve Move
content: Scenario: 10Y up 25bp, 2Y up 5bp (bear steepening) What happened: Long-end sold off more than short-end. Decomposition: - Fed policy expectations (short-end): Barely moved → market doesn't expect near-term rate changes - Term premium (long-end): Increased → either fiscal supply concerns, inflation risk premium, or foreign selling - Real rate vs breakeven: Check if the 25bp was real rates (growth/fiscal) or breakevens (inflation) Portfolio impact: - Duration-sensitive positions: 10Y DV01 = $87.50/bp per $1M notional → $2,187.50 loss per $1M exposure on 25bp move - Mortgage REITs: Crushed (funding at short end, assets at long end, spread compressed) - Banks: Mixed — deposit costs stable (short end flat), loan rates higher, but credit risk up if term premium reflects growth concern - Equities: Growth stocks hurt more (higher discount rate), value/commodity relatively better ---

Monetary tight Monetary easy --- --- --- Fiscal expansionary CONFLICT: Deficit spending adds demand while Fed removes it → rates higher, dollar stronger, crowding out ALIGNED STIMULUS: Both adding demand → risk of overheating, asset prices ↑↑ Fiscal contractionary OVERKILL: Both removing demand → deep recession risk, deflation CONFLICT: Fiscal drag partially offsets easing → muddles recovery Current US (April 2026): Fiscal expansionary (~6% deficit/GDP) + monetary restrictive (3.75% + QT). Classic conflict. Result: sticky inflation (fiscal keeps demand up), higher long-end rates (supply), and constrained Fed flexibility.

$$\Delta \left(\frac{D}{Y}\right) = (r - g) \times \frac{D}{Y} + \text{Primary Deficit}$$ Where: - $D/Y$ = debt-to-GDP ratio - $r$ = average interest rate on government debt - $g$ = nominal GDP growth rate - Primary deficit = government deficit excluding interest payments The key insight: If $g > r$ (growth exceeds interest costs), the debt ratio can be stabilized even with moderate primary deficits. If $r > g$ (interest costs exceed growth), the debt ratio spirals without primary surpluses. Current US numbers: - Debt/GDP: ~125% - Weighted average interest on Treasury debt: ~3.2% (rising as old low-rate debt matures and is refinanced) - Nominal GDP growth: ~4.5% - Currently g > r, but the gap is narrowing as rates stay elevated and refinancing occurs Why this matters for portfolio risk: 1. Term premium: If markets doubt fiscal sustainability, they demand higher yields on long bonds → bear steepening 2. Crowding out: Government bond supply competes with corporate issuance → wider credit spreads 3. FX impact: Fiscal dominance perception → currency weakness (see UK gilt crisis September 2022: Truss mini-budget → GBP -5% in days, 30Y gilt +150bp) 4. Rating downgrades: S&P downgraded US in 2011, Fitch in 2023. Future downgrades would trigger forced selling from mandated-investment-grade portfolios

What happened: UK PM Truss announced £45B unfunded tax cuts. Markets repriced UK fiscal trajectory. 30Y gilt yields spiked ~150bp in days. LDI (liability-driven investment) pension funds hit margin calls → forced selling of gilts → further price drops → doom loop. RM lessons: 1. Fiscal credibility matters — markets can revolt against sovereign borrowers 2. Leverage + illiquidity + rates = toxic (LDI funds were levered 4-7x on gilt exposure) 3. Cross-asset contagion: gilt → GBP → UK equities → global rate vol → US Treasuries 4. Central bank as backstop: BoE intervened with £65B emergency purchasing — but only after the crash ---

Three time horizons, three drivers: Horizon Primary driver Key relationship --- --- --- Short-term (days-weeks) Interest rate differentials, risk sentiment, positioning Higher rates → currency appreciation (carry) Medium-term (months-quarters) Current account, relative growth, policy divergence BoP flows, portfolio rebalancing Long-term (years) Purchasing power parity (PPP), productivity Currencies converge toward fair value

Covered interest rate parity (CIP): $$F = S \times \frac{(1 + r d)}{(1 + r f)}$$ Where $F$ = forward rate, $S$ = spot rate, $r d$ = domestic rate, $r f$ = foreign rate. In theory, the forward premium/discount exactly offsets the interest rate differential, so there's no riskless profit. CIP deviations (basis) signal banking system stress or regulation-driven distortions. Uncovered interest rate parity (UIP) — often violated: The currency with the higher interest rate should depreciate to offset the carry. In practice, it doesn't (at least in the short run) — this is the carry trade's foundation.

$$\text{Carry P\&L} = \text{Notional} \times (r {\text{invest}} - r {\text{fund}}) + \text{FX P\&L}$$ Example (USDJPY carry): - Borrow JPY at 0.50%, invest in USD at 3.75% → 325bp positive carry per year - If USDJPY stays flat: pure carry profit - Risk: If JPY appreciates 3.25%+ per year, the carry is wiped out - Tail risk: Sudden JPY appreciation of 10-15% in days (August 2024: USDJPY fell from 161 to 141 in weeks) RM implications: - Carry trades are short vol — they earn steady returns until they blow up - Portfolio scanning: Check for embedded carry across asset classes (EM bonds, basis trades, cross-currency positions) - Crowding indicator: When the same carry trade is consensus, the unwind is violent

Factor Assessment metric Fragile signal --- --- --- Current account CA/GDP ratio Deficit > 5% of GDP FX reserves Reserves / short-term external debt < 100% (Guidotti rule) External debt FX-denominated debt / GDP > 60% Inflation differential Domestic CPI vs US CPI > 5pp → likely depreciation Real rate differential Policy rate - CPI vs Fed rate - US CPI Negative real rate differential → outflow risk Political stability Institutional quality, rule of law Elections, regime uncertainty The "Fragile Five" template: Any EM with twin deficits (fiscal + current account), high FX debt, and low real rates is vulnerable to a Fed-driven tightening in global dollar liquidity. History: 2013 taper tantrum hit BRL, INR, TRY, ZAR, IDR.

The USD strengthens in two opposite scenarios: 1. US exceptionalism: US growth outperforms → capital inflows → USD ↑ (right side of smile) 2. Global crisis: Flight to safety → USD ↑ regardless of US fundamentals (left side of smile) 3. Middle (weakness): Global synchronized growth → risk appetite → capital flows to non-US markets → USD ↓ Current position on the smile: Debatable. Oil shock could push toward left side (risk-off, safe haven USD) OR right side (US relative outperformance if it's self-sufficient in energy). The ambiguity itself creates vol. ---

Growth ↑ Growth ↓ --- --- --- Inflation ↑ REFLATION — Pro-cyclical overheating. Equities +, commodities +, bonds -. STAGFLATION — Worst quadrant. Equities -, commodities +, bonds -. Nothing works except energy/commodities and CTA trend. Inflation ↓ GOLDILOCKS — Best quadrant. Equities ++, bonds +, credit tight, vol low. DEFLATION — Risk-off. Equities -, bonds ++, credit widens, USD ↑. Asset allocation implications by regime: Regime Equities Duration Credit Commodities Vol FX --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Goldilocks OW growth Neutral OW tight UW Sell Short USD Reflation OW value/cyclical UW (rates up) Neutral OW Neutral EM +, commodity FX + Stagflation UW UW (rates up) UW OW energy Buy USD +, commodity FX + Deflation UW OW (rates down) UW (defaults) UW Buy USD ++ (safe haven) Where are we now (April 2026)? Transitioning from Goldilocks (late 2025 — disinflation + moderate growth) toward Reflation or Stagflation depending on whether the oil shock kills growth. This is the crux of the current debate. If growth holds → Reflation (manageable). If growth cracks → Stagflation (dangerous).

This is ONE of the most important concepts for portfolio risk: Regime Stock-bond correlation Why Diversification --- --- --- --- Growth-driven (pre-2020) Negative — stocks down → bonds up Rate cuts expected in downturn 60/40 works, bonds hedge equities Inflation-driven (2022-2023) Positive — stocks down AND bonds down Inflation → rates up → both sell off 60/40 breaks, need alternatives Current (2026) Unstable — oscillating between regimes Oil shock creates inflation-growth ambiguity Correlation can flip intra-day $$\rho {\text{stock,bond}} \approx f(\text{inflation vol}, \text{growth vol}, \text{policy regime})$$ Rule of thumb: When inflation is the dominant risk, correlation is positive. When growth is the dominant risk, correlation is negative. When both are risk, correlation is unstable and hedging is hardest. RM action: Monitor rolling 3M stock-bond correlation daily. When it flips sign, reassess all hedge ratios. A portfolio hedged with duration in a negative-correlation regime will suffer if correlation turns positive — the hedge becomes an additional source of loss.

Trough: Banks cautious, spreads wide, defaults peaking → Survivors refinance at high rates Recovery: Banks loosen, spreads tighten, defaults falling → Credit creation expands, animal spirits rise Expansion: Easy money, tight spreads, covenants weaken → Leverage builds, zombie companies survive on cheap debt Peak: Spreads at cycle lows, leverage at highs, complacency → Any shock → defaults spike Downturn: Banks tighten, spreads widen, defaults rising → Forced selling, margin calls, contagion Key metrics: - HY OAS: Current vs 5Y average → tighter = late cycle - IG issuance: High issuance at tight spreads = peak behavior - Default rate forecast: Moody's trailing 12M default rate - Leverage ratios: Net debt/EBITDA for HY universe - Senior Loan Officer Survey (SLOOS): Tightening standards predict recession with 4-6 quarter lead ---

Release Frequency Key number Market reaction logic --- --- --- --- Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP) Monthly, 1st Friday Headline jobs + unemployment rate + AHE >250K = strong (hawkish), <100K = alarm (dovish), AHE MoM >0.4% = wage spiral CPI Monthly, ~10th-15th Core MoM = THE number >0.3% = too hot → rates up, equities down. <0.2% = progress → rates down, equities up FOMC Decision 8x/year Rate decision + statement + dot plot + presser Tone matters more than action when rate is on hold PCE Deflator Monthly, end of month Core PCE YoY = Fed's target measure Confirms/contradicts CPI signal GDP Quarterly (advance, 2nd, 3rd) Real GDP QoQ annualized <1% = recession worry. >3% = overheating worry

Release Key insight What professionals focus on --- --- --- ISM Manufacturing PMI Leading indicator of cycle turn New orders vs inventories ratio (below 1.0 = contraction signal) ISM Services PMI 80% of economy represented Prices Paid sub-index (inflation pipeline) Retail Sales Consumer spending health Ex-auto, ex-gas "control group" = GDP input JOLTS Labor demand leading indicator Job openings / unemployed (Beveridge curve) Initial Claims Real-time labor distress 4-week moving average > 250K = deterioration PPI Upstream price pressure → future CPI Final demand vs intermediate goods pipeline Michigan Sentiment Consumer inflation expectations 1Y and 5-10Y expected inflation

section: How to Read an NFP Report
content: 1. Headline number (births-deaths adjustment matters): Large positive = strong labor market (hawkish), but check prior month revision — often revised ±50K 2. Unemployment rate: U-3 (headline) vs U-6 (broad, includes underemployment). U-6 gap tells you about labor market slack 3. Average Hourly Earnings (AHE): MoM > 0.4% = wage-push inflation concern. YoY > 4% with 2% productivity = unit labor costs rising 4. Labor force participation rate: If UE falls because people leave the workforce (not because they got jobs), it's not a strong signal 5. Sectoral breakdown: Government hiring doesn't count the same as private. Healthcare vs goods-producing tells you about the economy's composition

section: How to Read a CPI Print
content: 1. Headline MoM and YoY — the press headline. If MoM > 0.3%, markets react negatively 2. Core MoM — strip food/energy. >0.2% = too hot for the Fed. Annualize it: 0.3% MoM ≈ 3.6% YoY pace 3. Shelter — is it finally declining? 36% weight. The pipeline (new lease data, Zillow) leads CPI shelter by 12-18 months 4. Supercore (core services ex-housing) — Powell's obsession. Driven by wages. If sticky, Fed stays hawkish 5. Energy — how much is passing through to non-energy categories? Compare energy CPI to core CPI diffusion index 6. Diffusion: How many categories are above 2% annualized? Broad-based > 60% = entrenched inflation

$$\text{Surprise} = \text{Actual} - \text{Consensus}$$ Markets price consensus before the release. The move comes from the surprise, not the level. A CPI print of 3.5% that was expected at 3.5% = no move. A print of 3.3% vs 3.5% expected = dovish surprise → rates down, equities up. Citi Economic Surprise Index: Aggregates surprises across all releases. Rising = data beating expectations. Falling = data disappointing. This tends to be mean-reverting — consensus catches up with 4-8 week lag. ---

You are not a macro strategist. But every position in the portfolio has embedded macro assumptions: Position Embedded macro assumption What breaks it --- --- --- Long equities Growth continues, no recession GDP contraction, earnings miss Long duration Fed cuts, disinflation continues Inflation re-accelerates, term premium rises Short vol Low-vol regime persists Regime shift (Goldilocks → Stagflation) Long EM bonds EM central banks credible, USD stable Fed higher-for-longer, USD strength Curve steepener Growth/term premium rises Flight-to-quality bull flattening Long credit Default rates stay low Recession, refinancing wall at higher rates Long energy Supply disruption persists Diplomacy resolves Hormuz, demand destruction RM job: Surface these embedded assumptions, stress test them, and ensure the PM has consciously chosen the macro view rather than stumbling into it.

The same VaR/volatility number means different things in different macro regimes: Metric Goldilocks interpretation Stagflation interpretation --- --- --- VaR $5M (95%) Losses likely contained; correlations benign Understated — correlation breakdown means tail events are larger Vol 15% Normal, well-diversified False comfort — could spike to 30%+ as regime shifts Sharpe 1.5 Genuine alpha May be beta-driven carry that reverses in regime change Beta 0.8 Slightly defensive Beta is unstable — could be 1.5 in a sell-off HY spread 350bp Tight but not extreme Floor — could blow out to 600+ if defaults spike

Pair Goldilocks Stagflation Deflation --- --- --- --- SPX / UST 10Y -0.3 (hedge works) +0.4 (hedge fails) -0.5 (strong hedge) SPX / USD -0.2 -0.4 (both hurt portfolios) -0.5 Oil / SPX +0.3 -0.4 (diverge: oil up, SPX down) +0.3 Gold / UST 10Y -0.2 -0.3 (gold up, bonds down) +0.4 (both safe haven) HY / IG +0.8 +0.6 (HY underperforms) +0.5 (HY collapses more) RM application: Run correlation regime scenarios. If portfolio assumes Goldilocks correlations and we shift to Stagflation, recalculate portfolio VaR with Stagflation correlation matrix. The difference = regime risk.

section: The Daily Macro Checklist for Risk Managers
