content: Time Check Action --- --- --- 06:00 Overnight: Asia/Europe indices, FX, oil Flag any >1σ moves 07:30 Key data release preview (if any) Flag PM positions exposed to surprise 08:30 US data release (NFP Friday, CPI Wednesday) Real-time: watch core number vs consensus 09:30 US equity open — first 30 minutes Monitor sector rotation, VIX, credit 10:00 ISM/JOLTS/Consumer confidence (release times) Cross-reference with PM theses 14:00 FOMC decision (if meeting day) Pre-position review, scenario P&L 15:30 MOC flows, ETF flows Positioning signals 16:00 Post-close — summarize macro signal for PMs Daily risk note with macro context ---

A stress test is only useful if the macro assumptions are internally consistent. "Equities -20% and rates -100bp" is a recession scenario. "Equities -20% and rates +100bp" is a stagflation scenario. They have completely different implications.

Scenario GDP CPI Fed 10Y UST SPX HY OAS Oil USD --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Base case +2.0% 3.0% Hold 3.75% 4.25% +5% 380bp $85 Stable Soft landing +2.5% 2.5% Cut to 3.00% 3.75% +12% 300bp $75 Weaker Recession -1.5% 1.5% Cut to 1.50% 3.00% -25% 700bp $55 Stronger Stagflation +0.3% 5.0% Hike to 5.00% 5.50% -20% 650bp $120 Stronger Hormuz escalation -0.5% 6.0% Hold (trapped) 5.25% -30% 800bp $150 Stronger China hard landing +0.5% 1.0% Cut to 2.00% 2.75% -20% 650bp $50 Stronger Financial crisis -3.0% 0.5% Cut to 0%, QE 2.00% -40% 1200bp $40 Initially ↑↑, then ↓ (QE)

Each scenario must answer: 1. What is the shock? (Demand vs supply, domestic vs external) 2. What is the transmission mechanism? (Through rates, credit, FX, commodities?) 3. What is the policy response? (Accommodative, restrictive, or constrained?) 4. What are the second-order effects? (Contagion, feedback loops, non-linearities) 5. What is the timeline? (Acute shock = days, slow grind = months)

Shock: Full Strait of Hormuz closure (20% of global oil transit). Transmission: 1. Oil → $140-150 immediately (supply shock) 2. Energy CPI → +3pp within 2 months 3. Headline CPI → 6-7% YoY within 3 months 4. Core CPI → starts rising at month 3 as energy feeds through (transport, production) 5. Consumer spending → -2% (energy crowds out discretionary) 6. Corporate margins → compress 200bp (input costs, unable to fully pass through) 7. Earnings → -15% to -20% → SPX -25% to -30% 8. Fed → TRAPPED. Inflation says hike. Growth says cut. Likely holds and hopes for diplomatic resolution. 9. Credit → HY spreads +400bp as energy-intensive issuers face liquidity crunch 10. FX → USD strengthens (safe haven + US relatively energy-independent) 11. EM → Importers crushed (India, Thailand, Philippines). Exporters benefit (Saudi, UAE, Norway) P&L sensitivity for a balanced portfolio ($500M): Sleeve Weight Scenario return P&L impact --- --- --- --- US equities 40% -25% -$50M Duration (10Y) 25% -8% (rates +100bp) -$10M HY credit 10% -15% -$7.5M Commodities 10% +35% +$17.5M Cash/MM 15% +3.75% +$2.8M Total -$47.2M (-9.4%) Hedge analysis: The commodities sleeve absorbs only ~37% of the equity+duration loss. Need additional protection: long put spreads on SPX, short EM FX basket, long energy vs energy-consumer relative value. ---

Crisis Macro type Duration Peak drawdown (SPX) Policy response Key lesson --- --- --- --- --- --- Volcker (1980-82) Policy-induced recession 18 months -27% FFR to 20% → crushed inflation Credibility > short-term pain Asian Crisis (1997) EM BoP crisis → contagion 12 months -6% (US), -60% (Thai SET) IMF bailouts, capital controls FX pegs + hot money + leverage = fragile LTCM (1998) Leverage + convergence trade 3 months -19% Fed rate cut + orchestrated bailout $125B leverage on $4.7B equity → 27x. Crowded trades unwind non-linearly GFC (2008-09) Credit + housing + banking 18 months -57% ZIRP + QE1/2/3, TARP, FDIC Subprime → CDOs → bank capital → everything. Counterparty risk cascades. Euro Crisis (2010-12) Sovereign debt + banking nexus 30 months -21% OMT ("whatever it takes"), ESM Sovereign-bank doom loop. Monetary union without fiscal union Taper Tantrum (2013) Policy communication shock 4 months -6% Bernanke clarified, delayed taper Forward guidance matters. EM FX bore the brunt. COVID (2020) Exogenous demand shock 2 months -34% Unlimited QE, fiscal bazooka Fastest crash AND recovery. Policy scale matters. 2022 Inflation Supply + demand → rate shock 10 months -25% Fastest hiking cycle in 40Y Stock-bond correlation flipped positive. 60/40 worst year since 1970s. UK Gilt Crisis (2022) Fiscal credibility shock 1 week -8% (FTSE) BoE emergency QE, PM resigned Fiscal policy can break markets. LDI leverage exposed.

section: The Anatomy of a Macro Crisis
content: Phase 1: ACCUMULATION (months-years before) Leverage builds, risk premia compress, complacency └── Signs: VIX <15, HY OAS <350, yield-chasing, covenant-lite issuance Phase 2: TRIGGER (days) Exogenous shock or endogenous crack exposes the accumulated fragility └── Signs: Single-name blow-up, unexpected data, geopolitical event Phase 3: CONTAGION (days-weeks) Forced selling → margin calls → liquidity withdrawal → correlation spike └── Signs: Cross-asset correlation → 1.0, spreads gap, vol explodes Phase 4: POLICY RESPONSE (days-weeks) Central bank intervenes, governments act └── Signs: Emergency meetings, new facilities, rate cuts, fiscal packages Phase 5: RESOLUTION (months) Markets stabilize, fundamentals reassessed, new regime established └── Signs: Vol declines, sectoral differentiation returns, flows normalize

1. Leverage is the accelerant: Every major crisis involved excess leverage somewhere — banks (2008), hedge funds (LTCM), pension funds (UK 2022), family offices (Archegos) 2. Correlations spike to 1 in a crisis: Diversification fails exactly when you need it. Cross-asset hedges that "worked" in normal times can fail simultaneously 3. Liquidity disappears when you need it most: Market depth evaporates. Bid-ask spreads widen 5-10x. Forced sellers face no bids 4. Policy response determines the trough: Markets bottom when policy intervention is sufficiently large and credible. "Whatever it takes" works; "measured response" doesn't 5. The next crisis looks different from the last one: Regulators fix the previous vulnerability (banks after 2008). Risk migrates to wherever regulation isn't — shadow banking, private credit, levered LDI, basis trades ---

section: Q1: "Walk me through how you'd think about the Hormuz oil shock from a risk management perspective"
content: Answer: "I'd structure this in three layers: immediate, medium-term, and tail. Immediate — I'd look at our direct energy exposure, both longs and shorts. Then I'd map the second-order exposures: which PMs have positions in energy-intensive sectors like airlines, transport, or materials? What's our total portfolio sensitivity to a $10/barrel oil move? I'd want that DV01-equivalent for oil running by end of day. Medium-term — the macro question is whether this stays as a cost-push shock that reverses or whether it feeds into core inflation via the wage-price transmission channel. I'd monitor the pipeline: PPI, ISM Prices Paid, then core CPI diffusion. If core CPI MoM starts exceeding 0.3% for two consecutive prints, we're in trouble because the Fed is trapped — they can't cut to support growth without re-igniting inflation, and they can't hike without deepening the growth shock. Tail scenario — a full, prolonged closure means $140+ oil, recession probability jumps to 60%+, and we're in a stagflation regime where stock-bond correlation flips positive and traditional hedges fail. I'd run the scenario through our portfolio with stagflation correlation assumptions rather than recent correlations, because the correlation regime shift is where the real risk is hiding. For the desk, I'd communicate this as: 'Our VaR number assumes recent correlations. If we enter a stagflation regime, effective portfolio risk could be 1.5-2x what VaR shows.' Then I'd recommend reviewing our hedge overlay — specifically, whether our duration hedges still work if both stocks and bonds sell off."

section: Q2: "The 10-year yield moved 15bp this morning. How would you decompose that?"
content: Answer: "I'd break it into three components. First, expected policy rates — what's the market's revised expectation for the Fed path? I'd check fed funds futures across the curve. If the 2-year moved proportionally, the move is policy-driven, meaning the market is repricing the number of rate hikes or cuts. Second, real rate versus inflation expectations — I'd compare the TIPS yield move to the nominal yield move. If the nominal 10Y moved 15bp and the TIPS 10Y moved 10bp, that's 10bp real rate increase and 5bp breakeven increase. Real rate moves suggest growth expectations or fiscal supply concerns. Breakeven moves suggest inflation repricing. Third, term premium — the residual after accounting for expected rates and inflation. Using the ACM term premium model from the New York Fed, if most of the move is term premium, it suggests investor confidence in holding long-duration government bonds has shifted, possibly from fiscal supply concerns, foreign selling, or reduced QE expectations. Then I'd map this to our portfolio: multiply the move by our aggregate DV01 for a first-order P&L estimate, then check which PMs have the most duration exposure and whether their positions are directional bets or hedged structures. A PM who's long a 2s10s steepener actually benefits from a bear steepening move, whereas a PM who's outright long 10Y duration is taking a loss."

section: Q3: "Where are we in the business cycle and what does that mean for portfolio risk?"
content: Answer: "I'd frame this using the growth-inflation quadrant. Right now we're in an unusual position — we were in Goldilocks territory in late 2025, with disinflation and moderate growth, but the oil shock is pushing us toward either Reflation or Stagflation, depending on whether growth holds up. The key data to distinguish: if ISM holds above 50 and payrolls stay above 150K while inflation re-accelerates, we're in Reflation — which means equities can still work but positioning needs to rotate toward value and commodities, and duration should be underweight. If ISM breaks below 48 and claims start rising while inflation stays elevated, we're entering Stagflation — the worst quadrant for a balanced portfolio because stock-bond correlation turns positive and traditional diversification breaks. For our book specifically, this regime ambiguity means I'd want to stress-test under BOTH scenarios and see which one causes more damage. I'd also want to check our hedge ratios — are we assuming negative stock-bond correlation? Because if we shift to a positive-correlation regime, our rate hedges could amplify losses rather than absorb them. That's the single biggest regime-change risk I'd flag to our PMs right now." Deep dive on current context: 10 Market Context Today Cross-asset cascade logic: 09 Market Intuition

A discretionary risk manager who watches only one asset class is not managing risk — they're monitoring a position. Markets are a coupled system: a move in oil changes inflation expectations, which changes rate expectations, which changes equity valuations, which changes credit spreads, which changes EM currencies, which changes central bank decisions that feed back into rates. Understanding these transmission chains — their speeds, magnitudes, conditional behaviors, and failure modes — is the core intellectual skill of a cross-asset risk manager. At Squarepoint, where discretionary PMs sit alongside systematic desks, cross-asset thinking is non-optional. A PM who's "only trading European equities" has embedded views on ECB policy, EURUSD, oil, and credit conditions. Your job is to surface those embedded exposures, quantify the cross-asset sensitivities, and ensure the PM's book survives the scenarios they haven't considered. The fundamental question: "If X moves, what else moves — in what order, at what speed, with what magnitude, and under what conditions does the usual relationship break?" ---

section: Chain 1: Oil → Inflation → Rates → Equities → Credit → EM
content: Oil spike (+20%) ├── IMMEDIATE (hours) │ ├── TIPS breakevens widen (inflation expectations repriced) │ ├── Energy equities rally │ ├── Airlines, transport sell off │ └── Fed funds futures reprice (fewer cuts / more hikes priced) ├── SHORT-TERM (days-weeks) │ ├── DXY strengthens (US relatively energy-independent) │ ├── Equity P/E multiples compress (higher discount rate) │ ├── EM oil importers: FX weakens, bonds sell off │ └── HY energy issuers rally; HY transport/consumer widen ├── MEDIUM-TERM (weeks-months) │ ├── Realized CPI prints higher (4-8 week lag to release) │ ├── Fed rhetoric shifts hawkish → curve bear flattens │ ├── Earnings estimates revised down (margin compression) │ └── Consumer spending shifts (gas crowds out discretionary) └── LONG-TERM (months-quarters) ├── Second-round inflation via wages (if sustained) ├── Credit cycle tightens → defaults in energy-intensive sectors ├── EM BoP crises (persistent trade deficit deterioration) └── Potential recession if oil stays elevated 6+ months Speed calibration (critical for risk management): Leg Transmission time What to watch --- --- --- Oil → breakevens Minutes-hours TIPS 5Y, 10Y intraday Oil → energy equity Same day XLE, individual names Oil → transport/airlines Same day DAL, UAL, FDX, UPS Oil → FX (EM importers) Hours-days USDINR, USDTHB, USDPHP Oil → realized CPI 4-8 weeks Monthly CPI report CPI → Fed repricing Same day (CPI day) Fed funds futures, 2Y UST Fed repricing → equity multiples Days-weeks Forward P/E compression Equity weakness → credit 1-5 days CDX HY, iTraxx XO Credit tightening → real economy 3-12 months SLOOS, bank lending 2022 case study (full chain): 1. Russia invades Ukraine (Feb 24) → Brent $90 → $130 in 2 weeks 2. CPI prints: 7.9%, 8.5%, 8.3%, 8.6%, 9.1% (June peak) 3. Fed: 25bp → 50bp → 75bp × 4 consecutive 4. 10Y UST: 1.5% → 4.2% in 9 months (+270bp) 5. SPX: -25% peak to trough (Jan high → Oct low) 6. HY OAS: 300bp → 600bp 7. EURUSD: 1.14 → 0.96 (below parity for first time in 20Y) 8. UK mini-budget → gilt crisis → LDI margin calls → BoE emergency QE 9. Crypto collapse (TerraLuna, FTX) — parallel deleveraging event

section: Chain 2: Rate Shock → Duration → FX → EM Contagion
content: Unexpected rate hike / hawkish pivot ├── Long-duration bonds reprice (10Y+, TLT) ├── Growth/tech equity multiples compress │ └── ARKK-type: -60% to -80% from peak ├── Real estate reprices (cap rates widen) ├── USD strengthens (rate differential widens) │ ├── DXY up → commodity prices in USD pressured │ ├── EM FX weakens (capital outflow) │ │ ├── Countries with FX-denominated debt face double hit │ │ │ └── Weaker FX + higher refinancing cost → default risk │ │ └── EM central banks forced to hike (defend currency, at cost of growth) │ └── US multinationals: FX translation headwind on earnings └── Mortgage rates spike → housing demand collapses → wealth effect reversal The "dollar wrecking ball": When US rates rise unexpectedly, the USD strengthens, draining dollar liquidity from the global system. Countries with: - USD-denominated debt → debt service costs spike - Commodity imports priced in USD → trade deficit widens - Foreign reserves in USD → depleted defending the peg/float Historical instances: 2013 Taper Tantrum (Fragile Five: BRL, INR, IDR, TRY, ZAR), 2018 EM sell-off (TRY -40%, ARS -50%), 2022 global dollar squeeze.

Shock event (exogenous or endogenous) ├── Phase 1: PRICE DISCOVERY (Day 1) │ ├── VIX spikes 10-30 points │ ├── Equity down 3-5% │ ├── Treasuries rally (flight to quality) — IF growth shock │ └── Credit spreads gap wider (CDX, iTraxx) ├── Phase 2: FORCED SELLING (Days 2-5) │ ├── Margin calls trigger → liquid assets sold first │ ├── Dealer hedging amplifies (short gamma = sell into weakness) │ ├── Correlation spikes → diversification fails │ └── ETF redemptions create selling pressure across baskets ├── Phase 3: LIQUIDITY CRISIS (Week 1-2) │ ├── Bid-ask spreads widen 5-10x │ ├── Market depth evaporates (thin order books) │ ├── Funding markets stress (repo, CP fails to roll) │ ├── EVEN safe havens can sell off (dash for cash) │ └── Redemption queues → gates → forced liquidation ├── Phase 4: POLICY RESPONSE (Week 2-4) │ ├── Central bank rate cuts / emergency meetings │ ├── Special lending facilities activated │ ├── Regulatory forbearance (mark-to-market relaxation) │ └── Fiscal stimulus packages announced └── Phase 5: STABILIZATION / RECOVERY ├── Vol elevated but declining ├── Dispersion returns (not everything correlated) ├── Credit differentiation resumes (zombies fail, quality recovers) └── Carry trades slowly rebuilt → new cycle begins March 2020 — The archetype: Day Event Key market moves --- --- --- Mar 9 Oil price war (Saudi-Russia) Brent -25%, circuit breakers triggered Mar 11 WHO declares pandemic SPX enters bear market (fastest ever from ATH) Mar 12 SPX -9.5% (worst since 1987) VIX touches 82 (all-time high) Mar 13-16 Margin calls everywhere Even Treasuries sold (TLT -5% in a week). Gold -10%. Mar 15 Fed emergency cut to 0% + $700B QE Not enough — futures hit limit down Mar 18-19 USD funding crisis (3M basis swap -150bp) Commercial paper market freezes Mar 23 Fed: unlimited QE + corporate bond buying SPX bottoms (-34% from peak). Recovery begins. Key lesson: In Phase 3, correlation goes to ~1.0. Even "safe" assets sell off because the dominant factor is LIQUIDITY, not fundamentals. The only true safe haven in a liquidity crisis is cash (specifically, USD cash or T-bills with < 1 month maturity).

Carry trade accumulation (months-years): Borrow low-rate (JPY 0.50%, CHF 1.50%) → Convert to high-rate currency → Invest in HY bonds, EM local debt, equity dividend → Earn positive carry spread Unwind trigger: Rate differential narrows (BoJ hikes / Fed cuts) OR vol spikes (carry is implicitly short vol) OR EM/HY shock (investment leg drops) Cascade: ├── Sell HY/EM positions (investment leg) ├── Buy back funding currency (JPY/CHF) ├── JPY/CHF spike → other carry traders underwater ├── MORE unwinds → self-reinforcing ├── EM FX crashes → EM central banks intervene (sell reserves) └── Volatility spikes further → triggers systematic de-risking (CTAs, risk parity) August 2024 carry unwind — Anatomy: Date Event Impact --- --- --- Jul 31 BoJ hikes to 0.25% (unexpected hawkish tilt) USDJPY: 153 → 149 Aug 1 Weak US ISM + jobless claims Recession fears → carry viability questioned Aug 2 Weak NFP (+114K, UE to 4.3%) USDJPY crashes to 146. Carry unwind accelerates. Aug 5 "Black Monday" — Nikkei -12.4% Worst single-day since 1987. VIX → 65 intraday. Global equity sell-off. Aug 6-9 BoJ deputy gov walks back hawkishness USDJPY stabilizes 145-148. Equities recover 50%. Carry = short volatility: The P&L profile of a carry trade is identical to selling options — small, steady income with occasional catastrophic loss. Position sizing and stop-loss discipline are critical.

section: Chain 5: Credit Impulse → Real Economy Feedback Loop
content: Financial conditions tighten (spreads widen, lending standards rise) ├── Corporate refinancing costs rise │ ├── Marginal borrowers can't roll debt → defaults │ ├── Capex plans deferred → investment drops │ └── Share buybacks slow → equity support removed ├── Consumer credit tightens │ ├── Auto loan / credit card rates rise → spending falls │ ├── Mortgage availability drops → housing weakens │ └── Wealth effect reverses → confidence drops ├── Bank capital consumed by losses │ ├── Loan loss provisions rise → earnings fall │ ├── Capital ratios tighten → lending further constrained │ └── Bank equity falls → market signals more stress └── FEEDBACK: Weaker economy → more defaults → tighter conditions → weaker economy The credit impulse (change in new credit / GDP) is one of the most powerful leading indicators. When credit growth is accelerating, the economy strengthens with a 6-9 month lead. When credit growth decelerates, recession risk rises. Monitoring: - Senior Loan Officer Survey (SLOOS) — quarterly, leads growth by 4-6 quarters - HY OAS level AND rate of change — widening from tight = late cycle - IG issuance volumes — if dry up at tight spreads = fear; if heavy at tight spreads = late-cycle greed - Bank CDS spreads — rising = market pricing bank stress - Private credit default rates (delayed reporting — shadow banking risk) ---

Pair Goldilocks Reflation Stagflation Deflation / Crisis What drives the shift --- --- --- --- --- --- SPX / UST 10Y -0.30 +0.20 +0.40 -0.50 Inflation vol vs growth vol dominance SPX / USD (DXY) -0.15 -0.20 -0.40 -0.50 Risk appetite channel SPX / Oil (Brent) +0.25 +0.30 -0.40 +0.20 Demand-driven vs supply-driven oil SPX / Gold -0.10 -0.20 -0.30 -0.20 Risk channel (gold = hedge) SPX / VIX -0.80 -0.75 -0.85 -0.95 Always inverse, strength varies SPX / HY OAS -0.70 -0.60 -0.80 -0.90 Credit + equity share default risk UST 10Y / Oil -0.20 -0.40 -0.50 +0.30 Oil drives inflation → rates; but in deflation, both safe haven Gold / UST 10Y (real) -0.40 -0.30 -0.30 +0.40 Real rate competition normally; co-safe-haven in deflation JPY / VIX +0.30 +0.20 +0.50 +0.60 JPY as safe haven + carry unwind EM FX / DXY -0.60 -0.50 -0.70 -0.80 Dollar liquidity channel

section: The Stock-Bond Correlation — The Master Variable
content: This is the SINGLE most important cross-asset relationship for portfolio construction: $$\rho {SB} = f(\sigma {\text{inflation}}^2, \ \sigma {\text{growth}}^2, \ \text{policy regime})$$ The regime framework: Era ρ(SPX, UST) Dominant risk Why --- --- --- --- 1965-1999 Positive (+0.2 to +0.4) Inflation Volcker era, inflation expectations volatile 2000-2021 Negative (-0.2 to -0.5) Growth Low stable inflation, growth shocks dominate 2022-2023 Positive (+0.3 to +0.5) Inflation Post-COVID inflation shock, rate hiking cycle Late 2024-2025 Negative (-0.1 to -0.3) Growth Disinflation, growth concerns return April 2026 Unstable (oscillating) Both Oil shock creates inflation risk while growth decelerating Why this matters more than anything else: - If ρ < 0: Duration hedges work → 60/40 portfolio diversifies → VaR accurately reflects risk - If ρ > 0: Duration hedges AMPLIFY losses → 60/40 is a concentrated bet → VaR UNDERSTATES risk - If ρ is unstable: Hedge ratios are unreliable → need to run multiple correlation scenarios RM action: Track rolling 21-day and 63-day SPX/TLT correlation. When the sign flips, immediately re-evaluate: 1. All hedge ratios 2. Portfolio VaR under both correlation assumptions 3. PM positions with embedded duration hedges

$$\rho {\text{conditional stress}} = \rho {\text{unconditional}} + \Delta\rho(\text{vol regime}, \text{liquidity})$$ Empirical fact: Correlations increase during stress. A portfolio that appears diversified in normal markets concentrates during crises. Lookback period Estimated ρ(SPX, HY) True stress ρ --- --- --- 3-year unconditional 0.55 — Conditional on VIX < 20 0.40 — Conditional on VIX > 30 0.75 — Conditional on VIX > 40 0.85 Diversification mostly gone Conditional on VIX > 60 0.93 Effectively same asset Practical rule: Your VaR model uses unconditional (or short lookback) correlations. During a crisis, actual portfolio risk is 1.5-2.5x what VaR reports because correlations spike. This is why stressed VaR and scenario analysis exist — to capture the gap.

Signal What it means Action --- --- --- Rolling 21d SPX/TLT correlation crosses zero Inflation vs growth dominance shifting Re-evaluate hedge ratios Cross-sector equity correlation > 0.7 Macro factor dominating stock-specific Reduce gross; beta risk exceeds alpha VIX-of-VIX (VVIX) rising while VIX stable Uncertainty about uncertainty Markets fragile; tail risk underpriced JPY and gold moving together sharply Multiple safe havens bid = genuine fear Reduce risk exposure IG-HY spread differential widening Credit differentiation → default cycle starting Reduce HY exposure ---

Asset Mechanism Reliability When it FAILS --- --- --- --- US T-bills (< 3M) Sovereign credit, USD, zero duration Most reliable Only if US defaults (never happened on USD debt) US Treasuries (10Y+) Flight to quality, duration rally in recession Very high Inflation shock (2022: TLT -31%) German Bunds European safe sovereign High in EU crises Inflation shock (same as UST) Gold No counterparty risk, inflation hedge Moderate Real rate spikes, liquidity crises (margin calls force sale) Japanese Yen Net creditor nation, carry unwind flow Moderate BoJ policy shifts (Aug 2024) Swiss Franc Neutrality, current account surplus Moderate SNB intervention (2015 peg removal — CHF +20% in minutes) US Dollar (DXY) Reserve currency, deepest market High in EM crises When the US IS the crisis (2008 briefly, dollar initially weakened)

section: Flight-to-Quality Velocity — How Fast Capital Moves
content: Phase Speed What moves --- --- --- Electronic / systematic Seconds-minutes CTA trend signals, risk-parity rebalancing, gamma hedging Liquid institutional Hours-days Mutual fund hedging, bank prop desk de-risking Slower institutional Days-weeks Pension rebalancing, insurance portfolio shifts Retail Weeks-months Panic selling, fund redemptions Implication: The first phase creates the gap move. By the time retail panic-sells, the move is often 60-80% done. Risk managers need to be ahead of Phase 2, not reacting in Phase 3.

section: When ALL Safe Havens Fail: The Dash for Cash
content: March 2020 proved that in a severe liquidity crisis, NOTHING is truly safe except cash: Asset Expected behavior Actual behavior (Mar 9-23) Why --- --- --- --- US Treasuries Rally (flight to quality) SOLD OFF (10Y yield +60bp, then -100bp) Forced sellers needed cash; basis trades blew up Gold Rally (safe haven) Fell -12% (Mar 9-19) Margin calls → sell everything liquid Investment Grade bonds Minor spread widening IG OAS +100bp in 2 weeks Redemption queues, ETF discount to NAV Money market funds Stable Some broke the buck (prime funds) CP market froze, redemption pressure What worked Cash, T-bills T-bills rallied (negative yields briefly) Pure cash equivalent = only true safe haven RM lesson: In tail scenarios, DON'T assume your "hedge" is a hedge. Model the scenario where everything correlates, hedges fail, and the only thing that works is cash. That's the portfolio's true tail risk. ---

Markets oscillate between two dominant modes. Identifying which mode is active determines how every cross-asset signal should be interpreted: Indicator Risk-On signature Risk-Off signature Transition signal --- --- --- --- VIX <18, term structure in contango >25, backwardation VIX crosses 20 with momentum S&P 500 Making new highs, low vol Falling, high vol Breaks 50-day MA Credit spreads (HY OAS) <350bp, tightening >450bp, widening Rate of change > 50bp/week US Dollar (DXY) Weakening Strengthening DXY breaks range EM FX (EMFX index) Appreciating Depreciating Broad-based not single country Gold Flat or softening Rising (usually) > $50/day moves Treasuries Yields rising (selling) Yields falling (buying) 10Y breaks key level Commodities (copper) Rising (demand-driven) Falling (recession fear) Dr. Copper disagrees with equity JPY crosses Weakening (carry on) Strengthening (carry unwinding) USDJPY multi-day trend reversal

Factor Risk-On (score +1) Neutral (score 0) Risk-Off (score -1) --- --- --- --- VIX level <15 15-25 >25 VIX term structure Steep contango Flat Backwardation HY OAS direction Tightening Stable Widening SPX vs 50d MA Above, rising At Below, falling USDJPY direction Rising (JPY weak) Flat Falling (JPY strong) Copper vs gold ratio Rising Flat Falling EM sovereign CDS Tightening Stable Widening MOVE index (rate vol) < 100 100-120 > 120 Score +4 to +8: Full risk-on. Correlations benign, VaR reliable, alpha opportunity high. Score -2 to +3: Neutral/transitional. Regime uncertain, monitor closely. Score -3 to -8: Risk-off. Correlations elevating, VaR understating, consider reducing gross. ---

Shock SPX 10Y Price HY OAS DXY Gold Oil VIX EM FX --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Surprise rate hike ↓↓ ↓↓ +30bp ↑↑ ↓ ↓ ↑↑ ↓↓ Surprise rate cut ↑↑ ↑↑ -25bp ↓↓ ↑ ↑ ↓↓ ↑↑ Hot CPI (core +0.4%) ↓ ↓↓ +15bp ↑ Complex ↑ ↑ ↓ Cold CPI (core +0.1%) ↑↑ ↑↑ -20bp ↓ ↓ Flat ↓↓ ↑ Strong NFP (+300K) Context ↓ -10bp ↑ ↓ ↑ ↓ Mixed Weak NFP (+80K) Context ↑↑ +20bp ↓ ↑ ↓↓ ↑↑ ↓ Oil supply shock (+20%) ↓ ↓ +25bp ↑ ↑ ↑↑↑ ↑↑ ↓↓ Recession confirmed ↓↓↓ ↑↑↑ +200bp ↑↑ ↑↑ ↓↓ ↑↑↑ ↓↓↓ Geopolitical shock ↓ to ↓↓ ↑ +30bp ↑ ↑↑ ↑ to ↑↑ ↑↑ ↓↓ Credit event ↓↓ ↑ +100bp ↑ ↑ ↓ ↑↑↑ ↓↓↓ Positive growth surprise ↑↑ ↓ -15bp Mixed ↓ ↑ ↓ ↑ Fiscal credibility shock ↓ ↓↓↓ +50bp ↓↓ (if domestic) ↑↑ Flat ↑↑ N/A NFP is narrative-dependent: In "inflation is sticky" regime, strong NFP = hawkish = equities down. In "soft landing" regime, strong NFP = economy is fine = equities up.

Equity sector Oil β 10Y yield β DXY β Copper β VIX β Key driver --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Energy (XLE) +0.7 -0.1 +0.1 +0.2 -0.3 Oil price dominant Financials (XLF) +0.1 +0.4 +0.2 +0.2 -0.5 Yield curve slope (NIM) Tech (XLK) -0.1 -0.3 -0.2 +0.1 -0.6 Real rates (duration) Utilities (XLU) -0.1 -0.5 -0.1 0 -0.2 Bond proxy, interest sensitivity REITs 0 -0.6 -0.1 +0.1 -0.3 Direct rate sensitivity Materials (XLB) +0.2 +0.1 -0.3 +0.6 -0.3 Commodity prices, China Industrials (XLI) +0.1 +0.2 -0.1 +0.4 -0.4 Capex cycle, PMI Consumer Disc. (XLY) -0.2 -0.1 -0.1 +0.1 -0.5 Consumer confidence, employment Staples (XLP) 0 -0.2 +0.1 0 +0.2 Defensive, low beta Healthcare (XLV) 0 -0.1 0 0 +0.1 Defensive, regulatory RM application: When a macro variable moves, instantly estimate sector-level P&L impact: $$\Delta P\&L {\text{sector}} \approx \text{sector exposure} \times \beta {\text{factor}} \times \Delta\text{factor}$$ Example: If oil moves +10% and the portfolio has $50M XLE long and $30M XLY long: - XLE impact: $50M × 0.7 × 10% = +$3.5M - XLY impact: $30M × (-0.2) × 10% = -$0.6M - Net oil impact: +$2.9M But this is just the first-order effect. Oil also moves rates, USD, and VIX — calculate the second-order effects through other betas. ---

Data print "Growth scare" narrative "Inflation scare" narrative "Goldilocks" narrative --- --- --- --- Strong NFP (+300K) ↑ Equities (no recession!) ↓ Equities (Fed won't cut) ↑ Equities (but less; "too hot?") Weak NFP (+80K) ↓↓ Equities (recession!) ↑ Equities (Fed WILL cut) ↓ Equities (growth slipping) Hot CPI (core +0.4%) Equities mixed (Fed trapped) ↓↓ Equities (inflation entrenched) ↓ Equities (cracks the Goldilocks) Cool CPI (core +0.1%) ↑ Equities (Fed can cut soon) ↑↑ Equities (inflation solved!) ↑ Equities (confirming the dream) ISM below 50 ↓↓ Equities (recession) ↑ Equities (demand down = disinflation) ↓ Equities (wobble) The "good news is bad news" regime: When the dominant narrative is about the Fed being too hawkish, good economic news is interpreted NEGATIVELY because it means fewer rate cuts. This inverts normal cross-asset relationships: $$\frac{\partial\text{SPX}}{\partial\text{NFP}} < 0 \quad \text{in "Fed vigilance" regime}$$ $$\frac{\partial\text{SPX}}{\partial\text{NFP}} > 0 \quad \text{in "growth scare" regime}$$ How to identify the prevailing narrative: 1. Read the first 5 minutes of price action after a data release — the DIRECTION tells you the narrative 2. Check Fed funds futures reaction — if the rate path reprices more than equities, the narrative is "Fed-driven" 3. Watch financial media framing — which angle dominates the headline 4. Cross-reference: if SPX and UST move in the SAME direction after data, the narrative is growth-driven. If OPPOSITE, it's inflation/Fed-driven. ---

section: Gamma Exposure — How Dealer Hedging Amplifies Cross-Asset Moves
content: Options dealers delta-hedge their books. The direction and magnitude of their hedging depends on their aggregate gamma position: Short gamma regime: $$\text{Dealer hedge} = \Delta \text{delta} \times \text{spot move} = -\Gamma \times (\Delta S)^2 / S$$ When dealers are short gamma: - Market falls → their delta becomes more negative → they SELL more equity to re-hedge → amplifies the fall - Market rises → their delta becomes more positive → they BUY more equity → amplifies the rise - Result: Volatility higher, moves magnified, cross-asset cascades faster Long gamma regime: - Market falls → dealers BUY (they were over-hedged) → dampens the fall - Market rises → dealers SELL → dampens the rise - Result: Volatility suppressed, "sticky" markets, cross-asset signals muted Where gamma estimates live: - SpotGamma, GEX indicators - Major bank options desks publish daily estimates - Zero Gamma Level: the price level where dealer gamma flips sign — acts as an attractor/repulsor

Daily expiring SPX options now represent ~50% of total SPX options volume. Their effects: Feature Impact --- --- Massive intraday gamma Pin risk around major strikes → SPX pins at round numbers Rapid gamma decay θ → ∞ as expiry approaches → dealers' hedge adjustments accelerate Intraday regime shifts AM can be long-gamma, PM can be short-gamma in the same day Cross-asset amplification SPX 0DTE gamma hedging creates intraday VIX spikes, Treasury Vol

Greek Effect on dealer hedging Cross-asset impact --- --- --- Gamma Delta sensitivity to spot → buy/sell equity to hedge Directly amplifies/dampens equity moves Vanna Delta sensitivity to volatility → if vol rises, dealers must adjust delta VIX spike → additional equity selling → feedback loop Charm Delta sensitivity to time → as options approach expiry, delta drifts Creates predictable end-of-day flows → MOC imbalances Vanna feedback loop (the danger): - Market drops → VIX spikes → vanna effect forces dealers to sell more delta → market drops further → VIX spikes more → loop continues This is how a 2% sell-off can become a 5% sell-off in a day when vanna flows dominate.

section: Systematic Strategy Flows as Cross-Asset Transmitters
content: Strategy Mechanism Speed Cross-asset impact --- --- --- --- CTA / managed futures Trend-following → sell when trend breaks Days (momentum signals) Can accelerate duration and equity trends; flip adds fuel Risk parity Target constant risk contribution → deleverage when vol spikes Hours-days Sells BOTH stocks AND bonds when vol rises → amplifies positive correlation Vol targeting Reduce exposure when realized vol rises Days Systematic equity selling into weakness → gamma-like amplification Passive / rebalancing Buy low / sell high to maintain weights Monthly/quarterly Counter-cyclical — provides liquidity. Month-end/quarter-end flows visible CTA positioning as a crowding indicator: - When CTAs are max long → any trend reversal triggers massive sell flow → amplifies the sell-off - When CTAs are max short → any bounce triggers short-covering → amplifies the rally - Track via CFTC Commitment of Traders (CoT) for futures positioning, bank CTA models (SocGen, GS, JPM) ---

Channel Mechanism Speed Example --- --- --- --- Information News changes expectations globally Seconds COVID announcement → global equity sell-off simultaneously Portfolio balance Loss in one asset → sell others to raise cash Hours-days GFC: US housing → bank equity → fire-sale of all assets Funding Lender pulls credit from all borrowers, not just impaired Days-weeks 2008: repo market freeze → all holders of MBS affected Wake-up call Shock reveals vulnerability in "similar" exposures Days-weeks Greek crisis → market reprices all peripheral European sovereigns Margin/collateral Losses → margin calls → forced selling → more losses Hours-days LTCM: Russia default → spread blow-out → margin calls → spiral Institutional linkage CCP, prime broker, custodian failure Hours Lehman: PB failure → hedge fund assets frozen → liquidation

Domestic shock ├── LIQUID MARKETS (seconds-minutes): Equity futures, FX, sovereign bonds ├── CREDIT (hours-days): CDX/iTraxx → single-name CDS → cash bonds ├── FUNDING (days): Repo → CP → bank lending → interbank ├── CROSS-BORDER (hours-days): Correlated sovereigns → EM → commodity FX ├── REAL ECONOMY (months): Tighter credit → less investment → lower GDP └── POLICY FEEDBACK (days-weeks): Central bank response → new equilibrium

section: Identifying Contagion Risk Before It Happens
content: Vulnerability Metric to watch Danger threshold --- --- --- Leverage concentration Gross/net leverage by prime broker Rising leverage + falling vol = accumulation Crowded trades CFTC positioning extremes, ETF flow concentration >2σ from mean positioning Funding fragility FRA-OIS spread, cross-currency basis FRA-OIS > 25bp, 3M basis < -30bp Counterparty concentration CDS of major PBs and dealers Any bank CDS +50bp in a week Correlation convergence Cross-sector, cross-asset dispersion indices Average pairwise correlation >0.6 sustained Volatility surface Skew richness, VVIX level VVIX >120 with VIX still low = tension ---

$$R p = \alpha + \beta {\text{equity}} \cdot R {\text{SPX}} + \beta {\text{rates}} \cdot \Delta y {10} + \beta {\text{credit}} \cdot \Delta \text{OAS} + \beta {\text{oil}} \cdot R {\text{oil}} + \beta {\text{FX}} \cdot \Delta \text{DXY} + \epsilon$$ Why this matters: A PM claims they're generating alpha. Run the factor regression. If their returns are explained by $\beta {\text{oil}} = 0.6$, they're not generating alpha — they're long oil through equity proxy positions. Different risk compensation, different drawdown profile, different hedge requirement. Method: 1. Run rolling 60-day regression of PM returns against cross-asset factors 2. R² > 0.6 = macro-dominated book (not alpha-driven) 3. Track factor betas over time — stable = intentional positioning; drifting = unintentional accumulation 4. Flag when implied factor exposure contradicts stated PM mandate

Create a "universal risk measure" by expressing all portfolio exposures in terms of a common unit: Factor Sensitivity unit Conversion to $ risk --- --- --- Rates (10Y) DV01 = $ P&L per 1bp yield change DV01 × expected move (bp) Equity Beta × notional Beta × notional × expected % move Credit CS01 = $ P&L per 1bp spread change CS01 × expected spread move Oil $ P&L per $1 oil move Oil beta × notional ÷ price × $1 FX $ P&L per 1% FX move FX-denominated notional × 1% Vol Vega = $ P&L per 1 vol point Vega × expected vol move Aggregation: With a common $ unit, you can sum sensitivities to answer: "What's our total portfolio P&L if oil rises $10, 10Y yields rise 25bp, and DXY strengthens 2%?" — applying the conditional correlation structure.

Standard correlation measures LINEAR co-movement. In tails, the relationship is non-linear: $$\lambda U = \lim {u \to 1} P(X > F X^{-1}(u) Y > F Y^{-1}(u))$$ Translation: What's the probability that asset X has an extreme loss GIVEN that asset Y has an extreme loss? This "tail dependence" is almost always HIGHER than what normal correlation implies. Pair Linear ρ Tail dependence (estimated) Implication --- --- --- --- SPX / EuroStoxx 0.80 0.6 High — sell-offs are joint events SPX / HY bonds 0.55 0.5 Higher than linear suggests — credit blow-ups hit equity SPX / UST 10Y -0.20 0.15 (same direction tails) LOWER magnitude than linear → bonds are better hedges than expected in most crises (except inflation) SPX / Gold -0.10 0.05 Very low → gold is a genuine diversifier in tail events ---

Check Source What you're looking for --- --- --- Asia close / Europe open Futures screens Any >1σ overnight move; gaps vs US close Oil Brent/WTI Direction and magnitude → >3% = immediate cascade assessment FX DXY, USDJPY, EURUSD Dollar direction sets EM/commodity tone VIX futures VX term structure Contango/backwardation regime 10Y UST yield Bonds futures Down >5bp = flight to quality; Up >5bp = hawkish repricing Credit CDX HY, iTraxx Main Any widening overnight = risk-off signal Data calendar Econoday, Bloomberg Key releases today? What's the consensus? PM exposure to surprise?

Check Frequency What it means --- --- --- SPX direction vs 10Y direction Continuous Same direction = growth-driven. Opposite = inflation/Fed driven. VIX level relative to SPX move Continuous VIX rising MORE than SPX warrants = fear premium building HY spread behavior Hourly HY widening while equities flat = early warning signal Sector rotation After first 30 min Defensive outperforming cyclical = risk-off rotation Dollar vs commodities Continuous Divergence from usual pattern = narrative shift Dealer gamma level Daily estimate Below zero gamma level = amplified moves expected

section: Cross-Asset Stress Red Flags — Immediate Escalation
content: Signal Severity RM Action --- --- --- Stocks AND bonds selling off together 🟡 ALERT Confirm inflation-driven; reassess hedge ratios VIX > 30 AND HY OAS widening > 25bp/day 🔴 CRITICAL Systemic risk — prepare gross reduction plan USDJPY dropping > 3% in a day 🔴 CRITICAL Carry unwind — check EM, HY, yield-seeking exposure Oil > $120 AND breakevens rising 🟡 ALERT Stagflation setup — run regime scenarios FRA-OIS > 30bp 🔴 CRITICAL Funding market stress — pre-crisis indicator Gold falling DURING equity sell-off 🔴 CRITICAL Dash for cash — margin calls liquidating EVERYTHING IG-HY spread ratio widening rapidly 🟡 ALERT Credit differentiation → default cycle accelerating VVIX > 140 with VIX < 20 🟡 ALERT Markets are calm but fragile — vol of vol signals tension Correlation spike (avg pairwise > 0.7) 🟡 ALERT Macro factor dominating — alpha can't overcome beta ---

section: Case Study 1: The 2022 Correlation Regime Shift
content: Pre-2022: Markets assumed negative stock-bond correlation (the post-2000 regime). 60/40 portfolios relied on this. Risk parity strategies were levered on this assumption. What happened: - Inflation rose from 2% → 9% - Fed hiked from 0% → 5.25% in 16 months - SPX: -25%. AGG (bonds): -13%. 60/40 portfolio: -18% (worst since the 1970s) - Stock-bond correlation flipped to +0.5 for extended periods - Risk parity strategies forced to deleverage → selling both stocks AND bonds → amplifying the correlation Lessons for RM: 1. The stock-bond ρ is not a constant — it's regime-dependent 2. When inflation is the dominant risk, neither stocks nor bonds protect you 3. Risk models trained on 2000-2021 data (ρ < 0) were WRONG for 2022 (ρ > 0) 4. Need regime-conditional VaR, not a single VaR number

section: Case Study 2: August 2024 — Carry Unwind → Cross-Asset Cascade
content: Transmission chain (72 hours): Time Event Cross-asset impact --- --- --- Jul 31 PM BoJ hikes 15bp (hawkish surprise) USDJPY: 153 → 150 Aug 1 Weak ISM manufacturing (46.8) 10Y UST: 4.20% → 4.05%. Recession fears. Aug 2 8:30AM NFP miss: +114K, UE 4.3% (Sahm rule triggered) USDJPY: 150 → 146. Carry unwind intensifies. 10Y: 3.97%. Aug 5 Asia Nikkei -12.4% (worst since 1987) VIX: 23 → 65 intraday. SPX futures -3%. Aug 5 US SPX -3% at open, recovers to -1.8% HY OAS +40bp. Gold flat. UST 10Y: 3.78%. Aug 6-7 BoJ deputy governor walks back USDJPY stabilizes 145-148. VIX back to 27. Recovery. Lessons: 1. Carry unwinds transmit from FX → equity → vol → credit → EM in HOURS, not days 2. The BoJ is a global VOL event, not just a Japan story 3. VIX can spike 3x its level in a single session when carry + macro + positioning align 4. Recovery can be equally fast if the trigger reverses (BoJ walked back)

section: Case Study 3: UK Gilt Crisis (Sep 2022) — Fiscal → Fixed Income → Equity → FX
content: Sep 23: Truss mini-budget (-£45B unfunded tax cuts) └── 30Y gilt yield: 3.5% → 5.0% in 4 days Sep 26-27: LDI pension funds hit margin calls └── Forced selling of gilts → price falls further → more margin calls └── DOOM LOOP: margin call → sell → price drop → more margin calls Sep 28: BoE emergency intervention: £65B gilt purchasing └── 30Y gilt yield: 5.0% → 4.0% in 1 day (BoE backstop) └── GBP: 1.12 → 1.07 (within 50 pips of parity to USD) └── FTSE -8% over the week, bank stocks destroyed Oct 14: Truss resigns (shortest-serving PM in UK history) Oct 24: Sunak becomes PM, reverses fiscal plans └── GBP recovers to 1.15. Gilt yields normalize. Cross-asset lessons: 1. FISCAL policy can break FIXED INCOME markets as violently as monetary policy 2. Leverage in hidden places (LDI pension funds, 4-7x) creates non-linear responses 3. FX is the pressure valve — when bond markets can't absorb the shock, FX takes the hit 4. Central bank credibility as backstop works — but only when accompanied by policy reversal 5. Contagion path: Sovereign bonds → leveraged holders → FX → equity → global rate vol ---

section: Q1: "Oil spiked 8% this morning. Walk me through what you'd check and how you'd communicate the risk"
content: Answer: "I'd structure this as three concentric rings: direct, secondary transmission, and tail. Direct (first 30 minutes): Which PMs have energy positions — long or short? What's our portfolio's aggregate oil beta? I'd compute this quickly: multiply each position's oil sensitivity by its weight. If we have sell-side research with energy basket betas, I can estimate net P&L impact within 15 minutes. For example, if we're net +$500K per dollar of oil through energy longs but -$200K through airlines and transport shorts, our net direct impact from an $8 oil move is about +$2.4M. That's the easy part. Secondary transmission (first 2 hours): Check TIPS breakevens — have they repriced? If 5Y breakevens widened 10bp, that changes the market's rate path expectations. I'd check fed funds futures — are rate cut expectations being pulled forward or pushed back? That affects our duration positions and growth/tech exposure. Check DXY — oil shocks typically strengthen the dollar, which hurts our EM and non-USD exposure. At this point I'm building a cross-asset sensitivity table: oil impact + rates impact + FX impact + credit spread impact. Tail scenario (communicate by end of day): If oil stays at this level or rises further, what's the macro regime implication? I'd run two scenarios: 'Hormuz resolved in 2 weeks' where the spike reverses, and 'sustained $120+ oil' where we enter stagflation. Under the stagflation scenario, I'd recalculate portfolio VaR using positive stock-bond correlation assumptions, because our current VaR assumes recent negative correlation. My guess is effective portfolio risk would be 1.5-2x what VaR reports. I'd communicate this to the PM as: 'Our direct oil P&L is +$2.4M, but our cross-asset exposure through rates, credit, and FX is -$3.8M. Net, we're down roughly $1.4M on the day assuming current marker. More importantly, if oil sustains at this level, our effective portfolio risk under a stagflation correlation regime is approximately 70% higher than current VaR. I'd recommend reviewing our duration hedges, because they may not work if stock-bond correlation flips positive.'"

section: Q2: "The 10-year yield dropped 15bp and equities also dropped 2%. What's happening and what does it mean for the portfolio?"
content: Answer: "That combination — yields down AND equities down — tells me the market is pricing a GROWTH shock, not an inflation shock. In an inflation shock, you'd see yields up and equities down. Here, the market is saying: recession risk just increased. I'd decompose the 15bp move. First, check if it's real rates or breakevens: look at 10Y TIPS. If TIPS yield fell 12bp and breakevens fell 3bp, the move is overwhelmingly about lower growth expectations compressing real rates, with a slight disinflationary signal. Second, check the trigger. Was there a data release? ISM miss? Earnings warning from a bellwether? The trigger determines whether this is a one-day adjustment or the start of a trend. If ISM services broke below 50, that's a regime change signal. Third, check cross-asset confirmation. Is HY widening? That confirms growth scare. Is JPY strengthening? That confirms risk-off. Is copper falling? That confirms the industrial-demand story. If ALL of these confirm, we're in a genuine risk-off regime, not just noise. For the portfolio: Our equity positions are losing on the sell-off, and our bond positions are gaining from the rally. The QUESTION is whether these offset. If our duration is properly calibrated as a hedge, we should be roughly flat on the day. If our equity P&L is -$3M and our bond P&L is only +$1M, our hedge ratio is too low for a growth-shock regime. I'd recalculate the optimal hedge ratio using the current SPX/UST regression beta: if SPX is moving 2% per 15bp in bonds, we need roughly 2% × equity notional / DV01 × 15 in duration to be hedged. If we're underexposed, I'd recommend increasing."

section: Q3: "How do you think about correlation in a multi-asset portfolio? When does diversification fail?"
content: Answer: "Correlation is regime-dependent, and that's the whole ballgame for portfolio risk. The unconditional correlation between, say, equities and bonds over the last 20 years is about -0.20. But that number is an average of two very different regimes. In growth-shock periods, it's -0.40 to -0.50 — bonds are a great hedge. In inflation-shock periods, it's +0.30 to +0.50 — bonds are an additional source of risk. A VaR model that uses a single correlation number is averaging over these regimes and is WRONG in both of them. Diversification fails specifically when three conditions coincide. First, the dominant factor shifts from asset-specific to macro-systemic — when everything becomes about the same thing, like 'will inflation stay sticky?' Then all assets respond to the same factor, and pairwise correlations converge toward 1. Second, forced selling creates a mechanical channel: margin calls don't respect correlation — you sell whatever is liquid. In March 2020, even Treasuries sold off because hedge funds needed cash. Third, when volatility spikes, risk-parity and vol-targeting strategies mechanically deleverage, selling across asset classes simultaneously. For practical risk management, I track three things. First, the stock-bond correlation on a rolling 21-day basis — if it flips sign, I immediately flag that our hedge ratios need review. Second, cross-sector equity correlations — if average pairwise correlation exceeds 0.6, the portfolio is more concentrated than it looks on paper. Third, I run our portfolio VaR under two correlation matrices — recent empirical and a stress correlation matrix where correlations are elevated by a factor reflecting the current VIX regime. The gap between those two VaR numbers IS our diversification risk. The punchline: diversification is a fair-weather friend. It works when you don't need it and fails when you do. That's why stress testing and scenario analysis — not VaR — are the real risk management tools." --- Related files: → P Macro Fundamentals — macro drivers that create the cascades → R Current Market Context — today's specific market numbers and catalysts → A Cash Instruments — asset classes referenced in cross-asset chains → I Hedging Strategies — how to hedge cross-asset exposure → U Scenario Analysis — scenario framework applied to cross-asset events → 09 Market Intuition — detailed macro calendar and microstructure

A junior RM who knows every formula but can't navigate a live trading floor is useless. A junior RM who can read a blotter in 5 minutes, sense when a PM is rationalizing a losing position, flag the one cross-asset risk that matters today, and communicate it in 30 seconds to a busy Head of Risk — that person is invaluable from week one. This domain is the JUDGMENT layer. Technical knowledge gets you in the room. Judgment keeps you there. At Squarepoint, you'll sit between discretionary PMs who think in conviction and narrative, and a systematic infrastructure that thinks in quantitative constraints. Your job is to bridge both — translate quant frameworks into actionable PM guidance, and translate PM intuition into risk-budgeted positions. Every section below is designed for immediate practical application. ---

When you sit down at 7:00 AM, you'll see something like this on your screen: Name Dir Notional ($M) % NAV Sector Delta Gamma Vega DV01 ($K) P&L Today ($K) P&L MTD ($K) Days Held --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- 1 XOM Long 80 8.0% Energy 1.00 — — — +950 +2,100 34 2 AAPL Long 45 4.5% Tech 0.85 0.02 — — -320 -850 22 3 SPX Jun 5200P Short 30 3.0% Index +0.35 -0.04 -180 — -410 -600 15 4 TLT Long 25 2.5% Rates 1.00 — — 38 -150 -400 40 5 BUND Jun 130C Long 15 1.5% EU Rates 0.45 0.01 65 12 +30 -120 18 6 BRL/USD Short 20 2.0% EM FX — — — — +80 -200 28 7 CVX Long 35 3.5% Energy 1.00 — — — +420 +900 34 8 NVDA Jun 900C Long 12 1.2% Tech 0.55 0.03 95 — -180 -500 10 Portfolio summary: - Gross: $262M Net long: $182M (net/gross = 69% long-biased) - NAV: $1,000M - VaR (95%, 1d): $8.2M (limit: $10M) - Margin utilization: 72% - MTD P&L: +$330K

section: The Systematic Scan — 8 Checks in 5 Minutes
content: 1. CONCENTRATION (30 seconds) - XOM at 8.0% NAV → large single-name. Combined with CVX at 3.5% → 11.5% in energy. Any energy-specific shock (oil reversal, earnings miss) causes 10%+ portfolio impact. - Rule of thumb: >5% NAV in one name = flag. >15% in one sector = escalate. 2. DIRECTION (30 seconds) - Net long $182M on $1B NAV → 18.2% net equity exposure - But the short puts ADD to effective long when market falls. At SPX -5%, those puts' delta increases → effective long exposure could reach 25%+ - Question: is this intentional directional allocation or drift? 3. GREEKS — ACCELERATION RISK (45 seconds) - Short gamma via SPX puts (-0.04) = PM gets LONGER as market falls. This is the toxic convexity profile. - Short vega (-180) via puts + long vega (+95 + 65) via calls = net short vega -20. Manageable, but if vol spikes 5 points, that's -$100K from vega alone. - Key calculation: if SPX drops 3% in a day: $$\text{New put delta} \approx 0.35 + 0.04 \times \frac{0.03 \times 5200}{1} \approx 0.35 + 6.24 = \text{capped at } {\sim}0.65$$ Put delta roughly doubles → adds ~$9M to effective long exposure → total portfolio gets 50% longer at the worst possible time. 4. CORRELATION — ARE "DIFFERENT" POSITIONS THE SAME BET? (45 seconds) - XOM + CVX = both long oil → obvious energy concentration - But also: BRL/USD short → Brazil is an oil exporter → if oil rallies, BRL strengthens → the short BRL position LOSES money. So this is partially hedging the energy longs... or is it an independent macro view? - TLT long = supposed equity hedge → but in a stagflation scenario, equities AND bonds sell off. The "hedge" becomes additional loss. Check: is the stock-bond correlation currently positive or negative? 5. P&L TRAJECTORY — WHAT'S BLEEDING? (30 seconds) - AAPL: -$850K MTD, held 22 days. That's -1.9% on the position. Modest, but what's the thesis? Is this a conviction hold or a stale position? - NVDA calls: -$500K MTD in only 10 days, with 0.55 delta on a long call = time decay is eating this. At what point does the PM cut? - BRL: -$200K, held 28 days. No signs of thesis working. Time-horizon question. 6. HEDGE EFFECTIVENESS (30 seconds) - TLT hedge: $25M notional with DV01 of $38K. For a $182M net equity portfolio, this provides partial duration offset but minimal equity hedge UNLESS stock-bond ρ < 0. - Quick check: rolling 21d SPX/TLT correlation. If ρ > 0 (currently likely due to oil shock), TLT is NOT hedging — it's an independent risk source. 7. LIQUIDITY (30 seconds) - All names are liquid large-cap or deep options markets. No liquidity flags today. - But: in a stress scenario, bid-ask on deep OTM puts widens 3-5x. The short puts become harder to close at fair value. 8. STRESS — WORST SINGLE DAY (30 seconds) - Scenario: SPX -5%, oil -10%, rates +20bp (stagflation shock) - Quick estimate: Equity: -$182M × 5% × beta(~1.1) = -$10M. Energy: XOM+CVX -$115M × 5% extra = -$5.8M. Puts: gamma adds $9M long exposure, then loses on that too. TLT: -$38K × 20 = -$760K. -

After the 5-minute scan, you produce: MORNING RISK SNAPSHOT — Date PM: Name NAV: $1,000M VaR: $8.2M/$10M (82%) TOP 3 RISKS: 1. Energy concentration: XOM + CVX = 11.5% NAV. Oil reversal -10% → estimated -$5.8M (58% of VaR limit consumed by ONE factor). 2. Short gamma: SPX puts → effective exposure increases 50% on SPX -3%. Book gets longest at the worst time. 3. TLT hedge may not work: stock-bond ρ is currently positive. In stagflation scenario, TLT adds $760K loss, not protection. KEY EVENTS TODAY: CPI release 08:30. Consensus core MoM +0.3%. If hot (>0.35%): expect rates +10bp, SPX -1-2%, oil impact ambiguous. PM EXPOSURE: Long equities + short puts = max pain on a sell-off day. MY RECOMMENDATION: Consider rolling SPX puts down 100 strikes to reduce gamma exposure ahead of CPI. Cost: ~$200K in premium. Benefit: VaR drops to $7.5M, tail exposure capped. ---

Low probability (<10%) Medium probability (10-40%) High probability (>40%) --- --- --- --- High impact (>$5M) MONITOR — stress tests, contingency plans. Don't dismiss because "unlikely." These are the cases that end careers. PREPARE — active hedge, position size review, IC discussion within 24h ACT NOW — reduce, hedge, or get explicit IC sign-off. No debate. Medium impact ($1-5M) ACCEPT — standard operating risk WATCH — daily monitoring, pre-defined action triggers ADDRESS — within 1-2 days, ensure intentional Low impact (<$1M) IGNORE — don't waste bandwidth ACCEPT — limits handle this ROUTINE — manage through standing limits

Severity Trigger Who to tell When How --- --- --- --- --- GREEN Approaching soft limit (70-80%) PM only As it happens Verbal: "FYI, approaching limit " YELLOW Breaching soft limit / unusual risk concentration PM + note for desk head Within 1 hour Message: "At 85% of limit . Recommendation: action " ORANGE Approaching hard limit (90%+) / correlation breakdown PM + desk head + risk committee Same day Formal: documented escalation with alternatives RED Hard limit breach / margin call / system failure Everyone up to CRO Immediately Call + written documentation. Timestamp everything.

section: What Gets Flagged and When — The Decision Tree
content: New information arrives (market move, news, PM request) │ ├── Does it change our risk profile by > 10% of any limit? │ ├── YES → Quantify immediately. Prepare recommendation. │ │ ├── Is it approaching/breaching a HARD limit? │ │ │ ├── YES → ESCALATE to IC. No discretion. │ │ │ └── NO → Talk to PM. "Here's what I see. Here's what I recommend." │ │ │ ├── PM agrees → Implement │ │ │ └── PM disagrees → Document PM's rationale. If you believe genuine │ │ │ risk exists, escalate to desk head with data. │ └── NO → Log it, monitor, include in next regular check │ ├── Is a PM requesting a new trade / position increase? │ ├── YES → Pre-trade risk check: │ │ ├── Impact on VaR, concentration, margin, Greeks │ │ ├── Does it breach any soft/hard limit? │ │ ├── Does it change the portfolio's factor profile? │ │ └── Present: "Adding this position does X to your risk profile. │ │ You'll be at Y % of limit . Proceed?" │ └── NO → Continue monitoring │ └── Is this a BEHAVIORAL signal from PM? ├── YES → See Part 7: Behavioral Red Flags └── NO → Standard workflow

At 10:15 AM, you notice all of these: Issue Impact Probability Priority --- --- --- --- --- 1 XOM approaching 10% NAV (limit: 10%) Medium High (current drift) YELLOW — talk to PM in next 30 min 2 Overnight EM crisis → BRL gap open -4% Small ($400K) Already happened GREEN — inform PM, it's small 3 SPX -2% in first 45 min, gamma adding exposure High ($3M+ at risk) Real-time ORANGE — prepare recommendation now 4 AAPL earnings tonight — PM hasn't mentioned it Medium Known catalyst YELLOW — ask PM about overnight risk plan 5 IT tells you the VaR model didn't run overnight Unknown (flying blind) Already happened RED — escalate to desk head immediately Correct triage order: 5 → 3 → 1 → 4 → 2 You handle 5 first (you can't manage risk you can't measure), then 3 (active P&L risk), then 1 and 4 (medium-priority conversations), and 2 last (small, already happened). ---

Level What they do Example PM perception --- --- --- --- Level 1: Compliance Reports limit breaches after they happen "Your VaR is $12M against a $10M limit. You need to reduce." "Useless. I already know." Level 2: Monitoring Tracks metrics in real time, sends alerts "You're at 92% of concentration limit in energy." "At least they're watching." Level 3: Diagnosis Decomposes risk and explains where it comes from "Your VaR is $12M. 60% is from short gamma, 30% from energy concentration, 10% from FX." "OK, that's helpful." Level 4: Solutions Provides actionable alternatives with trade-offs "To get VaR to $10M: Option A — roll puts down 100 (saves $2.5M VaR, costs $200K premium). Option B — trim XOM by 20% (saves $1.8M VaR, reduces your best thesis). Your call." "This person makes me better." Level 5: Anticipation Identifies risks before they materialize and builds frameworks to prevent them "CPI is in 2 hours. If core prints >0.4%, your short gamma + energy concentration creates a -$8M scenario. I recommend a pre-CPI adjustment: specific trade . Here's the cost-benefit." "I won't trade without them."

Situation: Oil up 8% Bad RM Good RM --- --- "Oil is up 8%. Here's the P&L." "Oil up 8%. Your net P&L is +$900K, mostly XOM/CVX. But here are two second-order risks you should know about:" (Stops there) "1. Your short SPX puts are now deeper short gamma — if the market sells off on stagflation fear, your puts add $9M to your long exposure at exactly the wrong moment." "2. TIPS breakevens widened 8bp, which means the market is repricing rate cut expectations. If fed funds futures reprice to 'no cuts this year,' your TLT and BUND positions lose another $500K." "Recommendation: roll the SPX puts down 100 strikes to flatten gamma. Cost: $200K. Or add a 5100/5000 put spread as a crash hedge — cost: $350K." Situation: PM wants to double a losing position Bad RM Good RM --- --- "That will breach your concentration limit." "I want to understand the thesis first. When you entered AAPL at $185, the thesis was iPhone cycle + AI services. It's now at $172 — what's changed in the fundamental story?" (End of conversation) If PM says nothing has changed : "OK, I can support adding if the thesis is intact. But let me show you the portfolio impact: VaR goes from $8.2M to $9.7M. You'll be at 97% of limit. And if AAPL reports earnings and misses, combined with your existing positions, max loss is $4.2M on that name alone — that's 42% of your VaR budget on one event. Alternative: add $15M instead of $45M, which captures 80% of the thesis with half the concentration risk." If PM says "it has to come back" : "That's not a thesis — that's hope. I need to flag this to the desk head. I'm not blocking you, but I need an explicit sign-off with that rationale documented." Situation: Vol running 30%+ above target Bad RM Good RM --- --- "The portfolio's realized vol is above target." "Realized vol is running 18% vs your 12% target, for 8 straight days. I decomposed it:" "• 60% from market vol increase (VIX moved from 16 to 22 — that's beta, not your fault)" "• 40% from XOM/CVX concentration, which is running 3x the vol of the rest of the book right now" "If you want to bring vol back to target: trimming XOM by 25% does it. If you want to keep the position because the thesis is strong, we need IC sign-off on elevated vol. Which path do you prefer?"

Your job is not to minimize risk. Your job is to maximize INFORMED risk-taking. Every dollar of risk should be: 1. Intentional — the PM explicitly chose to take this risk (not drift, not accident) 2. Sized — within the risk budget, with buffer for stress 3. Understood — at the portfolio level, including second-order effects and cross-asset transmission 4. Compensated — the expected return justifies the risk (Sharpe > 0 on marginal capital) 5. Hedgeable — there's a way out if the thesis is wrong (or the PM consciously doesn't hedge and accepts the tail) ---

Time Task Output --- --- --- 06:30 Overnight scan: Asia close, Europe open, oil, rates, FX, any >1σ moves 3-line overnight summary 07:00 Position check: Re-mark to overnight levels. Has anything moved enough to change Greeks or VaR materially? Updated P&L and Greeks estimate 07:15 News scan: Geopolitical, company-specific (earnings after close yesterday?), central bank commentary Tag anything that affects current positions 07:30 Stress test refresh: Re-run top 3 scenarios with current marks Updated stress P&L numbers 07:45 Data calendar check: Any Tier 1 releases today? Consensus for each? Pre-position analysis: PM exposure to surprises 08:00 Morning summary: Compile one-page snapshot for PM Deliver to PM as they arrive, or ping pre-market 08:30 Data release (if applicable): NFP/CPI/GDP — watch real-time vs consensus Immediate: "Core CPI came in at 0.4% vs 0.3% expected. Here's impact on our book." 09:00 PM conversation: Walk through morning summary. "Here are the 2-3 things I'd focus on today." Aligned on priorities for the day

Task Frequency What to look for --- --- --- P&L monitoring Continuous Any position losing > $1M or > 2% of entry → flag Greeks monitoring Every 30 min Gamma drift: are we getting longer/shorter as market moves? Limit utilization Hourly VaR, concentration, sector, Greeks limits → dashboard Cross-asset regime Continuous SPX and TLT moving same direction? Regime flag. Oil >3%? Cascade check. PM requests As they come "Can I add $20M?" → Run pre-trade check → "Impact: VaR +$1.2M, 90% of limit. Margin +5%. Proceed?" Anomaly detection Continuous Unusual vol in held names, liquidity deterioration, basis widening, ETF discount Event monitoring Scheduled FOMC at 14:00? → Pre-position: "Here's our exposure to each outcome."

Time Task Output --- --- --- 16:00 Mark-to-close P&L Official daily P&L with attribution (delta/gamma/vega/theta/residual) 16:15 Limit status update All limits: current utilization vs limits. Any approaching/breaching. 16:30 End-of-day risk report Full report: P&L, P&L attribution, Greeks, factor exposures, stress scenarios 17:00 Tomorrow's catalysts Earnings, data releases, options expiry, geopolitical. What could gap overnight? 17:15 Scenario update If market conditions changed today, refresh scenario assumptions 17:30 PM debrief (if needed) "Today we gained/lost $X. The key risk going into tomorrow is Y ."

section: Weekly Deep Dive (Friday afternoon or Monday morning)
content: Analysis What it reveals Action --- --- --- Factor decomposition Is the PM generating alpha or harvesting beta? If R²(SPX) > 0.6, the "alpha" is really equity beta. Flag. Correlation evolution Are positions becoming more correlated? If avg pairwise ρ > 0.6, diversification is collapsing. Flag. P&L attribution by source Where's the money coming from? Delta? Gamma? Theta? If >50% from theta (selling options premium), the strategy has tail risk Drawdown analysis How far is the PM from max drawdown limit? <70% remaining → monitor. <40% → discuss position reductions. Position age vs thesis Are there "zombie" positions past their time horizon? Positions >60 days with no P&L → challenge: "Is this still a live thesis?" Liquidity scoring Can we exit the portfolio in N days at what cost? If total exit > 5 days at 2% impact, we're too concentrated/illiquid ---

section: How to Talk to Traders / PMs
content: Rule 1: Lead with P&L impact, not Greek letters. - ❌ "Your gamma is -0.04 on the SPX puts" - ✅ "If SPX drops 2%, your short gamma adds $9M to your long exposure and your P&L swing is -$3.5M" Rule 2: Speak their language. - ❌ "The portfolio's standard deviation is elevated" - ✅ "Your book moves $2.3M per 1% SPX move — that's up from $1.5M last week" Rule 3: Time it right. - 🟢 Pre-market and post-close: deep conversations, thesis reviews, structural adjustments - 🟡 During market hours: alerts, quick answers, pre-trade checks - 🔴 During active trading/execution: only urgent alerts (limit breach, margin call, system failure) Rule 4: Be definitive, not wishy-washy. - ❌ "Maybe we should think about potentially reducing some of the energy exposure..." - ✅ "I'd recommend reducing XOM by $15M. Here's why: 30 seconds . Your call." Rule 5: Pick your battles. Seriously. - If you flag 10 things a day, you get tuned out by day 3 - Flag the 1-2 things that TRULY matter. Earn credibility through precision, not volume. - The PM should think: "When this person speaks up, I listen, because they only speak up when it matters."

Step 1: Validate the thesis first "I understand the trade is X because you believe Y . I think the thesis has merit." Step 2: Add the risk dimension "My concern isn't the thesis — it's the sizing relative to what else is in the book." Step 3: Bring data, not opinions "Under the current scenario set, this position alone generates a -$15M loss in a March 2020 replay. Combined with the rest of the book, total drawdown is -$22M, which breaches your 5% NAV limit." Step 4: Offer alternatives "Instead of $45M notional, what about $25M plus a call spread overlay? You get 80% of the upside thesis with 55% of the risk. VaR stays within limit." Step 5: Escalate with documentation if needed "If you want to proceed at $45M, I respect your judgment, but I need to flag this to the desk head with the scenario numbers. Not blocking — just ensuring visibility."

section: Talking to Senior Risk (CRO / Head of Risk / Constantine)
content: Format: Situation → Quantification → Recommendation → Trade-offs. 90 seconds max. Template: > "PM name is at X % drawdown against Y % limit. The concentration is in sector/factor , currently Z % of NAV. The concern is specific scenario : if event , portfolio loss is estimated at $ number , which would breach/approach limit . I recommend action A . PM prefers action B because their rationale . I'm requesting visibility/IC review/limit exception ." Example: > "PM Chen is at 4.2% drawdown against a 5% limit. Concentrated in energy — XOM + CVX = 11.5% NAV — and short SPX gamma. If oil reverses 10% and SPX drops 5% simultaneously, estimated loss is $16M, which breaches the VaR limit and approaches the drawdown limit. I recommend trimming energy by 25% and rolling the puts. PM disagrees — believes oil hasn't peaked. Requesting IC review before the close." What they want to hear: 1. Numbers — not "it's getting risky" but "$16M loss scenario" 2. Context — how does this compare to limits, historical, other PMs 3. Your judgment — are you concerned or just reporting? If concerned, say so explicitly 4. What you need — a decision, visibility, or approval. Be specific. ---

section: When a PM Wants to Put on a New Trade
content: Every new trade request should trigger a rapid risk check before execution: The Pre-Trade Checklist (2 minutes): Check Calculation Output --- --- --- VaR impact Marginal VaR: ΔVaR from adding this position "VaR goes from $8.2M → $9.7M. You're at 97% of limit." Concentration New position size as % NAV, combined with same-sector exposure "This takes energy to 14.5% NAV. Above our 12% soft limit." Margin impact Additional margin required + new margin utilization "Margin utilization goes from 72% → 81%. Leaves thin buffer." Correlation How does this position correlate with existing book? "ρ = 0.7 with rest of energy book. Not diversifying." Factor overlap Does this add new factor exposure or amplify existing? "This adds another $800K of oil beta. You already have $2.1M." Liquidity Can we exit this position in 1 day at <1% impact? "ADV is $50M. $35M position = 70% ADV. Exit takes 2-3 days." Worst case If this position goes wrong, what's the max loss? "If it gaps -15%, that's -$5.3M. Combined with existing book: -$21M." The delivery: "Adding $35M long in name . Here's the impact: VaR +$1.5M to $9.7M (97% of limit). Energy concentration goes to 14.5% (above 12% soft limit). Margin to 81%. The position is correlated 0.7 with your existing energy book — so you're amplifying, not diversifying. If you still want to proceed, I'd recommend either (a) trimming CVX by $15M to make room, or (b) getting IC sign-off on the limit exception. Which do you prefer?"

section: PM Says: "What if I restructure it as options?"
content: You should be able to immediately respond: Structure Risk profile vs equity Advantage Disadvantage --- --- --- --- Long stock ($35M) Full delta, no optionality Full upside, no time decay Full downside, max margin Long call (ATM, 3M) Delta ~0.5, long gamma, long vega Defined loss, less margin Premium cost ($1.5M), time decay Call spread (ATM/+15%) Delta ~0.35, defined upside Cheaper than call, defined risk Capped upside at 15% Risk reversal (sell put, buy call) Delta ~0.7, short downside gamma Zero premium Naked downside risk, margin-intensive Collar on existing Caps upside and downside No premium, defined range No participation above cap Your job: Help the PM find the structure that best expresses their thesis within the risk budget. "If your thesis is 'XOM goes up 10% in 2 months with limited downside,' a call spread captures that at 40% of the marginal risk. Want me to price it?" ---

Red flag What it really means What to say Escalation? --- --- --- --- Position > 5% NAV in single name Concentration risk — one event can cause significant damage "At 8% NAV, this is your largest position. An earnings miss could cost you $4M. Is this size intentional?" If >8% and PM won't reduce, document and flag to desk head Adding to a losing position (no new catalyst) Loss aversion / averaging down on hope "Walk me through what's changed since entry. The stock is down 12% — is there new information supporting the thesis?" If PM says "it has to come back" → ESCALATE. Most dangerous sentence in trading. Thesis has changed 3+ times Post-hoc rationalization Document each thesis version with dates. Present the timeline: "You entered for reason A, then it was B, now C. Is this still a high-conviction idea?" Document. This IS the escalation evidence. Stop-loss moved lower repeatedly Discipline breakdown "The stop was originally at $165, then $155, now $148. The original risk/reward assumed a $165 stop. At $148, the trade has a very different risk/reward." Yes — this pattern ends in blow-ups Position held past original time horizon Thesis drift, dead capital "This was originally a 3-week catalyst trade. It's been 8 weeks. Is the thesis still live or is this tying up risk budget?" Flag in weekly review PM trading much larger after a win streak Overconfidence bias Don't say "you're overconfident." Instead: "Your position sizes have grown 40% this month. The average trade is $40M vs $28M last month. Is this a deliberate strategy shift or should we recalibrate?" Flag in weekly review if persistent PM refusing to take ANY losses Inability to accept being wrong Monitor the aggregate of held losers. If 60%+ of positions are underwater with no catalysts, flag holistically Escalate if aggregate drawdown approaching limits

Red Flag Detection Response --- --- --- All positions profitable in the same way Factor decomposition: if one factor explains >60% of P&L "Your 8 positions are actually 1 bet on oil . If oil reverses 10%, all 8 lose simultaneously. Is that intentional?" Vol persistently above target (>1 week) Realized vol / target vol > 1.3 for 5+ consecutive days Decompose: market vol (beta) vs position-specific (alpha controllable). Recommend action on the controllable part. Margin utilization > 80% Daily margin report "You have 20% buffer. One bad day forces involuntary liquidation at the worst prices. I recommend creating buffer by reducing lowest conviction position ." Growing "unexplained" P&L residual P&L attribution: residual > 10% of total P&L for 3+ days Investigate: cross-gamma? Correlation change? Data error? Model mis-specification? Don't ignore — this is the model's blind spot. Hedge ratio drifting Ratio of hedge P&L vs portfolio P&L in stress scenarios shifting >20% from target Rebalance hedges. If stock-bond ρ has changed, the old hedge ratio is wrong. All positions entered in the last 5 days Position age distribution PM may have over-traded after stress/drawdown. Check if this is a considered rebuild or emotional re-entry. ---

section: Typical Multi-Strategy Hedge Fund Limit Architecture
content: Limit type What it controls Typical threshold Hard vs soft Breach consequence --- --- --- --- --- VaR (95%, 1d) Overall portfolio risk 1-2% of NAV Hard Must reduce within 24h or IC approval Single-name concentration Idiosyncratic risk 5-8% NAV Soft (5%), Hard (10%) Soft: PM discretion. Hard: mandatory reduction. Sector concentration Sector tail risk 15-25% NAV net Soft Review with desk head Gross exposure Leverage / total capital deployed 200-400% NAV Hard Must reduce Net exposure Directional bias ±30-50% NAV Soft PM must justify directionally Max drawdown Stop-loss on PM pod 3-5% of allocated capital Hard Triggers capital reduction / PM review Greeks (gamma) Convexity risk Fund-specific Soft Case-by-case review Greeks (vega) Vol exposure Fund-specific Soft Case-by-case review Liquidity (days-to-exit) Portfolio can be unwound in N days 3-5 days for full book Soft Flag illiquid positions Factor budgets Intended vs unintended factor loading Factor-specific Soft Decompose and discuss with PM Correlation limit Avg pairwise ρ in portfolio < 0.5 target Soft Flag hidden concentration

Limits interact. A portfolio can be within every individual limit and still be dangerously risky: Example: - VaR: 92% of limit (fine individually) - Energy concentration: 14% (fine individually, under 15% limit) - Short gamma: moderate (within Greeks limit) - Margin: 78% (fine individually) BUT: In a stagflation scenario (oil -10%, SPX -5%, rates +20bp): - Energy concentration → -$6M - Short gamma adds effective long exposure → -$4M more - Margin call triggered → forced to sell at worst prices → -$2M additional slippage - Total: -$12M on a $10M VaR limit → breach Lesson: The RM's job is to check limits INDIVIDUALLY and IN COMBINATION. The scenario analysis catches what individual limits miss.

Limits should not be static. Review monthly: Question If YES --- --- Has market vol regime changed? VaR limit may need tightening (same $ limit means more market risk in high-vol) Has the PM's strategy evolved? Greeks and factor limits may need adjusting Are correlations structurally different? Concentration limits may need tightening (higher ρ = less true diversification) Has the PM hit drawdown? Reduce capital allocation / risk limits proportionally Has the PM demonstrated consistent alpha? May warrant limit increase with IC approval ---

$$\text{Margin utilization} = \frac{\text{Total initial margin required}}{\text{Available margin (equity − haircuts)}}$$ Utilization Status Action --- --- --- < 60% Comfortable No action needed 60-75% Normal operating range Monitor daily 75-85% Elevated Flag to PM: "Running tight. One bad day = margin call." 85-95% Danger zone Active discussion: which position to trim for buffer? > 95% Margin call imminent Emergency: identify most expendable position. Act within hours.

When a margin call arrives: 1. Verify the call — check the numbers. Margin systems have errors. Confirm with PB/clearing. 2. Quantify the shortfall — how much do we need to post or liquidate? 3. Triage positions — rank by: (a) conviction, (b) liquidity, (c) contribution to margin requirement 4. Simulate liquidation options: Option Capital freed Market impact Strategy damage --- --- --- --- Sell AAPL ($45M, liquid) $12M margin Low (0.1% ADV) Moderate — removes a conviction position Close SPX puts ($30M, liquid) $18M margin Low Low — reduces risk and margin simultaneously Trim XOM by 30% ($24M) $8M margin Medium (1.5% ADV) High — reduces best-performing position 5. Recommend — "I'd close the SPX puts first — frees $18M margin, reduces our biggest risk (short gamma), and has minimal market impact. That covers the margin call with buffer. If we need more, trim AAPL $15M." 6. Execute — coordinate with PM and trading desk 7. Document — timestamp everything. Post-mortem: why did we get here? How do we prevent recurrence?
