$$\text{Days to exit} = \frac{\text{Position size}}{\text{ADV} \times \text{participation rate}}$$ Participation rate Context Market impact estimate --- --- --- 5% Minimal impact, patient exit ~5-10bp 10% Normal, moderate impact ~15-25bp 20% Aggressive, visible ~40-75bp 25%+ Fire sale, price-sensitive ~100bp+ Portfolio liquidity profile example: Position Notional ADV Days @ 10% Days @ 20% Classification --- --- --- --- --- --- XOM $80M $2.5B 0.3 0.2 Highly liquid AAPL $45M $10B 0.05 0.02 Ultra-liquid Small-cap name $15M $25M 6.0 3.0 ILLIQUID — flag SPX puts $30M Deep market 0.1 0.05 Liquid (but spread widens in stress) Key insight: Liquidity is a fair-weather friend. A position that trades $25M/day normally might trade $5M/day in a crisis as market-makers pull back. Always stress-test liquidity: $$\text{Stressed days to exit} = \text{Normal days} \times \frac{1}{\text{liquidity stress factor (0.3-0.5)}}$$ That small-cap position that's "6 days to exit" in normal conditions could be 15-20 days in a crisis. If the PM needs to get out during a sell-off, they're trapped. ---

section: Building Scenarios That Actually Help PMs
content: Bad scenarios are arbitrary: "SPX -20%." Good scenarios are macro-coherent stories that connect: Element Must specify Why --- --- --- Trigger What causes the scenario? Determines speed and cross-asset mechanics Macro path Growth, inflation, policy response Ensures internal consistency Cross-asset Rates, FX, credit, commodities Cascades are where real risk lives Time horizon Acute (1 day), short (1 week), medium (1 month) Some exposures are only dangerous over time Magnitude Calibrated to historical analogues Prevents both over- and under-estimation Correlation assumption Which correlation regime? The same portfolio has dramatically different losses under different ρ assumptions

section: The Scenario Set for a Discretionary Energy-Heavy Book
content: Scenario SPX 10Y Oil DXY HY OAS Portfolio P&L Source --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- 1 Base case (no change) 0% 0 0% 0% 0 $0 — 2 Goldilocks: soft CPI + moderate growth +3% -15bp -3% -1% -15bp +$2.1M Disinflation continuation 3 Growth scare: ISM <47, claims rising -5% -25bp -8% +2% +50bp -$6.3M Late-cycle rollover 4 Inflation shock: core CPI +0.5% MoM -3% +25bp +5% +1.5% +25bp -$4.7M Stagflation entry 5 Oil crash: Hormuz resolution + demand weakness -1% -10bp -25% -0.5% +10bp -$8.2M Energy-specific tail 6 Oil spike: full Hormuz closure -8% +15bp +30% +3% +75bp -$3.4M Energy buffer helps, but gamma kills 7 March 2020 replay: -34% equity -12% -40bp (flight) -20% +4% +200bp -$18.5M Liquidity crisis 8 2022 replay: inflation + rate shock -6% +50bp +10% +3% +80bp -$11.2M Correlation regime shift 9 Vol spike: VIX 15 → 35 -4% -10bp -5% +1% +30bp -$7.8M Short gamma gets killed 10 Custom PM scenario (PM defines) (Calculate) PM's own worst fear Key insight from the scenario table: Scenario 5 (oil crash) is the WORST scenario for this energy-heavy book — worse than a broader equity crash — because the energy concentration dominates. The PM's biggest risk isn't the market; it's oil reversal. This is the kind of insight that makes the RM indispensable.

Do NOT present a 10-row table and say "here are the numbers." Instead: > "I ran 10 scenarios. Three things stood out: > > First, your biggest single risk isn't a market crash — it's an oil reversal. Scenario 5 (oil -25%) costs you $8.2M. That's worse than a 12% equity crash because your portfolio is so concentrated in energy. > > Second, in the inflation shock scenario, your TLT hedge COSTS you $760K instead of helping, because stock-bond correlation is positive in that regime. Your effective hedge ratio is about 30% of what you think it is. > > Third, the short gamma on SPX puts creates asymmetric exposure: you get longer as the market falls. In any scenario with SPX -5% or more, your effective portfolio delta is 50% higher than what the blotter currently shows. > > My recommendation: specific trade adjustments ." ---

Situation: PM is at 4.2% drawdown against 5% limit. Step 1 — Quantify remaining budget: $1B × (5% - 4.2%) = $8M remaining risk budget. At current VaR ($8.2M), one average bad day could breach the limit. Step 2 — Identify P&L drivers: What positions are causing the drawdown? Where is P&L concentrated? Step 3 — Present options: Option Risk reduction P&L impact PM acceptance --- --- --- --- Reduce all positions pro-rata by 20% VaR: $8.2M → $6.6M Reduces recovery potential equally Low — blunt instrument Cut losing positions only VaR: variable Crystallizes losses but stops bleeding Medium — emotional resistance Hedge with index puts VaR: $8.2M → $6.0M Costs $300K premium but preserves upside High — retains thesis Cut the position most correlated with primary loss driver VaR: targeted reduction Most efficient risk reduction per $ High — surgical, smart Step 4 — Recommend: "I'd recommend reducing XOM by 25% (cuts VaR by $1.5M, low market impact) and buying a 2-week put spread on SPX ($300K cost, caps tail at -$5M). This preserves your oil thesis at reduced sizing while capping your drawdown exposure. Total cost: $300K in premium. Budget freed: $2.3M in VaR."

T-minus 1 day: - Map PM exposures to potential outcomes (beat, meet, miss) - Run scenarios for ±1σ and ±2σ surprises - Identify: "If X , which positions gain/lose the most?" - Prepare recommendations for each outcome: "If hot CPI: action . If cold CPI: no action needed ." T-minus 30 minutes: - Send PM the scenario summary: "In 30 minutes, CPI prints. Your exposure: summary . If hot, estimated impact: -$X. If cold: +$Y. I recommend Z if you want to de-risk ahead." T-zero: - Watch the number. Immediately compare to consensus. - Within 2 minutes, estimate PM-specific P&L impact. - If the print is in the tail (>2σ surprise), reach out immediately: "Core CPI +0.5%. This is a 2σ miss. Your estimated hit is -$4.7M across positions . Recommend: action ." T-plus 15 minutes: - Check if the reaction is following the "expected" cross-asset pattern or something unusual - If unusual (markets not reacting as expected to the data), that's information — the narrative may be shifting

You wake up to: "Missiles reported near Strait of Hormuz. Oil futures +12%. Equity futures -3%." 1. Assess direct exposure (5 min): Energy longs, EM FX, shipping/transport, defense. Quick P&L estimate. 2. Assess second-order (15 min): Cross-asset cascade: oil → rates → equities → credit → FX. Run stagflation scenario. 3. Check liquidity (5 min): Will markets open normally? Any positions in affected regions? Circuit breaker risk? 4. Prepare 1-page brief (15 min): - Direct P&L impact: +$Y (energy) / -$Z (equity, FX) - Net impact: $ number - Scenarios: escalation path vs de-escalation path - Recommendations: "Hold energy longs (thesis confirmed). Reduce SPX short gamma immediately (dangerous in vol spike). Consider adding USDJPY hedge for carry unwind risk." 5. Be at desk before PM arrives. Hand them the page. "Here's where we stand."

Situation: A position's bid-ask spread has widened 5x. Volume has dropped 80%. 1. Mark check: Is the position marked correctly? In illiquid conditions, mid-market marks can be misleading. Check: last trade price, dealer quotes, comparable names. 2. Size the problem: What % of NAV is this position? At current liquidity, how many days to exit? 3. Impact estimate: If we sell into this market, what's the expected slippage? 4. Alternatives: Can we hedge the exposure synthetically (correlated ETF, index, options) rather than selling the illiquid position directly? 5. Communicate: "The bid-ask on name has widened from 5bp to 25bp. We can't exit our $15M position without significant impact (~100bp = $150K slippage). I recommend hedging with ETF/index at 10bp cost while we wait for liquidity to normalize." ---

section: Q1: "Walk me through how you'd read a risk report for a discretionary PM's portfolio"
content: Answer: "I'd do a systematic 8-check scan, prioritized by what can hurt us most. First, concentration — is any single name more than 5% of NAV or any sector more than 15%? I want to know what one surprise can do. Second, direction — what's the net exposure? A book that's 70% net long is making a directional bet whether the PM intends it or not. I'd check if that's deliberate. Third, Greeks — specifically short gamma and short vega. Short gamma is the most dangerous because it makes the portfolio longer when the market falls. I'd calculate: if the market drops 3%, how much does our effective exposure increase? If it doubles, that's a flag. Fourth, correlation — are positions that look different actually the same bet? I'd run a quick factor decomposition. If 60% of the book is explained by one factor, everything falls together. Fifth, I'd check hedge effectiveness. If the PM has TLT as a hedge, but we're in a positive stock-bond correlation regime, that hedge isn't working — it's an independent source of risk. I'd flag that immediately. Sixth, P&L trajectory — anything bleeding for more than 2 weeks without a catalyst gets a thesis review. Seventh, liquidity — can we exit every position in less than 2 days at under 50bp impact? Eighth, stress — what's the worst plausible single-day scenario? I'd run it through the current book and check: does it breach any limits? The output of all this is a 1-page morning summary for the PM with the top 3 risks and my specific recommendation for each."

section: Q2: "A PM wants to add $40M to a losing energy position. What do you do?"
content: Answer: "First, I'd want to understand the thesis — not challenge it, but genuinely understand it. What was the entry thesis, and what has changed? If the stock is down 15% and the PM says 'nothing has changed, the thesis is intact, and the market is mispricing this,' that's one conversation. If the PM says 'it has to come back,' that's a very different conversation — that's hope, not a thesis, and I'd flag it. Assuming the thesis is intact, I'd run the numbers. Adding $40M takes this name to X % of NAV — is that within concentration limits? It takes energy sector exposure to Y % — within sector limits? What's the VaR impact — how much risk budget does this consume? And critically, what's the portfolio impact if this position AND the rest of the energy book move against us? I'd present the correlated tail loss. Then I'd offer alternatives. 'Instead of $40M in stock, what about $20M in stock plus a $20M-notional call spread? You get 80% of the upside conviction with 55% of the downside risk, and VaR stays within budget.' Or: 'If you want the full $40M, I'd recommend trimming CVX by $15M to make room — you're effectively concentrating the energy bet in your highest-conviction name.' If the PM wants to proceed at full size and it breaches a limit, I'd say: 'I respect your conviction, but this triggers a limit discussion. I need to flag it to the desk head with the scenario numbers. Not blocking, but ensuring visibility.'"

section: Q3: "How do you distinguish between a genuine risk issue and normal noise?"
content: Answer: "Three filters. First, magnitude relative to limits. If a daily P&L move is 1% of VaR, that's noise. If it's 30% of daily VaR, something structural may have shifted on the day. I track a trailing ratio of realized P&L to predicted P&L from the previous night's VaR — if realized consistently exceeds predicted, the risk model is under-estimating something. Second, persistence. One day of elevated vol or an unusual correlation is noise. Five consecutive days is a regime shift. I track rolling 5-day metrics against 60-day norms. If the 5-day realized vol is more than 1.5x the 60-day average, I flag it. Same for correlations — if a hedge that was -0.3 correlated is now +0.1, that's not noise, that's a structural shift. Third, source identification. I decompose the unexpected P&L. Did it come from delta (market moved)? Gamma (market moved a LOT)? Vega (vol changed)? Correlation (assets moved together unexpectedly)? Or is it unexplained residual? If the residual is growing, there's something the model isn't capturing, and THAT is the genuine risk issue — what you can't measure is more dangerous than what you can. When I find a genuine issue, I apply the urgency matrix: high impact × high probability = act now. High impact × low probability = monitor with scenarios. Most 'noise' is low impact regardless of probability — I let those go. The skill is not flagging everything. It's flagging the right thing at the right time." --- Related files: → K Behavioral Biases — full behavioral framework for PM biases → H PnL Attribution — the daily P&L decomposition skill → Q Cross Asset Relationships — cross-asset cascades for scenario design → F Risk Measures — VaR, stress testing, risk metrics

Every interviewer at Squarepoint will test whether you can think , not just calculate. The trader asks: "What do you think the Fed does next?" The senior RM asks: "Where's the biggest risk to the book?" Your future desk head asks: "What would YOU focus on day one?" - "I don't have a view" → you're a calculator — replaceable by a spreadsheet. - "I think the Fed should cut because..." → you're playing trader — not your job. - "From a risk perspective, the asymmetry is..." → you're a risk manager with a brain . This file teaches you the complete system for forming, calibrating, defending, and expressing risk-informed opinions — the skill that separates junior RM candidates who get offers from those who don't. ---

Dimension Trader Risk Manager --- --- --- Core question "What WILL happen?" "What COULD happen that the market under-prices?" Objective Directional P&L Portfolio resilience Success metric Right more than wrong Identified the risk BEFORE it materialized Time horizon Trade-specific (days-weeks) Regime-level (weeks-months) Confidence req'd High (they're sizing on it) Moderate (they're protecting against it) Expression Position Recommendation

1. Consensus complacency — the market is pricing X but Y is plausible 2. Payoff asymmetry — the loss from being wrong exceeds the gain from being right 3. Portfolio vulnerability — where OUR book is specifically exposed > Key insight: You don't need to be RIGHT about what will happen. You need to be right about what MATTERS — i.e., where the uncompensated risk sits.

section: Why This Matters at Squarepoint Specifically
content: Squarepoint runs discretionary strategies inside a systematic infrastructure. PMs have quantitative tools but make human judgment calls. The RM who can engage with market views through a risk lens — rather than just policing limits — becomes an intellectual partner , not a bureaucrat. ---

section: Step 1: Know What The Market Is Pricing
content: Before you can have a view, map the consensus. These are your "implied assumptions": Instrument What It Tells You How To Read --- --- --- Fed funds futures Cuts/hikes priced, by when CME FedWatch → prob-weighted path VIX / VSTOXX Expected vol (annualized σ) VIX 20 ≈ 1.26% daily moves VIX term structure Contango = calm, backwardation = stressed Front/2nd month ratio Put/call skew (25Δ) Demand for downside protection Elevated skew = fear is specific to downside Credit spreads (CDX IG/HY) Default risk priced by market IG 100bp ≈ benign; HY 500bp+ = stress TIPS breakevens Inflation expectations 5Y BEI vs 10Y BEI; slope = forward inflation shift Oil term structure Supply/demand balance Backwardation = tight now; deep contango = glut USD (DXY) Risk appetite + rate differentials Strong USD = tightening financial conditions globally Gold Real yield + tail hedging demand Gold up while USD flat = fear bid Worked example — reading the dashboard: > VIX at 24, skew at 8 vol points, HY spreads 450bp, 2s10s at -40bp, oil in backwardation, gold at $2,300. > Translation: Market expects elevated but not crisis-level volatility. Skew says downside hedging demand is firm. Credit is stressed but not distressed. Yield curve inverted = recession priced. Oil tight now but not pricing prolonged disruption. Gold elevated = some tail fear. > Gap: VIX at 24 implies ±1.5% daily. Recent realized has been ±2.5%. Realized > implied → vol is underpriced.

section: Step 2: Identify Where Consensus Could Break
content: The market prices the modal outcome (most likely). It consistently under-prices: - Binary events where probability × impact doesn't match the implied price - Regime shifts that invalidate the last 6-12 months of correlation patterns - Second-order effects (oil up → inflation up → rates up → credit stress → contagion) - Positioning-driven amplification (crowded trades that create forced selling cascades) The "What If I'm Wrong" Test For any market consensus, ask: 1. What assumption needs to hold for consensus to be right? 2. What would falsify that assumption? 3. What's the magnitude of the move if it fails? 4. How quickly would our book feel it? Consensus Key assumption Falsification trigger Impact if wrong --- --- --- --- "Fed will cut in June" Inflation continues cooling Core CPI MoM > 0.4% Rates +30bp, equities -3-5% "Oil spike is temporary" Geopolitical resolution in weeks Hormuz blocked through summer Brent $130-150, gasoline $6+ "Credit is fine" Defaults stay below 3% Leveraged sector blow-up HY spreads +200bp, equity -8% "China stimulus works" Property sector stabilizes New developer default EM equities -10%, copper -15% "AI trade continues" Earnings growth justifies multiples Revenue miss at mega-cap Nasdaq -12%, sector rotation

This is where you differentiate yourself. Don't just say "there's a risk." Put numbers on it. Asymmetry Score Formula: $$\text{Asymmetry} = \frac{P {\text{tail}} \times \text{Loss} {\text{tail}}}{P {\text{base}} \times \text{Gain} {\text{base}}}$$ If asymmetry > 1.0, the risk-weighted outcome is against the current position. Worked example: > Market pricing: 70% chance Fed cuts (equities +2%), 30% chance hold + hawkish (equities -5%) > Asymmetry = (0.30 × 5%) / (0.70 × 2%) = 1.5 / 1.4 = 1.07 > Conclusion: Even though a cut is more likely, the expected value of the equity position is slightly negative risk-weighted. The portfolio needs tail protection. Another way to express this to a PM: > "We make $2M if the base case plays out, but lose $5M in the tail. Spending $200K on protection gives us 25:1 payoff in the tail while only reducing the base case by 10%."

Your view must connect to this book, today . Generic macro commentary is useless. For each risk you identify: 1. Which positions are exposed? (name + notional) 2. Through which channel? (delta, gamma, vega, correlation, liquidity) 3. What's the estimated P&L impact under the scenario? 4. What would change the thesis? Template: RISK: description EXPOSED POSITIONS: name, notional, channel SCENARIO P&L: base case → $X, tail case → -$Y ASYMMETRY: ratio RECOMMENDATION: specific action + cost

Risk managers who just identify problems are annoying. Risk managers who solve them are invaluable. The Recommendation Stack (always present these 3): Option Description Cost VaR reduction --- --- --- --- Option A Reduce exposure (trim position by X%) Opportunity cost -30% VaR Option B Hedge with derivatives (specific structure) Premium $ -50% tail VaR Option C Accept risk, tighten stops $0 direct cost -0% (but bounded losses) Critical: You RECOMMEND one option, but you present all three. "I'd lean toward Option B because cost is low relative to the tail risk, but it's your call."

Every view needs a falsification trigger — a specific data point or price level that would make you change your mind. - "I'll drop this concern if core CPI MoM comes in below 0.3%" - "If VIX sustains below 18 for 5 days, the vol regime has normalized" - "If Hormuz shipping resumes above 80% of normal capacity, the oil premium unwinds" Why this matters: It prevents anchoring bias. It shows the interviewer you think probabilistically, not ideologically. ---
> "I think the market is risky because of tariffs and geopolitical tensions." - No specifics, no numbers, no portfolio connection. Sounds like a news headline.
> "VIX is at 24, credit spreads are widening, and the Fed faces a difficult decision." - Cites data but doesn't interpret it. No view, no recommendation.
> "VIX at 24 implies ±1.5% daily moves, but realized vol is running at 2.5%. The market is underpricing volatility ahead of CPI." - Interprets data, identifies a gap. But no portfolio implication.

> "Realized vol exceeding implied by 60% means our delta-hedging is more expensive than our models assume. With CPI Friday, I'd recommend the book moves to shorter gamma rebalancing intervals and we stress-test the hedging P&L for realized vol at 30." - Identifies risk → quantifies → connects to book → recommends action.

> "We're in a positive stock-bond correlation regime with realized vol exceeding implied. This combination means our portfolio's Sharpe ratio math is broken — diversification assumptions overstate our risk-adjusted returns by ~20%. Before CPI, I'd recommend: (1) shrink gross by 10% to match the true diversification benefit, (2) move from correlation-based hedges to explicit puts, and (3) widen the house stress scenarios to include 'hot CPI + bond sell-off' coincidence. Cost: $400K in premium + opportunity cost. Benefit: eliminates the uncompensated tail that's sitting in four PM books." - Regime-aware, quantified, specific, cost-benefit framed, actionable. This is the standard. ---

section: PART 4: BUILDING VIEWS ON 8 KEY RISK THEMES
content: For each theme, here's the complete thinking structure:

What to track: Fed funds futures, dot plot, OIS curve, CB communication tone Where consensus breaks: When the data forces a policy choice that conflicts with forward guidance Scenario Signal it's coming Book impact RM action --- --- --- --- Dovish surprise (cut) NFP < 100K, core CPI < 0.2% MoM Rates rally, equity up, USD down, gold up Check for short-duration positions that get hurt; reduce hedges that assumed hawkish Hawkish surprise (hold/hike) Core CPI > 0.4%, wage growth > 5% Rates sell off, growth stocks hammered, USD up Check for levered long-duration; add rate hedge pre-event Communication gaffe Fed member contradicts consensus Vol spike, whipsaw Warn PMs to reduce event-day gamma; widen stops Framework phrase for interview: "I don't predict what the Fed does. I map the scenarios, assess which one the book is most vulnerable to, and make sure we're either compensated for that risk or protected against it."

What to track: Oil term structure, shipping data, inventories (EIA/API), geopolitical news Where consensus breaks: Supply disruption duration — markets consistently underestimate how long conflicts last Key transmission chain: Oil up → input cost inflation → margin compression (airlines, transport, chemicals) → consumer spending down → earnings revisions → equity sell-off BUT energy/defense sectors rally → cross-asset correlation spikes → diversification fails

What to track: CDX IG/HY spreads, leveraged loan prices, fallen angel count, bank lending standards Where consensus breaks: Credit cycles turn suddenly — spreads are calm until they aren't Red flags: - HY-IG spread ratio widening → lowest quality getting hit first - CCC-rated paper underperforming B-rated → bottom of the barrel cracking - Leveraged loan repricing → private credit stress - Bank loan officer survey tightening → upstream supply of credit shrinking

What to track: VIX level, VIX term structure shape, realized vs implied spread, VVIX (vol of vol) Where consensus breaks: VIX can be low for months then explode — it mean-reverts but from catastrophic levels, not from low VIX regime Implication RM posture --- --- --- < 14 Complacent. Vol is cheap. Recommend buying tail protection (cheap insurance) 14-20 Normal. Markets pricing moderate uncertainty. Standard monitoring 20-30 Elevated. Hedging is expensive but may be necessary. Tighten gross, shorten gamma rebal frequency 30-40 Stressed. Consider if positions can withstand this. Active de-risking, margin monitoring > 40 Crisis. Liquidity dries up. Emergency mode: see R playbooks

What to track: CFTC COT data, prime broker positioning reports, ETF flows, short interest Where consensus breaks: When everyone is on the same side, the exit is too small Crowding indicators: - Record net long/short in futures → reversal risk - Concentrated short interest → squeeze risk - ETF inflow streak → momentum fragility - Low dispersion + high correlation → herding RM application: "Our PM is long the same trade that every macro fund is long according to COT data. If this reverses, liquidity will evaporate because everyone is hitting the same exit. I'd recommend reducing size by 20% or adding explicit stop-losses, even though the fundamental thesis is intact."

What to track: DXY, rate differentials, FX vol (CVIX), EM FX basket, risk reversals Where consensus breaks: Dollar moves affect everything — it's the most underrated cross-asset driver Transmission: Strong USD → EM stress → commodity prices down → carry trade losses → vol spike → risk-off cascade

What to track: Fed balance sheet, bank reserves, repo rates, bid-ask spreads, market depth Where consensus breaks: Liquidity is invisible until it's gone Warning signals: - ON RRP dropping toward zero → reserve scarcity approaching - Repo rate spikes → funding stress - Bid-ask widening in treasuries → market-maker withdrawal - Flash-crash frequency increasing → microstructure fragility

What to track: Forward EPS revisions, sector earnings breadth, multiple compression/expansion Where consensus breaks: Earnings estimates are always wrong — the question is by how much and in which direction RM lens on earnings: "I don't care if earnings beat by 5%. I care about the REVISION TREND. If forward estimates are being cut, the market is pricing hope. If our book has beta-1 to equities, every 1% earnings revision down = ~1.5% equity downside via multiple compression." ---

Your views should be held probabilistically , not dogmatically. Assign a confidence level and tie it to evidence quality: Confidence Evidence quality How to express Position sizing analog --- --- --- --- 90%+ Multiple confirming data, consistent across instruments "I'm highly confident that..." Full recommended hedge 70-90% Strong data but some ambiguity, conflicting signals exist "The balance of evidence suggests..." Partial hedge, contingency plan 50-70% Mixed signals, limited data, high uncertainty "I lean toward X but could easily be wrong..." Monitor, prepare but don't act < 50% Too many unknowns "I don't have a strong view — here's the framework..." No directional view, focus on risk bounds

When new data arrives: 1. What was your prior? (what you believed before) 2. What does the data say? (the signal) 3. How much should you update? (depends on signal quality vs prior confidence) Rules of thumb: - One data point rarely justifies a view change → "noisy signal" - Three consecutive data points in the same direction → trend → update - One outlier data point → investigate, don't react - Data that contradicts your highest-confidence view → take it seriously; that's the most informative signal Worked example: > Prior: 70% probability inflation is cooling (based on 3 months of declining core CPI) > New data: Core CPI MoM prints 0.5% (consensus was 0.3%) > Update: This is a 2σ miss. One month doesn't prove a trend, but it breaks the declining pattern. New estimate: 50% inflation cooling, 50% re-acceleration. > Portfolio action: "My cooling view was 70/30. After this print, it's closer to 50/50. The asymmetry has shifted — I'd recommend adding rate protection for the re-acceleration scenario. Cost: $150K in swaption premium." ---

Structure: "Three things on my radar today." 1. VIX TERM STRUCTURE — Backwardation for 3 consecutive days. Last time this persisted: pre-correction March 2023. Implication: Near-term vol priced above medium-term → market expects imminent catalyst. Our gamma exposure needs review. 2. CREDIT — CDX HY widened 25bp this week while equities are flat. Divergence means credit is seeing something equities aren't. If credit is right, equities catch down 3-5%. Watch for convergence. 3. POSITIONING — CFTC shows record net long in S&P futures. If we get a 2% sell-off, the unwind pressure multiplies the move. I'd recommend our systematic-exposed PMs tighten stops.

Structure: Lead with THEIR book, not your macro view. "Your two biggest risks today are A and B . Here's why: A connects to your XOM/CVX positions through mechanism . If scenario , P&L impact is -$X. B is your gamma profile — you're short $Y in gamma, and with CPI tomorrow, a 3σ print gives you a realized vol problem. My recommendation: specific hedge or position adjustment + cost ."

Structure: Regime → House exposure → Recommendation → Cost/benefit "We're in a regime description — characterized by 3 data points . The firm's aggregate exposure has X vulnerability via channels . I'm recommending specific firm-level action at a cost of $Y , which reduces tail VaR by Z% . Kill condition: I'd unwind this if specific data point or price level ."

Structure: Framework → Example → Portfolio connection "I think about market views through a risk lens rather than a directional one. Take current example : the market is pricing consensus , but the asymmetry is in direction . Specifically, quantified scenario comparison . For a book that's typical discretionary positioning , I'd recommend specific action because the cost of protection is small relative to the tail exposure." ---

PMs and interviewers will challenge you. They're testing if you crumble, get defensive, or hold your ground with data. Challenge 1: "You're being too bearish" > Bad response: "No, I'm right, look at the data." > Good response: "I might be. The base case is constructive and I agree with that. My concern is specific: the tail scenario carries 3x the P&L impact of the base case, and it's only 30% priced. I'm not saying we de-risk — I'm saying we should understand what we lose if consensus is wrong, and whether the cost of hedging that tail is worth paying." Challenge 2: "VIX is already elevated, protection is expensive" > Good response: "VIX at 24 implies daily moves of ±1.5%, but we've realized ±2.5% three times this week. Implied is actually CHEAP relative to what's happening. And if you're worried about cost, we can structure a put spread — gives us downside coverage for about half the price of a naked put. The marginal cost vs the tail protection is favorable." Challenge 3: "But the fundamental thesis is intact" > Good response: "Your thesis may be 100% right on direction and still lose money due to path. If vol stays at 30, your stop-loss gets triggered before your thesis plays out. Sizing is a risk management input, not a conviction input. I'd suggest reducing size to survive the vol, keeping the thesis." Challenge 4: "Why should I listen to a risk manager about the market?" > Good response: "You shouldn't listen to me about direction — that's your edge. Listen to me about the DISTRIBUTION of outcomes. I see the aggregate book every day. I know where our exposures are correlated across PMs. I know when positioning is crowded. And I know what the worst day looks like. That's information you don't have in isolation." Challenge 5: "I've seen this pattern before, it always resolves" > Good response: "Survivorship bias. We remember the times it resolved because those are boring. We forget the times it didn't because the people who stayed levered into those aren't around to tell the story. What's the cost of being protected if you're right? It's small. What's the cost of being unprotected if you're wrong?"

1. Acknowledge first. "You might be right that..." → then pivot to asymmetry. 2. Data, not emotion. Numbers, scenarios, historical precedent — never "I feel like..." 3. Frame as risk geometry, not direction. "I'm not saying it goes down. I'm saying the down scenario isn't priced." 4. Always offer solutions. The view without a recommendation is just complaining. 5. Know when to step back. PM heard you, accepts the risk, it's within budget → document and move on. You did your job. ---

section: Trap 1: Anchoring to Your First View
content: What happens: You form a view at 7am. Data changes by 10am. You ignore the new data. Fix: Set specific data thresholds that trigger view updates (kill conditions). Review your view after every material data point. "Was I right for the right reasons?"

What happens: You build a compelling story and start fitting all new data into it, even contradictory data. Fix: For every view, write down in advance what data would DISPROVE it. If that data arrives, update.

What happens: You make your view so nuanced that it can never be wrong because it covers every scenario. Fix: Force yourself to state your view in ONE sentence with a specific number. "I think realized vol will exceed implied by at least 30% over the next two weeks." Measurable. Falsifiable.

What happens: You automatically assume the market is wrong because you want to look contrarian. Fix: Consensus is right most of the time. The value is in identifying the SPECIFIC SPOTS where it's wrong. Start from "the market is probably right" and work from there.
What happens: You flag 15 risks every morning. None are actionable. PMs tune you out. Fix: Flag 1-2 things that ACTUALLY MATTER today. Specificity beats comprehensiveness.

section: Trap 6: Overconfidence After a Good Call
content: What happens: You predicted the last sell-off correctly. Now you think you can predict markets. Fix: You didn't predict it — you identified the asymmetry. Stay humble. Risk management isn't about being right; it's about being prepared. ---

section: Building Your Mental Model (Not Just Reading News)
content: The goal isn't to know everything. It's to maintain a coherent mental model of market regimes, narratives, and pivot points that you update continuously. Tier 1: Daily Must-Read (10-15 min) Source Why What to extract --- --- --- Bloomberg TOP/FIRST Market-moving overnight headlines Any new information that changes your prior Fed funds futures (CME FedWatch) Rate expectations Any shift > 5% in meeting probabilities VIX + term structure + skew Market fear gauge Regime context for your book Credit spreads (CDX IG/HY) Early warning system Divergence from equities = canary in coal mine Your firm's risk report YOUR book, today Concentration, P&L, limit utilization Tier 2: Daily Value-Add (15-20 min) Source Why --- --- FT Alphaville Contrarian analysis, often ahead of consensus Matt Levine (Money Stuff) Market structure, plumbing, regulatory risk WSJ Markets / Heard on the Street Mainstream narrative — know what consensus IS Sell-side research (GS, JPM, MS top picks) Know what the street is recommending Tier 3: Weekly Deep (30-60 min weekly) Source Why --- --- Fed minutes / CB communications Forward guidance shifts BIS quarterly review Systemic risk, cross-border flows CFTC Commitments of Traders Positioning data Prime broker positioning reports Hedge fund crowding Tier 4: As-Needed Source When --- --- CPI/PPI/NFP release details Data day, within 30 min of release FOMC statement + presser Meeting day, live or within 1hr Earnings (for book-relevant names) Specific PM exposure Geopolitical analysis (IISS, Stratfor) When geo event affects book

section: How To Read (Not What To Read)
content: 1. Read for SURPRISES, not confirmation. The most valuable information is what contradicts your prior. 2. Extract the NUMBER, not the headline. Headline: "Inflation rising." Your extraction: "Core CPI MoM 0.42% vs 0.30% expected = 4σ miss." 3. Ask: "Who cares?" For every piece of information: which PM in our book is affected? If no one, move on. 4. Maintain a running "What changed?" list. By end of day, you should be able to say: "Three things changed today: A , B , C . Here's what it means for our book." ---

Before you open Bloomberg, answer from MEMORY: 1. What's the biggest risk to the book TODAY? - Must be specific: "CPI print in 4 hours could show re-acceleration, and we're net long 5Y duration" - Not vague: "markets are risky" 2. Is the market pricing it? - Check: VIX, skew, relevant futures, credit spreads - If YES → no edge in protection, but validate our sizing - If NO → asymmetry exists → proceed to recommendation 3. What would I recommend IF it materializes? - Must be specific: "Reduce duration by $10M DV01, buy 1-month 25Δ SPX puts" - Not vague: "we should de-risk"

Keep a running log. Accountability mechanism + pattern recognition: Date View Confidence Kill condition Outcome What I learned --- --- --- --- --- --- Apr 7 RV > IV for 2 more weeks 75% VIX term structure normalizes (contango > 5%) Pending - Apr 3 HY-IG spread ratio signals credit stress 60% CDX HY back below 400bp Wrong — HY tightened Spread widening was technical (ETF rebalance), not fundamental Mar 28 Oil backwardation deepens through April 80% EIA inventory build > 5M bbl Right Geo premium persists longer than consensus expects Why this matters in interviews: "I keep a view journal. It forces me to be specific, accountable, and honest about when I'm wrong. Over the last month, I've been right about X because evidence , wrong about Y because reason I missed . The mistake taught me specific lesson ."

Having a view is important. Knowing when NOT to express one is equally important. Stay silent when: - You're less informed than the PM on their specific sector - The risk is within normal operating parameters and they clearly see it - You just flagged this yesterday and nothing has changed - The meeting already has 5 agenda items and yours is 7 in importance Speak up when: - You see cross-PM correlation risk that no individual PM can see - The risk has migrated from "within tolerance" to "approaching threshold" - New data materially changes the asymmetry - The PM is in a behavioral pattern you've seen before (adding to losers, anchoring) ---

section: What To Do When You Genuinely Don't Know
content: The most honest and dangerous answer in risk management is: "I don't know." Make it useful: The Uncertainty Template: > "I don't have a strong view on X . Here's what I do know: > - The range of outcomes is wide/narrow > - The market is pricing consensus > - Our book's exposure to the tails is quantified > - Regardless of direction, the risk management action is specific " Example: > "I don't know if the Fed cuts or holds. The market is pricing 55/45. What I do know: > - If they hold, our duration position loses $3M. If they cut, it gains $1M. > - The asymmetry says we should have less duration going in, regardless of view. > - Cost of reducing duration exposure: ~$50K in transaction costs. > - The risk-adjusted case for protection is clear even without a directional opinion."

section: Scenario-Based Thinking as an Alternative to Conviction
content: When you can't form a view, form a decision tree : Market / \ Cut (55%) Hold (45%) / \ / \ + Dovish Neutral + Hawkish Neutral guidance guidance (30%) (25%) (25%) (20%) Eq +3% Eq +1% Eq -5% Eq -2% Expected = 0.30(+3) + 0.25(+1) + 0.25(-5) + 0.20(-2) = 0.90 + 0.25 - 1.25 - 0.40 = -0.50% → Expected equity return is NEGATIVE despite the cut being more likely. → This justifies protective positioning. ---

section: Q: "What's a market risk you'd be watching right now?"
content: > "The risk I find most interesting right now is the divergence between credit and equity signals. CDX High Yield has widened about 60 basis points over the past three weeks while the S&P is roughly flat. Historically, when credit leads equities by more than 2 weeks, equities tend to converge to credit's signal about 70% of the time. > > For a discretionary equity book, this means one of two things: either credit is seeing a real fundamental deterioration — in which case equities will catch down 5-8% — or credit is mispricing idiosyncratic stress as systematic. The data I'd watch to distinguish: if the widening is concentrated in CCC/B names and not BB, it's likely idiosyncratic and less contagious. > > My recommendation to a PM would be: reduce net long exposure by 10-15% until the divergence resolves, and buy 1-month SPX put spreads. Cost is modest relative to the insurance value. If credit tightens back and I'm wrong, we gave up a few basis points. If credit is right, we're protected."

section: Q: "How do you form a market view differently from a trader?"
content: > "A trader needs to be right directionally — they're betting capital on 'this goes up.' My job is different. I need to identify where the DISTRIBUTION of outcomes is mispriced, especially in the tails. > > My process has six steps: I start by mapping what the market is pricing across instruments — not just equities but rates, credit, vol, and FX because they often disagree. Then I identify where consensus could break, quantify the asymmetry of outcomes, map it to our specific portfolio exposures, formulate a recommendation with cost-benefit, and set a kill condition for when I'd change my mind. > > For example, right now the VIX term structure is in backwardation for the third straight day while realized vol is running 60% above implied. That tells me the market expects near-term trouble but thinks it's short-lived. But historically, sustained backwardation precedes corrections. My view isn't 'markets crash' — it's 'the distribution has a fatter left tail than the market is pricing, and at current vol levels, protection is actually cheap relative to what's being realized.'"

section: Q: "Give me an example of when you'd push back on a PM vs. when you'd stay silent?"
content: > "I'd push back when I see something the PM can't see from their individual book — specifically, cross-PM correlation risk. > > If three PMs all independently added energy exposure this week, each position might look fine in isolation. But in aggregate, the house is now 2x our sector concentration limit in energy, and all three positions lose in the same scenario: oil reversal. That's invisible to any individual PM and it's my job to surface it. I'd go to each PM with the data: 'Your position alone is fine. But the house is concentrated. We need one of you to hedge or reduce by $X to stay within aggregate limits.' > > I'd stay silent when a PM has a position I personally disagree with directionally — say they're long a stock I think is overvalued — but the position is within risk limits, properly sized, and they clearly have a thesis. That's not my call. My job is risk geometry, not stock-picking. I'd document that I reviewed it, note the key risk factors, and move on." --- Related files: → P Macro Fundamentals — the WHY behind markets → T Scenario Analysis — turning views into structured scenario exercises → R Practical Risk Judgment — daily workflows and communication frameworks

When Brent jumps 8% overnight, your PM doesn't want a paragraph — they want ONE PAGE telling them what happened, what it means for the book, and what you recommend. When the CRO asks "what's our worst-case loss from a Hormuz closure?", you need a number, not a shrug. Scenario analysis is the core deliverable of a risk manager. It's where all your knowledge — Greeks, correlations, macro, judgment — converges into something the business can actually use. At Squarepoint, this is especially critical: discretionary PMs make judgment calls in real time. The RM who can model the scenario, quantify the impact, and present an actionable recommendation in 15 minutes is the one who earns a seat at the decision table. ---
Every scenario analysis follows this exact sequence. Memorize it — it's the same whether you're handling an overnight oil shock or a weekly stress-test refresh.

section: Step 1: FACTS — What Actually Happened? (2 minutes)
content: Rules: - Headlines lie. Check the NUMBERS: magnitude, percentile, timing (gap or grind), volume. - Context the move: is it a 1σ, 2σ, or 3σ event? Use trailing realized vol to calibrate. - Map the co-movements: what else moved? If oil is up 8% and gold is flat, the market doesn't believe it's a true supply shock yet. If oil up 8% AND gold up 3% AND USD up 1%, the fear is real. Fact-gathering checklist: Check Why --- --- Asset price move: $ and % Magnitude Percentile rank (trailing 1Y) Historical context (>95th = extreme) Gap or grind? Gap = information shock; grind = positioning unwind Volume vs average High vol = conviction; low vol = illiquid overshoot Co-movements (6 assets) Rates, FX, credit, vol, commodities, equities News catalyst Is there a specific trigger or is this technical? Time of day / market session London vs NY vs Asia → liquidity context Calibrating the sigma: $$\sigma {\text{daily}} = \frac{\text{Annualized RV}}{\sqrt{252}}$$ > If Brent trailing 30-day RV is 35% annualized → $\sigma {\text{daily}} = 35/15.87 = 2.2\%$. An 8% move = 3.6σ event. This happens once every ~6,000 trading days. It's NOT normal. React accordingly.

section: Step 2: EXPOSURE MAPPING — Who Gets Hurt? (3 minutes)
content: Direct exposure: Positions in the asset that moved. Straightforward: check notional, delta, mark-to-market. Indirect exposure (where the value-add is): Channel How it transmits How to measure --- --- --- Beta/correlation Oil up → equity indices down (growth concern) Portfolio equity beta × index move Supply chain Oil up → airlines, trucking, chemicals crushed Sector beta to oil (historical regression) Inflation channel Oil up → CPI expectations up → rates up → duration losses DV01 × implied yield change FX channel Oil up → oil-importing EM currencies weaken FX exposure in EM positions Volatility channel Oil shock → VIX spikes → short gamma/vega positions lose Vega × IV change + gamma P&L Margin channel Higher vol → higher margin requirements → forced selling Margin utilization post-shock Correlation channel Stress → all correlations → 1 → diversification benefit vanishes Stressed correlation VaR vs normal Exposure mapping template: DIRECT: Position Notional Delta P&L from move Greeks impact INDIRECT: Equity beta channel → est. P&L: -$X Duration channel → est. P&L: -$Y Volatility channel → est. P&L: -$Z Margin impact → utilization goes from A% to B% TOTAL ESTIMATED P&L: -$W LIMIT PROXIMITY: X% of drawdown limit consumed

section: Step 3: SCENARIO CONSTRUCTION — What Could Happen Next? (5 minutes)
content: Always build exactly 3 scenarios: Scenario Probability Purpose --- --- --- Base case 50-60% Most likely path — consensus outcome Adverse 25-35% Plausible bad outcome — the one we need to prepare for Extreme 5-15% Tail scenario — the one that threatens the fund 6 elements every scenario must include: 1. Trigger — what specifically causes this path 2. Macro transmission — how it moves through the economy 3. Cross-asset impact — specific price levels for rates, FX, equity, credit, commodities 4. Time horizon — does this play out in days, weeks, or months 5. Magnitude — specific numbers, not "big move" 6. Correlation assumption — do normal diversification benefits hold or break? Critical: reverse-engineer the extreme scenario from the limit. If the fund's hard drawdown limit is 5% of NAV, work backwards: "What market move wipes out 5% of NAV?" That's your extreme scenario. Then ask: has that magnitude of move happened historically? If yes — it's not a tail, it's a plausible adverse outcome.

section: Step 4: QUANTIFICATION — Put Numbers On It (5 minutes)
content: For each scenario, compute: Metric How --- --- Portfolio P&L (delta) Σ(position delta × scenario move) Gamma P&L adjustment For each option: $\frac{1}{2}\Gamma S^2 (\Delta\%)^2$ Vega P&L Σ(vega × IV change in scenario) Correlation P&L Re-run VaR with stressed correlation matrix Margin requirement change Estimate using vol-scaled initial margin Liquidity cost If forced to exit: Σ(position / (ADV × participation rate)) × impact model Worked example — Oil +$15 scenario on a $500M multi-PM book: Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- Energy longs ($30M notional) +$15 × delta sensitivity +$4.5M Equity beta drag ($200M net long) -3% × $200M -$6.0M Duration (DV01 = $50K/bp, +20bp) -$50K × 20 -$1.0M Short gamma on SPX puts ½ × Γ × S² × (3%)² -$0.8M Vega loss (long vol book, but wrong spot) Mixed -$0.3M NET -$3.6M As % of NAV $3.6M / $500M -0.72% Drawdown limit consumed If 3% limit, 0.72/3.0 24%

section: Step 5: SENSITIVITY TESTING — What Breaks First? (3 minutes)
content: Don't only run the point scenario. Test the sensitivity grid: Oil shock sensitivity matrix: Oil move Equity reaction Rate reaction Portfolio P&L Limit % consumed --- --- --- --- --- +$5 (4%) -1.0% +5bp -$0.9M 6% +$10 (8%) -2.5% +12bp -$2.1M 14% +$15 (13%) -3.5% +20bp -$3.6M 24% +$20 (17%) -5.0% +30bp -$5.8M 39% +$30 (26%) -8.0% +40bp -$9.2M 61% +$40 (35%) -12.0% +50bp, curve inverts -$14.1M 94% ⚠️ Breakpoints: The oil move that breaches 50% of the drawdown limit ($7.5M loss) is approximately +$22, or ~Brent $133. That's the action trigger — the price level where you MUST have a response plan ready.

Format — never deviate: ═══════════════════════════════════════════ RISK SCENARIO BRIEF — TITLE — DATE/TIME ═══════════════════════════════════════════ SITUATION: 2 sentences max. What happened. Magnitude + percentile. CURRENT P&L IMPACT: $ and % of NAV SCENARIOS: BASE (55%): 1 sentence. Expected P&L: $X ADVERSE (30%): 1 sentence. Expected P&L: -$Y EXTREME (15%): 1 sentence. Expected P&L: -$Z KEY RISK: 1 sentence. The single biggest vulnerability. BREAKPOINT: The price level that breaches 50% drawdown limit RECOMMENDATION: Option A: action — VaR impact: -X%, cost: $K Option B: action — VaR impact: -Y%, cost: $K → I recommend A/B because 1 sentence justification KILL CONDITION: Specific data/price that would make me change this ═══════════════════════════════════════════

Scenarios aren't static. Set specific review triggers: - Time-based: "Refreshing this analysis in 24 hours regardless" - Price-based: "If Brent crosses $125, I escalate from base to adverse" - Data-based: "After CPI print, I'll update the rate channel estimate" ---

Facts: Brent $111 → $120. 3.6σ move. Gold +2%, SPX futures -2.1%, 10Y +8bp. Volume 3x normal. Exposure map: - Direct oil longs: +$X (mark-to-market gain, but DON'T celebrate — correlations will hurt) - Equity book: -$Y via beta (SPX beta of 0.8 × -2.1% × equity notional) - Duration: -$Z (DV01 × 8bp) - Short puts: -$W (gamma acceleration as SPX falls) - FX: EM importers (INR, TRY) weaken — any EM exposure adds to loss Three scenarios: Oil SPX 10Y Credit P&L Action --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Base (50%) : Diplomatic de-escalation $112-115 Recovers flat -3bp Unchanged -$0.5M Monitor Adverse (35%) : Further restriction, OPEC can't offset $125-135 -4% +20bp IG +15bp -$5.2M Trim equity, add put protection Extreme (15%) : Full blockade, 20% global supply at risk $140-160 -10%+ +40bp then reversal (deflation fear) IG +50bp, HY +200bp -$14M+ Emergency: de-risk, IC call, margin plan Recommendation: "Lock in partial profit on energy longs. Buy 1-month SPX 95% put spreads. Cost: $250K. Reduces adverse P&L from -$5.2M to -$3.1M."

section: Playbook 2: PM Down 15% in a Week
content: Facts: PM Alpha's book: -15% WTD. MTD now -12% (was +3%). Attribution: 70% from single-name event (TSLA position), 30% from delta acceleration. Diagnostic questions (Socratic, not accusatory): 1. "Walk me through the thesis as it stands today — what's changed vs entry?" 2. "If you had no position, would you enter at these levels?" 3. "What's your catalyst timeline? Is there a definitive date?" 4. "Where's your stop? Has it moved since inception?" 5. "What position size would let you sleep tonight?" Decision tree: Step 1: Hard limit breached? ├─ YES → Mandatory reduction to IC. No discretion. └─ NO → Step 2: Soft limit zone (>80%)? ├─ YES → Formal PM discussion. Document thesis. │ Set 48-hour review date. No new risk. └─ NO → Step 3: Thesis intact? Catalyst valid? ├─ YES → Monitor daily. Tighten stop. │ Flag behavioral red flags (Part 5). └─ NO → Recommend reduction. Present alternatives. Escalate if refused. 3 scenarios: Outcome P&L trajectory RM action --- --- --- --- Recovery (30%) Thesis plays out, TSLA rebounds -15% → -5% over 2 weeks Document vindication. Review stop discipline. Stagnation (45%) No catalyst, position drifts -15% → -18% (theta + opportunity cost) "Dead capital. Opportunity cost = $X/week in theta. Recommend trim 50%." Deterioration (25%) Thesis wrong, further decline -15% → -22%, approaching hard limit "3 points from hard limit. Mandatory discussion now. Options: cut 50% or hedge with puts." Communication: "PM Alpha is -15% WTD, -12% MTD. 70% attributable to TSLA. We're at 75% of the soft drawdown limit with $1.5M of cushion. Three scenarios show expected value of -$800K from here. I recommend reducing TSLA by 40% and the remainder should have a stop at level . Meeting request: tomorrow 8AM."

Facts: Target portfolio vol 12%. Realized trailing 10-day: 15.6%. Delta: +30% above target. Decomposition framework (this is the skill): Source Contribution Actionable? --- --- --- Market vol increase (VIX 16 → 24, beta-1 book) ~50% of excess (explains 1.8pp of the 3.6pp gap) Reduce beta if desired Concentration (top position 12% of NAV, running 50% vol) ~25% of excess Trim or hedge the name Correlation spike (pairwise ρ from 0.3 → 0.6) ~20% of excess Structural — reduce overlapping exposures Leverage (notional/NAV increased) ~5% of excess Delever Specific response by source: If 50% market-driven: "Half is market vol — can't hedge the market being volatile. Options: reduce beta to 0.7 (costs $X in expected return) or accept and tighten stops." If 25% concentration: "XOM is contributing 25% of excess vol. Trimming by 20% reduces portfolio vol from 15.6% to 14.2%. Cost: $50K in transaction costs." If 20% correlation: "Your equity and credit books are now ρ = 0.6, up from 0.3 last month. Diversification benefit has collapsed. Either reduce one leg or find a genuinely uncorrelated hedge." Recommendation format: "Vol is 30% above target. Decomposition: 50% market, 25% concentration, 20% correlation, 5% leverage. To return to target: (A) trim XOM 20% + reduce credit to half → vol drops to 12.8%, or (B) accept elevated vol with IC sign-off and tighter stops. I recommend (A)."

Facts: Market priced 55% cut. Fed holds + hawkish dots. SPX -2%, 2Y +15bp, 10Y +10bp, DXY +0.8%, gold -1.5%. Transmission channels: 1. Rates: Duration longs lose immediately. DV01 × bp move = direct hit. 2. Equities: Growth/tech stocks hit hardest (long-duration equity). Value relatively outperforms. 3. Credit: If "higher for longer," leveraged companies face refinancing pressure. HY spreads widen 10-20bp. 4. FX: USD strengthens → any book with EM or short-USD positioning loses. 5. Vol: VIX jumps 3-5 points. Short gamma/vega positions compound equity losses. Scenarios: Timeline Magnitude P&L Action --- --- --- --- --- Base (45%) : Orderly repricing 1-2 weeks SPX -3%, 10Y +15bp total -$2.5M Monitor, no panic Adverse (35%) : Hawkish repricing cascade 2-4 weeks SPX -6%, 10Y +30bp, credit widens -$5.5M Reduce duration + equity, add tail hedge Extreme (20%) : Stagflation narrative 1-3 months SPX -12%, 10Y +50bp, HY +150bp -$12M Full de-risk. Emergency IC. Review all PM allocations.

section: Playbook 5: Correlation Spike — All PMs Down Simultaneously
content: Facts: 4 out of 5 PMs down on same day. "Diversified" books all bleeding. Aggregate loss: -$8M (-1.6% NAV). THIS IS THE MOST DANGEROUS SCENARIO. Each PM thinks they have an idiosyncratic loss. The aggregate is a systemic exposure nobody authorized. Diagnostic: 1. Factor decomposition: regress each PM's daily P&L against {SPX, Oil, 10Y, DXY, HY CDX} 2. Find the common loading: "All 4 PMs have effective oil beta between 0.3 and 0.8" 3. Aggregate: "Collectively, we have $400M effective oil exposure across PMs who each think they have $80M of 'independent' risk" 4. The REAL portfolio VaR is 2-3x what the sum-of-parts suggests Communication: "Four PMs down today totaling -$8M. Factor decomposition shows the common driver is effective oil exposure: aggregate $400M vs the $150M that shows up in position-level reports. Diversification assumptions are overstating risk-adjusted capacity by 2.5x. I recommend an emergency aggregation review with the CRO. Short-term fix: reduce the book with the highest marginal oil sensitivity by 30%."

Facts: Overnight margin recalculation. Broker requires additional $15M by 2PM. Current excess margin: $5M. Shortfall: $10M. Triage framework (90 minutes to execute): Step Time Action --- --- --- 1. Verify T+0 to T+10min Confirm the call with prime broker. Check for errors. 2. Quantify T+10 to T+20min How much to raise? Rank positions by: (margin consumed per $ notional) ÷ (conviction + liquidity) 3. Triage T+20 to T+40min Identify cheapest $10M to liquidate: low-conviction positions, liquid names, pairs where only one leg has margin efficiency 4. Simulate T+40 to T+60min Run post-liquidation P&L and risk. Make sure cutting doesn't create new risks (e.g., unbalancing a pair) 5. Approve T+60 to T+75min PM sign-off on liquidation order. IC if needed. 6. Execute T+75 to T+90min Execute in order of liquidity. Monitor fills. 7. Document T+90 to T+120min Full record: what was cut, why, P&L impact, what would prevent recurrence Liquidation priority matrix: Priority Description Liquidate? --- --- --- 1st Low conviction + high margin + liquid YES — first to go 2nd Medium conviction + high margin YES — reluctantly 3rd High conviction + low margin NO — protect these 4th Hedges LAST — removing hedges increases risk

Facts: XYZ Corp downgraded from BBB- to BB+ (fallen angel). $20B of bonds must be sold by IG mandates. Stock -15%. CDS widens 200bp. Transmission: - Direct: any XYZ exposure in bond or equity books - Forced selling: IG-only funds must liquidate → price overshoots fundamentals - Index rebalancing: XYZ exits IG indices, enters HY indices → mechanical selling/buying flows - Contagion: same-sector names trade down in sympathy; sector CDS widens - Correlation: if XYZ is large enough, CDX IG composition changes → hedges misalign Opportunity vs risk: For a discretionary book, fallen angels create opportunities once the forced selling is exhausted. The RM role: ensure the PM doesn't catch the falling knife during the forced-selling window (48-72 hours) but has dry powder for the stabilization.

Facts: SPX drops 4% in 12 minutes. VIX spikes from 22 to 38. Bid-ask spreads in single stocks widen 5x. Market-maker quotes disappear. Key RM actions: 1. DO NOT trade unless forced. Liquidity is gone — any market order will get murdered on spread. 2. Check for resting stop-loss orders that might execute at terrible prices → cancel or widen. 3. Monitor margin: the 4% move may trigger intraday margin calls. 4. Assess: is this mechanical (CTA stop-outs, gamma hedging cascade) or fundamental (real news)? 5. If mechanical: the bounce is coming (usually within 30-60 minutes). Protect, don't panic-sell. 6. If fundamental: this is the opening act, not the show. After the event (within 2 hours): - Full P&L attribution - Which stops were triggered? At what levels vs. intended? - Post-mortem: were our limit orders in illiquid names? Fix going forward.

Facts: AAPL misses revenue by 8% and guides down 15% for next quarter. Stock -12% after-hours. Analysis framework: 1. Direct P&L: How much AAPL in the book? Delta × move = immediate hit. 2. Second-order: AAPL drags tech sector. Other holdings: MSFT, GOOG, NVDA — correlation drag. 3. Basis risk: If hedging AAPL exposure with QQQ puts, the hedge may not cover the single-stock move (tracking error). 4. Implied vol: AAPL IV was 35% into earnings, realized move is ~4σ relative to daily vol. IV will reprice for the sector. 5. Alpha vs beta: Decompose: how much of the loss is AAPL-specific (alpha loss) vs sector drag (beta loss)? The PM should only be accountable for alpha. If 80% is idiosyncratic alpha loss, it's a thesis failure. If 80% is sector beta, it's a market event.

Facts: Saturday night: major geopolitical event . Markets open in 14 hours. Pre-open preparation (the golden hours): Phase Time (hours to open) Action --- --- --- Assessment H-14 to H-12 Gather facts. Historical analogs. Map transmission channels. Scenario build H-12 to H-10 3 scenarios with specific price targets. Exposure check H-10 to H-8 Pull current book. Map to scenario P&L for each scenario. Pre-write H-8 to H-6 Draft the 1-pager. Fill in as much as possible. PM coordination H-6 to H-4 Email PMs: "Here's what I see. Here are scenarios. Let's discuss pre-market." Pre-market H-2 to H-0 Finalize based on futures/overseas markets. Deliver final 1-pager. Open H-0 Real-time monitoring. Update scenario probabilities based on price action. ---

Instead of "what happens if oil moves X" → ask " what scenario causes us to lose Y%? " Process: 1. Set the loss threshold (e.g., 5% NAV = $25M) 2. Identify the portfolio's factor sensitivities 3. Work backwards: what combination of moves produces -$25M? 4. Ask: how plausible is that combination? Has it happened historically? Example: > $25M loss requires: SPX -7% AND oil +20% AND credit widening 50bp simultaneously. > Historical precedent: October 2018 (SPX -7%, oil -11%, credit +40bp) — partial match. > Assessment: The exact combination is rare but the individual components have hit these levels 3x in 5 years. Under stress, they're CORRELATED, making the joint probability higher than independence suggests.

"If X happens, what's the probability of Y?" These are the scenarios that catch funds by surprise: If... Then probability of... Consequence --- --- --- VIX > 30 Correlation > 0.7 across equities 85% → diversification fails Oil > $130 Core CPI MoM > 0.4% within 3 months 65% → Fed can't cut 2s10s inverts > 50bp Recession within 12 months 60% (historical base rate) HY-IG spread > 400bp At least one large LBO restructuring 50% SPX drawdown > 10% Fed verbal intervention 90% (the "Fed put" conditional)

Single-period scenarios miss the PATH. A PM can survive a 5% drop but not five 1% drops with margin calls each day. Path-dependent scenario template: Day Event Market Portfolio P&L Cumulative Margin --- --- --- --- --- --- 1 Oil +4% SPX -1.5% -$1.2M -$1.2M 72% 2 Further escalation SPX -2.0% -$1.8M -$3.0M 79% 3 Margin call triggered Forced selling at bad levels -$2.5M -$5.5M 85% 4 Contagion to credit HY widens 50bp, IG +20bp -$1.5M -$7.0M 88% 5 Capitulation selling SPX -3% on volume -$3.0M -$10.0M 94% ⚠️ Key insight: The cumulative loss from a 5-day grind can be WORSE than a single 8% crash, because margin calls force liquidation at exactly the wrong time, locking in losses and removing the possibility of recovery. Path-dependent scenarios reveal this — point-in-time scenarios miss it. ---

Real crises involve MULTIPLE factors moving simultaneously. The 2022 drawdowns weren't just "rates up" — they were rates up + equities down + correlation flip + liquidity dry-up + margin pressure. You need to model the JOINT distribution.

For each pair of risk factors, estimate the conditional co-movement: Primary shock Equities Rates Credit Vol Oil --- --- --- --- --- --- Equities -5% — -10bp (flight to safety, usually) +30bp HY +5 VIX points -3% (demand fear) Rates +30bp -3% (duration drag) — +15bp HY +2 VIX Flat Credit HY +100bp -5% (contagion) -15bp (flight to safety) — +6 VIX -5% (demand collapse) VIX +10 points -4% (mechanical) -10bp (flight) +20bp HY — -3% Oil +$20 -4% (inflation fear) +15bp (inflation) +10bp HY +4 VIX — How to use: When oil shocks, don't just model the oil impact. Use the row to estimate the JOINT scenario: oil +$20 → equity -4% → rates +15bp → credit +10bp → VIX +4. THEN compute portfolio P&L across ALL channels simultaneously.

The same shock has different impacts depending on the regime: Shock In "risk-on" regime In "risk-off" regime --- --- --- SPX -3% Normal pullback, dip-buyers arrive, recovers in 2-3 days Acceleration: SPX -3% → -5% → -8% as stops cascade VIX +5 pts Vol sellers step in, mean-reversion trade works Vol sellers blown out, gamma hedging amplifies Credit +30bp Contained to single name, no contagion Systematic widening, fallen angel fears Oil +$10 "Growth strong enough to absorb," equity resilient Stagflation narrative → equity -5% additional RM implication: Always state your regime assumption in the scenario. "This analysis assumes we remain in the current elevated-vol regime. If we shift to full risk-off, multiply the adverse scenario by 1.5x." ---

section: How PMs Distort Scenarios (and How to Counter)
content: PM behavior What they'll say What it really means Your counter --- --- --- --- Anchoring "This isn't like 2020, it's not that bad" Comparing to the worst case makes anything look mild "Let's compare to the P90 outcome, not the P99. Our adverse scenario is a 1-in-10 event." Optimism bias "It'll come back" Refusing to accept the thesis is broken "What's your catalyst and timeline? If we hit price , do you cut?" Sunk cost "I'm already down 10%, no point cutting now" Losses feel like wasted money if realized "The market doesn't know your entry price. The question is: would you put this on TODAY?" Denominator neglect "30% tail probability isn't that high" 30% = happens roughly once every 3 attempts "Flip two coins. Heads-heads is 25%. Would you bet your career on heads-heads NOT happening?" Narrative fallacy "My scenario makes more sense than yours" Confusing storytelling quality with probability "Let's skip the narratives. Here are the numbers: P&L in base vs tail. The math speaks."

section: Red Flags That the Scenario Analysis Itself Is Flawed
content: Signal Problem Fix --- --- --- "No scenario shows a loss > 2%" You're being too conservative with shock magnitudes Use historical worst-case: "What was the worst week for oil in the last 20 years?" Use that. "Base case has 80% probability" Overconfident in modal outcome Check: are you really 80% sure? Or are you allocating 80% because it's comfortable? "We're hedged so the extreme scenario is fine" Hedge may fail under stress (correlation, liquidity) Stress the HEDGE too. "What if the put is illiquid and you can't exercise at fair value?" "Historical correlations show diversification benefit" Using calm-period correlations for stress scenarios Use CRISIS correlations (all ρ → 0.8+) for adverse/extreme scenarios Only running scenarios that confirm the house view Confirmation bias in scenario design Deliberately include the scenario that the firm is MOST exposed to, even if it feels unlikely ---

section: Using History as a Calibration Tool
content: You don't invent scenarios from scratch. You calibrate against actual events: Event Date Key moves Duration Why it's relevant --- --- --- --- --- COVID crash Feb-Mar 2020 SPX -34%, VIX 82, IG +150bp, oil -65% 5 weeks to bottom Speed + magnitude. Everything correlated. Margin cascades. Rate tantrum 2022 Jan-Oct 2022 SPX -25%, 10Y +250bp, 60/40 worst year ever 10 months Stock-bond correlation flip. Long-duration equity destroyed. GFC credit Sep 2008 SPX -50%, HY +1000bp, Lehman → counterparty chain 18 months Counterparty contagion. Liquidity = zero. LTCM unwind Aug 1998 Spread +400bp, arb blowup, vol 45% 3 months Crowded quant trade unwind. Basis blow-out. Oil crash 2014-16 Brent -75%, HY energy defaults 10%+ 18 months Supply shock → credit → contagion to non-energy Aug 2024 carry unwind Aug 5, 2024 Nikkei -12%, VIX 65 (intraday), JPY +7% 3 days Carry-funded leverage unwinding. Correlation = 1. UK gilt crisis Sep 2022 30Y gilt +150bp in 3 days, GBP -8% 2 weeks LDI margin spiral. Forced selling → more margin → more selling. Volmageddon Feb 5, 2018 VIX +115% in 1 day, XIV wiped out 1 day Short vol products amplify → feedback loop → destruction Flash crash May 6, 2010 SPX -9% intraday then recovered 36 minutes Liquidity evaporation. Market structure risk. Stops executing at -10%. How to reference in interviews: "When I design the extreme scenario for an oil supply shock, I calibrate against the 2014-16 experience where Brent fell 75% over 18 months and HY energy defaults exceeded 10%. Applied conversely to an upside oil shock, the magnitude and duration suggest our extreme scenario should include sustained $140+ oil and credit contagion to the broader market over months, not days." ---

Audience Time What they need Format --- --- --- --- PM 2-3 min "What does this mean for MY book?" 1-pager: P&L, top risk, recommendation CRO / Risk Committee 5-10 min "What's the firm-level exposure?" Aggregated view: total P&L, limit proximity, cross-PM correlation IC / Board 10-15 min "Should we change anything structurally?" Regime assessment, capital allocation implications, strategic hedging

Memorize this structure: > "Three things you need to know about event : > One — your direct P&L from this move is ±$X . That puts you at Y% of your drawdown limit. > Two — the bigger risk isn't today, it's the adverse scenario where Z happens . That takes you to -$W , which is V% of your hard limit. > Three — I recommend specific action . It costs $K and reduces your tail exposure by N% . Alternative: brief option B . Your call."

Mistake Why it's wrong Fix --- --- --- 3-page report PM stops reading after page 1 One page. No exceptions. Base case probability = 80% Feels reassuring but puts you to sleep If truly 80%, the scenario brief isn't urgent enough to write "We should consider hedging" Vague, unactionable "Buy 1-month SPX 5800 puts. Cost: $180K. Payoff in extreme: $2.1M." Not including the cost of inaction PM thinks doing nothing is free "If we don't hedge and the adverse scenario plays out, P&L impact is -$5.2M. The hedge costs $200K. That's 26:1 risk-reward." Missing the cross-asset impact Only looking at the obvious channel Always include 2nd-order: "Oil shock affects your equity book too via beta"

After writing any scenario analysis, ask: "So what?" If the answer isn't obvious, rewrite. - ❌ "VIX rose 5 points" → So what? - ✅ "VIX rose 5 points, which means your short-gamma position lost $800K in hedging slippage, and if VIX holds here for 3 more days, the monthly theta gain doesn't cover it. I recommend closing the short gamma." ---

Type What it does When to use --- --- --- Historical Replay actual events (e.g., "what if March 2020 repeats?") Monthly reporting, new position review Hypothetical Custom scenario based on current market conditions Ad-hoc, event-driven, weekly review Reverse "What kills us?" → work backward from a loss threshold Quarterly, board reporting Sensitivity Move one factor, hold others constant (ceteris paribus) Daily Greeks monitoring Joint Move multiple factors simultaneously with correlation Full scenario analysis (Part 1-4) Path-dependent Multi-day sequences with feedback loops Margin and liquidity analysis

section: Building the Stress Matrix for a Weekly Report
content: 10-scenario standard matrix: Scenario SPX Oil 10Y VIX CDX HY DXY Portfolio P&L % NAV --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- 1 Base: status quo 0% 0% 0bp 0 0bp 0% $0 0.00% 2 Equity correction -5% -3% -15bp +8 +30bp +1% -$X 3 Rate shock -3% 0% +40bp +4 +15bp +1.5% -$Y 4 Oil spike -3% +25% +15bp +5 +10bp -0.5% -$Z 5 Credit event -4% -2% -10bp +6 +100bp +1% -$W 6 Dollar crash +1% +5% +5bp -1 -5bp -5% ±$V 7 Vol explosion -6% +2% -20bp +15 +40bp +1% -$U 8 Correlation spike -4% +3% +10bp +7 +25bp +0.5% -$T 9 Liquidity freeze -8% 0% +5bp +20 +80bp +2% -$S 10 Recovery rally +5% -5% +10bp -5 -20bp -1% +$R Presentation insight: "Scenarios 2-9 all show losses between $A and $B . The worst is scenario 9 (liquidity freeze) at -$S, which consumes X% of our drawdown limit. The key vulnerability across all scenarios is our short-gamma position, which amplifies losses in 7 out of 8 stress scenarios." ---

Interviewers don't want your scenario — they want to see your PROCESS. They're evaluating: 1. Can you structure chaos into a framework? 2. Do you think in probabilities, not certainties? 3. Do you connect macro to portfolio? 4. Do you quantify? 5. Do you recommend, not just flag?

The interviewer says: "Oil just spiked 10%. Walk me through what you'd do." Your answer (2-3 minutes max): > "First, I'd establish the facts: magnitude, percentile, co-movements. A 10% oil move on Brent is approximately a 4σ event — this is extreme. I'd check gold, rates, FX, and equity futures to understand whether the market is treating this as a temporary shock or a sustained supply disruption. > > Second, I'd map our exposure. Direct oil positions give me the P&L immediately. But the bigger question is indirect exposure: our equity book has a negative beta to oil through airlines and consumer discretionary. I'd estimate the cross-asset drag as roughly -1.5% on our equity exposure per 5% oil move, based on historical sensitivity. > > Third, I'd build three scenarios. Base case — diplomatic resolution, oil retraces 50% of the move within a week. Adverse — further escalation, oil reaches $135, equity markets down 5%, correlation spike breaks our diversification. Extreme — full supply disruption, oil $150+, margin calls across the house, liquidity evaporates. > > For each, I'd compute the portfolio P&L including second-order effects and present a one-page brief to the PM within 30 minutes. My recommendation would depend on the numbers, but generally I'd suggest locking in gains on any energy longs and buying put protection on the equity book for the adverse scenario. The cost-benefit ratio on puts after a vol spike isn't great, so I might look at put spreads or collars instead. > > I'd also set a kill condition: if oil retraces below the pre-spike level within 48 hours, I'd downgrade from adverse back to base and unwind the hedge." ---

section: The 7 Deadly Sins of Scenario Analysis
content: Sin Description Fix --- --- --- 1. Precision theater Building complex models with false precision "This model is approximately right, not precisely wrong." 2. Fighting the last war Only running scenarios that already happened Include at least one "novel" scenario each week 3. Ignoring non-linearity Assuming P&L scales linearly with the shock Include gamma, convexity, and margin feedback 4. Static correlation Using calm-period ρ in stress scenarios Use conditional/stressed correlations (ρ → 0.8+) 5. Ignoring liquidity Computing P&L without considering exit costs Add illiquidity premium: stressed spreads × position size 6. Missing 2nd-order effects Modeling the shock but not the feedback loops Chain: shock → margin call → forced selling → further shock 7. No "so what?" Producing numbers without recommendations Every scenario ends with: "therefore I recommend..." ---

Build this in Excel or Python — a live dashboard that answers: "What happens to the book if X today?" 6 pre-computed scenarios, refreshed daily: Scenario Trigger Auto-computed P&L RAG status --- --- --- --- Equity -2% SPX sensitivity → delta × -2% + gamma adj -$X 🟡 if > 25% of DD limit Equity -5% Same + correlation stress -$Y 🔴 if > 50% of DD limit Rates +25bp DV01 × 25 + FX cross -$Z 🟡/🔴 Oil ±15% Oil sensitivity + equity beta -$W / +$V 🟡/🔴 Vol spike (+10 VIX) Vega × 10 + gamma hedging cost -$U 🟡/🔴 Correlation → 1 Re-run portfolio VaR with ρ = 0.8 for all pairs -$T 🔴 if materially different from base VaR RAG thresholds: - 🟢 GREEN: < 25% of drawdown limit in that scenario - 🟡 YELLOW: 25-50% of drawdown limit - 🔴 RED: > 50% of drawdown limit → escalate Value to PMs: "I run these 6 scenarios every morning. Currently, your book is green across all except 'oil +15%' which is yellow at 35% of your drawdown limit. That's the scenario I'd watch today, especially with the EIA inventory report at 10:30." ---

section: Q: "Walk me through how you'd run a stress test on a portfolio."
content: > "I follow a 7-step process. First, I'd establish the facts — what happened, magnitude, percentile rank, and co-movements across asset classes. Second, I map exposure — not just direct but indirect through beta, correlation, FX, volatility, and margin channels. Third, I build three scenarios — base, adverse, and extreme — each with specific price levels, not just 'bad' or 'worse.' > > Fourth, I quantify: for each scenario, I compute delta P&L, gamma adjustment for any options, vega impact if vol changes, and the margin consequence. Fifth, I run a sensitivity grid — not just one point estimate, but the full surface. This reveals breakpoints: for instance, 'the oil level that breaches 50% of our drawdown limit is $133.' > > Sixth, communication — a one-page brief: situation, P&L impact, three scenarios, key risk, and a specific recommendation with cost. Finally, I set review triggers so the analysis stays current. The whole process takes about 30 minutes from event to deliverable."

section: Q: "How do you decide which scenarios to present?"
content: > "I select scenarios based on three criteria: where the book is MOST vulnerable, what the market is CURRENTLY pricing, and what has HISTORICALLY caused the most damage. > > For vulnerability, I run a reverse stress test — 'what scenario causes us to breach 50% of our drawdown limit?' That scenario always makes the list. For market pricing, I look at where implied vol or skew suggests the market IS worried and where it ISN'T — the under-priced tail is the most dangerous. For history, I keep a reference library of 9 crisis analogs and calibrate my extreme scenarios against actual moves. > > The one scenario I always include is the one nobody wants to see — the one that hurts the most. If the CRO looks at my stress matrix and says 'I already knew about all of these,' I've failed. My job is to surface the risk hiding in the blind spot."

section: Q: "What's the difference between a good and a bad scenario analysis?"
content: > "A bad scenario analysis tells you what happened. A good one tells you what to DO. > > Specifically, a bad analysis says: 'If oil rises 15%, we lose $3M.' That's a calculator output. A good analysis says: 'If oil rises 15%, we lose $3M, which consumes 40% of our drawdown limit. The breakpoint is oil $133 — at that level we're at 50%. The loss is concentrated in 3 positions through the equity beta channel. I recommend buying 2-week SPX 5800/5600 put spreads at a cost of $200K, which caps the adverse P&L at $1.8M. If oil retraces below $118 within 48 hours, I'd unwind the protection.' > > The difference: quantified impact, breakpoint, concentration analysis, specific hedge structure with cost-benefit, and a kill condition. That's what separates a risk manager from a spreadsheet." --- Related files: → R Practical Risk Judgment — daily workflows, communication, and limit frameworks → S Opinion Formation — forming the views that drive scenario selection → U Case Studies — real scenarios that actually happened

For each case study, internalize: 1. What happened — facts, timeline, magnitudes 2. The risk failure chain — the sequence of breakdowns that turned a loss into a catastrophe 3. What the RM should have caught — the specific signals that were visible BEFORE the blow-up 4. The systemic lesson — the principle that applies to ANY fund, ANY market 5. Your interview angle — the 60-second version that demonstrates risk judgment The goal is NOT to memorize dates. It's to build a mental library of failure patterns so that when you see the SAME PATTERN forming today, you recognize it instantly. ---

Date Event --- --- 1994 Fund launches. Meriwether + Scholes + Merton (Nobel laureates). Initial capital: $1.25B. 1994-97 Annualized returns ~40%. Capital grows to $4.7B. Strategy: convergence/relative value across global bonds. Early 1998 Leverage: ~25:1. $4.7B equity → $125B assets → $1.25T notional derivatives. Aug 17, 1998 Russia defaults on domestic debt, devalues ruble. Aug-Sep 1998 Global flight to quality. ALL spreads widen simultaneously. Every LTCM position loses.
