Sep 23, 1998 Fed-organized rescue: 14 banks contribute $3.6B. LTCM wound down over 12 months. Final loss ~$4.6B — essentially 100% of equity.

High leverage (25:1) ↓ Illiquid positions (EM debt, off-the-run Treasuries, swap spreads) ↓ Russian default triggers global flight to quality ↓ ALL spread trades widen simultaneously (ρ → 1) ↓ "Diversified" portfolio becomes a single bet on spread compression ↓ Margin calls → can't liquidate without moving the market ↓ Every other convergence fund has the same positions → no buyers ↓ $4.6B loss, systemic bailout required

Metric Value Why it matters --- --- --- Leverage 25:1 (assets/equity) $1 of equity supporting $25 of positions Effective leverage (notional) ~250:1 ($1.25T / $4.7B) True risk exposure was astronomical Daily VaR (their model) $45M at 99% Model said max daily loss ~$45M Actual daily losses in Aug-Sep $100-500M VaR was off by 3-10x Correlation assumption ρ ≈ 0.1-0.3 across strategies In crisis, ρ jumped to 0.8-0.95 Monthly loss (August 1998) -$1.85B (-44% of equity) VaR model said this was ~20σ — "impossible"

section: What the RM Should Have Caught
content: Signal Available when What it meant Action --- --- --- --- Leverage at 25:1 with illiquid assets Continuously No margin of safety — ANY adverse move is fatal Hard leverage cap (10:1 for illiquid), enforced daily All positions loading on "spread compression" Q1 1998 Diversification was illusory — one factor drives everything Factor decomposition showing 85%+ variance from one factor Funding from 28 counterparties but all with same margin terms Continuously In stress, all call margin simultaneously Stagger maturities, maintain undrawn credit lines Liquidity of positions vs liquidation timeline Continuously Positions needed 3+ months to exit; fund could survive 3 weeks Liquidity-adjusted VaR; position limits based on days-to-exit, not notional VaR model assuming normal returns and stable ρ Continuously Fat tails and crisis correlations make VaR useless at the extremes Supplement VaR with stress tests using historical ρ spikes (92, 94 bond rout) Other funds replicating the same trades 1997-98 Crowding → when one unwinds, all must unwind → no exit Monitor market positioning; cap position as % of market open interest

Leverage is a liquidation timer, not a return amplifier. LTCM's trades mostly converged eventually — many of their positions were profitable by 2000. But "eventually right" doesn't matter if you're dead in September. The RM's question is never "will this converge?" but "can we survive the path to convergence?" $$\text{Survival} = f\left(\frac{\text{Equity cushion}}{\text{Maximum drawdown path}}\right)$$ If the maximum drawdown path exceeds equity at ANY point, the fund dies — regardless of terminal value. ---

Date Event --- --- 2013 Bill Hwang's Tiger Asia pleads guilty to insider trading. Hwang banned from Hong Kong markets. 2020 Hwang's family office (Archegos) builds concentrated positions via Total Return Swaps (TRS) with 6 prime brokers. Dec 2020-Mar 2021 ViacomCBS, Discovery, Baidu, GSX Techedu run up 100-400%. Archegos is the whale. Mar 22, 2021 ViacomCBS announces stock offering → stock drops 9%. Mar 24-25 Margin calls. Hwang can't meet them. Mar 26 (Fri) Goldman, Morgan Stanley, Deutsche begin liquidating $20B+ in block trades. Mar 29 (Mon) Credit Suisse and Nomura still holding. Final losses Credit Suisse: $5.5B. Nomura: $2.9B. Goldman/MS: minimal (sold first). Total: ~$10B.

Family office → no 13F filing → invisible to market ↓ Total Return Swaps → positions don't appear in equity filings ↓ 6 PBs, each seeing only their slice → no one sees the aggregate ($30B+ notional) ↓ Massive concentrated position: 50%+ of float in some names ↓ ViacomCBS equity raise → stock drops → margin calls ↓ Hwang can't meet margin → forced liquidation ↓ ALL PBs need to sell the SAME stocks simultaneously ↓ First-mover advantage: Goldman exits fast (Fri), CS exits slow (Mon) → $5.5B loss

Dimension What failed What should have existed --- --- --- Aggregate visibility No PB knew Hwang's total exposure across all PBs Cross-PB information sharing agreements or concentration limits Client due diligence CS gave Hwang favorable margin DESPITE SEC conviction Enhanced due diligence: prior enforcement = tighter terms, period Concentration per name Some names: Archegos held >50% of float via swaps Position limit: max 10% of average daily volume per client per name Margin methodology Static margin rates not reflecting crowding risk Dynamic margin: increase requirements as position grows relative to float Liquidation protocol CS waited days to liquidate, hoping for recovery Pre-agreed automatic liquidation at margin breach. No discretion. First-mover dynamics Goldman sold Friday night; CS waited until Monday Game theory: in multi-PB margin calls, speed = survival

> Archegos held ~$10B of ViacomCBS through TRS across 6 PBs. > ViacomCBS market cap at peak: ~$60B. Free float: ~$30B. ADV: ~$500M. > > Position as % of float: $10B / $30B = 33% > Days to exit at 10% participation: $10B / ($500M × 0.10) = 200 days > Days to exit at 25% participation: $10B / ($500M × 0.25) = 80 days > > Conclusion: This position is fundamentally unliquidatable in a stress scenario. At any meaningful participation rate, the selling pressure would destroy the stock. This alone should have been a red flag at EVERY prime broker.

You can't manage risk you can't see. Archegos exploited the seams between institutions. At Squarepoint, the analog is cross-PM exposure: PM A and PM B might each hold positions that look fine individually but create catastrophic aggregate concentration. The RM who only looks at individual books will miss the same failure that killed Credit Suisse's PB desk. ---

Date Event --- --- 1992 Nick Leeson posted to Singapore. Role: arbitrage Nikkei 225 futures between SIMEX and Osaka. 1992-94 Leeson takes directional bets, hides losses in "Error Account 88888." Reports profits to London. Jan 17, 1995 Kobe earthquake. Nikkei drops 1,000 points. Leeson's long Nikkei positions implode. Feb 23, 1995 Leeson flees Singapore. Total losses discovered: £827M. Feb 26, 1995 Barings Bank declared insolvent. 233 years old. Sold to ING for £1.

Leeson runs BOTH front office (trading) AND back office (settlement) ↓ No separation of duties → he can hide his own trades ↓ Losses hidden in Error Account 88888 (since 1992) ↓ London sees reported "profits" from arbitrage → Leeson treated as star ↓ No one asks: "How is arb generating 20%+ returns?" ↓ Kobe earthquake (Jan 1995) → Nikkei crashes ↓ Leeson doubles down → writes strangles to finance margin on longs ↓ Losses explode: £827M → more than Barings' entire equity (£540M) ↓ Bank collapses. 233-year institution → sold for £1.

Red flag Why it should have been caught RM principle --- --- --- Same person trading + settling Zero internal controls on position reporting Segregation of duties : trader can NEVER reconcile their own book Arbitrage "profits" of £28M in 1994 Nikkei arb should make 2-5%, not 20%+ Too-good-to-be-true test : when returns exceed strategy capacity, investigate Growing margin calls from SIMEX Arb should require constant margin; growing margin = directional position Independent margin reconciliation : match exchange margin calls to reported positions Error account with £50M balance Error accounts should be near-zero, reconciled daily Error account policy : any balance > $X requires sign-off within 24 hours Leeson requested £742M in "financing" from London £742M for an arb book? The notional makes no sense. Funding requests must reconcile with reported positions Internal audit (1994) flagged dual role as "a significant risk" The audit was done AND IGNORED Audit follow-up : findings must have remediation deadlines + escalation

Operational risk is the risk that nobody wants to talk about until it kills you. Every control that Barings lacked is now standard at fund administrators and prime brokers. But the principle remains: if you rely on a trader's self-reported P&L without independent verification, you're one rogue trader away from £827M. At Squarepoint, verify positions against PB statements, not trader reports. ---

Date Event --- --- 2011 Bruno Iksil in JPM's Chief Investment Office (CIO) builds credit derivative positions as "portfolio hedge." Q4 2011 Position grows to ~$51B notional in CDX IG and HY tranches. Jan 2012 CIO changes VaR model → reported VaR drops from $129M to $67M overnight. Mar 2012 Position grows to ~$157B notional. Market participants notice → "London Whale." Apr 6, 2012 Bloomberg/WSJ publish stories about "the Whale." JPM CEO Dimon calls it "a tempest in a teapot." May 10, 2012 JPM discloses $2B in losses (later grows to $6.2B). Dimon: "egregious mistakes." CIO total loss $6.2B

CIO tasked with "hedging" JPM's overall credit portfolio ↓ Position starts as legitimate hedge → generates P&L ↓ Management likes the P&L → position treated as profit center ↓ Position grows: $51B → $157B notional (200% increase in 3 months) ↓ Size becomes too large → market participants front-run it ↓ VaR model changed (Jan 2012): $129M → $67M overnight ↓ New model hides true risk → limits not breached on paper ↓ Market moves against position → losses accelerate ↓ CIO marks positions using favorable internal prices (mark-to-model gaming) ↓ $6.2B loss + regulatory action + CEO credibility destroyed

Model change What it hid What should have happened --- --- --- VaR formula revised → hazard weighting changed Risk looked 50% lower overnight Model changes require independent validation team approval BEFORE live deployment Confidence interval adjusted Tail losses disappeared from reporting Any change that reduces reported risk by >10% triggers automatic review P&L marks moved from external to internal pricing Losses understated by $660M Independent price verification (IPV) must use external/market prices, not trader marks The "VaR Halving" test: If a risk metric drops >20% without any change in position, it's ALWAYS a red flag. Either the model changed (suspicious) or the market changed (investigate). A 50% drop with no position change = guaranteed model gaming or calculation error.

This case defines the concept of hedge drift — when a legitimate hedge morphs into a speculative position: Phase Size Purpose P&L attribution RM should say --- --- --- --- --- 1 — Hedge $20B Offset bank's credit exposure ~$0 (hedge works) "Fine — hedge ratio is appropriate." 2 — Profitable hedge $51B Hedge + some P&L on basis +$400M "Hedge is now generating P&L. Size exceeds hedge need by 2x. Re-justify." 3 — Profit center $100B+ Actively trading credit for P&L +$1B cumulative "This is NOT a hedge. It's a proprietary position. Apply full risk limits." 4 — Trapped position $157B Too big to exit, market is front-running -$6.2B Crisis management. RM principle: Any "hedge" generating more than ±10% of the hedged portfolio's P&L volatility has drifted into a directional trade. Reclassify and apply full limits. ---

Date Event --- --- 2005 Brian Hunter makes $1B+ from NG calendar spreads post-Hurricane Katrina. Star trader. Jan 2006 Amaranth AUM: ~$9B. Multi-strategy fund. NG positions grow. Aug 2006 Hunter's NG positions: ~$30B notional. Fund risk: >50% in one strategy. Sep 14-20, 2006 NG winter-summer spread collapses. Losses: ~$6B in one week. Sep 29, 2006 Fund liquidated. Lost 65% of NAV. Done.

Hunter makes $1B in 2005 (Katrina) → management treats him as superstar ↓ "He's earned the right to size up" → limits RAISED to accommodate him ↓ $30B notional in NG calendar spreads → 50%+ of fund risk in one strategy ↓ Position = significant % of NG futures open interest → CAN'T EXIT ↓ NG spread moves against him in September ↓ Can't exit because position IS the market ↓ Other liquid positions sold to meet margin → those positions also lose ↓ $6B loss in one week → fund dead

section: Concentration Metrics That Should Have Screamed
content: Metric Threshold Amaranth actual Verdict --- --- --- --- Single trader P&L as % of fund P&L < 30% > 70% CATASTROPHIC Single strategy as % of fund VaR < 25% > 50% CATASTROPHIC Position as % of NG open interest < 5% ~30% UNLIQUIDATABLE Days to exit at 10% participation < 10 days > 60 days DEAD Prior P&L used to justify current risk? Never "He made $1B last year" TEXTBOOK FAILURE

Date Risk limit Rationale What RM should have said --- --- --- --- Jan 2005 $500M VaR Initial allocation "Appropriate for NG allocation." Jun 2005 $750M VaR "Hunter is performing, deserves more" "Performance doesn't change risk. Market liquidity hasn't grown." Dec 2005 $1.5B VaR "He made $1B this year" "STOP. Past profits are not risk capital. Limit stays at $750M." Jun 2006 $2.5B VaR "This is our best strategy" "ESCALATE. We're a multi-strat fund with single-strat risk. IC override required."

"He's earned the right to size up" is the most dangerous sentence in fund management. Past P&L is informative about the trader's skill but tells you NOTHING about future risk. The market doesn't know your YTD P&L. The next trade's risk is independent of the last trade's return. Any risk limit that gets raised because a trader is performing well is no longer a risk limit — it's a performance reward disguised as risk management. ---

Date Event --- --- 2018-20 Hedge funds build leveraged Treasury basis trades: long cash Treasuries, short Treasury futures. Leverage: 50-100x. Feb 20, 2020 COVID sell-off begins. SPX starts falling. Treasuries initially rally (safe haven). Mar 9, 2020 Oil crashes 30% (Saudi-Russia price war + COVID). Equities in freefall. Mar 11-12, 2020 Treasury SELL-OFF begins. Cash Treasuries drop faster than futures. Basis WIDENS instead of converging. Mar 12-16, 2020 Margin calls on basis trades. Forced selling of Treasuries. Bid-ask in on-the-run 10Y widens from 1/32 to 4/32. Mar 15, 2020 Fed cuts to 0% + announces $700B QE. Not enough. Mar 23, 2020 Fed announces UNLIMITED QE. Treasury market stabilizes.

"Risk-free" convergence trade: long cash Treasury, short futures ↓ Leverage 50-100x (because spread is only 2-5bp) ↓ COVID sell-off → flight to quality → Treasuries initially RALLY (trade works) ↓ Then: PANIC selling → everyone sells everything INCLUDING Treasuries ↓ Cash Treasuries drop faster than futures → basis WIDENS → leveraged trade LOSES ↓ Margin calls on 50-100x leveraged positions ↓ Funds sell Treasuries to meet margin → more selling → basis widens further ↓ Treasury market liquidity EVAPORATES ↓ Fed must intervene with unlimited QE to prevent Treasury market collapse ↓ The "safest" asset class nearly broke the financial system

Metric Normal March 2020 --- --- --- 10Y Treasury bid-ask 1/32 ($312.50 per $1M face) 4-8/32 (up to $2,500) Basis spread (cash-futures) 2-5bp -15 to -25bp (NEGATIVE — complete inversion) Margin requirement 1-2% of notional 4-6% of notional (2-3x increase) Fund equity as % of position 1-2% (at 50-100x leverage) ZERO — margin calls exceed equity Market depth (10Y) $300M per 1bp < $50M per 1bp The lethal formula: $$\text{Leverage} \times \text{Adverse basis move} > \text{Equity}$$ $$50\text{x} \times 25\text{bp adverse} = 12.5\% \text{ of notional loss} \gg 1\text{-}2\% \text{ equity}$$ The fund is dead 5x over before the position has a CHANCE to converge.

Path risk — not terminal risk — kills levered trades. The basis trade converges at maturity BY DEFINITION. Every fund that blew up would have been profitable if they could have held to maturity. But they couldn't, because margin calls forced them out. The RM question is always: "Can we survive the worst path to the destination?" If the answer requires "markets stay liquid" or "correlations stay stable," the answer is NO. ---

Date Event --- --- Jan-Mar 2020 COVID lockdowns destroy oil demand. Global demand drops ~30%. Mar 2020 Saudi-Russia price war adds supply. Oil collapses from $65 to $20. Apr 2-17, 2020 Cushing, OK storage fills to 80%+. Front-month WTI at $20. Apr 17 (Fri) May WTI futures: $18.27. Expiry: April 21. Apr 20 (Mon) May WTI crashes to -$37.63 — the first negative commodity price in history. Apr 21 (Tue) May contract expires. Anyone still holding must take physical delivery.

COVID + price war → oil demand collapses 30% ↓ Storage fills up globally. Cushing at 80%+ capacity. ↓ May WTI = PHYSICAL delivery contract. Holding to expiry = receive 1,000 barrels. ↓ No physical storage available → no one wants delivery ↓ Sellers MUST exit May contract before expiry ↓ Buyers know sellers are desperate → bid collapses ↓ Price goes BELOW ZERO → you PAY someone to take oil ↓ Risk models with $0 floor: BROKEN. P&L calculations: WRONG. Greeks: UNDEFINED.

Assumption Why it existed Why it failed Fix --- --- --- --- "Oil can't go below $0" Commodity has intrinsic value Storage at capacity → holding cost > commodity value Remove hard floors; model storage cost as negative option "Smooth price distribution" Normal/log-normal returns Physical delivery creates discontinuity at expiry Model physical delivery mechanics for near-dated contracts "Futures converge to spot" Arbitrage keeps them aligned When arb is impossible (no storage), basis explodes Track storage capacity as a risk input "Roll risk is small" Historically true for liquid contracts USO needed to roll 80K contracts in 4 days → front-ran Monitor ETF roll mechanics as a separate risk factor

USO (United States Oil Fund) held ~80,000 near-month WTI futures contracts ($4B notional). It was forced to roll from May to June in a 4-day window. The market KNEW this. Impact: - Market makers sold May and bought June ahead of USO → widened the calendar spread by $15+ - USO's rolling losses were approximately $1.5B+ over the roll period - Retail investors holding USO thought they were "buying cheap oil" — they were actually funding market-maker spread extraction RM lesson: Any position large enough that its MECHANICS (roll, rebalance, expiry) are predictable to the market is at risk of being front-run. Track position as % of open interest AND as % of daily volume. ---

Date Event --- --- Sep 6, 2022 New UK PM Liz Truss appoints Kwasi Kwarteng as Chancellor. Sep 23, 2022 "Mini-budget": £45B in unfunded tax cuts announced. Markets react violently. Sep 23-28, 2022 30Y gilt yield: 3.7% → 5.1% (+140bp in 3 days). GBP -5% vs USD. Sep 28, 2022 Bank of England emergency intervention: temporary unlimited gilt purchases. Oct 14, 2022 Kwarteng fired. Mini-budget reversed. Truss resigns Oct 20 (45 days PM).

The crisis was CAUSED by pension fund risk management structures (LDI — Liability-Driven Investment): Mini-budget → gilt yields spike +140bp in 3 days ↓ LDI funds (pension vehicle) used leveraged gilt positions to match liabilities ↓ Gilt price drops → margin calls on LDI funds ↓ LDI funds sell gilts to raise cash for margin ↓ Selling gilts → gilt prices drop further → MORE margin calls ↓ DOOM LOOP: sell → price drops → more margin → more selling ↓ Gilt liquidity evaporates (bid-ask widens 10x) ↓ BoE forced to intervene as buyer of last resort ↓ Without BoE, UK pension system faced SYSTEMIC FAILURE

Day 30Y gilt yield LDI margin call (est.) Forced selling Net effect --- --- --- --- --- Sep 23 3.7% → 4.0% £5B £5B of gilts sold Self-reinforcing Sep 26 4.0% → 4.5% £15B £15B of gilts sold Accelerating Sep 27 4.5% → 4.9% £25B+ Liquidation panic Systemic Sep 28 4.9% → 5.1% (peaked) Total est: £50-65B BoE intervenes Stabilized The horrifying math: > LDI funds had ~£1.5T in assets, levered 3-4x on gilts. > A 100bp yield rise ≈ 20% price decline on 30Y gilts. > 20% × 3-4x leverage = 60-80% of equity wiped out. > These funds were managing pension money for millions of UK workers.

The LDI crisis has a DIRECT parallel to multi-PM fund risk: LDI crisis element Multi-PM fund analog --- --- Leveraged position in one instrument (gilts) PM concentrated in one sector/factor Margin call → forced selling → price moves against you Margin call → forced liquidation at worst prices All LDI funds held the same position All PMs loading on the same factor (cross-PM correlation) No one modeled "gilt yield +140bp in 3 days" Scenarios that seem impossible get excluded Doom loop required external intervention (BoE) At fund level, CRO/IC must break the loop before it becomes terminal

Margin structures create reflexive feedback loops that turn orderly drawdowns into existential crises. The RM's job is to BREAK THE LOOP before it accelerates: 1. Maintain cash/liquidity buffers sized for 2x the worst historical margin call 2. Pre-identify liquidation candidates BEFORE the margin call arrives 3. Model margin requirements under stress, not just current conditions 4. Never assume the central bank or any external party will rescue you ---

Date Event --- --- Feb 5, 2018 SPX drops 4.1% in regular session. VIX jumps from 17 to 37 (intraday peak: 50). Feb 5, 4pm+ XIV (inverse VIX ETN) NAV drops 93%. Triggers "acceleration event" → product terminated. Feb 6, 2018 Credit Suisse terminates XIV. $1.9B in investor capital → ~$100M. Broader impact Short vol funds globally wiped out. $6B+ in total losses across short-vol strategies.

SPX drops 4% → VIX jumps from 17 to 37 ↓ Short VIX products (XIV, SVXY) must BUY VIX futures to rebalance ↓ Buying VIX futures → VIX rises further ↓ VIX rising further → more rebalancing needed → MORE buying ↓ FEEDBACK LOOP: short vol rebalancing AMPLIFIES the vol spike ↓ VIX reaches 50 intraday (from 17 → 194% increase) ↓ XIV NAV goes to ~$0 (inverse product: 2x levered × 194% VIX increase > 100% loss) ↓ Product terminates. $1.9B → $100M.

XIV was a -1x daily inverse VIX futures ETN. Its rebalancing requirement: $$\text{VIX futures to trade} = -1 \times \text{NAV} \times \frac{\Delta\text{VIX}}{\text{VIX} {\text{prev}}}$$ When VIX doubles, XIV must buy VIX futures equal to 100%+ of its NAV. But it's already lost most of its NAV. The rebalancing demand exceeds the product's ability to survive. The critical insight for risk managers: Any leveraged daily-rebalancing product has an embedded convexity trap . The bigger the move, the more it needs to rebalance, which amplifies the move, which requires MORE rebalancing. This is a mechanical doom loop — no human decision required.

Short vol is picking up pennies in front of a steamroller — literally. XIV generated steady returns for years (VIX term structure in contango → short front-month profits from roll-down). But a single day destroyed the product entirely. At Squarepoint: - Any strategy that implicitly or explicitly shorts vol must be stress-tested for a VIX tripling scenario - Monitor "gamma contribution" — how much does our rebalancing amplify or dampen market moves? - Products with mechanical rebalancing rules (leveraged ETFs, risk parity, vol-targeting) create PREDICTABLE forced flows — use them as inputs, not exposures ---

Date Event --- --- Jul 31, 2024 Bank of Japan raises rates from 0-0.1% to 0.25%. JPY starts strengthening. Aug 1, 2024 Weak US manufacturing ISM. Recession fears. Tech earnings disappoint. Aug 2, 2024 US NFP misses (114K vs 175K exp). Unemployment jumps to 4.3% → triggers Sahm Rule. Aug 5, 2024 Nikkei -12.4% (worst since 1987). VIX spikes to 65 intraday (from 23 → 183% increase). SPX -3%. JPY appreciates 7% in 3 days. Aug 6-9, 2024 Recovery. Nikkei bounces +10%. VIX collapses to 20. Carry trades partially re-established.

BoJ rate hike + US recession fears → JPY strengthens ↓ Carry traders (borrow JPY, invest in USD/EM assets) face losses ↓ JPY rising → carry trade P&L negative → margin calls ↓ Forced selling of carried assets (US equities, EM, crypto) ↓ Selling US equities → SPX drops → VIX spikes ↓ VIX spike → vol-targeting strategies must REDUCE exposure ↓ Vol-targeting selling → MORE equity selling → MORE VIX increase ↓ CTA trend-followers hit stop-losses → ADDITIONAL selling ↓ Nikkei -12% (carry unwind + Japanese exporter FX impact + margin) ↓ Cross-asset correlation → 1. Everything falls together. ↓ Recovery in 3 days once forced selling exhausts itself

Layer Mechanism Timing Detectable in advance? --- --- --- --- 1. Fundamental BoJ hike + weak US data Days before YES — rate decision was announced; data was scheduled 2. Carry unwind JPY appreciation forces selling of carried assets Hours PARTIALLY — CFTC data showed record JPY shorts 3. Systematic amplification Vol-targeting + CTA selling Minutes-hours YES — know the mechanical rules (vol target = vol up → sell) 4. Liquidity evaporation Market-makers widen spreads, depth disappears Minutes INDIRECTLY — pre-event market depth data 5. Recovery Forced selling exhausts, dip-buyers arrive 1-3 days PROBABILITY — most forced-selling-driven crashes recover quickly

The August 2024 crash was 90% mechanical, 10% fundamental. The actual news (BoJ hike, weak NFP) justified maybe a 2% sell-off. The other 10% was carry unwind → vol-targeting → CTA stops → liquidity death → correlation = 1. Implication for Squarepoint: When you see a 2% equity sell-off that turns into 12%, don't ask "what fundamental news caused this?" Ask: "what MECHANICAL selling is amplifying this?" If the answer is carry unwind + systematic strategies, the move is likely to reverse within days. Your RM job: ensure the fund SURVIVES the mechanical cascade with enough capital to benefit from the recovery. ---

Every blow-up maps to a combination of these 7 failure modes: Failure mode Cases Detection method --- --- --- --- 1 Excessive leverage LTCM, Basis trade, LDI, Amaranth, Archegos Leverage ratios × illiquidity score 2 Illusory diversification LTCM, Amaranth, Aug 2024 Factor decomposition → "how many independent bets?" 3 Concentration Archegos, Amaranth, London Whale HHI concentration index, % of open interest 4 Model failure / gaming LTCM, London Whale, Negative oil VaR backtest exceptions, model change reviews 5 Operational / governance Barings, London Whale, Archegos Segregation of duties, audit follow-up 6 Margin / liquidity doom loop Basis trade, UK gilts, Aug 2024 Stressed margin utilization, days-to-exit 7 Mechanical amplification Volmageddon, Aug 2024, negative oil Identify forced flows: rebalancing, rolls, stops

LTCM Archegos Barings Whale Amaranth Mar 2020 Oil -$37 UK Gilts Volmageddon Aug 2024 --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- Leverage ✓✓✓ ✓✓ ✓ ✓ ✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓ ✓ Illusory diversification ✓✓✓ ✓✓ ✓ ✓✓ Concentration ✓ ✓✓✓ ✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓ Model failure ✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓ Operational/governance ✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓ ✓ Margin doom loop ✓✓ ✓ ✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓ Mechanical amplification ✓ ✓✓ ✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓✓✓ ✓ = present, ✓✓ = significant, ✓✓✓ = primary cause

1. Leverage + Illiquidity + Margin call (LTCM, Basis trade, UK gilts) → the path-to-convergence problem: right at maturity, dead before maturity 2. Concentration + Past-performance-as-justification (Amaranth, Archegos) → "he's earned it" kills funds 3. Model trust + Assumption rigidity (Negative oil, LTCM VaR) → the model is a map, not the territory 4. Mechanical selling + Correlation = 1 (Volmageddon, Aug 2024) → feedback loops that amplify small shocks into catastrophes 5. Operational gaps + Perverse incentives (Barings, Whale) → when traders benefit from hiding losses and CAN hide losses ---

section: Q: "Walk me through a risk management failure that you've studied and what you learned."
content: > "I'll use LTCM because it illustrates three overlapping failures that are directly relevant to a multi-PM fund. > > LTCM ran convergence trades across global bond markets at 25:1 leverage with $1.25 trillion in notional derivatives. Their models showed diversified positions — different instruments, different countries, different maturities. But in August 1998, when Russia defaulted and triggered a global flight to quality, every single spread widened simultaneously. Correlation jumped from 0.2 to 0.95. Their 'diversified' portfolio was actually one trade: spread compression. > > What kills me about LTCM is that their trades were mostly RIGHT. Many of those positions converged by 2000 and would have been profitable. But you can't profit if you're dead. Leverage is a liquidation timer — 25:1 on illiquid assets means you survive maybe 4% against you before margin calls force liquidation. And when every other convergence fund has the same positions, there are no buyers when you need to sell. > > The RM lessons I take: First, factor decomposition is non-negotiable — 'how many independent bets do we actually have?' usually reveals the real number is 2-3, not 20. Second, leverage limits must be liquidity-adjusted — 10:1 on liquid instruments is fine; 3:1 on illiquid is already dangerous. Third, VaR is necessary but insufficient — it told LTCM their max daily loss was $45M; they lost $500M in a day. You need stress tests with stressed correlations, not just point-in-time VaR."

section: Q: "How would you apply the lessons from these case studies in your daily work?"
content: > "I'd build a daily checklist based on the failure patterns I've studied. Every morning, I'd ask five questions: > > One — Concentration: is any single name, sector, or factor responsible for more than 30% of house VaR? If yes, flag it. This is the Amaranth/Archegos check. > > Two — Cross-PM correlation: are multiple PMs loading on the same factor without knowing it? I'd run a simple factor regression weekly. This is the LTCM check — illusory diversification. > > Three — Model integrity: has any risk metric moved more than 20% without a corresponding position change? If VaR drops 50% overnight, something is wrong with the model, not the risk. This is the London Whale check. > > Four — Margin adequacy: if our worst-case scenario hits, does our margin utilization exceed 85%? If yes, pre-identify what to liquidate BEFORE the margin call. This is the UK gilt / March 2020 check. > > Five — Mechanical amplification: are there systematic strategies (CTAs, vol-targeting, ETF rebalancing) that would amplify a sell-off in our positions? If yes, size the forced-selling estimate and include it in our stress test. This is the Volmageddon / August 2024 check. > > That's five questions, five minutes, every morning — and it covers over 90% of the failure patterns that have killed funds in the last 30 years."

section: Q: "What's the single most important lesson from studying these blow-ups?"
content: > "That every catastrophic loss shares the same anatomy: a risk that was VISIBLE was either not measured, not communicated, or not acted upon. > > Barings: the audit flagged Leeson's dual role — it was ignored. London Whale: the VaR model was gamed — nobody challenged it. Amaranth: the concentration was visible — limits were raised instead of enforced. Archegos: each prime broker saw their slice — nobody aggregated. > > None of these were Black Swans. They were visible, measurable risks that the risk management function either couldn't see, chose not to see, or saw but couldn't stop. That tells me the RM's most important skill isn't modeling — it's the combination of SEEING the risk, COMMUNICATING it clearly to people who don't want to hear it, and having the INSTITUTIONAL authority to enforce action. The technical work is necessary but not sufficient. The human system around it is what determines whether the warning actually prevents the loss." --- Related files: → T Scenario Analysis — scenario frameworks and stress testing methodology → R Practical Risk Judgment — daily workflows, escalation, and communication → Q Cross Asset Relationships — correlation regimes and contagion mechanics → F Risk Measures — VaR, stress testing, and risk metrics

Round 3 is with a quant researcher. They won't expect you to derive GARCH from scratch — but they WILL expect you to: 1. Define each concept in one sentence — no hand-waving 2. Explain why it matters for risk management with a concrete example 3. Know the limitations — when the model breaks and what to do about it 4. Ask intelligent questions — the kind that a good RM asks a quant team 5. Connect math to P&L — translate statistical concepts into portfolio risk language The RM's job isn't to build the models. It's to challenge them, interpret their output, and know when to override them. ---

Definition: A time series is stationary if its statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation structure) don't change over time. Why it matters for risk: Almost every risk model assumes stationarity — VaR uses a historical window and assumes the future distribution looks like the past. If the regime changes (low-vol → high-vol, deflation → inflation), the model is using the wrong distribution. Types of non-stationarity: Type What changes Market example Risk impact --- --- --- --- Mean shift Average return level Rates from 0% → 5% (2020-2023) Carry assumptions wrong Variance shift Volatility level VIX from 12 → 35 in a week VaR immediately stale Structural break Entire distribution COVID lockdown (Mar 2020) All historical relationships invalid Trending variance Vol slowly increases/decreases Post-GFC volatility compression Lookback-dependent: short window = different answer than long window How to detect non-stationarity in practice: - Rolling statistics: Plot rolling 30d mean and 60d vol. If they're trending, you have non-stationarity. - Chow test / breakpoint test: Formally test whether model parameters differ across sub-samples. - The "gut check": If someone says "the model worked great until 6 months ago" — that IS the non-stationarity. The regime shifted and the model didn't adapt. Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test: Tests whether a time series has a unit root (non-stationary). A p-value < 0.05 means you can reject the null of non-stationarity → the series IS stationary. Most price series: non-stationary. Most return series: stationary. The RM question to the quant: "How sensitive is this model to the lookback window? Show me how the output changes if we use 1 year vs. 3 years vs. the full history."

Definition: Correlation of a time series with its own lagged values. If today's return tells you something about tomorrow's return, autocorrelation exists. Autocorrelation What it means Market example Risk implication --- --- --- --- Positive (momentum) Trends persist CTA trend-following works Multi-day VaR UNDERESTIMATES risk (moves compound) Negative (mean reversion) Moves reverse Stat arb, pairs trading works Multi-day VaR OVERESTIMATES risk (moves cancel) Zero (i.i.d.) No serial dependence Textbook assumption √T scaling is valid The square-root-of-time scaling problem: $$\text{VaR} {T\text{-day}} = \text{VaR} {1\text{-day}} \times \sqrt{T}$$ This formula is ONLY valid if returns are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.). When autocorrelation exists: $$\text{VaR} {T\text{-day}} = \text{VaR} {1\text{-day}} \times T^H$$ where $H$ is the Hurst exponent: - $H = 0.5$ → random walk (i.i.d.) → standard √T scaling - $H > 0.5$ → momentum (positive autocorrelation) → risk grows FASTER than √T - $H < 0.5$ → mean reversion → risk grows SLOWER than √T Worked example: > 1-day VaR = $1M. What's 10-day VaR? > > - If H = 0.5 (i.i.d.): $1M × 10^0.5 = $3.16M > - If H = 0.7 (momentum): $1M × 10^0.7 = $5.01M (58% higher!) > - If H = 0.3 (mean reversion): $1M × 10^0.3 = $2.00M (37% lower) > > The same portfolio has 2.5x different 10-day risk depending on autocorrelation structure. RM implication: If the quant team uses √T scaling for regulatory capital or margin estimates and the portfolio trades momentum strategies, you're UNDERSTATING multi-day risk by up to 60%. Ask: "What's the Hurst exponent of our strategy returns? Are we adjusting the scaling factor?"

Definition: Real market returns have more extreme events than a normal distribution predicts. The distribution has excess kurtosis — fatter tails and a sharper peak. How fat are real tails? Event Normal distribution says Reality --- --- --- 3σ daily move (e.g., SPX -3%) Once every ~1.5 years Once every ~6 months 4σ daily move (e.g., SPX -4%) Once every ~126 years Once every ~2-3 years 5σ daily move (e.g., SPX -5%) Once every ~14,000 years Once every ~10-20 years 6σ daily move (e.g., SPX -6%) Once every ~1.5 million years Has happened multiple times since 2000 Kurtosis: - Normal distribution: kurtosis = 3 (excess kurtosis = 0) - Typical daily equity returns: excess kurtosis = 3-10 - FX returns: excess kurtosis = 2-8 - Commodity returns: excess kurtosis = 5-30 (especially energy) Why fat tails break VaR: A normal-distribution VaR at 99% uses $z = 2.33$. But if the true distribution has excess kurtosis of 6, the actual 99th percentile is more like $z = 3.0$ to $z = 3.5$, depending on skewness. $$\text{True 99% loss} \approx 1.3\text{x to }1.5\text{x the normal VaR}$$ Alternative distributions that capture fat tails: Distribution Key feature When to use --- --- --- Student-t Fat tails controlled by degrees of freedom (ν) General-purpose; ν = 3-5 for equities Generalized Pareto (GPD) Models only the tail beyond a threshold Extreme value theory; tail risk quantification Stable / Lévy Infinite variance possible Heavy-tailed assets (crypto, commodities) Mixture of normals Two regimes blended Regime-switching (low-vol + high-vol) RM question: "Does the VaR model assume normal returns? If yes, what's the kurtosis of our actual return distribution? How much is VaR understated?"
Two assets correlated at 0.7 might both be driven by a third factor (USD, risk appetite, rates). The RESIDUAL correlation after removing common factors is what matters for hedging.

The most dangerous property in finance: Regime Stock-bond correlation Implication --- --- --- Normal (pre-2020) -0.3 to -0.5 Bonds hedge equities → 60/40 works Inflation shock (2022) +0.3 to +0.6 Both fall together → 60/40 fails → portfolio VaR explodes Deflation scare -0.5 to -0.8 Strong negative → bonds super-hedge Liquidity crisis +0.5 to +0.9 Everything sells off → ρ → 1 The asymmetry: Correlations tend to INCREASE in sell-offs and DECREASE in rallies. This means diversification benefits disappear exactly when you need them most.

Pearson correlation only captures LINEAR relationships. Two assets can have ρ = 0 but strong tail dependence (they move independently in normal markets but crash together in crises). Measuring tail dependence: - Copulas: Model the joint distribution of extreme events independent of marginal distributions. The Clayton copula captures lower-tail dependence (crash-together risk). The Gaussian copula (used in CDOs pre-2008) famously understated tail dependence. - Conditional correlation: ρ(X,Y both < 5th percentile) vs. ρ(X,Y unconditional). If conditional correlation is much higher, you have tail dependence. RM question: "What's our correlation matrix using the last 3 months vs. the last 3 years? If they're very different, which one is the model using, and why?"

Definition: Skewness measures the asymmetry of the return distribution. - Negative skew (left tail fatter): Typical for equities and credit — small frequent gains, large rare losses. "Picking up pennies in front of a steamroller." - Positive skew (right tail fatter): Typical for long options, trend-following, tail hedges — small frequent losses, large rare gains. Why skew matters for the RM: A portfolio with negative skew LOOKS good on average metrics (high Sharpe) but has hidden blow-up risk. The Sharpe ratio doesn't distinguish between negatively and positively skewed strategies: Strategy Annual return Vol Sharpe Skew Max drawdown --- --- --- --- --- --- Short vol 12% 8% 1.5 -3.0 -50% Long vol / tail hedge 3% 15% 0.2 +2.5 -20% Strategy A looks better on Sharpe. But it has a hidden -50% left tail. The RM must look at Sharpe AND skew together. Sortino ratio adjusts for this by penalizing only downside deviation: $$\text{Sortino} = \frac{R p - R f}{\text{Downside deviation}}$$ ---

Model Complexity Key feature Limitation --- --- --- --- Simple historical vol Low σ = stdev(returns, N days) Treats all observations equally EWMA Low-Medium Recent obs weighted more (λ) Only one parameter; no mean reversion GARCH(1,1) Medium Vol clustering + mean reversion Symmetric — ignores leverage effect GJR-GARCH / EGARCH Medium-High Asymmetric: down moves increase vol more More parameters → calibration risk Stochastic vol (Heston) High Vol itself is random with mean reversion Harder to calibrate; used more in options pricing Realized vol (high-freq) High Uses intraday data (5-min returns) Microstructure noise; data-intensive

$$\sigma^2 t = \lambda \cdot \sigma^2 {t-1} + (1 - \lambda) \cdot r^2 {t-1}$$ - λ = 0.94 (RiskMetrics standard for daily data): yesterday's return gets 6% weight, two days ago gets 5.64%, etc. - Half-life: The point where cumulative weight reaches 50%. For λ = 0.94: half-life ≈ 11 days. For λ = 0.97: half-life ≈ 23 days. $$\text{Half-life} = \frac{-\ln(2)}{\ln(\lambda)}$$ Worked example: > SPX daily returns for last 5 days: +0.5%, -1.2%, +0.3%, -2.8%, +0.1% > Using λ = 0.94, the most recent return (-2.8%) gets much more weight than +0.5% from 5 days ago. > After the -2.8% day, EWMA vol jumps significantly — the model "reacts" to the shock quickly. When EWMA fails: - It has no concept of "long-run average vol" — after a quiet period, vol just decays toward zero, which is unrealistic. - It reacts to large moves but doesn't mean-revert to a sensible level after the shock fades.

$$\sigma^2 t = \omega + \alpha \cdot r^2 {t-1} + \beta \cdot \sigma^2 {t-1}$$ Three parameters with specific meanings: Parameter Role Typical value (equities) Intuition --- --- --- --- ω (omega) Constant — anchors long-run variance 0.000002 - 0.00001 "Where vol settles in the long run" α (alpha) Shock reaction — how much yesterday's return matters 0.05 - 0.15 "How jumpy is the model?" β (beta) Persistence — how slowly vol decays 0.80 - 0.95 "How sticky is high vol?" Key relationships: $$\text{Long-run variance} = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha - \beta}$$ $$\text{Persistence} = \alpha + \beta$$ - If α + β = 0.99 → vol shocks are EXTREMELY persistent (half-life ≈ 69 days) - If α + β = 0.95 → vol shocks still persistent but decay faster (half-life ≈ 14 days) - If α + β ≥ 1.0 → IGARCH (integrated GARCH) — vol shocks NEVER decay. This means there's no long-run mean. Be very suspicious. $$\text{Half-life of vol shock} = \frac{-\ln(2)}{\ln(\alpha + \beta)}$$ Worked example — interpreting GARCH output: > Quant presents: ω = 0.000005, α = 0.08, β = 0.90 > > - Persistence = 0.08 + 0.90 = 0.98 (very persistent) > - Half-life = -ln(2) / ln(0.98) = 34 days (vol shock takes 34 days to halve) > - Long-run annualized vol = √(0.000005 / (1 - 0.98) × 252) = √(0.000005 / 0.02 × 252) = √(0.063) = 25.1% > > Your questions: > 1. "Persistence of 0.98 means a vol spike takes over a month to halve. Is that consistent with what we see in our asset class?" > 2. "Long-run vol of 25% — is that used for anything? If our VaR lookback is shorter than 34 days, we're not capturing the mean-reversion effect."

Standard GARCH treats positive and negative shocks equally. But in reality, negative returns increase vol MORE than positive returns of the same magnitude (the "leverage effect"): $$\sigma^2 t = \omega + (\alpha + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1} {r {t-1}<0}) \cdot r^2 {t-1} + \beta \cdot \sigma^2 {t-1}$$ where $\gamma$ is the asymmetry parameter and $\mathbb{1} {r {t-1}<0}$ is an indicator that equals 1 when the previous return was negative. - If γ > 0: negative returns increase vol more than positive returns → leverage effect captured - Typical equity γ: 0.05-0.15 (down moves have 50-100% more vol impact than up moves) Why the RM cares: If you use symmetric GARCH in an equity portfolio, you'll UNDERSTATE vol after sell-offs and OVERSTATE vol after rallies. Your post-crash VaR will be too low when it matters most.

Measure Source Time horizon Contains --- --- --- --- Realized vol Historical returns Backward-looking What DID happen Implied vol Option prices Forward-looking Market's EXPECTATION of what will happen The Variance Risk Premium (VRP): $$\text{VRP} = \text{Implied Vol} - \text{Realized Vol}$$ - VRP is almost always positive: implied > realized (options are "expensive" on average) - Typical equity VRP: 2-5 vol points - When VRP collapses or goes negative → the market is pricing in a vol INCREASE → warning sign VRP as a risk indicator: VRP level Signal Action --- --- --- > 5 vol pts Market pricing in much more vol than is occurring Short-vol strategies attractive but complacency possible 2-5 vol pts Normal range Standard risk posture 0-2 vol pts Market barely pricing in premium Elevated caution — market may be complacent < 0 (negative) Realized > implied → market in crisis and STILL pricing more to come Maximum caution — vol selling is suicidal ---

What it does: Takes N correlated variables and finds a smaller set of uncorrelated factors (principal components) that explain most of the total variation. The canonical example — US yield curve: The yield curve has 10+ points (1Y, 2Y, 3Y, 5Y, 7Y, 10Y, 20Y, 30Y), but PCA reveals that ~95% of all yield curve movement is explained by just 3 factors: Component % Variance explained Interpretation Sensitivity --- --- --- --- PC1 ~80% Level — entire curve shifts up/down All maturities load positively, roughly equally PC2 ~10% Slope — short end vs. long end Short maturities positive, long negative (or vice versa) PC3 ~5% Curvature — belly vs. wings 2Y and 30Y positive, 5-10Y negative PC4-10 ~5% combined Noise / idiosyncratic Unstable — changes with sample Worked example — interpreting PCA for risk: > Your rates portfolio has the following exposures: > - PC1 (level) DV01: +$50K/bp → long duration, hurt by rising rates > - PC2 (slope) DV01: -$200K/bp → positioned for curve flattening > - PC3 (curvature) DV01: +$30K/bp → butterfly trade, long wings > > Interpretation: This portfolio is 4x more sensitive to the slope than to the level. The dominant risk isn't "rates going up" — it's "curve steepening." Your stress test should focus on steepener scenarios, not parallel shifts. > > The standard mistake: Running a +100bp parallel shift stress test and concluding the portfolio is fine. A +50bp steepener would do much more damage. PCA stability warning: Factor loadings are estimated from historical data and can CHANGE over time. In particular: - During QE, PC1 explained less variance (central bank suppressed level moves) and PC2 explained more - During rate-hiking cycles, PC1 dominance increases - If you calibrated PCA during QE and use it during hiking, your risk decomposition is wrong

Standard factors (Fama-French + extensions): Factor What it captures Typical premium When it fails --- --- --- --- Market (β) Exposure to broad equity market 5-7% p.a. Always "on" but variable Size (SMB) Small vs. large cap 1-3% p.a. Negative in risk-off (small cap underperforms) Value (HML) Cheap vs. expensive stocks 2-4% p.a. historically Can underperform for YEARS (2017-2020) Momentum (WML) Past winners vs. past losers 5-8% p.a. Violent reversals (momentum crashes) Quality (RMW) Profitable vs. unprofitable 3-5% p.a. Most stable Low vol (BAB) Low-beta vs. high-beta 3-6% p.a. Underperforms in aggressive rallies Why factor decomposition matters for the RM: If a PM claims to generate alpha but their returns are 90% explained by factor exposures, they're not adding value — they're just loading on systematic factors. You could replicate that exposure more cheaply with factor ETFs. $$R p = \alpha + \beta 1 F 1 + \beta 2 F 2 + ... + \beta k F k + \epsilon$$ The test: Run a multi-factor regression on PM returns. If the adjusted $R^2 > 0.80$ and $\alpha$ is not statistically significant, the PM is a closet beta manager. Cross-PM factor overlap — the portfolio-level risk: > PM A: long tech momentum, factor exposure = Market +1.2, Momentum +0.8, Size -0.3 > PM B: long biotech momentum, factor exposure = Market +1.0, Momentum +0.9, Size -0.5 > > These look like different strategies (tech vs. biotech) but have nearly identical factor exposures. A momentum crash would hit BOTH PMs simultaneously. The risk isn't visible at the name level — only at the factor level. RM question: "Show me the factor attribution of each PM's YTD P&L. How much of the fund's total return is explained by the market factor alone?"

After removing factor exposures, the unexplained portion ($\epsilon$) is idiosyncratic risk — the risk that IS stock-specific alpha. This is what you WANT in a multi-PM structure. $$\text{Idiosyncratic risk ratio} = \frac{\text{Var}(\epsilon)}{\text{Var}(R p)}$$ - Ratio > 50% → PM is genuinely picking stocks, not loading on factors (good for alpha generation) - Ratio < 20% → PM is essentially a leveraged index fund with factor tilts (bad for alpha, can be replicated cheaply) ---

Model risk is the risk that a model's output leads to poor decisions because the model is wrong, misused, or misinterpreted. SR 11-7 (Fed's model risk guidance) defines three sources: Source Description Example --- --- --- 1. Model error Model is fundamentally wrong Gaussian copula for CDO tranches (mispriced tail dependence) 2. Implementation error Model is right but coded wrong Excel error in London Whale VaR calculation (copy-paste using SUM instead of AVERAGE) 3. Misuse Model output used for wrong purpose Using a pricing model for risk measurement (different objectives)

section: The Eight Deadly Sins of Model Risk
content: Sin Description Detection --- --- --- --- 1 Gaussian assumption Assuming normal returns when reality has fat tails Kurtosis test; Q-Q plot 2 Stable correlations Using one correlation matrix for all regimes Compare crisis vs. calm correlations 3 ∞ liquidity Model assumes positions can be liquidated instantly Compare position to ADV; model liquidation cost 4 Stationary parameters Using fixed model params during regime changes Rolling calibration; breakpoint tests 5 Overfitting Too many parameters relative to observations In-sample vs. out-of-sample R² comparison 6 Survivorship bias Dataset only includes entities that survived Check: does data include delisted/defaulted entities? 7 Look-ahead bias Using information not available at decision time Ensure all inputs are lagged appropriately 8 Mark-to-model Pricing illiquid positions with favorable internal models Independent price verification (IPV) against external sources

section: VaR Model Limitations — The Comprehensive List
content: Limitation Why it matters Mitigation --- --- --- Assumes portfolio is static Position changes during the holding period Use "dynamic VaR" or impose trading restrictions during stressed periods Single confidence level (99%) Tells you nothing about the 1% tail Supplement with Expected Shortfall (CVaR) which averages the tail Distribution assumption Normal underestimates tails Use historical simulation or Student-t VaR Lookback period sensitivity Short window = reactive to recent regime; long window = includes stale data Use multiple lookback windows and compare Aggregation method Adding VaR across desks assumes correlation structure Report both diversified and undiversified VaR Intraday risk not captured VaR is end-of-day; intraday losses can exceed VaR Intraday limit checks; real-time risk monitoring Expected Shortfall (ES / CVaR) — the VaR improvement: $$\text{ES} \alpha = E L \mid L > \text{VaR} \alpha $$ ES answers: "Given that we're in the worst 1% of outcomes, what's the AVERAGE loss?" This captures the shape of the tail, not just the boundary. Metric VaR (99%) ES (99%) --- --- --- Question answered "Worst loss exceeded 1% of the time" "Average loss in the worst 1%" Normal distribution 2.33σ 2.67σ Fat-tailed distribution 2.33σ (same → WRONG) 3.5-5σ+ (much higher → CAPTURES tail) Regulatory status Basel II.5 (historical) Basel III FRTB (current standard) RM insight: If VaR and ES give similar numbers (ratio < 1.2), the tail is thin (close to normal). If ES >> VaR (ratio > 1.5), the tail is fat and VaR is dangerously understating extreme risk.

The basic test: If you have a 99% 1-day VaR, you should breach it ~2.5 times per year (250 trading days × 1% = 2.5 expected breaches). Basel traffic light system: Zone Breaches (250-day window) Implication Regulatory response --- --- --- --- 🟢 Green 0-4 Model is acceptable No action 🟡 Yellow 5-9 Model questionable Capital multiplier increases 🔴 Red 10+ Model is broken Mandatory model revision + penalty Kupiec test (unconditional coverage): Tests whether the breach rate matches the expected probability. Under H₀ (model is correct): $$\text{LR} \text{uc} = -2 \ln\!\!\left \frac{(1-p)^{T-N} \cdot p^N}{(1-\hat{p})^{T-N} \cdot \hat{p}^N}\right \sim \chi^2 1$$ where $p$ = expected breach rate (0.01 for 99% VaR), $\hat{p}$ = observed breach rate = $N/T$. Christoffersen test (conditional coverage): Tests not just frequency but CLUSTERING. If all 4 breaches happen in one week, the model is failing to capture vol clustering. Independence of breaches matters as much as their number. Practical interpretation: Observation What it means RM action --- --- --- 0-1 breaches in 250 days Model may be too conservative Investigate — are limits higher than they need to be? Capital cost? 2-4 breaches Model working as designed Maintain 5-9 breaches Model underestimates risk Increase capital charge; review model assumptions 10+ breaches Model is fundamentally wrong Stop using it; revert to simpler model while rebuilding Breaches all clustered in one period Model doesn't capture regime changes Add regime-switching or stressed VaR overlay

Parameter perturbation: Change each model input by ±10%, ±25%. If the output changes by >50% for a 10% input change, the model is FRAGILE — this parameter dominates and must be estimated precisely. Boundary testing: Test at extreme values. What happens when: - Correlation → +1 or -1? - Vol → 3x current level? - Time horizon → 10x? - Liquidity → 0 (can't trade)? If the model gives nonsensical output at boundaries, it has structural problems. ---

Definition: A model that fits the historical data perfectly but fails on new data. It learned the noise, not the signal. The bias-variance tradeoff: $$\text{Total Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$ - Underfitting (high bias): Model is too simple → misses real patterns - Overfitting (high variance): Model is too complex → captures noise → fails out-of-sample - Sweet spot : Enough complexity to capture real structure, not so much that it fits noise How to detect overfitting: Signal Safe zone Danger zone --- --- --- Parameters vs. observations 1 param per 20+ observations 1 param per < 5 observations In-sample R² 0.3-0.6 > 0.90 (suspiciously good) Out-of-sample R² Within 80% of in-sample < 50% of in-sample (or negative!) Performance on rolling walk-forward Stable Degrades over time Sensitivity to start date Robust to ±30 days Changes dramatically Cross-validation for the RM: The quant should show walk-forward (out-of-sample) performance , not just backtested P&L: 1. Train model on data up to 2020 2. Test on 2021 (out-of-sample) 3. Retrain on data up to 2021 4. Test on 2022 (out-of-sample) 5. Report the OUT-OF-SAMPLE performance across all test periods If they only show the full-sample backtest, they haven't proven the model works.

section: Alpha Decay — Strategies Have a Shelf Life
content: Definition: A trading signal that worked historically but gradually loses effectiveness over time. Why alpha decays: 1. Crowding: Other funds discover the same signal → arbitrage competes it away 2. Structural change: Market microstructure evolves (e.g., electronic trading killed floor-based signals) 3. Regime shift: The economic environment that generated the signal changes 4. Partially spurious: Part of the historical performance was luck, not skill How to measure alpha decay: $$\text{IC}(t) = \text{Correlation}(\text{signal} t, \text{return} {t+1})$$ The Information Coefficient (IC) measures how well the signal predicts future returns. Plotting IC over rolling windows reveals decay: IC level Signal quality Action --- --- --- > 0.05 Strong Allocate with conviction 0.02-0.05 Moderate Allocate but monitor closely 0.01-0.02 Weak Review sizing; signal may not justify trading costs < 0.01 Dead Signal has decayed below noise; consider retiring RM implications of alpha decay: - Monitor rolling Sharpe ratio for each PM/strategy quarterly. If trending down, alpha may be decaying. - A PM whose Sharpe has declined from 2.0 → 0.8 over 3 years hasn't just had "bad luck" — the strategy is losing edge. - Backtested Sharpe is ALWAYS higher than live Sharpe. Rule of thumb: expect live Sharpe ≈ 50-70% of backtested Sharpe.

Phase Characteristics RM posture --- --- --- 1. Discovery New signal found, untested, high IC Small allocation; strict risk limits 2. Exploitation Signal generating returns, limited crowding Full allocation; standard risk limits 3. Crowding Other funds trading same signal, returns compressed Reduce allocation; tighten stops 4. Decay IC declining, costs rising relative to alpha Minimal allocation; sunset review 5. Death IC ≈ 0, negative after costs Retire strategy entirely RM question: "Where are each of our strategies in their lifecycle? What's the IC trend over the last 2 years? Are we allocating to any phase-4 strategies?" ---

Monte Carlo generates thousands of possible future scenarios by: 1. Sampling random returns from a specified distribution 2. Simulating portfolio P&L under each scenario 3. Building a distribution of outcomes When Monte Carlo > analytical VaR: - Non-linear portfolios (options): analytical VaR uses delta-normal approximation and misses gamma/convexity - Path-dependent risk: margin calls, stop-losses, rebalancing triggers - Complex correlations: copula-based dependencies that can't be captured in a covariance matrix - Stress scenarios: custom-designed shocks beyond historical data

For a portfolio with significant gamma, the distribution of P&L is NOT normal even if the underlying returns are: $$\text{P\&L} \approx \Delta \cdot dS + \frac{1}{2} \Gamma \cdot (dS)^2 + \Theta \cdot dt + \mathcal{V} \cdot d\sigma$$ The $\Gamma \cdot (dS)^2$ term creates ASYMMETRY: - Long gamma: large moves (either direction) generate positive P&L → positively skewed P&L - Short gamma: large moves generate losses → negatively skewed P&L Analytical VaR misses this entirely. Monte Carlo captures it naturally by simulating the actual non-linear payoff. Worked example: > Portfolio: short 1000 SPX put options, delta-hedged. > - Delta-normal VaR (99%, 1-day): $2.3M (only uses delta) > - Monte Carlo VaR (99%, 1-day, 10,000 paths): $4.8M (captures gamma acceleration on large down moves) > - VaR is understated by 2x when gamma is significant

Pitfall Why it happens Fix --- --- --- Not enough simulations Convergence requires 10K-100K+ paths Run convergence test: does VaR change by >1% when doubling paths? Wrong distribution inputs Garbage in → garbage out Validate input distribution against actual data (Q-Q plots, kurtosis) Ignoring correlation structure Simulating each asset independently Use correlated sampling (Cholesky decomposition of correlation matrix) False precision Reporting "$4,832,419 VaR" from MC with 10K paths Round to appropriate precision; report confidence intervals Seed dependence Different random seeds → different answers Use variance reduction (antithetic variates, control variates) ---
VaR and GARCH model the BODY of the distribution. EVT models the TAIL — the 1% (or 0.1%) of extreme events that cause catastrophic losses. This is where risk management lives.

section: 1. Block Maxima → Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution
content: Divide your data into blocks (e.g., months). The maximum loss per block follows a GEV distribution: $$F \text{GEV}(x) = \exp\left\{-\left 1 + \xi \left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\right ^{-1/\xi}\right\}$$ The shape parameter ξ (xi) controls the tail behavior: - ξ > 0: Fréchet → heavy tail (typical for financial returns — ξ ≈ 0.2-0.4) - ξ = 0: Gumbel → exponential tail (lighter) - ξ < 0: Weibull → bounded tail (finite maximum loss — unrealistic for markets)

section: 2. Peaks Over Threshold (POT) → Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD)
content: Instead of block maxima, model all observations exceeding a high threshold $u$: $$P(X - u \leq y \mid X > u) = G {\xi,\sigma}(y) = 1 - \left(1 + \frac{\xi y}{\sigma}\right)^{-1/\xi}$$ This is the more useful approach for risk management because: - You use ALL extreme observations, not just one per block - You can estimate the probability of events you've NEVER seen - It directly gives you high-quantile VaR and ES Practical application: > Using GPD fitted to daily losses beyond the 95th percentile: > - 99.0% VaR (normal model): $15M > - 99.0% VaR (GPD model): $22M (47% higher) > - 99.9% VaR (normal model): $22M > - 99.9% VaR (GPD model): $45M (105% higher!) > > The further into the tail you go, the more the normal model underestimates. RM question: "For our tail risk estimates, are we using normal quantiles or EVT quantiles? What's the estimated ξ for our portfolio's loss distribution?" ---

Markets don't operate in one state — they switch between regimes with different return and vol characteristics: Regime Typical vol Typical return Correlation behavior --- --- --- --- Risk-on (calm) Low (VIX 12-18) Positive drift Low to moderate cross-asset Risk-off (stress) High (VIX 20-35) Negative Correlations spike → diversification fails Crisis (panic) Very high (VIX 35+) Strongly negative ρ → 1 for all risky assets Recovery Declining from elevated Positive drift, high dispersion Correlations normalize but unevenly The problem: A model calibrated to regime 1 is dangerously wrong in regime 3. A model calibrated across all regimes is wrong in all of them (averaging conflicting regimes).

An HMM assumes: 1. Markets are in one of K hidden "states" (e.g., calm or stressed) 2. Each state has its own mean (μ) and variance (σ²) 3. Transitions between states follow a probability matrix Typical 2-state HMM for equities: Parameter State 1 (calm) State 2 (stressed) --- --- --- Daily mean return (μ) +0.04% -0.10% Daily vol (σ) 0.8% 2.5% Probability of STAYING in state 98% 90% Probability of SWITCHING 2% 10% Implications: - Average calm period duration = 1 / (1 - 0.98) = 50 days - Average stress period duration = 1 / (1 - 0.90) = 10 days - Stress vol is 3x calm vol — VaR should be 3x higher but most models use an AVERAGE The "regime-conditional VaR" concept: $$\text{VaR} {\text{true}} = P(\text{calm}) \times \text{VaR} {\text{calm}} + P(\text{stress}) \times \text{VaR} {\text{stress}}$$ If you're 80% likely to be in the calm regime: > VaR (averaging) = 0.8 × $5M + 0.2 × $15M = $7M > > But if you're ACTUALLY in the stress regime (you just don't know it yet): > True VaR = $15M — more than double the blended estimate RM question: "What regime does the model think we're in right now? What's the probability we've transitioned to a stress regime? How different is VaR conditional on stress vs. calm?"

You don't need a full HMM to detect regimes. Practical indicators: Indicator Calm regime Transitioning Stress regime --- --- --- --- VIX level < 18 18-25 > 25 VIX term structure Contango (VIX < VIX3M) Flat Backwardation (VIX > VIX3M) HY credit spread < 350bp 350-500bp > 500bp Realized/implied vol ratio < 0.8 0.8-1.0 > 1.0 (realized exceeding implied) Cross-asset correlation < 0.3 0.3-0.6 > 0.6 Equity factor dispersion High Declining Low (everything moving together) ---

section: Where ML Adds Value in Risk Management
content: Application ML method Why ML > traditional Watch out for --- --- --- --- Anomaly detection Isolation Forest, Autoencoders Catches unusual trading patterns without rules False positives; needs human review Regime classification Random Forest, XGBoost on regime indicators Combines many indicators non-linearly Overfitting to historical regime transitions Default prediction Logistic regression → Gradient boosted trees Better rank-ordering of credit risk Black-box: regulators want explainability Fraud / rogue trading Neural networks on transaction patterns Detects subtle deviations from normal behavior Privacy; data requirements Stress test scenario generation GANs, VAE Generate plausible scenarios beyond historical Mode collapse; unrealistic scenarios NLP on news flow Transformer models Real-time sentiment from news/filings Hallucination; false signals from noise

When a quant proposes using ML in a risk context, ask: Question Why it matters --- --- "What's the out-of-sample accuracy? Show me walk-forward performance." In-sample accuracy means nothing for ML; overfitting is the default failure mode "How do you prevent overfitting?" Must answer: regularization (L1/L2), dropout, cross-validation, early stopping "Can you explain individual predictions?" Regulators and management need explainability; SHAP values help "What happens in a regime we haven't seen?" ML has NO theory → it can't extrapolate to novel regimes "What's the training data lookback? Does it include crisis periods?" If trained on 2015-2019 (calm), it won't work in 2020 (crisis) "How often is the model retrained?" Stale ML models decay faster than parametric models

section: ML vs. Traditional — When to Use What
content: Situation Use traditional Use ML --- --- --- Linear risk factors ✓ Overkill Well-understood dynamics (rates, FX) ✓ Maybe supplements Explainability required ✓ Only with SHAP/LIME Non-linear payoffs (options) Monte Carlo + Greeks ✓ (pricing approximation) Anomaly detection Rule-based (crude) ✓ (far superior) Regime detection HMM (interpretable) ✓ (more flexible but black-box) Many features, unknown interactions Hard to specify ✓ (XGBoost, neural nets) Regulatory reporting ✓ (required) Not yet accepted by most regulators ---
When a quant presents ANY model, analysis, or backtested result, systematically probe:

section: 1. ASSUMPTIONS — "What could be wrong with the foundation?"
content: - "What distribution does this assume?" → If normal, challenge with kurtosis data - "What correlation structure?" → If constant, challenge with regime-dependent evidence - "What about liquidity?" → If infinite liquidity assumed, challenge with position-to-ADV ratio - "How sensitive is this to the lookback window?" → If very sensitive, model is fragile

- "Does the data include crisis X ?" → If not, the model hasn't been stress-tested naturally - "Survivorship bias?" → Data that only includes winners (e.g., current stocks but not delisted ones) - "Look-ahead bias?" → Using information that wasn't available in real-time - "How are missing data and outliers handled?" → Dropping outliers = removing the most important data for risk

- "In-sample vs. out-of-sample performance?" → If big gap, model is overfit - "VaR backtest results? Breach count and clustering?" → Traffic light status - "Walk-forward test?" → Retrain and test on unseen data iteratively

- "What scenario would make you lose confidence in this model?" — This is the BEST single question - "What regime change would invalidate this?" → Every model has a regime where it fails - "Has this been stress-tested against tail scenario from case studies ?"

Sometimes the RM needs to OVERRIDE the model. This requires both skill and institutional authority: Condition Model says RM should say Authority needed --- --- --- --- VaR dropped 40% but positions unchanged "Risk decreased" "Model changed or broke; investigate before acting on lower VaR" CRO/IC sign-off on model changes Model trained on calm period, now in stress "Low risk" "Model is stale; use stressed VaR or add a buffer" Risk committee override Model shows no concentration risk "Diversified" "But 3 PMs have the same factor exposure" Factor-level overlay analysis Backtest looks perfect, Sharpe = 3.0 "Great strategy" "Sharpe 3.0 in backtest → probably 1.0-1.5 live. Overfitting likely." Pushback in strategy approval Monte Carlo VaR = $2M, tail event = $20M "99% VaR is $2M" "The tail risk is 10x the VaR. We need ES or tail scenarios, not just VaR" Supplementary risk metrics reporting ---

section: Numbers That Should Make You Suspicious
content: Red flag What it suggests Investigate --- --- --- Sharpe ratio > 2.5 in backtest Overfitting or look-ahead bias Out-of-sample test; transaction cost sensitivity Max drawdown < 5% for a strategy running 5+ years Survivorship bias or cherry-picked start date Full-period analysis; include all periods Correlation = 0 between "hedged" positions Model assumes hedge is perfect; it isn't Basis risk; what's the worst the hedge has slipped? VaR breaches exactly at the boundary Someone is adjusting marks to avoid breach Check: are marks consistently one tick inside the limit? Any metric that halves without position change Model change or calculation error Audit the model change; look for formula errors Risk that looks constant despite market vol changes Model not using market-implied inputs Check: does the risk system react to VIX spikes? Alpha that's exactly inversely correlated with fund AUM Strategy capacity issue being masked Segregate performance by AUM band Perfect PCA explaining 99%+ of variance Likely using too few PCs or circular regression Check: are the inputs and outputs the same data?

Before diving into formulas, apply common sense: 1. "Does the return source make economic sense?" If a strategy profits from a pattern, WHY does that pattern exist? Who is on the other side? If no good answer → probably spurious. 2. "Is the trade too crowded?" If every quant at every conference is presenting the same signal, it's already arbitraged away. The alpha is in the DISCOVERY, not the execution. 3. "Would this survive transaction costs?" A signal with IC = 0.02 and annual turnover of 12x might be alpha-positive before costs and alpha-negative after costs. Always check net-of-cost performance. 4. "Is the data snooped?" If someone tested 500 signals and found 5 that "work" at 99% confidence, that's EXACTLY what you'd expect from random chance. Adjust for multiple testing (Bonferroni correction or similar). ---

section: Q: "What's the most important thing you understand about quantitative models?"
content: > "That models are maps, not territory. A VaR model that says our 99% daily loss is $5M is making three assumptions that are almost certainly wrong to some degree: that returns are drawn from a specific distribution, that correlations are stable, and that positions can be liquidated at current market prices. > > But that doesn't make the model useless — it makes it a starting point. The number $5M tells me something about relative risk: if it was $3M last week and $5M today, risk has increased ~67%, and I need to understand why. If three PMs all show decreasing VaR but the house VaR is increasing, there's a correlation shift I need to investigate. > > I think the RM's skill is using the model output as an information signal while knowing its specific failure modes. For example, VaR systematically understates risk during regime transitions and for portfolios with negative gamma. So I'd supplement VaR with stressed scenarios and Expected Shortfall, and flag any delta-hedged options books where gamma risk is being hidden by a delta-normal VaR approximation."

section: Q: "How would you evaluate whether a quant's model is trustworthy?"
content: > "I'd use a four-part framework: assumptions, data, validation, and failure modes. > > On assumptions: I'd ask what distribution they're using and whether it captures the fat tails in our actual returns. If they're using normal, I'd want to know the excess kurtosis — for most equity portfolios it's 3 to 10, which means the normal model underestimates 99th percentile events by 30 to 50 percent. > > On data: does the training period include crisis episodes? If a model was calibrated on 2015-2019, it's never seen a COVID-style shock or a rate hiking cycle. That's survivorship bias at the macro level. > > On validation: show me the out-of-sample performance. If in-sample R-squared is 0.9 and out-of-sample is 0.3, the model is overfit. For VaR models specifically, I'd check the backtest: how many breaches in the last 250 days? Are they clustered? > > The single best question I'd ask is 'what scenario would make you lose confidence in this model?' If they can't answer that, they don't understand their own model's limitations."

section: Q: "Explain GARCH to me and how you'd use it in risk management."
content: > "GARCH models how volatility evolves over time, capturing two empirical facts: first, volatility clusters — high-vol days tend to follow high-vol days — and second, volatility mean-reverts to a long-run level. > > The model has three parameters. Alpha controls how much yesterday's return shock affects today's vol estimate — a large alpha means the model reacts fast to new information. Beta controls persistence — how slowly the vol shock fades. And omega anchors the long-run variance level. The sum alpha plus beta tells you how persistent vol shocks are. If it's 0.98, a vol spike takes about 34 days to halve. > > In practice, I'd use GARCH output two ways. First, to set dynamic risk limits: when GARCH vol is 2x its long-run level, I'd want tighter position limits because the market is in a high-vol regime. Second, to improve VaR — instead of using a fixed volatility over the lookback window, I'd weight recent observations more heavily, which is exactly what GARCH does. > > The limitation I'd flag is that standard GARCH is symmetric — it treats a +3% day and a -3% day as having the same impact on vol. In reality, the leverage effect means down moves increase vol more than up moves. So for equity portfolios, I'd prefer GJR-GARCH or EGARCH, which capture this asymmetry." --- Related files: → W Math Stats Probability — deeper mathematical foundations → F Risk Measures — VaR, stress testing, and risk metrics → H PnL Attribution — using quantitative decomposition practically → U Case Studies — where quant models failed spectacularly → X Coding Data — implementing these concepts in Python

Round 3 is with a quant researcher. They won't expect you to derive GARCH from scratch — but they WILL expect you to: 1. Understand what the models do and what they assume 2. Know when those assumptions break 3. Ask intelligent questions about model output 4. Not be intimidated by quantitative concepts The RM's job isn't to build the models. It's to challenge them. ---

What it is: A time series is stationary if its statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation) don't change over time. Why it matters for risk: Almost every risk model assumes stationarity. VaR uses a historical window and assumes the future will look like the past. If the regime changes (inflation era → deflation) the model is wrong. How to spot non-stationarity: - Rolling mean is trending up or down - Rolling variance is expanding - "This used to work but hasn't in 6 months" Your question to the quant: "How sensitive is this model to the lookback window? What happens if we're in a regime change and the last 2 years aren't representative?"

What it is: Correlation of a time series with its own lagged values. If today's return predicts tomorrow's → autocorrelation exists. Why it matters: - Positive autocorrelation (momentum) → trends persist → trending markets - Negative autocorrelation (mean reversion) → moves reverse → range-bound markets - VaR assumes returns are independent (no autocorrelation). If momentum exists, multi-day VaR underestimates risk. The T+1 / T+10 problem: If returns are independent, 10-day VaR = 1-day VaR × √10. But if there's autocorrelation, this scaling is wrong. Positive autocorrelation → 10-day VaR > 1-day × √10 (trends amplify risk). This is the "square root of time" rule — it only works if returns are i.i.d.

What it is: Real market returns have more extreme events than a normal distribution predicts. The tails are "fatter." How fat? - Normal distribution: a 4-sigma daily move should happen once every ~126 years. - Reality: In equity markets, it happens roughly every 2-3 years. Why it matters: If your VaR uses a normal distribution, it systematically underestimates the probability of large losses. This is why historical simulation and stressed VaR exist — they don't assume normality. Your question: "Does this model assume normal returns? If not, what distribution? How do we capture tail risk?"

- Correlation: Two things tend to move together. Could be causal, could be coincidence, could be driven by a third factor. - In markets: Almost everything is correlated to "risk sentiment." Two stocks looking correlated might just both be correlated to SPX. The residual correlation (after removing market factor) is what matters. - Regime-dependent correlation: The most dangerous kind. Stocks and bonds are negatively correlated (stocks down, bonds up) in most environments. But in 2022 (inflation shock), both went down together. The hedge stops working exactly when you need it most. ---
- Simple: standard deviation of returns over N days. - Problem: treats a -5% move 60 days ago the same as one yesterday. But yesterday's move tells you more about current risk.

- Puts more weight on recent observations, less on old ones. - Controlled by lambda (λ): typical value ~0.94 for daily data. - λ = 0.94 means yesterday's observation gets 6% weight, the day before gets 0.94 × 6% = 5.64%, etc. - Used by RiskMetrics. Simple, fast, no calibration headaches. - Problem: only one parameter. Can't capture the full dynamics of vol.

- σ²ₜ = ω + α·r²ₜ₋₁ + β·σ²ₜ₋₁ - Three parameters: ω (long-run variance level), α (reaction to new information), β (persistence of old variance). - α + β close to 1 → volatility is highly persistent (vol clustering). - α large → vol reacts sharply to new shocks (fat-tailed vol). - Key insight: GARCH captures "volatility clustering" — high vol days tend to follow high vol days. This is real and important for risk. Your question to the quant: "What persistence parameter are you using? If α + β ≈ 0.99, you're saying vol shocks last essentially forever. Is that appropriate for this asset?"

- Implied vol: the market's forecast of future vol (extracted from option prices). - Realized vol: what actually happened. - Implied is almost always higher than realized (the "variance risk premium") → options are expensive on average. - When implied < realized → the market is complacent. This is a warning sign. ---

What it does: Takes N correlated variables and reduces them to a smaller number of uncorrelated factors that explain most of the variation. Example in rates: - US yield curve has 10+ maturities (1Y, 2Y, 3Y, 5Y, 7Y, 10Y, 20Y, 30Y...). - PCA says: ~95% of yield curve movement is explained by 3 factors: - PC1 (~80%): Level — the whole curve shifts up or down. - PC2 (~10%): Slope — short end moves opposite to long end. - PC3 (~5%): Curvature — belly moves opposite to wings. Why it matters for risk: Instead of tracking 10+ rate exposures, you can monitor 3 factor exposures. If your portfolio has zero PC1 exposure but huge PC2 exposure, you're making a steepener/flattener bet. Your question: "Can you show me the PCA decomposition of our portfolio? What percentage of risk is explained by the first 3 components?"

- Beyond PCA: Fama-French factors (market, size, value, momentum, quality), macro factors (rates, oil, dollar, VIX). - The point: understand what's DRIVING the portfolio's returns. Is it stock picking (alpha) or factor exposure (beta)? - If a PM's P&L is 90% explained by factor exposure, they're not adding much alpha — they're just loading on a factor you could get more cheaply via ETFs/futures. ---

The basic test: If you have a 99% 1-day VaR, you should breach it ~2.5 times per year (250 trading days × 1% = 2.5 expected breaches). Kupiec Test (the one to know): - If you observe more breaches than expected → model UNDERSTATES risk (bad). - If you observe fewer breaches → model OVERSTATES risk (also bad — you're leaving money on the table by being too conservative). - The test has specific confidence intervals.
