With 250 observations and 99% VaR: - 0-1 breaches: model may be too conservative - 2-4 breaches: model is fine - 5+ breaches: model underestimates risk — needs review Christoffersen Test: Not just "how many breaches?" but "are they clustered?" If all 4 breaches happen in the same week, the model is wrong about volatility dynamics (it doesn't capture vol clustering).

What it is: A model that fits the historical data perfectly but fails on new data. It learned the noise, not the signal. How to spot it: - Model has 20 parameters for 100 observations → almost certainly overfit - In-sample R² = 0.95, out-of-sample R² = 0.15 → overfit - Model performance degrades sharply with any small change to lookback window → overfit Your question: "What's the out-of-sample performance? How many parameters vs. observations?"

What it is: A trading signal that worked historically but gradually loses effectiveness over time. Why it happens: - Other people discover the same signal - Market structure changes - The signal was partly spurious to begin with Risk implication: If a PM's strategy relies on a signal with decaying alpha, future risk-adjusted returns will be worse than backtested returns. The RM needs to monitor strategy Sharpe ratios over time. ---

section: The RM's Cheat Sheet for Questions
content: When a quant presents a model or analysis, use this framework:
- "What distribution does this assume?" - "How sensitive is this to the lookback window?" - "What correlation structure does this use?"
- "How far back does the data go? Does it include crisis X ?" - "Any survivorship bias in the dataset?" - "How do you handle missing data?"
- "How does this backtest? How many VaR breaches?" - "What's the out-of-sample performance?" - "Has this been stress-tested against scenario ?"

- "Where does this model break down?" - "What regime change would invalidate this?" - "What does this model NOT capture?" The best question in any model review: "What would make you lose confidence in this model?" ---

section: PART 6: WHAT TO READ BEFORE ROUND 3
content: You don't need to derive anything. You need to: 1. Define each concept in one sentence 2. Explain why it matters for risk management 3. Know the limitations / when it breaks 4. Ask one intelligent question about it Concept One-sentence definition When it breaks --- --- --- Stationarity Statistical properties don't change over time Regime changes (COVID, rate hike cycles) GARCH Volatility model that captures clustering and mean reversion Extreme jumps (it models continuous vol, not gaps) PCA Dimension reduction to find uncorrelated drivers When factor structure itself changes VaR backtesting Testing if your model's predicted loss frequency matches reality Short samples, clustered breaches Fat tails More extreme events than normal distribution predicts Always present — the question is degree Correlation Co-movement of assets Regime-dependent — flips in crisis Overfitting Model learned noise, not signal Model with too many parameters relative to data --- Related files: → 11 Technical Fundamentals — math & stats review → 07 Risk Reference — VaR, Greeks, risk measures → H PnL Attribution — using quantitative decomposition practically → V Case Studies — where quant models failed spectacularly

This file covers the mathematical toolkit you need for a quant-adjacent risk role. You won't be asked to prove theorems, but you WILL be expected to: 1. Apply these concepts to real risk problems (not just define them) 2. Interpret quantitative output correctly (what does a p-value of 0.03 MEAN for this PM?) 3. Spot errors when someone misuses a concept (using Sharpe without adjusting for skew, etc.) 4. Do mental math quickly and confidently under interview pressure This file is the FOUNDATION. V Quant Mindset is the APPLICATION to risk management. ---

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ Properties: Symmetric, bell-shaped, fully described by mean (μ) and variance (σ²). Sum of independent normals is normal. Critical quantiles to MEMORIZE: z-score Tail probability VaR use Finance meaning --- --- --- --- 1.00 15.87% one-tail — "1-sigma move" 1.28 10% one-tail 90% VaR — 1.645 5% one-tail 95% VaR "Within-day stress" 1.96 2.5% one-tail — 95% CI boundary (two-tail) 2.326 1% one-tail 99% VaR Standard regulatory VaR 2.576 0.5% one-tail 99.5% — 3.09 0.1% one-tail 99.9% "Catastrophic tail" Sigma ranges: Range Probability within Practical meaning --- --- --- ±1σ 68.27% "About two-thirds of trading days" ±2σ 95.45% "Once a month event if breached" ±3σ 99.73% "Should see ~1 per year in a daily context" ±4σ 99.994% "Normal says every 126 years; reality ≈ every 2-3 years" Where normal works: Portfolio returns (CLT), short-horizon log returns for liquid assets, Monte Carlo inputs (as a starting point). Where normal FAILS: Individual equity returns (fat tails), crisis returns (regime shift), options P&L (non-linear), credit losses (zero-one default events).

If $\ln(P T / P 0) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $P T$ is log-normally distributed. Why prices are modeled as log-normal: - Prices can't go below zero (mostly — oil in April 2020 proved exceptions exist) - Returns are multiplicative: $P T = P 0 \cdot e^{r}$, where $r \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ - Log-normal is right-skewed: large gains possible, losses bounded at -100% Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM): $$dS = \mu S \, dt + \sigma S \, dW$$ This is the foundation of Black-Scholes. It says price changes are proportional to price level (percentage returns, not dollar returns, are the natural unit). Key relationship (continuous compounding): $$S T = S 0 \cdot \exp\left (\mu - \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2)T + \sigma\sqrt{T} \cdot Z\right $$ The $-\frac{1}{2}\sigma^2$ correction (the "convexity adjustment") is why the expected future price is NOT $S 0 \cdot e^{\mu T}$ — volatility drags returns down. This matters for long-horizon risk calculations. Worked example — volatility drag: > Stock has μ = 10% annual return, σ = 30%. > - Arithmetic mean return: 10% > - Expected geometric return: 10% - ½ × (30%)² = 10% - 4.5% = 5.5% > - Over 10 years: $100 × e^(0.055 × 10) = $173 (geometric) > - Naively: $100 × e^(0.10 × 10) = $272 (arithmetic — WRONG for compounded returns) > - Volatility drag costs 37% over a decade at 30% vol

When to use it: Fat-tailed data or small samples where normal is inappropriate. Degrees of freedom (ν) Tail behavior Finance application --- --- --- ν = 3 Very heavy tails; no finite kurtosis Extreme asset classes (crypto, commodities) ν = 5 Heavy tails; finite kurtosis = 9 Typical equity returns ν = 10 Moderate tails; kurtosis = 4 FX, bond returns ν = 30 Approaching normal Diversified portfolio returns ν → ∞ Converges to normal Textbook (unrealistic) Practical use: When fitting a VaR model, replace the normal with Student-t. The 99th percentile of a t(5) distribution is ~3.36σ vs. 2.33σ for normal — that's a 44% higher VaR estimate. This is often more realistic.

$$f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$ - Mean: $1/\lambda$ - Memoryless property: $P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)$ Finance application — inter-event times: If major market dislocations arrive at rate λ = 0.5 per year: - Expected time between events: 1/0.5 = 2 years - P(event in next 6 months): $1 - e^{-0.5 \times 0.5}$ = 1 - 0.779 = 22.1% - P(no event for 3 years): $e^{-0.5 \times 3}$ = 22.3%

$$P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$$ Models count of events in a fixed period. Mean = variance = λ. Finance application — VaR breach counting: > If the VaR model is correct at 99%, expected breaches in 250 days: λ = 2.5. > - P(0 breaches) = e^(-2.5) = 8.2% (model may be too conservative) > - P(exactly 3 breaches) = (2.5³ × e^(-2.5)) / 3! = 21.4% (normal) > - P(≥8 breaches) = 1 - P(X ≤ 7) = 0.9% (model probably broken)

Bernoulli: Single trial with probability p. Mean = p, Variance = p(1-p). Binomial: n independent Bernoulli trials. $P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ Finance application — drawdown probability: > PM has 55% win rate on daily trades. In a 20-day month: > - P(15+ winning days) = $\sum {k=15}^{20} \binom{20}{k} (0.55)^k (0.45)^{20-k}$ = 6.7% > - P(≤7 winning days) = $\sum {k=0}^{7} \binom{20}{k} (0.55)^k (0.45)^{20-k}$ = 5.2% (bad month) > - P(losing streak of 5+) requires geometric/Markov analysis — surprisingly common even at 55% win rate ---

section: Bayes' Theorem — The Most Important Formula in Risk
content: $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$ In words: Posterior = (Likelihood × Prior) / Evidence

section: Classic Interview Problem — The Disease Test
content: A disease affects 1 in 10,000 people. A test is 99% accurate (sensitivity AND specificity). You test positive. Probability you have the disease? Term Value Meaning --- --- --- P(disease) = prior 0.0001 Base rate is very low P(+\ disease) = sensitivity 0.99 Test catches 99% of true cases P(+\ no disease) = 1 - specificity 0.01 Test has 1% false positive rate P(+) = evidence 0.99 × 0.0001 + 0.01 × 0.9999 = 0.010098 Total rate of positive tests $$P(\text{disease} \mid +) = \frac{0.99 \times 0.0001}{0.010098} = 0.0098 \approx \textbf{0.98\%}$$ Answer: ~1%, NOT 99%. The base rate dominates. A "99% accurate" test with a rare event mostly produces false positives.

This is DIRECTLY applicable to risk: when a PM has a bad month, how much should you update your belief about their skill? Worked example — PM evaluation: > - Prior: You believe there's a 70% chance this PM is skilled (positive alpha). > - A skilled PM has a bad month (≥2σ drawdown) 5% of the time. > - An unskilled PM has a bad month 15% of the time. > > After observing a bad month: > $$P(\text{skilled} \mid \text{bad month}) = \frac{0.05 \times 0.70}{0.05 \times 0.70 + 0.15 \times 0.30}$$ > $$= \frac{0.035}{0.035 + 0.045} = \frac{0.035}{0.08} = \textbf{43.75\%}$$ > > One bad month drops your confidence from 70% to 44%. Two bad months: > $$P(\text{skilled} \mid 2\text{ bad months}) = \frac{0.05 \times 0.4375}{0.05 \times 0.4375 + 0.15 \times 0.5625} = \frac{0.0219}{0.0219 + 0.0844} = \textbf{20.6\%}$$ > > After two 2σ drawdowns, you're 80% confident this PM is unskilled. Act accordingly.

Situation Prior Evidence Updated belief --- --- --- --- PM reports +$5M day "PM is average" Unusually large profit Slightly more confident PM is skilled VaR breached 3 times this month "Model is calibrated" 3x the expected rate Model likely mis-specified; investigate Stress indicator flashes "Markets are calm" Yield curve inverted Update toward recession probability PM changes style (new names) "PM is staying within mandate" Portfolio turnover doubled Update toward style drift; investigate

Statement: The sample mean of n independent random variables approaches a normal distribution as n → ∞, regardless of the underlying distribution, with: $$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$ Standard error of the mean: $\text{SE} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ Why CLT matters for risk: 1. Portfolio diversification: Even if individual stock returns aren't normal, a portfolio of 50+ stocks has returns that are approximately normal (sum of many independent-ish variables). This is why VaR works better for diversified portfolios than for single names. 2. Estimation precision: To halve the standard error of your volatility estimate, you need 4x the data (because SE scales with 1/√n). 1 year of data → SE/2 requires 4 years. 3. CLT failure mode: If returns have infinite variance (some fat-tailed distributions), CLT doesn't apply. This is why extreme events can persist even in large portfolios.

$$\bar{X} n \xrightarrow{p} \mu \quad \text{as } n \to \infty$$ Finance application — PM track record evaluation: Track record length What you can conclude Statistical power --- --- --- 6 months Almost nothing; noise dominates Extremely low 1 year Slight signal if Sharpe > 2 Low 3 years Meaningful if Sharpe > 1 Moderate 5+ years Genuine evaluation possible Good 10+ years High confidence Strong The "how long to prove skill?" formula: $$n \geq \left(\frac{z \alpha}{\text{Sharpe}}\right)^2 \text{ (in years)}$$ At 95% confidence ($z = 1.96$): - Sharpe 0.5: needs $(1.96/0.5)^2$ = 15.4 years - Sharpe 1.0: needs $(1.96/1.0)^2$ = 3.8 years - Sharpe 2.0: needs $(1.96/2.0)^2$ = 0.96 years Insight: Most hedge fund PMs have 2-3 year track records and Sharpe ratios around 1.0. That's NOT ENOUGH to statistically prove skill at 95% confidence. We allocate on faith, judgment, and qualitative assessment — not statistical significance.

Multiplication rule: $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$ Independence: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$, which means $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$. Critical risk insight — conditional vs. unconditional: > - Unconditional P(SPX down >3% tomorrow) ≈ 1% > - P(SPX down >3% tomorrow VIX > 35 today) ≈ 8-12% > - P(SPX down >3% tomorrow VIX > 35 AND dollar strengthening AND credit spreads widening) ≈ 15-20% > > Risk is ALWAYS conditional. The RM's job is to identify the RIGHT conditioning information. ---

Step What to do Example --- --- --- 1 State H₀ (null: no effect) and H₁ (alternative) H₀: PM alpha = 0. H₁: PM alpha > 0. 2 Choose significance level α α = 0.05 (5% false positive rate) 3 Compute test statistic t = (observed - expected) / SE 4 Compare to critical value or compute p-value p < α → reject H₀ 5 Interpret IN CONTEXT "PM's alpha is statistically different from zero"

section: Type I and Type II Errors
content: H₀ true (no alpha) H₀ false (real alpha) --- --- --- Reject H₀ ("PM has alpha") Type I error (false positive): you think PM is skilled but they're not Correct (true positive) Fail to reject H₀ ("no evidence of alpha") Correct (true negative) Type II error (false negative): you miss a skilled PM In risk management terms: - Type I = investing in a PM who's actually just lucky → you lose money slowly - Type II = passing on a PM who's actually skilled → opportunity cost - Type I in model validation = concluding VaR model is broken when it's fine → costly conservatism - Type II in model validation = concluding VaR model is fine when it's broken → blow-up Power = 1 - β = P(reject H₀ when H₁ is true). Power increases with: - Larger sample size (more data) - Larger effect size (bigger alpha) - Higher significance level (more willing to accept Type I errors)

$$t = \frac{\hat{\alpha}}{\text{SE}(\hat{\alpha})} = \frac{\hat{\alpha}}{s / \sqrt{n}}$$ where $\hat{\alpha}$ = estimated daily alpha, $s$ = daily return standard deviation, $n$ = number of trading days. Worked example 1 — "Good" PM: > PM averages +8bps/day over 500 trading days (~2 years). Daily vol = 60bps. > $$t = \frac{0.08}{0.60 / \sqrt{500}} = \frac{0.08}{0.0268} = 2.98$$ > p-value ≈ 0.001 (one-tailed). Reject H₀ at 1% level. Evidence of genuine skill. > Annualized alpha: 8bps × 252 ≈ 20%. Annualized Sharpe: $\frac{0.08}{0.60} \times \sqrt{252}$ ≈ 2.1. Worked example 2 — "Lucky" PM: > PM averages +5bps/day over 252 trading days (1 year). Daily vol = 80bps. > $$t = \frac{0.05}{0.80 / \sqrt{252}} = \frac{0.05}{0.0504} = 0.99$$ > p-value ≈ 0.16 (one-tailed). Cannot reject H₀. Not statistically significant. > Annualized alpha: 5bps × 252 ≈ 12.6%. Annualized Sharpe: $\frac{0.05}{0.80} \times \sqrt{252}$ ≈ 1.0. > > Key insight: A PM with a Sharpe of 1.0 over 1 year CANNOT be distinguished from luck. You need ~4 years to prove it.

section: Multiple Testing Problem — The Data Mining Trap
content: If you test 100 strategies at α = 5%, you expect 5 false positives by chance. The strategies that "pass" are mostly noise. Corrections: Method Approach When to use --- --- --- Bonferroni Divide α by number of tests: $α^ = α / m$ Conservative; when tests are independent Holm-Bonferroni Step-down: order p-values, test sequentially Less conservative than Bonferroni Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR) Controls false discovery rate, not family-wise error When some false positives are acceptable Haircut Sharpe Deflate backtest Sharpe: $\text{SR}^ = \text{SR} - \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln(m)}{n}}$ Quick adjustment for strategy screening Worked example — Haircut Sharpe: > Quant tested 500 strategies, found one with Sharpe = 2.5 over 5 years (n = 1260 days). > $$\text{Haircut} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \ln(500)}{1260}} = \sqrt{\frac{12.43}{1260}} = \sqrt{0.00987} = 0.099$$ > $$\text{Adjusted Sharpe} = 2.5 - \sqrt{252} \times 0.099 = 2.5 - 1.57 = \textbf{0.93}$$ > > The 2.5 Sharpe becomes 0.93 after adjusting for data mining. Suddenly much less impressive. ---

$$y i = \beta 0 + \beta 1 x i + \epsilon i$$ Minimizing: $\sum {i=1}^n (y i - \hat{y} i)^2$ $$\hat{\beta} 1 = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\text{Var}(X)} = \rho {X,Y} \cdot \frac{\sigma Y}{\sigma X}$$ $$\hat{\beta} 0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta} 1 \bar{x}$$ R² = fraction of variance explained: $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SS} \text{res}}{\text{SS} \text{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum(y i - \hat{y} i)^2}{\sum(y i - \bar{y})^2}$$ $$R^2 = \rho^2 \quad \text{(for simple OLS)}$$ Adjusted R²: Penalizes for additional variables. $$R^2 \text{adj} = 1 - \frac{(1-R^2)(n-1)}{n-k-1}$$ where $k$ = number of predictors. This prevents overfitting: adding a useless variable increases R² but decreases adjusted R².

$$r p = \alpha + \beta 1 F 1 + \beta 2 F 2 + ... + \beta k F k + \epsilon$$ Full worked example — PM factor decomposition: > Regressing PM monthly returns on 4 factors over 36 months: > > Factor β estimate t-stat p-value Interpretation > --- --- --- --- --- > Intercept (α) +0.25%/month 1.85 0.07 Alpha marginally significant (not at 5%) > Market (SPX) 1.15 8.23 <0.001 Very significant; PM runs 1.15x market beta > Size (SMB) -0.42 -2.91 0.006 Significant; tilted to large cap > Momentum (WML) 0.38 2.44 0.02 Significant; positive momentum exposure > Value (HML) 0.08 0.52 0.61 NOT significant; no value tilt > > R² = 0.78. Adjusted R² = 0.75. > > RM interpretation: > - 78% of this PM's returns are explained by factors → only 22% is idiosyncratic > - Alpha of 0.25%/month ≈ 3%/year, but t = 1.85 → NOT statistically significant at 5% > - PM is essentially running a leveraged (β = 1.15), large-cap, momentum portfolio > - "You could replicate 78% of this PM's performance with 4 ETFs at 20bps cost vs. 2/20 fees"

section: Regression Diagnostics — What Can Go Wrong
content: Problem What happens Detection Fix --- --- --- --- Multicollinearity Predictors correlated → β estimates unstable, signs may flip VIF > 5 is concerning; VIF > 10 = problem Remove redundant variables; use PCA Heteroscedasticity Variance of errors not constant → wrong standard errors White's test; Breusch-Pagan test; plot residuals vs. fitted Use robust (White) standard errors Autocorrelation Serial correlation in errors → overstated significance Durbin-Watson stat; should be ≈ 2 Newey-West HAC standard errors Omitted variable bias Missing important factor → β estimates biased Theory says a factor matters but it's not in the model Add the factor Endogeneity X and ε correlated → β is biased Economic reasoning (reverse causality?) IV estimation (instrumental variables) Structural breaks Relationship changes over time → single model inappropriate Chow test; rolling regression with unstable β Rolling regression; regime-switching model The most important regression pitfall in finance — spurious regression: > Two unrelated non-stationary series (e.g., GDP and SPX price level) will show high R² simply because both trend upward. The correlation is meaningless. > > Fix: Regress on RETURNS (stationary), not levels. Or use cointegration tests.

$$\rho {X,Y} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma X \cdot \sigma Y} = \frac{E (X-\mu X)(Y-\mu Y) }{\sigma X \cdot \sigma Y}$$ Properties: $-1 \leq \rho \leq 1$. Symmetric: $\rho {X,Y} = \rho {Y,X}$. Correlation pitfalls in finance: Pitfall Example Why it's wrong --- --- --- Correlation ≠ causation Oil up ↔ energy stocks up Causation here; but not all correlations are causal Correlation is LINEAR only Two assets with V-shaped P&L relationship → ρ ≈ 0 Use Spearman rank correlation or copulas for non-linear Regime-dependent Stock-bond ρ = -0.4 (normal) → +0.5 (inflation) Which ρ does your model use? Time-varying ρ(SPX, crude) was +0.3 in 2015, +0.7 in 2020, +0.1 in 2024 Rolling correlation windows Outlier-sensitive One extreme co-move can distort ρ for the entire sample Use robust correlation; winsorize outliers Partial correlation — removing confounders: $$\rho {X,Y \cdot Z} = \frac{\rho {X,Y} - \rho {X,Z} \cdot \rho {Y,Z}}{\sqrt{1 - \rho {X,Z}^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \rho {Y,Z}^2}}$$ This controls for a third variable Z. Example: what's the correlation between oil and airline stocks AFTER removing the effect of overall market (SPX)? The partial correlation is the "pure" relationship. ---

Strict stationarity: Joint distribution of $(X t, X {t+1}, ..., X {t+k})$ is the same for all $t$. Weak stationarity (covariance stationarity): Mean and autocovariance don't depend on time. Testing for stationarity: Test Null hypothesis Reject means --- --- --- Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) Series has unit root (NON-stationary) Series IS stationary Phillips-Perron (PP) Same as ADF (non-parametric correction) Series IS stationary KPSS Series IS stationary Series is NON-stationary Best practice: Use ADF AND KPSS together. If ADF rejects and KPSS fails to reject → strong evidence of stationarity. Typical results in finance: Series ADF result Stationary? --- --- --- Stock prices Fail to reject ❌ Non-stationary Stock returns Reject ✅ Stationary VIX level Reject (mean-reverting) ✅ Stationary Interest rates Mixed — near unit root ⚠️ Near-non-stationary GDP Fail to reject ❌ Non-stationary (trending)

$$X t = c + \phi 1 X {t-1} + \phi 2 X {t-2} + ... + \phi p X {t-p} + \epsilon t$$ AR(1): $X t = c + \phi X {t-1} + \epsilon t$ φ value Behavior Market example --- --- --- \ φ\ < 1 Stationary, mean-reverting VIX, credit spreads φ = 1 Random walk (unit root) Stock prices \ φ\ > 1 Explosive (unstable) Bubble dynamics (short-lived) φ > 0 Positive autocorrelation → momentum CTA signals φ < 0 Negative autocorrelation → mean reversion Stat arb signals Half-life of mean reversion: $$t {1/2} = \frac{-\ln(2)}{\ln( \phi )}$$ If φ = 0.95: half-life = -ln(2) / ln(0.95) = 0.693 / 0.0513 = 13.5 days . A deviation from the mean takes ~14 days to halve.

Two non-stationary series are cointegrated if a LINEAR COMBINATION of them is stationary. Example: SPY and IVV both track S&P 500. Each is non-stationary (prices trend), but: $$\text{SPY} t - \beta \cdot \text{IVV} t \approx \text{stationary spread}$$ Why it matters for risk: - Two correlated assets can decorrelate temporarily (ρ can drop to 0 or flip sign). - Two COINTEGRATED assets have a LONG-RUN equilibrium relationship — the spread mean-reverts. This is the foundation of pairs/stat arb trading. - For hedging: cointegrated hedge ratios are more stable than correlation-based ratios. Engle-Granger test: 1. Regress Y on X ($Y t = \alpha + \beta X t + e t$) 2. Test residuals ($e t$) for stationarity using ADF 3. If residuals are stationary → X and Y are cointegrated ---

1. Specify the probability distribution of each risk factor (returns, rates, vol) 2. Generate N random scenarios from those distributions 3. Compute portfolio P&L for each scenario (including non-linear effects) 4. Extract risk metrics from the P&L distribution

Step by step: 1. Estimate μ and σ from historical returns 2. Generate N ~ 10,000+ standard normal random draws: $Z i$ 3. Compute simulated returns: $r i = \mu + \sigma \cdot Z i$ 4. Compute simulated P&L: $\text{PnL} i = \text{Position Value} \times r i$ 5. VaR (99%) = negative of the 1st percentile of P&L distribution Worked example: > Portfolio value: $100M. μ = 0.03% daily. σ = 1.2% daily. > 10,000 simulations: r i = 0.0003 + 0.012 × Z i > PnL i = $100M × r i > 1st percentile of PnL = -$2.75M > → 99% 1-day VaR = $2.75M > > Compare to analytical: VaR = $100M × 2.326 × 1.2% = $2.79M (close — as expected when distribution is normal and payoff is linear)

If asset returns are correlated, you can't simulate them independently. Method: 1. Build the correlation matrix $\Sigma$ 2. Compute the Cholesky decomposition: $\Sigma = L L^T$ 3. Generate independent standard normals: $Z$ 4. Transform: $\text{Correlated returns} = L \cdot Z$ 2-asset example: > Asset A: μₐ = 0.05%, σₐ = 1.0%. Asset B: μᵦ = 0.03%, σᵦ = 1.5%. ρ = 0.6. > > Correlation matrix: $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ > > Cholesky: $L = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.6 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}$ > > For independent draws $Z 1, Z 2$: > - $r A = \mu A + \sigma A \cdot Z 1$ > - $r B = \mu B + \sigma B \cdot (0.6 \cdot Z 1 + 0.8 \cdot Z 2)$ > > Now A and B are correlated with ρ = 0.6 in each simulation.

Situation Why analytical fails Why MC works --- --- --- Options portfolios (non-linear) Delta-normal VaR ignores gamma MC simulates actual payoff including curvature Path-dependent risk Margin calls, stop-losses trigger during the path MC can simulate day-by-day paths Fat tails Normal assumption wrong MC can sample from t-distribution, GPD, etc. Complex correlations Covariance matrix too simple MC can use copulas for tail dependence Stress testing Need specific scenario combinations MC can condition on stress events

Technique How it works Improvement --- --- --- Antithetic variates For each Z, also simulate -Z Reduces variance by ~30-50% Control variates Use a variable with known expected value to adjust Reduces variance proportional to correlation Importance sampling Oversample the tail region of interest Critical for rare event estimation Stratified sampling Divide distribution into strata, sample from each Ensures tail representation ---

Moment What it measures Formula (excess) Normal value --- --- --- --- 1st: Mean (μ) Central tendency $E X $ Any 2nd: Variance (σ²) Dispersion / risk $E (X-\mu)^2 $ Any 3rd: Skewness (γ₁) Asymmetry $E\left \left(\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^3\right $ 0 4th: Kurtosis (κ) Tail thickness $E\left \left(\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^4\right - 3$ 0 (excess)

Skew Distribution shape Typical strategy Risk character --- --- --- --- γ₁ < -1 Heavy left tail Short vol, sell puts, carry Frequent small gains, rare large losses γ₁ ≈ 0 Symmetric Market beta, balanced Symmetric risk γ₁ > +1 Heavy right tail Long vol, trend following Frequent small losses, rare large gains The Sharpe-Skew deception: A short-vol strategy with Sharpe = 1.5 and skew = -3.0 LOOKS great until the tail event arrives. The Sharpe ratio treats upside and downside volatility equally — it REWARDS negative skew because the denominator (total vol) is inflated by rare large losses that haven't happened yet in the sample. Risk-adjusted metrics that account for skew: $$\text{Sortino} = \frac{R p - R f}{\sigma \text{downside}}$$ $$\text{Omega}(\theta) = \frac{\int \theta^\infty 1 - F(x) \, dx}{\int {-\infty}^\theta F(x) \, dx} = \frac{\text{Expected gains above threshold}}{\text{Expected losses below threshold}}$$ $$\text{Adjusted Sharpe} = \text{SR} \times \left 1 + \frac{\gamma 1}{6} \text{SR} - \frac{(\kappa - 3)}{24} \text{SR}^2\right $$

Excess kurtosis Tail behavior Example --- --- --- κ = 0 Normal tails Theoretical baseline κ = 1-3 Moderately fat tails Large-cap equity indices κ = 3-10 Significantly fat tails Individual equities, FX κ = 10-30 Very fat tails Commodities (energy, ags) κ > 30 Extremely fat tails Crypto, emerging markets Practical test: If kurtosis is high (>3), then: - VaR at 99% is understated by roughly $(1 + \kappa/8) \times$ the normal estimate (rough approximation) - Expected Shortfall diverges even more from the normal estimate - The Jarque-Bera test will reject normality $$\text{JB} = \frac{n}{6}\left(\gamma 1^2 + \frac{(\kappa)^2}{4}\right) \sim \chi^2 2$$ ---

What scales HOW Formula Assumption --- --- --- Returns scale linearly $R \text{annual} = R \text{daily} \times 252$ Additive returns (log returns) Variance scales linearly $\sigma^2 \text{annual} = \sigma^2 \text{daily} \times 252$ Independent returns (i.i.d.) Volatility scales with √T $\sigma \text{annual} = \sigma \text{daily} \times \sqrt{252}$ Independent returns (i.i.d.) Sharpe scales with √T $\text{SR} \text{annual} = \text{SR} \text{daily} \times \sqrt{252}$ Independent returns VaR scales with √T $\text{VaR} {T} = \text{VaR} 1 \times \sqrt{T}$ Independent returns, same distribution

Conversion Multiplier Approximate --- --- --- Daily → annual return × 252 Daily → annual vol × √252 × 15.87 Weekly → annual vol × √52 × 7.21 Monthly → annual vol × √12 × 3.46 Daily → 10-day VaR × √10 × 3.16 Daily → monthly VaR × √21 × 4.58

> "PM has daily vol of 0.8%. What's annual vol?" > 0.8% × 16 ≈ 12.8% (exact: 0.8% × 15.87 = 12.7%) > "40% annualized vol. What's daily?" > 40% / 16 ≈ 2.5% per day (exact: 40% / 15.87 = 2.52%) > "Daily VaR at 99% for a $200M portfolio with 1.5% daily vol?" > $200M × 2.33 × 1.5% = $6.99M ≈ $7M > "Convert that to 10-day VaR?" > $7M × √10 ≈ $7M × 3.16 = $22.1M

Condition Effect on scaling Actual 10-day VaR vs. √10 estimate --- --- --- Positive autocorrelation (momentum) VaR grows FASTER than √T HIGHER (up to 60%+) Negative autocorrelation (mean reversion) VaR grows SLOWER than √T LOWER (down to 40%) Volatility clustering (GARCH effects) Short-horizon vol may spike between t and t+10 HIGHER (path-dependent) Liquidity risk (can't exit position) Forced to hold → 10-day VaR isn't optional HIGHER (locked in) Jump risk (discrete large moves) Returns not continuous → √T approximation poor HIGHER (jumps don't average out) ---

$$H(X) = -\sum {i} p i \log 2(p i)$$ Measures uncertainty. Higher entropy = more uncertainty = more disorder. Distribution Entropy Interpretation --- --- --- Certain outcome (p = 1) 0 bits No uncertainty at all Fair coin (p = 0.5, 0.5) 1 bit Maximum uncertainty for binary Biased coin (p = 0.9, 0.1) 0.47 bits Less uncertain — you can predict heads Uniform over 8 outcomes 3 bits Maximum uncertainty for 8 states Finance application — market concentration: $$H \text{portfolio} = -\sum {i=1}^n w i \log 2(w i)$$ A portfolio with equal weights (1/n each) has maximum entropy. As concentration increases (one position dominates), entropy decreases. Entropy is an alternative to HHI for measuring portfolio diversification.

$$D \text{KL}(P \ Q) = \sum i P(i) \log\frac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$$ Measures how different distribution P is from distribution Q. NOT symmetric: $D \text{KL}(P\ Q) \neq D \text{KL}(Q\ P)$. Risk application: How different is today's return distribution from the historical distribution the model was calibrated on? Large KL divergence → regime has shifted → model is stale. ---
Options pricing, GBM, and most continuous-time finance models require stochastic calculus. You need to know the CONCEPTS, not the proofs.

$W t$ is Brownian motion if: 1. $W 0 = 0$ 2. Increments are independent: $W t - W s$ is independent of $W u$ for $u \leq s$ 3. Increments are normal: $W t - W s \sim N(0, t - s)$ 4. Paths are continuous Key properties: - $E W t = 0$ (no drift) - $\text{Var}(W t) = t$ (variance grows linearly with time → vol grows with √t) - $dW t \sim N(0, dt)$ - $(dW t)^2 = dt$ (this is the KEY identity for Itô's lemma)

section: Itô's Lemma — The Chain Rule of Stochastic Calculus
content: If $X t$ follows $dX t = \mu \, dt + \sigma \, dW t$ and $f$ is a twice-differentiable function: $$df(X t) = f'(X t) \, dX t + \frac{1}{2} f''(X t) \, \sigma^2 \, dt$$ $$= \left f'(X t) \mu + \frac{1}{2} f''(X t) \sigma^2\right dt + f'(X t) \sigma \, dW t$$ The extra term $\frac{1}{2} f''(X t) \sigma^2 dt$ is the "Itô correction" — it's what makes stochastic calculus different from regular calculus. It comes from $(dW)^2 = dt$. Why it matters for you: This extra term is WHY log returns differ from simple returns (the $-\frac{1}{2}\sigma^2$ convexity adjustment in GBM). It's also the mathematical foundation of gamma P&L: $$d(\text{Option value}) \approx \Delta \, dS + \frac{1}{2} \Gamma \, (dS)^2 + \Theta \, dt$$ The $\frac{1}{2} \Gamma (dS)^2$ term IS the Itô correction applied to options.

Under the risk-neutral measure $\mathbb{Q}$, the expected return on ALL assets equals the risk-free rate $r$. This allows us to price derivatives by discounting expected payoffs at $r$. The stock follows $dS = rS \, dt + \sigma S \, dW^{\mathbb{Q}} t$ under $\mathbb{Q}$ (not $dS = \mu S \, dt + ...$). Black-Scholes comes directly from this. You don't need to derive it — just know that the real-world drift μ doesn't appear in option prices; only σ and r matter. ---

Flip a coin twice. Given that at least one flip was heads, what's P(both heads)? Sample space of two flips: {HH, HT, TH, TT}. Conditioning on "at least one heads" removes TT → remaining: {HH, HT, TH}. $$P(\text{both heads} \mid \text{at least one heads}) = \frac{1}{3}$$ Twist: If told "the FIRST flip was heads," then: {HH, HT} → P(both) = 1/2. The conditioning information matters.

section: Puzzle 2: The Three Cards (Monty Hall Variant)
content: 3 cards: red/red, blue/blue, red/blue. You draw a card, see a red side. P(other side is also red)? There are 3 red faces in total. 2 belong to the red/red card, 1 to the red/blue. Given you see a red face, each face is equally likely to be the one observed. $$P(\text{other red}) = \frac{2}{3}$$

Two dice thrown. P(sum = 7)? Favorable: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1) = 6 outcomes. Total: 36 outcomes. P = 6/36 = 1/6 . Bonus: P(sum = 7) > P(sum = any other number). Seven is the most likely sum.

Expected coin flips to get first heads? $$E X = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2$$ Expected flips for two heads IN A ROW? Let E = expected flips. After each flip: - Tails (prob 0.5): wasted 1 flip, restart → contributed $0.5 \times (1 + E)$ - Heads then tails (prob 0.25): wasted 2 flips, restart → contributed $0.25 \times (2 + E)$ - Heads then heads (prob 0.25): done in 2 flips → contributed $0.25 \times 2$ $$E = 0.5(1 + E) + 0.25(2 + E) + 0.25 \times 2$$ $$E = 0.5 + 0.5E + 0.5 + 0.25E + 0.5$$ $$E = 1.5 + 0.75E$$ $$0.25E = 1.5$$ $$E = 6$$

How many people needed in a room for a >50% chance that two share a birthday? $$P(\text{no shared birthday among } n) = \prod {i=0}^{n-1} \frac{365-i}{365}$$ For n = 23: P(at least one match) ≈ 50.7% . Answer: 23 people . Finance analog: In a portfolio of 23+ positions, there's a >50% chance two have highly correlated (>0.8) drawdowns on the same day. Diversification is harder than it looks.

Drawer has red and blue socks. How many must you pull to guarantee a matching pair? 3 socks. With 2 colors, by the pigeonhole principle, 3 draws guarantee at least 2 are the same color. Generalization: With k colors, you need k + 1 draws to guarantee a match.

section: Puzzle 7: Expected Value of Max of Two Dice
content: $$E \max(D 1, D 2) = \sum {k=1}^{6} k \cdot P(\max = k)$$ $P(\max = k) = P(\text{both} \leq k) - P(\text{both} \leq k-1) = (k/6)^2 - ((k-1)/6)^2$ = $\frac{1}{36}(1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 11) \times (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6)$ weighted $$E \max = \frac{1}{36}(1 \times 1 + 2 \times 3 + 3 \times 5 + 4 \times 7 + 5 \times 9 + 6 \times 11) = \frac{161}{36} = \textbf{4.47}$$ Finance analog: If two PMs independently generate returns, the expected max return is higher than either individual expected return. This is why multi-PM structures outperform single-PM structures (option value of selection).

section: Q: "Walk me through how you'd determine if a PM's performance is due to skill or luck."
content: > "I'd compute the t-statistic on their excess returns — daily alpha divided by its standard error. The formula is alpha-hat divided by daily vol over square root of N trading days. > > The key insight is how much data you need. A PM with a Sharpe of 1.0 — which is quite good — needs nearly 4 years of data to be statistically significant at the 5% level. At Sharpe 0.5, you need over 15 years. Most PM track records are 2-3 years, which means we almost never have ENOUGH data to definitively prove skill. > > In practice, I'd supplement the t-test with two other checks. First, factor decomposition: if 80% of the PM's return is explained by market, momentum, and size factors, the 'alpha' is mostly factor beta that could be replicated cheaply. Second, I'd look at the DISTRIBUTION of returns, not just the average. Negative skew with high kurtosis suggests the PM is harvesting carry or selling vol — the Sharpe looks good because the blow-up hasn't happened yet in-sample. The adjusted Sharpe ratio, which penalizes for negative skew and fat tails, often tells a very different story."

section: Q: "Explain Bayes' theorem and give a risk management application."
content: > "Bayes says your updated belief equals your prior belief multiplied by the likelihood of the evidence, divided by the overall probability of that evidence. In plain English: how much should new information change your mind? > > In risk management, I'd use it for PM evaluation. Say I start with a 70% belief that a PM is skilled. A skilled PM has a 2-sigma drawdown month about 5% of the time; an unskilled PM has one about 15% of the time. After observing a bad month, Bayes updates my belief to about 44%. After two bad months, I'm down to about 21% confidence in their skill. > > The power of this is that it's CALIBRATED updating. Without Bayes, people either overreact — fire a PM after one bad month — or underreact — keep a PM for years because 'they'll come back.' Bayes tells you the mathematically correct amount to update, which depends on your prior and the relative probabilities of the evidence under each hypothesis."

section: Q: "Why can't you just use the normal distribution for risk management?"
content: > "Three reasons, all of which cause the normal distribution to UNDERSTATE tail risk. > > First, fat tails. Real return distributions have excess kurtosis of 3 to 10 for equities. A 4-sigma daily move should happen once every 126 years under normal assumptions — in reality, it happens every 2 to 3 years. So 99% VaR is consistently too low. > > Second, skewness. Most equity and credit strategies have negative skew — small frequent gains and rare large losses. The normal distribution is symmetric and doesn't capture this. A short-vol strategy with Sharpe 1.5 can look great for 3 years and then lose 50% in a week. > > Third, regime dependence. The normal distribution uses one mean and one variance, but markets switch between low-vol and high-vol regimes. A single normal calibrated to 'average' conditions understates risk in stress AND overstates risk in calm. Mixture distributions or regime-switching models are more appropriate. > > In practice, I'd use the normal as a baseline but supplement with Expected Shortfall — which captures the tail shape — and stress tests using historical crisis returns, which don't assume ANY distribution." --- Related files: → V Quant Mindset — applying these concepts to model risk and quant challenge → F Risk Measures — VaR, ES, Sharpe that USE these foundations → D Greeks — calculus-based derivatives sensitivity → X Coding Data — implementing these concepts in Python

At Squarepoint, the risk team works alongside quants who code in Python, C++, and kdb+/q daily. You won't be building production models, but you WILL be expected to: 1. Read and understand code that a quant shows you 2. Write simple scripts for ad-hoc risk analysis (VaR, P&L attribution, stress tests) 3. Query databases for position data, market data, and trade history 4. Discuss systems design for a daily risk pipeline intelligently 5. Solve coding puzzles that test logical thinking under time pressure This file gives you WORKING CODE for every major risk concept in the study guide. ---

python class RiskLimit: """Risk limit with automatic breach detection.""" def init (self, limit name: str, max value: float): self. name = limit name self. max value = max value self. current value = 0.0 self. breach history: list dict = @property def utilization(self) -> float: """Limit utilization as percentage.""" return (self. current value / self. max value) 100 if self. max value else 0 def update(self, new value: float) -> bool: """Update current value and return True if limit is breached.""" self. current value = new value breached = self. current value > self. max value if breached: self. breach history.append({ "timestamp": datetime.now(), "value": new value, "limit": self. max value, "excess": new value - self. max value }) return breached @property def rag status(self) -> str: """Return RAG status based on utilization.""" util = self.utilization if util < 70: return "GREEN" elif util < 90: return "AMBER" return "RED" Why encapsulation matters for RM: The breach history is private — no one can accidentally modify it. The rag status is computed (not stored), so it's always consistent with the current value.

python from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class Instrument(ABC): """Base class for all financial instruments.""" def init (self, ticker: str, quantity: float, price: float): self.ticker = ticker self.quantity = quantity self.price = price @abstractmethod def market value(self) -> float: """Current market value in fund currency.""" pass @abstractmethod def delta exposure(self) -> float: """First-order sensitivity to underlying price.""" pass class Equity(Instrument): def market value(self) -> float: return self.quantity self.price def delta exposure(self) -> float: return self.market value() Delta-1 for equities class Bond(Instrument): def init (self, ticker: str, face value: float, clean price: float, accrued: float, modified duration: float): super(). init (ticker, face value, clean price) self.accrued = accrued self.mod duration = modified duration def market value(self) -> float: return self.quantity (self.price + self.accrued) / 100 def delta exposure(self) -> float: return -self.market value() self.mod duration DV01 direction def dv01(self) -> float: """Dollar value of 1bp rate move.""" return self.market value() self.mod duration 0.0001 class Option(Instrument): def init (self, ticker: str, quantity: float, price: float, delta: float, gamma: float, vega: float, theta: float, underlying price: float, multiplier: int = 100): super(). init (ticker, quantity, price) self.delta = delta self.gamma = gamma self.vega = vega self.theta = theta self.underlying price = underlying price self.multiplier = multiplier def market value(self) -> float: return self.quantity self.price self.multiplier def delta exposure(self) -> float: return self.quantity self.delta self.underlying price self.multiplier def gamma pnl(self, price move pct: float) -> float: """P&L from gamma for a given % price move.""" dollar move = self.underlying price price move pct return 0.5 self.quantity self.gamma dollar move 2 self.multiplier

python def portfolio risk summary(instruments: list Instrument ) -> dict: """Works for ANY instrument type — equities, bonds, options.""" return { "total mv": sum(i.market value() for i in instruments), "total delta": sum(i.delta exposure() for i in instruments), "position count": len(instruments), "largest position": max(instruments, key=lambda i: abs(i.market value())).ticker, }

section: --- Generator for streaming large trade files ---
content: def stream trades(filepath: str): """Memory-efficient: processes one trade at a time.""" with open(filepath) as f: header = next(f).strip().split(',') for line in f: yield dict(zip(header, line.strip().split(',')))

content: Why Polars matters: Rust backend, zero-copy Arrow memory, multi-threaded by default. The lazy API lets you write a full query plan and the engine optimizes it (predicate pushdown, projection pushdown) before touching data. For the cross-asset correlation monitor at Natixis, Polars processed 10M+ rows/day where pandas would choke.

section: --- Time-series cross-validation (NEVER use random split for time data) ---
content: tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n splits=5) for train idx, test idx in tscv.split(X): model.fit(X train idx , y train idx ) score = model.score(X test idx , y test idx ) One-liner: Scikit-learn provides PCA for risk factor decomposition, linear regression for factor attribution, StandardScaler for normalization before any ML, and TimeSeriesSplit because you never randomly shuffle financial data.

python import numpy as np import pandas as pd from scipy import stats def parametric var(returns: pd.Series, confidence: float = 0.99, portfolio value: float = 1e6) -> float: """Parametric (variance-covariance) VaR assuming normal distribution.""" mu = returns.mean() sigma = returns.std() z = stats.norm.ppf(1 - confidence) z = -2.326 for 99% var return = mu + z sigma return -var return portfolio value def historical var(returns: pd.Series, confidence: float = 0.99, portfolio value: float = 1e6) -> float: """Historical simulation VaR — no distribution assumption.""" cutoff = returns.quantile(1 - confidence) return -cutoff portfolio value def monte carlo var(mu: float, sigma: float, portfolio value: float = 1e6, confidence: float = 0.99, n sims: int = 100 000) -> float: """Monte Carlo VaR with normal returns.""" simulated = np.random.normal(mu, sigma, n sims) simulated pnl = portfolio value simulated return -np.percentile(simulated pnl, (1 - confidence) 100)

python def expected shortfall(returns: pd.Series, confidence: float = 0.99, portfolio value: float = 1e6) -> float: """ES = average loss beyond VaR. Better tail risk measure than VaR.""" cutoff = returns.quantile(1 - confidence) tail returns = returns returns <= cutoff return -tail returns.mean() portfolio value
var 99 = historical var(returns, 0.99) es 99 = expected shortfall(returns, 0.99) tail ratio = es 99 / var 99 >1.5 → fat tail warning

python def stress test(positions: pd.DataFrame, scenarios: dict str, dict ) -> pd.DataFrame: """ Apply stress scenarios to a portfolio. scenarios = { "Rates +100bp": {"rates shock": 0.01, "equity shock": -0.05, "vol shock": 0.3}, "COVID Redux": {"rates shock": -0.015, "equity shock": -0.30, "vol shock": 1.5}, "Oil Spike": {"oil shock": 0.40, "equity shock": -0.08, "vol shock": 0.2}, } """ results = {} for scenario name, shocks in scenarios.items(): pnl = 0.0 for , pos in positions.iterrows(): Delta P&L from equity shock if "equity shock" in shocks and pos "asset class" == "equity": pnl += pos "market value" pos "beta" shocks "equity shock" DV01 P&L from rates shock if "rates shock" in shocks and pos "asset class" == "bond": pnl += pos "dv01" shocks "rates shock" 10 000 DV01 per bp Vega P&L from vol shock if "vol shock" in shocks and pos.get("vega", 0) != 0: pnl += pos "vega" shocks "vol shock" Gamma P&L (second order) if "equity shock" in shocks and pos.get("gamma", 0) != 0: dollar move = pos "underlying price" shocks "equity shock" pnl += 0.5 pos "gamma" dollar move 2 results scenario name = pnl return pd.DataFrame.from dict(results, orient="index", columns= "Stress P&L" )

python def drawdown analysis(cumulative pnl: pd.Series) -> pd.DataFrame: """Identify all drawdown periods with peak, trough, recovery, and duration.""" peak = cumulative pnl.cummax() drawdown = cumulative pnl - peak drawdown pct = drawdown / peak.replace(0, np.nan) Identify drawdown periods in drawdown = drawdown < 0 dd groups = (~in drawdown).cumsum() results = for group id, group in cumulative pnl in drawdown .groupby(dd groups): if len(group) == 0: continue start = group.index 0 trough date = group.idxmin() trough value = drawdown.loc trough date peak value = peak.loc start dd pct = trough value / peak value 100 Find recovery date post trough = cumulative pnl.loc trough date: recovered = post trough post trough >= peak value recovery date = recovered.index 0 if len(recovered) > 0 else None duration = (group.index -1 - start).days results.append({ "start": start, "trough date": trough date, "max drawdown": trough value, "drawdown pct": dd pct, "recovery date": recovery date, "duration days": duration, }) return pd.DataFrame(results).sort values("max drawdown").head(10)

python def pnl attribution(positions: pd.DataFrame, market moves: dict str, float ) -> pd.DataFrame: """ Decompose daily P&L into risk factor contributions. market moves = {"SPX": -0.012, "rates 10y": 0.0005, "vol": 0.02, "oil": -0.03} """ positions = positions.copy() Delta P&L positions "delta pnl" = positions "delta exposure" market moves.get("SPX", 0) Gamma P&L spx move dollar = positions "underlying price" market moves.get("SPX", 0) positions "gamma pnl" = 0.5 positions "gamma exposure" spx move dollar 2 Rates P&L (DV01 × bp move) bp move = market moves.get("rates 10y", 0) 10 000 positions "rates pnl" = -positions "dv01" bp move Vega P&L positions "vega pnl" = positions "vega exposure" market moves.get("vol", 0) Theta P&L (daily) positions "theta pnl" = positions "theta exposure" Unexplained (residual) positions "total pnl" = positions "actual pnl" explained = positions "delta pnl", "gamma pnl", "rates pnl", "vega pnl", "theta pnl" .sum(axis=1) positions "unexplained" = positions "total pnl" - explained return positions

python from sklearn.decomposition import PCA from sklearn.linear model import LinearRegression def rolling correlation(returns a: pd.Series, returns b: pd.Series, window: int = 60) -> pd.Series: """Rolling correlation between two return series.""" return returns a.rolling(window).corr(returns b) def factor decomposition(pm returns: pd.Series, factor returns: pd.DataFrame) -> dict: """ Regress PM returns on factor returns. Returns alpha, betas, R², and factor attribution. """ Align dates aligned = pd.concat( pm returns, factor returns , axis=1).dropna() y = aligned.iloc :, 0 .values X = aligned.iloc :, 1: .values model = LinearRegression().fit(X, y) r squared = model.score(X, y) residuals = y - model.predict(X) Annualize alpha daily alpha = model.intercept annual alpha = daily alpha 252 T-statistic for alpha se alpha = residuals.std() / np.sqrt(len(y)) t stat = daily alpha / se alpha return { "alpha daily bps": daily alpha 10 000, "alpha annual pct": annual alpha 100, "t stat alpha": t stat, "betas": dict(zip(factor returns.columns, model.coef )), "r squared": r squared, "idiosyncratic ratio": 1 - r squared, } def pca risk decomposition(returns: pd.DataFrame, n components: int = 5) -> dict: """PCA decomposition of portfolio risk factors.""" pca = PCA(n components=n components) pca.fit(returns.dropna()) return { "explained variance ratio": pca.explained variance ratio , "cumulative explained": np.cumsum(pca.explained variance ratio ), "loadings": pd.DataFrame( pca.components .T, index=returns.columns, columns= f"PC{i+1}" for i in range(n components) ), }

python def monte carlo portfolio var(returns: pd.DataFrame, weights: np.ndarray, portfolio value: float = 1e7, confidence: float = 0.99, n sims: int = 50 000, use t dist: bool = False, t df: int = 5) -> dict: """ Multi-asset Monte Carlo VaR with optional fat tails (Student-t). Uses Cholesky decomposition for correlated simulation. """ n assets = returns.shape 1 mu = returns.mean().values cov = returns.cov().values L = np.linalg.cholesky(cov) Generate random scenarios if use t dist: z = stats.t.rvs(df=t df, size=(n sims, n assets)) else: z = np.random.normal(size=(n sims, n assets)) Apply correlation structure correlated returns = mu + z @ L.T Portfolio-level P&L portfolio returns = correlated returns @ weights portfolio pnl = portfolio value portfolio returns var = -np.percentile(portfolio pnl, (1 - confidence) 100) es = -portfolio pnl portfolio pnl <= -var .mean() if any(portfolio pnl <= -var) else var return { "VaR 99": var, "ES 99": es, "ES VaR ratio": es / var if var > 0 else 0, "mean pnl": portfolio pnl.mean(), "worst case": -portfolio pnl.min(), "simulation count": n sims, "fat tails": use t dist, }

python def performance metrics(returns: pd.Series, risk free rate: float = 0.04) -> dict: """Comprehensive performance metrics suite.""" daily rf = risk free rate / 252 excess = returns - daily rf Sharpe sharpe = excess.mean() / excess.std() np.sqrt(252) Sortino (downside deviation only) downside = excess excess < 0 downside std = np.sqrt((downside 2).mean()) sortino = excess.mean() / downside std np.sqrt(252) if downside std > 0 else 0 Max drawdown cumulative = (1 + returns).cumprod() peak = cumulative.cummax() max dd = ((cumulative - peak) / peak).min() Calmar ratio (return / max drawdown) annual return = returns.mean() 252 calmar = annual return / abs(max dd) if max dd != 0 else 0 Skewness and kurtosis skew = returns.skew() kurt = returns.kurtosis() Excess kurtosis Hit rate hit rate = (returns > 0).mean() Best/worst days best = returns.max() worst = returns.min() return { "annual return pct": annual return 100, "annual vol pct": returns.std() np.sqrt(252) 100, "sharpe": sharpe, "sortino": sortino, "max drawdown pct": max dd 100, "calmar": calmar, "skewness": skew, "excess kurtosis": kurt, "hit rate pct": hit rate 100, "best day pct": best 100, "worst day pct": worst 100, "trading days": len(returns), } ---

sql -- Net position per ticker with market value SELECT p.ticker, SUM(p.quantity) AS net qty, m.last price, SUM(p.quantity) m.last price AS market value FROM positions p INNER JOIN market data m ON p.ticker = m.ticker WHERE p.book = 'DISC MACRO' AND p.trade date = CURRENT DATE GROUP BY p.ticker, m.last price HAVING SUM(p.quantity) <> 0 ORDER BY ABS(SUM(p.quantity) m.last price) DESC; sql -- Exposure by sector and PM SELECT p.pm name, s.sector, SUM(p.quantity m.last price) AS gross exposure, SUM(p.quantity m.last price) / SUM(SUM(ABS(p.quantity m.last price))) OVER (PARTITION BY p.pm name) 100 AS pct of pm book FROM positions p JOIN market data m ON p.ticker = m.ticker JOIN security master s ON p.ticker = s.ticker WHERE p.trade date = CURRENT DATE GROUP BY p.pm name, s.sector ORDER BY p.pm name, gross exposure DESC;

sql -- Running P&L with drawdown tracking SELECT date, pm name, daily pnl, SUM(daily pnl) OVER (PARTITION BY pm name ORDER BY date) AS cumulative pnl, MAX(SUM(daily pnl) OVER (PARTITION BY pm name ORDER BY date)) OVER (PARTITION BY pm name ORDER BY date) AS peak pnl, SUM(daily pnl) OVER (PARTITION BY pm name ORDER BY date) - MAX(SUM(daily pnl) OVER (PARTITION BY pm name ORDER BY date)) OVER (PARTITION BY pm name ORDER BY date) AS drawdown FROM daily pnl WHERE date >= DATE '2026-01-01'; sql -- Daily VaR breach detection SELECT date, portfolio pnl, var 99, CASE WHEN portfolio pnl < -var 99 THEN 'BREACH' ELSE 'OK' END AS var status, COUNT(CASE WHEN portfolio pnl < -var 99 THEN 1 END) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 249 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS breaches 250d FROM daily risk report ORDER BY date DESC; sql -- Rolling 20-day realized volatility WITH daily returns AS ( SELECT ticker, date, (close - LAG(close) OVER (PARTITION BY ticker ORDER BY date)) / LAG(close) OVER (PARTITION BY ticker ORDER BY date) AS ret FROM prices ) SELECT ticker, date, STDDEV(ret) OVER (PARTITION BY ticker ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 19 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) SQRT(252) AS realized vol 20d FROM daily returns;

sql -- PM performance ranking with Sharpe-like metric WITH pm stats AS ( SELECT pm name, AVG(daily pnl) AS avg daily pnl, STDDEV(daily pnl) AS daily vol, COUNT( ) AS trading days, SUM(daily pnl) AS total pnl, MIN(daily pnl) AS worst day, SUM(CASE WHEN daily pnl > 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)::FLOAT / COUNT( ) AS hit rate FROM daily pnl WHERE date >= DATE '2026-01-01' GROUP BY pm name ), pm rankings AS ( SELECT , avg daily pnl / NULLIF(daily vol, 0) SQRT(252) AS sharpe approx, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY avg daily pnl / NULLIF(daily vol, 0) DESC) AS sharpe rank, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total pnl DESC) AS pnl rank FROM pm stats ) SELECT pm name, ROUND(total pnl / 1e6, 2) AS total pnl mm, ROUND(sharpe approx, 2) AS sharpe, ROUND(hit rate 100, 1) AS hit rate pct, ROUND(worst day / 1e6, 2) AS worst day mm, sharpe rank, pnl rank FROM pm rankings ORDER BY sharpe rank; sql -- Concentration analysis: HHI by PM WITH pm positions AS ( SELECT pm name, ticker, ABS(quantity last price) AS abs mv, SUM(ABS(quantity last price)) OVER (PARTITION BY pm name) AS total abs mv FROM positions p JOIN market data m USING (ticker) WHERE trade date = CURRENT DATE ) SELECT pm name, COUNT(DISTINCT ticker) AS n positions, SUM(POWER(abs mv / NULLIF(total abs mv, 0), 2)) AS hhi, MAX(abs mv / NULLIF(total abs mv, 0)) 100 AS max single position pct FROM pm positions GROUP BY pm name HAVING SUM(POWER(abs mv / NULLIF(total abs mv, 0), 2)) > 0.15 -- Flag concentrated books ORDER BY hhi DESC;

Concept When to use Notes --- --- --- INNER JOIN Both tables must match Positions + market data LEFT JOIN Keep all left rows Positions + optional sector mapping CROSS JOIN Every combination Positions × scenario shocks WHERE vs HAVING WHERE filters BEFORE group; HAVING AFTER HAVING SUM(qty) > 0 LAG / LEAD Previous/next row values Daily returns from prices ROW NUMBER / RANK Ordering within partitions PM rankings ROWS BETWEEN Rolling window frame Rolling volatility CTE (WITH) Named subquery, readable Complex multi-step analysis INDEX B-tree for fast lookup On (ticker, date) for time-series EXPLAIN ANALYZE Query performance Identify slow full-table scans ---

- Column-oriented: Aggregations over billions of rows in milliseconds (vs. minutes in SQL) - In-memory: Data lives in RAM — sub-millisecond query latency - Time-series native: As-of joins, sliding windows, and temporal fills are first-class operations - Write once, read many: Optimized for append-heavy workloads (tick data)

section: Core Syntax — What You Need to Recognize
content: q / Read CSV trades: ("DSFJI"; enlist ",") 0: :trades.csv / SQL-style query (q-SQL) select avg price, sum size by sym from trades where date = 2026.04.07 / VWAP (volume-weighted average price) select vwap: size wavg price by sym from trades / As-of join: last quote at each trade time / This is what makes kdb+ essential — aligning asynchronous timestamps aj sym time; trades; quotes / Rolling 20-day volatility update vol20: 20 mdev log ratios close by sym from daily / Functional select (programmatic column selection) ? trades; enlist (>; size;1000); (enlist sym)!enlist sym; avgPrice totalSize!((wavg; size; price);(sum; size)) / Apply function across groups {select avg price, max price, min price by 5 xbar time.minute from x} each (select from trades where sym in AAPL MSFT GOOG)

Concept What it means Why it matters --- --- --- As-of join ( aj ) Match each row to the last available data at/before a timestamp Aligns trades to quotes for P&L at execution time Window join ( wj ) Aggregate within a time window around each row Rolling VWAP, volume profile Partitioned tables Data stored by date on disk, loaded lazily Can query years of tick data without loading everything Splayed tables Each column stored as a separate file Read only the columns you need q-SQL SQL-like syntax but column-oriented (operations on vectors, not rows) Intuitive for SQL users Atoms vs. lists Everything is a vector — scalar = 1-element list Vectorized operations are the default, not a special case

section: What to Say If Asked About kdb+
content: > "I used kdb+ at BNP Paribas for tick-level market data analysis — FX forwards, swaps, and corporate hedging flows. The key advantage is speed: aggregations over millions of rows that would take minutes in SQL return in milliseconds. As-of joins were essential for aligning trade timestamps to market data snapshots for accurate P&L attribution and slippage analysis. I'm comfortable reading q code and writing basic queries, though I wouldn't claim to be a production-level q developer." ---

section: Why C++ at Squarepoint (and Why You Used It)
content: At Natixis, I built the SABR calibration core in C++ and wrapped it with a Python API via pybind11. Why? Calibration requires minimizing a non-linear objective function over thousands of swaption vols — each evaluation calls the Hagan formula, and the optimizer (Levenberg-Marquardt) needs hundreds of iterations. In pure Python, calibrating one expiry takes ~2 seconds. In C++, it takes ~5 milliseconds. When you need to recalibrate the full surface (20+ expiries) every time the market moves, 400x speedup is the difference between real-time and useless. Squarepoint's systematic infrastructure runs C++ for anything latency-sensitive: pricing engines, risk calculations, market data processing. As an RM for the discretionary team, you won't write production C++ daily, but you need to READ it, UNDERSTAND performance tradeoffs, and DISCUSS architecture with quants.

cpp include <vector> include <map> include <unordered map> include <set> include <array> include <string> // vector: dynamic array, contiguous memory → cache-friendly, fast iteration std::vector<double> prices = {100.5, 101.2, 99.8, 102.3}; prices.push back(103.0); // O(1) amortized prices.reserve(10000); // Pre-allocate to avoid reallocation // map: ordered key-value (red-black tree), O(log n) lookup std::map<std::string, double> greeks; greeks "delta" = 0.55; greeks "gamma" = 0.02; // Iterates in sorted key order — useful for tenor-ordered risk factors // unordered map: hash table, O(1) average lookup — when order doesn't matter std::unordered map<std::string, double> positions; positions "AAPL" = 1'000'000.0; // C++14 digit separator positions "MSFT" = 750'000.0; // array: fixed-size, stack-allocated, zero overhead vs C array std::array<double, 10> tenor buckets = {0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30}; // set: unique sorted elements, O(log n) insert/find std::set<std::string> active tickers; active tickers.insert("SPX"); bool exists = active tickers.count("SPX"); // 1 = found

cpp include <memory> class PricingEngine { public: virtual double price(double spot, double strike, double vol, double rate, double T) const = 0; virtual ~PricingEngine() = default; }; class BlackScholesEngine : public PricingEngine { public: double price(double S, double K, double sigma, double r, double T) const override { double d1 = (std::log(S/K) + (r + 0.5 sigma sigma) T) / (sigma std::sqrt(T)); double d2 = d1 - sigma std::sqrt(T); return S norm cdf(d1) - K std::exp(-r T) norm cdf(d2); } private: static double norm cdf(double x); // Standard normal CDF }; // unique ptr: single owner, no overhead, RAII auto engine = std::make unique<BlackScholesEngine>(); double px = engine->price(5200, 5300, 0.18, 0.045, 0.25); // shared ptr: reference-counted, multiple owners (use when genuinely shared) auto shared engine = std::make shared<BlackScholesEngine>(); // Reference count = 1 auto another ref = shared engine; // Reference count = 2, both point to same object // NEVER use raw new/delete in modern C++ // Bad: PricingEngine engine = new BlackScholesEngine(); ... delete engine; // Good: auto engine = std::make unique<BlackScholesEngine>(); One-liner: unique ptr = sole ownership, zero overhead, use by default. shared ptr = shared ownership via reference counting, use only when truly shared. Raw new / delete = never in modern C++.

section: OOP in C++ — Virtual Functions and Inheritance
content: cpp include <vector> include <memory> include <iostream> class Instrument { public: Instrument(const std::string& ticker, double notional) : ticker (ticker), notional (notional) {} virtual ~Instrument() = default; // Virtual destructor — essential for polymorphism virtual double market value() const = 0; // Pure virtual → abstract class virtual double delta exposure() const = 0; const std::string& ticker() const { return ticker ; } double notional() const { return notional ; } protected: std::string ticker ; double notional ; }; class EquityPosition : public Instrument { public: EquityPosition(const std::string& ticker, double qty, double price) : Instrument(ticker, qty price), qty (qty), price (price) {} double market value() const override { return qty price ; } double delta exposure() const override { return market value(); } private: double qty , price ; }; class BondPosition : public Instrument { public: BondPosition(const std::string& ticker, double face, double clean price, double mod duration) : Instrument(ticker, face), face (face), clean price (clean price), mod dur (mod duration) {} double market value() const override { return face clean price / 100.0; } double delta exposure() const override { return -market value() mod dur ; } double dv01() const { return market value() mod dur 0.0001; } private: double face , clean price , mod dur ; }; // Polymorphism: one function handles any instrument type double total delta(const std::vector<std::unique ptr<Instrument>>& portfolio) { double total = 0.0; for (const auto& inst : portfolio) { total += inst->delta exposure(); // Virtual dispatch — correct method called } return total; }

cpp // Function template: works for any numeric type template<typename T> T portfolio variance(const std::vector<T>& weights, const std::vector<std::vector<T>>& cov matrix) { T var = 0; for (size t i = 0; i < weights.size(); ++i) for (size t j = 0; j < weights.size(); ++j) var += weights i weights j cov matrix i j ; return var; } // Works with double, float, or any numeric type auto var d = portfolio variance<double>(weights d, cov d);

cpp include <cmath> include <vector> // Hagan SABR implied vol formula (simplified ATM case) double sabr implied vol(double forward, double strike, double expiry, double alpha, double beta, double rho, double nu) { if (std::abs(forward - strike) < 1e-10) { // ATM approximation double fmid = std::pow(forward, 1.0 - beta); double term1 = alpha / fmid; double term2 = ((1.0 - beta) (1.0 - beta) alpha alpha) / (24.0 fmid fmid); double term3 = (rho beta nu alpha) / (4.0 fmid); double term4 = (2.0 - 3.0 rho rho) nu nu / 24.0; return term1 (1.0 + (term2 + term3 + term4) expiry); } // Full Hagan formula for non-ATM strikes (omitted for brevity) // In production, this is ~50 lines handling log(F/K), z/x(z), etc. return 0.0; } // Objective function for Levenberg-Marquardt optimizer double calibration objective(const std::vector<double>& params, const std::vector<double>& market vols, const std::vector<double>& strikes, double forward, double expiry, double beta) { double alpha = params 0 , rho = params 1 , nu = params 2 ; double sse = 0.0; for (size t i = 0; i < strikes.size(); ++i) { double model vol = sabr implied vol(forward, strikes i , expiry, alpha, beta, rho, nu); double diff = model vol - market vols i ; sse += diff diff; } return sse; // Minimize this — Levenberg-Marquardt adjusts params } Why C++ for calibration: Each sabr implied vol call involves ~20 floating-point operations. The optimizer calls it hundreds of times per expiry, across 20+ expiries. In Python, function call overhead + interpreter overhead makes this ~400x slower. C++ compiles to native machine code with zero overhead per call. The pybind11 wrapper exposes the calibrated surface back to Python for downstream risk analytics.

Aspect Python C++ --- --- --- Execution Interpreted, GIL limits threading Compiled to native code, true multi-threading Memory Objects have ~100 bytes overhead each Structs pack tightly, cache-friendly Function calls ~100ns per call (interpreter overhead) ~1ns per call (inlined by compiler) Best for Data analysis, prototyping, glue code Pricing engines, calibration, real-time risk At Squarepoint Risk analytics, dashboards, ad-hoc analysis Production pricing, execution, market data

section: C++ ↔ Python — How They Work Together
content: cpp // pybind11: expose C++ SABR calibrator to Python include <pybind11/pybind11.h> include <pybind11/stl.h> namespace py = pybind11; PYBIND11 MODULE(sabr engine, m) { m.def("calibrate", &calibrate sabr, "Calibrate SABR parameters", py::arg("market vols"), py::arg("strikes"), py::arg("forward"), py::arg("expiry"), py::arg("beta") = 0.5); m.def("implied vol", &sabr implied vol, "SABR implied vol at a strike"); } python

section: Calibrate — runs in C++ (5ms instead of 2s)
content: params = sabr engine.calibrate( market vols= 0.18, 0.19, 0.21, 0.24, 0.28 , strikes= 0.02, 0.025, 0.03, 0.035, 0.04 , forward=0.03, expiry=5.0, beta=0.5 ) alpha, rho, nu = params

section: Use calibrated params in Python for downstream risk
content: vol at strike = sabr engine.implied vol(0.03, 0.035, 5.0, alpha, 0.5, rho, nu)

section: What to Say If Asked About C++
content: > "I built the SABR calibration core in C++ at Natixis — the optimizer calls the Hagan vol formula hundreds of times per expiry, and across 20+ expiries the Python version was 400x too slow for real-time recalibration. C++ gave us 5ms per surface vs 2 seconds. I wrapped it with pybind11 so the risk team consumed it as a Python module. I'm comfortable with modern C++: STL containers, smart pointers, templates, virtual functions. I wouldn't claim to be a systems-level C++ developer, but I can read production code, profile performance bottlenecks, and build compute-intensive modules that integrate with a Python analytics stack." ---

1. DATA INGESTION (6:00 AM) ├── Pull positions from OMS/PMS ├── Pull market data (prices, rates, vol surfaces) ├── Pull reference data (sector mapping, factor exposures) └── Reconcile vs. PB statements 2. RISK CALCULATIONS (6:30 AM) ├── Mark-to-market all positions ├── Compute Greeks (delta, gamma, vega, theta per position) ├── Compute VaR (historical + parametric + Monte Carlo) ├── Run stress tests (pre-defined + ad-hoc scenarios) └── Factor decomposition (PCA + Fama-French) 3. LIMIT CHECKS (7:00 AM) ├── Compare VaR vs. limits (house, PM, strategy) ├── Check concentration limits (single name, sector, factor) ├── Check Greeks limits (net delta, gamma, vega) ├── Flag any breaches → RAG status └── Identify limit utilization >80% (AMBER warnings) 4. REPORTING (7:30 AM) ├── Summary dashboard (total VaR, P&L attribution, limit status) ├── PM-level detail (exposure, VaR, Greeks by PM) ├── Breach report (any RED items → escalation email) └── CRO morning brief document

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod from dataclasses import dataclass from typing import Protocol @dataclass class RiskResult: """Standardized output from any risk calculation.""" metric name: str value: float unit: str confidence: float None = None as of date: str = "" class RiskModel(Protocol): """Interface that all risk models must implement.""" def compute(self, positions: pd.DataFrame, market data: dict) -> list RiskResult : ...

class HistoricalVaRModel: def init (self, lookback days: int = 500, confidence: float = 0.99): self.lookback = lookback days self.confidence = confidence def compute(self, positions: pd.DataFrame, market data: dict) -> list RiskResult : ... implementation ... return RiskResult("VaR Historical", var value, "USD", self.confidence) class ParametricVaRModel: def compute(self, positions: pd.DataFrame, market data: dict) -> list RiskResult : ... implementation ... return RiskResult("VaR Parametric", var value, "USD", 0.99) class StressTestModel: def init (self, scenarios: dict str, dict ): self.scenarios = scenarios def compute(self, positions: pd.DataFrame, market data: dict) -> list RiskResult : results = for name, shocks in self.scenarios.items(): pnl = self. apply scenario(positions, shocks) results.append(RiskResult(f"Stress {name}", pnl, "USD")) return results

section: --- Risk engine that runs all models ---
content: class RiskEngine: def init (self): self. models: list RiskModel = def register model(self, model: RiskModel) -> None: self. models.append(model) def run all(self, positions: pd.DataFrame, market data: dict) -> list RiskResult : results = for model in self. models: results.extend(model.compute(positions, market data)) return results

engine = RiskEngine() engine.register model(HistoricalVaRModel(lookback days=500)) engine.register model(ParametricVaRModel()) engine.register model(StressTestModel(scenarios=STRESS SCENARIOS)) results = engine.run all(positions df, market data dict) Why this design: - Open/Closed Principle: Add new risk models without modifying existing code - Single Responsibility: Each model does one thing - Dependency Inversion: Engine depends on the RiskModel interface, not specific implementations - Testable: You can mock any model for unit testing

section: Common Design Patterns in Risk Code
content: Pattern Use case Example --- --- --- Strategy Swap risk calculation method at runtime Parametric vs. historical vs. MC VaR Observer Notify when limits are breached Limit breach → email + dashboard update Factory Create instruments from raw data InstrumentFactory.create("equity", row) Decorator Add logging/timing to existing functions @timer , @cache , @validate inputs Pipeline Chain data transformations Load → Clean → Calculate → Report Singleton One instance only Market data cache, config manager ---

python import functools import time import logging def timer(func): """Measure execution time of any function.""" @functools.wraps(func) def wrapper( args, kwargs): start = time.perf counter() result = func( args, kwargs) elapsed = time.perf counter() - start logging.info(f"{func. name } took {elapsed:.3f}s") return result return wrapper def validate returns(func): """Validate that return series is clean before computation.""" @functools.wraps(func) def wrapper(returns: pd.Series, args, kwargs): if returns.isna().any(): returns = returns.dropna() logging.warning(f"Dropped {returns.isna().sum()} NaN values") if len(returns) < 30: raise ValueError(f"Insufficient data: {len(returns)} < 30 observations") return func(returns, args, kwargs) return wrapper @timer @validate returns def compute var(returns: pd.Series, confidence: float = 0.99) -> float: return -returns.quantile(1 - confidence)

python from contextlib import contextmanager import logging @contextmanager def risk calculation context(calc name: str): """Log start/end of risk calculations and catch errors.""" logging.info(f"Starting: {calc name}") start = time.perf counter() try: yield except Exception as e: logging.error(f"FAILED: {calc name} — {e}") raise finally: elapsed = time.perf counter() - start logging.info(f"Completed: {calc name} in {elapsed:.3f}s")
with risk calculation context("Daily VaR Calculation"): var = compute var(returns, confidence=0.99)

python def rolling var(returns: pd.Series, window: int = 252, confidence: float = 0.99): """Memory-efficient rolling VaR using generator.""" for i in range(window, len(returns)): window returns = returns.iloc i - window:i yield returns.index i , -window returns.quantile(1 - confidence)

section: Lazy evaluation — only computes when iterated
content: var series = pd.Series(dict(rolling var(daily returns, 252)))

python from dataclasses import dataclass, field from typing import Optional from datetime import date @dataclass class Position: ticker: str quantity: float entry price: float current price: float asset class: str sector: Optional str = None pm name: str = "" @property def market value(self) -> float: return self.quantity self.current price @property def unrealized pnl(self) -> float: return self.quantity (self.current price - self.entry price) @property def return pct(self) -> float: return (self.current price / self.entry price - 1) 100 @dataclass class PortfolioSnapshot: as of: date positions: list Position = field(default factory=list) @property def total mv(self) -> float: return sum(p.market value for p in self.positions) @property def total pnl(self) -> float: return sum(p.unrealized pnl for p in self.positions) def exposure by sector(self) -> dict str, float : sectors: dict str, float = {} for p in self.positions: key = p.sector or "Unknown" sectors key = sectors.get(key, 0) + p.market value return dict(sorted(sectors.items(), key=lambda x: -abs(x 1 ))) ---

python import pytest import numpy as np import pandas as pd class TestHistoricalVaR: """Unit tests for VaR calculation.""" def test known distribution(self): """VaR of standard normal returns should approximate z-score.""" np.random.seed(42) returns = pd.Series(np.random.normal(0, 0.01, 10 000)) var = historical var(returns, confidence=0.99, portfolio value=1 000 000) 99% VaR of N(0, 0.01) ≈ 0.01 2.326 1M = $23,260 assert 20 000 < var < 27 000, f"VaR {var} outside expected range" def test var increases with vol(self): """Higher vol → higher VaR.""" low vol = pd.Series(np.random.normal(0, 0.005, 1000)) high vol = pd.Series(np.random.normal(0, 0.02, 1000)) var low = historical var(low vol) var high = historical var(high vol) assert var high > var low def test es exceeds var(self): """Expected Shortfall must be ≥ VaR by definition.""" returns = pd.Series(np.random.normal(0, 0.01, 5000)) var = historical var(returns) es = expected shortfall(returns) assert es >= var, "ES must be >= VaR" def test empty returns raises(self): """Empty series should raise ValueError.""" with pytest.raises(ValueError): historical var(pd.Series(dtype=float)) class TestStressTest: def test long equity hurt by sell off(self): """A long equity position should lose money in an equity sell-off.""" positions = pd.DataFrame( { "asset class": "equity", "market value": 1 000 000, "beta": 1.0, "dv01": 0, "vega": 0, "gamma": 0, "underlying price": 100, } ) result = stress test(positions, {"Crash": {"equity shock": -0.10}}) assert result.loc "Crash", "Stress P&L" < 0

Principle What it means Anti-pattern --- --- --- DRY Don't Repeat Yourself Copy-pasting VaR code for each PM instead of parameterizing KISS Keep It Simple Building a framework when a function will do YAGNI You Aren't Gonna Need It Adding "flexible" features nobody asked for Fail fast Validate inputs early Silently returning 0 when data is missing Immutability Don't modify inputs df = df.copy() before mutation Naming Descriptive > short compute 99 var() not calc() ---

python def find pairs summing to(arr: list float , target: float) -> list tuple : """Find all pairs in sorted array that sum to target.""" arr.sort() left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1 pairs = while left < right: total = arr left + arr right if total == target: pairs.append((arr left , arr right )) left += 1 right -= 1 elif total < target: left += 1 else: right -= 1 return pairs

python def max drawdown window(pnl: list float , window: int) -> float: """Maximum drawdown within any rolling window.""" max dd = 0 for i in range(len(pnl) - window + 1): w = pnl i:i + window peak = w 0 dd = 0 for val in w: peak = max(peak, val) dd = min(dd, val - peak) max dd = min(max dd, dd) return max dd

python from collections import Counter def most traded tickers(trades: list dict , top n: int = 10) -> list tuple : """Top N tickers by trade count.""" counts = Counter(t "ticker" for t in trades) return counts.most common(top n) def sector concentration(positions: list dict ) -> dict: """HHI concentration index by sector.""" sector mv = Counter() total mv = 0 for p in positions: mv = abs(p "market value" ) sector mv p "sector" += mv total mv += mv hhi = sum((mv / total mv) 2 for mv in sector mv.values()) return {"hhi": hhi, "sectors": dict(sector mv)}

python import bisect def find var breach date(sorted pnl dates: list tuple , var limit: float) -> int: """Find first date where cumulative P&L breaches VaR limit. sorted pnl dates: (date, cumulative pnl), ... sorted by date. """ pnls = p 1 for p in sorted pnl dates idx = bisect.bisect left(pnls, -var limit) return sorted pnl dates idx 0 if idx < len(pnls) else None

python def max profit k trades(prices: list float , k: int) -> float: """Maximum profit with at most k buy-sell transactions.""" if not prices or k == 0: return 0 n = len(prices) if k >= n // 2: Unlimited trades return sum(max(prices i+1 - prices i , 0) for i in range(n-1)) DP: dp j i = max profit using at most j transactions up to day i dp = 0 n for in range(k + 1) for j in range(1, k + 1): best buy = -prices 0 for i in range(1, n): dp j i = max(dp j i-1 , prices i + best buy) best buy = max(best buy, dp j-1 i - prices i ) return dp k n-1

python from functools import lru cache @lru cache(maxsize=None) def expected flips consecutive heads(n: int) -> float: """Expected number of coin flips to get n consecutive heads.""" if n == 0: return 0 if n == 1: return 2 Geometric: E X = 1/p = 1/0.5 = 2 After getting n-1 consecutive heads (expected E(n-1) flips): - Heads (p=0.5): done!
