Average trade size: ~200 shares 4. Trades per day: 4.8B / 200 = ~24 million trades 5. But many are algorithmic/broken into smaller orders: adjust up ~2x → ~50M Actual (2024-2025): ~30-50M equity trades/day across NYSE. ✓ ---

section: Problem 25: Finance Fermi — Daily VaR for Squarepoint
content: > Estimate what Squarepoint's 99% 1-day VaR might be. 1. AUM: ~$10-15B (multi-strat) 2. Gross leverage: ~5-8x → gross exposure ~$60-80B 3. But multi-strat is diversified across many uncorrelated pods → net risk is much lower 4. Typical annualized fund vol target: 4-6% → daily vol = 5% / √252 ≈ 0.32% 5. 99% VaR ≈ 2.326 × daily vol ≈ 0.74% of NAV 6. VaR on $12B NAV ≈ ~$90M 7. Sanity check: per-pod VaR is maybe $2-5M, 30+ pods, diversification benefit → total lines up This is a reasonable order-of-magnitude estimate. The actual number would depend on specific position data. ---

> 8 identical-looking coins. One is slightly heavier. You have a balance scale. Minimum weighings to find the heavy coin? Answer: 2 weighings. 1. Split into groups of 3, 3, 2 2. Weigh group A (3) vs group B (3): - If equal → heavy coin is in group C (2). Weigh them against each other. 1 more weighing = 2 total. - If unequal → heavy coin is in the heavier group (3 coins). Take 2 of those 3, weigh them. If equal → it's the third. If unequal → it's the heavier. 1 more weighing = 2 total. Generalization: With k weighings, you can identify the odd coin among 3^k coins. So 3 weighings handles 27 coins, 4 handles 81, etc. ---

> 3 switches outside a room, 1 light bulb inside. You can flip switches as much as you want, then enter the room ONCE. Which switch controls the bulb? Answer: 1. Turn switch 1 ON for 5 minutes, then turn it OFF 2. Turn switch 2 ON 3. Enter the room: - Bulb ON → Switch 2 - Bulb OFF but WARM → Switch 1 - Bulb OFF and COLD → Switch 3 Key insight: You have 3 unknowns but seemingly 1 measurement. The trick is using a DIFFERENT dimension of information (temperature) to encode a second bit. Risk analog: When you can only observe one metric (P&L), you can extract more information by observing it across DIFFERENT conditions (calm market vs. volatile) — two conditions × one metric = two bits of information. ---

> 4 people must cross a bridge at night with one flashlight. Max 2 people at a time. The flashlight must go back. Crossing times: 1, 2, 5, 10 minutes. Bridge takes the SLOWER person's time. Minimum total time? Naive approach: Fastest person escorts everyone: 1+2 + 1+5 + 1+10 = 19 minutes. Optimal: 17 minutes. 1. A(1) + B(2) cross → 2 min. Total: 2. 2. A(1) returns → 1 min. Total: 3. 3. C(5) + D(10) cross → 10 min. Total: 13. 4. B(2) returns → 2 min. Total: 15. 5. A(1) + B(2) cross → 2 min. Total: 17. Key insight: Send the two slowest TOGETHER so their times overlap rather than being additive. The cost is one extra fast return trip. ---

> 100-story building. Egg breaks if dropped from floor F or above, survives below F. You have 2 eggs. Minimum worst-case drops to find F? Answer: 14 drops. Strategy: Drop first egg from floors 14, 27, 39, 50, 60, 69, 77, 84, 90, 95, 99, 100... The trick is choosing intervals that DECREASE by 1 each time, because if the first egg breaks, you use the second egg to linearly search the gap — and each subsequent gap is 1 smaller to compensate for the additional first-egg drop already used. $$n + (n-1) + (n-2) + \cdots + 1 = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \geq 100$$ $$n = 14 \quad \text{(since } 14 \times 15/2 = 105 \geq 100\text{)}$$ Generalization: With k eggs and n floors: minimum worst-case drops ≈ $n^{1/k} \cdot k$. More eggs → binary search becomes possible (with ∞ eggs, just binary search: ceil(log₂(100)) = 7). ---

> Two ropes. Each takes exactly 60 minutes to burn completely, but they burn NON-UNIFORMLY (some parts faster, some slower). How do you measure exactly 45 minutes? Answer: 1. Light both ends of Rope 1 AND one end of Rope 2 simultaneously 2. Rope 1 burns out in 30 minutes (it's burning from both ends) 3. When Rope 1 is done, light the OTHER end of Rope 2 4. Rope 2 has 30 minutes of burn remaining, but now it's burning from both ends → finishes in 15 more minutes 5. Total: 30 + 15 = 45 minutes ✓ Key insight: Burning from both ends halves the remaining time regardless of non-uniformity. ---

> 100 prisoners in a line. Each gets a random red or blue hat. Each can see all hats IN FRONT but not their own or behind. Starting from back, each must guess their own hat color. They can strategize beforehand. Optimal strategy? Answer: Guarantee 99 correct, with the first person having 50/50 odds. Strategy: The person at the back counts the number of red hats they see. If odd, they say "red"; if even, they say "blue". This gives a 50/50 chance for them. Every subsequent person can now deduce their own hat color by tracking the running parity of announced colors and visible hats. Result: At least 99 out of 100 are guaranteed correct. The first person has P(correct) = 50%. ---

Value Result Where it shows up --- --- --- √2 1.414 2-day VaR scaling √3 1.732 3-day VaR scaling √10 3.162 10-day VaR scaling (Basel) √252 15.87 Daily → annual vol √52 7.21 Weekly → annual vol √12 3.464 Monthly → annual vol ln(2) 0.693 Half-life, Rule of 72 e 2.718 Continuous compounding 1/e 0.368 Derangement probability, secretary problem e^0.1 1.105 Quick continuous compound ln(1.1) 0.0953 ≈ 10% growth → ~9.5% continuous

Doubling time = 72 / annual rate Rate Doubles in --- --- 2% 36 years 4% 18 years 6% 12 years 8% 9 years 10% 7.2 years 12% 6 years 15% 4.8 years 20% 3.6 years Halving time for decay also = 72 / rate. A strategy losing 10%/year halves in ~7 years.

Formula Value Use case --- --- --- n! Permutations of n items Ordering, arrangements $\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$ Choosing k from n Combinations, portfolios $\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ Pairs from n items Pairwise correlations needed $2^n$ Subsets of n items Possible hedge combinations Quick binomial coefficients: $\binom{n}{k}$ k=0 k=1 k=2 k=3 k=4 k=5 --- --- --- --- --- --- --- n=5 1 5 10 10 5 1 n=6 1 6 15 20 15 6 n=10 1 10 45 120 210 252 n=20 1 20 190 1140 4845 15504 ---

> You have a biased coin (P(heads) unknown). How do you generate fair 50/50 outcomes? Von Neumann's trick: 1. Flip twice 2. HT → call it "outcome A" 3. TH → call it "outcome B" 4. HH or TT → discard and repeat P(HT) = p(1−p) = P(TH) → equal probability regardless of p. Expected flips per fair outcome: 1 / (2p(1−p)). For p = 0.7: need ~4.8 flips per outcome. For p = 0.9: ~11 flips. Risk analog: Even with biased data (asymmetric return distributions), you can construct symmetric bets by taking the difference of two identically-distributed events. Pairs trading does exactly this. ---

> A stock is at $100. In 1 year it will be $120 or $80 with equal probability. Risk-free rate = 5%. Price a call option with strike $100. Binomial model: - Up move: stock = $120, call pays $20 - Down move: stock = $80, call pays $0 Risk-neutral probability: $$p^ = \frac{e^{rT} - d}{u - d} = \frac{1.05 - 0.8}{1.2 - 0.8} = \frac{0.25}{0.40} = 0.625$$ Call price: $$C = e^{-rT} p^ \times 20 + (1-p^ ) \times 0 = \frac{0.625 \times 20}{1.05} = \frac{12.5}{1.05} = \$11.90$$ Key insight: We use risk-neutral probabilities (0.625/0.375), NOT real-world probabilities (0.5/0.5). The real-world drift doesn't matter for option pricing — only the risk-free rate and the volatility (encoded in the up/down magnitudes). ---

section: Problem 34: The Paradox of Volatility Drag
content: > Stock returns +20% one year, then −20% the next. What's the total return? $100 × 1.20 × 0.80 = $96. You LOST 4%, not 0%. The general formula: If vol = σ, geometric return ≈ arithmetic return − σ²/2. Arithmetic return Vol Geometric return --- --- --- 0% 10% −0.5% 0% 20% −2.0% 0% 30% −4.5% 0% 50% −12.5% 10% 20% 8.0% 10% 40% 2.0% 10% 50% −2.5% Risk analog: A zero-expected-return strategy with 50% vol loses ~12.5% per year in geometric terms. This is why vol control matters — even with no directional edge, reducing vol improves geometric returns. Leveraged ETFs suffer from this: 2× leveraged SPX in sideways markets bleeds capital from vol drag alone. ---

section: Problem 35: Expected Loss from a VaR Breach
content: > Your 99% 1-day VaR is $5M. Over 250 trading days, how many breaches do you expect? If the tail follows a Pareto distribution with shape α = 2, what's the expected loss GIVEN a breach? Expected breaches: 250 × 0.01 = 2.5 per year For Pareto with α = 2: $$E \text{Loss} \text{Loss} > \text{VaR} = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha - 1} \times \text{VaR} = \frac{2}{1} \times \$5M = \$10M$$ So your ES = $10M, giving ES/VaR = 2.0 — a moderately fat tail. For α = 1.5 (fatter tail): ES = 3 × VaR = $15M For α = 3 (thinner tail): ES = 1.5 × VaR = $7.5M ---

> Two assets each have 20% annual vol and 0.5 correlation. Portfolio is 50/50. What's portfolio vol? $$\sigma p = \sqrt{0.5^2 \times 0.2^2 + 0.5^2 \times 0.2^2 + 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 0.2 \times 0.5}$$ $$= \sqrt{0.01 + 0.01 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.03} = \mathbf{17.3\%}$$ > Now in crisis, correlation jumps to 0.95. New portfolio vol? $$\sigma p = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.01 + 0.019} = \sqrt{0.039} = \mathbf{19.7\%}$$ Vol increased from 17.3% → 19.7% — a 14% increase — purely from correlation change, with NO change in individual asset vol. > If individual vols ALSO double in crisis (common), new portfolio vol? $$\sigma p = \sqrt{0.5^2 \times 0.4^2 + 0.5^2 \times 0.4^2 + 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.4 \times 0.95}$$ $$= \sqrt{0.04 + 0.04 + 0.076} = \sqrt{0.156} = \mathbf{39.5\%}$$ From 17.3% → 39.5% — vol more than DOUBLED. This is why VaR computed from calm correlations and vols is useless in a crisis. ---

> You drive one lap of a racetrack at 30 mph. How fast must you drive the second lap so that your AVERAGE speed over both laps is 60 mph? Instinctive (wrong) answer: 90 mph. "30 + 90 = 120, divide by 2 = 60." This confuses the arithmetic mean of two speeds with average speed. Correct answer: IMPOSSIBLE. Why: 1. Let the track length be D miles. 2. Average speed = Total distance / Total time = 2D / T total 3. To achieve 60 mph average over 2 laps: T total = 2D / 60 = D/30 hours. 4. But the first lap at 30 mph already took: T₁ = D / 30 hours. 5. Time remaining for the second lap: T₂ = D/30 − D/30 = 0 hours. 6. You would need to drive the second lap in zero time — i.e., infinite speed. The mathematical trap: Average speed is the harmonic mean of the two speeds, not the arithmetic mean: $$\text{Average speed} = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{v 1} + \frac{1}{v 2}} = \frac{2 v 1 v 2}{v 1 + v 2}$$ Setting this equal to $2v 1$ (double the first speed): $$\frac{2 v 1 v 2}{v 1 + v 2} = 2v 1 \implies v 2 = \frac{v 1(2v 1)}{2v 1 - v 1} \to \infty \text{ as the target approaches } 2v 1$$ The constraint: The harmonic mean of two positive numbers can NEVER reach twice the smaller one. The maximum possible average speed over two laps where one lap was driven at speed $v$ is strictly less than $2v$, achieved only in the limit as the second lap speed → ∞. Concrete example: If you drive the second lap at 90 mph: - T₁ = D/30, T₂ = D/90 - T total = D/30 + D/90 = 4D/90 - Average speed = 2D / (4D/90) = 45 mph — not even close to 60. Even at 300 mph for the second lap: Average = 2/(1/30 + 1/300) = 2/(11/300) = 54.5 mph. Still under 60. Risk analog: This is the time-weighted vs. money-weighted return trap in portfolio management. A PM who loses 50% of a $100M book (down to $50M), then gains 50% ($50M → $75M) reports "average return = 0%" but actually lost $25M. The harmonic/geometric reality is always worse than the arithmetic illusion. In risk, always ask: "average of WHAT, weighted by WHAT?" ---

Step Time What to do --- --- --- 1. Restate 5 sec "So I need to find..." — confirms understanding, buys time 2. Classify 5 sec Probability? Estimation? Logic? Optimization? 3. Simplify 10 sec "Let me try a smaller version first" (2 items instead of 100) 4. Identify the trick 10 sec Conditional probability? Counting faces not cards? Using extra information? 5. Solve 20 sec Clear steps, speak aloud 6. Sanity check 10 sec "Does this answer make sense? Is it between 0 and 1?"

section: Common Traps & How to Avoid Them
content: Trap Example Correct instinct --- --- --- Forgetting conditional P(both heads \ at least one heads) ≠ 1/2 Draw out the sample space explicitly Counting objects not faces Three cards problem → count faces, not cards Ask "what am I counting?" Ignoring base rates Disease test: 99% accurate but 1/10,000 prevalence → PPV is ~1% Always apply Bayes: prior × likelihood Arithmetic vs geometric +20% then −20% ≠ 0% Multiply: 1.2 × 0.8 = 0.96 Path dependence Gambler's ruin — past flips don't affect future E future doesn't change, but P(ruin) does Infinite expectation St. Petersburg: E = ∞ but median → 0 Check MEDIAN alongside mean Symmetry assumption "50/50" when priors aren't equal Challenge whether equal probability is justified

Signal How to demonstrate --- --- Structured thinking "I'll break this into three cases..." Communication Narrate your thought process. Don't go silent. Edge case awareness "If n = 1 this simplifies to..." Intellectual honesty "I'm not sure about the exact constant, but I know the structure is..." Connection to finance After solving: "This is like... risk analog " Comfort with uncertainty For estimation: "My confidence interval is 100-500, with best guess 250" ---

section: Q: "Walk me through how you'd estimate the number of trades our risk system processes daily."
content: > "I'd decompose it. Squarepoint runs roughly 30-40 pods across equities, rates, FX, credit, and commodities. A systematic pod generating signals across, say, 500 instruments might execute 200-500 orders per day. A discretionary pod trades less — maybe 20-50 per day. Weighting roughly 60% systematic and 40% discretionary, I'd estimate: 25 systematic pods × 350 trades + 15 discretionary pods × 35 trades = 8,750 + 525 ≈ 9,000-10,000 orders per day. If each order generates multiple fills, the actual trade count hitting the risk system could be 3-5x that — so 30,000-50,000 trade records processed daily. The risk system then needs to reconcile, mark-to-market, and aggregate these into positions by 6:30 AM. That's the throughput constraint that drives the technology choices."

section: Q: "If I give you a biased coin and you don't know the bias, how would you use it for decision-making in a risk framework?"
content: > "I'd use Von Neumann's trick — flip twice. HT maps to one outcome, TH maps to another, and I discard HH or TT. Since P(HT) = p(1-p) = P(TH) regardless of the bias, this gives me perfectly fair binary decisions. The cost is efficiency — I need 1/(2p(1-p)) flips per decision, so the more biased the coin, the more flips I need. > > The analog in risk: even if the underlying data is skewed (which market returns always are), I can construct symmetric risk measures by using paired observations. Pairs trading exploits exactly this — two correlated stocks with individual biases, but the spread between them is approximately symmetric."
Junior Risk Manager · Squarepoint Capital · Interviewer: Suhas Vennam ---

Q1. Walk me through your CV. French national judo athlete — trained discipline, pressure, performing under stress. Double masters in quantitative engineering and finance. Started in investment banking, then BNP Paribas sales trading as an assistant, moved into quant risk management on the sell side at BNP for nearly two years — that's the core of my experience. Currently at Natixis as a cross-asset quant analyst. Sell-side risk background gives me a rigorous process-driven foundation; I'm now looking to apply that in a buy-side environment where risk is directly tied to P&L. --- Q2. Why risk management? Risk management sits at the crossroads of technical knowledge, market intuition, and P&L creation. Traders look for opportunities — I look for vulnerabilities and make sure they're patched for maximum risk-adjusted return. It requires depth in all three dimensions simultaneously, which is what draws me to it. --- Q3. Why Squarepoint? Squarepoint is building out its discretionary arm and applying quantitative rigor to it — that combination is unusual and genuinely interesting from a risk perspective. The infrastructure, the research platform, the calibre of the traders and quants — it's the environment where I can grow the fastest. And frankly, I've been watching Squarepoint since I lived across the street from the NYC office. It's been a goal. --- Q4. What do you know about Squarepoint's discretionary business? Squarepoint traces back to nQuant, founded inside Lehman in 2000, then absorbed by Barclays after the 2008 acquisition. It was spun out as an independent firm in December 2014, founded by four École Polytechnique alumni — Durantel, Schneider, Fillet, and Fortin. It's grown to over $125bn AUM as of recent filings, predominantly systematic, with offices in NYC, London, Paris, Bangalore, Hong Kong and others. The discretionary arm is more recent and expanding cross-asset. What makes it interesting from a risk standpoint is applying quantitative risk frameworks to strategies driven by human judgment — a more complex monitoring challenge than purely systematic books. --- Q5. Where do you see yourself in 3 years? Senior risk manager, owning a book of discretionary strategies end-to-end — limits, monitoring, escalation — and actively contributing to building out the risk framework for the discretionary business. I want to be the person traders come to when they need a risk perspective, not just the person who sends breach alerts. ---

Q6. What is VaR? How do you compute it? VaR is the maximum loss not exceeded at a given confidence level over a given horizon. 95% 1-day VaR of $1M means: on 95% of days, losses won't exceed $1M — but on the remaining 5% they will, and VaR says nothing about how bad those days are. Three methods: - Historical : sort past returns, take the 5th percentile directly. No distributional assumption. - Parametric : assume normality, compute mean and std, apply z-score (1.645 for 95%). - Monte Carlo : simulate thousands of paths, take the percentile of simulated outcomes. --- Q7. What are the limitations of VaR? - Ignores everything beyond the threshold — tells you nothing about the size of tail losses - Historical VaR assumes the past represents the future — misses new risk regimes - Parametric VaR assumes normality — real returns are fat-tailed and skewed - Not sub-additive: portfolio VaR can exceed the sum of individual VaRs (not coherent) - Easily gamed: a strategy can show low VaR while taking on large tail risk - Lookback period matters enormously — a calm 2-year window gives false comfort --- Q8. What is CVaR / Expected Shortfall? Why is it better? CVaR is the average loss in the cases where VaR is breached — the expected value of the tail. If 95% VaR is -$1M, CVaR tells you: on the 5% of bad days, you lose on average $X. It's more informative because it characterises the shape of the tail, not just the threshold. It's also sub-additive (coherent). Limitation: still model-dependent and harder to backtest than VaR. --- Q9. What is the difference between market risk and operational risk? - Market risk : losses from market price moves — equities down, rates up, vol spike, FX move. Driven by external market forces. - Operational risk : losses from failures in processes, systems, or people — wrong order entered, settlement failure, system outage, rogue trader. Driven by internal mechanics and human error. Both are real and material — operational losses can be as large as market losses (e.g. Barings, Société Générale). --- Q10. What is stress testing? How does it differ from VaR? Stress testing applies predefined extreme shocks to a portfolio and measures the P&L impact. Unlike VaR, it's not probabilistic — it asks "what happens if X occurs" not "what's the 95th percentile loss." Two types: - Historical scenarios : GFC 2008, COVID March 2020, 1987 crash — replay observed market moves - Hypothetical scenarios : US-China trade war escalation, Middle East escalation, mass QE unwind VaR gives a daily probabilistic estimate under normal conditions. Stress testing prepares you for the extremes VaR can't capture, and informs liquidity planning, collateral requirements, and portfolio construction decisions. --- Q11. What is a risk limit? How would you set one? A risk limit is the maximum exposure a trader or desk is authorised to take. Limits cascade: firm-level → desk-level → individual trader. Types: notional (gross/net), VaR budget, delta limit, concentration

Q16. What is delta? What does a delta of 0.6 mean? Delta has two interpretations: - Hedge ratio : for every $1 move in the underlying, the option value moves $0.60. To delta-hedge, you take an offsetting position of 0.60 shares per option. - Moneyness proxy : roughly approximates the probability the option expires in-the-money. Strictly, the risk-neutral ITM probability is N(d2), but delta = N(d1) is commonly used as a quick approximation since they're close for short-dated ATM options. Net portfolio delta = total dollar move of the portfolio per $1 move in spot. A risk manager monitors net delta to ensure the book is within its directional exposure limit. --- Q17. What is gamma? Why does a risk manager care? Gamma is the rate of change of delta — how much delta shifts when spot moves. It determines how quickly your hedge goes stale. - Long gamma : delta adjusts automatically as spot moves — you're naturally rehedged, but you pay for it via theta (time decay). Large moves benefit you. - Short gamma : you collect theta but are exposed to large sudden moves. If spot gaps, your delta hedge is wrong and you lose on both legs. Risk managers care because short gamma positions can generate large losses in volatile markets — exactly when you least want it. --- Q18. What is vega? How does a vol spike affect a long options book? Vega is the dollar change in option value per 1 percentage point move in implied volatility. A long options book has positive vega — when vol spikes, option values increase, generating a profit. The risk manager monitors aggregate vega to understand the book's sensitivity to volatility regime changes. In the current environment (VIX ~30), vega exposure is a primary risk driver. --- Q19. What is theta? Who benefits? Theta is daily time decay — the amount an option loses in value per day, all else equal. Theta is highest (fastest decay) in the final 30 days before expiry, lowest for long-dated options. Option sellers (short premium) collect theta — it's the "rent" they earn for taking on gamma risk. Option buyers pay theta. A risk manager tracks net theta to understand the daily carry cost or gain of the book. --- Q20. If a trader is long gamma, is that good or bad to monitor? Long gamma is a cleaner risk profile to monitor — the position self-adjusts as spot moves, reducing the risk of runaway losses. The main risk is theta bleed (daily cost) and vega exposure. Short gamma is harder to monitor and more dangerous in crisis conditions — losses can accelerate non-linearly as spot moves. For a risk manager, short gamma positions require more frequent rehedge monitoring and tighter limits. ---

Q21. A trader is at 90% of their daily VaR limit — what do you do? Proactively reach out — don't wait for a breach. Understand what's driving the exposure: is it a single concentrated position or broad market move? Check what upcoming events (data, Fed, earnings) could push them through the limit. Flag to senior risk manager. Monitor intraday, don't wait for EOD reports. If they're adding risk, that's a conversation; if it's mark-to-market movement, assess whether it's transient. --- Q22. A trader breaches their limit at market open — walk me through your response. Market open breaches are often driven by overnight fills and early volatility — the mark may recover within minutes. Don't immediately force a position unwind. Steps: 1. Verify the breach is real, not a stale price or system error 2. Flag immediately to senior risk manager with full context 3. Speak to the trader — understand their view and intended action 4. Analyse what caused it: new position or market move? 5. Document everything — paper trail is non-negotiable 6. Monitor closely through the first hour; if still in breach by mid-morning, escalation required --- Q23. Two strategies are highly correlated — why is that a problem? One effective exposure, not two — the firm is taking more concentration risk than the limit structure implies. More critically, if one strategy is discretionary and the other systematic, an algo may react to a market move automatically, selling exactly when the discretionary trader wants to buy — compounding the loss. Crowded positioning across correlated strategies can create violent unwinds. At the portfolio level, you must adjust notional limits to reflect the true net exposure. --- Q24. A model shows low VaR but the trader is taking large directional bets — what's happening? Several possibilities: - The VaR lookback window captures a calm period — the model is underestimating the current volatility regime - The directional bets are in instruments or tenors not well captured by the model (illiquid, model blind spot) - The trader has structured positions to appear hedged on the model's risk factors while retaining the actual directional exposure Action: don't trust the model number. Go to the raw positions, stress test them manually against realistic scenarios, and present the discrepancy to senior risk management. The worst thing a risk manager can do is defer to a model showing green when the book looks obviously risky. --- Q25. Markets gap down 5% at open — what's your first call? 1. Pull live P&L across all discretionary books immediately 2. Identify who has the largest net long delta exposure — they're most impacted 3. Call those traders: do they have hedges in place? Are they intending to add or reduce? 4. Calculate estimated P&L impact at current spot vs limit thresholds 5. Escalate to senior risk management with a one-page summary: net delta, estimated loss, limit status, trader actions 6. Monitor continuously — a 5% gap can reverse or extend; the f

Q26. What's your view on current market conditions? VIX is elevated around 30, SPX is down approximately 10% YTD driven by tariff uncertainty and policy risk. The volatility regime has shifted — what was a low-vol environment in 2024 is now structurally higher. For a discretionary book, this means delta exposure is more expensive to hold, vega exposure is more valuable, and risk limits that were calibrated for a calm environment may need revisiting. --- Q27. VIX at 30 — what does that mean for a discretionary options book? Implied vol at 30 prices in approximately 1.9% daily moves in SPX. Options are expensive — buying protection costs more, selling premium collects more but with higher risk. A long options book is profitable on the vega position. The key risk manager question is whether the book's gamma and vega positions are sized for a sustained high-vol regime or were calibrated for a calmer environment. --- Q28. How does a tariff shock affect equity vol? Tariffs introduce earnings uncertainty — margins compress, supply chains reprice, and the growth outlook becomes less predictable. This raises the equity risk premium and pushes implied vol up even before actual earnings impact. Sector dispersion also increases (domestic vs. international earners behave differently), making correlation assumptions less reliable. For a discretionary book with cross-asset exposure, tariff risk can manifest through equities, FX, and commodity prices simultaneously. ---

Q29. What is counterparty credit risk? The risk that a counterparty to a contract defaults before final settlement, leaving you with an unrealised gain you can't collect. It applies to OTC derivatives (swaps, forwards, OTC options), repo, securities lending — anything where settlement is in the future and the contract has positive MTM in your favour. Listed/cleared products (futures, exchange options) push this risk to the central counterparty (CCP) via daily margining, which is why post-Dodd-Frank a lot of swaps were forced into clearing. --- Q30. What is current exposure vs potential future exposure (PFE)? - Current Exposure (CE) = max(MTM, 0). The replacement cost today if the counterparty defaulted right now. Backward/present-looking. - Potential Future Exposure (PFE) = a high quantile (typically 95% or 99%) of the future exposure distribution at a given horizon, simulated forward. It answers "how bad could the exposure plausibly get before this trade matures?" PFE is generated by Monte Carlo: simulate market factors forward, revalue the netting set at each step, take the quantile of positive MTM at each horizon. The PFE profile is a curve over time — usually peaks somewhere in the middle of a swap's life (diffusion has built up but amortisation hasn't dominated yet). --- Q31. How are PFE, EPE, and EAD related? - PFE(t) = quantile of positive exposure at time t (tail of the distribution) - EE(t) = expected exposure at t = E max(MTM t, 0) - EPE = average of EE(t) over the life of the trade (mean of the curve) - EAD (Exposure at Default, Basel) ≈ alpha × EEPE, where alpha is typically 1.4 PFE is what you use for setting counterparty limits and trade approval. EPE feeds CVA (credit valuation adjustment) — the price you charge for counterparty credit risk in the trade. --- Q32. How do you mitigate counterparty risk? - Netting (ISDA Master Agreement) : aggregate all trades with one counterparty into a single net exposure rather than gross - Collateral (CSA) : post variation margin daily based on MTM moves; initial margin for residual gap risk - Central clearing : novate the trade to a CCP (LCH, CME); the CCP becomes counterparty to both sides - Credit limits : per-counterparty PFE caps, monitored pre-trade - Diversification : don't concentrate exposure to one bank - CDS hedging : buy protection on names you have large exposure to (less common buy-side, more common bank treasury) --- Q33. What is wrong-way risk? When your exposure to a counterparty increases at the same time the counterparty's credit quality deteriorates — the two are positively correlated. Classic example: buying CDS protection on Italy from an Italian bank. If Italy's sovereign credit blows out, your protection is worth more (bigger exposure) but the bank is also more likely to default. Or: Russian bank counterparty on a long-RUB FX forward in 2022 — RUB collapsed, your MTM gain on the trade went up while the bank's solvency simultaneously deteriorated. Specific wrong-way risk is

Q34. Walk me through a covered call. Long stock + short call (typically OTM). You collect the call premium, capping upside above the strike. Used by long-only managers to enhance yield on holdings they're comfortable being called away. Risk profile: keeps full downside, gives up upside above strike. Net delta is below 1 (long 100 shares + short ~30-40 delta call = ~60-70 net delta). --- Q35. Protective put? Long stock + long put (usually OTM). The put acts as insurance: floors your downside at the strike, costs you the premium. Equivalent payoff to a long call (put-call parity). Used to hedge a concentrated long position when you don't want to sell (tax reasons, conviction, mandate). The cost is the drag — buying puts continuously is expensive in steep contango vol surfaces. --- Q36. What's a vertical spread? When would you use it? Buy one option, sell another of the same type and expiry, different strikes. - Bull call spread : long lower-strike call, short higher-strike call. Bullish, cheaper than outright call, capped upside. - Bear put spread : long higher-strike put, short lower-strike put. Bearish, capped downside. Used when you have a directional view but want to cheapen the trade or define max loss. You give up the tail upside in exchange for a lower premium and known risk. --- Q37. Straddle vs strangle? - Straddle : long ATM call + long ATM put (same strike, same expiry). Bet on a large move in either direction. Expensive (two ATMs). - Strangle : long OTM call + long OTM put (different strikes). Cheaper, but needs a bigger move to pay off. Both are long vol / long gamma trades — used ahead of binary events (earnings, central bank, election) when you expect a move but don't know the direction. Risk: theta bleed. If the event passes without a move, IV crushes and both legs lose. --- Q38. What's a butterfly? An iron condor? - Butterfly : e.g. long 1 K1 call + short 2 K2 calls + long 1 K3 call (K1 < K2 < K3, equal spacing). Max profit if spot pins exactly at K2 at expiry. Defined risk, defined reward, cheap. Bet on low realised vol or pinning. - Iron condor : short OTM put spread + short OTM call spread. Profit if spot stays in the wings. Short vol trade with defined max loss. Common for premium-collection strategies in low-vol regimes. --- Q39. Risk reversal? Collar? - Risk reversal : long OTM call + short OTM put (or reverse). Synthetic forward exposure with skew bet baked in. The skew of the surface determines whether you pay or collect premium. Standard FX trade for expressing directional view. - Collar : long stock + long OTM put + short OTM call. Floors the downside, caps the upside. Often structured zero-cost (call premium funds the put). Used by executives with concentrated stock positions to lock in a band. --- Q40. Calendar spread? Long a longer-dated option + short a shorter-dated option, same strike. Profits from the front-month decaying faster than the back-month. Long vega, near-zero net delta at inception. Used to express a vie

Q41. What instruments would you expect to see in a discretionary cross-asset book? - Equities : cash equities (single names, ETFs, ADRs), index futures (ES, NQ, EuroStoxx, Nikkei) - Listed options : index options (SPX, SX5E), single-name options - Rates : government bonds (USTs, Bunds, OATs, JGBs), bond futures (ZN, ZB, RX), interest rate swaps, swaptions, OIS/SOFR - FX : spot, forwards, NDFs (for EM), FX options - Credit : corporate bonds (IG, HY), CDS single-name and indices (CDX IG, iTraxx Main) - Commodities : futures (WTI, Brent, gold, copper, nat gas), commodity options - Volatility : VIX futures and options, variance swaps A risk manager needs to map every position to its primary risk factors (delta, DV01, vega, CS01, FX delta) and aggregate consistently across asset classes. --- Q42. What's the difference between a future and a forward? - Future : exchange-traded, standardised, daily margined (no counterparty risk via CCP), liquid, cash or physical settlement. - Forward : OTC, customisable (size, date, underlying), no daily margining → counterparty risk accumulates, less liquid, typically physical settlement. Pricing under no-arbitrage is the same: F = S × e^((r-q)T). But forwards and futures can diverge slightly when rates are stochastic and correlated with the underlying (futures convexity adjustment). --- Q43. How does a swap work? What's an interest rate swap? A swap is an agreement to exchange cash flows. Vanilla IRS: one party pays fixed, the other pays floating (SOFR, EURIBOR), on a notional that's never exchanged. The PV at inception is zero (par swap rate is set so both legs are equal). Over time, as rates move, the swap accrues MTM. DV01 of a 5Y receiver swap with $100M notional ≈ $50K. Swaps are now mostly cleared (LCH SwapClear) post-Dodd-Frank, removing bilateral counterparty risk. --- Q44. Bond futures — what's the cheapest-to-deliver? Bond futures (ZN, ZB, RX) are physically settled against a basket of deliverable bonds. The short can choose which bond to deliver, and rationally picks the one that minimises their cost — the cheapest-to-deliver (CTD). The CTD has the highest implied repo rate among deliverables. The future tracks the CTD's price, scaled by a conversion factor. Risk manager point: a CTD switch can cause the future's effective duration to jump, so DV01 isn't static — it depends on which bond is currently CTD. --- Q45. What's a CDS? How does it work? Credit Default Swap. Protection buyer pays a periodic premium (the spread, in bps of notional). Protection seller pays par minus recovery if a credit event (bankruptcy, failure to pay, restructuring) occurs on the reference entity. Settled via auction (cash settlement) or physical delivery of a defaulted bond. Example: 5Y CDS on company X trades at 200bps. On $10M notional, buyer pays $200K/year. If X defaults with 40% recovery, buyer collects $10M × (1 - 0.40) = $6M. CDS indices (CDX.IG, iTraxx Main) are baskets of 125 names — used for macro credit views, much mor

Q48. What is the volatility smile / skew? Empirically, implied vol is not constant across strikes (as Black-Scholes assumes) — it varies. In equity indices, OTM puts trade at higher IV than OTM calls (a downward-sloping curve in strike) — that's "skew," priced in since the 1987 crash. In FX and single-name equities you see a more symmetric "smile." The shape of the surface tells you what the market is pricing for tail risk. Risk managers monitor skew because a steepening skew often precedes vol spikes — it's a market-implied stress signal. --- Q49. What's the term structure of vol? Contango vs backwardation in vol? The IV curve across maturities at fixed moneyness (usually ATM). - Contango (upward-sloping) : long-dated vol > short-dated vol. The "normal" regime — calm now, more uncertainty later. VIX futures usually in contango → systematic short-vol strategies earn the roll. - Backwardation (downward-sloping) : short-dated vol > long-dated. Stress regime — market expects the storm to pass. Happens during crises (March 2020, Aug 2024). Inversion of the VIX curve is a real-time stress indicator. --- Q50. What is realised vs implied vol? When does the spread matter? - Realised vol : backward-looking, computed from historical price moves (typically annualised stdev of log returns). - Implied vol : forward-looking, extracted from option prices. The spread (IV − RV) is the variance risk premium — what option sellers earn on average for taking on tail risk. Persistently positive (option buyers overpay for protection). When IV << RV, options are cheap relative to actual market chop — buying gamma is attractive. Risk managers track this because a book that's systematically short vol is collecting the premium but exposed to the tail. --- Q51. What's vanna and volga? Why do they matter? Second-order Greeks: - Vanna = ∂Delta/∂Vol = ∂Vega/∂Spot. How delta moves when vol changes (or vega when spot changes). Matters for skewed positions — a vol spike shifts your delta. - Volga (vomma) = ∂Vega/∂Vol. Convexity of vega. OTM options have high volga — they gain value disproportionately in a vol spike. A risk manager monitoring an exotic or barrier book needs vanna/volga because BS Greeks alone don't capture how the position behaves through a stress event. ---

Q52. Market liquidity vs funding liquidity — what's the difference? - Market liquidity : ability to exit a position without moving the price against you. Measured by bid-ask spread, depth, ADV. - Funding liquidity : ability to finance positions — access to cash, repo, prime brokerage. Margin calls, haircut increases, withdrawal of credit lines. The two are linked: a funding liquidity squeeze (e.g., LTCM 1998, Bear Stearns 2008) forces fire-sales, which destroys market liquidity, which forces more sales — the doom loop. A risk manager has to monitor both: position-level liquidity (how fast can I exit) and firm-level funding (can I survive a margin call). --- Q53. What is initial margin vs variation margin? - Variation margin (VM) : daily MTM movement. If you lose $1M today, you post $1M cash to the counterparty/CCP overnight. Settles the realised exposure. - Initial margin (IM) : a buffer posted upfront to cover potential losses between the last VM settlement and the close-out of the position if you default. Sized as a quantile (e.g., 99% 5-day VaR for cleared swaps; SIMM for uncleared). Risk implication: a vol spike inflates IM requirements (margin call) even if you haven't lost money — that's a funding shock. Archegos partly blew up because IM doubled across multiple prime brokers simultaneously. --- Q54. What is repo? Why does it matter for risk? Repo = sale of a security with agreement to buy it back later at a higher price. Economically, a collateralised loan: cash for bonds for a few days. The "haircut" is the lender's protection — lend $98 against $100 of collateral. Why it matters: hedge funds finance long bond positions via repo. If repo rates spike, or haircuts widen, the levered bond carry trade implodes (basis trade in March 2020, again in 2023). The September 2019 repo spike (rates to 10%) was a flash funding crisis with no obvious trigger — this is why the Fed runs the standing repo facility now. --- Q55. What's the basis trade and why is it a systemic risk concern? Hedge funds buy cash Treasuries, sell Treasury futures, and capture the basis (cash trades cheap to futures because futures embed financing). Levered ~50-100x via repo. Profitable in normal times — tiny spread × massive leverage. In March 2020, when futures sold off and repo seized up, funds were forced to unwind, dumping cash Treasuries → Treasury market broke → Fed had to intervene with $1T+ in purchases. SEC and Fed are now scrutinising it. For a discretionary book, the lesson: levered convergence trades can destroy you in a liquidity event even if the trade is "right." ---

Q56. What is P&L attribution? Why does a risk manager care? Decomposing daily P&L into the contributions from each risk factor: - Delta P&L : spot move × delta - Gamma P&L : ½ × gamma × (spot move)² - Vega P&L : vol move × vega - Theta : -theta × dt - Carry/financing : rates accrual - Residual : unexplained A clean attribution should leave a small residual. A large residual means either the model is wrong, the Greeks are stale, or there's a position you don't know about. P&L attribution is a daily sanity check that the risk view of the book matches the actual P&L — a key control on rogue trading and model error. --- Q57. What is VaR backtesting? Compare ex-ante VaR forecasts to actual realised P&L. For 95% 1-day VaR, you expect ~5% of days to breach (12-13 days/year). Count the breaches: - Too few → VaR is too conservative (capital inefficient) - Too many → model underestimates risk Formal tests: Kupiec POF (number of breaches), Christoffersen (independence — breaches shouldn't cluster). Basel "traffic light": green (≤4 breaches/year), yellow (5-9), red (10+) → capital multiplier increases. A red zone forces model review and regulatory escalation. --- Q58. What's a model risk? How do you manage it? Model risk = the risk that a model produces wrong answers and drives bad decisions. Sources: wrong assumptions (normality), bad calibration (stale data), implementation bugs, misuse outside the model's domain. Management: - Independent validation : a separate team revalues using a different model - Backtesting : compare model output to realised - Benchmarking : compare to industry standard or alternative model - Reserves : hold capital against model uncertainty - Use limits : cap exposure to instruments where model confidence is low The 2012 JP Morgan "London Whale" loss involved a VaR model swap that halved reported risk — classic model governance failure. ---

Q59. Why annualise vol by √T (square root of time)? Under Brownian motion, returns are independent and identically distributed, so variance scales linearly with time: Var(r T) = T × Var(r 1). Standard deviation scales with √T. So daily vol × √252 ≈ annual vol. Caveat: assumes IID returns — breaks down with autocorrelation (momentum/mean-reversion) or fat tails. In practice the √T rule is a useful approximation but not exact. --- Q60. If returns are normal, what's the probability of a 3-sigma move? About 0.27% (two-tailed) — roughly once every 370 trading days, ~1.5x per year. A 4-sigma move: 0.006%, once every ~16,000 days, ~63 years. A 5-sigma: once in ~3.5 million days. Punchline: real markets see "5-sigma events" several times per decade. Returns are NOT normal — they have fat tails. Risk managers know that the normal distribution underestimates tail probabilities by orders of magnitude, which is why historical and Monte Carlo VaR with realistic distributions matter. --- Q61. Two assets each with vol 20%, correlation 0.5, equal-weighted — portfolio vol? σ² p = w₁²σ₁² + w₂²σ₂² + 2 w₁ w₂ ρ σ₁ σ₂ = 0.25(0.04) + 0.25(0.04) + 2(0.5)(0.5)(0.5)(0.2)(0.2) = 0.01 + 0.01 + 0.01 = 0.03 σ p = √0.03 ≈ 17.3% If correlation were 1: portfolio vol = 20% (no diversification). If 0: ~14.1%. If -1: 0%. The diversification benefit comes entirely from correlation < 1. --- Q62. What's a Brownian motion? Why use it for asset prices? Continuous-time random process with: starts at 0, independent increments, normally distributed increments with variance proportional to time, continuous paths. Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM) for prices: dS/S = μ dt + σ dW. Used because: - Tractable (closed-form solutions like Black-Scholes) - Captures drift + volatility cleanly - Log-normal prices ⇒ no negative prices Limitations: constant vol (no smile), normal log returns (no jumps), continuous paths (no gaps). Real markets jump and have stochastic vol — modern models (Heston, SABR, jump-diffusion) extend GBM to address these. ---

Q63. What's the bid-ask spread? What drives it? Spread = ask − bid. The compensation a market maker requires to take the other side of your trade. Drivers: - Adverse selection : risk that you're informed → spread widens - Inventory risk : market maker holds the position until they offset → vol drives spread - Order processing cost : fixed minimum - Competition : more market makers → tighter spreads In stress, spreads explode (March 2020 Treasury market: 10x normal). For a risk manager, position sizing must account for execution cost in stress, not just normal-day spread. --- Q64. What is market impact? How do you size around it? Market impact = the price move you cause by trading. Square-root law (empirical): impact ≈ k × σ × √(volume / ADV). Trading 10% of ADV in a name with 30% vol moves the price ~10%×30%/√10 ≈ 1% against you. Implications: - Large positions can't be exited at "screen price" - Liquidation cost is a separate risk, not captured by market vol - Liquidity-adjusted VaR adds an exit-cost component Squarepoint runs systematic strategies — market impact modelling is core to their alpha capture. --- Q65. What is slippage? Difference between expected execution price and actual fill. Sources: bid-ask spread, market impact, latency, adverse selection on resting orders. A risk manager looks at slippage as a hidden cost that erodes strategy P&L over time — strategies that look profitable on paper can be unprofitable after realistic execution costs. ---

Q66. What is beta? How do you compute it? Beta = Cov(r asset, r market) / Var(r market). Slope of the regression of asset returns on market returns. Interpretation: - β = 1: moves with the market - β > 1: amplifies market moves (high-beta stocks: tech, cyclicals) - β < 1: dampens (utilities, staples) - β < 0: inverse (gold sometimes, VIX always) Risk manager use: a "market-neutral" book should have aggregate beta near zero. If beta drifts, the book has unintended directional exposure. --- Q67. What's a factor model? Name the standard ones. A model decomposing asset returns into systematic factor exposures + idiosyncratic. r i = α i + Σ β ij × F j + ε i. - CAPM (1-factor) : market only - Fama-French 3-factor : market + size (SMB) + value (HML) - Carhart 4-factor : + momentum - Fama-French 5-factor : + profitability + investment - Barra-style risk models : 50+ factors (industry, country, style) For a discretionary equity book, factor decomposition tells you: how much of P&L is alpha vs beta exposure? A trader who claims alpha but has 80% of returns explained by momentum factor isn't picking stocks, they're riding a factor. --- Q68. What is PCA in a risk context? Principal Component Analysis: decompose the covariance matrix of returns into orthogonal components ranked by variance explained. For a yield curve, typically: - PC1 (~80-90%): level shift (parallel) - PC2 (~5-10%): slope (steepening/flattening) - PC3 (~1-5%): curvature (butterfly) Used to: reduce dimensionality (hedge with 2-3 instruments instead of 10), build factor-based risk limits, identify regime changes (eigenvalue distribution shifts in crisis). ---

Q69. What biases ruin a backtest? - Look-ahead bias : using info not available at the time (e.g., today's revised earnings to trade yesterday) - Survivorship bias : testing only on companies still listed today (delisted = bankrupt = your strategy "avoided" them by accident) - Data snooping / p-hacking : testing 1000 strategies, picking the best — guaranteed to find spurious "alpha" - Overfitting : too many parameters relative to sample size — fits noise, not signal - In-sample vs OOS leakage : tuning on the test set - Transaction cost omission : ignoring slippage, market impact, financing - Regime bias : strategy backtested only in bull markets A risk manager validating a new strategy demands an out-of-sample test with realistic costs and an explanation of why the strategy should work (economic rationale), not just "the numbers look good." --- Q70. What's overfitting? How do you detect it? A model that captures noise rather than signal — high in-sample fit, poor out-of-sample. Detection: - Train/test split with degraded test performance - Cross-validation - Penalised regression (Lasso, Ridge) — punish large coefficients - Number of parameters vs sample size (rule of thumb: <1 parameter per 10 obs) - Robustness checks: small data perturbation should not change conclusions Every quant strategy is at risk. Overfit risk in a backtest = model risk in production. Risk managers ask: "show me the equity curve on data the model has never seen." ---

Q71. How do you hedge a long equity book? Options ordered by cost and precision: - Index futures short (ES, SX5E): cheapest, removes broad market beta. Residual = stock-specific risk + factor tilt. - Sector ETF shorts : hedges sector concentration if your longs are clustered - Single-name shorts (pair trade): if you have idiosyncratic conviction - Index put options : defined cost, kicks in only on downside, leaves upside. Premium drag in calm markets. - VIX calls : convex payoff in vol spikes — but VIX futures are in contango most of the time → bleed - Tail hedge funds / structured products : outsource the hedge, pay an explicit fee Risk manager perspective: every hedge has a cost (spread, theta, basis risk). The right hedge depends on what risk you're trying to remove and at what price. --- Q72. What is a tail hedge? Pros and cons of long puts vs long VIX? Tail hedge = position designed to pay off in extreme downside, not normal moves. - Long OTM SPX puts : precise downside payoff, expensive theta bleed if nothing happens. Cost ~1-2% of notional/year for 25-delta puts. - Long VIX calls or futures : convex payoff in vol spikes, but VIX futures in persistent contango → roll cost ~5-15%/year. You pay for the optionality. - Long gold / long Treasuries : cheap "soft" hedges that work in some crises (deflationary) but fail in others (stagflation) Universa-style tail hedging argues you should accept the bleed because the payoff in a tail event funds decades of losses. But for a discretionary book, the bleed is real P&L that competes with active strategy returns — most funds don't run permanent tail hedges. ---

Q73. What lessons did Archegos teach risk managers? Archegos (March 2021) — Bill Hwang's family office blew up, took $10B+ from prime brokers (Credit Suisse, Nomura, Morgan Stanley). Mechanics: held massive concentrated long positions in a few names (ViacomCBS, Discovery, Chinese ADRs) via Total Return Swaps (TRS) across multiple prime brokers. Each PB only saw their slice — none saw the aggregate exposure. When the names started falling, margin calls hit simultaneously, Archegos couldn't meet them, PBs unwound the TRS positions into a falling market. Lessons: - TRS hide leverage from public disclosure (no 13F filing required) - Prime brokers had no visibility into total client exposure across the street - Concentration risk: single positions were 10%+ of float - Margin models underestimated correlation in stress - Post-Archegos: SEC pushing for swap position disclosure, PBs raised IM significantly --- Q74. LTCM 1998 — what happened and what's the takeaway? Long-Term Capital Management ran arbitrage trades (convergence) at 25-30x leverage. Russia defaulted Aug 1998 → flight to quality → spreads widened instead of converging → losses → margin calls → forced unwinds in illiquid markets → death spiral. NY Fed organised a $3.6B bailout to prevent systemic contagion. Lessons: - Liquidity dries up exactly when you need to exit - Models assuming normal correlations fail in crisis (correlations → 1) - Leverage amplifies survivable losses into terminal ones - Even Nobel laureates with PhDs blow up - Position size matters more than position quality --- Q75. UK LDI crisis 2022 — what happened? UK pension funds used Liability-Driven Investment strategies — long Gilts + leveraged via repo to match long-duration liabilities. Sept 2022 mini-budget caused Gilt yields to spike 100bps in days → mark-to-market losses → margin calls → forced Gilt selling → yields spiked further → death spiral. BoE intervened with emergency Gilt purchases. Lessons: - Levered duration trades have non-linear blow-up risk in rate spikes - "Hedging" liabilities with leverage adds new risk - Coordinated forced selling creates feedback loops - Risk managers must model the funding side, not just the asset side ---

Q76. A senior trader pushes back hard on your risk view. How do you handle it? Don't escalate emotionally; escalate analytically. Steps: 1. Listen — they may know something you don't (e.g., a hedge offsetting the position you flagged) 2. Restate their argument back to them to confirm understanding 3. Show your data — exact position, the limit, the breach calculation 4. Ask: "If you're right, what would change my numbers?" Test their thesis against the model 5. If still in disagreement, escalate jointly to senior risk — not behind their back A risk manager isn't there to be liked or to win arguments. The job is to be right and to make the firm's risk explicit. Traders respect risk managers who do their homework and don't fold under pressure. --- Q77. Tell me about a time you made a mistake. FILL IN WITH REAL STORY — STAR format - Situation : brief context (project, role) - Task : what you were responsible for - Action : what you did wrong, when you realised, how you responded - Result : outcome + what you learned + what you do differently now Key: own it cleanly, don't blame, focus on the lesson and the change in behaviour. Interviewers want to see self-awareness and growth, not perfection. --- Q78. Why are you leaving Natixis? Natixis is a strong cross-asset shop and I've learned a lot . Two reasons: I want to be on the buy side where risk decisions tie directly to P&L, not regulatory capital. And Squarepoint specifically — the combination of systematic infrastructure with the growing discretionary book is a research environment that doesn't exist on the sell side. This isn't away-from, it's toward-something. ---

Q79. You flip a fair coin until you see two heads in a row. Expected number of flips? Let E = expected flips. Two states by progress: - From "0 heads in a row": flip → with p=½ go to "1 head", with p=½ stay at 0. E₀ = 1 + ½ E₁ + ½ E₀ - From "1 head in a row": flip → with p=½ done, with p=½ back to 0. E₁ = 1 + ½ × 0 + ½ E₀ Solve: E₁ = 1 + ½ E₀. Substitute: E₀ = 1 + ½(1 + ½ E₀) + ½ E₀ = 1.5 + ¾ E₀ → ¼ E₀ = 1.5 → E₀ = 6. Answer: 6 flips . --- Q80. Two assets, both with Sharpe 1, correlation 0.5 — what's the Sharpe of an equal-weighted portfolio? Equal weights w = ½. Both have same μ and σ (since same Sharpe). Portfolio: - Mean return: μ p = μ - Variance: σ² p = ½ σ² + ½ × 0.5 × σ² = 0.75 σ² → σ p = σ × √0.75 ≈ 0.866σ Sharpe p = μ / (0.866σ) = 1 / 0.866 ≈ 1.155 . Generalises: combining uncorrelated equal-Sharpe strategies improves Sharpe by √n. With correlation 0.5, you get less than √2 lift but still a real improvement. This is the math behind multi-strat funds. --- Q81. A stock is at 100, you sell a 105 call for $2 and buy a 95 put for $1. What's your max loss and max gain? This is a "risk reversal" (synthetic short with band): - If stock at expiry > 105: short call assigned → lose (S − 105) on call, +$2 premium, put expires → -$1. Net: -(S − 105) + 1. Loss grows linearly above 105 — unlimited max loss . - If 95 ≤ S ≤ 105: both options expire worthless. Net P&L = +$2 − $1 = +$1 (max gain region) . - If S < 95: long put pays (95 − S), call expires, premiums net +$1. Net: (95 − S) + 1. Gains as stock falls — max gain unbounded on downside (if no stock position). Note: this is a payoff question, not a directional bet — interviewer wants to see you draw the payoff cleanly. If asked the same with long stock + this collar overlay, max loss = stock @ 100 → put @ 95 = -$5 + $1 net premium = -$4 , max gain = stock @ 100 → call @ 105 = +$5 + $1 = +$6 . ---

1. How does the risk team interact day-to-day with the discretionary traders — is it reactive (breach alerts) or proactive (regular risk reviews)? 2. What does the first 90 days look like for someone in this role? 3. How are risk limits currently structured for the discretionary book — VaR-based, notional, or a combination?

Concept One-liner --- --- Stock An ownership claim on a company's residual cash flows — you get what's left after everyone else has been paid. Bond A loan that you can trade — the issuer borrows money, pays you regular interest, and returns principal at maturity. FX rate The price of one currency in terms of another — EUR/USD = 1.08 means one euro costs $1.08. Commodity A standardized physical good (oil, gold, wheat) traded on exchanges, mostly via futures rather than physical delivery. Credit The risk that a borrower won't pay you back — traded via corporate bonds (cash) and credit default swaps (synthetic). Duration How much your bond's price changes for a 1% move in rates — duration 7 means ~7% loss per 1% rate rise. Convexity The curvature of the price-yield relationship — tells you how inaccurate your duration estimate will be for large moves. Credit spread The extra yield a corporate bond pays above the risk-free Treasury rate — it's the price tag of default risk. Carry trade Borrow in a low-rate currency, invest in a high-rate currency, pocket the difference — works until a violent reversal. CDS An insurance contract — buyer pays a regular premium and gets paid if the reference company defaults. Crowding Too many funds own the same stocks — works until everyone tries to exit through the same door at the same time. Contango Front month cheaper than later months — storage costs make future delivery more expensive. Negative roll yield. Backwardation Front month more expensive than later months — tight supply now creates a scarcity premium. Positive roll yield. Yield curve Plots interest rates by maturity — tells you what the market believes about growth, inflation, and policy. ---

Master equation: Price = Earnings × Multiple Valuation Method What it does Limitation --- --- --- Relative (P/E, EV/EBITDA) Compare to peers/history "Cheap vs expensive" is relative, not absolute DCF Discount future cash flows at WACC 60-80% of value comes from terminal value — tiny assumption changes → huge output swings Sum-of-parts Value each segment separately Ignores synergies ---

Metric Formula Meaning Trap --- --- --- --- P/E Price / EPS Years of earnings you're paying for Useless for negative earnings Forward P/E Price / Next yr EPS Paying for future earnings Only as good as the estimate EV/EBITDA (Mkt cap + Debt - Cash) / EBITDA Price of operating business regardless of leverage Ignores capex FCF Yield FCF / Mkt cap Cash return on investment. Higher = cheaper Can be manipulated Dividend Yield Div / Price Current income High yield can signal distress ("yield trap") Short Interest Shares short / Float Bearish positioning. >15% elevated, >25% squeeze risk High SI might be correct Beta Cov(stock,mkt) / Var(mkt) Market sensitivity. 1.3 = 30% more volatile than market Backward-looking, changes in stress ---

Factor What it is --- --- Market (beta) Systematic risk — the tide that lifts or sinks all boats. Not diversifiable. Sector Same-industry correlation. 30 stocks isn't diversified if 20 are tech. Idiosyncratic Company-specific: earnings miss, fraud, CEO departure. Diversifiable across 30+ names. Value Cheap vs expensive. Outperforms long-term, can underperform for years. Momentum Recent winners keep winning — until violent reversals. Size Small caps outperform historically but with much higher volatility. Quality High-profitability companies are defensive in downturns. ---

Type Key feature Unique risk --- --- --- Treasury Risk-free benchmark Rate risk only Corporate IG Spread above Treasuries Rate + credit Corporate HY High yield, significant default risk Trades like equity in a crisis MBS Prepayment risk → negative convexity Lose on both sides: rates up (stuck) and rates down (prepaid early) TIPS Principal adjusts with CPI Real yield instrument Floating rate Coupon resets with SOFR + spread Very low duration Convertible Can convert to equity Hybrid: bond floor + equity upside. Has embedded option. ---

Measure Use case --- --- Coupon rate Cash income only. Not return. Current yield Better — uses market price. Ignores gain/loss at maturity. YTM Standard measure. Assumes reinvestment at same rate (unrealistic). YTW Use for callable bonds — issuer calls at worst time for you. Spread to benchmark Isolates credit risk. What credit traders actually watch. ---

Type What it measures --- --- Macaulay Weighted-average time to receive cash flows (years) Modified Price sensitivity: ΔP/P ≈ -D × Δy Effective For bonds with embedded options (MBS, callables) Key rate (KRD) Sensitivity to specific curve points (2yr, 5yr, 10yr, 30yr) What increases duration: Longer maturity, lower coupon, lower yield, zero coupon (duration = maturity). DV01 = Duration × Market Value × 0.0001 → dollar loss per 1bp rate rise. ---

Shape What it signals --- --- Normal (upward) Healthy economy, term premium Flat Slowing economy, uncertain outlook Inverted Market expects rate cuts → recession signal (every US recession since 1970 had one) Bear flattening Fed hiking aggressively → pre-recession Bull steepening Short rates falling faster → early easing ---

> Extract zero-coupon (spot) rates from observed market prices, one maturity at a time, each using previously solved rates. Maturity Instrument Method --- --- --- O/N – 6M T-bills, SOFR, repo Direct observation (zero-coupon → yield IS the spot rate) 6M – 2Y SOFR futures (SR3) Futures price → forward rate → compound to spot rate 2Y – 30Y Interest rate swaps Set PV(fixed leg) = 1, solve for unknown spot rate using known shorter rates The bootstrap logic: For an n-year swap at par rate $c$: - Discount each coupon by the spot rates you already know ($z 1, z 2, ..., z {n-1}$) - Solve for $z n$ — the only unknown - Repeat for each successively longer maturity What you get from spot rates: - Discount factors: $DF(T) = 1/(1+z T)^T$ — price ANY fixed cash flow - Forward rates: Market-implied rate between two future dates - Par rates: Coupon at which a bond prices at 100 (= the swap curve) Post-2008: Multi-curve bootstrapping — SOFR for discounting, separate curve for projection. Two curves, not one. RM relevance: Every derivative is priced off this curve. Different interpolation methods → different forward rates → different derivative prices and hedge ratios. ---

Rating Spread (bps) Default risk --- --- --- AAA/AA 20-60 Negligible — spread is mostly liquidity premium A 60-120 Very low BBB 120-200 Cliff edge — downgrade to BB triggers forced selling BB 200-400 Junk. First to blow out in stress. B 400-700 Significant default probability CCC+ 700+ Distressed. Trades on recovery value. IG vs HY: IG = rate bet. HY = equity bet in a bond wrapper. Don't confuse them. ---

Pair Personality --- --- EUR/USD Most liquid. US vs Eurozone rates/growth. Stable trends. USD/JPY Risk appetite + carry trade. Violent snap-backs on unwinds. BOJ intervention above 155. GBP/USD UK economy. More volatile. Flash crash risk. USD/CNH China managed float. Fix signals policy intent. EM (TRY, ZAR, BRL) EM risk barometers. Can move 5-10% in days. Liquidity vanishes in stress. DXY Dollar index (EUR 57.6%, JPY 13.6%, GBP 11.9%, CAD 9.1%). Standard "USD strength" reference. FX drivers (in order): Rate differentials → Growth differentials → Central bank policy → Risk appetite → Trade/capital flows → Intervention. ---

Commodity What drives it Key fact --- --- --- Crude Oil OPEC+, China demand, geopolitics (Hormuz), inventory Most important commodity — feeds into inflation, rates, FX, equities Natural Gas Weather, storage, LNG Extremely volatile. Amaranth blew up here ($6B loss). Gold Real rates (inverse), USD (inverse), safe haven Real rate hedge, NOT an inflation hedge per se Copper China industrial demand (50%+), EV transition "Doctor Copper" — leading indicator of global growth Wheat/Corn Weather, trade policy, fertilizer Supply shocks can be enormous (Ukraine 2022) Unit conversions: 1 barrel = 42 gal. 1 WTI contract = 1,000 barrels. 1 gold contract = 100 troy oz. 1 corn contract = 5,000 bushels. ---

- Buy protection: Pay spread quarterly. If default → receive (par - recovery). - Sell protection: Collect spread. Bear default risk. = Being long the credit. - Spread widening = more default risk = bearish. - CDS-bond basis = CDS spread minus cash bond spread. Large gap = liquidity stress or structural issue. Example: 5yr CDS at 200bps on $10M notional → buyer pays $200K/yr. If default with 40% recovery → buyer receives $6M. ---

section: RM RED FLAGS BY ASSET CLASS
content: Asset Class Red flag --- --- Equities Gross >200%, top 5 >25%, hidden factor concentration, any position >10% ADV Fixed Income DV01 exceeding budget, >80% in one maturity bucket, large negative convexity unaware FX Large unhedged position not matching PM view, concentrated EM carry Commodities Large long in deep contango (bleeding roll), approaching expiry with no roll plan Credit Sudden CDS widening in portfolio names, >30% BBB on negative watch, rising default rate >3%

Concept One-liner --- --- Physical Real barrels/tonnes changing hands for near-term delivery — producers, consumers, trading houses Cash/Forward OTC contracts for physical delivery priced vs a benchmark Futures Exchange-traded, standardized, margined — what financial players trade Contango Front month cheaper than back months — storage costs. Negative roll yield (you bleed on every roll) Backwardation Front month more expensive — tight supply now. Positive roll yield (you earn on every roll) Crack spread Product price minus crude price — the refiner's gross margin Spark spread Power price minus (gas price × heat rate) — the gas generator's margin Crush spread (Soy meal + soy oil revenue) minus soybean cost — the processor's margin Basis Price at a specific location vs the benchmark — reflects local supply/demand and logistics Heat rate Efficiency of converting gas to electricity (MMBtu/MWh). Lower = more efficient Merit order Generators dispatched cheapest to most expensive. The marginal unit sets the price for all Roll yield Gain or loss from rolling an expiring futures contract to the next month — can dominate returns ---

Benchmark Exchange Settlement What it represents --- --- --- --- WTI NYMEX (CL) Physical @ Cushing, OK US domestic. Light sweet. Can go negative (did in April 2020). Brent ICE (B) Cash-settled Global benchmark. ~75% of world crude priced off Brent. Dated Brent OTC (Platts) Physical North Sea cargoes The PHYSICAL benchmark. Futures converge to this. Dubai/Oman DME Physical (Oman crude) Asian sour crude benchmark. Prices Middle East → Asia flows. Brent-WTI spread: Normally Brent +$2-5. Reflects Atlantic Basin balance. Blew out to $25 in 2011 (Cushing glut). Crude quality: API gravity (higher = lighter = more valuable). Sulfur (sweet <0.5%, sour >0.5%). Light sweet commands a premium. ---

Product Exchange/Ticker Size Season What it is --- --- --- --- --- RBOB Gasoline NYMEX (RB) 42,000 gal Summer (May-Sep) Motor fuel ULSD (Diesel) NYMEX (HO) 42,000 gal Winter (heating) Diesel + heating oil Jet Fuel OTC (vs Platts/ULSD) No standard Year-round Aviation fuel, priced as crack to ULSD Fuel Oil (HSFO/VLSFO) ICE, SGX Various Year-round Shipping fuel. IMO 2020 split into VLSFO (0.5% S) and HSFO (3.5% S) Crack spreads: - Simple: Product − Crude - 3-2-1: (2 × RBOB + 1 × ULSD − 3 × Crude) / 3 = US Gulf refiner margin - Wide cracks → refiners maximize throughput → pulls crude demand - 2022 diesel cracks hit $70+/bbl (normal $15-25) — Russia sanctions ---

Region Benchmark Exchange Unit Price Range --- --- --- --- --- US Henry Hub (HH) NYMEX (NG) $/MMBtu ~$2-4 (cheap, shale) Europe TTF ICE Endex €/MWh Volatile ($30-340 in 2022) UK NBP ICE p/therm Trades vs TTF spread Asia JKM (Platts) OTC $/MMBtu Competes with TTF for LNG cargoes Conversion: 1 MMBtu ≈ 0.293 MWh. $3/MMBtu ≈ €9.5/MWh (rough). Key concepts: - Associated gas: Gas from oil drilling. More oil drilling → more gas → gas prices FALL even as energy demand rises - Storage report: EIA every Thursday 10:30 AM ET. Market trades deviation from consensus. - LNG arbitrage: Cargoes flow to whoever pays more — JKM > TTF → cargoes go to Asia; TTF > JKM → cargoes to Europe - Oil-indexed LNG: Asian long-term contracts priced as ~14.5% of JCC crude. $80 oil → $11.60 gas. - TTF became global LNG benchmark after 2022 energy crisis

Hub Where What it reflects Basis to HH --- --- --- --- Henry Hub Erath, Louisiana THE benchmark. 13 pipelines converge. — Waha West Texas (Permian) Oversupplied associated gas, pipeline-constrained Deep discount (sometimes negative) Dominion South Appalachia (Marcellus) Shale gas production, pipeline bottlenecks Discount Algonquin Citygate New England Northeast heating demand. Winter spikes. Massive winter premium ($10-30+ in polar vortex) SoCal Citygate Southern California West Coast power generation Moderate premium Chicago Citygate Midwest Heating + industrial. Well-connected. Small premium or parity AECO Alberta, Canada Canadian gas, depends on US export pipes Discount Transco Zone 6 NYC delivery Urban Northeast. Extreme winter premiums. Premium Katy Hub SE Texas Gulf Coast industrial + LNG export demand Near parity Why basis matters: In the 2014 polar vortex, HH = $4.50 while Algonquin = $78 — a 17x difference. Hedging New England exposure with HH futures covers almost nothing when pipelines are maxed. LNG export terminals: Sabine Pass (~4.2 Bcf/d), Freeport (~2 Bcf/d), Cameron, Calcasieu Pass, Corpus Christi. Feed gas data (gas flowing into terminals) is watched daily — drops signal operational issues. ---

ISO/RTO Region Key Feature --- --- --- PJM Mid-Atlantic + Midwest Largest US market. Capacity market. Coal/gas mix. ERCOT Texas (isolated) NO capacity market → extreme spikes. $9,000/MWh in Feb 2021 freeze. CAISO California Solar-heavy. Duck curve: oversupply midday, spike at sunset. NYISO New York Congested transmission. NYC ≠ upstate prices. ISO-NE New England Winter gas/power spikes (pipeline constraints). MISO Midwest Wind-heavy. Low prices when windy. SPP Central Plains Very wind-heavy. Negative prices when oversupplied. Key formulas: - Implied heat rate = Power price / Gas price → profitability of burning gas - Spark spread = Power − (Gas × Heat rate) → generator margin. Negative = uneconomic to run. - Dark spread = Power − (Coal × Emission cost) → coal generator margin. Negative prices: Power can go negative when renewables overproduce and generators pay to stay connected. ---

Metal LME Code Lot Size Key Driver Approx Price --- --- --- --- --- Copper CA 25 t China (50%+), EVs, green transition $8,000-10,000/t Aluminium AH 25 t Energy costs (smelting), China cuts $2,200-2,800/t Zinc ZS 25 t Galvanizing steel, mine supply $2,400-3,000/t Nickel NI 6 t Stainless steel, EV batteries, Indonesia $15,000-25,000/t Lead PB 25 t Car batteries, declining demand $2,000-2,300/t Tin SN 5 t Electronics soldering $25,000-35,000/t LME is unique: 3-month benchmark, daily prompt dates, physical delivery via LME warehouses, open-outcry ring. COMEX Copper: 25,000 lbs. Cents/lb. Convert: LME $/tonne ÷ 2,204.6 × 100 = cents/lb. Doctor Copper: Leading growth indicator — copper goes into everything (wiring, plumbing, EVs, grids). 2022 Nickel squeeze: Tsingshan short → Russia war spiked nickel → $25K to $100K in 24 hours → LME cancelled trades → credibility destroyed. ---

Metal Exchange Lot Size Key Driver --- --- --- --- Gold COMEX 100 troy oz Real rates (INVERSE), USD (inverse), central bank buying Silver COMEX 5,000 troy oz Gold ×0.8 correlation + industrial (solar, electronics) Platinum NYMEX 50 troy oz Diesel autocatalysts, hydrogen fuel cells, South Africa (70%) Palladium NYMEX 100 troy oz Gasoline autocatalysts, Russia (40%), South Africa Gold is NOT an inflation hedge — it's a REAL RATE hedge. Watch 10yr TIPS yield: falls → gold rises. Rises → gold falls. LBMA Gold Price: Set twice daily (10:30 AM, 3:00 PM London). Used by central banks, ETFs, physical contracts. Gold-Silver ratio: >80 = stressed. <65 = risk-on. ---

Product Lot Size Unit Key Driver --- --- --- --- Corn 5,000 bu cents/bu Ethanol (40% of US corn), Iowa/Illinois weather, China Soybeans 5,000 bu cents/bu China crush (70% of global soy imports), Brazil competition Wheat (SRW) 5,000 bu cents/bu Black Sea exports (Russia/Ukraine 30%), weather Soybean Oil 60,000 lbs cents/lb Biodiesel, palm oil competition Soybean Meal 100 short tons $/ton Animal feed Cotton 50,000 lbs cents/lb China/SE Asia textiles, US weather Live Cattle 40,000 lbs cents/lb Cattle cycle, feed costs, drought

Product Lot Size Key Feature --- --- --- Milling Wheat 50 t European benchmark (Euronext Paris) Rapeseed 50 t European oilseed, biodiesel (Euronext Paris) White Sugar 50 t Refined sugar (ICE London, $/t) Cocoa 10 t West Africa 60%+ supply. 2024: prices tripled (disease + weather) Robusta Coffee 10 t Vietnam 1. Red Flag Why it matters --- --- Position >5% of open interest You ARE the market — liquidation moves prices against you Large position near expiry, no roll plan Operational disaster — especially physically-settled (WTI) Large long in deep contango Bleeding roll cost every month regardless of spot "Diversified" commodities that are all growth bets Long crude + NatGas + copper = one macro bet, not three Unmonitored basis risk Hedging jet fuel with WTI ≠ hedged Margin increases during vol Forces liquidation at worst time Geopolitical binary (Hormuz, Black Sea) Overnight gaps with no hedge Fighting seasonality without thesis Long NatGas in April needs a strong reason

Concept One-liner --- --- Derivative A contract whose value is derived from an underlying asset — lets you take exposure without owning it Future A binding agreement to buy/sell at a set price on a set date — both sides OBLIGATED Option The RIGHT (not obligation) to buy/sell at a set price by a set date — premium is the price of that right Call Right to BUY at the strike. Want underlying UP. Put Right to SELL at the strike. Want underlying DOWN. Swap OTC agreement to exchange cash flows over time — typically fixed for floating IRS Exchange fixed-rate for floating-rate payments. Most traded derivative in the world. TRS Get total economic exposure (price + dividends) WITHOUT owning the asset — synthetic leverage (Archegos) CDS Insurance against default — buyer pays premium, seller pays if default occurs Variance swap Pure vol bet — pays realized variance minus strike. Short variance = unlimited downside (Volmageddon) Implied vol The vol the market is pricing into an option — backed out from the market price via Black-Scholes Put-call parity C − P = Forward. A call minus a put = synthetic forward position. Arbitrage relationship. ---

Type Exercise Pricing Model Where Used Cost vs European --- --- --- --- --- European Only at expiry Black-Scholes (closed-form) Index options (SPX), FX, most OTC Baseline American Any time before expiry Binomial tree (check early exercise at each node) US single-stock options (AAPL, MSFT) ≥ European (early exercise right has value) Bermudan Specific dates (e.g., quarterly) Lattice / Monte Carlo Swaptions, callable bonds Between European & American Asian At expiry, payoff on AVERAGE price Monte Carlo (path-dependent) Commodity hedging, FX hedging < European (averaging reduces vol) Barrier Knock-in/out at a price level Analytical or Monte Carlo Structured products, FX, commodities Cheaper (conditional activation) Digital At expiry, fixed payout if ITM Modified BS Structured products Depends on structure Why different models? BS works when you know WHEN exercise happens. Add optionality about WHEN (American) or path-dependency (Asian) → need numerical methods (trees, simulation). ---

Factor ↑ Calls Puts Why --- --- --- --- Higher implied vol More expensive More expensive More uncertainty → option has higher expected value More time to expiry More expensive More expensive More time for something to happen Higher interest rates More expensive Cheaper Forward price increases Higher dividends Cheaper More expensive Forward price decreases ATM strike Most time value Most time value Maximum uncertainty about ITM/OTM Deep OTM strike Cheap Cheap Low probability of payoff ---

Price = Intrinsic Value + Time Value Component Call Put --- --- --- Intrinsic max(0, S − K) max(0, K − S) Time value Premium − Intrinsic Premium − Intrinsic Moneyness: Deep ITM (δ ≈ ±0.90–1.00) → ITM → ATM (δ ≈ ±0.50, max gamma/time value) → OTM → Deep OTM (δ ≈ ±0.01–0.10) ---

Inputs: Spot (S), Strike (K), Time (T), Rate (r), Dividend yield (q), Volatility (σ) Output: Option price + Greeks Where it breaks: - Fat tails (crashes more frequent than bell curve) - Vol isn't constant (vol surface exists) - No continuous trading (gaps on earnings, weekends) - No early exercise (need binomial for American) ---

Strategy View Max Gain Max Loss Greeks --- --- --- --- --- Long Call Bullish Unlimited Premium +Δ, +Γ, −Θ, +ν Short Call Neutral/Bearish Premium Unlimited −Δ, −Γ, +Θ, −ν Long Put Bearish Strike − premium Premium −Δ, +Γ, −Θ, +ν Short Put Neutral/Bullish Premium Strike − premium +Δ, −Γ, +Θ, −ν Pattern: Long options = +Γ, +ν, −Θ (pay rent, get protection). Short options = −Γ, −ν, +Θ (collect rent, bear tail risk). ALWAYS.
