Strategy Construction View Key Insight --- --- --- --- Bull call spread Buy low-K call + sell high-K call Moderately bullish Caps upside, reduces cost. Defined risk. Bear put spread Buy high-K put + sell low-K put Moderately bearish Caps profit, cheaper than outright. Straddle Buy ATM call + ATM put Big move, direction unknown Most expensive vol bet. Double theta. Strangle Buy OTM call + OTM put Big move, cheaper than straddle Wider breakevens, needs bigger move. Collar Long stock + buy put + sell call Protect downside, cap upside Can be zero-cost. Most common hedge. Covered call Long stock + sell OTM call Income on existing position Synthetically = short put (put-call parity). Butterfly Buy 1 low + sell 2 mid + buy 1 high Pin at middle strike Cheap defined-risk low-vol bet. Iron condor Bear call spread + bull put spread Range-bound Defined-risk premium selling. Risk reversal Sell OTM put + buy OTM call Strong directional Zero/near-zero cost. Measures skew. Calendar spread Sell near-dated + buy far-dated (same K) Theta decay play Near decays faster than far.

Exotic What it is Key risk --- --- --- Barrier Knock-in/out at a level Pin risk near barrier, delta jumps Asian Payoff on AVERAGE price Cheaper, lower vol Variance swap Pure vol bet Short = unlimited downside Swaption Option on an IRS Vol-of-rates Convertible Bond + embedded equity call Complex Greeks, arb strategy ---

Risk What happens Example --- --- --- Leverage $500K premium controls $20M notional. Losses scale to notional. Barings (Leeson), futures leverage Non-linearity (gamma) Losses ACCELERATE: delta × move + gamma × move²/2 Any short gamma blowup Liquidity illusion Tight spreads in calm → no quotes in stress OTC derivatives in March 2020 Model dependence Model wrong → "hedged" position isn't hedged Feb 2018 vol models, LME nickel 2022 Margin cascade Vol up → margin up → forced selling → more vol → spiral Amaranth, MF Global Counterparty risk Counterparty defaults → you lose unrealized gains AIG 2008 ($182B bailout)

Concept One-liner --- --- Trade lifecycle Order → route → execute → clear (CCP novation) → settle (delivery vs payment) Market order Guaranteed execution, NOT guaranteed price. Can cross multiple levels in a thin book. Limit order Guaranteed price, NOT execution. You may never get filled. CCP Steps between buyer and seller → eliminates bilateral risk but creates concentration risk Novation CCP replaces original contract with two: A→CCP and CCP→B Settlement US equities T+1 (since May 2024). European equities T+2. FX spot T+2. ISDA Constitution of OTC derivatives — governs netting, close-out, events of default CSA Collateral framework — thresholds, eligible collateral, haircuts, rehypothecation Initial margin Deposit to OPEN a position. Performance bond. Variation margin Daily mark-to-market settlement. Losing side pays. Maintenance margin Minimum level. Below → margin call (post to IM, not maintenance). Pro-cyclical margin Exchanges RAISE margin in vol spikes → demand cash when you can least afford it Margin cascade Margin call → forced selling → price drops → more margin calls → spiral Repo Collateralized short-term loan. Sell securities today, agree to buy back tomorrow. Haircut Discount on collateral: post $100M bonds, receive $98M cash (2% haircut). Gross leverage (Longs + Shorts) / NAV → total exposure Net leverage (Longs − Shorts) / NAV → directional exposure Market liquidity Can you sell at fair value in size? Bid-ask, depth, ADV, impact. Funding liquidity Can you access cash? Credit lines, repo, margin availability. Liquidity spiral Market illiquidity → funding pressure → forced selling → more illiquidity → doom loop Short squeeze Forced buying feedback loop — shorts cover → price rises → more shorts forced to cover Days to cover Short interest / ADV. Above 10 = crowded short, squeeze risk. Gamma flip Price where dealer gamma goes from positive (dampening) to negative (amplifying moves) ---

Type Guarantees Risk Use case --- --- --- --- Market Execution ✓ Bad price in thin books Small/urgent, liquid names Limit Price ✓ No fill if moves away Have a target, can wait Stop Exit (once triggered) Gap through stop level Automatic risk management Stop-limit Price floor (once triggered) May not execute at all Want stop but refuse to sell below floor MOC Closing price Must submit before cutoff Benchmark to close, rebalancing Iceberg Stealth Can be detected by algos Large orders, minimize info leakage ---

Algo How Best for --- --- --- VWAP Volume-profile weighted Benchmark to VWAP TWAP Even time intervals Even execution Implementation Shortfall Front-loaded Minimize slippage from decision price Liquidity-seeking Finds pockets of liquidity Minimize impact at all costs POV (% of Volume) Fixed % of real-time volume Adapt to live conditions Market impact ∝ √(order size / ADV). Double size → ~40% more impact. ---

CCP Clears --- --- DTCC/NSCC US equities, corporates CME Clearing Futures, cleared IRS LCH (SwapClear) ~90% of global cleared IRS ICE Clear Energy futures, CDS OCC US listed equity options Eurex Clearing Euro Stoxx, Euro rates CCP waterfall: Defaulter's IM → Defaulter's default fund → CCP capital → Other members' default fund → Assessment. ---

Market Cycle --- --- US equities T+1 US Treasuries T+1 European equities T+2 FX spot T+2 (CLS Bank) Futures Daily MTM OTC derivatives Per ISDA/CSA Herstatt risk: Time zone mismatch in FX settlement → CLS Bank created to settle simultaneously. ---

Term What it means --- --- Netting Multiple trades → single net amount if default. Reduces exposure 80-90%. Close-out All trades terminated and netted on credit event. No cherry-picking. Threshold Below this, no collateral required. Higher = more unsecured exposure. MTA Minimum transfer amount. Small moves ignored. Haircut Discount on non-cash collateral. Rehypothecation Receiver re-uses your collateral. Efficient but risky (Lehman trapped $65B+). SIMM ISDA's standard model for bilateral IM calculation. Sensitivity-based. ---

Model Used by Method --- --- --- SPAN CME, futures exchanges 16 scenarios, worst-case loss TIMS OCC (equity options) Scenario-based SIMM Bilateral OTC (ISDA) Sensitivity-based (delta, vega, curvature) VaR-based Prime brokers Historical/MC VaR, 99%, 10-day ---

Term Detail --- --- Repo rate ≈ SOFR for UST repo Haircut UST: 1-2%. IG corp: 3-5%. HY: 10-25%. Equity: 15-25%. Term Mostly overnight (70%+). Also 1W, 1M, 3M, open. GC General collateral — any acceptable security Special Specific bond demanded — lower repo rate (scarce collateral) Reverse repo Mirror: LEND cash, receive securities Leverage via repo: Buy $100M bonds → repo for $99M → buy more → repo again → 50x leverage on $2M equity. A 2% move = total wipeout. ---

Profile Gross Net Interpretation --- --- --- --- Market neutral 6x 0.2x Lots of positions, little directional risk Levered directional 6x 4x Maximum risk Conviction, low lev 1.5x 1.2x Concentrated longs, modest leverage Conservative hedged 1.5x 0.1x Low leverage, well hedged Better than notional: Risk-adjusted leverage, DV01/NAV, marginal VaR leverage, effective leverage (gross × beta). Why leverage kills: -50% needs +100% to recover. -75% needs +300%. Margin calls force selling AT the bottom. ---

Tier Exit time Examples --- --- --- Tier 1 < 1 day G10 FX, SPY, index futures, on-the-run UST Tier 2 1-5 days Large-cap stocks, IG bonds, liquid options Tier 3 5-20 days Small-cap, HY bonds, EM credit, off-the-run UST Tier 4 > 20 days Distressed debt, exotic OTC, private placements Red flag: Tier 3+4 > 15% NAV for open-ended fund = redemption mismatch. ---

Dealer position They do Market effect --- --- --- Long gamma Buy dips, sell rallies DAMPENS moves, range-bound Short gamma Sell declines, buy rallies AMPLIFIES moves, vol spikes Gamma flip: Below this level → dealer short gamma → sell-offs accelerate. ---

Time Pattern RM implication --- --- --- 9:30-10:00 Opening vol, gap fills Noise, not signal 8:30/10:00 Econ data (CPI, NFP, PMI) Genuine information events 11:30-1:30 Lunch lull, thin books Don't trust moves 3:00-3:50 Power hour, real flow High conviction moves 3:50-4:00 MOC imbalance, close Large rebalancing flows ---

Level Trigger Action --- --- --- Level 1 SPX -7% 15-min halt (before 3:25 PM) Level 2 SPX -13% 15-min halt (before 3:25 PM) Level 3 SPX -20% Market closes for the day LULD Single stock outside band Individual halt ---

Component Detail --- --- Process Locate → borrow → sell → wait → cover (buy back & return) Borrow fee Easy: 0.25-0.50%. Hard-to-borrow: 5-50%+ You pay Borrow fee + dividends to lender You earn Short rebate on restricted cash proceeds Max loss Unlimited (price can rise infinitely) Buy-in risk Lender recalls → forced cover at market Squeeze signals: SI > 20% float, days-to-cover > 10, borrow utilization > 90%, rising borrow cost. ---

Metric Red flag --- --- Margin utilization > 70% in calm markets Stressed margin > 90% at VIX +20 Gross leverage > 4x for discretionary Days-to-close (DTC) Any position > 5 days Tier 3+4 % NAV > 15% for open-ended fund Overnight repo funding > 50% on long-dated assets Borrow utilization (shorts) > 80% on any position Bid-ask vs 30d avg > 2x normal = liquidity deteriorating

Greek One-liner Analogy --- --- --- Delta (Δ) Dollars gained/lost per $1 move in underlying. Your effective stock-equivalent position. Volume knob — controls how loud the stock plays through to your P&L. ITM=cranked to 10. ATM=halfway. OTM=barely a whisper. Gamma turns the knob. Gamma (Γ) How fast delta changes. Acceleration of P&L. THE most important Greek for risk. Airbag — long Γ deploys on impact (cushions + profits from crashes). Short Γ = airbag ripped out (damage accelerates). Theta (Θ) Daily rent for holding optionality. Long options pay it, short options earn it. Parking meter — ticks slowly far from expiry, eats quarters near expiry. Are you feeding it or do you own it? Vega (ν) P&L per 1-point change in implied vol. Your exposure to fear itself. Weather forecast, not the weather — you profit from the forecast getting scarier, not the storm itself. Rho (ρ) P&L per 1% change in interest rates. Minor for short-dated, material for LEAPS/FX. River current — short kayak trip = barely notice; multi-day expedition = carries you miles off course. Vanna How delta shifts when vol moves. Your hedges get stronger or weaker when fear arrives. Shape-shifter — directional exposure morphs when vol regime changes. Volga How vega changes when vol moves. Long volga = tail protection (vol convexity). Vol convexity — does a vol spike make things better or worse? Progressively. Charm Delta drifts overnight from time passing. Critical near expiry and over weekends. Silent thief — steals your hedge overnight while you sleep. Speed How gamma changes with spot. Important near barriers. Gamma of gamma — is your gamma itself stable or about to shift? Color How gamma evolves with time. Gamma spikes into expiry. Gamma's clock — measures how fast gamma concentrates approaching expiry. Dollar delta Δ × contracts × multiplier × spot. The REAL number — not option delta. — Dollar gamma P&L for 1% move from gamma = ½ × Γ$ × (%move)². Squared → accelerating. — Gamma/theta Efficiency metric: how much gamma per dollar of theta. Low = overpaying for optionality. — ---

Greek Formula Units --- --- --- Delta ∂V/∂S $/$ (per $1 move) Gamma ∂²V/∂S² = ∂Δ/∂S Δ per $1 move Theta ∂V/∂t $/day Vega ∂V/∂σ $ per 1 vol pt Rho ∂V/∂r $ per 1% rate Dollar gamma P&L = ½ × Γ$ × (%ΔS)² → always positive for long Γ, always negative for short Γ ---

Greek Long Call Short Call Long Put Short Put --- --- --- --- --- Δ + (0 to +1) − (0 to −1) − (−1 to 0) + (0 to +1) Γ + − + − Θ − (pay) + (earn) − (pay) + (earn) ν + − + − ρ + − − + Universal pattern: - LONG any option → +Γ, +ν, −Θ (always) - SHORT any option → −Γ, −ν, +Θ (always) - Only Δ and ρ differ between calls and puts ---

Greek Highest when Lowest when --- --- --- Gamma ATM + near expiry + low vol OTM/ITM + far expiry Theta ATM + near expiry OTM/ITM + far expiry Vega ATM + far expiry Near expiry (time too short for vol to matter) Key insight: Want gamma? → Buy near-dated ATM. Want vega? → Buy far-dated ATM. Different tenors, different Greeks. ---

Factor Call Δ Put Δ --- --- --- Spot ↑ → 1 (more ITM) → 0 (less ITM) Spot ↓ → 0 (more OTM) → −1 (more ITM) Vol ↑ OTM Δ rises, ITM Δ falls (compress to 0.50) Same compression Vol ↓ OTM Δ falls, ITM Δ rises (polarize to 0 or 1) Same polarization Time passes Same polarization as lower vol Same ---

Profile Gamma Theta P&L shape Risk --- --- --- --- --- Long Γ, short Θ + − Many small losses, occasional big wins Prolonged low vol → bleed to death Short Γ, long Θ − + Many small wins, occasional BIG losses One tail event wipes months of income No free lunch: Cannot have +Γ AND +Θ with vanilla options. Break-even: Gamma scalping breaks even when realized vol = implied vol. ---
Days to expiry Relative daily decay --- --- 90 1x (slow) 30 1.7x 7 3.6x 1 9.5x (explosive) $$\Theta \propto 1/\sqrt{T}$$ Weekend = 2 calendar days of theta for 1 day of hold. ---

Greek Formula One-liner Key insight --- --- --- --- Vanna ∂Δ/∂σ Shape-shifts your delta when fear arrives — hedges get stronger or weaker at the worst moment. Puts become better hedges in a vol spike. Drives dealer flows. Volga ∂ν/∂σ Convexity of your vol bet — vol spike makes things progressively better or worse. Long OTM wings = long volga = tail protection (vol convexity) Charm ∂Δ/∂t Silent thief — steals your delta hedge overnight while nothing moves. Delta-hedged Friday → unhedged Monday. Critical for 0DTE. Speed ∂Γ/∂S Gamma of gamma — is your gamma stable or about to shift? Important near barriers Color ∂Γ/∂t Gamma's clock — how fast gamma concentrates into expiry. Gamma spikes into expiry ---
SPX ↓ 1% → VIX ↑ ~2-4 pts (avg) SPX ↓ 5% → VIX ↑ ~15-25 pts SPX ↑ 5% → VIX ↓ ~5-8 pts (asymmetric!) Short vega is correlated with long delta. Both hurt in a sell-off. Compounding losses. ---

Bucket What drives it --- --- 0-30d Event risk, realized vol, gamma scalping 30-90d Earnings, vol term structure 90d-1Y Macro uncertainty, structural vol 1Y+ Monetary policy regime Can be flat total vega but long front/short back = vol term structure trade. ---

Limit Purpose --- --- Delta ±$XM Cap directional exposure Short gamma < $Y/1% Cap convexity risk Vega ±$Z/vol pt Cap vol exposure Theta −$W/day max Cap optionality bleed Concentration Max % Greeks from single name/strike Near-expiry gamma Reduce 5 days before expiry (pin risk) ---

Component Calculation P&L --- --- --- Delta (+$30M) +$30M × −5% −$1,500,000 Gamma (−$800K/1%) ½ × −$800K × 25 −$10,000,000 Vega (−$250K/pt) −$250K × 12 −$3,000,000 Theta (+$45K/d) +$45K +$45,000 TOTAL −$14,455,000 PM thought: "$30M delta, 5% drop = $1.5M." Reality: 10x worse. Short gamma + short vega = compounding tail losses. ---

Gamma: "Gamma tells you WHERE YOU'LL BE after a move. Delta tells you where you are NOW. For risk, gamma matters more." Gamma-theta: "No free lunch. Long gamma = pay daily rent for crash protection. Short gamma = earn rent but write tail insurance. RM checks: is the tradeoff fair? Is the gamma/theta ratio efficient?" Second-order: "Vanna makes your put hedges MORE effective in a crash (delta drifts in your favor). Volga makes short OTM puts INCREASINGLY dangerous (vega loss accelerates). These drive dealer flows that move billions daily." Vol crush: "Buying straddles into events: you need the realized move to exceed the implied move. If IV was 25% and realized is 10%, you lose on vega crush despite being 'right' about the event."

Concept One-liner --- --- Realized vol Measures how much the asset ACTUALLY moved — the backward-looking truth. Implied vol The market's consensus forecast of future volatility — backed out of option prices. It's the weather forecast, not the weather. Vol risk premium Implied vol is almost always higher than subsequent realized vol. The difference is the insurance premium — what makes selling vol profitable on average, and catastrophic in the tail. Skew Measures how much more expensive downside protection is relative to ATM options — it's the market's fear of crashes priced in real-time. Term structure Tells you whether the market expects current vol conditions to persist or mean-revert. Vol regime Vol doesn't just have a level — it has a REGIME. Regime shifts are the most dangerous events in finance because everything correlated changes simultaneously. VIX The 30-day expected volatility of SPX, derived from option prices. You CAN'T trade it directly — only through derivatives on VIX. Variance swap Pure vol bets — linear in VARIANCE (= vol²), which makes them convex in vol. Long variance is a crash bet on steroids. Vol vs correlation Vol tells you how much individual assets move. Correlation tells you whether they move TOGETHER — and in a crisis, they all do. ---

Concept Definition Key fact --- --- --- Realized vol StdDev of actual returns × √252 Backward-looking. Lookback window matters (5d vs 20d vs 252d). Implied vol Vol backed out of option prices (solve BS for σ) Forward-looking. Contains expected vol + VRP. VRP (Vol Risk Premium) IV − subsequent RV +2-4 pts SPX average. Positive ~85% of months. Makes selling vol profitable on average, catastrophic in tails. IV ≠ expected vol. IV = expected RV + insurance premium. ---

Estimator Inputs Advantage --- --- --- Close-to-close Log returns Simple standard. Misses intraday. Parkinson High, Low ~5x more efficient. Garman-Klass O, H, L, C Most efficient for continuous processes. Yang-Zhang O, H, L, C + overnight Best all-around. Handles gaps. ---
Two dimensions: Strike (skew) × Expiry (term structure)

- Equities: "smirk" — OTM puts have HIGHER IV than ATM. Pre-1987 this didn't exist. - Why: demand for puts + fat tails + leverage effect - 25Δ risk reversal = IV(25Δ put) − IV(25Δ call). SPX typical: −5 to −8. More negative = steeper. - 25Δ butterfly = ½ IV(25Δ put) + IV(25Δ call) − IV(ATM). Measures curvature/kurtosis. - Steep skew = fear. Flat skew = complacency. Sticky-strike vs sticky-delta: - Sticky-strike: vol stays at the strike → ATM vol rises when spot drops. Empirically correct for equities. - Sticky-delta: vol stays at the delta → ATM vol unchanged when spot moves. More FX. - Affects delta-hedging: different assumptions → different deltas.

Shape Name Meaning --- --- --- Upward Contango Normal — more uncertainty over longer horizons Flat Flat Transition Inverted Backwardation Near-term fear (event risk). Market expects resolution. Humped Humped Known event at specific future date ---

Model Key idea Strength Weakness Used for --- --- --- --- --- BS Constant vol Simple, closed-form Wrong Reference Local vol (Dupire) σ(S,t) deterministic Fits any surface Wrong dynamics (forward skew too flat) Exotics Heston Stochastic vol, mean-reverting Better dynamics Hard to calibrate Exotics, research SABR Stochastic vol, analytical Fast, industry standard Single-expiry, wing issues Rates/FX vanillas LSV Local + stochastic Best of both Complex Front-office exotics Rough vol Fractional BM (H≈0.1) Matches empirical dynamics New, expensive Research frontier

Param Controls Calibration --- --- --- α ATM vol level From market ATM vol β Backbone (β=0 normal, β=1 lognormal) FIXED (not calibrated) ρ Skew (spot-vol correlation) Negative ρ → downside skew ν Wings/curvature (vol of vol) Higher → steeper smile ---

Regime VIX Realized Term structure Correlation Character --- --- --- --- --- --- Low 10-15 5-12% Steep contango Low (15-25%) Grinding rally. Complacency. Short vol works. Normal 15-22 12-18% Mild contango Moderate (25-40%) Healthy. Standard risk. Elevated 22-35 18-30% Flat/mild backwardation High (40-60%) Corrections. Hedging expensive. Crisis 35-80+ 30-100%+ Sharp backwardation Very high (60-80%+) Panic. Liquidity gone. Models break. Asymmetry: Vol shifts UP in hours/days, shifts DOWN over weeks/months. Vol clustering: Big moves follow big moves. After a 3σ day, expect more. Don't relax. ---

VIX = 30-day SPX expected vol from OTM option prices (model-free, not BS). Daily move conversion: VIX / √252 → VIX 20 ≈ 1.26% daily move. VIX Daily move Context --- --- --- 10-14 ±0.6-0.9% Complacency. Protection cheap — BUY IT. 15-20 ±0.9-1.3% Normal. 20-30 ±1.3-1.9% Elevated. Tighten limits. 30-40 ±1.9-2.5% Fear. Daily IC calls. 40-60 ±2.5-3.8% Severe stress. 60-80+ ±3.8-5.0%+ Existential. 2020 record = 82.69. Properties: Negatively correlated with SPX (ρ ≈ −0.80). Mean-reverting. Non-tradeable directly. ---

Product Payoff Short risk --- --- --- Straddle ~Linear in vol Capped-ish Vol swap Linear in vol Limited Variance swap Linear in variance (vol²) → CONVEX in vol UNLIMITED Variance swap: RV of 40 vs strike of 20 → payoff on 1600−400=1200 of variance, NOT 40−20=20 of vol. ---

Dealer position Market effect Realized vol --- --- --- Long gamma (normal) Buy dips + sell rallies → DAMPEN RV < IV Short gamma (danger) Sell dips + buy rallies → AMPLIFY RV > IV Gamma flip level (GEX): SPX price where dealer gamma switches sign. - Above = suppressed, mean-reverting. Below = amplified, trending. Vanna flows: Vol change → delta change → forced dealer hedging → billions daily. Charm flows: Time decay → delta drift → systematic morning selling near OpEx. 0DTE: ~50% of SPX options volume. Massive intraday gamma. Pin/de-pin around round strikes. Traditional VaR doesn't capture this. OPEX: Week before = suppressed (gamma pin). Week after = potentially explosive (gamma removed). ---

$$\sigma {\text{index}}^2 \approx \bar{\sigma} {\text{stocks}}^2 \times \bar{\rho}$$ Correlation Meaning --- --- 20-35% Stocks independent. Stock-picking works. Diversification real. 40-55% Macro dominating. Sectors correlating. 60-80%+ Crisis. "All correlations → 1." Diversification illusion broken. Dispersion trade: Short index vol + long stock vol = short correlation. Collect premium. Lose in crisis. ---

Model Key idea --- --- EWMA (λ=0.94) Exponential weighting. Simple. No mean reversion. GARCH(1,1) Mean-reverting. α = shock sensitivity, β = persistence, α+β = persistence measure. GJR-GARCH GARCH + asymmetric term for negative returns (leverage effect). HAR-RV Multi-horizon realized vol inputs. Best out-of-sample performance. Implied vol More informationally efficient than time-series models (aggregates market info). Biased high (VRP). ---

Metric Red flag --- --- VIX level > 25 and rising VIX term structure Backwardation (inverted) IV/RV ratio < 0.90 (market underpricing risk) 25Δ risk reversal More negative than −8 Implied correlation > 60% Dealer GEX Negative (vol-amplifying) VVIX > 130 Cross-asset vol MOVE + EVZ + OVX all spiking = systemic ---

Regime Priority Action --- --- --- Low vol Buy hedges (they're cheap!) Flag short-vol concentration. Size tail risk. Rising vol Review hedge effectiveness. Stress test. De-risk short gamma. Add near-dated hedges. High vol Daily/intraday communication Tighten ALL limits. Hedge in back of curve (cheaper). Declining from elevated Don't rush back. Aftershocks common. Selectively sell vol. Roll hedges from near to far-dated. ---

IV vs RV: "Implied is the forecast + insurance premium. Realized is the actual weather. The gap is the VRP — why selling vol is profitable on average and catastrophic in the tail." Vol surface: "Skew = fear of crashes priced in. Term structure = how long the market expects current conditions to last. Together they determine every option price and every Greek." Dealer gamma: "Long gamma = dealers buy dips, sell rallies → suppress vol. Short gamma = opposite → amplify. Below the gamma flip level, the market loses its shock absorber." VIX: "Below 15 = buy protection while it's cheap. Above 30 = tighten everything. Backwardation = 'crisis is now.' Always check VVIX — uncertainty about uncertainty." Variance swaps: "Linear in variance, convex in vol. A realized vol of 40 vs strike of 20 doesn't pay 2x — it pays on 1600 vs 400 of variance. That's why short var swap blew up XIV."

> One-liner: VaR is the maximum loss at a given confidence level over a given horizon under NORMAL conditions — and it says absolutely nothing about what happens when conditions stop being normal. 99% 1-day VaR = $5M → "On 99/100 days, loss ≤ $5M. On day 100, it's WORSE — and VaR says nothing about how much worse."

Method How Best for Fatal weakness --- --- --- --- Parametric Normal dist × σ × z-score Linear instruments, fast Misses fat tails + nonlinearity (options) Historical Replay last N days, take percentile No dist assumption Hostage to history. Equal weighting. Ghost effects. Monte Carlo Simulate 10K+ scenarios, full reprice Options, exotics, complex books Expensive. Model risk. False precision. z-scores: 95% = 1.645 97.5% = 1.96 99% = 2.326 Time scaling: VaR 10d ≈ VaR 1d × √10 (crude but standard)

Type What it tells you --- --- Component VaR Position i's contribution to total VaR (sum to total, accounts for diversification) Marginal VaR ∂VaR/∂wᵢ — how VaR changes if you add $1 to position i Incremental VaR VaR(with position) − VaR(without position) — full add/remove effect

1. Not worst case — threshold only, silent on tail magnitude 2. Assumes normal conditions — crisis ≠ calm (correlations, liquidity, gaps) 3. Backward-looking — calm history → low VaR → just when risk is building 4. Not subadditive — VaR(A+B) can > VaR(A) + VaR(B) 5. Gameable — sell deep OTM options → low VaR, massive hidden tail 6. Point-in-time — EOD snapshot misses intraday 7. Correlation instability — calm-period matrix ≠ crisis matrix > "VaR is a speedometer, not a crash detector." ---

> One-liner: ES answers the question VaR refuses to ask — "When we breach VaR, how bad does it actually get?" $$\text{ES} {99\%} = E \text{Loss} \text{Loss} > \text{VaR} {99\%} $$ Property VaR ES --- --- --- Tail severity Blind Measures it Subadditive No Yes (coherent) Regulatory Old (Basel II/III) New (FRTB → 97.5% ES) Gameable Easy Much harder

Ratio Tail Typical position --- --- --- 1.0-1.5 Thin/normal Diversified equity, futures 1.5-2.5 Moderate Mixed books 2.5-4.0 Fat — investigate Short gamma, concentrated 4.0+ Cliff — DANGER Short OTM options, short var swap Normal distribution: ES/VaR = 1.15. Student-t (ν=5): ES/VaR = 1.83. Actual equity markets: 1.4-1.6 diversified, 2.0-5.0+ options books. > Rule: ES/VaR > 3.0 → investigate for hidden concavity. ---
> One-liner: VaR tells you what happens on a normal bad day. Stress tests tell you what happens on the WORST day — the one that actually threatens the fund's survival.

> One-liner: How much money you make or lose for a 1bp move in yield. THE unit of rates risk. Measure Definition Formula --- --- --- DV01 P&L per 1bp yield move ModDur × Price × Notional / 10,000 CS01 P&L per 1bp credit spread widening SpreadDur × MV / 10,000 KRD Duration by tenor bucket Captures non-parallel curve moves (steepener/flattener) JTD Jump-to-default loss (1 − Recovery) × Notional. Concentration risk. DV01 ladder matters more than total DV01. A portfolio with DV01 = 0 can still have +$50K/bp at 2Y and −$50K/bp at 30Y = massive steepener bet. ---

Key: Rp = annualized portfolio return. Rf = risk-free rate (T-bill/OIS). σp = annualized portfolio vol. σ down = downside deviation (only below-target returns). Metric Formula What it measures Good level --- --- --- --- Sharpe (Rp − Rf) / σp Return per unit of total risk 0.7-2.0 (HF). >3.0 = suspicious. Sortino (Rp − Rf) / σ down Return per unit of downside risk Higher than Sharpe = positive skew (good) Calmar Ann. return / Max DD Return per unit of worst loss >1.0 = very good IR (Rp − Rb) / TE Consistency of benchmark outperformance >0.5 = good Omega Gains above threshold / Losses below Full distribution comparison >1.0

1. Symmetric penalty — punishes upside vol equally with downside 2. Short-vol gaming — selling tail risk → high Sharpe until blowup 3. Time noise — need ~4 years monthly data to distinguish Sharpe 1.0 from 0 4. Factor-blind — doesn't separate alpha from beta > Quick diagnostic: Sortino << Sharpe → negative skew hidden → investigate.

$$R p = \alpha + \beta R m + \epsilon$$ - β > 0.3 → significant market exposure (not pure alpha) - Multi-factor: remove market + size + value + momentum → residual = real alpha - PM claims alpha but has β momentum = 0.8? That's factor exposure, not skill. ---

$$\text{Fund VaR budget} = \Sigma \text{ Desk allocations} + \text{Reserve}$$ Concept Definition --- --- Utilization Actual VaR / VaR budget. Normal = 60-90%. Over-utilization >100%. Requires position cut or IC approval. Diversification benefit Total fund VaR < sum of desk VaRs (if uncorrelated) RAROC Desk P&L / VaR allocation. Risk-adjusted performance. ---

> One-liner: A position isn't just risky because it can lose value — it's risky because you might not be able to EXIT when you need to. $$\text{LVaR} = \text{VaR} + \text{Liquidation Cost}$$ $$\text{Days to Liquidate} = \frac{\text{Position Size}}{\text{ADV} \times \text{Participation Rate (10\%)}}$$ Tier Liquidity DTL VaR treatment --- --- --- --- 1 (Liquid) ADV > $50M < 3d Standard 1-day VaR 2 (Semi) ADV $5-50M 3-10d √DTL scaling 3 (Illiquid) ADV < $5M 10-30d √DTL + stress spread 4 (Very illiquid) No secondary mkt 30d+ Full position at risk ---

> One-liner: A VaR model that's never tested is just an expensive guess. Backtesting asks: "How many times did actual losses exceed VaR?" and if the answer is too many (or too few), the model is broken. 99% VaR, 250 days → expected exceptions: 2.5/year Exceptions Basel zone Action --- --- --- 0-4 🟢 Green Model OK 5-9 🟡 Yellow Under review. Higher multiplier. 10+ 🔴 Red Model rejected. Kupiec test: Are exceptions the right NUMBER? Christoffersen test: Are exceptions INDEPENDENT (not clustered)? Conditional coverage: Both combined (gold standard). Result Diagnosis --- --- Too few exceptions VaR too conservative → capital inefficiency Correct + evenly spaced Well-calibrated Too many exceptions VaR underestimates risk → dangerous Clustered exceptions Model doesn't adapt to regimes → switch to EWMA/FHS ---

> One-liner: Every risk measure is the output of a model. If the model is wrong, the risk measure is worse than useless — it creates false confidence. Source Risk --- --- Wrong distribution VaR underestimates tails 2-5× Stale parameters Correlations from calm → wrong in crisis Missing risk factors "Unknown unknowns" Wrong pricing model Greeks wrong → hedges leak Implementation bugs Most common actual source of failure P&L explain: Model-predicted P&L vs actual. Persistent gap = model is wrong. ---

VaR limitations: "Speedometer, not crash detector. Five flaws: tail-blind, assumes normality, backward-looking, not subadditive, gameable. Supplement with ES, stress tests, and judgment." ES vs VaR: "Both have same VaR = $5M. Portfolio A's ES = $34M (short OTM puts — concave). Portfolio B's ES = $6.5M (diversified equity — linear). Only ES reveals Portfolio A is 5× more dangerous." Stress testing: "Three layers: historical (did we survive 2008?), hypothetical (what if Taiwan?), sensitivity grids (where does P&L break?). Fourth: reverse stress test — start from the loss, find the scenario. If it's plausible, hedge now." Sharpe: "Standard metric but three fatal flaws: symmetric vol penalty, short-vol gaming, small-sample noise. Always check Sortino — if Sortino << Sharpe, there's negative skew hiding." Drawdown: "−20% needs +25% to recover. −50% needs +100%. That's why hard limits exist — past −20%, recovery becomes a multi-year project. Past −50%, the fund is done."
Quick reference for correlation, diversification, factors, concentration, basis, tail, liquidity, construction, and the RM dashboard ---

Concept One-liner --- --- Correlation A chameleon — it looks stable in calm markets and shape-shifts violently the moment you need it to stay put. Diversification The only free lunch in finance — until the restaurant closes during a crisis and you discover everyone was eating at the same table. Factor models Look through the 500 individual positions and tell you the 8 real risks driving the fund — because 500 positions with the same factor exposure is not diversification, it's a leveraged bet on that factor. Concentration A portfolio with 100 names can be more concentrated than a portfolio with 10 — if those 100 names all load on the same factor, you don't own 100 bets, you own one. Basis risk The risk that your insurance policy pays out on a DIFFERENT house than the one that burned down. Fat tails Normal distributions say a 5-sigma daily move happens once every 14,000 years. Markets produce them every few years. Any risk framework built on normality is a framework for losing money in the tail. Liquidity risk A position isn't risky because it can lose value — every position can lose value. It's risky because you might not be able to EXIT when you desperately need to. RM vs PM A PM constructs portfolios for return. The RM constructs CONSTRAINTS for survival. The intersection — portfolios that maximize return subject to risk constraints — is where the real edge lives. Dashboard Your cockpit — the 20 numbers that tell you whether the fund is safe or whether you need to act before the next market open. ---

Formula: ρ = Cov(X,Y) / (σ X · σ Y). Range: −1 to +1. Danger property What it means --- --- Regime-dependent ρ from calm period ≠ ρ in crisis Asymmetric ρ is HIGHER in sell-offs than rallies Non-stationary Drifts even within a regime Only linear ρ=0 ≠ independent (tail dependence can exist) Estimation error 500 assets → 124,750 pairwise ρ → massive noise Key table: Pair Calm ρ Stress ρ --- --- --- SPX vs single stocks 0.30 0.70+ SPX vs HY credit 0.50 0.85+ SPX vs UST 10Y −0.30 −0.50 (usually) BUT +0.40 in 2022 Cross-PM portfolios 0.10-0.20 0.50-0.80 Models: Rolling window → EWMA (λ=0.94) → DCC-GARCH → Factor-implied → Copula Eigenvalue diagnostic: Dominant eigenvalue explains >60% → portfolio is ONE factor bet disguised as many positions. ---

Formula: σ p = σ √(1/N + (N−1)/N · ρ̄). Floor = σ√ρ̄ (can never diversify below this). Diversification Ratio: DR = Σ(w i · σ i) / σ p. DR → 1.0 = no diversification. DR > 1.5 = good. Effective Number of Bets: ENB = DR² or Shannon entropy of eigenvalues. N positions ρ=0.0 ρ=0.20 ρ=0.40 ρ=0.80 --- --- --- --- --- 1 25% 25% 25% 25% 10 7.9% 12.1% 15.0% 21.1% 100 2.5% 11.2% 14.1% 20.7% ∞ 0% 11.2% 15.8% 22.4% > Most benefit captured by 15-20 uncorrelated positions. After that, reduce CORRELATION, not add names. When diversification fails: Crisis → ρ spikes → DR drops → portfolio vol jumps → margin calls → forced selling → more ρ → SPIRAL. ---

Return decomposition: r i = α + Σ β ik · f k + ε i Three families: Type Examples Strengths Weaknesses --- --- --- --- Fundamental (Barra/Axioma) Market, Size, Value, Momentum, Quality, Low Vol + sectors Pre-defined, interpretable Misses novel factors Macro SPX, Δ10Y, ΔIG spread, ΔWTI, ΔDXY, ΔVIX Direct economic meaning May miss micro factors Statistical (PCA) PC1 ≈ market, PC2 ≈ growth/value, PC3 ≈ size No assumptions, data-driven Hard to interpret, unstable Factor risk contribution: Factor k % = (β² pk · σ² k) / σ² p Alpha check: R² high + α ≈ 0 → PM is selling factor exposure at 2/20 fees.

HHI: Σ w i². Effective N = 1/HHI. HHI Effective N Level --- --- --- 0.01 100 Very diversified 0.05 20 Moderate 0.10 10 Concentrated 0.50+ ≤2 Single bet Five types: Type What it is How to detect --- --- --- Single-name One stock dominates HHI, position % of NAV Sector Same industry everywhere Sector weights Factor Hidden common exposure Factor regression, PCA Counterparty One PB holds too much PB margin allocation Crowding Same trade across industry 13F overlap, short interest Crowding blow-ups: Quant quake (Aug 2007), Short vol (Feb 2018), Momentum reversal (Sep 2020), Japan carry (Aug 2024). ---

Definition: Basis = hedged asset price − hedge instrument price. Unstable basis → hedge fails. Hedge effectiveness: R² = 1 − Var(hedged) / Var(unhedged). R² > 0.95 = good. R² < 0.50 = not a hedge. Optimal hedge ratio: h = ρ × σ position / σ hedge. Naive 1:1 leaves basis risk. Worst case: Lose on BOTH position AND hedge (2022: long IG bonds hedged with Treasuries → credit AND rates both lost). ---

Sigma event Normal says Reality Underestimation --- --- --- --- 3σ 1 in 370 days 1 in 50-70 days ~6× 4σ 1 in 126 years 1 in 2-3 years ~50× 5σ 1 in 3.5M days 1 in 20-30 years ~500× Excess kurtosis: SPX daily ≈ 20-25. Normal = 0. EVT (Generalized Pareto): Shape parameter ξ > 0 → power-law fat tails. Equities ξ ≈ 0.2-0.4. Tail dependence: Gaussian copula has λ L = 0 → MISSES joint crashes. Student-t or Clayton copula captures tail co-movement.

Tiering: Tier 1 (ADV >$100M, DTL <1d) → Tier 4 (bespoke OTC, DTL 20+d, full notional at risk). ---

Use Half or Quarter Kelly. Over-betting decreases growth. Full Kelly is too volatile. Vol targeting Leverage = σ target / σ realized De-risks in high-vol. Procyclical short-term.

"Portfolio risk beyond VaR?" → "Five layers: ES + ES/VaR ratio for tail shape, factor decomposition for what's DRIVING risk, stress tests for regime breaks, liquidity analysis for exit capacity, cross-desk aggregation for hidden factor concentration. Most critical at a multi-PM fund: eigenvalue decomposition of inter-desk correlation — if first eigenvalue is >60%, your 'four independent desks' are actually one bet." "Most underappreciated risk in a multi-PM fund?" → "Hidden factor correlation across PMs. Each PM is independent but they load on the same macro factors. In calm markets, idiosyncratic masks the common factor. In stress, correlations spike and you discover you have 1.5 effective bets, not 4. Fix: daily cross-desk factor aggregation + aggregate factor limits + effective-number-of-bets metric." "Is a PM generating alpha or factor exposure?" → "Multi-factor regression. R² high + α ≈ 0 = selling factor as alpha. Need α positive + t-stat >2.0 + ~4 years of data to confirm skill." "Build a risk dashboard from scratch?" → "Six layers: Exposure (size of bet) → Concentration (is it spread or stacked) → Factors (nature of bet) → Risk measures (how much can we lose) → Liquidity (can we exit) → Drawdown (where are we vs survival limits). Most important single metric: ENB from eigenvalue decomposition."
Quick reference for Greek-based and factor-based P&L decomposition, patterns, P&L explain, and fraud detection ---

Concept One-liner --- --- P&L attribution Splits yesterday's dollar outcome into the building blocks that caused it — so you know whether the PM was RIGHT about their thesis, LUCKY from market moves, or EXPOSED to a risk they don't understand. Greek vs factor Greek-based attribution tells you WHICH INSTRUMENTS drove P&L. Factor-based attribution tells you WHICH BETS drove P&L — market direction, sector rotation, style tilt, or genuine stock selection. P&L explain Compares what your model PREDICTED the P&L should be against what ACTUALLY happened. The gap is your model error — and persistent gaps mean your risk system is lying to you. Signature reading Certain P&L signatures are diagnostic — like a doctor reading an ECG, the RM reads daily P&L to diagnose the risk the PM is actually taking. Daily brief The PM wants to know three things: Am I up or down? Why? What changed in my risk? Deliver all three in 30 seconds or less. Fraud detection Every major rogue trading scandal has one thing in common — the P&L didn't match the risk. If P&L attribution had been taken seriously, the fraud would have been caught earlier. ---

$$\text{Total P\&L} = \Delta \text{P\&L} + \Gamma \text{P\&L} + \Theta \text{P\&L} + \mathcal{V} \text{P\&L} + \rho \text{P\&L} + \text{Cross} + \text{Unexplained}$$ Component Formula What it measures --- --- --- Delta Σ δ i × ΔS i Directional bet — was the PM right? Gamma ½ Σ γ i × (ΔS i)² Convexity payoff — always positive for long γ Theta Σ θ i × Δt Time decay — rent for holding options Vega Σ ν i × ΔIV i Vol change — is PM secretly a vol trader? Rho Σ ρ i × Δr Rate impact — small for short-dated, big for FI Cross Vanna, charm, volga, speed Normally <5% of total. 20-30% in crises. Unexplained Actual − Attributed Red flag if >5%. Investigate immediately if >15%. Theta-gamma breakeven: $\Delta S {BE} = \sqrt{2 \theta /\gamma}$ — move needed for gamma to offset theta. ---

Ratio What it reveals Red flag --- --- --- Vega % of total Vol trader or directional? >50% → PM is trading vol, not stocks Theta % of total How much is carry income? >70% consistently → short gamma collector Gamma / Theta (cumulative) Fair rent for gamma? <0.5 → overpaying for protection Cumulative theta / Tail loss Pennies-vs-steamroller check <0.1 → earning $2M to risk $20M Delta % of total Pure directional bettor? >90% → just riding beta Factor / Total Alpha or factor exposure? >80% → delivering factor, not skill Unexplained / Total Model working? >15% → escalate Cross-effects / Total Higher-order risk material? >20% → crisis regime, need full repricing > Quick diagnosis: Theta = 75% of P&L → short gamma collector (Sharpe looks great, tail will destroy). Vega = 87% of P&L → vol trader who might not know it. ---

$$R p = \alpha + \sum k \beta k f k + \epsilon$$ Layer What it answers --- --- Greek Which INSTRUMENTS drove P&L? Factor Which BETS drove P&L? (market, momentum, rates...) Alpha vs factor Is the PM skilled or just delivering factor exposure? Carry vs active Passive income vs active trading decisions? Brinson (long-only): Allocation effect + Selection effect + Interaction effect. Rates desk: P&L = Carry + Rolldown + Duration + Curve + Spread + Convexity + Residual. ---

Vol added $3.3K, theta cost $750. Flag: effective delta grew via gamma — now 12,600 vs 12,250 yesterday." ---

$$\text{P\&L Explain Ratio} = \frac{\text{Predicted P\&L (from Greeks)}}{\text{Actual P\&L}}$$ Ratio Quality Action --- --- --- 0.95-1.05 Excellent No action 0.80-0.95 Acceptable Monitor 0.60-0.80 Poor Formal review <0.60 Failed Model is wrong — escalate Gap patterns: - Always positive → conservative Greeks (capital inefficiency) - Always negative → underestimates losses → DANGEROUS - Spikes on big-move days → higher-order terms missing → need full repricing - On specific names → wrong pricing model for those positions ---

Flag: growing position / approaching limit / catalyst / basis drift ." 30 seconds. Done. Instead of... Say... --- --- "Your position is too risky" "Effective delta grew 30% via gamma. Intentional?" "You're bleeding theta" "Options cost $80K/day. What's the thesis for holding?" "You're just running momentum" "85% of monthly alpha is momentum factor. View on sustainability?" ---

"Explain a PM's daily P&L?" → "Two dimensions: Greek-based (delta = direction, gamma = convexity, theta = time, vega = vol) and factor-based (market, sector, style, macro → residual alpha). If PM thinks they're trading direction but 60% is vega → they're a vol trader. If 90% is momentum factor → they're selling factor at alpha prices. Unexplained >5% → model is missing something or worse." "Unexplained P&L keeps growing?" → "Escalating red flag. Quantify (2% = noise, 15% = broken model). Check direction (always negative = DANGEROUS). Diagnose: stale prices? Wrong Greeks? Missing risk factor? If it persists after data/calibration checks → model whose VaR and stress tests should not be trusted." "Short gamma — how to identify?" → "P&L signature: consistent positive theta, near-zero gamma P&L on calm days, then massive loss on big-move day. Sharpe looks great pre-blowup. Track cumulative theta vs worst-case tail. If $2M theta vs $20M tail loss → 10:1 against. Ensure drawdown limits can absorb the tail." "Alpha vs factor?" → "Multi-factor regression. R² shows how much is explained. Alpha intercept needs t-stat >2.0 + 3-4 years of data. Plot cumulative alpha vs factor over time — flat alpha + rising total = pure factor delivery."

Concept One-liner --- --- No free hedge Every form of protection extracts payment in some currency — premium, upside, basis risk, complexity, or illiquidity. The RM's job is to make the currency explicit. Diagnose first Before picking an instrument, diagnose the disease. A put doesn't help if the risk is basis. A futures hedge doesn't help if the risk is convexity. Match the hedge to the SPECIFIC risk. Static vs dynamic Static hedging is "set and forget." Dynamic hedging is "adjust as conditions change." Both have costs — dynamic costs execution/slippage; static costs missed optimization opportunities. Greek impact Adding a hedge doesn't just reduce risk — it changes your ENTIRE Greek profile. A put adds gamma, theta, vega, and cross-Greeks that interact with the existing book. Roll cost Rolling a hedge is not neutral — you're closing one position at a loss (theta decayed) and opening a new one at market price (potentially higher IV). The roll is where hedge cost compounds. False confidence A hedge that doesn't perform in stress is worse than no hedge — because it created FALSE CONFIDENCE that the position was protected. ---

section: THE FIVE TRADEOFFS (EVERY HEDGE HAS A PRICE)
content: Tradeoff Tension PM question --- --- --- Cost vs protection Better protection = more premium "How much drag can your Sharpe absorb?" Precision vs liquidity Single-name = perfect but expensive; Index = cheap but basis risk "Exact coverage or directional protection?" Horizon mismatch Position = no expiry; Hedge = expires → roll costs "Event (1 week) or regime (6 months)?" Upside surrender Collars/call overwriting cap gains "Max return you'd accept for zero premium?" Timing Cheapest when you don't need it; most expensive when you do "Pay insurance when the sun is shining?" ---

1. What SPECIFIC risk? → Delta, vega, gamma, DV01, credit, concentration, tail, basis, liquidity 2. What horizon? → 1-5 days (weekly options) 1-4 weeks (monthlies) 1-6 months (quarterlies) 6M+ (LEAPs/rolling) 3. Upside tolerance? → None (puts) Capped (collar) Partially capped (put spread) 4. Premium budget? → Max ~15% of expected alpha for hedge cost 5. Basis risk accepted? → R² > 0.90 (excellent) 0.70-0.90 (good) < 0.70 (reconsider instrument) ---

Instrument Cost Gap protection Convexity Upside impact Best for --- --- --- --- --- --- Outright put High (1-3% ann.) Yes Yes (Δ+Γ+ν) None Event risk, full protection Put spread ~50% of put Yes (capped) Partial None Budget hedging, moderate corrections Collar ~Zero Yes (floored) ~Zero (cancels) Capped Zero-premium constraint Short futures Margin only No (linear) None Loses upside proportionally Temp delta/beta reduction Pairs (L/S) Borrow cost No None Reduces net exposure Alpha isolation VIX calls Carry cost Indirect Yes (on vol) None Crash/vol spike protection Deep OTM puts 1.5-3% ann. Yes HIGH None Tail/survival protection ---

Beta hedge sizing: $$N = \frac{V p \times (\beta {\text{current}} - \beta {\text{target}})}{V f}$$ Optimal hedge ratio: $$h^ = \rho \times \frac{\sigma {\text{position}}}{\sigma {\text{hedge}}}$$ Hedge effectiveness: $$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{Var(hedged)}}{\text{Var(unhedged)}}$$ Delta-hedged P&L: $$\text{P\&L} = \frac{1}{2}\Gamma(\Delta S)^2 - \Theta \Delta t$$ Breakeven daily move: $$\Delta S {\text{BE}} = \sigma {\text{implied}} \times S \times \sqrt{\Delta t}$$ Tail hedge EV: $$\text{EV} = P(\text{tail}) \times \text{Payoff} - (1 - P(\text{tail})) \times \text{Annual cost}$$ ---

Hedge Δ Γ Θ ν Net effect --- --- --- --- --- --- Long put − + − + Safer, costs carry, more convex Put spread − (mod) Mixed Less − Mixed Cheaper, less convex, capped Collar ~Modest − ~0 ~0 ~0 Budget-neutral, bounded, near-zero Greek change Short futures − 0 0 0 Pure delta, no convexity VIX calls ~0 on SPX ~0 − + Vol hedge, not delta hedge Key insight: Puts get BETTER as crash deepens (gamma + vega spike). Futures respond linearly. The deeper the crash, the greater puts' advantage. ---

Instrument Frequency Cost/roll Annualized --- --- --- --- Monthly OTM puts 12×/yr 0.15-0.30% 1.8-3.6% Quarterly OTM puts 4×/yr 0.35-0.60% 1.4-2.4% 6-month puts 2×/yr 0.60-1.00% 1.2-2.0% Equity futures 4×/yr 1-2bp ~0.05% VIX futures Monthly 3-5%/month 30-50%+ (!!) Rule: Roll at ~2 weeks before expiry (monthlies), ~3 weeks (quarterlies). ---

1. Hedging too late → Buy when VIX is LOW, not after the sell-off 2. Over-hedging → Theta drag > alpha = portfolio can't make money 3. Wrong instrument → R² < 0.70 = find a better instrument 4. Set-and-forget → Roll calendar with alerts, no coverage gaps 5. Ignoring roll costs → Monthly puts = 3%+ annualized drag 6. "Free" collar illusion → Quantify upside surrender in DOLLARS 7. Wrong Greek → Futures = delta only; puts = delta + gamma + vega 8. Correlation-dependent → Monitor ρ weekly, have Plan B ---

Regime Stocks-bonds ρ Implication --- --- --- Deflation/growth scare (1998-2021) −0.30 Bonds hedge equities well Inflation regime (2022-2023) +0.40 Bonds AMPLIFY equity losses Financial stress (March 2020) +0.50 Only cash protects ---

section: HEDGING BY ASSET CLASS — QUICK MAP
content: Asset Primary hedge Secondary --- --- --- Equity β Short SPX futures SPX puts Single-name Single-name put Collar, partial exit Sector Short sector ETF Sector puts Duration Treasury futures IRS Credit CDS Short HY ETF FX Forwards FX options Commodities Futures Options on futures ---

"Hedge concentrated equity?" → Framework first (5 questions) → Single-name put (cleanest, expensive) → Put spread (halves cost, caps protection) → Collar (zero cost, caps upside) → Futures (free but basis risk, R² ≈ 0.6 for single name vs index) → Make tradeoff explicit to PM "When NOT to hedge?" → (1) Cost > risk: hedge EV is negative → (2) Hedge adds MORE risk via fragile correlation → (3) PM's thesis IS the risk and it's within limits "Puts vs futures?" → Futures: free, liquid, delta-only, linear, no gap protection → Puts: premium cost, delta + gamma + vega, convex, GAP protection, hedge IMPROVES as crash deepens "How evaluate hedge effectiveness?" → R² overall + R² stress on tail days → If R² stress << R² overall, hedge gives false confidence

Concept One-liner --- --- Sizing The PM decides WHAT to trade. The RM helps decide HOW MUCH. Sizing is the largest single driver of portfolio P&L variance. Liquidity ceiling You can't size a position larger than you can EXIT. Liquidity is not a soft constraint — it's a hard wall. Conviction Isn't "I feel strongly." It's a structured assessment of variant perception, catalyst, downside, and edge durability — each of which can break independently. Risk budget A CONCESSION of trust from the firm. Breach it and you lose trust. Manage it well and you earn more. RM monitoring role The PM builds the portfolio. The RM monitors whether the portfolio the PM ACTUALLY has matches the portfolio they MEANT to have. Position lifecycle Every position has a birth, a life, and a death. The RM tracks all three stages with a different lens at each. Scaling The PM's primary expression of changing conviction. The RM ensures scaling decisions are CONSCIOUS, not reactive. Risk contribution A 5% position in a high-beta, high-correlation stock might contribute 20% of portfolio VaR. The RM's job is to make RISK CONTRIBUTION visible, not just dollar weight. Morning dashboard Every morning, the RM should be able to answer 10 questions about the book in under 60 seconds by looking at their dashboard. ---

Framework Formula / Rule Best for --- --- --- Equal weight $w i = 1/N$ New desk, unproven PM, high-turnover stat arb Conviction-based Tier 1: 5-8%, Tier 2: 2-4%, Tier 3: 0.5-2%, Tier 4: 0.25-0.5% Discretionary PM with structured conviction Vol-weighted Notional = Target risk / σ i Equal risk contribution per position Kelly $f^ = (pb - q)/b$ → use ¼ or ½ Kelly in practice Theoretical ceiling on sizing Max-loss Notional = Acceptable loss / stop-loss distance (%) Risk-budget-driven sizing Vol-weighted quick example: $200K risk budget per position → JNJ (15% vol) gets $1.33M, TSLA (50% vol) gets $400K. ---

Pillar Question If missing → --- --- --- Variant perception What do I see the market doesn't? No edge Catalyst What event + WHEN? Dead money Downside How much can I lose? Position unsized Edge durability Why won't it get arbed away? Already priced Score: 4/4 → Tier 1 3/4 → Tier 2 2/4 → Tier 3 1/4 → Tier 4 0/4 → Reject ---

1. Is the thesis intact? → If yes, hold/add 2. Has NEW INFORMATION arrived? → If yes, reassess from scratch (price movement alone ≠ new info) 3. Would you put this on TODAY? → If no, the position is alive only because of anchoring. CUT IT. ---

$$\text{Max notional} = \text{ADV}(\$) \times \text{Participation rate (15\%)} \times \text{Max exit days}$$ Check Threshold --- --- Days to exit (normal) < 5 days Days to exit (stress @ 0.5× ADV) < 10 days Position as % of ADV < 100% Estimated impact < 1% one-way Ownership % of float < 5% Market impact: $\text{Impact} \approx \sigma \times \sqrt{Q/V} \times k$ ---

Zone Budget consumed Key restriction --- --- --- 🟢 Green 0-25% None 🟡 Yellow 25-50% Daily review, run broken-thesis tests 🟠 Orange 50-75% Gross capped 80%, no new Tier 1 positions 🔴 Red 75-100% Gross capped 60%, only risk-reducing trades ⚫ Stop-out 100% All positions closed/hedged flat ---

Dimension Soft Hard --- --- --- Daily P&L loss −$500K −$1M Monthly P&L loss −$2M −$4M VaR (1d 99%) $3M $5M Gross $80M $100M Net ±$30M ±$50M Single name 5% NAV 8% NAV Sector 25% gross 35% gross Top 5 40% gross 55% gross ---

Metric Formula --- --- Gross \ L\ + \ S\ Net L − S Net/Gross Net / Gross (0 = neutral, 1 = fully long) Beta-adjusted net Σ(w i × β i) — true directional exposure Trap: PM says "I'm modestly net long at $30M" but β-adjusted net = $43M because longs have higher beta than shorts. ---

Metric Green Yellow Red --- --- --- --- Top 1 (% gross) <8% 8-12% >12% Top 5 (% gross) <35% 35-50% >50% Sector (% gross) <20% 20-30% >30% Correlated cluster (ρ>0.7) <15% 15-25% >25% Effective N >15 8-15 <8 ---

$$\text{Component VaR} i = w i \times \beta {i,p} \times \text{VaR} p$$ Key insight: A 15% dollar-weight position with high vol + high correlation can be 33% of portfolio VaR. The RM makes RISK CONTRIBUTION visible, not just dollar weight. ---

Stage RM checks --- --- Entry Risk budget, marginal VaR, correlation, factor exposure, liquidity (days to exit), conviction tier match, crowding, hedge plan Hold Daily: P&L + VaR. Weekly: thesis + factors + correlation. Monthly: full review. Exit (profit) Challenge disposition effect (cutting winners too early) Exit (loss) Run 3 broken-thesis tests Exit (forced) Limit breach, liquidity event, compliance, redemption ---

Adding: Only if (1) thesis confirmed by new data, (2) risk budget has room, (3) PM pre-planned the scale-in Trimming: When (1) valuation target hit, (2) position grew beyond conviction tier, (3) concentration risk, (4) risk budget constraints Red flag: PM averaging down without passing broken-thesis tests = loss aversion disguised as conviction ---

"PM at 85% risk budget wants new position?" → Check MARGINAL VaR → If diversifying (low ρ), might barely move budget → If correlated, could push to 95% → Make tradeoff explicit → "Trim something or accept the risk?" "How know position is too large?" → 3 lenses: (1) Risk contribution >25% of VaR = portfolio IS that position, (2) >5 days to exit = illiquid, (3) Stop-loss would consume >30% of monthly loss budget "How manage a drawdown?" → Structured escalation: 25% = conversation, 50% = restrictions, 75% = defense mode, 100% = stop-out "PM keeps averaging down?" → Show data: (1) position grew from Tier 2 to Tier 1 by price drop not conviction, (2) risk contribution doubled, (3) needs 15% recovery just for breakeven → PM must pass 3 broken-thesis tests to justify

Concept One-liner --- --- PM vs trader bias Traders face a different bias landscape than PMs because they make HUNDREDS of decisions per day. PM biases compound over weeks. Trader biases compound within hours. RM immunity You're not immune. Recognizing your OWN biases is what separates an effective RM from a compliance box-ticker. Detection Don't wait for the P&L to tell you there's a bias problem. Build a detection system that flags behavioral patterns BEFORE they destroy returns. Fixing biases You can't fix biases by telling people to be less biased. That doesn't work. You fix them by building STRUCTURAL countermeasures into the trading process. Confronting PMs Telling a PM "you're biased" is like telling someone "you're ugly." It's accurate but counterproductive. The skill is in making the pattern visible without triggering defensiveness. ---

Bias PM version Trader version Detection --- --- --- --- --- 1 Anchoring Won't sell below entry price Won't take a loss on fill price "Would you buy this TODAY?" 2 Confirmation Re-interprets all data as bullish Reads order flow selectively "What would make you wrong?" 3 Loss aversion Holds losers, cuts winners Revenge trading, refuses stops Avg win:loss ratio < 0.5 4 Disposition effect Sells winners early, holds losers Scalps 5 wins, lets 1 loss run Hold period: winners < losers 5 Overconfidence "90% probability" → 60% hit rate "I feel the market today" Conviction-outcome calibration 6 Recency Sizes up in low vol regime Sizes on trailing streak Size vs trailing P&L R² > 0.3 7 Thesis refitting 4 thesis changes, same position "Now it's a mean-reversion trade" Monthly thesis audit

Bias What it is P&L signature --- --- --- Revenge trading Increasing size after a loss to "make it back" Good AM → cliff PM Action bias Trading when correct action is to wait High turnover + flat P&L Gambler's fallacy "Down 5 days → due for a bounce" Counter-trend losses Hot hand fallacy Sizing up on a win streak Oversized loss breaks streak Sunk cost "I've spent $350K rolling — can't close now" Escalating committed capital

Bias Danger Fix --- --- --- Authority Defer to senior PM Document concern IN WRITING Outcome "PM made money so process was fine" Evaluate process, not result Normalcy "Never happened → won't happen" Run reverse stress tests Complacency Green dashboard 6 months → stop reading Rotate focus weekly Anchoring to models "VaR says $4M so $4M is risk" Triangulate: VaR + ES + stress + concentration

Metric Detects Red flag --- --- --- Avg win $ / Avg loss $ Disposition + loss aversion < 0.5 Hold period: wins vs losses Disposition effect Winners held < ⅓ the time Realized − unrealized P&L gap Loss aversion Large positive gap growing Post-loss size change Revenge trading Size up > 30% after loss day Thesis change count Thesis refitting > 2 changes Conviction vs outcome Overconfidence No difference in hit rates Turnover vs P&L Action bias High turnover, flat P&L Size vs trailing 5-day P&L Recency / gambler's R² > 0.3 13F overlap Herding > 40% shared with top HFs Drawdown recovery time Loss aversion hangover > 2 weeks to restore gross

Bias Fix --- --- Anchoring Pre-trade thesis sheet with exit criteria Confirmation Mandatory devil's advocate on Tier 1 positions Loss aversion Mechanical stops — remove human from exit Disposition Quarterly "keep 5 of 15" portfolio clean-out Overconfidence Position limits requiring IC override Recency Regime-aware stress tests (2008, 2020, 2022) Thesis refitting Monthly thesis audit vs original Revenge trading Mandatory pause at 50% daily budget consumed Action bias Track turnover-to-P&L ratio Herding 13F overlap + short interest monitor

Do say Don't say --- --- "Would you buy this today at $88?" "You're being biased" "The thesis has evolved A→B→C. Would you put this on fresh?" "You're loss-averse" "Size is up 40% today — opportunity or morning P&L?" "You should cut this" "What's the strongest bear case?" "I told you so"

Right-and-early Wrong-and-stubborn --- --- --- Thesis Same as inception Changed 2+ times Catalyst Specific, not yet invalidated Original catalyst passed or failed Language Specific: "15% EPS beat on margin expansion" Vague: "it's just cheap" Fresh-eyes test "Yes, I'd buy this today" Hesitation or narrative shift

- Detecting bias: "I look at DATA patterns: thesis changes, win/loss size ratios, post-loss sizing, hold-period asymmetry" - Right-and-early vs wrong: "Same thesis + specific catalyst not invalidated vs thesis changed 2+ times + vague language" - Own biases: "Outcome bias (evaluate process not P&L), authority bias (document and hold ground), normalcy bias (proactive stress tests)" - Communication: "Surface the pattern without accusing — data-driven questions, not labels"
Concept One-liner --- --- Multi-strat model Understanding how multi-strats allocate capital, charge costs, and manage pods is critical because Squarepoint operates this model. ---

Vehicle Jurisdiction Investors Key feature --- --- --- --- Offshore Cayman/BVI Non-US, tax-exempt US Tax-neutral entity Onshore Delaware LP US taxable Pass-through (K-1) Master-feeder Both feed → Cayman master All Most common large-fund structure Managed account Investor custody $500M+ allocators Full transparency, LP controls UCITS Ireland/Luxembourg European Strict regulation, daily NAV

$$\text{Buffer ratio} = \frac{\text{Equity} - \text{Maintenance requirement}}{\text{Equity}}$$ Target: 15-25%. De-risk before PB forces you.
Collateral Haircut Rate vs SOFR --- --- --- UST on-the-run 1-2% Flat to +5bp Agency MBS 3-5% +10 to +30bp IG credit 5-8% +25 to +75bp Equities 25-50% +40 to +150bp

PM payout example ($500M allocation): - Gross P&L: +$30M - Less costs (financing, tech, data, infra): −$14M - Net P&L: +$16M - PM payout (15%): $2.4M Pod termination triggers: - −5% drawdown → 50% de-risk, warning - −10% → full liquidation, stop-out - Repeated limit breaches → reduced allocation

Risk Key metric --- --- --- 1 Redemption liquidity Days to liquidate 25% of NAV 2 Leverage fragility Margin buffer ratio 3 PB counterparty PB CDS spread, rehypothecation level 4 Fee incentive PM distance below HWM 5 Pro-cyclical margin Buffer under 2× margin stress 6 Correlation convergence Firm VaR at crisis correlations

Market stress → Leveraged P&L loss → PB margin increase → Forced selling → Market impact → LP redemptions → More selling → Repo lines pulled → Fire sale → Fund terminates RM job: Break the chain at step 2 by maintaining margin buffer and pre-planning liquidation order.

- Structure & risk: "Liquidity mismatch (quarterly redemptions ≠ position liquidity), leverage fragility (3× means 5% move = 15% NAV hit), pro-cyclical margin (PBs tighten exactly when you're losing), HWM behavioral pressure" - Margin call: "Three phases: (1) PB notification, (2) respond — post cash, post securities, or reduce positions (sell liquid low-conviction first), (3) aftermath — reassess portfolio for another call" - Multi-strat risk: "Pod correlations spike in stress (0.3 → 0.85). Diversification benefit vanishes. Must stress-test firm VaR at crisis correlations, not normal-market."

Not easily (alpha decays with AUM) Better (fewer positions)

- Run weekly regression of disc returns vs systematic factor loadings - If R² > 0.3 against any factor → NOT diversified, DOUBLED up - Solutions: factor overlay hedge, shift to idiosyncratic positions, or CRO reduces firm gross

Element Systematic Discretionary --- --- --- Position limits Coded — model can't exceed Policy — PM needs IC to override VaR limits Auto-constrained in optimizer Calculated daily, PM notified Stop-loss Algorithmic, automatic Discussion-based + escalation Factor neutrality Enforced by construction Monitored, intentional tilts OK Drawdown Vol-scaling auto-reduces Escalation ladder (manual) Thesis docs N/A Required at inception, audited monthly Kill switch Halt all trading instantly Full de-risk (2-5 days)

section: Quant Tools Applied to Discretionary Risk
content: Tool Application --- --- Factor decomposition "60% of variance is momentum — you have factor risk, not alpha" Regime detection "High-vol regime → adjust position sizes to reflect higher per-position VaR" Correlation heatmap "PC1 explains 70% of variance — one bet, not eight" Scenario engine "500 scenarios: worst 10 all involve China hard landing" Alt data "Short interest hit 25% — headline risk incoming"

- Why discretionary RM at a systematic firm? "Judgment matters most day-to-day. I use the quant toolkit (factor decomp, stress tests, correlation) to support a PM where each position has a story, catalyst, and thesis I can audit. Quantitative rigor + human judgment intersection." - Cross-desk correlation? "Weekly regression of disc returns vs systematic factors. R² > 0.3 → doubled up, not diversified. Present data to PM + CRO with solutions: factor hedge, idiosyncratic shift, or gross reduction." - Biggest challenge? "Credibility both ways. CRO wants quant language; PM wants behavioral intelligence. Infrastructure built for 3,000 positions needs qualitative overlays for 40. Supplement VaR with stress + scenario + thesis audit."

Fund What killed it Category --- --- --- Barings (1995) No segregation of duties Operational LTCM (1998) PBs withdrew financing at 25× leverage Counterparty + structural Knight Capital (2012) Bad code deployment, no kill switch Technology Archegos (2021) TRS opacity across 5 PBs Counterparty opacity

Concept Definition --- --- CVA Price of counterparty credit risk: EE × PD × LGD DVA Price of YOUR credit risk to them (perverse: deterioration → accounting gain) FVA Cost of funding collateral (funding spread − OIS × duration) PFE Max expected exposure at 95% confidence Wrong-way risk Exposure ↑ as counterparty creditworthiness ↓ Netting ISDA — net across all trades with same CP → reduces exposure 50-90% CSA Collateral agreement: frequency, threshold, eligible collateral, haircuts
$$\text{CVA} \approx \text{EE} \times \text{PD} \times \text{LGD}$$ Example: $15M EE × 10% cumulative PD × 60% LGD = $900K CVA charge

Trade Counterparty Scenario Why it's wrong-way --- --- --- --- Buy CDS on Italy Italian bank Italy stress Bank weakens when payout owed Long oil via TRS Energy bank Oil crash Bank's portfolio also bleeding Short USD/buy TRY Turkish bank TRY crisis Exposure grows as bank weakens

section: Regulatory Landscape — What Affects a Hedge Fund
content: Regulation Key impact on HF --- --- Basel III/IV + SLR PB capital constraints → less leverage, higher financing cost FRTB PBs: ES replaces VaR, illiquid instruments cost more → flows to HF Dodd-Frank Volcker Rule, CCP mandates, Form PF reporting EMIR EU clearing mandate, trade reporting, bilateral margin rules MiFID II Best execution, research unbundling, transparency AIFMD EU authorization, leverage reporting, depositary requirement 13F Long equity disclosure (but shorts + TRS = blind spot = Archegos)

Feature Old (VaR) FRTB --- --- --- Risk metric VaR (99%, 10d) ES (97.5%, variable horizon) Tail risk Ignored beyond VaR Captured (avg loss beyond) Liquidity Single 10d horizon 10d (liquid) to 120d (illiquid) P&L test None Must prove model ≈ actual P&L
Captures Misses --- --- Long equity positions Short positions Options (calls) TRS / synthetic positions Manager-level aggregate Cross-PB exposure Archegos exploited ALL three gaps → $10B bank losses.

Risk Key metric Escalation --- --- --- Counterparty Net exposure / CVA / CDS Single CP > 5% NAV Collateral Coverage ratio, disputes Under-collateralized > $5M CCP IM requirements, default fund IM increase > 20% Operational Trade breaks, system uptime >3 breaks/week; downtime >30 min Regulatory Filing deadlines, pipeline changes <5 days to deadline

- Beyond market risk: "Counterparty (managed via ISDA/CSA/CCP, watch wrong-way risk), Operational (segregation of duties + 3-way recon + kill switch), Regulatory (Basel impacts PB capacity; 13F gap enabled Archegos)" - Monitor counterparty: "Daily: net exposure, collateral coverage, CDS spreads. Structural: ISDA netting, CSA margin, clearing. 1. Excessive leverage — leverage is a liquidation timer, not a return amplifier 2. Illusory diversification — ρ → 1 in crisis; "how many INDEPENDENT bets?" 3. Concentration — single name/factor/trader dominating risk 4. Model failure / gaming — VaR drops 50% without position change = red flag 5. Operational / governance — can't manage risk you can't see 6. Margin / liquidity doom loop — sell → price drops → more margin → more selling 7. Mechanical amplification — forced flows (rebalancing, rolls, stops) amplify shocks

Survival test: $$\text{Survival} = f\left(\frac{\text{Equity cushion}}{\text{Max drawdown path}}\right)$$ Leverage kill math (basis trade): $$50\text{x leverage} \times 25\text{bp adverse} = 12.5\% \text{ notional loss} \gg 1\text{-}2\% \text{ equity}$$ Concentration check: - Position as % of float → >10% = dangerous - Days to exit = position / (ADV × participation rate) → >20 = illiquid

Illusory diversification LTCM 3 Risk metric moved >20% without position change? Model gaming London Whale 4 Stressed margin utilization > 85%? Doom loop UK gilts, Mar 2020 5 Mechanical amplifiers in our names? Forced selling cascade Volmageddon, Aug 2024

1. Leverage + illiquidity + margin call → right at maturity, dead before it 2. Concentration + past-performance justification → "he's earned it" kills funds 3. Model trust + assumption rigidity → map ≠ territory 4. Mechanical selling + ρ → 1 → feedback loop amplifies small shocks 5. Operational gaps + perverse incentives → can hide losses AND benefit from hiding

- LTCM: "Leverage is a liquidation timer. Their trades converged by 2000 — but the fund was dead in Sep 1998." - Archegos: "You can't manage risk you can't see. Each PB saw their slice; no one saw the $30B aggregate." - Barings: "Trader ran front AND back office. £827M hidden in Error Account 88888 for 3 years." - London Whale: "VaR halved overnight with no position change. That's guaranteed model gaming." - Amaranth: "'He's earned the right to size up' is the most dangerous sentence in fund management." - UK Gilts: "Margin doom loops turn orderly drawdowns into existential crises. RM must break the loop." - Aug 2024: "90% mechanical, 10% fundamental. Carry unwind → vol-targeting → CTA stops → ρ = 1."

Fat tails More extreme events than normal predicts Always present (kurtosis 3-10) "What distribution? How underestimated is VaR?" Correlation Co-movement; regime-dependent! ρ → 1 in crisis; flips in inflation "Crisis vs. calm correlation matrix?"

- Persistence = α + β (typical: 0.95-0.99) - Long-run vol = √(ω / (1 - α - β) × 252) - Half-life = -ln(2) / ln(α + β) - α + β ≥ 1.0 → IGARCH → vol shocks NEVER decay → suspicious

Survivorship bias (winners-only data) 7. Look-ahead bias (future info leak) 8. Mark-to-model (favorable internal pricing)

Always check out-of-sample."

section: Monte Carlo — When to Use Over Analytical
content: - Non-linear portfolios (options/gamma) - Path-dependent risk (margin calls, stops) - Fat-tailed inputs (t-distribution, GPD) - Complex correlations (copulas) Cholesky: Correlated returns = L × Z (L = Cholesky factor of Σ)

Concept One-liner --- --- Encapsulation Bundle data and methods together and HIDE internal state — callers use the public interface, they can't touch the guts. Inheritance Child classes get parent behaviour for free and can override it — Equity IS-A Instrument , so it automatically has ticker , quantity , price and only needs to implement what's different. Polymorphism One function works on many types — portfolio risk summary() calls .market value() on every item in the list and doesn't care whether it's an Equity , Bond , or Option . Abstraction Define WHAT a class must do (interface) without saying HOW — Instrument declares market value() as @abstractmethod ; you can't instantiate it until you've implemented the method. Composition Prefer "has-a" over "is-a" — Portfolio HAS a list of Instrument objects; it doesn't inherit from Instrument . Looser coupling, easier to change. @property A method that looks like an attribute — limit.utilization computes on the fly, always consistent, no risk of stale stored state. @staticmethod A utility function that lives on the class but needs no instance or class state — Instrument.from bloomberg ticker("AAPL US") . @classmethod An alternative constructor that receives the class as first arg — Position.from csv row(row) lets you build objects from different data sources. repr What you see in the debugger — implement it so positions print as Equity(AAPL, qty=1000, px=185) not <object at 0x...> . eq / hash Required to put objects in sets or use them as dict keys — define equality by ticker so two Equity("AAPL", ...) objects match.

Principle One-liner --- --- Single Responsibility One class = one reason to change — RiskCalculator computes risk; it does NOT format reports. Open/Closed Open for extension, closed for modification — add CDS(Instrument) without touching Equity . Liskov Substitution A child must be usable wherever its parent is — if code takes Instrument , passing an Option must work correctly. Interface Segregation Don't force a class to implement methods it doesn't need — a thin Priceable interface beats one giant FullInstrument interface. Dependency Inversion Depend on abstractions, not concretions — RiskEngine depends on the RiskModel protocol, not HistoricalVaRModel directly.

Pillar Key idea Risk example --- --- --- Encapsulation Private state, public interface RiskLimit. breach history — can't modify externally Inheritance Parent → Child Instrument → Equity , Bond , Option Polymorphism Same interface, different behavior portfolio risk summary() works for any Instrument Abstraction ABC with @abstractmethod Instrument.market value() — every instrument must implement ---

Pattern One-liner definition Risk use case --- --- --- Strategy Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable at runtime without changing the calling code. Swap VaR method (hist/param/MC) at runtime — same engine.compute() call, different model injected. Observer When one object changes state, all registered dependents are automatically notified — publisher/subscriber without tight coupling. Limit breach → automatically triggers email alert + dashboard update + audit log. Factory Centralise object creation logic so callers don't need to know which concrete class to instantiate. InstrumentFactory.create("equity", row) returns the right class based on instrument type from the DB. Decorator Wrap a function or class to add behaviour (logging, caching, validation) without modifying its source code. @timer measures execution, @validate returns checks for NaNs — both stack on top of compute var() . Pipeline Chain a sequence of processing steps where each step's output is the next step's input. Load → Clean → Calculate → Report — each stage is independent and testable. Singleton Ensure a class has only one instance and provide a global access point to it. Market data cache, config manager — you want one shared source of truth, not multiple copies. ---

- How: Shocks to flat price, curve shape, vol, basis → recalc P&L using delta/gamma/vega/DV01 - Why position-level: Aggregate hides basis risk (long crude / short gas looks flat) - Validation: Replay COVID March 2020 (within 5%), cross-check Bloomberg MARS, sensitivity analysis - Best catch: Liberation Day (Apr 2 2025) — tariffs blew out US vs. international commodity basis. - Output: OI by strike/maturity, day-over-day change, net dealer gamma heatmap, skew metrics - Gamma pin: Strike where dealer gamma = 0. Below = short gamma (amplifies vol — dealers sell into declines, buy into rallies). Above = long gamma (dampens vol). Danger = crossing from long-gamma into short-gamma territory. - Net dealer gamma calc: Assume dealers short options → gamma × OI × multiplier, aggregate by strike

- 4 params: α (vol level), β (CEV backbone, FIXED at 0.5 for rates), ρ (skew, spot-vol correlation), ν (vol-of-vol, controls wings) - Calibration: Levenberg-Marquardt, minimize squared error vs market swaption vols. β fixed before calibration. - Result: 45bps → 30bps RMSE. Matters for: accurate Greeks + PLAT compliance - Limitations: (1) Per-expiry only — no joint surface fit. (2) Wings: Hagan approx breaks down for deep OTM → arbitrage violations. (3) Negative rates: need Free Boundary SABR for EUR swaptions post-2015. - Why SABR over Heston for rates: Analytical (closed-form) = millisecond repricing. Heston needs numerical integration (Fourier) = slower. Speed wins for rates desks pricing full swaption grids in real time. - PLAT thresholds: Spearman correlation test + ratio test. Green = pass both. Amber = fail one ≤20% of days over 12mo. Red = lose IMA status → Standardised Approach → 2-4× higher capital. - NMRFs: Risk factors with <24 verifiable prices in 12 months → punitive stress scenario add-on instead of model-based ES. Fix: improve data sourcing to reclassify as modellable. - Bridge back: "...but the most impactful risk work was at BNP NYC"

- Normal: -0.3 to -0.5 correlation (yields ↓, gold ↑) - Q4 2025: Turned positive = 3σ event. Cause: central bank gold buying (de-dollarization) - Fix: Chow breakpoint test → adjusted correlation → 15% drawdown reduction

- SBM = 3 charges: Delta risk + Vega risk + Curvature risk. Summed, no diversification across the three. - Delta/Vega: Sensitivities to prescribed risk factors → aggregated with regulatory correlation matrices across buckets. Three correlation scenarios (low/medium/high) → take max charge. - Curvature: Full reval under ±shocks to each risk factor → worst-case net P&L. - What I fixed: (1) IR delta was mapped to wrong tenor buckets → inflated charge by ~€2M. (2) Commodity cross-bucket correlation was double-counted → ~€1.5M. (3) Curvature shocks used flat ±1% instead of risk-class-specific sizes → ~€1.5M. - Result: €42M → €37M = €5M relief (12%). At 12-15% ROE target → €600-750K annual earnings capacity freed. - SA limitations: Static correlations (ignore regime), no cross-asset diversification, systematically 1.5-3× more conservative than IMA.

- Problem: 5 desks (Rates, Equities, FX, Commodities, Credit) each had own spreadsheets/scripts. Greeks incomparable — different bump sizes, curve methods, day-count conventions. - Solution: Common risk factor taxonomy + standardized sensitivity methodology (1bp rates, 1% equities/FX/commo) + unified aggregation with cross-desk netting. - Value: CRO gets single aggregated Greek report across all desks. Cross-desk offsets captured (e.g., EUR duration in rates desk + EUR rate exposure embedded in FX positions). - Status: Live on Rates, Equities, Commodities, FX. Credit last (complex exotics) — initially targeted Q1 2026 but slipped; expected end of Q2 2026. - Why still in implementation: Data normalization (different booking systems) + change management (running parallel with old reports until reconciled).

- Architecture: PostgreSQL on AWS RDS (data) → Python/numpy on EC2 (processing) → RDS + dashboards (output). - Purpose: VaR model backtesting. Replay 1M trades/day × 2 years historical data → compare hypothetical vs. model-predicted P&L → Kupiec + Christoffersen tests. - 50% speedup: (1) Vectorized P&L with numpy (~30%). (2) Parallel SQL by asset class (~10%). (3) Cached static data (~10%). - AWS rationale: Scale for quarter-end peaks + multi-user concurrent access. VPC + KMS encryption for compliance. - Limitations: First-order P&L approximation (gamma/vanna error on large moves). No automated data quality framework.
